title: LeFevour pitch After setting NCAA records as Central Michigan's QB, dual-threat dynamo will try to find success at the next level
content:
All Dan LeFevour wanted was an opportunity.

LeFevour wasn't inundated with major-college scholarship offers during his high school football career at Benet Academy in Lisle, but Gary Goforth would tell everyone he was coaching the best quarterback in the state.

There weren't many believers as Big Ten schools such as Illinois, Northwestern and Indiana showed middling interest but didn't get close to making a scholarship offer. Only four of those came LeFevour's way at Benet.

One was from Central Michigan. It turned out to be the opportunity of a lifetime for the Downers Grove native.

"Once I got that I showed I could play pretty well," said the 6-foot-3, 227-pound LeFevour. "I set goals in increments from where I was at that point, and I started wanting to accomplish more and more as I started getting more experience on the field."

What he has accomplished in four seasons with the Chippewas has put his name all over NCAA and Mid-American Conference record books and on NFL draft boards.

LeFevour goes into today's GMAC Bowl (6 p.m., ESPN) in Mobile, Ala., against Troy as the only player in NCAA history to pass for 12,000 yards (12,510) and rush for 2,500 (2,936) in a career.

His 148 career touchdowns (101 passing, 46 rushing and 1 receiving) are the most in Football Bowl Subdivision history. He has led Central Michigan to three MAC titles and four bowl games.

"Nothing he has done surprises me," Goforth said. "In terms of what he's accomplished, you always thought, ‘Wow, you knew he was good,' but it's incredible what he's done."

Not that LeFevour would would ever pop off about what he has accomplished.

"He's a pretty humble kid," said Mike Bijakian, who has been Central Michigan's offensive coordinator and quarterbacks coach the last three seasons. "He's very well-rounded, keeps everything in perspective and doesn't get too high or too low.

"Combined with his ability to run the ball and throw the ball — the numbers don't lie. When you think about what he's been able to do and what he's been able to accomplish, he hasn't done it quietly."

Even at Benet the interest in LeFevour was relatively quiet. The program was successful when LeFevour was there, but Joliet Catholic usually hoards most of the attention in the East Suburban Catholic Conference.

LeFevour also was in a run-oriented offense with receivers Goforth said were good but not big speed threats.

"They're all looking for the guy from the perennial powerhouse … and I've learned over the years it's hard to understand what recruiters want," Goforth said. "I thought Dan was the best quarterback in the state his senior year. There was no doubt he was the best quarterback on the field every game."

That hasn't changed much since he arrived at Central Michigan to run an offense led by Brian Kelly.

"It has to be the right mix of things coming in at the right time," LeFevour said. "There's a lot of talent at Central Michigan and great players to play with, and that presents you with the opportunity to do great things."

Even after Kelly left at the end of LeFevour's freshman year for what turned out to be a stopover at Cincinnati en route to taking the Notre Dame coaching job.

Bijakian came in after a three-year stint as the Bears' offensive quality control coach in which he worked with the wide receivers during their 2006 NFC championship season.

LeFevour's work ethic and diligent film study made it a seamless transition. As a sophomore he put himself on the map with some of college football's biggest names.

He joined Vince Young of Texas in 2005 as the only FBS players to pass for 3,000 yards and rush for 1,000. LeFevour and Florida's Tim Tebow became the only players in FBS history to throw and rush for 20 touchdowns apiece in the same season.

"He does have that special talent of being a true dual-threat quarterback," Bijakian said. "That term gets thrown around a lot and often refers to a quarterback who is good at running who throws the ball.

"Dan definitely goes beyond that with his ability to throw the ball. That's what makes him special to me and one of the best quarterbacks in the country."

LeFevour, who was invited to play in the Senior Bowl on Jan. 30 in Mobile, Ala., does have to overcome the perception of putting up big numbers out of a spread offense in a conference not regarded as a national powerhouse.

But those who know LeFevour well believe he can follow the successful MAC to NFL path that includes the Steelers' Ben Roethlisberger and Dolphins' Chad Pennington.

"He's definitely got the ability and work ethic and mental capability to succeed at the next level," Bijakian said. "Every team in the NFL has been through our hallways and some people have some knocks on him. Arm strength is one of the things people knock a little bit.

"I tell them to watch the film. How I put it to scouts is he makes every throw."

LeFevour knows he has to prove he can make all of them — short, intermediate and deep with touch or zip — under center in a pro style offense.

Goforth compares LeFevour's ability to make all of them with the Ravens' Joe Flacco, who came out of Delaware in the Football Championship Subdivision (formerly Division I-AA).

"People aren't going to realize how strong his arm is until they see him in person," Goforth said. "He doesn't overthrow.

"There is no doubt that Dan being a student of the game, whoever gets him will have just found a gold coin, so to speak."

Not unlike all of the golden moments LeFevour has produced at Central Michigan.

"It's been amazing," LeFevour said. "To be able to win three conference championships in four years and do all the great things we've been able to do, I'll never forget it and it will last me the rest of my life."

title: From family feud to savoring price is right
content:
SAN DIEGO -- Jon Gruden hadn't slept for two days. His voice was practically gone, his body running on adrenaline. But within two hours of landing in a private jet that had whisked him across the country to catch up with his team, he already had offered up the most telling moment thus far of Super Bowl XXXVII.

Gruden, the boy-wonder coach whose stock is rising like Microsoft in the 1990s, was talking about the original Oakland Raider/Chucky Doll he still proudly displays despite being traded 11 months ago to coach the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

Suddenly, he stopped and shared this story:

"My 2-year-old boy, Jayson? Every time he sees football (on television)? He says, 'Ray-duhz,' " Gruden said, using his Davis imitation for the last word.

"It's the only word he can say. 'Football?' 'Ray-duh.'

"He can't say 'Buccaneers' yet."

Gruden paused, allowing what he said to sink in.

Then he belted a hearty laugh.

Welcome to Chucky Bowl I, one of the most amazing, juicy and, for the key participants, even surreal matchups in Super Bowl history.

Super Bowl XXXVII is Gruden against a Raiders franchise he spent four years shaping back to championship form, now leading a Bucs team making its first Super Bowl appearance after 27 years.

It's Gruden against dominating Oakland owner Al Davis, who shipped him off for an unprecedented bounty after contract extension talks broke off and turned the end of the 2001 season into something of an in-house Ice Bowl.

It's Gruden against one of his best friends, Bill Callahan, who, after being thrown in as successor 11 months ago, led the Raiders to their first Super Bowl since 1983.

It's Gruden against MVP Rich Gannon, Jerry Rice, Charlie Garner and all those Raiders stars acknowledging this week that they came to Oakland mainly because of him.

It's also as strange as one of those new NFL television commercials playing out in real life inside the Gruden home.

"That freaks me out, man," Gruden says of the super scenario. "I still can't believe it."

Many, after all, are picking Gruden, 39, the NFL's youngest head coach, to have his Bucs (14-4) outsmart the favored Raiders (13-5) because he knows them so well.

"It's weird, man. It's exciting. It's going to be awesome, man," he says. "I'm really looking forward to this."

As are a lot of people. The Super Bowl hasn't had this many team-to-team story lines since, well, the last time the Raiders -- the real Raiders bad boys -- were involved.

"The stars have got to get aligned for something like this to happen," Callahan says. "It's a situation . . . it's just bonkers.

"It's absolutely crazy."

Callahan snapped back into coach mode, saying once the ball is kicked off Sunday, all the Chucky Bowl talk will exit for both teams. In the same way he told his players at the start of the week to not let this game become them vs. Gruden.

"I mean, it is an unbelievable coincidence," Raiders Pro Bowl tackle Lincoln Kennedy says. "But this is so much bigger than that."

The NFL has never seen anything like this, however. As former Buc Frank Middleton, now an anchor guard of the Raiders' dominant offensive line, put it, "Who wrote thisbook?"

Midnight call does the trick

Two years ago, the Super Bowl saw two great friends face off with New York Giants coach Jim Fassel pitted against Baltimore's Brian Billick. Two years before that, the feud between Denver's Mike Shanahan and former Broncos coach-now Atlanta coach Dan Reeves came full circle.

But this? Imagine if Jimmy Johnson had led Miami against Jerry Jones and the Dallas Cowboys in the Super Bowl the year after the two staged a public divorce.

"And I thought it was crazy when we played San Francisco," says Gruden's mother, Kathy, referring to the NFC divisional playoff two weeks ago.

That game pitted Gruden against his father, Jim, a longtime scout with the 49ers, the team his son broke in with and still emulates. And there was the matchup of coaching buddies Gruden and Steve Mariucci, who both nearly went to Tampa on the same day.

The moment it was clear that Gruden would face the Raiders in the Super Bowl, everyone went back to Feb. 17. Gruden faxed back and forth with members of the Glazer family who own the Bucs, signing off on the blockbuster trade that would land Davis $ 8 million and four high draft picks and get Gruden a five-year deal worth $ 17.5 million.

The next morning, Gruden told Callahan to go run the coaches' meeting. Callahan has ever since, his defense bolstered by Davis' parlay.

The Bucs saved face, too, after a month-long debacle to replace popular Tony Dungy, including broken deals with Bill Parcells and Marvin Lewis and initially being rejected by Davis to land Gruden. On the same day they interviewed Mariucci, the Glazers made another shot at Gruden. They knew Davis and Gruden were rarely communicating.

At issue was the coach's contract, which was to pay Gruden through this season. Gruden had turned down two of his dream jobs, Ohio State in 2000 and Notre Dame in 2001, to hopefully see his Raiders team all the way to the Super Bowl. Despite leading the Raiders to the AFC title game in his third season, Gruden was paid just $ 1 million in 2001 -- far under scale, especially with how popular he had become.

In some ways, Gruden actually would become bigger than Davis, the Howard Hughes-like Hall of Famer. Oakland fans were gobbling up Gruden-Chucky dolls via the Internet; Gruden was listed in People magazine for his boyish good looks.

But in football terms, Gruden was still a classic grinder, a master student of the West Coast offense, already one of the best at calling the two-minute offense.

"He is not going to allow himself to get out-coached once he gets" to the Super Bowl," says St. Louis Rams scout David Razzano, who, with Gruden, is part of a brat pack of NFL talent breaking in with the 49ers in the early '90s. "That's why he stayed back on Monday."

That's also how Gruden convinced future Hall of Famer Rice to join the Raiders in 2000. And land Gannon in '99, the start of a love-hate relationship that pushed the former journeyman quarterback into a consistent MVP candidate.

So when Davis' extension offer was far from what the Raiders had indicated in money and length -- Davis says it was for three years and $ 9.5 million -- Gruden felt betrayed after all his hard work. He had, after all, turned the Raiders from being classic underachievers to a team loaded with true football players, capturing the first two of their three AFC West titles in a row.

"I have some memorabilia there, sure," Gruden says. "I'm very proud of the four years there. Are you kidding me? Proud as hell."

Gruden was grateful to get the Raiders job instead of Art Shell or Denny Green in 1998. Yet his relationship with Davis had slipped over the years, the owner often sending messages through assistants. Their goal was always the same. But after Gruden confronted Davis and right-hand man Bruce Allen regarding the change in extension offer, the relationships frosted. Still, most expected Gruden to work until his contract expired after this season.

"I don't think he was driven away," Davis said after the trade.

This week, however, even former Raiders coach turned TV analyst John Madden, who has remained close with Davis, said, "Bottom line was, Al Davis wasn't going to pay Jon Gruden four or five million dollars."

So after the Glazers called Davis, the owner woke Gruden close to midnight. Late-night calls from Davis are nothing new for any Raiders head coach. But this sounded like a dream to Gruden, whose family had lived in Tampa for years.

One of Gruden's closest friends in the business was the Bucs' grandfatherly defensive coordinator, Monte Kiffin; the two vacation with their families each summer on the Florida coast. Aside from Gruden, Kiffin and his defense are the main reason the Bucs are in the Super Bowl, with a legitimate chance of derailing the top-rated Raiders offense.

Problem was, Gruden never said goodbye to his Raiders players, leaving many feeling abandoned.

"It was the only way to do it," Gruden says. "I was traded."

Outlandish scenarios realized

Behind his fiery image, Gruden was clearly hurt by the talk after he left Oakland. Even this week, Raiders star receiver Jerry Porter was unabashed in criticizing Gruden for not getting him the ball as promised -- although it was Gruden who fought to draft Porter in the first place.

But, as expected, most close to the situation have taken the high road. While Davis maintained his recluse mystique, Gannon, Rice and Garner said they wouldn't be Raiders if not for Gruden. Callahan went even further, reminding people he wouldn't be in the league if not for Gruden, who brought him to the Philadelphia Eagles as an assistant in 1995.

"I'm just going to love it, man," Gruden says. "I could be like, 'Hey, Bill, remember that time I talked you into coming out of Wisconsin to go to Philly to be the line coach? Can you believe we're coaching Super Bowl XXXVII?'

"That's the thing I laugh about, man. We sat underneath the Vet, arguing about how we were going to block '92 Weak.' Ten years later we're coaching against each other in the Super Bowl!"

Gruden's mother laughed about the scenario, as well, before sharing another story:

When Gruden returned from winning the conference title Sunday at Philadelphia, his mother offered to stay behind with Jayson so the coach and his wife, Cyndy, could have fun with their older sons during the Super Bowl festivities.

"Mom," Gruden said, "You are going to the Super Bowl. And Jayson is going to the Super Bowl. That's it."

It was time, apparently, to teach the kid another word.Continued fromCover storyCover storyCover story

title: Bucs Back To Work, But Hardly Business As Usual
content:
MOVING FORWARD MINUS KEY WON'T BE EASY

TAMPA - Meanwhile, back on the practice field, the Bucs began preparing for the New York Giants. And for life without Keyshawn.

On the first day of the flamboyant wide receiver's exile, Joe Jurevicius took Johnson's place in the starting lineup, Charles Lee moved up a notch on the depth chart and the Bucs, en masse, moved on.

"It's what we have to do," quarterback Brad Johnson said. "We have to move on. I mean, we dealt with Rickey Dudley going down, we dealt with Mike Alstott going down and we dealt with Joe going down. We'll deal with this, too."No one expects it to be easy. Though he had become intolerable off the field, Johnson is still considered something special on it.

One NFL team executive on Wednesday described him as a "deluxe first-down maker who will block for you and make the tough catch over the middle."

Jurevicius is cut from the same mold, but not even he is willing to say he's capable of doing all Johnson did for the Bucs offense.

"I don't know if I have the accolades that Keyshawn has in terms of [drawing] double teams," he said.

The Bucs aren't sure Jurevicius has those accolades either. They're confident he will make plenty of plays, but chances are they'll have to alter their offense to make up for Johnson's absence.

"They'll probably have to replace him with scheme," the executive said. "They'll have to find other ways to make first downs."

Throwing to the backs more often is one way. Making more use of screen passes and the tight ends is another.

"You'd like to think we're going to get a few more balls," tight end Dudley said, "but either way, the coaches will do a good job of moving guys around and setting them up."

That's Coach Jon Gruden's strength. He finds ways to create mismatches, and his remaining players are confident he'll continue to do that.

"You have to think about what you have and not worry about what you don't have," Brad Johnson said. "We have to be positive about it. That's the way we have to look at it.

"I mean, it's a great opportunity for Joe and a great opportunity for Charles. This is how I [first] got my job [as an NFL starting quarterback]. One guy goes down and the next guy plays."

Until last week, Jurevicius wasn't capable of playing. He was still recovering from a torn right knee ligament. He said Wednesday, though, that he's strong enough to do all that his new role requires.

"I made it through another practice today and I mentioned earlier that I was able to step off the field on Sunday and that's big," he said. "I remember making a few cuts out there where, after I ran my pass pattern, I realized that if I didn't blow my knee out on that one, then I'm not blowing my knee out."

Lee has been capable of playing all year, but he lost the opportunity to do so during a preseason in which he dropped almost as many passes as he caught.

Faster and slightly bigger than Johnson, Lee will now get plenty of opportunities to prove he can be that first-down maker the Bucs lost when they decided to deactivate Johnson for their final six games.

"I can do that for this team," Lee said. "It's all about opportunity, being focused and paying attention to detail and being ready when your chance comes."

Johnson's absence may result in leading receiver Keenan McCardell getting more chances, but there is just as good a chance the Bucs will wind up throwing less to McCardell now that Johnson is gone.

"I'll probably see more double teams now," McCardell said when asked to assess the effect Johnson's absence from the lineup will have. "We were both seeing our share before. Now it'll probably be more toward me.

"But we have guys like Joe and Charles Lee and Reggie Barlow and Karl Williams that have to step up and I think they will. I have the utmost confidence in those guys that they'll do their job."

So does Brad Johnson. He said the loss of Keyshawn will not alter the way he goes through his progressions.

"I don't think twice about who's in there," he said. "If he's part of the route progression and he's open, then I'll throw to him. If he's not open, then I won't throw to him."

Having the time to locate those receivers may be the real key to success. That's how Gruden sees it. When asked how he would make up for Johnson's loss, he put the onus on his struggling offensive line.

"We've had 56 offensive penalties this year, including 17 false starts, 15 of which we've committed at home," Gruden said. "Those are the concerns we're addressing. If we can clean that up, we'll be fine."

The Bucs attempted to clean up that area Wednesday by revamping their offensive line yet again. This time they reverted to the lineup that started the season. Roman Oben is back at left tackle, Cosey Coleman at left guard, John Wade at center, Jason Whittle at right guard and Kenyatta Walker at right tackle.

The last time the Bucs started a game with that unit, they beat the Washington Redskins 35-13.

title: 2DAY
content:
09.24.10 THE GAME PLAN

THE BUZZ Brett Favre lined up as a defensive back during group drills in practice Thursday, which is certainly one way to cut down on Favre's early interception habit.

REMOTE PATROL The prescription for preseason hockey fever? Truth be told, we didn't know the condition existed. But still, check out Wild at Blues (7 p.m., Ch. 45).

Click pick RB Chester Taylor apologized to Bears fans on Twitter for not knowing how to change his profile picture from one that has him in a Vikings uniform.

STAR GAZING NBA Commissioner David Stern advised Gilbert Arenas to stop talking about his locker room gun incident and to talk "only about the future."

FAST FACT Craig Finn, noted Twins fan and lead singer of The Hold Steady, has teamed up with The Baseball Project for a new song: "Don't Call Them Twinkies."

WHAT THEY MIGHT BE DOING TODAY ...

Jose Bautista might be waking up and realizing, "I HIT 50 HOME RUNS THIS SEASON!"

Ichiro Suzuki might be the envy of hitters everywhere with his 200 hits in 10 straight years.

Jay Cutler might be raising expectations in Chicago, only to break Bears fans' hearts.

FIVE THINGS

To reiterate, the Nos. 5-9 hitters in the Twins' lineup Wednesday in a 5-1 victory over Cleveland:

-Jose Morales

-Matt Tolbert

-Ben Revere

-Jason Repko

-Drew Butera

Got a good sports list? Send it to mrand@ startribune.com

QUOTE OF THE DAY

"My agents and teams interested did everything to make it happen, but [the Chargers] stopped it. I just want to play football. It feels unethical and I am disappointed." -- Chargers WR Vincent Jackson, in a text message to the NFL Network.

Howard Sinker: Do the Twins really need two lefty relievers in playoffs?

Prevailing wisdom right now is that either Scott Baker or Kevin Slowey will be left off the roster because the Twins need only four starting pitchers -- and one of those two will be the long man in the bullpen. That would make sense, except that Jose Mijares, Glen Perkins and Randy Flores aren't exactly lefties with a track record of retiring lefties. Should any of those three be on the roster? We've written a few times that Flores has faced eight lefties since joining the Twins, retiring one of 'em. For the season, including his time in Colorado, lefties have a .293 average/.388 on-base percentage/.888 OPS against him. Mijares? His numbers are .283/.321/.812 -- and lefties have extra-base hits against him in nine of 57 plate appearances. Perkins? This season, his tiny sample numbers against lefties are .190/.261/.499 in 23 PAs. But his career numbers are .318/.392/.833. If the Twins had a more reliable second lefty, it wouldn't be much of an issue. But by the numbers and in their makeup, I'd rather have any of the three set-up righties facing a left-handed bat than the current versions of Mijares or Perkins. And I'd rather have Slowey and Baker on the roster than one of those lefties.

A Fan's View: www.startribune.com/twins

title: SIDELINES Celebrity jinx
content:
HIDDEN TALENTS

RUGBY players Jimmy Gopperth and No 8 Thomas Waldrom unveil their lesser- known sportingtalents during the annual Hurricanes Surf to Peak triathlon in Wellington today.

Gopperth is the defending champion of the event -- which includes a swim at Lyall Bay, a cycle to town and a run up Mt Victoria -- after clocking a winning time of 1h 8min in 2006. Waldrom is said to be weak on the cycle, but if he is close going into the final leg he's tipped as the dark horse after dominating several long pre-season runs.

GOING FOR BROKE

LA LAKERS coach Phil Jackson has always had a slightly unusual approach to basketball, but it's unclear where his latest comments fit within his Zen philosophies.

Following Tuesday night's game in San Antonio, Jackson was asked if too much penetration had given the Spurs outside shooters too much space after they made 13 three- pointers in their 107-92 win.

"We call this a Brokeback Mountain game, because there's so much penetration and kickouts," Jackson said. "It was one of those games."

The 2005 film, which won three Oscars, depicts two cowboys who conceal their homosexual affair.

"The remarks are in poor taste, and the Lakers have assured us such remarks will not occur in the future," NBA spokesman Brian McIntyre said.

FATAL DISTRACTION

BLACK CAP Brendon McCullum may have been on the front foot on the park during the Chappell-Hadlee series, but two glamorous teenage girls in Sydney had him playing a less than convincing backfoot defensive shot outside the team hotel on Saturday.

McCullum was looking straight down the camera answering serious questions about the New Zealand cricket team's chances against Australia when the girls worked their way into his eye-line and started smiling at him.

The interviewer quickly wrapped things up after McCullum's answers became increasingly distracted.

"I couldn't hold it together at the end," a blushing McCullum told Sidelines.

--------------------

CAPTION:

Celebrity jinx. CELEBRITY Jessica Simpsonis no longer welcome at Dallas Cowboys NFL matches after her boyfriend, quarterback Tony Romo,produced the worst statistical performance of his career in a 10-6 loss to the Philadelphia Eagles earlier this week. Simpson turned up to the match wearing a pink No 9 jersey, which she proudly showed off for television cameras. Dallas Cowboys fans and players are urging her to stay away for the remainder of the season with wide receiver Terrell Owens saying: "Right now, Jessica Simpsonis not a fan favourite in this locker room or in Texas Stadium." The problem for Simpson is Romo's previous worst game came last December, also at home to the Eagles, when his then-girlfriend Carrie Underwood was in attendance. Sidelines is unsure whether Dan Carter's partner Honor Dillon attended the World Cup quarterfinal in Cardiff.
