title: Who's got game? The Super Bowl is the goal, but even the best have a tough time getting there
content:
They've tracked their prey this far.

Now they move in for the kill, 12 teams taking aim.

Their quarry: Big game. Actually, the Big Game. The elusive prize so often at the heart of the story about the one that got away.

The Super Bowl is out there, stalked desperately by many, landed by few.

Tiki Barber can tell that story. Tony Dungy, too. The ardent pursuers never stop. Heartbreak won't restrain Peyton Manningor Marty Schottenheimer, and previous tastes of glory only drive others -- Bill Parcells, Mike Holmgren, Tom Brady-- to feast again.

It's out there.

And so the hunt begins.

There's more involved here than the thrill of the chase. At the end, in an NFL championship, lies an intimation of immortality and confirmation of a life's work.

Here in the NFL playoffs is the reward, the tangible culmination of effort and ability and the bright line between the best and all the rest.

"Good," says San Diego Chargers coach Marty Schottenheimer, "is the enemy of great. Your ability to focus on being better than you are will enable you to move on successfully. If you're content with being good, you can never be great."

True. Was the known world conquered by Alexander the OK? No. Alexander the Great. Was Russia modernized by Peter the Adequate? No. Peter the Great. Step up or step out.

Who would better understand this than Schottenheimer, whose 20-year coaching career includes 200 victories but no Super Bowls and plenty of wrenching losses in a 5-12 playoff career? Well, lots of people.

Take the Indianapolis Colts.

Their president and general manager, Bill Polian, built a Buffalo Bills team that went to four Super Bowls (three while he was on staff) but won none. He constructed the expansion Carolina Panthers, who went to the NFC Championship Game in their second season, only to come up short. The Colts have won 50 regular-season games in capturing four consecutive division titles and they're in the playoffs for a fifth year in a row, but they've reached one conference championship game (2003 season) and lost.

Their coach, Tony Dungy, has been to the playoffs nine of his last 11 seasons (including his time with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers) and twice to conference championship games, yet the big one -- like some ornery old catfish -- taunts from the shadows.

Same goes for the quarterback, Peyton Manning,who heard too much about "not winning the big one" at the University of Tennessee, when Florida owned the Volunteers, and hears it still.

Playoff success and a Super Bowl. That's the measuring stick.

"That's the way our league is set up and that's how you win championships, by the playoffs," Dungy says. "So, that's when you have to produce and you have to play well."

Maybe greatness lies ahead and maybe not. Twelve teams have a chance at it and 20 do not.

"All you can really ask for," Manning says, "is an opportunity."

Winning it all 'just hard to do'

The safari approaches and the beasts scatter. So it is with the Big Game, which sometimes seems certain it does not want to be captured by its most ardent pursuers. Winning the Super Bowl, Seattle Seahawks coach Mike Holmgren says, "is just hard to do."

He knows. As head coach of the Green Bay Packers, he helped turn a dormant franchise into a perennial contender. The Packers won Super Bowl XXXI and went back a year later, only to lose. Holmgren is one of five coaches (the other active one is the Dallas Cowboys' Bill Parcells) to take two franchises to the Super Bowl. But no coach has won with both. Holmgren's Seahawks were beaten last February by the Pittsburgh Steelers.

The playoffs are different than the regular season, just as the Big Game varies from everything that precedes it. That is a lesson Dungy wants his team to understand. Nothing it has done to this point -- good or bad (and have you seen that rushing defense?) -- counts anymore.

"I told the team everything starts over in the playoffs. It doesn't matter what your record was, how you finished, whether you were resting, whatever you were doing. It doesn't matter. It's how you play these next 60-minute increments," Dungy says. "You have to play one game and do a good job and win that one before you can worry about the next one. It's just the playoffs are different and we've got to be ready."

They're different in this way, too.

"If you lose, you come home, pack your bags and you're done," says New York Giants running back Tiki Barber whose retirement plans make any game potentially his last.

The Colts, with all of their experience and heartache, know the stakes as well. But how are the New Orleans Saints supposed to traverse the terrain? This team has 32 new players from last year's 3-13 band of nomads and last reached the postseason in 2000. Its playoff history would make one of the thinnest books in sports publishing -- five previous ventures onto winner-take-all turf, one victory.

"This is my sixth year here and my first opportunity to get in the playoffs," says Saints running back Deuce McAllister. "It's probably like being a little kid, in that aspect, or just excited about going and seeing the emotions of it and seeing how everything picks up."

Or falls apart. This group of 12 features a few balanced teams that seem to have the stuff of champions -- the Chargers, the Baltimore Ravens, maybe the New England Patriots, simply because they've bagged the Big Game three times in this decade. Maybe the Chicago Bears because they'll play at home and the NFC is so weak.

The rest of the big-game hunters? Duck and cover when they start shooting.

A little bit of everything

There's a worst-to-first group in the Philadelphia Eagles, who were last in their division a year ago and atop it this time. Same goes for the Saints. Neither has ever won a Super Bowl, though the Eagles went two years ago with Donovan McNabb at quarterback. This time, rejuvenated retread Jeff Garcia, who last appeared in the postseason following the 2002 season with the San Francisco 49ers, is at the helm.

There's a first-to-worst entry, the Giants, who won the NFC East a year ago and qualified for these playoffs as the sixth and final NFC seed at 8-8. There are the Baltimore Ravens, 6-10 a year ago and close to seeing their coach dismissed and now 13-3 and the AFC's second seed.

There are the teams with the long memories.

The Cowboys, who won three Super Bowls in four years in the 1990s, last won a playoff game in 1996. And there is the team the Cowboys chased out of Dallas back in the day when the NFL and AFL fought for turf -- the Kansas City Chiefs.

They are a tournament team for the second time in nine years and they have not won a playoff game since January 1994. Five consecutive losses. Their last blast of glory? Sticking to the Big Game standard, Super Bowl IV. The one coming up on Feb. 4? Super Bowl XLI.

Last playoff go-round? Colts 38, Chiefs 31, following the 2003 season, at Arrowhead Stadium. That spectacle featured 842 yards of offense and no punts. This one takes place at the Colts' cozy dome, where they were 8-0.

Herman Edwards loves challenges, and the Chiefs' first-year coach has one.

"Their team has played in a lot of playoff games. This team has not. That's something we're going to have to understand when we go there," he says. "There's a different mindset, a different feel in how you play. We're playing against a team that has a lot of experience in playoff games and you've got a coach that's coached in a lot of playoff games and won games. I've coached against Tony and I've been on the sidelines with him in a lot of playoff games, too. I know how he thinks and how he prepares his football teams."

There are hot teams (the Eagles have won their last five) and cold (Dallas lost its last two games and three of four). Quarterbacks who are peaking (Garcia) and some who are freaking (the Giants' Eli Manning, Chicago's Rex Grossman and Dallas' Tony Romo). Quarterbacks who bounced back from injuries (New Orleans' Drew Brees, the Jets' Chad Pennington) and those who rarely miss a snap (Peyton Manninghas started every game of his nine-year career). Those noted for how they show in big games (two-time Super Bowl MVP Tom Bradyof the Patriots) and first-time callers (San Diego's Philip Rivers).

There are running backs in their prime (San Diego's LaDainian Tomlinson, Seattle's Shaun Alexander, Kansas City's Larry Johnson, Philadelphia's Brian Westbrook), those in their rookie years (New England's Laurence Maroney, Indy's Joe Addai) and one on the exit ramp (Barber).

There are first-time head coaches making miracles (the Saints' Sean Payton, the New York Jets' Eric Mangini) and the elders who mentored them (Parcells, the Patriots' Bill Beli-chick).

There are teams that have won recently (the Patriots) and those with but one trophy from long ago (the Jets, Super Bowl III, lone appearance).

Parcells, twice a Super Bowl winner with the Giants, knows that many take aim but few bag the big one. Such defining moments separate players and coaches from their peers. As Parcells puts it, "How many pelts does he have on his pony?"

And so the hunt begins.

title: Earth to Redskins' Portis: Just run the ball and not your big mouth
content:
Washington Redskins running back Clinton Portis, known for his outspoken viewpoints, had plenty to say on the radio Tuesday about female reporters in NFL locker rooms.

Addressing the NFL’s investigation of the New York Jets’ treatment of TV Azteca reporter Ines Sainz, Portis said that both sides have wandering eyes when the sexes are mixed in such a setting.

“I think you put women reporters in the locker room in position to see guys walking around naked, and you sit in the locker room with 53 guys, and all of the sudden you see a nice woman in the locker room. I think men are going to tend to turn and look and want to say something to that woman,” Portis said on 106.7 The Fan in Washington, D.C.

The Jets are being investigated by the NFL after a few players made catcalls directed at Sainz on Saturday while she was waiting to interview quarterback Mark Sanchez.

Portis said he wasn’t aware of what went on with Sainz, but he offered his opinion on what he considered to be a female reporter’s perspective.

“You know, somebody got to spark her interest, or she’s going to want somebody. I don’t know what kind of woman won’t, if you get to go and look at 53 men’s (bodies),” Portis said.

“I know you’re doing a job, but at the same time, the same way I’m going to cut my eye if I see somebody worth talking to, I’m sure they do the same thing.”

The league swiftly condemned his comments as inappropriate and offensive, and Portis later issued an apology through the Redskins.

“I was wrong to make the comments I did, and I apologize,” Portis said in a statement. “I respect the job that all reporters do. It is a tough job and we all have to work and act in a professional manner. I understand and support the team on these issues.”

Want to be like Muhammad Ali?

A new 5th Street Gym will open next month on the site of the famous Miami Beach gym where Muhammad Ali and other top boxers trained.

Angelo Dundee, who trained Ali, is a partner in the ownership group.

His brother, Chris Dundee, who died 12 years ago, opened the original gym in 1951 and operated it for three decades.

Chris Dundee promoted weekly cards on Tuesday nights at the Miami Beach Auditorium and Convention Center in the 1950s and ’60s. The original gym was torn down in 1993.

The new gym will be for those seeking workouts and for professional boxers and mixed martial arts fighters in training. Trivia time

What NFL quarterback threw 70 passes in a single game?

From the bunker to a bail hearing

Headline in the London Daily Mail after greenskeepers at the Royal North Devon golf club discovered a Land Rover on its side in a sand trap and two men asleep in it after an apparent night of partying: “Time to get the driver out.”

Human punchline

From Steve Simmons of the Toronto Sun: “A fighter whose name is better than his jab: Tyson Fury.”

Trivia answer

Drew Bledsoe, who completed 45 passes in 70 attempts in a 26-20 overtime win against the Minnesota Vikings on Nov. 13, 1994.

COMPILED BY John keller FROM NEWS SERVICES, ONLINE REPORTS

title: SHERMAN GIVES TEAM FIVE-DAY REPRIEVE
content:
The Green Bay Packers bolted for the airport Thursday with a five-day pass from coach Mike Sherman, who gave them extra time off because of the schedule.

With their next game on a Monday night, Nov.4, against the Miami Dolphins, Sherman called off practices until Wednesday.

"Normally, I would have given them just the weekend," Sherman said.

Cornerback Mike McKenzie (groin) and fullback Najeh Davenport (hamstring) returned

to practice Thursday, leaving just quarterback Brett Favre and left tackle Chad Clifton, who have sprained knees, on the sideline.

Sherman said he thought McKenzie would return at left cornerback against the Dolphins. He has missed the last three games

"I think it looks good for Mike. He's responding well to rehab and appears to be on target to practice next week, whether it's Wednesday or Thursday, I can't really say just yet," Sherman said.

Clifton was expected to return next week, but center Mike Flanagan might make his second start at left tackle if he doesn't heal quickly enough.

"Clifton is responding well, but I'm a little bit more unsure of him right now," Sherman said.

Tuaolo to come out

Esera Tuaolo, who played nine NFL season as a defensive lineman, will reveal he is gay on HBO's "Real Sports" on Tuesday and in the next issue of ESPN The Magazine, ESPN.com reported.

Tuaolo was a third-round draft choice by the Green Bay Packers in 1991. He also played for the Panthers, Vikings, Jaguars and Falcons.

Extra points

The Chicago Bears turned to quarterback Chris Chandler for the second consecutive week, naming him the starter after trainers ruled out Jim Miller, who has tendinitis in his throwing shoulder and elbow. … Linebackers Dan Morgan and Mark Fields both returned to practice for the Carolina Panthers, but rookie running back DeShaun Foster, still recovering from a knee injury that has sidelined him all season, was downgraded to doubtful. … Art Shell was hired by the NFL to handle player appeals of on-field disciplinary action. … San Diego Chargers safety Rodney Harrison was suspended without pay for one game by the NFL for his third serious rules violation in the past 16 games, a helmet-to-helmet hit on Oakland's Jerry Rice last Sunday. … Emmitt Smith has agreed to give the Pro Football Hall of Fame either the jersey or helmet he wears when he becomes the NFL's career rushing leader.

title: BETTOR'S GUIDE. INSIDE THE MATCHUPS
content:
GIANTS at PACKERS

4:15 p.m., Ch. 5, Packers by 3, 43

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: It's the first meeting between Big Blue and Team Cheese since the NFC title game three seasons ago. A trip to the Super Bowl was on the line that day, and a playoff spot hinges on this Sunday's outcome. The Jints can clinch a spot with a victory while the Pack needs to win out (Green Bay hosts the Bears next week) to reach the postseason. So, have the Giants put last week's collapse behind them? We'll see against a Packer team whose defense is tied for first in points allowed (15.7 per game) and will have Aaron Rodgersback at QB. Clay Matthews, who leads the NFC with 13 sacks, will try to make it a long day on the tundra for Eli Manning.But with Justin Tuck and Osi Umenyiora being the only teammates in the league with 10 sacks apiece, a little holiday advice for Rodgers: Duck!

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Giants and the over.

JETS at BEARS

1 p.m., Ch. 2, Bears by 1, 361/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: Only the Jets could shift the focus off a pivotal game such as this toward what amounts to a grown-up version of "This little piggy went to market!" Well, they better refocus here as the Bears, despite wrapping up the NFC North last Monday, can get a first-round bye by winning out. This is Gang Green's first visit to Soldier Field in 13 seasons and what the Jets have waiting for them is the league's third-ranked run defense (89.8 yards a game). With Mark Sanchez nursing a sore shoulder, you would expect the Jets to grind it out with LaDainian Tomlinson and Shonn Greene, keeping the ball away from the suddenly resurgent Jay Cutler. The Jets special teams may also want to kick the ball AWAY from Devin Hester, who has an NFL-record 14 kick returns for TD. At least the Jets won't have Matt Dodge doing their punting. All in all, we think Rex Ryan will kiss the feet of LT and Greene if they can shred the Bears defense. IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Bears and the over.

LIONS at DOLPHINS

1 p.m., Dolphins by 31/2, 411/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: Detroit finally nailed down a road win in Tampa last week and what better place to try for a replay than South Beach? The Lions will be trying for a third straight win (when was the last time that sentence was written?) against a team that simply refuses to win at home. Miami (1-6 at home, 6-1 on the road) has lost eight of its last nine at home and, quite frankly, that's not sitting too well with the octogenarian crowd in South Florida. So, to quote the gang from "A Christmas Story," we double dog dare the Lions to win on the road again. Or is it we double dog dare the Dolphins to win at home? Decisions, decisions.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Lions and the under.

PATRIOTS at BILLS

1 p.m., Patriots by 8, 441/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: So you get underwear and a Chia Pet this Christmas and think your holiday stinks? Try being the Bills. What better way to spend Christmas than entertaining New England? Having the Patriots in your home for the holidays is like having that annoying couple from down the block that has everything babbling about how expensive their gifts were while you are spreading Nutella on crackers and calling them hors d'oeuvres. The poor Bills have dropped 14 straight to the Pats, and with New England looking to wrap up home field throughout the playoffs, we like the annoying Patriots to make it 15 straight.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Patriots and the over.

COLTS at RAIDERS

4 p.m., Colts by 3, 47

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: It's been a turnaround season for the Raiders, and let's face it, the NFL is so much more fun when the Raiders are good. But unless Ken Stabler, Ted Hendricks, Jack Tatum, Fred Biletnikoff and Marv Hubbard hop into their hot tub time machine, we think the Silver and Black's run for a postseason spot ends here. The Colts will be without Austin Collie and Darren McFadden is helping to spark an Oakland offense that has run up almost 1,000 yards the last two games. But as we saw again last week, you can never count out Peyton Manning when his back is to the wall, like it is here.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Colts and the over.

BOLTS at BENGALS

4 p.m., Chargers by 71/2, 441/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: The Bengals, who played as if they went on Christmas vacation in October, will be without Terrell Owens (surgery, done for the year) and possibly Chad Ochostinko (bum ankle) while Cedric Benson will be facing a Chargers defense that has allowed seven points in its last two games. While Cincy may not have anything to play for - like that ever stopped the Bengals from playing like, well, like they had nothing to play for - San Diego is still in the hunt for the AFC West crown. And don't expect the cold to hinder Philip Rivers. He's 10-0 in his career in December games. Sort of the NFL's answer to Mr. October.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Chargers and the over.

49ERS at RAMS

1 p.m., Ch. 5, Rams by 21/2, 40

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: While the Patriots and Falcons try to reach the penthouse in their respective conferences, the Rams and Niners are battling for the NFL outhouse (aka the NFC West title). The only thing more disturbing than learning that Rex Ryan's wife may go by the handle, "ihaveprettyfeet" (our handle is "bunion-boy") is that one of the mediocre-at-best teams in this division of dregs is actually going to host a playoff game next month. The Rams have the better shot at being that team. Look for Sam Bradford to snap out of his recent funk (0 TDs, 5 INTs in last 3 games) and put the Rams one step closer to their first division title since 2003.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Rams and the under.

RAVENS at BROWNS

1 p.m., Ravens by 31/2, 391/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: The Browns are coming off a loss to dreadful Cincy, saw both Peyton Hillis and Colt McCoy hobble all week through practice and now face a Ravens team looking to not only clinch a third straight playoff appearance, but one that is also in the running for a division title. Sounds like a good spot to be the Browns, no? Cleveland should expect a steady diet of (Ray) Rice, who is coming off a season-best 153 yards vs. New Orleans. After losses to Buffalo and Cincy the last two weeks, Cleveland's Mike Adams said, "It leaves a nasty taste in your mouth." Well, you're not supposed to win this one, Mike. So your mouth may be minty fresh come late Sunday.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Ravens and the under.

VIKINGS at EAGLES

8:30 p.m., Ch. 4, Eagles by 141/2, 431/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: We could think of more interesting things to do than watch Joe Webb run the Vikings offense this week: writing our Christmas list on an Etch-a-Sketch or getting a colonoscopy. The Vikes have been outscored 61-17 over their past two games and Philly will only add to that misery. The Eagles' top-ranked offense, which averages 403.9 yards a game, is also averaging 29.4 points per outing. This season the Vikes have fired their coach, traded for and tossed away Randy Moss, dealt with Brett and the "Little Viking" photos, played a home game in Detroit and another at a college stadium. What new torment awaits them Sunday?

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Eagles and the over.

SAINTS at FALCONS

Mon., 8:30 p.m., ESPN, Falcons by 21/2, 481/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: The Falcons are feeling pretty good about themselves these days. Home for the first time in a month, all Atlanta needs is a victory here and home-field advantage is all theirs throughout the playoffs. And what better team to clinch home-field advantage against than the reigning Super Bowl champs? Apparently there is no love lost between the two teams as Roddy White announced on his Twitter account: 'The Saints win one championship and want to go crazy. Well, y'all (butts) ain't winning this year. We gone make sure that doesn't happen." Roddy may not be a wordsmith, but we do agree with him. Matt Ryan is 19-1 all-time in the Georgia Dome.

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN: Falcons and the over.

BEST OF THE REST

SEAHAWKS at BUCS 4:15 p.m., Bucs by 6, 44

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN

Bucs and the under

TEXANS at BRONCOS

4 p.m., Texans by 21/2, 481/2

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN

Texans and the over

TITANS at CHIEFS

1 p.m., Chiefs by 5, 42

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN

Chiefs and the over

'BOYS at CARDS

Sat. 7:30, NFL Net., Cowboys by 7, 45

IF I WERE A BETTING MAN

Cowboys and the under

WHAT'S HOT, WHAT'S NOT

Packers are 48-29 ATS in December games since 1992.

Jets are 6-1 ATS in a road game where the total is between 35.5 and 38 pts.

Raiders are 5-16 ATS as a home underdog of three points or less since 1992.

Lions are 8-2 ATS in December games the last three years.

Dolphins are 7-17 ATS in home games the last three years.

Vikings are 0-6 ATS vs. a team with a winning record this season.

Rams are 6-1 ATS in games where the total is between 35.5 and 42 points.

LOOK BUT DON'T TOUCH

REDSKINS at JAGUARS

1 p.m., Jaguars by 7, 451/2

BRENNAN'S BEAUTIES: The Jags had a golden opportunity to clinch the AFC South last week and came up small - particularly with their vaunted ground game - against a woeful Colts run defense. Now they're forced to win their last two and hope for an Indy loss along the way to snag the division crown. They should be OK this week. Do you really think Mike Shanahan's Skins are going to stand in anybody's way?

CAN'T HELP YOURSELF? Jaguars and the over.

FOR THE RECORD

LAST WEEK 6-10

OVERALL 98-119-7

THIS WEEK'S BEST BET

Chargers. Bengals are already home for the holidays.

SEASON BEST BETS 6-9

GRAPHIC : OUR EXPERTS CALL THE SHOTS

For graphic see PDF

It was well past dinner Monday night and Vikings coach Mike Tice had yet to watch a minute of offensive or defensive film from Sunday's victory at New Orleans. He had spent all of his time watching special teams, particularly the kickoff coverage unit, which spent much of the game giving up big yardage.

     Tice's conclusion: Changes are necessary.

     "It's the worst part of our football team right now," he said. "We're going to have to make some personnel changes. We're going to have to get some guys out there who will play with some confidence. Because, obviously, we have some guys on the kickoff cover team who aren't playing with confidence, and not playing smart."

   Likely to be removed from the unit is Patrick Chukwurah, who was replaced Sunday after the Saints' Michael Lewis returned a kickoff 97 yards for a touchdown. Others could be replaced, or moved within the unit.

     One player who could get a look on kickoff coverage is linebacker Antonio Wilson, who played well on special teams earlier in the season. Tice also would like to see rookie Jack Brewer be put in more of a playmaking position. Right now the former Gophers safety is used outside, in a contain position. He could be moved inside, possibly to the position Harold Morrow usually plays.

     Defensive back Carey Scott, claimed off waivers Monday from Oakland, also could get a look, as could running back James Wofford. Tice also will consider changes on the kickoff return unit.

Short week

     What does a short NFL week mean for the Vikings? For the coaches, a very, very long Tuesday. For the players, one less day to get their legs back.

     The coaches did far less review of Sunday's game than they normally do, instead spending most of their time assembling the game plan for Saturday's game against Miami. The game plan has to be ready today, when the players return after having Monday off.

     "The coaches have a problem, because they have two days of film work [to do] in one day," Tice said. "So their backs are against the wall in the fact that they have to be sharp and precise in less than 24 hours."

     The Vikings have some familiarity with the Dolphins scheme, having played against Dave Wannstedt-coached Chicago teams in the past. They also beat Miami in 2000, Wannstedt's first season as Dolphins coach.

     "That helps, with a short week like this . . ." Tice said. "It's something we're used to playing against."

Roster moves

     As expected, offensive lineman Lewis Kelly's sports hernia landed him on injured reserve, ending his season.

     It was one of a few roster moves made Monday. The Vikings claimed Scott, who was with the team during training camp but cut in the final roster cutdown Sept. 1. The team also re-signed defensive lineman Cedric Killings, who was released last week.

     Meanwhile, safety Jason Perry and practice squad defensive end Mike Cecere were released. Perry had been with the team since Oct. 3; Cecere signed last week. Guard Kenny Sandlin was re-signed to the practice squad.

Etc.

- Linebacker Henri Crockett's strained pectoral muscle _ which kept him out of action in the second half Sunday _ will limit him in practice, at least early in the week. He is expected to practice with a protective harness, but should play without it Saturday.

- Defensive line coach George O'Leary said Monday night he had not received any formal inquiries from Alabama regarding its vacant head coaching position.

- Quarterback Daunte Culpepper and several teammates will host more than 150 people _ made up of families from the African American Adoption Agency _ at Winter Park this evening. During the event, Culpepper, who has worked with the AAAA since joining the Vikings, will be presented with the Adoption Excellence Award from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services for his volunteer work.

    _ Kent Youngblood is at kyoungblood@startribune.com.

title: Contrasting styles will be put to test
content:
Defense wins championships. Legitimate Super Bowl-winning axiom or tired bromide with little traction for a new NFL decade?

The Green Bay Packers had the NFC's No. 1 defense, but they're packing for winter vacation after last Sunday's 51-45 wild-card-round loss to the Arizona Cardinals, who rolled up 531 yards.

But last weekend's other winners successfully followed the old formula. The New York Jets, Dallas Cowboys and Baltimore Ravens -- who ranked 1-2-3 in scoring defense -- all held their opponents to 14 points, and all lived to fight another weekend.

That approach still gives offensive players pause.

"They've got the winning formula," San Diego Chargers running back LaDainian Tomlinson says of the Jets, who led the league in total defense, scoring defense and pass defense.

The Jets also have the No. 1 rushing offense, another factor perceived to predict postseason success. "They run the ball well. They play good defense," Tomlinson says. "The ones that have that winning formula are the ones who've been successful."

Yet the NFL continues to evolve into a pass-centric league, with 10 4,000-yard passers for the first time, including starters for all four bye teams (San Diego, the Indianapolis Colts, Minnesota Vikings and New Orleans Saints).

This weekend could reveal which blueprint is better. The contrasting styles will be on full display as the streaking Chargers host the Jets, and the Ravens, with a third-ranked defense that just shut down Tom Brady's New England Patriots,visiting MVP Peyton Manningand the Colts.

"We got the two best quarterbacks in the game back-to-back," Ravens linebacker Terrell Suggs says. "We definitely have to bring our 'A' game."

title: Broncos' Carswell seriously injured in wreck
content:
Denver Broncos offensive lineman Dwayne Carswell was seriously injured in a five-car accident yesterday morning while he was on his way to team headquarters.

He was in serious but stable condition at a Denver-area hospital, but team officials said his prognosis was "very good."

Carswell suffered multiple rib fractures, a ruptured diaphragm, ruptured spleen and internal damage on his left side, the Broncos said. He had surgery to remove his spleen and repair internal organ damage.

He was in intensive care after the operation, which took about 5 hours.

Coach Mike Shanahan addressed the team about Carswell's situation in morning meetings as they prepared for Sunday's game against the Eagles. After the team warmed up at the start of practice, the players broke their huddle by shouting "House" - Carswell's nickname.

Police said a summons was issued to 22-year-old Marquita Brister for careless driving. Police said she is believed to have caused the accident by making an abrupt lane change.

Officer Tony Guzman said Carswell was in one of three cars traveling south when two northbound cars collided and veered into the southbound lanes.

Television images showed both doors and much of the front and rear fenders on the driver's side of Carswell's car, a 1994 Chevrolet Caprice, were torn off and crumpled.

Carswell, 33, is a backup guard. He is in his 12th year in the NFL. A former tight end, he caught two touchdown passes against Jacksonville this year.
 
Noteworthy

* A judge sided with a high school civics teacher who sued to stop security "pat-downs" at Tampa Bay games. The American Civil Liberties Union of Florida filed a lawsuit on behalf of Bucs season ticketholder Gordon Johnston. Circuit Judge Perry Little agreed with Johnston, issuing a temporary injunction stopping the searches until the lawsuit is resolved. The suit seeks to stop Raymond James Stadium officials from conducting the "suspicionless" searches that began last month after the NFL implemented enhanced security at stadiums throughout the league.

The NFL said in a statement it was "disappointed" by the decision, but hadn't had a chance to review the court's opinion.

"Pat-downs at other NFL stadiums should not be affected by this ruling," the statement said.

Johnston testified in a hearing last week that the searches violate his constitutional rights because they were "invasive without necessity." Security would be more effective if staff spent more time watching ticket holders and less time touching them, he said.

"Why do I need to lose my rights to go to the game?" he said. "I'd say it's humiliating. If I did that to my students, do you know what would happen to me?"

* New England placed cornerback Tyrone Poole (ankle) on injured reserve, leaving a roster vacancy for Sunday's game against Buffalo that could be filled by linebacker Tedy Bruschi. Bruschi began practicing last week, 8 months after suffering a mild stroke.

* New Orleans starting middle linebacker Courtney Watson was charged with drunken driving, but is expected to play Sunday against Miami. Watson, 25, was driving on Interstate 10 when he was pulled over early Wednesday for erratic driving. They said an open bottle of beer was found in a cup holder. He was "combative" and failed a field sobriety test, but refused a breathalyzer sample, the police report said. He was released on $1,000 bond pending arraignment Dec. 5.

* Jacksonville linebacker Mike Peterson lost two classic, fully restored cars - a 1972 Chevrolet Impala and a 1967 Lincoln Continental - in a garage fire that caused about $500,000 in damage earlier this week.

* Atlanta Falcons cornerback DeAngelo Hall lost his endorsement deal with Reebok after wearing cleats with the Nike swoosh during Monday night's win over the Jets.

* Ken Dorsey, San Francisco's No. 3 quarterback 8 days ago, will start against Tampa Bay in place of injured rookie Alex Smith (strained knee ligaments).

title: Super Bowl ad contest peeks at stars' lives
content:
Consumers are not the only ones intrigued by Super Bowl TV commercials. So are NFL players. Hundreds of NFL stars ranging from veteran quarterbacks such as Matt Hasselbeck of the Seattle Seahawks to rookies such as Adrian Peterson of the Minnesota Vikings have responded to the league's call to pitch their own Super Bowl spots.

Under the NFL's "Super Ad: Who Wants it More?" contest, fans are voting online to decide which player's personal story would make the best ad during the big game. The 60-second TV commercial will air during halftime of Fox's broadcast of Super Bowl XLII on Feb.3.

Out of the NFL's 1,800 current players, 240, or 13.3%, volunteered to pitch their ideas to film crews in recent months, says Lisa Baird, the NFL's vice president of marketing.

Baird was surprised at how savvy the players were about advertising and their own personal brands -- and how eager they were to share their football stories during the most-watched television event of the year.

"They're pretty astute about what's good advertising and what's not going to work," she says.

Players bidding to star in their own Super Bowl spot include 18 selected for the 2008 Pro Bowl.

Among them: Seattle's Hasselbeck; Minnesota's Peterson; Cincinnati Bengals wide receiver T.J. Houshmandzadeh; and tight ends Tony Gonzalez of the Kansas City Chiefs and Jason Witten of the Dallas Cowboys.

The personal stories of the players at www.nfl.com/superad range from funny to risque. They provide a firsthand look at fame and fortune, NFL-style:

*Hasselbeck recalls his joy at receiving his first fan letter as a backup quarterback on the Green Bay Packers -- only to discover the fan wanted him to secure an autograph from teammate Brett Favre.

"That's when I knew I had not arrived yet, at all, in the NFL," the Super Bowl XL quarterback recalls.

*Peterson shares a story about a fan who was ready to leave her husband, on the spot, for him after meeting him and his brother outside a restaurant.

*Veteran quarterback Kurt Warner of the Arizona Cardinals recalls a St. Louis mother who wanted to take him home to autograph a poster on her son's bedroom wall.

*Willie Colon of the Pittsburgh Steelers recalls how the Cowboys skipped over him in the draft because his cellphone went dead -- and the team couldn't reach him.

*Punter Brian Moorman of the Buffalo Bills says he'll never forget the first time he saw a fan wearing a jersey with his name. "I couldn't help it. I had to go over and give the guy a high-five," Moorman says.

The contest has already generated more than 235,000 votes, Baird says. Starting Thursday, fans will be able to pick from nine finalists through Jan.6.

This season's contest follows a similar online promotion last year in which the NFL asked fans, not players, to pitch their own Super Bowl commercial. Bills fan Gino Bona won the contest. Just like last year, the winning idea will be turned into a finished spot by hotshot Madison Avenue director Joe Pytka.

title:
VIKINGS NOTES;
Positive test for Moss is confirmed
content:
Vikings coach Mike Tice refused to comment Monday on reports receiver Randy Moss was already in the NFL's substance-abuse program after testing positive for marijuana last year. However, a person with knowledge of the situation confirmed Moss is in the league's substance-abuse program.

     "That is something that Randy and the National Football League and the substance abuse program, it's supposed to be confidential," Tice said. "I don't know how that information got out there. . . . So I don't think at this time it would be very bright for me to comment on something that's supposed to be confidential."

   According to the league's policy, once a player tests positive for a banned drug, he can be tested as many as 10 times per month. A second positive test results in a four-game suspension.

     The Minneapolis city attorney is still in the process of deciding whether to charge Moss for misdemeanor possession of marijuana; a small amount of the drug was found in his car following his arrest last Tuesday.

     "How can I confirm it when I don't know it?" Tice said when asked further about Moss. "I didn't know he was in the program. I'm not supposed to know he's in the program. The head coach is not on the list of people made aware of when the players are in the program."

     Meanwhile, sources indicated Moss was tested by the league after his incident last week and that Moss' having played in Sunday's 48-23 loss to Seattle is an indication he passed that test.

Culpepper improves

     It might not have translated into a victory, but Tice and offensive coordinator Scott Linehan said that Sunday's game was quarterback Daunte Culpepper's best this season. Coming on the heels of his difficult outing against Carolina, it was reason for optimism.

     "He battled out there," Tice said. "He competed, he did a lot of things with his legs, moved around the pocket well."

     Culpepper matched his career high with 53 passes. And while he didn't throw a touchdown, several potential touchdown passes were dropped. Linehan said it was the best job Culpepper has done of working the game plan.

     "I though it was his best game so far," Linehan said. "I thought he played pretty well against Buffalo; you just had the turnovers, the fumbles. But in terms of executing what we were asking him to do, running the offense? It was his best game as far as I'm concerned."

     Culpepper's second-quarter interception that was returned for a touchdown was the result of miscommunication with Moss. A similar occurrence led to an incompletion late in the Carolina game.

     "It's two weeks in a row we've made that mistake," Linehan said. "Each time the play has been there, we just haven't executed it."

In the booth

     Linehan, meanwhile, said the move to the press box from the sidelines was a welcome one, for several reasons. First, it's where he had always been before this year. Second, it enables him to make decisions away from the hectic sideline. Third, it lets him spread his play sheet out and make adjustments on it during the game.

     "It's much more my comfort zone," he said. "And I thought the sideline communication was good with Alex [Wood, the quarterbacks coach who left the press box for the sidelines]. He did a good job moving back down there and relaying the plays in."

     Said Tice: "Scott wanted to be in the box, from Day 1. I kind of fought it a little bit, because I felt he had good communication with the quarterback."

     It was after talking to former Vikings coach Bud Grant that Tice decided to allow Linehan to move upstairs. Linehan will stay in the press box.

Unfair advantage?

     The Vikings lost use of their headsets early in Sunday night's game, and by NFL rule, the Seahawks were supposed to stop using theirs as well. Defensive coordinator Willie Shaw, however, said he saw Seahawks coach Mike Holmgren using his.

     "We couldn't get them to take theirs off," Shaw said. "From the start of the game we did not have headsets. They should not have had headsets. At one time, Mike [Tice] was yelling over at Holmgren, and Holmgren was [spreading out his arms], like, 'It ain't my deal.' But that was a huge advantage."

Etc.

- Film confirms that Moss dropped three potential touchdown passes.

- Linebacker Lemanski Hall had a precautionary MRI on his high ankle sprain Monday night. The preliminary diagnosis is that he will be out two to four weeks.

- There is a chance injured tight end Jimmy Kleinsasser could be back after the bye week.

.

    _ Kent Youngblood is at kyoungblood@startribune.com.

title: Despite Feeley's success, job belongs to Detmer Coach Andy Reid insists that if his second-string quarterback is healthy, he will start on Sunday. His third-stringer agrees.
content:
Eagles coach Andy Reid is keeping it black and white: You don't lose your position because of an injury.

Reid again said yesterday that if Koy Detmer is healthy, he will start at quarterback Sunday against the Washington Redskins.

"The policy we've had here is that when a player comes back that was a starter, he has the opportunity to come back in and be the starter," Reid said during his news conference a day after the Eagles improved to 10-3 with their 27-20 win in Seattle.

A.J. Feeley, the third-stringer who is 2-0 as a starter, explained his reason for supporting Reid's policy.

"I'm a true believer in that, because it's happened to me," Feeley said yesterday. "I didn't get the benefit of that. I lost my job [at Oregon] due to injury."

Because of Feeley's relative effectiveness against the St. Louis Rams and the Seahawks, Reid can afford to be careful with Detmer as the quarterback returns from a dislocated left elbow suffered two weeks ago in San Francisco. There is healthy, and there is completely healthy.

"We've got to see," the coach said. "I'm going to talk to Koy and see how he's doing. He needs reps to be in there, to work the game plan. That's for sure. I'll see how he is. I haven't had a chance to talk to him today."

There are always gray areas. Feeley knows that better than anyone. He lost his job in college after he had continued playing while injured and left two games - two games in which Joey Harrington went in and rallied Oregon for victories.

"It's got to be a feel thing by the head coach. He's the guy who makes all the calls," Feeley said, referring again to the don't-lose-your-job-because-of-an-injury theory. "I think he understands that's the way it should be done. But if he's got a little intuition, if he sees something different, has a feeling, it's his call."

Feeley said he wasn't trying to stir anything up.

"It all comes back to me knowing my role," he said. "My role - just because it changed in the last two weeks with me playing - I understand that Donovan's the quarterback here, Koy's the backup. My role is the third-string guy."

He said he would trade contracts with former Oregon teammates Harrington, now with the Detroit Lions, and Akili Smith, with the Cincinnati Bengals, but he wouldn't trade situations.

"I definitely think I'm fortunate," Feeley said. "Not many guys can come into a situation where the franchise is on the rise. The route I'm taking is fine. I don't mind sitting behind and watching in the wings, learning that way, rather than Joey Harrington or Akili Smith getting thrust into a team that's not so good."

There are plenty of examples of understudies taking over, with various levels of success. Feeley was invited to compare himself with Tom Brady, who took over as quarterback of the Patriots after Drew Bledsoe was hurt and led New England to a Super Bowl title.

"Obviously, I think I can win games if I play," Feeley said. "But I'm not here to create controversy. What Brady did, he won games, and they went to the Super Bowl as a result of that. If you're asking me if I think I can win games playing - yeah, of course I can. I think I can. But I don't know if I'd compare my story to Tom Brady."

However, Feeley offered another comparison between last season's Patriots and this season's Eagles.

"Something just kind of gets on a team and you just catch it," he said. "Winning becomes contagious. I think that's what happens here."

Feeley noted that football has never been a one-man game, that with McNabb out, "everybody's had to step up their game to the next level. We realize that on third and 20, if you're not hitting that short little pass, I'm not going to run for 40 yards for a touchdown like Don on a broken play, and neither is Koy. We realize the urgency of getting the ball in the guys' hands and letting them make plays."

Reid said he has seen McNabb throw the same kind of timing passes Detmer and Feeley have made.

"Let's not take away from Donovan," Reid said. "Donovan's a great quarterback, and the number of great plays that he's made and the position that he's put this team in and this offense in has been tremendous. The other guys are great supporters of him and great believers of him."

Reid, who again said "we're very fortunate" about what has happened at quarterback, hasn't had to make a decision through all the changes. But that's about to change.

Hobbling toward recovery. McNabb had the cast removed from his right foot yesterday. The fibula, which he broke three weeks ago, was examined by ankle specialist Kevin Varner in Houston. According to a statement released by the Eagles, McNabb's status is improving, but his foot still can't bear weight, and he is still walking with crutches. The club said McNabb will resume his rehabilitation program in Philadelphia today. The team has said that the normal healing time for his type of injury is six to eight weeks and indicated that nothing Varner saw had changed that.

Other injuries. Reid said running back Brian Westbrook "will struggle to be back for the game" with the lower ankle sprain he suffered trying to block a punt Sunday… . Running back Dorsey Levens has a shin contusion and "should be fine for the weekend," he said… . Safety Blaine Bishop is day-to-day because of a groin strain… . Defensive end Brandon Whiting's lower leg contusion "should be fine," Reid said. He used the same words for cornerback Bobby Taylor, who suffered a concussion and a slight neck strain while intercepting a pass, then stayed in the game and intercepted another.

Contact Mike Jensen at 215-854-4489 or mjensen@phillynews.com

title: Dolphins' dynamic duo hard to beat
content:
Forget the last play.

That's an essential mantra for any NFL cornerback worth his bump-and-run identification card. Got burned? Snatched a drive-killing interception? No matter. It won't be long before another tight spiral comes your way.

"You've got to have thick skin and a tough mentality to play this position," Miami Dolphins cornerback Sam Madison says. "Because you're going to get beat. People will pick on you. If you don't stand up for yourself, it will be a long road."

Madison and his sidekick on the opposite corner, Patrick Surtain, can take a bow for their work in Sunday's 18-15 victory against the Oakland Raiders. Tim Brown and Jerry Rice -- both of whom will someday be inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame -- were held to four catches for a paltry 27 yards.

"We've got these two dudes that get up in you," Dolphins defensive end Jason Taylor says. "I don't know if you've ever heard of them -- Madison and Surtain? Those are some bad boys."

NFL receivers, quarterbacks and offensive coordinators know all about Madison and Surtain. They are arguably the NFL's best pair of cornerbacks, a case made stronger when considering the Dolphins use them on maybe 90% of the defensive snaps in "press" man-to-man coverage.

They love such an approach, like a pure jump shooter might appreciate shooting three-pointers all game or a 100-mph pitcher hurling fastballs.

"When people throw between zones for touchdowns, it's like, 'Was that my fault? Or your fault?' " Madison says. "With us, you don't get that. If the ball goes to my receiver, you know it's on me because I'm man-to-man. If that ball goes to Patrick, you know."

Forget the last game.

That's as good a mantra as any for Madison and Surtain this week. Their reward for Sunday's game is a date at the TWA Dome with the high-flying St. Louis scheme that attacks with four receivers, multiple-threat Marshall Faulk and sharpshooter Kurt Warner.

Madison, a fifth-year pro, says Sunday's game marks the biggest challenge of his career. Sure tackling will be a key, given the Rams' penchant for big plays.

Adds Surtain, "There's no off week in the NFL."

That Madison and Surtain have clicked seems fitting.

Both were selected by Miami with second-round picks (Madison in 1997, Surtain in '98), in the same draft slot (44th). Both are 5-11 and physical (Surtain delivered a crushing blow on Raiders tight end Roland Williams to break up a key third-down pass; Madison took on a lineman before helping stuff Tyrone Wheatley at the corner on a two-point conversion attempt). Both came from Conference USA schools (Madison from Louisville, Surtain from Southern Mississippi).

And, as Surtain puts it, "We're both playmakers."

Statistical alchemy: Looking for a chink in the armor that is Denver's No. 2 offense? Try third downs. Denver has converted five of 23 third downs, a 21.7% rate that is second worst in the NFL. And Brian Griese, who has completed 71.7% of his passes and leads the NFL in pass efficiency (134.9), has the AFC's second-worst rating on third down at 47.5.

Moral of the stats: Denver pummels its opponents beforethird down. In 109 first- and second-down snaps during a 2-0 start, the Broncos have averaged a whopping 7.1 yards a play. And all eight of Denver's touchdowns this season came on first or second down. In addition to Griese's fine work (he hasn't thrown an interception), the Broncos have the NFL's leader for receptions (23) and receiving yards (277) in Rod Smith and the league's No. 3 rushing unit. All with such miserable stats on third downs.

Quick slants: So much was made during the offseason about Matt Hasselbeck's hot market value after riding the bench behind Brett Favre in Green Bay. Well, so far, Hasselbeck has made Mike Holmgren appear shrewder for his signing of Trent Dilfer as insurance. Hasselbeck still hasn't thrown a touchdown pass, and in completing just 50% of his passes he is the AFC's lowest-rated passer (46.6). . . . Not all's gloomy in Seattle. Led by Chad Brown and a revived John Randle, the Seahawks lead the NFL with eight sacks. . . . Perennial doormats-turned-unbeatens Cincinnati and San Diego meet Sunday. The Chargers haven't been 3-0 since 1994, en route to Super Bowl XXIX; the Bengals haven't seen 3-0 since 1990, the franchise's last playoff year.

title: Lane experience spurs on Johnson
content:
By Ken Elliott in San Diego

BRAD JOHNSON has made it to the Super Bowl in spite of the pitch at Tottenham's White Hart Lane. The 34-year-old quarterback with the NFL's Tampa Bay Buccaneers, who play the Oakland Raiders in San Diego on Sunday, gained the experience that launched his career in America by playing for a season with the London Monarchs in north London.

Although the Monarchs, who are no more, won only four of their ten games in the World League of American Football (now the NFL Europe League), Johnson values the learning curve that 1995 turned out to be even if that pitch did not always help him when passing the ball in a bid to score a touchdown.

"One of the things I always remember about the field at White Hart Lane was that it was not long enough," says the 6ft 5in passer. "It was 94 yards long, instead of the required 100 yards, and the padded walls were right on the line at the back of the end zone. It meant you couldn't throw deep to your receivers in the end zone because they would catch the ball and crash into the wall."

Life off the pitch was also uncomfortable for Johnson that year, on loan to the Monarchs from the NFL's Minnesota Vikings. "We were housed in an old police academy in Sutton with dirty floors and broken glass and we slept on bunk beds.

"But despite all that, it was one of the greatest moves I have ever made. I didn't play much football in college or in my first three to four years in the NFL and I needed to get out there and make plays and make mistakes and go back to the Vikings and learn from them."

That he did, and from a £10,000-a-season player in a London backwater, he is now a £4 million-a-year star in the NFL.

That may pale in comparison to the recent £70 million deal over 10 seasons - the biggest in the NFL - signed by Donovan McNabb of the Philadelphia Eagles.

But Johnson, who returned to the Vikings after his loan spell in London and then joined the Washington Redskins before signing for the Buccaneers, went one step further than McNabb on Sunday when the Buccaneers beat McNabb's Eagles in the NFC Championship game.

Johnson also bettered McNabb, finishing this year as the top quarterback in the NFC half of the 32-team NFL.

This Sunday, Johnson could walk off the field at Qualcomm Stadium, San Diego, as the best quarterback in the NFL if the Bucs can beat the Raiders.

And this time the pitch is plenty long enough to do it.

title: Tice gets promotion, may hire O'Leary
content:
Mike Tice was promoted from interim coach to head man of the Minnesota Vikings yesterday, and he pledged a quick return to success.

The Vikings, who reached the NFC Championship Game last season but fell to 5-11 this season, have "a very good nucleus" and can turn things around with just a few changes, Tice said.

"You're only one or two players away, as Chicago has proven this year, from changing things," said Tice, who was the offensive line coach until Dennis Green was forced out last week.

Green was replaced in part because of the perception he had lost control of some of his players, including wide receiver Randy Moss, who had said he plays hard when he feels like it.

Tice didn't evade any questions about that, saying he has expectations for every player and Moss is no different. Tice, who is popular with the players, joked that his goal was to make every player cry once during the season.

Meanwhile, there were indications that one of Tice's assistant coaches might be George O'Leary, who quit as coach of Notre Dame after revelations that he lied about his background.

The St. Paul Pioneer Press, without citing any sources, reported on its Web site that Tice would hire O'Leary as his defensive line coach. O'Leary coached Tice at Central Islip High School in Long Island, N.Y.

College stampede

Add LSU All-America wide receiver Josh Reed and Michigan State running back T.J. Duckett to the list of underclassmen declaring for the NFL draft.

Reed and Duckett, along with Tennessee wide receiver Donte Stallworth and Washington tight end Jerramy Stevens, said they're passing up a final year of eligibility to play in the NFL.

Several other underclassmen announced they were returning to school, including Maryland All-America linebacker E.J. Henderson, Wisconsin wide receiver Lee Evans and Penn State defensive tackle Jimmy Kennedy.

Today is the deadline for players with remaining college eligibility to decide whether to enter their names in the NFL draft.

Around the league

[] PATRIOTS: The team suspended Terry Glenn for the entire playoffs, accusing the problem wide receiver of repeatedly missing practices and meetings.

[] COMEBACK: 49ers running back Garrison Hearst, who overcame a severe broken leg, was named The Associated Press' NFL Comeback Player of the Year.

[] TITANS: Quarterback Steve McNair (throwing shoulder) and running back Eddie George (ankle) had arthroscopic surgery.

title: Sports in 60 seconds
content:
Mariners expected to hire Wedge:

The Seattle Mariners have reportedly chosen former Cleveland Indians skipper Eric Wedge as their new manager. SI.com first reported Friday that the Mariners selected Wedge to be their manager after their second 101-loss season in three years. Other media outlets had similar reports late Friday.

— A person with knowledge of the discussions said contract talks are going well between the St. Louis Cardinals and veteran manager Tony La Russa.

— Federal prosecutors submitted the lineup of witnesses they intend to call during the much-delayed Barry Bonds perjury trial, and it includes Rockies first baseman Jason Giambi and the home run king's former personal trainer at the center of the case. Bonds has pleaded not guilty to lying to a grand jury in 2003 when he denied knowingly taking steroids. The former Giants star's trial is scheduled to start March 21 in San Francisco.

Heat's Wade gets day in court:

Dwyane Wade denied allegations in a Chicago courtroom Friday that he abused and neglected his children and ex-wife, owned guns and used drugs. The Miami Heat superstar spent about 2 1/2 hours testifying in the custody case for their two young sons. He responded for the first time to claims Siohvaughn Wade made in court papers. He says he was glad to "have an opportunity to have my voice heard."

Favre's streak in jeopardy:

With an NFL-record streak of 289 regular-season starts on the line, Brett Favre is listed as questionable for Minnesota's game Sunday against Dallas. Favre had only limited practice this week for the Vikings while trying to rest his ailing right elbow, which has been bothered by tendinitis. Coach Brad Childress said Friday he would give his quarterback the 50-50 designation on the injury report and that a final decision on Favre's status would be made before the Cowboys game.

— Detroit Lions quarterback Matthew Stafford is practicing again in a limited role. Stafford, who hasn't played since injuring his throwing shoulder in the season opener against the Bears, was upgraded on the team's injury report Friday after being out of practice before that.

— New Orleans Saints leading rusher Pierre Thomas will miss his third straight game as he continues to rehabilitate a left ankle sprain.

— The NFL fined Cincinnati's Terrell Owens $5,000 for violating the league's ban on using social media shortly before games.

Mediate keeps lead at Frys.com:

Rocco Mediate holed a 7-iron approach from 160 yards for eagle on the par-4 fourth hole and finished with a 6-under 65 on Friday to increase his lead to 3 strokes in the Frys.com Open at San Martin, Calif. The 46-year-old Mediate, looking for his first PGA Tour victory in eight years, made the eagle on the 426-yard hole a day after a hole-in-one on the par-3 third hole. He had a 13-under 129 total on the CordeValle Golf Club course in the Fall Series event. Ryudi Imada (67) was second, and Dean Wilson (64) and David Duval (65) were at 9 under.

— Rookies Gwladys Nocera and Beatriz Recari each shot a 6-under 66 on and were tied for the lead after the second round of the CVS/pharmacy LPGA Challenge at

Danville, Calif.

— Maarten Lafeber of the Netherlands shot a 5-under 67 to take a 2-stroke lead after the second round of the European Tour's Portugal Masters at Vilamoura, Portugal.

Wolves prevail in overtime:

Jaime Sifers scored with 51 seconds remaining in overtime to give the Chicago Wolves a come-from-behind, 3-2 win over the host Milwaukee Admirals at the Bradley Center on Friday night. The Wolves (2-1-0-0) rallied with a pair of third-period goals by Andre Deveaux and Patrick Rissmiller. Goalie Edward Pasquale stopped 26 shots for the Wolves, who host the Admirals today in their home opener at 7 p.m. at Allstate Arena.

Red Sox group buys Liverpool:

After reviving the pride of New England, John Henry and Tom Werner will try to resuscitate one of Old England's most famous soccer teams. The Boston Red Sox ownership group won its fight for Liverpool on Friday, gaining control of the struggling and nearly bankrupt Premier League club from Tom Hicks and George Gillett Jr. in a trans-Atlantic legal tussle that replaced one set of American owners with another.

Etc.: Former DePaul guard and NBA player Kevin Edwards has joined the Blue Demons in an administrative role. Edwards will serve as the men's basketball team's director of community, corporate and professional relations, working as a liaison to Chicago's business community while handling relationships with pro franchises in the U.S. and overseas.

— Brad Keselowski is piling up the wins in the NASCAR Nationwide Series. Keselowski pulled away on a late restart at Charlotte Motor Speedway in Concord, N.C., earning his fifth Nationwide victory of the season. Keselowski extended his advantage in the season standings to 450 points, leading the final

35 laps.

— Former Purdue basketball coach Gene Keady is joining the staff of former assistant Steve Lavin at St. John's. Keady, who retired in 2005 after 27 seasons as a Division I head coach, will join the St. John's staff as a special assistant/adviser.

— Coach Jim Calhoun and other University of Connecticut officials spent Friday behind closed doors with NCAA investigators, hoping to convince them that the school has done enough to punish itself for recruiting violations in the men's basketball program.

— University of Nebraska regents approved a $9.25 million settlement that clears the way for the school to leave the Big 12

Conference next year for the

Big Ten without going to court.

title: GIANTS HIT WITH REALITY CHECK
content:
JIM FASSEL MET with Norv Turner on the field the other night before the Giants-Redskins game. Subject matter: The tendency to make judgments way too early in the season.

"We were talking about how we've played three games and everybody is about ready to throw the final shovel of dirt on them, and they think we're ready to go onto great things," Fassel said yesterday. "I said there's a lot of football to play before anybody is going to figure out who's really going to be good and who isn't."

The Giants were in position to get off to their first 4-0 start since 1990, bury the Redskins at 1-3, perhaps put Turner on the brink of getting fired and take a commanding lead in the NFC East.

Now, after losing to Washington 16-6 on Sunday night, the perspective is completely different: The Giants are facing the very real danger of dropping to 3-2 on Sunday, which will certainly create doubts about this team's ability to play with elite teams.

They travel to Nashville to play the AFC champion Titans, who are 10-0, including the playoffs, at their new stadium. Tennessee is coming off an exhilarating comeback victory, although it was only against the lowly Steelers.

"This league is funny," Kerry Collins said. "Every time you start thinking you're pretty good, something happens like this that knocks you back down to earth. I think we have the perspective that it's going to be tough, week-in and week-out, regardless of what happened the week before. It got cushy there for us the first three games. We had leads. Now we realize we're going to be in a dogfight every week."

This is one of those games that will help define the Giants' season. After all the talk about the improved offense, it has scored three touchdowns in the last two games. The Giants defensive concern all summer was the secondary and after a nice diet of Jake Plummer, Donovan McNabb and Cade McNown the first three weeks, they were exposed by Brad Johnson. He could not throw deep at all against the Cowboys and his longest completion in the first three games was 26 yards, but he burned the Giants with three passes over 45 yards.

So, what does that tell us about where the Giants are going? They got fat at 3-0 against the Cardinals, Eagles and Bears, then stepped up in class and lost to the Redskins. They hope they are as good as they looked the first three weeks, but it could be when they meet a playoff-caliber team they are in trouble. Tennessee is one of the four or five best teams.

"It certainly doesn't get any easier for us and we're going in their house and it is going to be tough," Collins said. "I think we'll get the opportunity to see what we're made of and see how we respond. If you want to be a good football team in this league, that's the way it goes."

Again, the tendency in the NFL is to make definitive judgments based on what happened the previous Sunday and that is not so good for the Giants this week. Still, the reality is they had the benefit of an easy opening three weeks. They could not have hand-picked a better way to start the season. If they want to make anything of this season, they have to load up against the bad teams.

But they faced their first good team Sunday and lost.

"There is no sense of panic in here," Jason Sehorn said. "There is a sense of we made some errors. We beat ourselves as equally as they beat us."

The last time the Giants had started 3-0 was 1994, Dan Reeves' second year. They proceeded to lose seven straight before winning their last six and just missing the playoffs. This team is talented enough to avoid any prolonged losing streaks, but after missing the playoffs the last two years, will find out in the next few weeks just how much it has improved.

"We just got to be like Rocky," linebacker Mike Barrow said. "Rocky didn't win all the matches, but he came back and won a rematch. So, that's our attitude."

The Giants were not playing the blame game yesterday. There's no reason - yet. Fassel breaks the season into quarters and sets a goal of 3-1 in each quarter. Goal met. But when the Giants reached the three victories with a legitimate shot of making it four and failed, it made them feel like they left something on the table.

A year ago, the Skins came here the second week and blew out the Giants, 50-21. In a sense, that game was easier to forget because no one or two plays made a difference. But the theme in the locker room yesterday was about missed opportunities and how a couple of plays turned the game around.

"Were we just outclassed and outmatched on the football field?" Sehorn said. "It didn't look that way."

THINGS CHANGE SO RAPIDLY in the NFL. After the Titans, the Giants get the Falcons on the road and the Cowboys at home before the bye week. They really should be no worse than 5-2. If they lose to the Titans, you know at this time next week all the talk will be about their season being on the line at Atlanta. If they beat the Titans, the bandwagon will load up again.

"You get in the 10th week and you see a little bit more of what the team is about," Fassel said. "Until that time, you are going to have some highs and lows."

Fassel's pregame talk with Turner at the Dec. 3 rematch in Washington should be interesting.

title:
SHERMAN HOPEFUL GREEN WILL PLAY;
   THE HALFBACK SITS OUT PRACTICE BUT HIS STATUS WON'T BE KNOWN UNTIL LATER IN THE WEEK.
content:
Halfback Ahman Green sat out Wednesday's practice with the bruised left kneecap he suffered Sunday vs. Chicago, and the Green Bay Packers will take a wait-and-see approach with his availability for Sunday's game vs. Minnesota.

"There's still some fluid there (on the knee). We just have to see where he is," Packers coach Mike Sherman said of Green, whose MRI Monday revealed no structural damage. "He's getting 'round-the-clock rehab and we're hopeful that he'll be back, but we won't know for sure until the end of the week."

Green is listed as questionable. Rookie Tony Fisher, who rushed 17 times for 91 yards vs. the Bears, took most of the reps with the No. 1 offense in practice. If Green isn't ready for this Sunday's game vs. Minnesota, Fisher would start, with fullback Tony Carter and recently signed halfback Jay Graham serving as the backups.

"I think we have to plan to go without (Green), and if we get him, it's a bonus," offensive coordinator Tom Rossley said. "Whatever we've got, that's what we'll go with."

Defensive tackle Gilbert Brown (questionable -- ankle), cornerback Tyrone Williams (questionable -- hamstring), wide receiver Terry Glenn (probable -- hip), defensive tackle Steve Warren (probable -- knee) and right tackle Earl Dotson (not listed on the injury report) also sat out practice.

His two cents

Quarterback Brett Favre said he continues to appeal the $5,000 fine levied against him by the NFL for his late hit out of bounds on Vikings safety Jack Brewer Nov. 17. Favre sounded off on the fine and Tampa Bay defensive tackle Warren Sapp's hit on Packers left tackle Chad Clifton.

"I think there's some double-standards going on," Favre said. "I still think it's ridiculous. I haven't talked to anyone at the league, but when they fine you to prove a point it's totally ridiculous. They don't fine Warren Sapp last week, and then they fine (Dallas safety) Darren Woodson $100,000 for a hit that you are taught as a 5-year-old to make? I mean, come on.

"The Sapp hit, it was legal, but has there ever been a better example of unneccesary roughness? (Sapp) saw (Clifton) coming, but was (Clifton) going to make the play? No. On mine, he was a yard out of bounds. He saw me coming. When I left my feet he was still on the sideline. I don't make tackles; I don't practice 'em. Maybe there was some frustration in there, but I was trying to make the play."

Extra points

Clifton, who suffered career-threatening damage to the ligaments in his hip and a collection of blood in his pelvis as a result of Sapp's hit, was placed on season-ending injured reserve. He was replaced on the roster by tackle Alcender Jackson, who spent the past two seasons with the Dallas Cowboys and was on the Packers' practice squad in early October. … The Packers also made a change on the practice squad, releasing tackle Pete Campion and bringing back return specialist J.J. Moses. … With more than 42 million votes cast by fans on NFL.com, Favre leads in the balloting for the 2003 Pro Bowl with 771,562 votes. Buffalo's Drew Bledsoe (628,288) ranks second. Favre, tight end Bubba Franks (494,514), safety Darren Sharper (182,432), center Mike Flanagan (150,615) and special-teams player Matt Bowen (91,270) lead at their positions in the NFC. The fan balloting counts one-third in the selection process, with coaches and players votes accounting for the other two-thirds. Their voting will be held Dec. 16-17.

title: SUPER MANN OF FAMILY NOW ELI. EXPERTS SAY GIANTS QB READY TO PASS PEYTON
content:
Eight months ago, Eli Manningwas the Super Bowl MVP. Now he's the MVM.

The Most Valuable Manning.

In the battle for supremacy in the First Family of the NFL, young Eli has - at least temporarily - overtaken his older brother as the best Manning in football. Four games into the season, Eli leads Peyton in every significant, quarterback-measuring category. And one quarter of the way through his fifth NFL season, the kid once dubbed Eli the Terrible is suddenly rated the fifth-best quarterback in the league.

"That doesn't surprise me," said Marshall Faulk, the former Rams running back and now an NFL Network analyst. "I've listened to Peyton talk about his little brother and Peyton says that this kid is something special. You've got to believe Peyton Manningknows something about quarterbacks and he's not just saying it because it's his little brother."

It is true that Peyton has been touting the merits of Eli ever since his kid brother was in high school, but it's only recently that Peyton has truly had to look up at him. Peyton, of course, was watching from a luxury box when Eli won Super Bowl XLII last February, and became the second straight Manning to win the MVP. And now, Peyton is the leader of the 2-2 Indianapolis Colts, while Eli is 4-0 and leading arguably the best team in the league.

Not only that, Peyton - an eight-time Pro Bowler on the fast track to the Hall of Fame - has slipped to 20th in the quarterback rankings. His passer rating of 79.2 not only puts him way behind Eli (99.7), but also behind signal-callers such as Chicago's Kyle Orton (87.6), Oakland's JaMarcus Russell (84.9), and San Francisco's J.T. O'Sullivan (82.2).

"It's very early and I would never count Peyton out," said one NFC scout. "But at the moment, it looks like Eli is set up to have the better year."

The consensus of scouts and analysts polled yesterday was that a family torch hasn't been passed. It's been only four games, and passer rating is generally considered a lousy way to judge quarterbacks. Plus, circumstances - such as key injuries to his teammates - have likely contributed to Peyton's slow start.

But the way the Giants are playing could give Eli an advantage going forward. The Giants have the NFL's best rushing attack (181.2 yards per game) while Peyton's Colts have the worst (67.8). The Colts also have the NFL's worst rushing defense (188.5 yards against per game), while the Giants' defense is ranked third in the league.

In other words, Eli appears to have the better supporting cast.

"This is more about finally getting some consistency with having better people around him," said former Steelers coach and current CBS analyst Bill Cowher. "He's got a running game he can rely on, so it's not just him running the offense. But he can make all the throws. His confidence has got to be sky high right now. He is, without a doubt, one of the premier quarterbacks in the NFL."

There isn't much that separates the brothers statistically, except in interceptions, where Eli has just one and Peyton has five. Eli has slim leads only in completion percentage (63.6 to 62.3), passing yards (1,032 to 1,031) and touchdown passes (6 to 5).

Peyton, of course, has a long history of better numbers. He's thrown for at least 4,000 yards in eight of the last nine seasons, and his 2007 numbers obliterated those of his brother. Even when comparing the first 59 starts of their NFL careers, Peyton's numbers are markedly better across the board.

But Eli is still only 27, five years younger than Peyton. And based on the last four games, he appears to be gaining in performance and stature. If nothing else, Eli is definitely building on the confidence he gained during the Giants' championship season.

"I think a lot of that is attributed to that," Tom Coughlin said. "Anytime you have that experience, and it's a positive one and you perform as he did, it's obviously a very good boost of confidence. And I think it gives him and all of us the idea that going forward we can do nothing but get better.

"And that is what I see for Eli, is the idea that he just continues to improve."
