title: Eagles Notes / Birds remember feeling of falling just short of goal
content:
This is what Donovan McNabb saw as he stood at the corner of the field in St. Louis a year ago, watching the Rams celebration. McNabb saw the Eagles playing in this year's NFC championship game.

And winning.

"It definitely is extra incentive," McNabb said. "That was pretty much the reason I went out to see the celebration in St. Louis. It was an emotional game for me. You get so close and obviously we didn't win the game.

"But you visualize getting back to that spot, again, and doing it at the Vet. That was our motivation going through the off-season: getting back to this point and, obviously, changing the outcome. It's been on my mind ever since the season started. We still have that taste in our mouths."

"A lot of guys did stay on the field and soak up that feeling," wide receiver James Thrash said. "We played with that bad taste in our mouths all season."

The Eagles returned to the threshold of the Super Bowl despite losing McNabb to a broken ankle for the final six games of the regular season. He came back for Saturday night's playoff victory over Atlanta, playing well, but feeling as if he could play better.

"I think I got rid of a little bit of rust," McNabb said. "There were some plays in the game that I felt I could have made better plays. That's something I can learn from. We had opportunities to score points and whatever it was held us back - if it was penalties, or me making better decisions. I think we all learned from watching the film."

The one drive-killer McNabb did not mention was, significantly, the rash of dropped passes on key downs. Asked specifically about the four or five drops, McNabb shrugged them off.

"In all fairness," he said, "it was the first playoff game we played."

That's one of the reasons his teammates respect him so much. McNabb completed 20 of 30 passes and threw the touchdown pass that sealed victory in the fourth quarter. It would have been easy to point out that half of his incompletions were good throws that clattered out of receivers' hands.

Thanks, but snow thanks. Asked if he would welcome snow to further chill out the warm-weather Bucs, McNabb's eyes widened.

"No," he said firmly. "We know it's going to be cold. That's good enough. If it decides to snow, then we have to play in that. We can't worry about conditions."

Vet perspective. McNabb said he wouldn't miss the Vet's "birds and cats and rats," but acknowledged the stadium's place in Eagles history.

"For the players who are playing now," he said, "it's pretty much defending the territory of the guys that have played there. So many great players played at the Vet and have done miraculous things at the Vet. So I'm sure those guys are shedding a tear - guys like Harold [Carmichael] and [Ron Jaworski]. This is our opportunity to send the Vet off on a good note."

Cornerback Troy Vincent, who grew up in Trenton and Bucks County, said this is a fitting final Eagles game for the stadium.

"To have the NFC championship game played here at home, it's a dream come true," Vincent said. "It's a great time for the community. All the people that are season-ticket holders have been coming down 95 and down Broad Street for so many years to Veterans Stadium."

"You take that and dedicate it to the fans because they've been so loyal," running back Duce Staley said. "That pushes you along the way. You want to get things done and you think about the fans and all the history they've been around and all the great players they've seen. Just to have one more game in the Vet, words can't describe it."

TV stars. During the last four months, the 12 best-rated TV programs in the Philadelphia market have all been Eagles games. Of the top 20 programs, 14 have been Eagles games and two more were NFL games.

The other four shows in the top 20 were all episodes of the CBS series CSI.

Not surprisingly, the top-rated show between Sept. 5 and Jan. 12 was the Eagles' playoff game against Atlanta on Saturday night. According to figures released by the NFL yesterday, it drew a 42.1 Nielsen rating and a 58 share. That means 42.1 percent of all TV households in the area were watching the game and 58 percent of televisions in use were tuned to the game.

The Eagles' Dec. 1 victory over the Rams (30.3 rating, 46 share) and the Dec. 28 loss to the Giants (29.7, 57) were next on the list.

Contact staff writer Phil Sheridan at 215-854-2844 or psheridan@phillynews.com

title: EAGLES ADD VICK TO ROSTER QUARTERBACK CAN PRACTICE WITH TEAM, BUT IS NOT ELIGIBLE TO PLAY UNTIL SEPT. 27
content:
Michael Vick can throw passes to his teammates instead of ball boys.

The Philadelphia Eagles elevated Vick to the team's 53-man roster yesterday, a move that allows the quarterback to practice with the team.

Wide receiver Hank Baskett was released to make room for Vick, who is eligible to play Sept. 27 against the Kansas City Chiefs. Vick will begin practicing with the team today, though he cannot play in Sunday's home opener against the New Orleans Saints. Vick was suspended for the first two regular-season games as the final league penalty for his role in running a dogfighting ring.

Kevin Kolb is expected to take the snaps with the starters today because Donovan McNabb has a cracked rib. Jeff Garcia was signed to back up Kolb in case McNabb can't play.

Vick was brought in to give the Eagles another dimension on offense. He ran Philadelphia's version of the wildcat offense and took some snaps as a traditional quarterback under center in two preseason games.

Vick hasn't played in a regular-season game since Dec. 31, 2006, when he was with the Atlanta Falcons. It's highly unlikely he would have started even if he were eligible to play this week. Eagles coach Andy Reid has maintained all along that Kolb is McNabb's backup.

b> BILLS

/b>

The front lawn of a home belonging to Buffalo cornerback Leodis McKelvin was vandalized after the Bills' season-opening loss to New England. Hamburg police confirmed the home of a Bills player was vandalized, but declined to release details of their investigation after an obscenity and the score of Monday night's game -- a 25-24 loss to the Patriots -- were painted in white on the player's lawn in suburban Buffalo. McKelvin fumbled a kickoff return with under two minutes to play and the Bills leading, 24-19. Bills starting defensive end Chris Kelsay is expected to miss at least a week with a bruised knee.

b> PATRIOTS

/b>

New England coach Bill Belichick said he doesn't think last year's NFL Defensive Rookie of the Year, linebacker Jerod Mayo, is done for the year after hurting his right knee in the first quarter of Monday night's opener.

b> SAINTS

/b>

NFL commissioner Roger Goodell has decided against enforcing four-game suspensions of New Orleans defensive ends Will Smith and Charles Grant at this time. Goodell said it would be unfair to suspend the two Saints players now when the league has been blocked from suspending Vikings defensive tackles Kevin Williams and Pat Williams for the same offense while their case is pending in Minnesota state court. The players tested positive last season for bumetanide, a banned diuretic that can be used to mask steroids. Also, New Orleans brought back kick return specialist and reserve receiver Courtney Roby. General manager Mickey Loomis announced the signing and said tight end Buck Ortega was waived to make room on the roster.

b> JETS

/b>

The NFL is investigating Browns coach Eric Mangini's role in New York's failure to list quarterback Brett Favre's arm problem on injury reports last season. Mangini coached the Jets during Favre's one season in New York before he was fired after the team collapsed down the stretch.

b> CHIEFS

/b>

Receiver Bobby Wade, a six-year NFL veteran with stops in Chicago, Tennessee and Minnesota, was signed by Kansas City. The Chiefs waived Turk McBride, who had been playing linebacker after being a starter most of last year at defensive end. However, still unclear is who would be throwing the ball. Chiefs coach Todd Haley said it was too early to know if quarterback Matt Cassel will miss a second consecutive start this weekend because of an injury to his left knee. If Cassel remains out, Brodie Croyle would make a second start.

b> REDSKINS

/b>

A group of American Indians who find the Washington Redskins' name offensive wants the Supreme Court to take up the matter. The group asked the justices to review a lower court decision that favored the NFL team on a legal technicality.

title: Browns Are On Right Track
content:
BY IRA KAUFMAN

ikaufman@tampatrib.com

Baby steps.

You don't play Vegas showrooms without working a few lounges along the way. Your first job out of residency isn't Chief of Pediatrics. You don't graduate law school and promptly become a candidate for chief justice.

And in the NFL, your first experience in the big time often ends in frustration.

So it was for the Cleveland Browns during Sunday's 31-28 setback at Heinz Field that dropped them two games behind the first-place Steelers in the AFC North.

At 5-4, the Browns are still on track for their first winning season since 2002, but the biggest test in Romeo Crennel's 41-game stint as head coach served as a vivid reminder Cleveland is still learning how to win.

"They've got a lot of confidence," said Pittsburgh linebacker Larry Foote, "but they're just not ready for us yet."

Just when it appeared the Browns would end an eight-game losing streak to their division rivals, Pittsburgh rallied from a 21-9 halftime deficit on the arm and feet of quarterback Ben Roethlisbergerand a defense that yielded only 48 yards after intermission.

"We showed today we can play with anybody," said Browns linebacker Willie McGinest, the former Patriot with 18 games worth of valuable postseason experience.

Derek Anderson, Cleveland's young quarterback enjoying a breakthrough season, directed a 16-play touchdown drive on his first series as the Browns went 4-for-4 on third down against the league's top-ranked defense.

Joshua Cribbs returned one kickoff for 90 yards and took another 100 yards for a score, but the Browns couldn't convert a third down in the second half, unable to cope with Pittsburgh's adjustments.

"We let one get away," Crennel said after Phil Dawson's 52-yard field goal attempt came up short in the final seconds. "If we had played the second half the way we played in the first, we win the game."

Cleveland is still in decent shape, with only one remaining game (Buffalo) against a club with a winning record. And after entering the season with his job in jeopardy, Crennel commands a dynamic young team on the rise.

"The Browns are going to be right there in the AFC wild-card chase," NFL Network analyst Steve Mariucci said.

HUDDLE UP: Don't be surprised if the 2009 Pro Bowl is shifted from Honolulu to Raymond James Stadium and played a week before the Super Bowl. The league is seriously considering moving its annual all-star matchup up by two weeks and placing it at the Super Bowl site, beginning with the '09 game in Tampa. Preliminary plans call for the AFC and NFC squads in '09 to be housed and trained in Orlando to avoid conflicts with Bay area preparations for the Super Bowl. If owners vote to stage the Pro Bowl on the weekend after the conference championship games, players on Super Bowl rosters would not participate. ? Carolina's 0-4 record and boring attack at Bank of America Stadium has turned off the home crowd. There were more than 10,000 empty seats in Charlotte during Sunday's listless loss to Atlanta. ? Donovan McNabb takes the snaps in Philly, but the most important Eagle remains RB Brian Westbrook. ? If the reeling Ravens lose at home Sunday to Cleveland, Brian Billick's sloppy team could easily finish 5-11. With upcoming games against the Chargers, Patriots and Colts in successive weeks, the Billick watch is on in earnest.

How can you give up 34 consecutive points to the Rams at home when you have an opportunity to tie Tampa Bay for the NFC South lead? That's the question despondent Saints fans are asking today. ? After nine games, it's clear Detroit can beat almost anyone at Ford Field and lose to anyone on the road. ? That Cowboys-Packers matchup at Texas Stadium on the evening of Nov. 29 could make us forget about Patriots-Colts. Too bad it's available only on NFL Network. ? Congratulations to Fred Taylor of the Jaguars for topping the 10,000-yard mark in career rushing. There was a time Taylor's skeptics said the ex-Gators standout was soft and injury-prone. ? With home games still ahead against the Jets, Ravens and Bengals, a 0-16 finish by the Dolphins remains unlikely. Still, Miami keeps finding novel ways to lose and a disgruntled fan base is curious to see what rookie QB John Beck can do. So are more than a few Dolphins players. ? Check out Carolina rookie LB Jon Beason, now starting for injured Dan Morgan in the middle. The former Hurricane has almost 20 more tackles than any other Panther and the 25th overall pick has the look of a perennial Pro Bowl selection.

(CHART) IRA KAUFMAN'S POWER RANKINGS

1. Patriots: Hunt for perfection continues in frosty Buffalo.

2. Cowboys: Romo-to-Owens connection looks unstoppable.

3. Colts: Suddenly finding ways to lose.

4. Packers: Favre has turned back the clock.

5. Steelers: Speaking of time, how about Big Ben?

6. Jaguars: Lost only once during a rare three-game road swing.

7. Giants: Must avoid their usual late-season malaise under Tom Coughlin.

8. Titans: Need Albert Haynesworth back pronto.

9. Chargers: Heading to Jacksonville with much to prove on the road.

10. Lions: They were due for a letdown.

11. Seahawks: Best of a soft division.

12. Bucs: Same phrase applies.

13. Browns: A few defensive players away.

14. Redskins: Must regroup quickly going to Big D.

15. Eagles: Still lurking in the NFC wild-card hunt.

16. Saints: Sunday's shocking loss could haunt 'em all season.

17. Bills: Almost burned while looking ahead to Pats.

18. Broncos: Maligned defense starting to stir.

19. Bears: Little margin for error in the stretch run.

20. Cardinals: Enough talent to push Seattle to the brink.

21. Texans: Catching New Orleans on the rebound.

22. Ravens: Fifteen points per game won't get it done.

23. Chiefs: Same here.

24. Panthers: Lambeau Field is a tough place to regroup.

25. Bengals: That's the aggressive defense Marvin Lewis demands.

26. Vikings: Can't compete without Adrian Peterson.

27. Falcons: Two-game winning streak brings hope as Bucs visit.

28. Raiders: Shouldn't be taken seriously with Josh McCown under center.

29. 49ers: Maybe they can beat St. Louis. Maybe.

30. Jets: Already eliminated from AFC East race.

31. Rams: Marc Bulger was masterful at the Superdome.

32. Dolphins: A lot of defense just went to waste vs. Buffalo.

title: USA FOOTBALL: TITANIC EFFORT LIKELY TO SINK COLTS' HOPES
content:
AFC high-fliers Indianapolis and Tennessee's processions towards the post-season were both rudely interrupted last week but the Titans can get back on track first when the pair clash in Nashville.

Tennessee talisman Steve McNair was struggling after a calf injury as his side went down to the improving Jets, but the risk of back-up Billy Volek being required this Sunday is reduced.

And it is also with remembering that all three of the

Titans' reverses have come on the road, while they have won their last ten home games.

True, one of those losses, and the most convincing one at that, came against Sunday's opponents in Week Two. But conditions in the Adelphia Coliseum will be vastly different than they were in the RCA Dome in September.

Indianapolis's defence was a large factor in their fast start, and that victory over the Titans gave them the narrow advantage in the AFC South that they still hold. But the performances that saw the Colts concede only 26 points in their first three games have been diluted by injuries and they have allowed opponents an alarming 25.88 points per game over the last eight weeks.

Colts running back Edgerrin James has been charging hard of late but he could struggle to make an impact against a

defence that has not allowed a 100-yard rusher for an NFL-best 26 home games.

The divisional title could well be decided on Sunday and it should be heading to Tennessee. All nine of the Titans' wins have been by more than today's handicap line and home advantage can help take that tally into double figures.

Recommendation

Tennessee -3.5

2pts 10-11 (general)

title: McBriar's Bowl dream shot down
content:
AUSTRALIAN punter Mat McBriar and his Dallas Cowboys' Super Bowl dreams were shot down by the Minnesota Vikings' gunslinging veteran quarterback Brett Favre.

Favre, 40, led the Vikings to a 34-3 win over the Cowboys in Minnesota yesterday with four laser-like touchdown passes and not one interception.

The Vikings play the New Orleans Saints next weekend in the NFC Championship game for a place in the Super Bowl.

``Today was like this season - wonderful,'' said Favre, who left the New York Jets last year and contemplated retiring before signing with Minnesota.

``I have to admit I was concerned going into this game because the Cowboys were playing outstanding.

``To win the way we did . . . was convincing to say the least.''

Before the weekend began there was a real prospect McBriar and fellow Aussie punter Ben Graham would meet next weekend in the NFC Championship, guaranteeing an Australian in the Super Bowl for the second consecutive year.

With Dallas bowing out to the Vikings and Graham's Arizona Cardinals thrashed 45-14 on Saturday by the Saints, the Vikings or the Saints will represent the NFC in the February 7 Super Bowl.

Melbourne's McBriar punted three times, averaging 46 yards, with two dropping inside the Vikings 20-yard line.

Favre defied his age with pinpoint passing, clocking up 234 passing yards, and the Vikings defensive unit stormed and harassed Cowboys quarterback Tony Romo throughout the game.

Romo was sacked six times, had three fumbles and threw one interception. Favre could not be stopped, hitting wide receiver Sidney Rice for three of four of the Cowboys' touchdowns, including a perfect 47-yard pass in the first quarter to set the groundwork for the lopsided win.

The Indianapolis Colts moved through to the AFC Championship game on Saturday by beating the Baltimore Ravens 20-3.

The Jets won for the seventh time in their past eight games by coming from behind to defeat San Diego, 17-14, in the AFC divisional playoffs at Qualcomm Stadium. The Chargers (13-4) had won 11 consecutive games and hadn't been held to fewer than 20 points all season. The Jets (11-7) will visit Indianapolis (15-2) on Sunday in the AFC title game. It will be the Jets' first appearance in an AFC final since the 1998 season.

title: WHAT THEY'RE SAYING
content:
It's four days to the Keyshawn Bowl, and the Keyshawn Johnson-Wayne Chrebet soap opera is raging louder than ever. Glenn Foley finds that a little bit sad. The former Jets quarterback, still friends with both wide receivers, wishes people would remember the way they played football together, not the other nonsense.WHATSAY.21

Foley, in a nostalgic mood, popped an old tape into his VCR the other day. It was Jets vs. Giants, the 1998 preseason game. The unemployed passer, cut recently by the Seahawks, sat back and enjoyed the show.

"When they played together, they were a joy to watch," Foley said from his home in New Jersey. "You should've seen them, catching the ball, running over people, blocking for each other, picking each other up, smacking each other's helmet. I was watching magic.

"They had their thing off the field," Foley continued, "but they were unbelievable together. When people talk about Wayne and Keyshawn, that's what I remember. They were the best 1-2 combination I've ever been around. That other stuff just sells tickets."

That "other stuff" won't go away, mostly because Johnson continues to take potshots at Chrebet. The other day, Johnson said comparing him to Chrebet was like is equating a star to a flashlight.

Down in Jacksonville, former Jets tight end Kyle Brady laughed when he was told that remark. To him, it was typical Keyshawn. Brady dressed next to Johnson and Chrebet for three years, and his memories of their infamous relationship aren't as fond as those of Foley.

"(Johnson) makes a lot of plays and he helps teams win," said Brady, now starting for the Jaguars, "but there was a tension that existed in the locker room. Not only between him and Wayne, but some of the other players as well. That's not real conducive to a winning atmosphere."

Rich Cimini,

New York Daily News

The investigation into the mysterious decline in the level of Vinny Testaverde's play as Jets quarterback is ongoing. We are working on leads that suggest he will be abducted at halftime of his first regular-season game in Tampa, since he fled the Bucs after the 1992 season.

We'd like to reveal that there is an inoperable breakdown in the hydraulic system of his bionic right arm, attached during top-secret surgery on a military base before he signed with the Jets in June 1998.

But we can't. The evidence just isn't there. For a bionic arm or for a breakdown.

Correction. There's lots of statistical evidence that says Vinny is not performing at his 1998 pace. He's produced touchdowns on 19.4 percent of his drives compared with 28.3 in the 1998 season. He's completing 48.7 percent of his passes now compared with 61.5 then. His passer rating is a pedestrian 73.2; even Jeff Garcia, Jeff Blake and Jay Fiedler have better ratings.

But the anecdotal evidence doesn't support this conclusion. Perhaps the Jets are delusional. Or perhaps they are not concerned.

"Vinny got his team in the end zone when they got down there, and he brought his team home a winner," coach Al Groh said after the win over Buffalo. "That's all that matters with a quarterback in this league."

Randy Lange,

Bergen Record

PITTSBURGH STEELER vs. BALTIMORE RAVENS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday, at Baltimore

Records: Steelers (1-0); Ravens (0-1)

The skinny: The Steelers have had success vs. Baltimore, winning five of the past six meetings (including playoffs). However, the Ravens won the last meeting, 13-10 in overtime in last year's season finale. The Ravens are banged up physically -- LB Peter Boulware, among others, won't play -- and they're banged up mentally after managing just a field goal in their season-opening loss to the Browns. The Steelers, meantime, are feeling good about winning a game that last year they seemed to lose. What's more, they have a chance to take a two-game lead on the team that is favored to win the AFC North. But early advantages don't mean much. Just ask the Vikings, who had a three-game division lead after six weeks last year … and missed the playoffs.

Prediction: Ravens, 13-10

CLEVELAND BROWNS vs. DALLAS COWBOYS

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at Dallas

Records: Browns (1-0); Cowboys (0-1)

The skinny: Cleveland was one of last week's surprises, not only because the Browns beat the Ravens but also because they held Jamal Lewis to just 57 yards rushing, which is 238 yards fewer than he had in the first meeting against them last year. What would be an even bigger surprise is if the Browns beat Dallas, because the Browns haven't started 2-0 since 1993 (OK, so they took a three-year sabbatical from the league in that time). The Cowboys got whacked in their season opener, 35-17, by the Vikings. The good news, though, is that 40-year-old Vinny Testaverde, after throwing 50 times in Minnesota, reported no stiffness in his arm. He also said he doesn't feel chilled and feels no compelling need to do a crossword puzzle.

Prediction: Cowboys, 28-21

CAROLINA PANTHERS vs. KANSAS CITY CHIEFS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Kansas City

Records: Panthers (0-1) at Chiefs (0-1)

The skinny: It was no surprise Kansas City gave up 34 points in a season-opening loss to Denver. The Chiefs defense gets pushed around more than a Giant Eagle shopping cart. But Carolina? The Panthers were easily handled by Green Bay in a 24-14 loss on Monday night, and their highly acclaimed defensive line got turned and creased like a pair of polyester pants. But, after giving up 119 yards to Ahman Green, the Panthers could get gouged again by Priest Holmes, who had 151 yards rushing against the Broncos. There must be something about playing in Missouri: The Chiefs have won a club-record 13 games in a row at home, an active streak exceeded only by the St. Louis Rams (14 in a row).

Prediction: Chiefs, 27-24

ST. LOUIS RAMS vs ATLANTA FALCONS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Atlanta

Records: Rams (1-0); Falcons (1-0)

The skinny: Atlanta got just what it wanted when it hired defensive-minded Jim Mora. Jr., as head coach. Mora, a former defensive coordinator with the 49ers, put his stamp on his new team in last week's 21-19 victory against his old team. Of course, the Falcons are looking for some defense to go with an offense that includes Mike Vick, Warrick Dunn, T.J. Duckett and Peerless Price. They'll get a better understanding of the quality of their defense against St. Louis. Last year, the Rams posted 36 points in a shutout victory against the Birds -- their first shutout in 10 years -- though it must be noted Vick did not play in that game. That won't happen this time.

Prediction: Falcons, 20-17

HOUSTON TEXANS vs. DETROIT LIONS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Detroit

Records: Texans (0-1); Lions (1-0)

The skinny: Playing at home has not been much of a problem for Detroit. After all, all five of the Lions' victories in '03 came at Ford Field. Winning on the road, now that's been a problem. But the Lions even ended that misery last week in Chicago, their first road victory in 26 games. All is not cheery, though, with the Lions. Charles Rogers, their No. 1 pick in '03 who missed 11 games last season with a broken collarbone, broke his collarbone again and is out for the season. That will leave their receiving corps in the very talented hands of rookie No. 1 Roy Williams, who had four catches for 69 yards in his NFL debut, including a dazzling Lynn Swann-type reception.

Prediction: Lions, 24-14

CHICAGO BEARS vs. GREEN BAY PACKERS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Green Bay

Records: Bears (0-1); Packers (1-0)

The skinny: Among the most impressive aspects of opening weekend was the play of the Green Bay defense, which harrassed Carolina quarterback Jake Delhomme and held Stephen Davis to 26 yards on nine carries in a 24-14 victory. The Packers looked every bit like a team capable of going to the Super Bowl, though they might have a difficult time getting out of their own division. Chicago, meantime, has not had much luck with the Packers, losing seven in a row. And there was a not whole lot for new coach Lovie Smith to like about the Bears' 20-16 home loss to the Lions. And he might not like the possible return of Packers CB Mike McKenzie.

Prediction: Packers, 34-14

DENVER BRONCOS vs JACKSONVILLE JAGUARS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Jacksonville

Records: Broncos (1-0); Jaguars (1-0)

The skinny: First it was Terrell Davis. Then Mike Anderson. Back to Davis. Then Olandis Gary and Clinton Portis. Now, meet Quentin Griffin, the latest in what is soon to be another 1,000-yard rusher in Denver. It doesn't seem to matter which back is plugged into the Broncos offense. Griffin is the most recent example. A No. 4 draft choice who is 5-foot-7, 195 pounds, Griffin rushed for 156 yards in his first NFL start and looked like a combination of Tony Dorsett and Emmitt Smith. Of course, Griffin was running against a Kansas City defense that would have a hard time catching a cold. He will have a more difficult time against Jacksonville's front seven, particularly DTs Marcus Stround and John Henderson.

Prediction: Broncos, 20-14

SAN FRANCISCO 49ERS vs. NEW ORLEANS SAINTS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at New Orleans

Records: 49ers (0-1); Saints (0-1)

The skinny: New Orleans is bracing for a storm, but it's not the 49ers. The only thing more tumultuous than Hurricane Ivan might be the after-effects of a loss to San Francisco, particularly at home. But, since 2000, the Saints are 15-18 at the Superdome, a record that was negatively affected last week when they lost the opener to Seattle, 21-7, a team that doesn't do well on the road. Certainly that was the case last season with the 49ers, who were 1-7 away from home. The Saints offense is much better than it showed against the Seahawks. Conversely, the 49ers offense will be worse than it showed against the Falcons, mainly because QB Tim Rattay is injured and not expected to play.

Prediction: Saints, 24-13

WASHINGTON REDSKINS vs. NEW YORK GIANTS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at New York

Records: Redskins (1-0); Giants (0-1)

The skinny: On his first carry with his new team, Clinton Portis ran 64 yards for a touchdown, a welcome-back present for Joe Gibbs in his first game with his old team. Washington's 16-10 victory against Tampa Bay was victory No. 500 for the franchise, and Gibbs has been responsible for 25 percent of those wins (125-60). Tom Coughlin, making a return to the team where he formerly was an assistant coach, did not have as much good fortune in his first game. The Giants were embarrassed by the Eagles, 31-17, with the lone bright spot being Tiki Barber's 72-yard touchdown run. Other than that, the Giants were about as entertaining as a lost episode of "Three's Company."

Prediction: Redskins, 17-16

INDIANAPOLIS COLTS vs. TENNESSEE TITANS

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Tennessee

Records: Colts (0-1); Titans (1-0)

The skinny: Some teams, like Houston, can't win back-to-back games. Others, such as Indianapolis, can't seem to lose back-to-back games. The Colts have gone 25 weeks without losing two games in a row, but they are staring hard at that possibility playing on the road against Tennessee. The Colts played well in their opening loss in New England and have beaten the Titans in their past two meetings. The game is a showcase of last year's co-MVPs, Peyton Manning and Steve McNair. But it will also feature the kicker who once had the league's longest consecutive field goal streak (Gary Anderson, 40) and the kicker who broke it (Mike Vanderjagt, 42).

Prediction: Colts, 28-24

SEATTLE SEAHAWKS vs. TAMPA BAY BUCCANEERS

When: 4:05 p.m. Sunday at Tampa

Records: Seahawks (1-0); Buccaneers (0-1)

The skinny: Playing on the road is a little more difficult for Seattle than most NFL teams because the Seahawks' lengthy plane trips are enjoyed only by frequent-flier extremists. Maybe that's why the Seahawks haven't won back-to-back road games since 1994. But, after last week's opening victory in New Orleans, the Hawks have a chance to end that drought and accumulate as many road victories already in 2004 as they did the entire 2003 season. It won't be easy, though, in Tampa Bay, where the Bucs are coming off a 16-10 loss to the Redskins in Week 1. If they want to avoid a repeat of last season, when the Bucs were 7-9, they have to beat the Seahawks.

Prediction: Buccaneers, 17-16

NEW ENGLAND PATRIOTS vs. ARIZONA CARDINALS

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at Arizona

Records: Patriots (1-0); Cardinals (0-1)

The skinny: New England is closing in on the NFL record for consecutive victories (18) after winning No. 16 in a row, including playoffs, against Indianapolis. There doesn't appear to be much standing in the Patriots way -- either for the record or the Super Bowl. The addition of Corey Dillon will only help the already efficient passing of Tom Brady. Arizona, though, nearly pulled the upset of opening week before losing in St. Louis to the Rams. Maybe the biggest surprise, though, was Emmitt Smith, who rushed for 87 yards, scored on an 11-yard run and increased his chances of becoming the first NFL player to collect a weekly salary and Social Security check at the same time.

Prediction: Patriots, 24-13

BUFFALO BILLS vs. OAKLAND RAIDERS

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at Oakland

Records: Bills (0-1); Raiders (0-1)

The skinny: It's bad enough losing your first game as NFL head coach. When it happens on the last play of the game, as it did with Buffalo's Mike Mularkey, the distress is akin to watching your new car get towed. That, though, is what Mularkey, the former Steelers offensive coordinator, had to endure in his debut -- the Bills losing, 13-10, on a last-second TD pass from Jacksonville's Byron Leftwich. Oakland's Norv Turner suffered a similar fate at Heinz Field, losing on a last-second field goal by Jeff Reed to ruin his return to head coaching. The Bills haven't played the Raiders on the road since 1992, when the Raiders were in Los Angeles.

Prediction: Raiders, 24-23

NEW YORK JETS vs. SAN DIEGO CHARGERS

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at San Diego

Records: Jets (1-0); Chargers (1-0)

The skinny: Curtis Martin spent the summer enduring a rigorous training regimen and wanting more carries in training camp. The idea was to be prepared for the start of the regular season. Turns out, it was a great idea. Martin had 196 yards rushing against the Browns, the seventh highest total for an opening weekend, in a 31-24 Jets victory. Martin, though, might not even be the best back on the field. La Dainian Tomlinson had 121 yards in a 27-20 victory in Houston, the third victory in the past five games for San Diego, dating to last season. Last time these teams met in San Diego (2002), the Jets scored 44 points, their most on the road since 1988.

Prediction: Jets, 31-24

MIAMI DOLPHINS vs. CINCINNATI BENGALS

When: 8:30 p.m. Sunday at Cincinnati

Records: Dolphins (0-1); Bengals (0-1)

The skinny: Storms have been blowing through South Florida, and not just ones named Charley and Frances. The latest is Jay, as in Fielder, who has been blown out as the starting quarterback in Miami. He has been replaced by A.J. Feeley, who was signed in the offseason from the Eagles, where he was 4-1 as a starter. Lots of good karma should be present for Feeley: The Fish have won nine in a row against Cincinnati, a streak that stretches to 1977 when the Bengals had Ken Anderson, Pete Johnson and Archie Griffin in their backfield. Also, they are 5-0 against the Bengals in primetime, irrefutable proof that Dolphins must have some traces of ham in them.

Prediction: Bengals, 24-20

MINNESOTA VIKINGS vs. PHILADELPHIA EAGLES

When: 9 p.m. Monday at Philadelphia

Records: Vikings (1-0); Eagles (1-0)

The skinny: Let's not get too excited about Minnesota, which, if it played the Super Bowl today, would be 4 1/2-point favorites. The Vikings did this impressive thing last year, too, remember? Started 6-0, looked like they would clinch the division by Thanksgiving, then missed the playoffs. Nonetheless, it's tough not to like what they did against Dallas. Daunte Culpepper threw a career-best five TD passes. It should make for a good matchup vs. Philadelphia, which was equally impressive vs. the Giants. Terrell Owens had three TD catches, though there was little celebration -- for T.O., anyway -- when he reached the end zone. That won't be the case in front of the MNF cameras.

Prediction: Vikings, 31-30

title: Consistency sets the Jets' Martin apart
content:
EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. -- The New York Jets' Curtis Martin stepped onto the snow-covered field Sunday at the Meadowlands almost three hours before kickoff and almost an hour earlier than usual.

Never mind that the Jets were opposing the Pittsburgh Steelers in a matchup of 5-8 teams going nowhere. Martin was a running back in search of an edge, and that meant cutting and slashing through the snow until he had the balance and the confidence that he could provide what was needed.

Martin's 30 carries for 174 yards helped the Jets blank the Steelers 6-0 and allowed the running back who normally eschews personal glory to achieve two major milestones.

Martin moved past John Riggins into 11th place on the NFL's all-time rushing list with 11,488 yards. He also surpassed 1,000 yards for the ninth consecutive season to open his career. Only the retired Barry Sanders has more successive 1,000-yard efforts, with 10.

To keep Sanders' company means everything to Martin, 30.

"I'm not big on statistics and individual goals," he says, "but this one is kind of special to me. I'm only the second person in the world ever to do this, and I'm grateful."

While Martin lacks the breakaway ability of some of the league's more prominent backs, he takes enormous pride in his week-in, week-out production.

He is all about consistency.

"We see it manifest itself in football," he says, "but it's something I believe as a person. That's what I want to be. Whatever I'm doing in my life, whether it's business, personal relationships, my occupation, I believe consistency is the true test."

Perhaps only the 5-11, 210-pound back can understand what he endured to bang out all those 1,000-yard seasons. He finished last year, for instance, with a career-low 1,094 yards and needed to overcome severe sprains to both ankles. He stayed in the lineup despite such agonizing pain that he remembers tears welling in his eyes before he took a handoff against the Miami Dolphins in Week 3.

When Martin struggled early this season while the Jets tried to find their way without injured quarterback Chad Pennington and with an offensive line that had yet to jell, there were whispers the nine-year veteran had lost a step after years of punishment.

That talk has ceased and been replaced by admiration for a quiet leader who continues to deliver, even when the standings are bleak and the weather is bleaker.

Teammates notice.

"He doesn't say much," safety Sam Garnes says. "Just the way he plays, you get impressed. He plays hard every game.

"For a guy to do what he did for nine years straight is unheard of except for Barry Sanders, and he was one of the best to ever play the game."

Jets coach Herman Edwards sees a back who is well on his way toward establishing Hall of Fame credentials.

"You will probably see the guy in Canton one day," Edwards says. "He just keeps running."

title: Homecoming king Former Ram Bettis is headed to Detroit for his first Super Bowl
content:
A year ago, veteran Steelers running back Jerome Bettis and rookie quarterback Ben Roethlisbergerstood together on the sideline at Heinz Field in Pittsburgh while the clock wound down in the AFC championship game.

Both were streaming tears as the New England Patriots wrapped up a 41-27 victory that sent them to the Super Bowl. Amid the blubbering, Roethlisberger made an impassioned plea to Bettis.

"He was boo-hooing," Bettis recalled, "and he turned to me and said, 'Come back, I'll get you to the Super Bowl. Just give me one more year.' It meant a lot."

It meant even more Sunday after Roethlisberger connected on 21 of 29 passes for 275 yards and two touchdowns as the Steelers turned back favored Denver 34-17 at Invesco Field, and the "Bus" punched a ticket for home: The Super Bowl will be played in Detroit, where he grew up.

Roethlisberger said making good on his promise has "been my driving force all year. I'm glad I didn't have to cry and apologize to him" again.

Bettis, who will turn 34 just 11 days after the Steelers meet Seattle on Feb. 5 at Ford Field, said: "I can't believe it; it's a dream come true. I'm so looking forward to showing everybody a good time in Detroit."

Bettis' dream nearly dissolved into a nightmare when he fumbled -- only his 42nd in more than 3,700 career "touches" and first this season -- at the goal line in the last 90 seconds last week in Indianapolis with the Steelers clinging to a 21-18 lead. Colts cornerback Nick Harper scooped up the loose ball and scampered the other way.

Roethlisberger, twisting and turning, hustled back and arm-tackled him. Then in the final seconds, Mike Vanderjagt -- the most accurate field-goal kicker in NFL history -- clunked a 46-yarder wide right, and Bettis was absolved.

Bettis decided to return for a 13th NFL season "because I knew we had a good team. We'd been on the doorstep a couple of times." He acknowledged, too, that the prospect of completing his career Hollywood-style -- playing in his only Super Bowl, and in Detroit -- entered into his thinking, too.

"You know the possibility exists," he said, smiling. "Once we got to the playoffs, we knew it was a realistic possibility."

Possible, but hardly probable. The Steelers, who had to win their last four regular-season games just to nab a wild-card berth, became the first sixth seed to win a conference title. They did so with three road victories, each time prevailing as the underdog.

"Nobody expected us to win, nobody was picking us to win," Bettis said. "That's fine ... as long as we believed."

Last season, Bettis became a backup to rookie Willie Parker. Bettis rushed for only 368 of his 13,662 career yards -- No. 5 in NFL history -- this season. He's used mostly in short-yardage situations, where he can use his considerable bulk -- he's listed at 255 pounds, which appears to be about 50 pounds shy of the truth -- to plow over defenders.

Bettis never complained about his diminished role. "He a classy guy, and he's such an inspiration," coach Bill Cowher said.

On Sunday, Bettis held on to the ball securely as he barged into the end zone from 3 yards out in the second quarter. "That's my job," he said. "It felt good to be able to get that score and get that Indianapolis game behind me."

Now, he can look ahead. He conceded that he might turn his cell phone off in the days leading to the Super Bowl. But otherwise, he plans to enjoy the experience. "We've played (in Detroit) a couple of times, so that's a benefit for me," Bettis said. "Nothing the likes of this type of game, of course. But I don't think there's going to be any more pressure on me."

If not for what one Pittsburgh writer has termed the "Great St. Louis Bus Robbery," Bettis probably wouldn't be headed back to Motown. In the spring of 1996, new Rams coach Rich Brooks became disenchanted with Bettis and sought to trade him.

The Steelers were in the market for a back, and Cowher asked assistant coach Dick Hoak to take a look at Bettis on tape. After only a few minutes, Hoak reported to Cowher: "Get him."

St. Louis got two draft picks; Bettis, who rushed for 3,091 yards in three seasons with the Rams, got a fresh start. "I don't think I would've had the same career had I not been traded," he said. "So, thank you Rams."

The Steelers rode the "Bus" to three AFC championship games, all losses on their home turf. This year, though, they took their act on the road and became a hit.

They have one more stop to make.

"It's a great feeling knowing that we get a chance to take Jerome back home," fellow running back Duce Staley said. "That's all he wanted all year. So now, the destination for Jerome is Detroit."

---

STLtoday.com/ramstalk Was trading Jerome Bettis to Pittsburgh the best for both him and the Rams? Sound off in Rams Talk.

title: OVATION CHEERS DEJECTED FASSEL
content:
TAMPA - The Giants postgame party was packed with 1,500 people but had one glaring no-show. Jim Fassel was in his hotel room with his family and had no desire to take the elevator downstairs for the get-together set up in a tent.

"I just wasn't in the mood," Fassel said yesterday.

He had returned from the stadium after the 34-7 loss to the Ravens in Super Bowl XXXV had put a disastrous ending on a great season. But Fassel's family convinced him to make an appearance at the party. It was the right thing to do.

As he entered the tent, his presence was felt immediately by the players, members of the organization and their families and friends who attended. "He just walked in and everybody gave him a standing ovation, which was kind of nice," Giants VP John Mara said. "I think he got a little emotional about it. It was kind of nice to see."

Jason Sehorn was on his feet. "The second he walked in, it was kind of amazing," Sehorn said. "People just knew and started clapping and realized what they were clapping for and just joined in. I stood up for him. He's your leader, the person who set the tone. I don't know anybody who couldn't respect the man for what he did this year. I know I do."

The party started at 11:30 p.m. Fassel showed up around midnight and stayed for one hour. He said the standing ovation "felt very good. That was very nice and probably as good as I felt. I wasn't in a partying mood. For them to do that, made me feel a lot better."

Clearly, he meant it, because as he talked yesterday about the reception he received, he got all choked up. The pain of the loss was still evident in Fassel's face 12 hours after the game. The further a team advances in the playoffs, the more crushing it is when they lose. The Giants had never known this feeling - they won their first two Super Bowls.

It was an emotional year for Fassel, first fighting to keep his job, then issuing the playoff guarantee, which lit a spark in his players as they ripped off a seven-game winning streak that ended in the Super Bowl, which turned out to be a one-sided loss.

Before the game was over, GM Ernie Accorsi was already in the locker room waiting for the team. He later sat with Fassel in the coach's office.

"I told Jim, 'Don't you for a second allow this to affect the great coaching job that you did,' " Accorsi said. "It was just him and I told him that. . . . He did a great coaching job. These things happen. They happened to great teams in the past. I'm the last guy who takes any solace in just getting here, believe me. But by the same token, you can't let them be too hard on themselves because they lost this game."

The Giants will soon work on Fassel's contract extension. Expect them to rip up the final year of his deal, worth $1.2 million, and give him a four-year pact that could reach $12 million. If winning the Super Bowl would have resulted in a better contract, Mara was not saying. "He's going to get a healthy increase. Let's put it that way," he said. "You won't be holding any benefits for him."

There was uncertainty about Fassel's future with the Giants going into the season. Now there is stability. . . . Fassel wasn't the only one choked up. Giants co-owner Wellington Mara addressed the team in the locke rroom after the loss. John Mara was standing in the back of the room as his father spoke softly to the the players and coaches. "I could tell he was pretty emotional," he said. "I could barely hear his words. I think it was basically, 'We're proud of you, don't hang your heads, it was a great year.' "IT'S NO TROPHY DAY FOR COMMISSIONERIT WAS a strange scene yesterday morning as Paul Tagliabue handed the MVP Trophy to Ray Lewis, who a year ago right after the Super Bowl was charged with double murder. The charges were dropped in June, but Lewis pleaded guilty to obstruction of justice and Tagliabue fined him $250,000. It was uncomfortable just watching Tagliabue and Lewis stand on the podium together. Based on how he played in the Super Bowl, Lewis deserved the prestigious award. But let's just say this presentation didn't have the sentimental value of last year when feel-good story Kurt Warner won it, or two years ago when John Elway was named MVP in what turned out to be the final game of his career. The NFL is so conscious of its image and now Lewis has won two of its most coveted awards in the last month - Defensive Player of the Year and Super Bowl MVP. Just my opinion, but I can't imagine that makes the league very happy. . . . Give the Ravens credit: They may have turned off many people with their trash-talking, but they backed it up. . . . If Jessie Armstead's interception wasn't called back on that curious holding call on Keith Hamilton, it would have been 7-7 in the second quarter with the potential for it to be a much different game. It was easily the biggest play of the game. If the play stood, I wonder if Brian Billick would have let Trent Dilfer throw another pass the rest of the way. . . . How can Billick not go after Redskins soon-to-be free agent QB Brad Johnson after the way Dilfer played in the Super Bowl? . . . Ravens defensive coordinator Marvin Lewis was supposed to interview for jobs with the Lions, Browns and Bills after the Super Bowl. And Giants defensive coordinator John Fox was to be interviewed by the Browns and Bills. Well, the Lions hired Marty Mornhinweg last week and the Browns hired University of Miami coach Butch Davis yesterday. One opening left in Buffalo and it's Lewis vs. Fox, with Lewis the frontrunner. . . . After losing three AFC title games to Elway and the Broncos in the '80s, Art Modell finally got the Super Bowl and won it. "We came so close time and time again, but no cigar - and now comes the cigar," he said. . . . With the Browns hiring Davis, a protege of Jimmy Johnson, on the day after the former Browns owner won the Super Bowl, it should take some of the sting out of it for the people of Cleveland. There had to be a lot of Giants fans in Cleveland on Sunday. . . . The Giants obviously need more firepower on offense next year as well as some help in the secondary. Donovan McNabb is only getting better in Philly, so the Giants better come up with some free-agent signings or the Eagles are going to win the NFC East next year.BLOWOUT TAKES TOLL ON COLLINSKERRY COLLINS played one of the worst games in Super Bowl history. His four interceptions tied Craig Morton, Jim Kelly and Drew Bledsoe for most in the Super Bowl. He completed just 15-of-39 for a minuscule 112 yards with a long of 19 yards. That comes two weeks after throwing for 381 yards and five TDs against the Vikings.

And he finished the Super Bowl with a slightly separated right shoulder, thanks to a Sam Adams hit in the third quarter with the Giants already down 24-7. He was in pain, but didn't find out until after the game it was separated. He said he will not need surgery.

Now the question is whether Collins can recover from such a miserable performance and whether it will have a carryover effect and ruin his career. "I've been able to recently put things behind me and move on," Collins said. "I've faced bigger challenges in my life than this. So, I'll learn from this. I'll learn a lot from this."

Jim Fassel talked to Collins after the game Sunday night. "I told him I'm proud as hell of him," Fassel said. "I'm proud of everything he's done, the way he handled himself, the way he played this year. I'm damn glad he's our quarterback. We're going to be around here together for a long time."

title:
MAKING HIS POINT;
 WARD CLARIFIES RECENT REMARKS, ENJOYS BIG SEASON
content:
Hines Ward has been many things for the Steelers. He is the best blocking wide receiver in the National Football League. He is the leading rushing wide receiver in the league. And he has more touchdown catches than any wide receiver in the league.

What's more, in the past five games, he's the hottest and most productive wide receiver in the league.

But he wants everyone to know he's also not the most selfish wide receiver in the league. Or the most envious. Or whatever it is his teammates or anyone who read his comments after the 29-21 victory against the Cincinnati Bengals think he is.

After Ward caught five passes for 125 yards -- his third 100-yard receiving game in a row -- and had a 64-yard touchdown catch, he said teammate Plaxico Burress "would be on the cover of USA Today" if he had that kind of game. The comment smacked of jealousy, a curious reaction from a player who is second in the NFL in receptions (81) and receiving yardage (1,039), behind only Marvin Harrison of Indianapolis.

"It got blown out of proportion," Ward said. "They didn't hear what I said. I just want people to understand, we have two great receivers, not just Plex the guy doing one thing. I didn't mean to make it derogatory toward Plex or anything. This whole offense, for me having a great year, it's a reflection of the offensive line blocking well, both Tommy [Maddox] and Kordell [Stewart] had opportunities to throw me the ball, and Plex getting double-teamed. All that combination is the reason I'm having my year.

"It's not from me trying to tell people, look at me, what I've done. I never tried to be a selfish thing. I like to think of myself as a pretty good receiver. Ever since I've been here, it's always been what Troy [Edwards] hasn't done, what Plex hasn't done. Nobody ever looked at the positive things both Bobby [Shaw] and myself had done. It was always the negative. That's all it was about. It wasn't about me trying to call myself out, like I'm better than the team."

Ward, a third-round pick in 1998, is a player with great pride. After the Steelers drafted Troy Edwards in the first round in 1999, Ward tied for the team lead with 61 catches and a team-high seven touchdowns. But, when they drafted Burress in the first round the following year, the Steelers moved Edwards to flanker and put Ward on the bench.

When Edwards was injured early in the 2000 season, Ward stepped in and finished as the team's leading receiver again, this time with 48 catches. But Ward also was developing into one of the most tenacious blocking receivers in the league, one of the reasons Jerome Bettis was having one of his best seasons that year.

Then, after leading the team with 94 catches and joining with Burress to become the first 1,000-yard receiving tandem in club history in 2001, Ward was moved to the slot in third-down passing situations in training camp in an attempt to get Edwards on the field and more involved in the offense. That was the final straw.

Ward was angered by the move, and told Coach Bill Cowher about his feelings. Ward was upset he had to move positions because Edwards did not know how to play the slot position. But he also was upset because he suspected the move was short-lived, especially because rookie Antwaan Randle El was drafted to play that position. Ward said he didn't want to "waste" his training camp playing a position he wouldn't play in the regular season.

"It's not about me being selfish," Ward said. "At the time, for me to become a better wideout, I wanted to stay at one position. I didn't want to be switched, especially if you had no intention of using me. I had done that before in college. Now I'm starting to come into my own. I am getting better, I'm staying at one position and I'm trying to master the position.

"It's hard when you're switching here and there and not being able to stay at one spot. You kind of sit there and look at it like, man, why am I here? Or, are they just using me? It was more of me saying, let me just become a great receiver and not use me here and there or the slot, especially if you have no intention of playing me there. I didn't want to sound selfish. I just want to become a better wideout."

Ward has become that, and more, heading into the game Sunday in Jacksonville.

He leads all NFL wide receivers with 11 touchdowns, one shy of the Steelers' single-season record held by Louis Lipps (1985) and Buddy Dial (1961). He leads all wide receivers with 114 yards rushing on seven carries, an average of 16.2 yards per attempt.

And Ward and Burress have combined for 134 catches -- second only to Buffalo's Eric Moulds and Peerless Price (138) -- and a league-best 1,887 receiving yards. Their 17 combined touchdown receptions also lead the league.

In the past five games, Ward, not Burress, has been the talk of the league. He has 43 catches, 608 yards and five touchdowns in that span. There is no reason to be envious.

"I'm not 6-5 … he's a first-rounder, I understand that," Ward said. "It's not for me to say, 'Hey, look what I've done, forget what they've done.' I never intended for it to be that way. I hope my teammates feel that way.

"If I was so selfish, would I really be out there blocking as hard as I want to or going out there and trying to do whatever it takes to sacrifice my body to make a catch over the middle?. It all got blown out of proportion. I never meant it to be a derogatory thing toward Plex.

"Who am I to talk about anyone? I would never do that. If people took it that way, I apologize for doing that. That wasn't me trying to single out myself. I'm not that type of guy anyway."

title: JETS' RUSH JOB STOPS FOES IN THEIR TRACKS
content:
OF ALL THE rotten defensive games over the past seven years, perhaps the Jets' low point occurred last Nov. 4 in an overtime loss to the Redskins. With two former Jets on their offensive line, the Skins rushed for 296 yards. It left scars.

"Maybe it's macho or bravado, but you want to stop the run. That's football," linebacker Eric Barton said yesterday. "When someone does something like that, it's embarrassing and takes a lot of your pride."

Not that they had much left. From 2001 to 2007, the Jets surrendered more than 14,000 yards on the ground, the third-highest total in the NFL. But now, after years of making running backs famous, they're finally starting to fight back.

By holding the Bengals to 43 yards in Sunday's 26-14 victory, the Jets lowered their average to 69 yards per game, making them No. 3 in run defense. Only the Ravens and Steelers, perennial defensive heavyweights, are ranked better.

"Wow, that's pretty good," said Barton, sounding a bit surprised by the lofty standing.

Let's not canonize them just yet - after all, the Bengals move slower than Congress - but there's no denying the Jets' dramatic improvement. Skeptics might try to spin it a different way, but 345 yards in five games is a world better than 296 in one game, regardless of the competition.

For the Jets (3-2), winners of two straight, the turning point occurred in late February, when they received permission from the Panthers to speak with Kris Jenkins about a potential trade. The veteran nose tackle recalled his initial meeting with GM Mike Tannenbaum and Eric Mangini, who let his 360-pound visitor know exactly why he was in the building.

"They said, 'If you come here, this is what we need you to do,' and a big part of that was stopping the run," Jenkins said.

The Jets traded third- and fifth-round choices for Jenkins, who replaced undersized nose tackle Dewayne Robertson. It was the first of three moves designed to add bulk to the front seven, a must in any 3-4 scheme. They signed free agent Calvin Pace, a 270-pound pass-rushing outside linebacker (he replaced Victor Hobson), and traded smallish inside linebacker Jonathan Vilma. Vilma became expendable with the emergence of David Harris, who started the final nine games in 2007.

"We got bigger . . . and the gaps are a whole lot smaller compared to last year," defensive end Shaun Ellis said.

Essentially, the only new players are Pace and Jenkins, whose ability to command double-teams has created opportunities for others.

"I've never seen anything like it before," Barton said of Jenkins' impact.

Against the Bengals, the defense was dominant, holding them to 171 total yards - the lowest total in the Mangini era. That the Jets faced journeyman backup Ryan Fitzpatrick, not injured flamethrower Carson Palmer, made it easier to concentrate on the run.

The Jets haven't faced a current top-10 rushing team, but Mangini believes his run defense is good and getting better.

"You always want to stop the run, and it'll be more important when weather becomes an issue," said Mangini, who finally put his stamp on the defense, jettisoning the players from Herm Edwards' Tampa-2 scheme.

On Sunday, the Jets face the dysfunctional Raiders (1-4), who can't do much of anything except run the ball. The addition of rookie Darren McFadden gives them a real threat in the backfield; Oakland is averaging an impressive 141 yards per game on the ground.

The last time the longtime rivals met in Oakland, in 2003, the Raiders thought so little of the Jets' defense that they opened the game with 21 consecutive runs - a humbling 21-run salute. That, too, left scars.

NO DEAL: The Jets aren't expecting any deals before today's 4 p.m. trading deadline. . . . Jets must make a roster move today to create a spot for S Eric Smith, who will be reinstated from his one-game league suspension for his illegal hit on Cards WR Anquan Boldin. Mangini hinted that Abram Elam, who replaced Smith in the starting lineup, could be part of a platoon. . . . Struggling rookie OLB Vernon Gholston wasn't part of any defensive packages against the Bengals, although he ended up playing 13 snaps when Bryan Thomas (calf) sat out the third quarter. Gholston still is looking for his first sack. . . . Only three of Brett Favre's 25 completions on Sunday traveled more than 10 yards in the air. . . . Jets ran 48 of their 62 plays in Cincinnati territory.

title: Dilfer was the rule, not the exception
content:
What's more astounding, that the Bears are an 8-2 team or that Jim Miller is the quarterback of an 8-2 team?  

Seriously, raise your hand if during training camp you believed the Bears could lead the NFC Central at this point of the season.  

So why didn't you?  

My guess is the quarterback position blinded us all to the other good things that were happening with this football team on defense, in the running game, in overall quickness and physicality.  

Shane Matthews, scheduled to start the opener, didn't inspire confidence. Miller, expected to eventually take the job, wasn't much better.  

Our problem as prognosticators was we were stuck in the past when you needed a Super Bowl quarterback to, well, reach the Super Bowl, or at least a playoff quarterback to reach the playoffs. He had to be a high draft choice and outstanding no later than his fifth NFL season.  

It isn't that way anymore. Look where the best current quarterbacks came from. Kurt Warner prepped in the Arena League, Jeff Garcia in Canada. Brett Favre was traded by Atlanta before heating up on Green Bay's frozen tundra. Rich Gannon looked like a career backup.  

Only Peyton Manning took the traditional route, and he's struggling this season.  

Instead of every NFL winning pitcher being a hard thrower who blows opponents away like Randy Johnson does, many now are softballers who bore people into submission like Jamie Moyer does.  

Apparently, the country's best athletes aren't raised to be quarterbacks anymore, even at a time when mobility is the position's rage. They're defenders in football, shortstops in baseball, point guards in basketball, left wings in hockey, Seekers in Quidditch …  

I don't know, maybe that's why a former sixth-round draft choice like Miller can flounder with multiple teams before becoming a winner with the Bears. All he needs to do now is fit in and be functional.  

We traditionalists ignored the trend, thinking Trent Dilfer was the exception rather than the rule while leading the Ravens to the championship last season.  

Ten years ago, it was difficult to name pro football's five best quarterbacks because there were so many more - John Elway, Dan Marino and Jim Kelly from the '83 draft alone, along with Joe Montana, Steve Young, Troy Aikman, Jim Kelly, Phil Simms and perhaps a couple more.  

Ten years later, it's difficult to name the five best because there aren't that many. You start with Favre and Warner, then go to Manning despite his slump, then anoint Garcia … then who?  

The journeyman Gannon? Fragile Mark Brunell? Enigmatic Steve McNair? Youngsters Donovan McNabb, Tim Couch and Daunte Culpepper?  

Maybe I'm missing a candidate or two from both 1991 and 2001, but you get the idea. Back then there was a long list of Hall of Fame candidates, while now there is a long list of guys who would need a spaceship to get to Canton.  

Look at the quarterbacks whose teams lead divisions today - Jay Fiedler, Vinny Testaverde, Kordell Stewart, Warner, Gannon, McNabb and Miller. Except for Warner, Miller compares reasonably well with this group.  

Sunday night, Miller completed 0 of 6 passes in the first quarter and finished the night at 10 of 21 for 97 yards, yet the Bears won anyway.  

Generally, Miller has been as eminently average as Dilfer was last year before the Ravens determined he was too average to be their quarterback this year.  

Monday on Sporting News Radio, Vikings coach Dennis Green sort of complimented Miller as "steady," a nice word for mediocre.  

So far, Miller and Matthews have been just mediocre enough for the Bears to lead their division over the spectacular Favre's Packers.  

And why shouldn't they be in first place? In today's NFL, mediocre is pretty good at the most important position on the field.  

Good enough to be an astounding 8-2 anyway.

title: NFL 5-Star Challenge
content:
All things considered, Oakland star lefty Mark Mulder and Minnesota All-Star catcher A.J. Pierzynski would rather be in the World Series preview pages of this newspaper. But since Mulder's A's lost to Pierzynski's Twins in the Division Series, and the Twins lost to the Angels in the ALCS, they can turn their attention to the NFL, work on their fantasy teams and pick Week 7 games in USA TODAY's Five-Star Challenge.

* * * * * Like pitching perfect game in Yankee Stadium.

* * * * Like getting 2 hits, 2 RBI and catching shutout.

* * * Like weaseling win out of bad game pitched.

* * Like other team is fighting to get to batter's box to face you, and you just seem to hit every bat.

* Like losing Game 5 in playoffs to Twins.

* * * * *

Tampa Bay at Philadelphia, 4:15 p.m.

Mark: McNabb may run circles around the Bucs if they aren't careful, but not many will reach the end zone. Eagles 17-14.

A.J. : It doesn't matter what the temperature is, the Bucs can't win in Philly. But I need Keyshawn to score a TD for my fantasy team. Eagles 24-20.

San Diego at Oakland, 4:05 p.m.

Mark: One of my three hometown picks, the Oakland offense is too much. Unlike my A's, the Raiders win 35-21.

A.J.: After going to Oakland for the playoffs, it's a tough place to play. Those people are crazy. Raiders 31-21.

* * * *

San Fran. at New Orleans, 1 p.m.

Mark: We'll have to wait and see what Terrell Owens signs this weekend. Maybe he can throw beads in the stands after he scores. 49ers 28-21.

A.J.: Aaron Brooks has a big day, and T.O. can't sign enough balls (I thought that was great, by the way). Saints 34-27.

Denver at Kansas City, 1 p.m.

Mark: My friend Jermaine Dye told me there's no way the Broncos can win in K.C. If they do, blame him. Chiefs 24-21.

A.J.: A great shootout. Denver recovers from its tough loss to Miami, Griese hits Smith for two TDs and Clinton Portis has a big day. Broncos 38-31.

Buffalo at Miami, 1 p.m.

Mark: Miami still has Ricky Williams, but without Jay Fiedler, Drew Bledsoe and that offense is too much. Bills 32-24.

A.J.: I usually pick Miami, but since (teammate Doug) Mientkiewicz is a Dolphins fan, I like Buffalo. Bills 28-24.

* * *

Indianapolis at Pittsburgh, Mon., 9 p.m.

Mark: In an upset, Tony Dungy's Colts win 24-21 on a last-second field goal by Mike Vanderjagt, my fantasy kicker last year.

A.J.: Tommy Maddox can't top Peyton Manning, and "The Bus" won't be able to do enough. Colts 34-17.

Washington at Green Bay, 4:15 p.m.

Mark: Spurrier and whichever QB he uses can't hang with Brett Favre and the home-field advantage. Pack 35-14.

A.J.: Spurrier wishes he had Favre. I'm a big Florida fan, so this is a tough pick for me. Packers 35-20.

Jacksonville at Baltimore, 1 p.m.

Mark: Even without Ray Lewis, the Ravens defense steps it up and feasts on those Jaguars. Baltimore 14-10.

A.J.: I'm a Jags fan. With no Lewis, Fred Taylor has a big day. There's not enough offense in Baltimore to keep up. Jags 20-14.

* *

Dallas at Arizona, 4:15 p.m.

Mark: Since I live in Arizona, I hope Plummer comes up big in the desert heat. But don't worry, it's dry heat. Cards 28-24.

A.J.: 0-0 going into overtime, with A-Rod hitting a three-run homer off Schilling to win it in the 10th 3-0.

Carolina at Atlanta, 1 p.m.

Mark: With Michael Vick back in control and running and throwing all over the field, I expect them to roll. Falcons 21-10.

A.J.: Vick is unstoppable and Chris Weinke went to Florida State and I can't pull for FSU (my wife went there). Falcons 24-7.

Seattle at St. Louis, 1 p.m.

Mark: The Rams might be out of the playoffs but at least they won last week, a step in the right direction. The Ramskeep it going 35-14.

A.J.: Torry Holt and Isaac Bruce are on my fantasy team (called Bloodflow). We need to get in win column. Rams 35-17.

*

Minnesota at New York Jets, 1 p.m.

Mark: Daunte Culpepper and Randy Moss finally get things rolling and beat the Jets in their house. Vikings 24-14.

A.J.: Since I play for the Twins, I can't go against Vikings in a national publication. Gary Anderson's extra point wins it 28-27.

Houston at Cleveland, 4:05 p.m.

Mark: After this week, I bet the Browns' faithful won't be booing Tim Couch in his own stadium. Browns 28-14.

A.J.: David Carr takes a beating from Butch Davis and his defense, and Couch doesn't get booed this week. Browns 24-7.

Chicago at Detroit, 1 p.m.

Mark: I grew up in Chicago, so all I have to say is "Da Bears" in a laughe, 65-negative 5!

A.J.: New stadium or not, any time you go into Detroit you want to win. Joey Harrington comes up short. Bears 20-14.

Last week: PWBA bowling star Carolyn Dorin-Ballard rolled a strike at 10-4; PBA star Pete Weber picked up a spare 9-5.

title: Holmes is top dog at midpoint
content:
MVP: Priest Holmes.

Holmes is proving last season was no fluke. And in Kansas City's offense, he is good for 20 fantasy points a week no matter your scoring. He has four games of more than 50 yards receiving and four 100-yard rushing games and has scored every week but one, including weeks of four, three (twice) and two touchdowns.

RUNNERS-UP: Donovan McNabb and Rich Gannon. When he's on, there is no one better than McNabb. Gannon hasn't thrown for fewer than 330 yards since the opener.

BIGGEST FLOP: (tie) William Green and Johnnie Morton.

Green was the rookie running back du jour at draft time, but the Browns pick has been a bust. His best game was a 36-yard effort (he needed 17 carries), and in four of eight games he has fewer than 20. It makes you wonder why Cleveland hasn't gone exclusively with fantasy producer Jamel White.

Morton was expected to thrive in Kansas City's pass-happy offense. Is he still even in the league? After averaging 1,000 yards receiving the past five years in Detroit's offense (we use the term loosely), he's on pace for 404.

RUNNERS-UP: Keyshawn and Kevin Johnson. One touchdown apiece for guys expected to star in new high-powered offenses?

BIGGEST SURPRISE: Deuce McAllister.

Though he wasn't a draft-day risk, few thought McAllister would do this: over 100 yards every week but two, over 1,000 yards total offense and nine touchdowns. He already has as many touchdowns as Ricky Williams had in any one season and is a certain bet to smash Williams' total yardage mark of 1,756 last season.

RUNNERS-UP: Drew Bledsoe and Clinton Portis. Did anyone think Bledsoe would be averaging over 300 yards passing a game? And Portis figured to be the running back of the future with Denver, but in a crowded backfield that included Olandis Gary and Mike Anderson, the future wasn't supposed to be now. On pace for 1,000 yards rushing, Portis has become a Rookie of the Year candidate.

KURT WARNER AWARD: Tommy Maddox.

Kordell Stewart (an honorable mention for the Flop Award) took Pittsburgh to the AFC Championship Game last season. Now he's watching as former Arena Leaguer/XFLer Maddox takes off.

From someone who had Maddox on his XFL Fantasy Football team, congratulations!

RUNNERS-UP: Former New Jersey Red Dog Michael Lewis (1,369 return yards, 2 TDs) and former Albany Firebird David Patten (458 yards, 3 TDs).

WORST FANTASY FOOTBALL COACH: Tony Dungy.

If anyone could come in and spoil the fantasy gold offense of Indianapolis, it's the bane of Bucs fans everywhere. Edgerrin James has 139 fewer rushing yards and two fewer touchdowns than he had at this time last season Marvin Harrison has four fewer touchdowns and Peyton Manning's numbers are slightly down as well.

RUNNERS-UP: Mike Martz and Steve Spurrier. It took Martz six games to figure out he had Marshall Faulk, until then just killing owners of the No. 1 pick in most leagues. And Spurrier led us on during preseason.

FINAL NOTE: I would like to thank all the well-wishers who sent cards and flowers last week upon hearing of the demise of my beloved 0-7 fantasy football team. Thank you again.

Okay, so you guys sent nothing. Nothing! Well, my team doesn't need anything, because it is alive and well (or at the very least on life support) after winning for the first time Sunday.

I don't need to mention whom I beat because that's not my style (Nebraska fan named Matt Waite; lives in New Port Richey), but in keeping with the wacky season, I won the week my top pick, Brett Favre, was off thanks to waiver pickup Tim Couch and the return of Derrick Mason. Go figure.

title: Whatever his motivation, Favre's skill is the key to his success
content:
USA TODAY's cover story on Brett Favre is appropriate considering the season he's having ("Through aches and pains, Favre still driven by dad," News, Friday).

Favre is similar to Fran Tarkenton, the former Minnesota Vikings great, in that he manages to avoid the big hit. That's how Favre played all those games consecutively. Still, with Favre it all begins and ends with that cannon of an arm he possesses.

Talk all you want about his ability to start games or his competitive fire. In the end, it always comes back to that gun called his right arm. Want to play QB in the NFL? You'd better have a grade-A arm.

Robert Peele

Rocky Mount, N.C.

Forever a fan

While away from my four sons and sitting on a flight from Denver to Salt Lake City, I couldn't forget the article about a Green Bay Packers fan who was inspired by Brett Favre playing in a game the day after his father died in 2003. I kept reflecting on the last line: "On that night, that flawed human being was a great man" ("Coping with grief, Favre inspired," Sports, Dec. 7).

I remember how captivated I was watching Favre through cheers and tears on Dec. 22, 2003. I felt the same emotion when I read about Cynthia King, the fan who lost her mother on the same day Favre lost his dad, and how she drew strength from Favre's playing despite his grief.

All those who resent Favre and his decision to play for the Minnesota Vikings should read this article and recall how sad we were for him, how proud he made us feel, and how inspired we were that night.

I intend to share this as a life's lesson with my sons and will stay a Favre fan.

Dustin Rusch

Fond du Lac, Wis.

Enough hero worship

Do you honestly think any of us care, six years later, about the game Brett Favre played after his dad died? My dad died a year ago. He was my best friend. He was my little-league manager, teacher, mentor and hero. In the aftermath of his death, I had to soldier on working in my profession and try to get past it.

What makes you think this egomaniac ballplayer is worthy of being sainted over doing what all of us have to do at some point?

Our hero worshiping of people who play a kids' game is one reason this country is headed into the toilet.

Steve Orton

La Grange, Ill.

title: Dolphins make splash
content:
From coach Tony Sparano chest-bumping players after a "character" win to two more Wildcat scoring strikes, the 5-4 Miami Dolphins are not only above .500 for the first time since 2005. They are legitimate contenders who are fun to watch.

When is the last time anyone said that about the Dolphins?

Sparano chest-bumped linebacker Joey Porter and defensive end Vonnie Holliday after his team bumped off the Seattle Seahawks 21-19 for a third consecutive win.

"I can't be held accountable for my actions at that point, but I think I might have chest-bumped somebody, yeah," Sparano sheepishly acknowledged.

The Dolphins resurrected their Wildcat twist on the single-wing direct snap to a running back. Only this time, the package quarterbacks coach David Lee imported from the University of Arkansas for running backs Ronnie Brown and Ricky Williams featured more of Williams, who scored on a 51-yard run.

Brown scored on a 16-yard run, Miami's eighth touchdown in 49 Wildcat snaps.

And quarterback Chad Pennington fired a perfect, 39-yard touchdown pass over two Seahawks defenders to Ted Ginn Jr.

Porter said he had never been chest-bumped by a head coach. But he's glad to be a chest-bumpee after going 1-15 last season. He's happy where the Dolphins are now, crediting Sparano's no-nonsense motivation and the talent brought in by executive vice president of football operations Bill Parcells and general manager Jeff Ireland.

"We showed that we are a team to be reckoned with," says Porter, whose 12 sacks lead the league. "Look at us. From where we came from to where we are right now, nobody picked us.

"Everything that we're doing offensively, defensively, everybody's buying into the system. We have a guy who's leading us in the right direction.

"Everybody believes in what the head coach is saying. They fear his voice. He's not just out there talking."

Parcells appears to have turned around the Dolphins faster than he did the New York Giants, New England Patriots, New York Jets and Dallas Cowboys.

"I think you have to start with Bill," Seahawks coach Mike Holmgren said. "Bill set the tone as he has wherever he went.

"He's gone to a number of teams and has a proven record of being able to do this."

Parcells hired Sparano, his former Cowboys assistant head coach/offensive line coach, and signed Pennington, the former Jets quarterback drafted 18th overall by Parcells in 2000.

Pennington is completing 66.5% of his passes and has thrown 24 completions of 20 yards or more, tied with Peyton Manning and more than Eli Manning's22, Brett Favre's 21 and Ben Roethlisberger's15.

Pennington, 32, has galvanized belief for a young team still learning to win, staying after practice to work with receivers such as Ginn.

After Miami's fifth win, Pennington set the focus for more.

"That doesn't mean we will rest on our laurels," he said. "We have a lot to work on. The difference between 4-4 and 2-6 is maybe a touchdown."

Out of the garage? Tampa Bay Buccaneers running back Carnell "Cadillac" Williams is expected to be activated off the physically unable to perform list today, 14 months after tearing the patella tendon in his right leg against the Carolina Panthers.

Williams' grueling rehabilitation has been a source of inspiration to teammates and coach Jon Gruden, who said during the offseason that he watched Williams run steps reminiscent of Sylvester Stallone in Rocky.

The 2005 offensive rookie of the year returned to practice Monday.

The banged-up Bucs backfield could use a boost Sunday against the Minnesota Vikings with Earnest Graham (knee) and Warrick Dunn (back) nicked up.

"It's almost like Christmas is here," Gruden says. "Christmas is (today), I hope.

"We'll see how he feels and talk to the trainers, the medical people. If my eyes don't deceive me, I think he's got a chance to be a player this year for our team."

title: FINISHING KICK SOARING EAGLES CAPTURE 6TH CONSECUTIVE VICTORY ON AKERS' WINNING FG WITH NO TIME LEFT
content:
Some of his teammates couldn't watch David Akers when he lined up for the winning kick, preferring to let the crowd's reaction tell them the outcome.

Given recent events around the NFL, nobody could blame them.

The fans' roar gave them the news -- snap, hold and kick were perfect this time.

Akers hit a 38-yard field goal with no time remaining to give the Philadelphia Eagles a 23-20 victory against the New York Giants in a wild-card playoff game yesterday.
