A day after Dallas lost to Seattle when quarterback Tony Romofumbled the hold on what could have been a go-ahead field goal, snapper Jon Dorenbos and holder Koy Detmer executed the seemingly routine play that has cost teams important games in recent weeks.

"Koy said, 'Those posts look big, don't they?' " Akers said. "I said, 'Don't worry. We're going to be celebrating in a minute.' "

Brian Westbrook, playing through stomach cramps that often sent him to the sideline, had 141 yards rushing, including a spectacular, slashing 49-yard touchdown run in the second quarter. His 65-yard punt return for an apparent score in the third quarter was nullified by a penalty on rookie Torrance Daniels.

Jeff Garcia threw for 153 yards, one touchdown and played efficiently enough to win for the sixth time in seven starts since replacing an injured Donovan McNabb.

Eli Manningthrew two touchdown passes to Plaxico Burress, including an 11-yard strike that made the score 20-20 with 5:04 left.

In his last game, Tiki Barber ran for 137 yards for the Giants. A three-time Pro Bowl running back, Barber plans to retire after 10 seasons in the NFL.

"This was one of the hardest places I've ever played in," Barber said. "It was fitting to come down here and play my last game."

The Eagles are far from finished, though.

Counted out after a knee injury ended McNabb's season in Week 11, the Eagles (11-6) have won six consecutive games. They'll play at New Orleans (10-6) Saturday night.

The Giants overcame a second-and-30 on their tying scoring drive as Manning completed consecutive passes of 18, 14 and 11 yards to Burress.

But the Eagles drove right down the field, mixing in short passes and Westbrook runs.

That set up Akers' winning kick, a moment with a little extra drama in the wake of the botched hold in the Dallas-Seattle game and a bad snap on a critical extra point in a late-season game between Cincinnati and Denver.

"Kicking balls are very, very slick," Akers said. "That might be something that needs to be addressed. A lot of people take the snap, hold and kick for granted. It just shows you how technical that is."

Detmer, the longtime backup quarterback signed this week specifically to be the holder, had no trouble putting the snap down for Akers. Dorenbos replaced injured long-snapper Mike Bartrum last month.

Led by Garcia and a rejuvenated defense, Philadelphia turned its season around after a 24-point loss in Indianapolis, captured its fifth division title in six years and earned the No. 3 seed in the conference.

Meanwhile, the dysfunctional Giants (8-9) fell apart after a 6-2 start. They lost four in a row and six of seven before a win at Washington last week put them in the playoffs.

While the Eagles moved within one victory of their fifth trip to the NFC championship game in six years, the Giants face an uncertain offseason. Besides Barber, coach Tom Coughlin might not be back, and the embattled Manning probably will hear more criticism after failing to win a playoff game for the second consecutive year.

Manning was 16 of 27 for 161 yards, two touchdowns and one interception. Garcia was 17 of 31.

title: Chargers' Chambers gets to know Rivers
content:
SAN DIEGO - Chris Chambers spent time getting to know Philip Rivers on Wednesday during his first practice with the San Diego Chargers.

That's certainly nothing new for Chambers, the wide receiver who was obtained Tuesday in a trade with the winless Miami Dolphins.

"I have a lot of experience with quarterbacks coming in and out," Chambers said. "I've had maybe 10 or 11 quarterbacks the last six, seven years, so I know how to talk to the guys. I know how to get comfortable with them at a fast pace because I've been having to do that for a long time, so I know what I have to do."

Except for getting a feel for the way Rivers throws the ball, Chambers is already pretty well versed with what's going on. Save for a nuance here or there, the Chargers use many of the plays, routes and formations the Dolphins use.

"I could pretty much just step right into the huddle and play a game," said Chambers, who also spent plenty of time with Hall of Famer James Lofton, the Chargers' wide receivers coach. "I'm game-ready."

He won't have to do that quite yet. The Chargers (3-3) have their bye Sunday, so Chambers will make his San Diego debut the following weekend at home against the Houston Texans.

Chambers played for four head coaches and six offensive coordinators during his six-plus seasons in Miami, and had 10 quarterbacks start with him.

Chambers hopes that instability -- along with all the losing -- is behind him.

"I felt like I haven't really been able to maximize my ability," he said. "I'm in the prime of my career right now, so it's good to be able to get with a team and hopefully stick around and build some continuity and see how good I really can be."

Chambers, a Pro Bowler in 2005, joins a team that had 11 Pro Bowlers and five All-Pros last year. His contract runs through 2009.

"These guys want to win. It feels good to be out here around a winning tradition right now," he said.

While the Dolphins are struggling under first-year coach Cam Cameron -- who was San Diego's offensive coordinator the last five seasons -- the Chargers have won two straight to right their early season wrongs and pull into a tie atop the AFC West with Kansas City.

"I hated to see him go," Miami defensive end Jason Taylor said. "But he won three games overnight by getting traded, and he goes to a team that has a chance."

One of those six offensive coordinators Chambers had at Miami, Norv Turner, is San Diego's head coach.

"He's a big-play receiver, an experienced receiver," Turner said. "This was a good move for us. I don't think it will take he and Philip long to get on the same page."

Chambers has 31 receptions for 415 yards and no touchdowns this season.

He'll be another target for Rivers, along with All-Pro tight end Antonio Gates, who's caught 43 of Rivers' 116 completions, for 547 yards and three touchdowns. Reigning league MVP LaDainian Tomlinson has 25 catches.

"I think once we get out to the field we'll figure that out," Chambers said. "It's going to feel weird the first game, I know that."

Gates thinks there will be plenty of balls to go around.

"I really think that it's a blessing to have a guy that's a proven guy in this league," Gates said. "I had a chance to play with him in the Pro Bowl one year. He's a spectacular playmaker. That makes it more interesting."

One other Dolphins-related number that Chambers would like to forget is this: He's gone 14 games without a touchdown catch.

"Last year was last year," he said. "This year, we weren't scoring as much, we didn't score as much in the red zone. We weren't really going down field, except pretty much the last game. Some stuff's out of my control, but it can be in my control if I can go out there and break tackles and try to score from 40, 50 yards out.

"I'm usually a guy that gets into the end zone very often, so I'm looking forward to picking that up."

Chambers ranks fifth in Dolphins history with 405 receptions, good for 5,688 yards and 43 touchdowns.

title: PACKERS DON'T TALK PLAYOFFS; THEY REMAIN FOCUSED ON MINNESOTA.
content:
There will be a lot of cliche buzzwords thrown around Green Bay Packers headquarters this week. It started Monday, 24 hours after a 26-13 victory over Detroit kept hope alive for at least one more week.

The one-game-at-a-time routine was especially popular Monday afternoon, as was the not-worrying-about-what-we-can't-control mantra. Sunday's upcoming game at Minnesota was, of course, referred to as a must-win.

But you won't hear much about the Packers' playoff chances. That's for sure.

''Playoffs -- it's not even part of my vocabulary at this present point,'' said Packers coach Mike Sherman, whose team has won three of its last four games to reach 7-7 and stay mathematically eligible for the sixth and final NFC playoff spot. ''We talked about this (Sunday) in the locker room -- I think part of our success recently is that we just focus on one thing. And right now that happens to be Minnesota.

''We have to let all the teams we play focus on the playoffs and where they stand and how they're going to get this and how they're going to get that. We're just going to keep playing each game individually and if they call us up at the end of the season and say, 'Hey, you're in the playoffs, you better get over here,' then we'll go. If they don't, then we'll go home. But I think our guys will keep battling and try to win the next game.''

The chances of that playoff call remain slim. And the odds will be reduced to zero if the Packers can't beat the Vikings Sunday at the Metrodome, where Green Bay has won just once in the last eight years.

But to have any chance at all, the Packers must win their last two games and get some help from Detroit, St. Louis, Tampa Bay and New Orleans. And the first part of that equation is what matters most.

''It'd be disappointing to know 9-7 could have gotten us in and we didn't take advantage of it,'' safety LeRoy Butler said.

It's just as disappointing for the Packers that they let three very winnable games slip away -- vs. Chicago Oct. 1, at Miami Oct. 29 and at Carolina Nov. 27 -- to put them in such a do-or-die situation. But that's all in the past.

''Those are three games that really stick in my craw,'' Sherman admitted. ''I was discouraged about that (Carolina) game, not about our playoffs as much as how we played. That was my disappointment.

''But I can't overstate enough the feeling I have for this football team -- how they hung together through the course of the season to get to where we are right now. We haven't done anything yet, we've been battling back all year long it seems. The fact that they've hung together . . . I think that's a great testimony to these guys.

''Obviously as we're nearing the end of the season, we'll do whatever we have to do. If we have to change something, we'll change it, personnel, scheme, how we look at things. We're going to look at ourselves very hard this week and check our tendencies and try to put ourselves in the best position to try to win this football game.''

Just don't ask about the playoff picture.

''I don't think anyone really cares about the playoffs right now. We shouldn't,'' quarterback Brett Favre said. ''All we care about is Minnesota.''

title: JETS PREPARE FOR HOME AWAY FROM HOME
content:
The Black Hole is beginning to feel like home for the New York Jets.

And well it should. They are headed to the Oakland Coliseum, with its outrageous fans and AFC powerhouse team, the Raiders, for the fourth time in 20 games.

That was made certain yesterday when the Steelers rallied to beat the Cleveland Browns in a wild-card game. The Jets, who routed Indianapolis, 41-0, Saturday and are one of the NFL's hottest teams, head west for a matchup Sunday with another very hot club.

"It's unbelievable to think we have to go play them again next year out there, too, it is laughable," Coach Herman Edwards said yesterday. That will make five consecutive regular-season trips to Oakland for the Jets.

"When we pull up to that place, the people know all the [Jets ] players' names. It's the fourth time in a year, that is amazing.

"The guy at the coffee place, Starbucks, he'll have my table ready when I go over there on Saturday. They know we are coming."

Not that Edwards thinks his team will be intimidated by the venue. The Jets lost, 26-20, there in a Monday night game Dec. 2. They also lost in the first-round of last season's playoffs at Oakland, 38-24, one week after edging the Raiders, 24-22, in the 2001 season finale to secure a playoff berth.

New York (10-7) has played relatively well at Oakland, and with what the Jets have gone through in rallying from 1-4 and 2-5 this season, it's unlikely they will be overwhelmed by any challenge.

"The thing I like about this team is the tougher the situation, it is like, 'OK,' " Edwards said. "It puts us in that kind of mind-set and that is a good mind-set."

Edwards believes the Jets are a better team, particularly on offense, than they were at the beginning of December. They have outscored their past three opponents, 113-34, when a loss would have eliminated them from the Super Bowl chase.

Next up, however, are the Raiders, who own the AFC's best record, have won seven of eight and, after two midseason losses at home, took their final four games at the Coliseum.

"You knew that where we started out, with a wild-card game, you are going to play good teams," Edwards said. "We would have to play two more. Now, it happens to be the Raiders, they are in our other division that we play in. They know us and we know them and it makes for some drama."

The Jets took all the drama out of the game Saturday with a precision that has highlighted their offense behind quarterback Chad Pennington. And their defense presented some confusing looks to Peyton Manning, who never got the Colts going.

Oakland presents just as big a challenge for a Jets defense that, after a weak start, also is on a roll.

"Right now, this team is playing better ball on every side of the ball than any team I've been a part of," running back Curtis Martin said.

"We're playing together," added Pro Bowl defensive end John Abraham. "We didn't have experience playing together at the beginning of the year, and now we do. We understand what everybody is trying to do and where everybody needs to be."

They need to be at the Black Hole Sunday, where they are 1-7-1 overall -- the Jets lost three games in Youell Field before the Coliseum was built.

The welcoming party will remind the Jets this is not a home away from home.

"We played three times out there and won once," Edwards said. "Our team feels we will be OK when we go out there. The crowd won't affect us that much."

title: TITANS' DEFENSE FEELING PRESSURE TO STOP THE RUN
content:
The Green Bay Packers know what the Tennessee Titans are going through.

Neither team has a defense that can stop the run.

That makes for an interesting game Monday night.

The Titans could come up with a string of excuses, starting with a rash of injuries. In the end, it doesn't matter why they can't stop the run, only that they can't.

And that isn't easy for the Titans to take.

They have prided themselves on having one of the NFL's best run defenses during coach Jeff Fisher's 10 seasons. They led the league in 2003, allowing 80.9 yards rushing per game (3.8 yards per carry).

Now, the Titans (1-3) are last among 32 teams with 5.5 yards allowed per carry. They are giving up 131.3 yards per game after facing Edgerrin James, Fred Taylor and LaDainian Tomlinson.

Opponents are averaging 4.9 yards per carry against the Packers (1-3), who are allowing 131.2 rushing yards per game.

"If we don't improve our run defense, there is not a back in the National Football League that won't make us look bad," Fisher said.

Stopping the run isn't the Titans' only problem on defense, either. They have given up 84 points during their three-game slide, unable to stop anyone inside the Tennessee 20, allowing nine touchdowns on 11 trips for a league-worst rate of 81.8 percent.

"First and foremost, we have to get back to the things we do well: attacking blockers, getting in our gap control and everyone doing their job. If we just do that and everyone concentrate on what they have to do individually, I think collectively, we'll do well," veteran tackle Kevin Carter said.

Vikings run down

At the start of training camp, the Minnesota Vikings' backfield was touted as the deepest in years. Now's the time to prove it.

Former University of Wisconsin athlete Michael Bennett is injured and could miss the next four games. Onterrio Smith is suspended and will miss the next four. Veteran Moe Williams is iffy with a calf problem.

That leaves rookie Mewelde Moore and first-year man Larry Ned to lead the backfield Sunday when the Vikings play at Houston.

Earlier this year, other teams reportedly showed interest in trading for one of Mike Tice's running backs, but the coach wasn't willing to part with any of them. He's feeling good about that decision now.

"We were not willing to trade any running backs for obvious reasons," Tice said Wednesday. "You're only one injury away from being short."

Asked if he would look at bringing in any more, Tice flatly said, "I have plenty of backs."

Extra points The Arizona Cardinals activated defensive tackle Wendell Bryant, a former UW standout, off the suspended list on Wednesday. The move followed a four-game suspension of Bryant for violating the NFL's substance abuse policy. He was arrested on a drunken-driving charge in Madison June 27. He had been suspended since Sept.6. … Miami Dolphins receiver Chris Chambers, a former UW athlete, is questionable this week after missing Sunday's loss to the New York Jets with knee tendinitis. … Second-year pro Chris Simms will make his first pro start at quarterback for the winless Tampa Bay Buccaneers Sunday against the New Orleans Saints, replacing Brad Johnson, who has lost 10 of his last 13 starts. … Oakland Raiders running back Tyrone Wheatley expects to miss two to four weeks with a shoulder injury.

title: Seattle view on QB controversy Holmgren says to commit to a choice; Hasselbeck says to put the team first.
content:
Mike Holmgren and Matt Hasselbeck didn't get specific when asked yesterday about quarterback controversies like the one now bubbling in Philadelphia.

But it was difficult not to overlay the conference call comments of Seattle's coach and quarterback onto the volatile situation now surrounding Andy Reid, the injured Donovan McNabb and A.J. Feeley, apparently the people's choice after his surprising performance against New England.

As they prepare for Sunday's Lincoln Financial Field meeting with the Eagles - and Holmgren said the Seahawks were expecting McNabb to play, despite his ailing ankle and thumb - both men reflected on their experiences with the most common and potentially damaging of NFL coaching dilemmas.

Holmgren's views, not surprisingly, reflected those Reid, his onetime assistant, had made public earlier in the day, when the Eagles coach reemphasized that, as long as he was healthy, McNabb was No.1.

"Commit," said Holmgren. "You must commit to that player for the sake of your football team. Everyone has to know where you're going in the organization. Just say it and off we go."

Hasselbeck, meanwhile, said he never experienced the phenomenon during his first three years in Green Bay.

"I played behind Brett Favre," he said, "so there was never a controversy."

But there was in Seattle in 2001. Shortly after he'd become the Seahawks' starter, Hasselbeck got banged up and quickly lost the job to Trent Dilfer.

The way he handled it, Hasselbeck said, was to try to play hurt and keep his mouth shut.

"That's when you get the opportunity to earn the respect of your teammates, playing through pain, playing through injuries," Hasselbeck said. "Or in my case, when I didn't play well through pain and injuries, I think I earned the respect of my teammates a little when I got benched.

"Guys get benched and immediately they start pointing the finger or they start talking trash behind the starter's back. . . . I said, 'OK, if I ever do get another opportunity' - which I hadn't really planned on - 'maybe I'll take advantage of it.' I think in some way that was when I first started to gain the respect of my teammates."

Holmgren recalled how an unresolved battle between Steve Young and Joe Montana nearly kept the 1988 San Francisco 49ers from winning the Super Bowl.

He was the quarterbacks coach when head coach Bill Walsh kept dividing the duties between the two future Hall of Famers.

"Joe would play, then Steve would go. We were kind of stumbling around," Holmgren said. "Until it was said, 'OK, we're just going to do this [play Montana] and live with it and see where it goes,' we didn't reach our potential."

Holmgren learned his lesson and, when he got the head job in Green Bay, he defused a brewing controversy involving Favre and backup Mark Brunell.

"There was a lot of sentiment in the building and on our staff that Mark Brunell should be our quarterback and not Brett," he said. "Brett was a little bit erratic and wild, and it wasn't happening. So I had a huge decision to make.

"It turned out we made the right decision."

Holmgren quickly learned to "avoid those things like the plague." Still, he had at least one lapse after he moved onto Seattle.

"One year I sat Jon Kitna down and went with our young draft choice, Brock Huard, thinking I'd get to see if the young guy could play. . . . It wasn't fair to Jon Kitna," he said."

Holmgren said the reason controversies don't pop up at safety or guard is that quarterback remains the most important position on the field.

"It's like if you're the bus driver and you've got other people sitting in the bus and you change the bus driver," he said. "That changes things. You take a passenger and you put him in the back of the bus or you take him off the bus, it doesn't mean as much."

While it appeared that Reid has followed Holmgren's advice on quarterbacks, the Seattle coach said he hadn't provided his Philadelphia protege any advice on his two sons' well-publicized legal problems.

"No, I phoned and offered my ear if he ever wanted to talk about anything," Holmgren said. "And he knows I'm there for him if he ever needs me for anything. But that's very private. [Holmgren's wife] Kathy and I, we know them so well. Heck, our daughter used to babysit for the kids. Anything he ever needs from me, all he's got to do is ask."

Contact staff writer Frank Fitzpatrick at 215-854-5068 or ffitzpatrick@phillynews.com.

title: HURTING HAMILTON JIM'S MAIN WORRY
content:
Still feeling the effects of a partially torn pectoral muscle, Keith Hamilton sat out the Giants' first practice of the week yesterday. Hamilton, who missed three games because of a shoulder injury, is Jim Fassel's chief concern on the injury front.

Also resting were CB Jason Sehorn (knee), CB Will Allen (eye) C Dusty Zeigler (concussion) and WR Thabiti Davis (knee, ankle) and RB Damon Washington (hamstring).

Acting lesson: Michael Strahan, still frustrated by what he feels were holding penalties that went uncalled in Minnesota, was intrigued when told that hockey players will dive to accentuate penalties. "Is that so? I've got to call my friend (Devils forward) Scott Gomez for some advice," Strahan said.

Mixing it up: Fassel cut short the Thanksgiving Day practice by about 20 minutes and juggled the format to include work by QB Kerry Collins and Strahan on the scout teams.

"I wanted to shake some things up a bit so guys wouldn't come out here and just go through the motions," he explained.

Omen: For Giants fans looking for some good signs heading into the Raiders game, here goes: The Giants are 11-1 in games played prior to their bye week. That's the best record in the NFL.

Chance to help: Giants are playing host a blood drive in the Stadium Club between gates C and D from 9 a.m. to 2 p.m. tomorrow. Call 1-877-90-BLOOD for information.

Wives drive: The Giants' annual coat drive, co-sponsored by Allied Hoffman Moving and Storage and New York Cares, takes place Sunday prior to the game. Giants wives will be on hand at stadium entrances to accept gently worn coats for distribution to the homeless and less fortunate of New York City.

Feeling better: Raiders went through a full practice yesterday after moving it to the morning. Ex-Giant RB Tyrone Wheatley, who missed last week's game with the flu, has returned to practice along with OT Barry Simms. CB Charles Woodson (turf toe) seems to be improving.

title: A year of hardships for the Favres; Wife's breast cancer latest off-field trouble for family of Packers quarterback
content:
More than ever, Brett Favre needs football as his escape.

His wife, Deanna, is fighting breast cancer, the latest bad news for the star quarterback's family, which has dealt with two deaths in 11 months.

"I enjoy playing the game," the Packers quarterback said Wednesday. "It's a way to take my mind off of, at least for a brief moment, some of the things in my personal life."

There are many.

Favre lost his father, Irv, to a heart attack last December. Deanna's brother, Casey Tynes, 24, was killed three weeks ago in an all-terrain vehicle accident on Favre's property near his home in Mississippi. Deanna, 35, was diagnosed with cancer the following week and underwent a lumpectomy at Sloan Kettering Cancer Center in New York.

"Professional athletes are not immune from tragic circumstances and cancer," Favre said. "Athletes sometimes think we are. I've never found myself saying 'Why me?' or 'Why us?' It takes too much time and it doesn't matter anyway. Because it is what it is, and you deal with the best way possible and that's what we're trying to do."

Favre's wife found a lump during a self-exam, got it checked out right away and is expected to make a full recovery because it was detected early, he said. She needs months of chemotherapy and radiation treatments and will probably stay in Green Bay to get them.

"We're both doing a lot better, but initially I was doing a lot worse than she was," Favre said. "She's a lot tougher than I am, but she's hanging in there."

Favre, also 35, has dealt with a litany of health concerns himself this season: a dislocated shoulder, a concussion, a bruised leg and a sprained right hand a year after playing with a broken thumb on his throwing hand.

Through it all, Favre has kept playing - and sparkling.

In his last two games, Favre has completed 48 of 67 passes for 515 yards, four touchdowns and no interceptions in back-to-back routs of Detroit and Dallas. He extended his NFL quarterback record to 215 straight starts, including playoffs.

Favre said he doesn't consider himself a Superman.

"Sometimes life is difficult and at some point we all have to go through - and have gone through - some difficult situations, and I'm not the only person who's had to deal with them and rise to the occasion," Favre said. "So, I don't consider myself out of the ordinary."

What's his explanation for his superb play through such adversity?

"I have no idea. None whatsoever," he said. "I take tremendous pride in what I do, always have. And I think in times like this, maybe I even take more."

Many teammates and fans wonder if all these hardships will weigh on Favre and hasten his retirement.

Favre says no.

"Do I think about it? Sure. Especially during times like this, I think about it. But it seems like every time something happens people want to jump on the retirement bandwagon," Favre said. "And the more people ask me, the more I want to stick around just to stick it to 'em. But it will not, as I stand here before you today, it will not speed up the decision. Now a month from now, who knows?"

Coach Mike Sherman said he's not surprised Favre's performances have been so good even as he's facing such trying times.

"He is a very resilient person, but I've got to be honest with you, everything that he has gone through, his wife has gone through and I'm not so sure who's the toughest one in that family," Sherman said. "He clearly is tough. But she has been there in support of him in every situation that he's been involved in. She's been a part of that. She's the unsung hero in that relationship. She's every bit as much a hero as he is in that family."

title: Yes, Limbaugh was being racist on ESPN
content:
Dear Rush Limbaugh,

Why are you such an idiot?

Why must you say idiotic things?

Why must you ruin a normal person's Sunday football with your so-called "insights" of the football fan (which would include me, a "terrible" lefty)?West Virginia U.

Why, all of a sudden, are all sportswriters a bunch of liberals who are part of the elitist "liberal" sports media, forever promoting an agenda for blacks, gays or whoever else offends your sensibilities?

Why must you drag the most divisive, frustrating element of American society into the one most Americans enjoy?

Why did ESPN think you were such a great idea?

Better yet, ESPN, what the hell were you thinking when you hired an ego bigger than Chris Berman's head?

Rush, it seems to me that last week's controversy was all too scripted and couldn't have been a better way for you to try and make some lame point about how ALL POLITICALLY CORRECT we are.

What better way for you to make yourself a martyr at the altar of liberalism?

But the fact is, you didn't just claim that Donovan McNabb is overrated by the media, it was that he is overrated by the media because he is black. I suppose one must go to great lengths if they are to politicize every aspect of American life.

You also claim that you were just trying to discuss what is wrong with the Philadelphia Eagles as a team, from a fan's point of view.

But does a fan ever say Donovan McNabb or any player is only overrated because of their skin color?

If McNabb is overrated, it is because he really sucks and hasn't played up to potential.

YOU brought up race in your analysis of Mr. McNabb. Not the media, not liberals, you did. If you are a real fan, you do not see a player for his color if they are overrated, underrated or the greatest ever; you don't care.

You weren't just trying to damn the "liberal" media that has often spurned your ignorant commentary because you offer no journalistic integrity except telling "dittoheads" Hillary Clinton is evil. Have you noticed that no one is calling you a racist -- just an idiot?

Though I can only hope this breaks you out of your little fantasy world of you versus the "elitist liberal" media, you'll (as you already have) retreat to your little world where listeners will accept every demagogic word you enunciate.

Never mind trying to explain that you are stuck in a world that can only see the world as black and white, liberal and conservative.

Never mind you can't seem to appreciate athletics for what it really is, achievement and excellence.

And by the way, McNabb is not overrated, any Mountaineer fan can tell you that.

Go Steelers!

Yours Truly, Andy Barnes

title: Not about to leave things half done
content:
The award winners

MVP

Steve McNair, QB, Tennessee -- His statistics are impressive, but his leadership puts him over the top. He understands when he has to take over a game and proceeds to deliver. He is the heart of the Titans.

Coach

Bill Belichick, New England -- His attitude simply is that there are no excuses for losing, and his Patriots reflect that. A master strategist, he has pushed his team successfully forward through adversity that includes an inordinate number of injuries.

Offensive player

Priest Holmes, RB, Kansas City -- Coming off last year's season-ending hip injury, there were plenty of concerns. He continues to be the most complete running back in the game.

Defensive player

Ray Lewis, LB, Baltimore -- Whether 40 yards downfield or 8 yards into the opponents' backfield, he makes his presence felt. Really felt. On every play.

Comeback

Quincy Carter, QB, Dallas -- After looking totally lost last season, he came into this season as the Cowboys' biggest question mark. He's found the answers under coach Bill Parcells.

Rookie

Anquan Boldin, WR, Arizona -- Has positioned himself among the most productive receivers and demonstrates the desire to work to stay there.

On the decline

Bruce Smith, DE, Washington

He needs two sacks to break Reggie White's career record, but that's also as many as he has had this season. Smith is 40, in his 19th season, and it's showing.

Kurt Warner, QB, St. Louis

After torching the league from 1999-2001 and grabbing two MVP awards in the process, Warner began to fail last season and now is a clear No. 2 behind Marc Bulger.

Rich Gannon, QB, Oakland

The reigning MVP who threw 26 touchdown passes last season, Gannon has just six scoring tosses and has watched the Raiders go from AFC champs to 2-6.

Emmitt Smith, RB, Arizona

He broke his shoulder a month ago during a humbling effort against his former team, the Cowboys. Even before then, he clearly was not the same player who is the league's all-time leading rusher.

Kordell Stewart, QB, Chicago

Given the chance to revitalize his career after being banished from Pittsburgh, Stewart instead has struggled mightily and now finds himself on the bench in place of Chris Chandler.

On the rise

Marc Bulger, QB, St. Louis

Having planted the seed of worth last season, Bulger is the third-rated quarterback in the NFC and has thrown for 2,086 yards.

Santana Moss, WR, New York Jets

Slowed by injury early in his career, Moss is averaging 15.3 yards a catch and has seven TDs.

Marcel Shipp, RB, Arizona

Since Emmitt Smith went down with an injury in Week 5, Shipp has blossomed big time. In the last two games, he has rushed for 306 yards.

Peter Warrick, WR, Cincinnati

In his fourth season after a ballyhooed career at Florida State, Warrick, with 44 catches, has benefited greatly from improved quarterback Jon Kitna and the philosophy of Marvin Lewis.

Roy Williams, S, Dallas

In only his second season, he's become one of the brightest young stars in the league. He's a physical force in the Cowboys secondary.

Midseason all-pro team

Offense

QB -- Steve McNair, Tennessee

RB -- Priest Holmes, Kansas City

FB -- Tony Richardson, Kansas City

LT -- Jonathan Ogden, Baltimore

LG -- Steve Hutchinson, Seattle

C -- Matt Birk, Minnesota

RG -- Will Shields, Kansas City

RT -- Kyle Turley, St. Louis

TE -- Tony Gonzalez, Kansas City

WR -- Marvin Harrison, Indianapolis

WR -- Randy Moss, Minnesota

WR -- Torry Holt, St. Louis

Defense

LE -- Michael Strahan, N.Y. Giants

T -- Richard Seymour, New England

T -- Warren Sapp, Tampa Bay

RE -- Mike Rucker, Carolina

OLB -- Derrick Brooks, Tampa Bay

MLB -- Ray Lewis, Baltimore

OLB -- Keith Bulluck, Tennessee

CB -- Patrick Surtain, Miami

CB -- Antoine Winfield, Buffalo

SS -- Roy Williams, Dallas

FS -- Darren Sharper, Green Bay

Special teams

K -- Mike Vanderjagt, Indianapolis

P -- Shane Lechler, Oakland

KR -- Dante Hall, Kansas City

PR -- Hall, Kansas City

Coverage -- Larry Izzo, New England

The best and the worst

Biggest disappointment

No matter what team you root for, didn't you feel a little empty only getting to see Michael Vick on the sideline?

Biggest spark

Kansas City return specialist Dante Hall created excitement every time he touched the ball.

Best performance

Jamal Lewis of Baltimore romping for 295 yards against Cleveland. The league's leading rusher (1,045 yards) even told the Browns he was going to have a career day.

Best move

Carolina signing Stephen Davis. His 992 yards rushing (5.1 a carry) fit this system perfectly.

Runner-up: Kansas City signing Vonnie Holliday. The defensive end has proven to be invaluable.

Worst move

Jacksonville signing Hugh Douglas. A Pro Bowl player in Philadelphia, the defensive end has 12 tackles and one sack.

Runner-up: Arizona signing Emmitt Smith. The big picture remains rather puzzling.

Worst motivational tool

Jacksonville punter Chris Hanson's season ended after he hacked his right (non-kicking) leg with an ax. The locker room ax and a log symbolized coach Jack Del Rio's call to "keep chopping wood" (work hard).

Best division

AFC South. Indianapolis and Tennessee are quality teams. Houston is steadily improving as Jacksonville rebuilds.

Worst division

AFC North. Baltimore? Cincinnati? Somebody has to win.

Best game

Indianapolis 38, Tampa Bay 35 (Oct. 6). The Colts erased a 21-point deficit with four minutes to go, won in OT. Unforgettable.

Worst game

St. Louis 36, Atlanta 0 (Oct. 13): So bad that the Falcons owner apologized to ABC for the clunker. Unforgivable.

USA TODAY's midseason top 10

Team, record

Observation

1. Kansas City, 8-0

Ram-iniscent of Dick Vermeil's last three-year plan.

2. Indianapolis, 7-1

Once dingy collection, now Dungy reflection.

3. New England, 7-2

Bill Belichick found his "lost" team.

4. Tennessee, 6-2

Play three of last four at home, where Titans are 3-0.

5. Seattle, 6-2

Road worriers face Baltimore, Minny, St. Loo, S.F. away.

6. Minnesota, 6-2

Mr. O'Leary's cow needs to light fire under defense.

7. Carolina, 6-2

Play Atlanta, Arizona and Detroit to begin December.

8. Tampa Bay, 4-4

Dangerous every other week.

9. Miami, 5-3

Getting this much talent to underachieve can't be easy.

10. Dallas, 6-2

Tuna surprise or Tuna meltdown? Three of last four on the road.

BALTIMORE - The New England Patriots are still perfect, but looking mighty vulnerable.

New England pulled off its greatest escape thus far to become the sixth team in NFL history to start a season with 12 victories. For the second straight week, it was a struggle against a losing team, but the incredibly resourceful Patriots got Tom Brady's8-yard touchdown pass to Jabar Gaffney with 44 seconds left to beat the Baltimore Ravens 27-24 Monday night.

The winning drive in the final minutes covered 73 yards and required two fourth-down conversions, one on a defensive holding penalty 6 seconds before Gaffney beat Dawan Landry in the left corner of the end zone.

Driven to distraction by a Baltimore defense at times reminiscent of the 2000 unit that led the Ravens (4-8) to a Super Bowl victory, Brady came through late - as he always seems to.

Eight days after edging Philadelphia 31-28, the Patriots barely kept alive their pursuit of the only unblemished record in NFL history, Miami's 17-0 in 1972.

With Pittsburgh (9-3) up next, the Patriots must recapture some of their record-setting ways from earlier in the season to keep their flawless record intact.

As the biggest road favorite in a non-replacement game (19 points), the Patriots nearly laid a dud in swirling winds and occasional snow flurries. They trailed for much of the game, but Brady relentlessly guided them downfield for the winning score and his 41st touchdown pass of the season. He's eight short of Peyton Manning's record with four games left.

And New England is four wins shy of the only 16-0 regular season ever. Remaining after the Steelers are the weak Dolphins and Jets, and a Saturday night visit to the New York Giants.

That the Ravens, losers of five straight, would test the Patriots so severely was stunning. Indeed, if not for untimely penalties and a fumble of an interception return by Ed Reed, the Ravens might have pulled off one of the biggest upsets in NFL history.

Baltimore got a brilliant effort from Willis McGahee, who rushed for 138 yards and a touchdown. Kyle Boller threw for two scores, and the Ravens managed three sacks.

But they couldn't manage to hang on, although a 52-yard completion to Mark Clayton got them to the New England 3 as time ran out.

The Ravens went in front 17-10 as McGahee starred with six carries for 48 yards on a third-quarter drive. He carried two defenders into the end zone on his 17-yard TD that lifted him over 1,000 yards rushing in his first season with Baltimore.

His score also woke up the dormant Patriots offense. Brady found a rhythm at last, as did the running attack, on a 72-yard drive capped by Randy Moss' 3-yard touchdown reception.

Brady joined Manning, Dan Marino and Kurt Warner as quarterbacks who threw for at least 40 touchdowns in a season, and Moss' 17th TD tied a career high for a season.

Yet back came the stunningly resilient Ravens, who rank 26th in total offense. McGahee contributed 40 yards of a 56-yard march to Daniel Wilcox's 1-yard TD reception, making it 24-17 with 35 seconds gone in the fourth quarter.

Could this be the night for the biggest upset of the NFL season and one of the biggest in league history? It sure looked that way after Brady was sacked on consecutive plays, then rookie Yamon Figurs returned a punt 33 yards to the New England 26.

But the Pats quickly bounced back. James Sanders intercepted Boller's pass into the wind and returned it 42 yards. Maroney, who earlier scooted 36 yards with a short pass, this time went 43 with one. That set up Stephen Gostowski's 38-yard field goal.

Baltimore forced a punt after its own three-and-out, but eventually punted back to New England, setting up the winning series.

The Ravens had more moments of brilliance Monday than the Pats, including their longest play of the season. It came on a 53-yard completion by Boller to Devard Darling. That first-quarter surprise, created by Boller escaping strong pressure, led to Boller's 4-yard TD pass to Derrick Mason for a 7-3 lead. Former Ravens linebacker Adalius Thomas just missed knocking down the pass.

Moments later, Brady was sacked on a blitz by Corey Ivy and the Patriots, who had outscored opponents 107-24 in first quarters this season, trailed by four after one period.

They fell behind 10-3 on Matt Stover's 29-yard field goal. That capped a precise 55-yard drive on which the Ravens' suddenly efficient offense converted a fourth-and-6 when the swirling winds precluded a field goal try from the New England 27.

Were the Patriots getting nervous in such an unfamiliar position? Uh, not exactly.

Brady went to the no-huddle offense on a 60-yard drive helped by two defensive penalties inside the 15. Heath Evans surged in from the 1.

The Pats had a chance to break the 10-10 tie late in the half, but Brady's long pass to Wes Welker was tipped by the receiver to Reed. He took off on a 34-yard runback, but fumbled when hit by Patrick Pass and New England recovered - only to sheepishly run out the clock.

title: Bucs' Moore a very confident man
content:
Dave Moore has been a quiet, competitive constant in Bucs huddles since Tony Dungy became coach in 1996. A solid but uncelebrated tight end who, like the eye of a hurricane, is a tough calm around which Tampa Bay football storms complex and controversial have churned.

  Ol' 83, he heard hundreds of quarterback calls from Trent Dilfer. Then Shaun King took over. Moore has experienced tackle-to-tackle personnel evolution in Tampa Bay's offensive line.MIZELL

On this island, Dave survives.

  He ran plays called by Mike Shula, until that Bucs offensive coordinator was replaced in February by Les Steckel. Dave adjusted to a new system. His eyes brightened this season upon finding vocal, gifted wide receiver Keyshawn Johnson among Moore's oft-criticized gang of 11.

  "We're still learning as we go," Moore said. "So much is still new. You can feel confidence increasing as this year's group gets more familiar with each other. We go cold for a while, then get hot. Now we really can get hot. I'm convinced that almost any highs are possible."

Moore eschews demeaning former offensive chief Shula. "Mike prepared us well, as does Coach Steckel," said the 258-pound former University of Pittsburgh fullback/tight end. "But as a game goes on, Les is more apt to surprise us with a play call.

"At times, when the call comes in and Shaun relays it to us, a little smile will come to my face, thinking, "Wow, we're really going after it.' No question it can be invigorating.

  "Against the Minnesota Vikings, there was a halfback pass called by Les. It was intended for (tight end) Todd Yoder. It went incomplete, and Coach Steckel took some grief for the call. I was 100 percent for what he did.

  "If that play clicks, which it should've, the whole tempo of the game can change. It can be a huge momentum swing. A lot of offensive coordinators aren't willing to go out on a limb like that. Steckel goes for what he believes in."

  Still painfully fresh on Bucs minds was Sunday's overtime loss against the Packers, costing Tampa Bay the NFC Central championship and a first-round playoff bye. Instead, the Bucs got a trip to Philadelphia for a wild-card chance against the Eagles.

  "We sputtered through the first half at Green Bay, with some errant passes, some balls being dropped, including by me," Moore said.

  "But with us trailing 14-3, Shaun said in the huddle, "This is ridiculous, guys. We've got to make some big plays.' He's so calm, no matter the situation, but there is also real competitive fire in our quarterback. When he gets into a good rhythm, Shaun can throw a football as well as almost anybody around.

  "We went into a hurry-up offense, which seemed to surprise the Packers. It clicked, and we pulled to 14-all before losing (17-14). What's clear is that our offensive limitations are now strictly dictated by how well we perform.

  "With a new OL, plus Keyshawn, the sky is now the limit. It's up to us, how we do our jobs. In the past we would pound it on the ground, and if everything went really well, we might score up to 24 points. But now we can score plenty of points. We have the resources. It's up to 11 guys functioning on the field."

  For four-plus seasons Moore enjoyed seeing his Pinellas County fishing buddy, Mike Alstott, doing most of the Bucs ball-carrying. But now, after injuries and fumbling problems affected the 260-pound Pro Bowl fullback, the totes are predominantly in the small but sure hands of Warrick Dunn, a scooter who weighs 180.

  Moore's faith in Alstott has not been dampened. "So many things are a matter of circumstance and opportunity in pro football," he said. "I was playing behind a good tight end, Jackie Harris. He had some injuries. I got my shot. That's why I'm around with the Bucs to play against the Eagles on Sunday.

  "Alstott is still a good, tough runner. He will contribute where and when Mike is asked. But no, I can't see us going away from Warrick getting most of the carries, considering how well Dunn has played the last month or so. But yes, Alstott will be back and doing well when the full chances again come his way."

  Moore grew up in Succasunna, N.J., about 40 miles west of the Meadowlands, where the Giants and Jets play, and about 85 milesfrom Philadelphia. He said the Moore clan was quite splintered as to NFL allegiances, but none of them rooted for the Eagles.

  "I was a Pittsburgh Steelers fan," Dave said. "They were so good in the '70s. My brother and father pulled for the New York Jets. Most of the rest of our family were for the Giants."

  But now, they are Bucs nuts all. "This one is pretty near my Jersey home country," Moore said. "I'll have 20-plus in the stands at Veterans Stadium, among all those Eagles fans.

  "I'm familiar with the Vet. While with Pitt, I played against Temple regularly in Philly. Of course, there were only 10,000 in the stands, a little quieter group than we will encounter Sunday. That field is a turf monster. Hard and ugly.

  "I expect us to handle it."

title: A healthy diagnosis for some ailing players
content:
Two weeks into the NFL season, the easy, feel-good fantasy stories to write about involve Jamal Lewis' record-setting day, Priest Holmes quieting all doubters with his sensational start or Clinton Portis' ability to shred any defense he faces.  

But what fun is that?  

Now is the time to diagnose our sickly, hemorrhaging patients to help you - their owners - figure out if there's any hope of reviving them.  

The list of early disappointments is enough to fill a hospital emergency room, so let's get started. I've assigned each patient a condition (critical, serious, fair) to help get us started.  

- Oakland Raiders offense: CRITICAL. Here's where we must spend the most time, for the wounds run deep.  

Rich Gannon and Co. have smacked into a large, brick wall and some may wonder if it has similarities to the Great Wall of China - old and immovable. It's probably too early to tell, but Jerry Rice was incensed after the Raiders barely beat Cincinnati last week.  

Gannon threw for all of 103 yards - a total he sometimes eclipsed in the first quarter last season.  

So what's going on?  

Tim Brown suggested too many new wrinkles were added during training camp. The All-Pro receiver felt the Raiders should have adopted the 'if-it-ain't-broke-don't-fix-it' adage. Instead, the timing is off and the entire offense is out of sync.  

Coach Bill Callahan defended the decision to revamp the offense.  

"There's a fine line," said Callahan, whose team has converted a league-worst 3 of 22 third downs. "It goes from being creative to doing too much. You can see the problem. You'd like to be more creative, but you still have to have your execution in place. When you don't, you can be the most creative and innovative coach in the league, but if you can't pick up first downs, you're going to come under a lot of scrutiny and criticism. And rightfully so."  

Another huge problem? Jerry Porter, perhaps the team's best receiver, is out 5-7 more weeks, although he said Wednesday that timetable should be shorter.  

"You guys miss me," he told Brown during the Bengals game.  

He's right.  

Diagnosis: Don't be afraid to sit the whole lot of these guys until they get things figured out. It's been ugly from preseason up through Week 2. Don't let their train wreck demolish your season.  

- Marty Booker: CRITICAL. Booker owners would be well advised to sit him until further notice. Kordell Stewart has no line to protect him, and he hasn't been able to establish a rapport with Booker - or anyone else, for that matter.  

Diagnosis: Start looking elsewhere.  

- Tony Gonzalez: SERIOUS. Four receptions? For 26 yards? What's going on here? For one thing, Gonzalez is playing with an injured knee and ankle. The other problem is Kansas City has too many options, starting first and foremost with Priest Holmes.  

Diagnosis: Keep him in there for now, and cross your fingers this is just a bad start.  

- Fred Taylor: SERIOUS. The Jaguars' offense is in disarray, mainly because WR Jimmy Smith was suspended for four games. But that's not the only problem. Mark Brunell has been hit too much over his career, and Jacksonville will struggle all season offensively.  

Diagnosis: Things should improve by Week 5 when Smith returns, but by how much is a mystery. Cross your fingers until then - and even after.  

- David Boston: SERIOUS. Fantasy magazines and Web sites figured Boston would bounce back with a huge season in San Diego. I just didn't see it. He's playing with an average quarterback with a coach that has a run-first philosophy in Marty Schottenheimer.  

Diagnosis: His heel injury aside, the superstar numbers many fantasy owners were hoping for don't seem to be forthcoming.  

- Marshall Faulk: SERIOUS. What has happened to the once sure- fire fantasy MVP? First, coach Mike Martz forgot about him in Week 1, then he gave Faulk the ball 18 times in Week 2. Faulk managed just 57 yards.  

Diagnosis: Pray Martz keeps giving him the ball. He'll come around eventually.  

- William Green: SERIOUS. Green's numbers -38 carries for 140 yards - aren't terrible. But he has no touchdowns as Cleveland's offense continues to struggle.  

Diagnosis: Watch for gradual improvement, but not the big season many of us were expecting.  

- LaDainian Tomlinson: FAIR. He's too good to stay down for too long.  

- Donovan McNabb: FAIR. The Eagles can't be this bad. Can they?  

- Peyton Manning: FAIR. Manning has struggled, throwing just 1 TD pass thus far. But the return of the old Edgerrin James is just what Manning owners need. It opens up the play-action pass, which Manning does better than anyone. Watch for some big weeks ahead.  

- Peerless Price: FAIR. What a horrendous start by this marquee wide receiver. He's bordering on moving to the 'SERIOUS' wing, but once Vick returns, Price should make fantasy owners happy again.  

Good bets  

- Vikings RB Moe Williams vs. Detroit. Until rookie Onterrio Smith starts getting too many touches, there's no reason to take Williams out of lineups.  

- Bucs QB Brad Johnson vs. Atlanta. Tampa Bay's mad and will take its frustrations out on a Falcons squad that allowed Washington QB Patrick Ramsey to rack up 356 yards last week.  

- Patriots QB Tom Brady vs. New York Jets. Brady bounced back nicely from his 4-interception nightmare in the opener. He'll rip the Jets for 2-4 TDs.  

- Colts WR Reggie Wayne vs. Jacksonville. We'll call this one a sleeper, considering Wayne has just 5 catches for 61 yards. But the Jaguars' defense is wretched, and Wayne might be a decent play as your third WR.  

- Steelers RB Amos Zereoue vs. Cincinnati. Much in the same vein as Wayne, this is another sleeper pick. Zereoue hasn't shown much, but the Bengals' defense has been beaten is on the ground.  

- Redskins QB Patrick Ramsey vs. New York Giants. Watch for Ramsey to exploit a Giants defense that's still reeling from a devastating loss on Monday Night Football.  

- Packers QB Brett Favre, WR Javon Walker and TE Bubba Franks vs. Arizona. Start all your Packers! Give Walker one more shot as your third WR, then sit him if he flops against the weakest team in the NFL.  

- Ravens RB Jamal Lewis vs. San Diego. For those of you who slept through last Sunday's 295-yard performance. Also, the last three runners - O.J. Simpson, Walter Payton and Corey Dillon - to break the rushing record all had big games the next week. Dillon and Payton both ran for 137 yards in their next games, while Simpson reeled off 203 more.  

- Niners RBs Garrison Hearst and Kevan Barlow vs. Cleveland. It's extremely rare that I'd suggest playing two running backs from the same team. But against the seemingly defenseless Browns, I'll go out on a limb. Hearst (22 carries, 145 yards, 2 TDs) and Barlow (26 carries, 107 yards, TD) could both have big days.  

Bad bets  

- Lions RB Olandis Gary vs. Minnesota. Gary (9 carries, 8 yards last week) looked tentative and unsure of himself against Green Bay. In a word, he's overrated.  

- Falcons RBs T.J. Duckett and Warrick Dunn vs. Tampa Bay. After Stephen Davis ran all over the Bucs to the tune of 142 yards, watch for a fired-up Tampa defense to redeem itself.  

- Titans RB Eddie George vs. New Orleans. It's looking more and more like there's too much mileage on George's legs. His two-game numbers: 35 carries, 88 yards. Who'da thunk fantasy owners would be muttering George's name in the same breath as Anthony Thomas'?  

- Jacksonville's Fred Taylor vs. Indianapolis. Say this for Taylor - he's been consistent. Two games, two 71-yard rushing days. Don't look for much more than that Sunday.  

- Steelers' QB Tommy Maddox vs. Cincinnati. Amazingly, the Bengals have done a bang-up job shutting down opponents' passing games. First, Jake Plummer threw 3 interceptions. Then, Rich Gannon threw for just 103 yards and no TDs. A trend? Maddox owners beware.  

- Rams QB Marc Bulger vs. Seattle. This is a risky pick, but the Seahawks are proving to have one of the best defenses in the NFL, having allowed a league-best 10 points. Granted, one of those foes was Arizona, but Ray Rhodes' unit shut down Aaron Brooks and New Orleans in the opener.  

- Browns RB William Green vs. San Francisco. A sputtering Cleveland offense means less red-zone opportunities for everyone.  

- Dolphins RB Ricky Williams vs. Buffalo. Risky pick? You bet. But the Bills' much-improved defense has shown their teeth, and if their high-octane offense kicks it into gear early, Miami may be forced to go to the air.  

- Raiders QB Rich Gannon and WR Tim Brown vs. Denver. Gannon owners (like myself) might want to start taking a look at their second and third options. For what it's worth, I'm going with Drew Bledsoe this week.  

You may e-mail John Dietz at jdietz@@dailyherald.com.

title: NEAR TIME TO JET-TISON ERIC. IT DOESN'T TAKE A MANGENIUS TO SEE HIS SHORTCOMINGS
content:
All season long, Eric Mangini has flatly drummed home his message about consistency and closing. Those are two of his big words, and they soon may be used to condemn the coach whose job is very much hanging in the balance on Sunday.

Mangini will be coaching for his own future at Giants Stadium, because he did a lousy job of coaching last Sunday in Seattle. He needs to beat a tough Miami team, because he didn't beat the dead-end clubs like the 49ers and Seahawks.

That's life, when you were once 8-3 and you're now calculating far-fetched tiebreakers. Mangini left potential points all over Qwest Field, on a snowy day when they were going to be very tough to get. And the problems go well beyond this one game. The Jets are 1-3 down the stretch against some very beatable sides, Brett Favre looks 50 years old and Chad Pennington comes to town to remind everyone that Mangini couldn't win with him, either.

Where is the consistency? How can a coach call his offense from the field on a fourth-and-1 on the Seattle 2-yard line, then later claim it is important to pin the Seahawks down near their goal line on a punt instead of attempting a 50-yard field goal attempt? Even if the Jets had failed to convert on that earlier fourth down, Seattle would have been in horrible field position under dreadful conditions - Mangini's own argument for punting later.

Where is the closing? Certainly not at the tail end of the schedule, or the final minutes of these defeats. We again saw no evidence that Mangini or his coaches have a good feel for Favre's strengths or weaknesses in the waning minutes of a tight game. If you can't engineer a late drive against the Seahawks' 25th-ranked passing defense, with a Hall of Fame quarterback, then it might be time to throw the baby out with the bathwater.

If the Jets don't win this game against the Dolphins, maybe it's time to start anew yet again for these cursed Jets. Maybe they chase Matt Cassel - not Tom Brady! - or draft a quarterback in the offseason. Maybe they find a coach who can run an offense when it matters.

They can't be sure they have one of those right now. Mangini didn't demonstrate faith in his guys until he was desperate late in the game, on fourth-and-2 from his own 20. Too late. Now we get to see if those players he didn't trust still have enough gumption and desire to rescue their coach, or whether they'll quit on Mangini against the Dolphins.

"That isn't a focal point of mine at all," Mangini said yesterday, about his own job status. "I'm looking to give the players a good plan to beat Miami."

Again, you just want to shake the coach, find some passion in there somewhere that might be transferred to his players under pressure. Mangini is big on bringing in motivational speakers and showing inspirational films. It's time he found some ardor within himself, along with an offensive game plan that gets more than one touchdown pass and six interceptions in the last four games from Favre.

If nothing else, Jet fans can comfort themselves these days with the thought they were right all along. The football gods really do hate them. They are not imagining any of it.

How else can you explain the Scylla-or-Charybdis choice now facing the team, on this upcoming Sunday: The Jets can lose to Miami, anointing Pennington a star and Bill Parcells a genius. Or if they beat Miami, the victory will likely propel Bill Belichick deep into the playoffs, where his Giant Brain will be celebrated for its ingenuity and resilience.

Which is worse? Hard to say. Although there remains some small hope the Jets may wriggle their way into the playoffs, the far more probable outcome is a bunch of coaching and quarterback questions that must be answered shortly.

A lot of congratulations were handed out early in the season. Then as it started slipping away, we still kept hearing about how the Jets still controlled their own destiny. Now they control nothing, except their own effort against Miami.

Mangini yesterday refused to define the Jets' success by whether or not they make the playoffs.

"I'll look at the whole course of the season," he said, whatever that means.

Forty years later, this franchise still can't buy a big win. But it can always purchase a new head coach.
