title: Steelers' Heart And Soul Polamalu Steers Pittsburgh's Defense
content:
BY MATT PAWLIKOWSKI

Sports Correspondent

PITTSBURGH - His hits are monstrous, routinely making ESPN, and his knowledge of the game second to none. Yet surprisingly, he is not one to take credit for an impressive play, in most cases deferring to teammates.

Soft-spoken, there is no rah-rah or trash talk from him. Instead, after a game he says a quiet prayer in his locker, which is scattered with religious items. He usually does this prior to interviews.

We're talking Troy Polamalu, the Steelers' All-Pro safety, who since being selected with the 16th pick of the 2003 draft, has been the heart and soul of their much-heralded defensive unit.

"If you are asking about Troy (Polamalu) the player, he is an integral part of every game that we play because he is that good," head coach Mike Tomlin said. "When he walks out of the tunnel with a helmet on, he's going to be a factor in the football game."

During the regular season Polamalu recorded 73 tackles and had career-highs with seven interceptions and 17 passes defensed.

"I always enjoy watching him play," cornerback Bryant McFadden said. "Sometimes I get caught looking at him when I'm on the field, just seeing how gifted of a guy he is."

Gifted may be an understatement, especially when it comes to his performance in the 2009 playoffs.

Playing with a strained calf muscle, he was a force against the Chargers in the Divisional round. Then against the Ravens in the AFC Championship game, it was all about Troy. He had two passes defensed, four tackles including one for a loss, and perhaps the biggest play of the game, his 40-yard interception for a touchdown that sent the Steelers to Super Bowl XLIII.

"You always want to score," Polamalu said. "On defense, we're probably more concerned with scoring than the offense is. Our mindset is to stop the opposition from scoring points, or to score every time you get the ball. I think that is everyone's mindset on this defense."

As for Polamalu's' mindset, an opposing quarterback might say he is in their head, considering his uncanny ability to read a play even before it is called.

"He comes out of nowhere," Steeler quarterback Ben Roethlisbergersaid. "When Troy starts running with the ball, you never know what's going to happen."

Polamalu says it's all about his teammates.

"We have fun with each other and I think what you see on the field is just natural to us," Polamalu said. "When you have the quarterback worried about the rush, it throws him off balance a bit and allows you to freelance in a way. A lot of times it doesn't work. I can tell you a number of times during the season when I messed up on plays."

It's no secret that over the years Pittsburgh has built a strong defensive reputation. This year is no different, but according to Polamalu, the no-name unit (players refuse to give it a name) is the best he's been associated with.

"This is the best defense I have been on since I've been here, and I think the difference is the turnovers," Polamalu said. "That makes all the difference. We were the No. 1 defense what, the past four or five years, but we did it on three and out. Whenever you can make a turnover, that is when you become an awesome defense.

"Also, we play team defense, so whenever you see guys making the big plays, there is always the unnoticed guy making tackles, because again we are disguising on the back end and holding up receivers."

Again, his humility shines through, as there is no mention of the fact he's been a driving force.

"There are a lot of guys in this league that you might question why they are here," Steelers receiver Nate Washington said. "Troy is not one of those guys. He plays with fire. He plays with his heart. That's what makes him a great leader on this team and a great player in the NFL."

title: Torn ligament clouds Little's status
content:
With a towel around his waist and a grimace on his face, Rams defensive end Leonard Little limped toward the showers in the visitors' locker room late Sunday afternoon at Qwest Field. It was a slow and agonizing walk.

What originally was thought to be a sprain of Little's left big toe has been diagnosed as a torn ligament. It limited his participation significantly in the 33-6 loss to the Seahawks and could well hamper him for the rest of the season - if not end it.

"He's going to have to surgery on it. Whether it's soon or at the end of the year depends on how well it holds up," defensive coordinator Jim Haslett said Monday.

Little has had problems with the toe for some time. He aggravated it the previous Sunday at Baltimore and didn't practice last week. "It's still a concern for us, and we'll determine (later) as to his availability this week," coach Scott Linehan said.

Even though Little has recorded just one sack this season, he'd been applying increasing quarterback pressure before the setback vs. the Ravens. His replacement at Seattle, Victor Adeyanju, was solid; his five tackles included two for lost yardage. But Adeyanju can't get into the backfield like Little.

"Victor stepped in and probably played the best game he's played, really good on the run," Haslett said.

Hall not ready

Don't look for wide receiver/kick returner Dante Hall to be back on the field until after the Nov. 4 bye. At the earliest.

Hall has missed two games since he was hurt Sept. 30 at Dallas. "It was a real serious high-ankle sprain," Linehan said. "He tested it a little bit (Monday), and it's not ready."

Where there's a Will ...

According to the coaches' film review, linebacker Will Witherspoon piled up 16 tackles Sunday, a career-high for the six-year veteran. His 66 stops are No. 1 on the team.

Improved line play has benefited all the linebackers, but especially Witherspoon in the middle, Linehan pointed out. "It's a direct reflection of our front playing better than a year ago and keeping him free to the ball," Linehan said. "He's always had the ability to get to the ballcarriers."

Weakside linebacker Pisa Tinoisamoa had 12 tackles a week after registering 15 vs. the Ravens.

The Rams have moved up to No. 18 in the 32-team NFL in total defense. But largely because of turnovers by the offense, they've given up more points (192) than any other team in the league.

A chance for offense

At 11.3 points per game, the Rams are the NFL's lowest-scoring outfit. But they haven't had a better opportunity - statistically, anyway - to break out of their funk than Sunday's matchup with Cleveland.

The Browns (3-3) are yielding 413 yards per game, worst in the league. They're No. 27 vs. the run and No. 29 vs. the pass.

Ram-blings

The combined passer rating for Marc Bulger and Gus Frerotte is a league-worst 58.7. ... At minus 8, the Rams are tied with New Orleans for last in turnover ratio.

title: 2007 USA TODAY All-USA team High School Football
content:
About this page

The 2007 All-USA high school football team was selected by USA TODAY's Jim Halley, in consultation with college and high school football coaches and recruiting analysts, including Tom Lemming of CSTV.com, Mike Farrell of Rivals.com and Allen Wallace of Superprep.com. All selections are seniors except running back Bryce Brown, who is a junior.

Offensive Player of the Year

Terrelle Pryor

Quarterback, 6-6, 210 pounds

Jeannette (Pa.)

Why him? First Pennsylvania high school player to pass for more than 4,000 yards (4,249) and rush for more than 4,000 (4,250) in his career. Led his team to the state's AA title and a 16-0 record as it scored a state-record 860 points. He can throw a football 75 yards, or 50 yards on his knees. Passed for 23 TDs and ran for 34. Also a top basketball recruit.

Colleges under consideration: Ohio State, Florida, Michigan, Penn State, Oregon.

Favorite NFL player(s): All quarterbacks. Vince Young, Tom Bradyand Michael Vick.

What people might not know about you: "They don't know my personality. Some people get the wrong impression about me."

Favorite entertainer: Rapper Lil' Wayne.

NBA or NFL? "The NBA. You can play longer because you don't get beat up as much."

Best lesson you've learned: "Just be around the right people and don't be around the wrong people. Stay away from trouble."

Last movie you saw: Superbad.

Who's in your five: Mom, Dad, friends Jason and Ted and coach Ray Reitz and coach Roy Hall.

Funniest recruiting story: "Somebody tried to land a helicopter on our field."

Defensive Player of the Year

Patrick Johnson

Defensive back, 6-1, 190 pounds

Blanche Ely (Pompano Beach, Fla.)

Why him? A physical shutdown defender with 4.37 speed in the 40-yard dash. He had seven interceptions, including one returned for a touchdown (three other interception returns for TDs were nullified by penalties). Accounted for nine more TDs on offense.

Colleges under consideration: Florida, Miami (Fla.), Florida State.

Favorite NFL player: He has two -- his cousin Bryant McFadden, a cornerback with the Pittsburgh Steelers, and Denver Broncos cornerback Champ Bailey. "I love Champ's style and the way he elevates his game, no matter who he's going against, to big-time standards."

Favorite teacher: Novice Johnson, his poetry teacher.

Why do you wear No. 7? "Because my dad wore that same jersey and I wanted to fulfill his dreams of someday playing in the NFL." (His father, Patrick Peterson, played for Ely from 1987 to 1989 and is the school's varsity defensive backs coach.)

Last book you read: Quiet Strength by Tony Dungy.

What people might not know about you: "I'm an A student. I graduated (early)."

Favorite entertainer: Actor Chris Tucker.

Who's in your five: "My mom, dad, grandmom, one of my friends and my best girl, who's a friend."

What friends tease you about: "My ears. My friends say they have a funny shape, but they look normal to me."

Best lesson you've learned: Because he was struggling with his grades as a freshman, his father took him off the football team as a sophomore. Ely lost in the playoffs 21-19 to St. Thomas Aquinas (Fort Lauderdale). "It was an eye-opener for me. I could have accomplished a lot more."

Coach of the Year

Tim Harris

Booker T. Washington,

Miami

Age: 42

Why him? He has taken a program never before in the Super 25 to No. 8, a school that didn't exist as a high school 10 years ago and turned it into one of the top teams in the country. In five years, his teams have gone 62-7. This year Booker T. Washington went unbeaten for the first time, winning the Florida Class 4A championship after falling in the semifinals four consecutive years. "The most valuable lesson I learned was just building a good relationship with the student-athlete and having a good relationship with your staff," Harris says. "That came from going to a lot of clinics."

Where his nickname "Ice" comes from: "Growing up, George 'Iceman' Gervin was one of the guys I emulated as a basketball player, and it stuck with me."

Greatest coaching influence: "My head coach at Miami High, Charles Bethel. He taught me a lot, that being a coach is more than putting on a whistle and shorts. It is about teaching and making sure your players stay focused in the classroom."

Family: He's been married to his high school sweetheart, Chonita, for 15 years. He has two sons, both of whom have played for him. His oldest, Tim, is a three-time All-America sprinter at the University of Miami. His youngest son, Brandon, was a senior cornerback and quarterback for Booker T. Washington this season.

title: EAGLES LOOKING TO GET OVER THE HUMP SUNDAY'S EFFORT WILL BE MORE THAN JUST ANOTHER GAME.
content:
For the Eagles, the get-over-the-hump day was yesterday. That's the day when, once it's over, up pops the horizon of another Sunday of purposeful grunts and groans and plays that matter.

It may matter even more considering that this time they're playing a get-over-the-hump game.

The Eagles have not been above .500 after five games since 1996.

That year, they finished 10-6 in Ray Rhodes' second season and made the playoffs as a wild card.

As unpredictable as this early part of the season has been, the Eagles now have an opportunity. Beat Atlanta on national television Sunday night, and they go into an emotional, who-knows-what-could-happen NFC East Division showdown with the Washington Redskins at 3-2.

Lose, and they are 2-3 and facing a Redskins team that has all that talent, old or not. They will be in a game desperately needing a win to avoid dropping to 2-4 before going back on the road.

True, the Eagles have made progress on the road. They're 2-1 away from Veterans Stadium this season, the first time since 1996 that they have won more than one road game.

Still, this is a hump game.

A win is significant progress.

A loss is more of that up-and-back, two-step dance.

"We know what we are capable of doing, so it's an opportunity for us to come out and show something positive this time," quarterback Donovan McNabb said.

McNabb was alluding to the Eagles' negative showing in their home opener three weeks ago. After embarrassing Dallas, 41-14, in Week 1, they had a nice test with the NFC East rival New York Giants, and they played uninspired in a 33-18 loss.

It was a 42-point turnaround, and one chance for progress this season evaporated.

Some of the Eagles have spent much of this week trying to downplay the game's importance, with the conventional rhetoric of saying that every game matters, not one more than the other. Of course, there is truth to that belief.

But there is also truth to saying that a young, unaccomplished team that has not won a lot needs to capitalize on even the smallest opportunities because you just never know how the rest of the season will unfold.

"I try to look at the big picture with things," coach Andy Reid said. "I've seen this before, and it's important that the guys continue to battle through whatever adversities are there. You've seen them do that in games, and that's an important ingredient to have. Eventually, this thing will smooth out a little bit and you won't have the real 'high-high-highs' or the real 'low-low-lows.' There's a process that takes place in getting to that."

Eventually, a team has to string together success and string together character. Right now, the Eagles appear to be forming constants, but more success is needed.

The defense looks good, but it still ranks 22d against the run. Break apart the defense, and you find a superb pass defense, which is ranked No. 2 in the league. The defensive line, boosted by Hugh Douglas' return from injury and the drafting of Corey Simon, has caused major havoc thus far.

However, as Douglas said, "We still have to keep it going and show that we're for real."

On the offensive side, when Duce Staley has daylight, he has proved to be a top-notch running back. But, being the offensive gem, teams focus on him and have been able to slow him.

"We're going to keep trying to give him the ball every way we can," offensive coordinator Rod Dowhower said of Staley. "Before it's over, he'll get his yards."

So, what you have right now is a football team with many positives, but even more question marks and few constants.

In the meantime, the Eagles need to ensure that they are the kind of answers they want to give.

"We're very confident in the guys that we have out there on the field," McNabb said. "For us to go out and play well wouldn't be something that's unexpected. It would be something that we've been doing all throughout the week of just preparing ourselves and going out and playing well."

Beating Atlanta would be a step. The consensus is that the teams are close to even. Atlanta has its woes, too. Its offensive line is bad. Its defense has been torched. Star running back Jamal Anderson must still prove he's a special player after coming off a season-ending knee injury.

"There is no doubt that this is a big game for us," wide receiver Torrance Small said, something he has repeated throughout the week. "We always have things to prove."

Jerry Brewer's e-mail address is jbrewer@phillynews.com

title: Words to wise: These will mean something
content:
A little primer on football terms you can expect to hear often in this weekend's league championship games. With apologies to George Carlin, note the similarities to war:

* Blitz: (blits) n. 1. A sudden, destructive attack, as by aircraft or tanks. 2. Football A sudden charge by a defensive backfield player through a gap in the line in an effort to tackle the opposing quarterback. . . . The WWII Germans and modern-day Steelers and Eagles lived/live by the blitz. We know what happened to the Germans. Let's see what happens to the Steelers and Eagles on Sunday. This will be the most overused word this weekend.

* Pocket: (POK-it) n. 1. A little bag or pouch, now usually sewn into or on clothing, for carrying money or small articles. 2. Football The protected area behind the quarterback from which the quarterback passes the ball. . . . Kurt Warner and Tom Brady prefer to stay in the pocket, risking the dreaded sack (see below); Donovan McNabb and Kordell Stewart, both considerably quicker, have a penchant for evacuating the pocket to avoid the dreaded sack. Both scenarios will happen often.

* Sack: (sak) vt. 1. The plundering or looting, especially by soldiers, of a captured city or town. 2. Football To tackle (a quarterback) behind the line of scrimmage. The Steelers led the NFL during the regular season with 55 sacks, and the Eagles, Patriots and Rams all had at least 40. The hunt for the quarterback will be relentless.

* Tuck: (tuk) vt. 1. To pull up or gather into a fold, or folds; draw together so as to make shorter. 2. Football Webster's does not yet have a football-related meaning, but after Brady's tuck that became a fumble that became an incomplete pass last weekend, look for one in the near future.

* Audible: (ODD-a-bull) adj. 1. That can be heard. Loud enough to be heard. n.2. Football A play decided upon and called by the quarterback at the line of scrimmage. This is another term that will be used often. If the quarterback picks up the blitz, he will call an audible to avoid a sack and could end up tucking the ball while he's in the pocket.

Got it?

title:
Putting a move on 'Playmakers';
CHRISTINA LURIE PRODUCING FOOTBALL FILM OF HER OWN
content:
IN CASE YOU missed it, Jeff

Lurie gave a major thumbs down to the ESPN series "Playmakers" last week.

The Eagles' owner and part-time TV

critic used the whole Rush Limbaugh/ Donovan McNabb hubbub as a golden opportunity to spew vitriol at the all-sports network for its controversial show about a fictional pro football team.

Turns out Lurie might have more reasons for not liking the show than simply the fact that

it portrays players as cocaine-snorting, Vicadin-swallowing, Johnny Walker Black-chugging, wife-abusing, dog-kicking sexaholics.

It's also competition.

Seems Lurie's wife, Christina, is putting

together a TV or movie project of her own about a pro football team.

Ron Howard, the

Eagles' director of marketing and communications, said he didn't know a lot about the project, but thought it still was a go. He said Christina even was in New York this summer to do some shooting for the film/series.

Christina first came up with the idea of producing a series or movie about a fictional pro

football team more than a year ago, long before "Playmakers" debuted.

Needless to say, her portrayal of pro football would be considerably more G-rated than

ESPN's. Last year, she even hired a big-time writer -

Angelo Pizzo, who wrote the screenplays for "Hoosiers" and "Rudy," neither of which gets into sex or drug use much, unless you count Dennis Hopper's fondness for the bottle in

"Hoosiers." But Pizzo got called away on another project, a movie called "Game of Their Lives," about the 1950 U.S. World Cup soccer team that beat England.

Christina has since hired another writer.

Jeff Lurie produced a handful of movies before he bought the Eagles from Norman Braman in 1994. But Howard said this project is strictly Christina's baby.

"As far as I know, Jeffrey's not involved in this," he said.

Around the league

* The good news for beleagured Chargers coach Marty Schottenheimer is that he won't be fired before the season ends. The bad news is he's almost

certainly going to be history

after it ends if his 0-5 team doesn't turn things around. "There will not be a coaching change during the season," Chargers general manager A.J. Smith said.

* The Giants put a lot of time and money into improving their special teams in the offseason. So far, it hasn't been money well spent. The kicker and long snapper they imported - Mike Hollis and Ryan Kuehl - both are on injured

reserve. Return ace Brian Mitchell is off to a slow start. He's averaging just 8.3 yards per punt return and 21.7 yards per kickoff return. The punter they signed, Jeff Feagles, is fifth in the NFC with a 43.0-yard average, but the

Giants' punt-coverage unit is ranked 25th in the league. After Hollis went down with a back

injury, the Giants re-signed their kicker from last year - Matt Bryant. But he suffered a hamstring injury last week and is out indefinitely. Brett Conway has been signed to replace him. "If it wasn't for bad luck, we wouldn't have any luck at all," Giants coach Jim Fassel said. "I can't believe it. I mean, you think you fix something. And now, here we are on our third kicker and our third snapper, and we even had to get a new holder in there last week because our holder [Feagles] had to kick. It's just unbelievable. I told Ernie [Accorsi, the club's GM] that if we bring

Conway in here and he gets hurt, we'll never be able to sign another kicker because nobody will come here."

* We'll find out a lot more about the 5-0 Vikings in the next 3 weeks than we discovered in the first 5. Their next three opponents are the 4-1 Broncos, the 2-2 Giants and the 3-2 Packers. Vikings coach Mike Tice called the next 3 weeks "a big credibility test for our football team."

* Former Cowboys president and general manager Tex Schramm, who died over the summer, will enter the club's Ring of Honor on Sunday at

halftime of the Cowboys' game against the Eagles.

* The Redskins already have been flagged for 19 false-start penalties in their first five games, including seven Sunday against the Eagles. The 'Skins' frequent use of audibles has been blamed for the plague of false starts. But coach Steve Spurrier said just seven of the 19 false starts have come on

audibles.

* The Bengals just signed ex-

Eagle punter Kyle Richardson. He's the 25th new player on their 53-man roster since the

beginning of last season.

By the numbers

* Further evidence of the importance of being able to run the football: The five teams that lead the league in rushing -

the Ravens, Panthers, Broncos, Vikings and Chiefs - are a

combined 20-3.

* The Bucs' vaunted defensive tackle tandem of Warren Sapp and Anthony McFarland has combined for zero sacks thus far.

* The Falcons have given up

an NFL-high five pass plays of 40 or more yards.

* In the Panthers' first four games, Stephen Davis has rushed for 111, 142, 153 and 159 yards. Those last three numbers are the second-, fourth- and sixth-best single-game rushing totals in franchise history.

* The Saints already have given up 10 touchdown passes. Opposing QBs have a collective 102.3 passer rating against them.

* The Redskins are the first team in NFL history to open the season with five games decided by three points or less.

* The Titans have yet to average more than 3 yards per carry in any game this season. *

Send e-mail to pdomo@aol.com

title: Vikings: Our turn for a new stadium With the U playing football on campus and the Twins headed downtown, the drive is on again to replace the Dome.
content:
With an undefeated team, a megastar quarterback and a big game coming up Monday, the Vikings were back at the State Capitol on Thursday to spark interest in the one thing they do not have -- a new stadium.

Vikings spokesman Lester Bagley again told legislators that the team would not renew its Metrodome lease, which expires after the 2011 season. Bagley also said owner Zygi Wilf is increasingly battling pressure from other National Football League owners.

"They don't ask how's (star running back) Adrian Peterson doing," Bagley said. "They ask him how is the stadium doing, because the NFL is subsidizing this market to the tune of $15 million to $20 million a year."

The Vikings, who are seeking an estimated $700 million in a "public-private partnership," have made their pitch to the Legislature on repeated occasions, to little effect.

But on Thursday they talked not finances, but football, highlighting star quarterback Brett Favre's game-winning touchdown pass Sunday, the team's always-high TV ratings and the feverish interest in Monday night's televised game against Favre's old team, the Green Bay Packers. Vikings officials even briefed legislators on how Vikings players had helped with a campaign to distribute flu shots.

Public subsidies unpopular

Team and stadium officials concede that little regarding their stadium situation has changed and that there remains scant evidence that public subsidies for a new stadium have gained traction among legislators. Metrodome officials even acknowledged that an economic benefit study presented Thursday was the same one shown to legislators in February.

But, they maintained, many Minnesotans do not want to see the Vikings leave.

"When you look at the upcoming Monday night game, you'll see that there's just a tremendous amount of interest in the Minnesota Vikings and the need to keep them here," said Roy Terwilliger, the chair of the Metropolitan Sports Facilities Commission, which owns the Dome.

Rep. Joe Atkins, chairman of the House Commerce and Labor Committee, which held the hearing, said he wanted to gauge the Vikings' economic benefit to the state, asking, "Does it make any difference to us to not have the team?"

After the hearing, Atkins, DFL-Inver Grove Heights, said he did not know whether a Vikings stadium would be pushed during the 2010 legislation session. Asked whether he might sponsor legislation for a stadium, Atkins smiled and said that "(I) haven't even given it a thought."

With Minnesota still reeling from the worst recession since World War II, many legislators have remained cool toward backing a heavily-remodeled Metrodome that could cost up to $954 million, two-thirds of which the team wants the public to pay for.

The 2010 legislative session is still four months away, and with a state budget deficit and upcoming gubernatorial race likely to dominate its proceedings, Thursday's presentation by the Vikings seemed to stir little emotion.

Only one legislator had questions for team and Metrodome officials after they finished talking.

Bagley said Thursday that the Vikings were not exploring leaving Minnesota, but continued to draw interest from other cities interested in having a pro football team.

Under a scenario outlined by Terwilliger, a major remodeling of the Dome -- the Vikings' home for 27 years -- would take up to three years and require the team to temporarily play elsewhere, probably at the University of Minnesota's on-campus football stadium, which is new this year.

Bagley said that the actual cost of a remodeled Metrodome with a new roof might drop by 10 percent because the existing site would be used, and he said the cost to taxpayers could be $50 million annually to retire the project's debt.

"It's a little more manageable," Bagley said of the annual debt payment figure.

But of late, the Vikings have done much better on the field than they have at the State Capitol. Three years ago, the Legislature rejected a financing plan for a Vikings stadium even though it approved funding for a university football stadium and also authorized a sales tax increase in Hennepin County to help fund a new park in Minneapolis for the Minnesota Twins.

After a quarter century as tenants at the Dome, the university vacated last month, moving into its new stadium. The Twins will be installed in their ballpark in time for the spring season, leaving the Vikings as the last remaining tenants in the aging Dome.

Pawlenty, Doyle wager

Although he has not championed a Vikings stadium, Gov. Tim Pawlenty showed Thursday that he was not immune from the team's hype. Pawlenty said that he and Wisconsin Gov. Jim Doyle were wagering on the Vikings-Packers game and Saturday's football game between the University of Minnesota and the University of Wisconsin. "Between a Gopher win at the new on-campus stadium and another great performance from Brett Favre, it's going to be an excellent few days in the land of Purple and Gold," Pawlenty said.

With that in mind, team officials reminded legislators Thursday that the state had not contributed to the building of the Dome but had received $234 million in revenues from the building's events over the past three decades. During a typical game, Vikings officials said, fans spent roughly $600,000 on items ranging from hot dogs to purple jerseys at the Dome.

"When FOX (TV) puts our game on on Sundays, they have a hundred people that come to Minnesota" for the broadcast, Bagley said. "Monday Night Football, for ESPN and ABC (TV), we have 400 credentials that go just to that production."

Mike Kaszuba - 651-222-1673

INCH BY INCH ON STADIUM PUSH

May 2005: Zygi Wilf buys the Minnesota Vikings for $600 million and soon begins pressing for a new outdoor stadium.

September 2005: The Vikings reach an agreement with Anoka County to build a $675 million stadium in Blaine. The plan would rely on $395 or more million from taxpayers.

November 2006: Anoka County ends its bid to help build a stadium with the Vikings after the team joins a study of downtown Minneapolis stadium sites.

June 2007: The Vikings tentatively agree to buy four city blocks for $45 million from Avista Capital Partners, then-owner of the Star Tribune, as part of a plan to build a new stadium near the Metrodome and to develop the land around it.

August 2007: The Vikings cancel the $45 million deal.

July 2008: The Metropolitan Sports Facilities Commission, which operates the Dome, unveil a $853 million design for a retractable-roof stadium, reusing part of the Dome.

2009: Despite a state budget deficit, proponents of a new stadium for the Vikings quietly try to rally interest in the Legislature, so far without success

Thursday: The team announces it will not renew its Metrodome lease when it expires after the 2011 season.

Saints have opportunity to open season with three wins for second time under coach Sean Payton and third time this decade. ... Bills are one win short of becoming seventh of eight original AFL franchises to register 350 regular-season victories. Jets are last with 333. ... RB Fred Jackson, filling in for suspended starter Marshawn Lynch, has 328 yards from scrimmage (220 rushing, 108 receiving) to rank second in NFL behind Tennessee RB Chris Johnson's 352. ... QB Trent Edwards is 229 yards passing short of 5,000. ... With Jonathan Scott prepared to start at RT in place of injured Brad Butler, Bills' OL enters game with combined 41 NFL starts, led by C Geoff Hangartner's 29. ... Opponents haven't scored 2-point conversion in three attempts against Bills this season, including two chances by New England.

When — 3:15 p.m.

Line — Broncos by 1 1/2

Record vs. spread — Denver 2-0; Oakland 2-0

Series record — Raiders lead 55-40-2

Last meeting — Raiders beat Broncos 31-10, Nov. 23, 2008

Last week — Broncos beat Browns 27-6; Raiders beat Chiefs 13-10

Streaks, stats and notes — Raiders look to beat Denver in back-to-back games for first time since 2002. ... Broncos have won five of six in Oakland, including 41-14 victory in Monday night opener last season. ... Denver QB Kyle Orton leads NFL with 152.1 passer rating in fourth quarter. ... Elvis Dumervil tied Denver record with four sacks last week. ... Denver's offense is only one in NFL that hasn't committed turnover this season. ... Broncos defensive coordinator Mike Nolan returns to Bay Area for first time since being fired by San Francisco last season. ... Denver has won last nine division openers. ... Raiders QB JaMarcus Russell is 27 for 37 for 332 yards, three TDs and no INTs in two starts against Denver. ... Oakland RB Darren McFadden had his only career two-TD game last year in Denver. ... Greg Ellis has three sacks and fellow newcomer Richard Seymour has two in just two games with Raiders.

When — 3:15 p.m.

Line — Chargers by 6

Record vs. spread — Miami 0-2; San Diego 0-2

Series record — Dolphins lead 14-12

Last meeting — Dolphins beat Chargers 17-10, Oct. 5, 2008.

Last week — Dolphins lost to Colts 27-23; Chargers lost to Ravens 31-26

Streaks, stats and notes — Not only do Dolphins lead series 14-12, but they've won seven straight against Chargers, including last year in Miami. ... Chargers are 8-6 against Dolphins in San Diego, including their last win in series, a 22-21 playoff victory following 1994 season, when Chargers made their only Super Bowl appearance. ... Miami RB Ronnie Brown ran for 136 yards Monday night against Colts, giving him 3,612 in career, good for fifth place on team list. He needs 32 yards to equal Jim Kiick (3,644) for fourth place. ... Dolphins held ball for more than 45 minutes vs. Indy and still lost. ... WR Ted Ginn Jr. had career-best 11 catches against Colts. ... Chargers coach Norv Turner is 10-10 in last 20 games. ... Chargers C Nick Hardwick had surgery on left ankle Wednesday and is expected to miss next eight weeks. ... Chargers QB Philip Rivers passed for career-best 436 yards in loss to Baltimore. ... RB/returner Darren Sproles has 524 all-purpose yards in two games.

Line — Steelers by 4

Record vs. spread — Pittsburgh 0-1; Cincinnati 1-1

Series record — Steelers lead 48-30

Last meeting — Steelers 27, Bengals 10, Nov. 20, 2008

Last week — Steelers lost to Bears 17-14, Bengals beat Packers 31-24

Streaks, stats and notes — Steelers have won five in a row against Bengals and their last eight games in Cincinnati, including 2005 playoffs. Bengals haven't beaten them at Paul Brown Stadium since 2001, when WR Chad Ochocinco was rookie named Chad Johnson. ... QB Ben Roethlisberger is 11-0 in his native state of Ohio: 6-0 in Cincinnati and 5-0 in Cleveland. ... Steelers haven't allowed 100-yard rusher in last 20 regular-season games and only three 100-yard rushers in past 84 games overall. ... WR Hines Ward needs 60 yards in catches to become first Steeler with 10,000 yards career. ... It will be first time Ward plays against Bengals LB Keith Rivers since his high hit last October broke Rivers' jaw and prompted offseason rules change. ... Bengals QB Carson Palmer missed both games against Steelers last year because of elbow injury. Palmer is 2-7 as starter against Steelers, including 2005 playoff game in which he suffered knee injury on his first pass.

Indianapolis chose Connecticut RB Donald Brown with No. 27 pick in draft, spoiling Arizona's hopes of getting him. Cardinals took Ohio State RB Beanie Wells at No. 31. ... Peyton Manning brought team from behind to win in the fourth quarter or OT for 37th time in Monday night's 27-23 victory at Miami. That ranks second among active quarterbacks behind Brett Favre's 39. ... Indianapolis has won 61 of its last 75 regular-season games. ... Colts had the ball for just under 15 minutes against Miami, scoring 27 points on 24 plays. ... Indianapolis is NFL's lightest team with an average weight of 239.11 pounds per player. ... Jacksonville was 11-1 at home against NFC foes before losing to Cardinals 33-17 on Sunday. ... After Kurt Warner's 131.2 passer rating against Jaguars, Arizona is 12-3 in 15 games in which Warner's passer rating has topped 100. ... S Antrel Rolle's 83-yard return of blocked field goal was his sixth career TD. He returned four interceptions and one fumble for previous scores. ... title: Five-star challenge
content:
Fresh off their selections as USA TODAY's All-USA high school offensive and defensive players of the year, respectively, Chris Leak and Antonio Cromartie know a little something about football. Leak, a quarterback from Independence High in Charlotte, threw for an amazing 64 TDs this season in leading his team to the 4A state title, and he'll play in the U.S. Army All-American Bowl in San Antonio on Jan. 5 (ESPN2). Cromartie, a defensive back and receiver from Lincoln in Tallahassee, Fla., had six interceptions, 53 tackles and 30 receptions and returned two kicks and three punts for TDs. Neither of the highly sought-after players has decided on a college, but each sees the NFL in his future. They offer their take on the final weekend of games:

* * * * * Like fielding a punt at the 1, finding a seam up the middle and outrunning 11 guys for the game-winning score.

* * * * Like North Carolina beating South Carolina or Florida beating California in the annual high school all-star game.

* * * Like having the top schools in the USA wanting you to play football there.

* * Like losing to your crosstown rival on the final play of the game.

* Like being the reason for that loss.

* * * * *

Miami at New England, 1 p.m.

Chris: Adam Vinatieri will be the difference again. Patriots 24-17.

Antonio: Dolphins are my favorite team, and Ricky Williams will run for 200 yards. Miami 24-21.

Baltimore at Pittsburgh, 1 p.m.

Chris: Steelers are playing well on both sides of the ball. Tommy Maddox will be a key factor. Steelers 21-10.

Antonio: When Maddox goes down, Kordell Stewart will step in and make the big plays. Steelers 28-24.

Philadelphia at N.Y. Giants, Saturday, 1:30 p.m.

Chris: The Eagles' offense is doing great without one of the most dangerous players ever (and my favorite player) in Donovan McNabb. Eagles 24-21.

Antonio: Duce Staley and the Eagles' receivers will carry them to the Super Bowl. By then McNabb should be back. Eagles 10-7.

Green Bay at New York Jets, 4:15 p.m.

Chris: Green Bay is pumped up to get the home-field advantage through the playoffs. Pack 23-12.

Antonio: Brett Favre is the leader and the man. Packers 28-14.

Atlanta at Cleveland, 1 p.m.

Chris: It's a treat watching Michael Vick play. He's one of the most explosive players ever to play the game. Falcons 32-27.

Antonio: Vick's speed and mobility are too much for the Browns. Falcons 17-7.

* * * *

Kansas City at Oakland, Saturday, 5 p.m.

Chris: The Raiders can clinch home-field advantage in the Black Hole, and they will 31-14.

Antonio: Look for Tim Brown and Jerry Rice to have their best game of the season. Raiders 14-0.

Carolina at New Orleans, 1 p.m.

Chris: Deuce McAllister is a double threat and will have a big game running and catching passes. Saints 24-17.

Antonio: The Saints have to win. Their defense will toughen up and Aaron Brooks will pass them to the victory 28-0.

Jacksonville at Indianapolis, 4:05 p.m.

Chris: Peyton Manning and Marvin Harrison are the NFL's most dynamic duo. Colts 32-28.

Antonio: Manning and Harrison will hook up all day. Colts 21-7.

* * *

Arizona at Denver, 4:15 p.m.

Chris: Denver's defense will stop Arizona 34-14.

Antonio: Even though Clinton Portis should've stayed another year at the University of Miami, he has great vision and field awareness. He's the reason the Broncos will win 10-0.

Seattle at San Diego, 4:15 p.m.

Chris: The Chargers will be productive on both sides of the ball. LaDainian Tomlinson will run over the Seahawks 21-14.

Antonio: Great defense will carry the Chargers to the win 21-7.

Tampa Bay at Chicago, 8:30 p.m.

Chris: The Bucs want to prove they can win in cold weather. Bucs 21-10.

Antonio: After being embarrassed by the Steelers last week, the Bucs are mad and will take it out on the Bears 35-14.

Tennessee at Houston, 1 p.m.

Chris: Steve McNair is having an MVP-caliber year. Titans 32-14.

Antonio: Eddie George is the man and will finally get his running game going full speed. Titans 21-9.

* *

San Francisco at St. Louis, Monday, 9 p.m.

Chris: Terrell Owens and Jeff Garcia have been on fire the past few weeks. 49ers 28-24.

Antonio: T.O. has fun on the field, and he'll have a field day against the Rams. 49ers 21-20.

Minnesota at Detroit, 1 p.m.

Chris: Daunte Culpepper was impressive in the win last week. He'll do it again. Vikings 27-17.

Antonio: Culpepper and Randy Moss are determined to end the season on a positive note. Vikings 28-21.

Dallas at Washington, 1 p.m.

Chris: Emmitt Smith will have a big game as he always does against the Redskins. Cowboys 28-14.

Antonio: Steve Spurrier's coaching will be the difference. Washington's passing game and running game are finally jelling. Redskins 24-14.

*

Cincinnati at Buffalo, 1 p.m.

Chris: Drew Bledsoe's passing will carry the Bills 38-17.

Antonio: Bledsoe will lead the Bills in the season finale 44-21.

Last week: TV talk show hosts Craig Kilborn and Tom Arnold couldn't surpass .500 in picking the Week 16 games. Kilborn finished strong to make it to 8-8, while Arnold faltered down the stretch, finishing 7-9.

title: Tomlin: Roethlisberger's shoulder 'a little sore'
content:
Steelers quarterback Ben Roethlisbergerwill be limited in practice Wednesday by a minor shoulder problem but coach Mike Tomlin said it is nothing serious.

"He's just a little sore," Tomlin said today at his weekly news conference.

Other Steelers that won't participate fully in practice include left tackle Marvel Smith (groin strain) and cornerback Deshea Townsend (heel contusion).

Both, along with Roethlisberger, are expected to play Sunday night when the Steelers try to run their winning streak against the Cleveland Browns.

The Browns are coming off a 10-win season and are expected to challenge the Steelers for the AFC North title this season.

Cleveland is coming off a disappointing 28-10 loss to Dallas at home but Tomlin said he expects the Browns to be much better in the second week of the season.

"A lot has been said and written about what this (Cleveland) is capable of," Tomlin said, "and deservedly so."

Two Steelers are up for NFL honors following the first week of the season.

Outside linebacker LaMarr Woodley is one of five finalists for the Defensive Player of the Week award while running back Willie Parker is one of three finalists for the FedEx Ground NFL Player of the Week.

Parker, playing in his first regular-season game since breaking his lower right leg last December, rushed for 138 yards and a career-high three touchdowns in the Steelers' 38-17 win over the Texans.

Woodley, making his first career start, had a sack and his first-career interception against the Texans. He also recovered a fumble.

Winners for the weekly awards are determined by fan voting.

To vote for the defensive player of the week go here. To vote for the NFL Player of the Week go here.

title: FAVRE WAS NEVER A ONE-MAN TEAM
content:
I'm wondering if the writer who declared that Brett Favre rebuilt Lambeau Field has a short memory.

As I recall, Favre wasn't out there by himself (though he acted like it sometimes) trying to win games. He had the support of an excellent general manager, brilliant head coach, future Hall of Famers like Reggie White and the best fans in the world. To suggest that somehow prima donna Favre did it all himself is sickening.

It's priceless that Favre beat the Packers at Lambeau, hey? That will make it all the sweeter when the new QB in town goes into the Humpty Dump and leads the Pack over that traitor and his new team in a few weeks!

- Corey Kunde, Oregon

Packer history lesson is in order

The letter writer who gave Brett Favre credit for single-handedly building the Green Bay Packers should study some Packer history.

Names such as Lambeau, McNally, Hutson, Hinkle, Lombardi, Starr, Hornung, Taylor, Nitschke, Gregg, Dickey, Lofton, Sharpe, White and others made the Green Bay Packers what they are today - not just Favre.

Lambeau Field has been sold out for decades and is sold out for decades from now, and would have been, regardless of Favre.

Thanks in part to fans like this, Favre's ego soared, making him believe he was bigger than his coaches, management and the team. He is no longer a Packer because he refused to follow a plan.

As a New York Jet, his wish to continue his streak came before the team's playoff chances, and he was run out of town along with the coach who hired him.

As a Viking, his success is largely due to his taking a reality check and actually following a plan for the first time in over a decade.

I'm thankful Green Bay's Favre era is over. Other teams now have to figure out how to defeat the Packers as a complete team and not just Favre.

There are many fans who would have liked to celebrate Favre and his years as a Packer, if not for feeling as if he's slapped us all in the face.

- Boyd Williamson, Reedsburg

Packer organization built by its owners

I read with disappointment another writer's conclusion that we "spiteful" Packer fans should realize that Lambeau Field and quite possibly the Packer organization were built by former quarterback Brett Favre.

While Favre certainly made his mark in Green Bay, some fail to realize he is not larger than the organization and, for that matter, the team itself. The organization was built by its owners, which happen to be members of the public.

The team's legacy was not built simply by one player. Have we forgotten about Nitschke, Lombardi, Starr, Lambeau, White, Hutson and all the other Packer greats who played before Favre?

Unfortunately we can get lost in the moment and believe that one person is larger than life, seeming to forget the true essence on which a team and an organization were built.

It's unfortunate that Favre's playing for one of our rivals, but as some need to learn, the Packer organization will continue without him. I think that's where Favre got lost.

- Jim Brigham, Lake Mills

Fair weather fans are the worst kind

Quick, who is the Super Bowl trophy named after: Vince Lombardi or Bud Grant? Which is the better stadium: Lambeau Field or the Metrodome? Who has 12 NFL championship titles and who has just one? These aren't difficult questions.

The Minnesota Vikings are one of the most pathetic franchises in sports. They blow it whenever they get close. Is there any good reason to cheer for them if you're not from Minnesota?

Many Packer "fans" are now for the Vikings because of Brett Favre, and it's pathetic. Did they root for the Packers before Favre? Do they want to give up those titles and embrace purple malaise?

Viking fans are even worse about Favre. They act like he's been their QB for a decade. I think Viking fans wear the purple #4 just to upset Packer fans. Where is their pride? If they win this year, it will always be Favre's title and not theirs.

Worse yet are Favre fans who want the Vikings to beat the Packers. Why? Because of one man - Ted Thompson. How sad is that?

- Matt Roellig, Madison

title: AMERICAN FOOTBALL: OAKLAND'S GANNON LEAVES YOUNG PRETENDER OUTGUNNED
content:
THE OAKLAND RAIDERS last appeared in a Super Bowl 19 years ago, but following their emphatic 30-10 victory over the New York Jets, the team that once set the standard for excellence is only one win away from a return to the grand stage.

In Florida at the weekend, the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, the laughing stock of the NFL for most of their history, also require one victory to reach San Diego after their equally compelling 31-6 triumph over the San Francisco 49ers.

Recent Raiders teams have failed lamentably at this time of year; last season they lost in controversial circumstances in New England, and the season before that, they were befuddled by Baltimore. Both of those teams went on to win the Super Bowl, but on the evidence of Sunday's triumph, Oakland are well placed to pick up their first trophy since beating the Washington Redskins in 1984.

The Jets, a team in a rich vein of form, kept it close until half-time, with the quarterback Chad Pennington throwing a touchdown pass to Jerald Sowell to cancel out Zack Crockett's score for Oakland, but in the second half there was only one possible outcome.

The Raiders, inspired by their veteran quarterback, Rich Gannon, went back to what they do best, which is to throw the ball often. The strategy yielded a prompt reward when Gannon fired a 29-yard strike to Jerry Porter, and when he tossed a nine-yarder to Jerry Rice early in the fourth quarter the visitors were finished.

The 37-year-old Gannon, having established a number of passing records over the season was named the NFL's Most Valuable Player, but like so many of his team-mates, his performances in the post-season have fallen short of expectation.

Many predicted that his rival, Pennington, would steal the show, but the youngster who has been compared to Joe Montana, looked out of his depth in the second half. Pennington threw two interceptions, lost two fumbles and was sacked four times as the relentless Raider defence tested his mettle. "There's a saying that as the quarterback goes, so does the team, and I didn't go today," he admitted. "I was struggling all day long, and I don't know why. It's one of those things I have to learn from."

While the Raiders are poised on the threshold of their fifth Super Bowl appearance, the Buccaneers, coached by John Gruden who was in charge of Oakland for four years until he left last summer, are close to their first championship game following their demolition of the 49ers.

Tampa's quarterback, Brad Johnson, returned to duty following a back injury, to throw touchdown passes to Joe Jurevicius and Ricky Dudley, while the powerful running of Mike Allstott produced another pair of scores. Leading 28-6 at half-time, the Buccaneers then let their top-ranked defence finish the job.

The San Francisco quarterback, Jeff Garcia, was hounded and harried into a series of mistakes as the 49ers struggled in vain to generate the momentum that had produced a stunning second-half revival against the New York Giants a week earlier.

"Nothing seemed to go right today," said Garcia. "When the first quarter didn't go the way we wanted, we started to try to force things and be urgent, but it never did us any good."

The defeat has led to speculation that the 49ers are poised to part company with their coach, Steve Mariucci: he faces an uncomfortable discussion about his future with senior management later today.

For the Buccaneers, however, the road to Super Bowl XXXVII now takes a disturbing detour through Philadelphia, where they will have to make history. Not only have Tampa Bay never won a play-off game away from home, they have also been eliminated in each of the last two seasons by the Eagles.

NFL RESULTS AND FIXTURES

AFC Divisional Play-off: Oakland 30 New York Jets 10. Conference Championship: Oakland v Tennessee (Sun 19 Jan, 23.30 GMT).

NFC Divisional Play-off: Tampa Bay 31 San Francisco 6. Conference Championship: Philadelphia v Tampa Bay (Sun 19 Jan, 20.00 GMT).

Super Bowl XXXVII: 26 Jan (San Diego, 23.00 GMT).

title: Harris: LeBeau made right call: listening to players
content:
Players play and coaches coach, but Steelers defensive captain James Farrior and his teammates had seen enough Sunday night.

No more sitting back and taking it.

Enough with Baltimore Ravens quarterback Joe Flacco picking the secondary clean like a leftover Thanksgiving turkey. If the Steelers were going to win the AFC North, someone was going to have to take a bullet for the rest of the team.

Someone was going to have to tell defensive coordinator Dick LeBeau to call more blitzes.

Who better than Farrior? He's one of four co-captains and old enough (35) not to care what people think.

It wasn't easy for a popular veteran like Farrior to make suggestions to his coach, especially during a game. To Farrior, though, the alternative of losing was too unflattering to consider.

"We definitely felt we could get some good pressure. We just had to get the right calls in,'' Farrior said in the afterglow of the Steelers' gritty 13-10 win.

"That's what our guys were preaching on the sideline to the coaches. If we were gonna go down, we were gonna go down blitzing. We definitely didn't want to sit back. Coach LeBeau listened to us.''

In the first half, Flacco had his way with the Steelers. He navigated the longest scoring drive of his NFL career -- 92 yards. He completed passes of 61 and 67 yards and was 9 of 14 for 179 yards and a touchdown with a 131.5 passer rating.

Other than a first-quarter sack by defensive end Ziggy Hood, Flacco was rarely under duress. He was on his way to another score and a career game when momentum shifted.

On third-and-15 from the Steelers 32 in the second quarter, cornerback Ike Taylor caught Baltimore with a surprise blitz and sacked Flacco for an 11-yard loss. The play knocked Baltimore out of scoring position. Instead of trailing 14-0 or even 10-0 at halftime, the Steelers faced only a 7-0 deficit.

In the second half, the tables turned. The Steelers had their way with Flacco, who was 8 of 19 for 87 yards with a 65.8 passer rating, mainly due to a better pass rush making it difficult for him to step into his throws.

With LeBeau dealing out blitzes like a blackjack dealer shuffles cards, the Steelers overwhelmed Flacco with pressure.

"When you dial up the blitz, it's usually hit or miss,'' Taylor said. "We hit (Sunday) night.''

That's a gamble outside linebacker James Harrison wants to take more often.

"I'm of the feeling if you're gonna kill me, kill me at what I like to do and that's blitz,'' said Harrison, the Steelers' sack leader this season with 10.

"In earlier weeks, we were dropping in coverage (instead of blitzing). At the end of the Buffalo game last week, we dropped a whole lot more. This game we brought a lot more pressure than we did in the first (Baltimore) game.''

Flacco threw the decisive 18-yard touchdown pass to T.J. Houshmandzadeh with 34 seconds left in the Ravens' 17-14 win over the Steelers on Oct. 3. The Steelers didn't blitz on the play, and Flacco was free to throw without pressure.

Two months later, the Steelers defense didn't repeat that mistake when Flacco faced second-and-5 at the Baltimore 43 late in the fourth quarter.

Trailing 10-6, the Steelers' defense needed to make a big play or lose any realistic chance of winning the division.

Strong safety Troy Polamalu delivered, sacking Flacco from the blind side and forcing a fumble that led to the Steelers' winning touchdown.

"We felt like we could disrupt, get in Flacco's face, if they gave us an opportunity,'' said Farrior, who recorded a team-high seven tackles and sacked Flacco in the fourth quarter.

"We don't question Coach LeBeau and the decisions he makes. Sometimes we just try to throw something out there and maybe he'll listen. Everybody has their own idea what we should be doing, but he's the guy.''

A smart guy, to be sure. Great coaches aren't too big to listen to their players. To his credit, LeBeau listened.

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Times staff writer Tom Jones looks back at the weekend in the NFL and ahead to this weekend.

THREE BEST PERFORMANCES

K Matt Bryant, Bucs

Unless one has experienced what Bryant and his family went through last week, it's impossible to describe or even begin to understand the grief they have gone through. Forget that Bryant was 3-for-3 on field goals for nine points - the difference in the Bucs' 30-21 victory over the Packers - the fact that he even showed up a day after burying his 3-month-old son, Tryson, was remarkable and inspirational.

QB Brett Favre, Jets

It looked liked 1998 all over again with Favre slinging the ball all over the field. Hard to believe he had never thrown for six touchdowns in a game before. He has now, after scorching the Cardinals in a 56-35 Jets victory. Hard to say if Favre will eventually get the Jets into the playoffs, but days like Sunday make the whole offseason soap opera and acquisition worth it to Gang Green.

Bucs defense

Speaking of looking like 1998 all over again, the Bucs defense treated Packers QB Aaron Rodgerslike a dog's chew toy. The defense had three sacks, three interceptions and a fumble recovery returned for a touchdown. Moreover, it allowed just 14 points, and those scores came, really, on two big plays, not long drives. It really did look like the classic Monte Kiffin defense.

THREE WORST PERFORMANCES

Jets secondary

All the buzz in the Big Apple is Jets QB Brett Favre, and it was Favre's performance that masked how dreadful the Jets secondary was Sunday. Arizona's 85-year-old quarterback, Kurt Warner, threw for 472 yards and two scores as the Cardinals racked up 35 points. True, most of Arizona's damage came after the Jets took a 34-0 lead, but still ... 472 yards? How many would he have had if the Cards didn't fumble the ball away four times?

QB Ryan Fitzpatrick, Bengals

This guy went to Harvard, and you would think he would be smart enough to turn down an offer to be the Bengals' starting quarterback. That's a joke, of course, but how bad are the Bengals? They are now 0-4, and even Fitzpatrick's wits couldn't help Sunday. He threw three picks as the Bengals lost to the cross-state rival Browns, who hadn't won a game.

Referee Ed Hochuli

Poor guy. A few weeks after costing the Chargers a victory against the Broncos with a bum call, Hochuli made another so-so call Sunday. He flagged the Panthers' Julius Peppers for roughing the passer on an apparent helmet-to-helmet hit on Falcons QB Matt Ryan. Replays appeared to show Peppers' shoulder made contact first. What made it so controversial is the penalty erased an interception for a touchdown. The good news is the call didn't cost Carolina the game. The bad news is these things usually run in threes, so Hochuli might have another bad call coming.

THREE THINGS THAT STRUCK US

Jets S Eric Smith needs longer suspension

The NFL sent a message by suspending Smith for one game. But the league should have sent a much stronger message by suspending him for three or four games. Smith's vicious head-to-head hit on Arizona WR Anquan Boldin is the type of thing the league must crack down on. Thankfully, Boldin wasn't injured more severely.

The NFL is just plain bonkers

The Patriots were beaten by the Dolphins last week. The Chiefs beat the Broncos on Sunday? The Colts are 1-2, and the Titans are 4-0, mostly without QB Vince Young. It just goes to show that the NFL has become as unpredictable as a feather in the wind. Anyone can win any time against any team. And, while some might think that makes the league great, we actually are turned off by it.

The Rams are a mess

The Rams, not surprisingly, fired coach Scott Linehan on Monday after starting the season 0-4. Did anyone really think the Rams would not be 0-4 at this point? Jim Haslett will take over, and things are not going to improve. Not because he's not a better coach than Linehan, but because he will be coaching the same talent - or lack of talent - that Linehan coached.

THREE TEAMS ON THE RISE

Washington Redskins

Where did this team come from? After losing to the Giants in the opener, the Redskins have rallied for three consecutive victories, and none was sweeter than Sunday when they won the final game in their bitter rivalry with the Cowboys at Texas Stadium. The key continues to be QB Jason Campbell, who has thrown no picks in four games.

Buffalo Bills

Actually, we're just lukewarm on this team. Yes, the Bills are 4-0 for the first time since 1992, but they have piled up those victories against teams with a combined record of 4-11. Plus, they struggled to beat Oakland and were in trouble for a while Sunday against the hapless Rams. Still, 4-0 is 4-0.

Jacksonville Jaguars

We thought the Jags might be 4-0 at this point. They easily could be 0-4. They're 2-2 and still don't look quite like the team we thought they would be. But they're starting to find ways to win games, even if that means needing final-play clutch kicks from Josh Scobee.

THREE TEAMS ON THE DECLINE

Denver Broncos

The Broncos are 3-1, but Sunday's loss came to the lowly Chiefs, and it wasn't even close - 33-19. Then you get to thinking about the Broncos. They got a gift in that game against the Chargers on a referee's bad call. Then they beat New Orleans, 34-32, after Saints K Martin Gramatica missed a 43-yard field goal with less than two minutes left, and that was after the Saints failed on a two-point conversion. This team was a smidge away from being 1-3.

Minnesota Vikings

Dang, we were sure the Vikings were going to be good this season. They started 1-2, but we thought they were better than that. They had a chance to go on the road Sunday and win a game against a good team with a great record. Instead, they got their hats handed to them by the Titans, who remain undefeated.

Green Bay Packers

New QB Aaron Rodgersisn't necessarily the problem, but the Packers already lost two games after losing three all of last regular season. So far, they've beaten two below-average teams (Vikings and Lions) and lost to two better-than-average teams (Bucs, Cowboys). That screams of a team that looks to be headed to .500. And if Rodgers misses any significant time at all with this shoulder injury, the Packers are in big trouble.

THREE BIG GAMES THIS WEEKEND

Washington at Philadelphia

If the Redskins can win this game, that will give them road wins over Dallas and Philly and a big jump on the Eagles in the NFC East. The Eagles are a good team. But after the loss to Chicago, they need to start playing like they're a good team.

Pittsburgh at Jacksonville

Doesn't it seem like the Steelers play in Jacksonville every season? A swing game for both teams, meaning a game neither can really afford to lose. Might even be more important for the Jags. A loss puts them at 2-3 and possibly three games behind the Titans in the AFC South.

Tampa Bay at Denver

Who would've thought this would be a matchup of two 3-1 teams? The way Carolina is playing, the Bucs must keep pace. The Broncos can't give away any games now that it seems the Chargers are getting back to the team everyone thought they would be before the season.

title: Who's Next?;
content:
Bucs vs. Bills, Sunday, 1 p.m., Raymond James Stadium   Team: Buffalo Bills (7-4, tied for second in AFC East)   Coach: Wade Phillips (third year, 28-15; fifth year overall, 44-31)   Series: Bucs lead, 4-2 (first meeting since 1991; every game has been played in Tampa)  Strength: Defense, Moulds come through

The league's fifth-ranked defense, third against the run, has more than survived the off-season  loss of DE Bruce Smith, S Kurt Schultz and CB Thomas Smith. Younger players have come into their  own, especially ILB Sam Cowart, the league's leading tackler and one of the best young players in  the league.

OLB Keith Newman has responded well to his first year as a starter with five sacks, and trails  only DE Marcellus Wiley (six) for the team lead. OLB Sam Rogers also has five sacks. Second-year S  Keion Carpenter is among the league leaders with five interceptions.

The real strength of this 3-4 defense is at DT, where massive Ted Washington and Pat Williams  are among the league's best run stoppers. They are especially effective when paired side by side,  but there is little drop-off when Williams occasionally replaces Washington.

WR Eric Moulds leads the league in receptions with 76. He already has more than 1,000 yards  receiving and four TD receptions.

PK Steve Christie has made 20 of 22 field goal attempts, including four in the final minute or  OT to tie a game or win it.  Weakness: Coverage, QB situation cause problems

If the Bucs are ever going to return a kickoff for a TD - they've failed on 1,474 attempts - this  might be the week. Buffalo is the same team that allowed the famous Home Run Throwback play that  the Titans used to win in the playoffs last year, and the Bills still have trouble on kick  coverage.

They're last in the league in average opponent drive start, 33.8, and have allowed one kickoff  return TD.

Phillips hasn't backed off his pledge to stay with QB Rob Johnson, despite an injury that kept  him sidelined for four games. Once Johnson was healthy enough to play, he returned to the lineup  despite QB Doug Flutie's 3-1 record in his absence.

Anonymous comments from an offensive player in Sports Illustrated three weeks ago caused an  uproar, but the team seemed to rally behind Johnson last week in Kansas City and the turmoil  appears to have passed - at least on the surface.

Buffalo remains ranked in the bottom third (20th) in run offense, but the return of rookie  starting RB Sammy Morris from injury this week should help.  Recap: Win streak erases early frustration

The Bills' youth movement is represented artistically on the cover of the team's media guide,  where you'll find stylized photos of Johnson, Cowart, Moulds, Wiley and CB Antoine Winfield. The  movement created some early growing pains, as the team started 3-4 with a three-game losing streak  mixed in.

Since a 31-27 loss to Minnesota, Buffalo has defeated, in order, the Jets, Patriots, Bears and  Chiefs. Three of those victories came with Flutie at QB.

Last week's 21-17 victory at Arrowhead Stadium against the Chiefs was, perhaps, the most  impressive victory of the season for Buffalo. Even with relatively little offensive production,  Buffalo showed it had the fortitude to win in a hostile environment.  Outlook: After this week, AFC East foes loom

The Bills don't exactly have the easiest path remaining to the playoffs. This week's game  against Tampa Bay is a prelude to a trio of games against AFC East opponents - Miami at home,  Indianapolis on the road, New England at home.

Buffalo then has to travel across country to finish the season at Seattle. The Seahawks will be  going through the motions by then, and that game will more than likely mean a lot to the Bills.

The key game probably will be in Indianapolis. By then, it's likely that both teams will need to  win to solidify a playoff berth, and Buffalo must make up for the earlier loss to the Colts.

title: NFL / Pro Bowl picks snub the Titans
content:
The Tennessee Titans have a new incentive in their quest for an NFL title: no Pro Bowlers.

"I'm quite sure a lot of players on this team deserved it, but that's how it works," quarterback Steve McNair said after the AFC South leaders were ignored in Pro Bowl balloting announced yesterday. "We don't have any control over that. This is how it works. But it's a team thing now for us. To solve all this, we just got to go to the Super Bowl and win it."

The Eagles, leading the race for the NFC's top playoff seeding, had the most Pro Bowlers with seven, including three-fourths of their secondary. San Francisco and Miami, two other division leaders, each had six.

But the Titans, leading the AFC South at 9-5, were ignored in voting conducted in thirds: one-third by players, one-third by coaches, and one-third by fans on NFL.com.

"We feel we had players who had seasons worthy enough to be selected over there; we just go on," coach Jeff Fisher, a former Eagles assistant coach, said. "We're disappointed for Steve's sake. But hey, his play speaks for itself, and we have a lot of unfinished business."

In contrast to Tennessee, the expansion Houston Texans had two players chosen, and the 1-13 Cincinnati Bengals had one, fullback Lorenzo Neal, who used to play for the Titans. The other teams without players chosen were .500 or worse: Cleveland, Detroit and Arizona.

The selections cover two generations.

Oakland wide receiver Jerry Rice was chosen for the 13th time at age 40, the first time he has made it since 1999, when he played with the 49ers. Raiders safety Rod Woodson, 37, was chosen for the 11th time. San Diego linebacker Junior Seau made it for the 12th straight season.

"It's definitely a big accomplishment and it's something that I'm not going to take lightly," Seau said.

At the other end of the spectrum were 22-year-old Atlanta quarterback Michael Vick and 22-year-old Jeremy Shockey, the New York Giants' tight end and the only rookie chosen.

The St. Louis Rams, 6-8 after being preseason favorites in the NFC, have offensive tackle Orlando Pace and running back Marshall Faulk on the squad. Pace will miss his sixth game to injury this week and Faulk will miss his sixth straight start.

Yet Faulk was chosen ahead of the Giants' Tiki Barber, who is third in the NFC in rushing and leads in combined rushing and passing yardage. Barber was the first alternate at running back.

"It just shows when I am healthy and when I was out there, people watch and they take notice," Faulk said.

Among those overlooked were McNair, who has carried the team to eight wins in its last nine games, and New England QB Tom Brady, the Super Bowl MVP. McNair was the AFC's first alternate at quarterback.

But Drew Bledsoe, who was traded to Buffalo after being replaced by Brady, made the team. The two other AFC quarterbacks were Rich Gannon of Oakland and Peyton Manning of Indianapolis.

Manning's favorite target, record-setting Marvin Harrison, also made the AFC team. Harrison already has set a single-season NFL mark with 127 catches.

The three NFC quarterbacks are Vick, Green Bay's Brett Favre and San Francisco's Jeff Garcia.

Favre's selection was his seventh.

The two Texans chosen are veterans - defensive tackle Gary Walker and cornerback Aaron Glenn. Both were among the high-salaried players chosen in the expansion draft.

Miami's Ricky Williams, traded by New Orleans to make room in the backfield for Deuce McAllister, made the AFC team. McAllister made the NFC team.

Raiders. Oakland cut short its workout after a lightning bolt struck close to the team's practice facility.

Thunderstorms had been pounding Northern California and knocked out power in several Bay area suburbs.

Coach Bill Callahan called a halt to the rain-drenched workout after the lightning bolt sent players scurrying for cover.

Local weather forecasts call for continued rains through the week, with a possibility of carrying over into Sunday's game between the Raiders and Denver that could decide the AFC West. Oakland can clinch its third straight division title.

title: Browns, Steelers share forgettable NFL link
content:
It is a category that joins Steelers and Cleveland Browns as reluctant participants.

Cleveland quarterback Derek Anderson completed 2 of 17 passes for 23 yards last week, but he still led the Browns past the Buffalo Bills, 6-3. Anderson's 11.7 completion percentage was the second-worst in NFL history for a winning starting quarterback with at least 15 attempts.

The dubious list of three worst passing performances also has the Steelers featured prominently.

The lowliest performance in NFL history was produced by a Cleveland quarterback against the Steelers in 1970. The third-worst came from a Steelers quarterback against Cleveland four years later.

Coincidentally, the Browns and Steelers play this week at Heinz Field. If that's not enough, the last time Cleveland played in Pittsburgh, their quarterback, Bruce Gradkowski, completed 5 of 16 passes for 18 yards.

Let's look at the worst of the worst, which if history has its say, shows Anderson might not be around much longer in Cleveland:

Don Gault, Oct. 3, 1970 at Cleveland's Municipal Stadium

Browns starting quarterback Bill Nelsen was out due to an injury, resulting in 24-year-old Hofstra product Don Gault getting his first career start.

It would be his last career start.

Gault became one of the most irrelevant record-holders the NFL has ever seen. He completed 1 of 16 passes before getting pulled in favor of Mike Phipps. Although Gault completed only one pass, it was a good one, a 44-yard strike to receiver Chip Glass. Gault also threw two picks before being pulled at the half.

Phipps completed 3 of 5 passes for 86 yards and hit backup running back Reece Morrison on a 53-yard touchdown pass in the third quarter that proved to be the winning points.

Coincidentally, it was Terry Bradshaw's first career start for the Steelers.

Gault never had a chance to redeem himself. Nelsen returned the following week, throwing for 226 yards in a 30-27 win over Cincinnati. Gault would throw three more passes that year, completing one and never played again.

Terry Hanratty, Nov. 17, 1974 at Cleveland's Municipal Stadium

Bradshaw was sidelined with an injury, and replacement Terry Hanratty was quite awful despite the Steelers winning, 26-16.

Hanratty completed 2 of 15 passes for 63 yards and three interceptions before Chuck Noll finally brought in Joe Gilliam. Hanratty did throw a touchdown pass to Ron Shanklin in the first quarter.

Hanratty exited early in the fourth after the Steelers took a 23-16 lead on J.T. Thomas' 14-yard fumble return for a score.

Bradshaw returned the next week on a Monday night in New Orleans and the Steelers won, 28-7.

Hanratty threw five more passes in his Steelers career — with zero completions — before moving on to Tampa Bay, where he started just one game before retiring.

Which brings us back to Anderson. He already has a leg up on Hanratty and Gault because he will start this week.

"What he did last week is no indication or has no bearing on what he's going to do this week," Steelers coach Mike Tomlin said.

Anderson sure hopes so, too.

Putrid passing

Cleveland quarterback Derek Anderson completed 2 of 17 passes in a win over Buffalo last week, joining a not-so-elite list with a Steelers/Browns flavor. Here are the worst completion percentages by a quarterback in a win and what those QBs did the next week:

1970: Cleveland's Don Gault, 1 for 16, 15-6 win over Steelers

Next week: DNP, starter Bill Nelsen returned from injury

2009: Cleveland's Derek Anderson, 2 for 17, 6-3 win over Buffalo

Next week: Sunday against Steelers

1974: Steelers' Terry Hanratty, 2 for 15, 26-16 win over Browns

Next week: DNP, starter Terry Bradshaw returned from injury

SCOTT BROWN'S POWER POLL

TOP FIVE

Giants (5-0): If Eli Manning stays healthy, they may be team to beat in the NFC.

Vikings (5-0): Can further validate themselves with win over Ravens Sunday.

Colts (5-0): Note to first-year coach Jim Caldwell: it won't always be this easy.

Saints (4-0): Can move to the top of the rankings with a road win over the Giants on Sunday.

Broncos (5-0): Denver looks legit after beating a quality opponent in New England.

BOTTOM FIVE

Titans (0-5): Time for Tennessee to find out if Vince Young is the quarterback of the future.

Buccaneers (0-5): Head coach Raheem Morris may be only 33, but he has to be aging fast.

Chiefs (0-5): Made Miles Austin look like Jerry Rice in OT loss to Cowboys.

Raiders (1-4): A complete joke — on and off the field.

Rams (0-5): At least they were competitive against the Vikings.

title: NFL NOTEBOOK: Cardinals fire Tobin; QBs continue to fall
content:
Vince Tobin did the virtually impossible two years ago --- he led the Arizona Cardinals to a playoff win.

But in the two years since, the woebegone franchise has returned to its home in the NFL's murky depths. That brought about Tobin's dismissal Monday, a day after the team lost 48-7 to Dallas.

Defensive coordinator Dave McGinnis will be the interim coach for the remainder of the season.CONSTITUTION

"I'm very sad to see Coach Tobin go," quarterback Jake Plummer said, "but at the same time I'm excited in a way to see Coach McGinnis come in and bring some new philosophies and some things that might help this team."

Tobin's record was 8-15 after a 9-7 playoff season in 1998. The playoff victory that season was the first in the franchise's 51-year history.

With a vote for a $ 331 million stadium proposal in Maricopa County coming up in two weeks, the franchise hoped for a turnaround this season. If the vote fails, the Cardinals could make its third move, possibly to San Antonio. The team began in Chicago before moving to St. Louis and then Phoenix.

Quarterback circus

At least three teams will have new quarterbacks come Sunday. The biggest name on the injured list is St. Louis' Kurt Warner. Last year's regular season and Super Bowl MVP broke the little finger of his right (throwing) hand in the Rams' first loss of the season Sunday.

Coach Mike Martz said Warner will miss four to six weeks. Trent Green, whose season-ending knee injury last preseason opened the door for Warner and his storybook season, will start.

Baltimore coach Brian Billick finally yanked starter Tony Banks. In a span of 49 consecutive possessions and just a little over four games, Banks had failed to lead the Ravens to a touchdown. Unfortunately for the Ravens, the replacement, Trent Dilfer, didn't do much better Sunday. He was 7-for-13 for 58 yards and didn't lead the team to a touchdown, either.

"Clearly, we have got to do something to change the impetus," Billick said.

Chicago's Cade McNown might be out for as much as a month after a probable separation of his left (throwing) shoulder in the Bears' loss to Philadelphia two days ago. Jim Miller, whom many fans wanted as McNown's replacement before the injury, gets the call. Miller, who had not taken any practice reps with the first team all season, led the Bears to three field goals but was 14- for-34 for 128 yards.

It's possible there could be changes in Pittsburgh, Seattle and San Diego, as well. Steelers quarterback Kordell Stewart came in for Kent Graham again Sunday, and Pittsburgh won. Stewart has played a larger role in three of the Steelers' four wins. Pittsburgh plays at Baltimore next week. The team has been less willing to play Stewart at home, where he has been booed.

In Seattle, Brock Huard, who took the starting job from Jon Kitna two weeks ago, suffered a concussion in Sunday's loss to Oakland, and then later in the game Kitna got a concussion as well. Kitna finished the game, but the status for both is uncertain.

In San Diego, coach Mike Riley has started Ryan Leaf, Moses Moreno and Jim Harbaugh. Leaf, who sprained his right wrist Sept. 24, first said he wouldn't be able to play next Sunday against Oakland, but then changed his mind. Harbaugh started last Sunday game for the 0-7 Chargers.

Smith on injured reserve

Chuck Smith, one of the big names the Carolina Panthers added to their defensive line this year, was placed on injured reserve because of an ailing right knee.

The move ends the season for Smith, who played in only two games for the Panthers (3-4) after signing a five-year, $ 21.75 million free agent contract in February.

Smith, 30, who had 10 sacks with the Falcons last year, had one tackle and no sacks this season.

Also Monday, Carolina's struggling offensive line was weakened further when doctors found a stress fracture in Clarence Jones' leg that will sideline the left tackle for at least four weeks.

In Jacksonville, right tackle Zach Wiegert was lost for the year with a torn anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee.

In Nashville, coach Jeff Fisher said Eddie George will be questionable for the Titans' game Monday against the Washington Redskins with a right knee sprain.

In Indianapolis, linebacker Rob Morris will miss the rest of the season with a ruptured patella tendon.

No longer No. 1

Sunday wasn't a total loss for the Falcons. New England punter Lee Johnson, whose team fell to Indianapolis, passed ex-Falcon offensive lineman Mike Kenn as the losingest player in NFL history. Johnson, a 16-year NFL veteran who played 11 years for the Bengals, has now been on the losing side in 147 games, one more than Kenn.
