"I think (congratulations) are appropriate," said Kenn, the Fulton County Commission chairman. "All records are made to be broken, and this was surely one of them."

Kenn played 17 seasons, all with the Falcons, and made five Pro Bowls. He was at the game Sunday when the team celebrated its 35th anniversary by introducing more than 100 former players to the Georgia Dome crowd at halftime.

"It was great," Kenn said. "I saw a lot of friends I really hadn't seen in a long time."

Dillon's day

Some notes from the 278-yard rushing day by Cincinnati running back Corey Dillon, who broke Walter Payton's single-game rushing total by 3 yards Sunday against Denver.

He only needed 22 carries.

Fourteen of them totaled 13 yards. So eight carries went for 265 yards, a 33.1 yards per carry average. "That's video game stuff," Bengals linebacker Takeo Spikes said.

Cincinnati lost its first six games, was shut out three times, and Dillon had run for a total of 323 yards.

Dillon's performance came against a defense that was completely selling out against the run. Cincinnati quarterbacks Akili Smith and Scott Mitchell completed 2-of-4 passes --- both in the first quarter --- to tie the team record for fewest completions in a game.

Mitchell said the Bengals ran the same three or four running plays throughout the game.

"It was the traditional 50 yards, 40 yards and a cloud of dust," Mitchell said.

''He literally scared the defensive backs," Bengals offensive tackle Willie Anderson said. "They did not want to hit him. It was a joke. It was like watching a big kid running against the other kids in pee wee football.''

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

title: This report card says Smith's performance is MVP material
content:
With the halfway point of the NFL season upon us, I thought this would be a good time to dish out some mid-season awards.  

There's a lot of ground to cover, so let's not waste any time:  

MVP  

Nominees: Rod Smith, Terrell Owens, Curtis Martin, Priest Holmes, Peyton Manning, Jeff Garcia.  

Most of the time, it would be hard for me to justify selecting a wide receiver for the MVP of a fantasy league. But Denver's Rod Smith has been unbelievable.  

When Ed McCaffrey was lost for the season, I wrote Smith could explode or disappear with defenses keyed to stop him. I figured the latter would happen and the Broncos would have a hard time getting him the ball.  

Well, Smith's gotten the ball 67 times for 849 yards and 8 TDs. He's ranked as the No. 1 WR in both of my leagues. Rarely has a player made opposing fantasy coaches cringe more than when they see they're going up against Smith.  

Top running back  

Nominees: LaDainian Tomlinson, Curtis Martin, Priest Holmes.  

This is a close call between Martin and (gulp!) Holmes. But my nod goes to Martin for his consistency in finding the end zone like no other time in his career. The Jets' tailback leads the NFL with 804 rushing yards and is second to Tomlinson in rushing TDs with 7.  

Top quarterback  

Nominees: Peyton Manning, Jeff Garcia, Daunte Culpepper, Brett Favre, Kurt Warner.  

This is a tough one, but after looking at the numbers in terms of fantasy points, Manning squeaks out the award over Garcia (13 TDs, 1,706 yards). The Colts' young gun has been wildly inconsistent, but is still tied for second with 13 touchdowns and is second behind Warner with 1,824 passing yards.  

There may be some out there scratching their heads over why Culpepper (8 passing TDs) is nominated. But it's his 5 rushing touchdowns that helped vault him to No. 3 and No. 1 in my two leagues.  

Biggest surprises:  

(Number in parentheses is average ranking at player's position in my two leagues).  

- Brett Favre (5.5). Packers' QB leads the NFL with 15 TD passes.  

- Tom Brady (12.5). Filling in nicely for injured Drew Bledsoe, all of his points have come in the last four weeks. In that time, Brady's scored the second-most fantasy points among QBs.  

- Priest Holmes (2.0). Where in the world did this guy come from? He's second in the NFL in rushing yards (711) and leads the league in yards per rush (5.3).  

- Shaun Alexander (7.0). The gifted tailback out of Alabama has really taken advantage of Ricky Watters' injury. The question is, what happens when Watters is healthy in a few weeks? Coach Mike Holmgren has hinted Watters will be back in the starter's role despite Alexander's 492 yards rushing and 6 TDs in just five games.  

- Anthony Thomas (10.0). The A-Train came out of nowhere and is busting through defenses with reckless abandon. He could challenge for most rushing yards in the NFC before the season's over.  

- Troy Brown (5.5). Believe it or not, the Patriots' receiver isn't even taken in one of my leagues. He's second in the NFL in receptions (53) and receiving yards (678), and he has a respectable 4 TDs.  

- Kevin Johnson (5.5). The Browns' receiver leads his team with 33 catches and 5 scores.  

- Bubba Franks (2.0). Six TDs? For a tight end with nine games remaining? Unreal.  

Biggest duds  

- Elvis Grbac (27.5). The loss of running back Jamal Lewis has been disastrous to Grbac. Injuries haven't helped either.  

- Derrick Alexander (64.0). Speaking of injuries, is Alexander ever going to get healthy? He was one of the top fantasy receivers last year, but can't even seem to get on the field now.  

- Keyshawn Johnson (48.5). Leading the NFC in receptions is fine and dandy. But where the heck are the touchdowns?!?!?!  

- Eric Moulds (38.0). Buffalo's struggles have hit Moulds particularly hard.  

- Randy Moss (41.5). Two touchdowns? Brutal.  

- Eddie George (47.0). One touchdown? Really brutal.  

- Duce Staley (54.0). The injury bug has kept Staley off the field much of the year.  

- Fred Taylor (90.0). Boy do I feel sorry for those of you who took Fragile Freddy in the first round.  

Good bets  

- Packers QB Brett Favre and WR Antonio Freeman vs. Bears. Favre's on pace for 30-plus TDs, and he should be able to exploit a Bears defense that stonewalls running backs, but can't generate the pass rush needed to defuse Favre.  

- Bears RB Anthony Thomas vs. Green Bay. Don't worry about the Packers NFC-leading defense. Thomas will find a way into the end zone.  

- Dolphins QB Jay Fiedler and WR Oronde Gadsen vs. Indianapolis. Fiedler's been playing better lately, and Gadsen's just plain due.  

- Rams WR Az Hakim vs. Carolina. Hakim is still undrafted in one of my leagues and wasn't picked until Week 5 in the other. He's a valuable guy on a high-scoring team. Don't be afraid to play him.  

- Chiefs RB Priest Holmes vs. New York Jets. Holmes (181 yards last week) may shred the AFC's worst run defense (150 yards per game).  

- Patriots WR Troy Brown vs. Buffalo. One of the most underrated wideouts in the league, Brown should be a staple in your starting lineup.  

- Bills QB Rob Johnson vs. New England. Looking for a modest 2- TD day out of Johnson.  

- Bengals RB Corey Dillon vs. Jacksonville. The Jaguars (2-5) figure to be losing confidence and if the Bengals (4-3) win, they're right in the playoff picture. Dillon will run often Sunday.  

- Chargers QB Doug Flutie vs. Denver. Coming off a rough Monday night loss, the Broncos will struggle against the resurgent Chargers.  

- Eagles QB Donovan McNabb vs. Minnesota. The circus that has become the Minnesota Vikings will not be able to handle the explosive McNabb.  

- Raiders QB Rich Gannon vs. Seattle. Gannon ripped apart the Seahawks in the first meeting between these teams (3 TDs). Expect more of the same.  

- Seahawks WR Darrell Jackson vs. Oakland. If CB Charles Woodson can't play for the Raiders, Jackson should break free for a score.  

- Time to pick some kickers for an added twist. I'll go with Dallas' Tim Seder, the Giants' Morten Andersen and Ravens' Matt Stover for Good Bets.  

Bad bets  

- Colts QB Peyton Manning vs. Miami. Not that I'd sit him, but I won't be surprised if Manning finishes with just 1 TD.  

- Chiefs TE Tony Gonzalez and QB Trent Green vs. New York Jets. Gonzalez's rough season continues, and he'll finish with 50-60 receiving yards. Don't expect much from the erratic Green, either.  

- Browns RB James Jackson, QB Tim Couch and WR Kevin Johnson vs. Pittsburgh. Cleveland's likely still reeling from blowing a 14- point lead to the Bears last week. Watch for the Steelers to hold the Browns to 2 field goals.  

- Broncos QB Brian Griese and WR Rod Smith. A gutsy call? Sure. But I've got a bad feeling about the Broncos this week.  

- Vikings WR Randy Moss vs. Philadelphia. Moss' struggles will continue against a physical Eagles secondary.  

- Titans RB Eddie George vs. Baltimore. George meets his nemesis in the Ravens. He'll finish with 30-55 rushing yards and no scores.  

- Time for some Bad Bet kickers - the Vikings' Gary Anderson, Seahawks' Rian Lindell, Packers' Ryan Longwell and Bears' Paul Edinger.  

If you have any questions or comments you may e-mail Dietz at jdietz@@dailyherald.com

title: Under the Microscope ...' Key Eagles in Sunday's game
content:
After a review of the game tape, here's a look at three key Eagles in Sunday's 36-31 loss to the New York Giants.

Jon RunyanAfter years of dealing with Michael Strahan,the Eagles' veteran offensive tackle has a new adversary in Giants defensive end Justin Tuck, who entered the game ranked sixth in the league with 81/2 sacks.

Neither Tuck nor the Giants, who led the NFC with 30 sacks, managed to drop quarterback Donovan McNabb in this game, although New York won the battle at the line of scrimmage against the run game, holding Brian Westbrook to 26 yards on 13 carries.

Runyan, however, was not part of the problem in the running game. In fact, he played an outstanding game. Tuck's only pressure came on the Eagles' first offensive play, when he got around Runyan and forced McNabb to throw the ball away.

On the next play, Runyan ran over cornerback Aaron Ross, helping pave the way for DeSean Jackson's 9-yard touchdown run out of the Wildcat formation. Correll Buckhalter had a nice block on that play, taking out safety Michael Johnson.

After that, Runyan did a solid job of blocking, but nothing spectacular on a night when the Giants made Westbrook pretty much disappear.

Stewart Bradley It was a fascinating night for Bradley, who seemed to be in on every play. He was credited with 14 tackles, but some of them came after big gains by a New York running game that averaged almost 5 yards per carry.

There were more than a few times when Giants center Shaun O'Hara and guard Rich Seubert got the best of Bradley. O'Hara steamrolled Bradley on a 23-yard run by Ahmad Bradshaw early in the fourth quarter, but the Eagles' middle linebacker also had his moments.

Bradley was the victim of a terrible facemask call late in the third quarter that was actually a tremendous play. After getting great penetration into the Giants' backfield, he pulled down Brandon Jacobs for no gain on a third-and-1 play, but was flagged for a personal-foul facemask penalty.

Referee Terry McAulay announced that Bradley grasped the facemask. He didn't. He made a clean tackle and the Giants should have been punting the football. Instead, they went on to kick a field goal. Amazingly, this was the same officiating crew that worked the game down in Dallas earlier this season and missed the Cowboys' Jason Hatcher's blatant facemask of Westbrook.

Compare Bradley's tackle of Jacobs to the one New York's giant running back made on Mike Patterson at the end of the 21-yard interception return in the first quarter and you'll notice only one difference: Jacobs was not flagged.

Asante Samuel Part of the reason the Eagles acquired Asante Samuel was for games like this one and the free-agent cornerback did a decent job in his first NFC East clash with the Giants.

Samuel spent much of the game going against Plaxico Burress, but he was not covering the Giants' star receiver on a first-quarter touchdown throw from Eli Manning. That was Burress' only catch of the game. Amani Toomer, however, did beat Samuel a few times in the first half.

Late in the first quarter, Samuel showed press coverage on Toomer, then backed off at the snap. Toomer took advantage of the extra cushion and cut in for a 20-yard gain on a second-and-3 play. A few plays later, Toomer beat Samuel again for a 10-yard catch. Samuel badly missed a tackle after a check-down pass to Derrick Ward late in the first quarter, allowing the Giants' running back to pick up an additional 10 yards for a 14-yard gain.

The veteran cornerback kept the Giants' third-quarter touchdown drive alive with a foolish illegal-contact penalty against Burress. Despite having coverage help, Samuel shoved Burress beyond five yards, giving the Giants a first down on a third-and-5 play. New York made the Eagles pay by scoring a touchdown five plays later.

Contact staff writer Bob Brookover at 215-854-2577 or bbrookover@phillynews.com.

title: Jets aren't great, but they mesh with Edwards
content:
At this point, the question is what will the other team bring to the table? What else is there to quarrel about?

The hotter team? Jets. The best motivator? Herm Edwards. The team with a quarterback? As of yesterday, the Jets had two ready to go: Vinny Testaverde and Chad Pennington.

Listening to all the Donovan McNabb critics, you'd wonder whether the Eagles even had a quarterback.

The Jets have Pennington, bubbling with anticipation, and Testaverde, who appears resigned to writing his own epitaph sometime during Sunday's game at Lincoln Financial Field.

The Jets are 2-4 with a coach who appears to be too loquacious for his own good. They may have a running back whose star appears to be descending; a defense that Eagles coach Andy Reid could rush for triple digits against; and far too many contributors wearing Redskins uniforms these days.

As Week 8 of this NFL season approaches, which team would you rather be?

A team with two straight wins? A coach that could give Tony Robbins a run for his money? A starting quarterback, returning from injury, that has star written all over him?

Or the Eagles, a team that is relying on what they hope was a season-saving punt return, that couldn't muster more than seven points on offense despite three turnovers and help from a defense that spent the afternoon putting the Giants on lockdown.

"[The Eagles] are a good football team and a physical football team on both sides of the football," Edwards told reporters on Wednesday. "They have a very good defense and don't allow a lot of points."

Edwards forgot to add that the beloved Iggles are also polite, well-mannered, and eat three square meals a day.

The next time the Eagles offense clicks into an impressive performance on a Sunday, it will be the first.

As of right now, these Iggles rank dead last in the NFL in both passing and total offense. Their defense, solid against the run, was ranked 28th against the pass until Bobby Taylor returned to the lineup last week.

This from a team that is 3-3 and still in the hunt for the NFC East title.

The 2-4 Jets come in with a coach whose job could be in jeopardy, but it seems Mr. Edwards is doing just fine.

Edwards, coaching in a gorgeous mosaic known for its intolerance of failure as much as for its affectionate embrace of diversity, brings the Jets to Lincoln Financial Field this weekend as a team that ranks 27th in total offense, 29th in rushing offense, 30th in rushing defense and 31st in scoring average. He's a few weeks removed from practically daring ownership to fire him if they didn't like the way he was doing his job.

And still, he is loved.

Can Edwards coach? Presumably, he can. But what matters most is that you don't hear a bunch of New Yorkers questioning his creativity, his motivational tactics and his willingness to change.

While a potential quarterback controversy is brewing, it appears to be only in the minds of everyone else. Testaverde, the veteran starter of 17 seasons who doesn't want to surrender his spot, is still willing to do so for the sake of team chemistry and everyone else is echoing the "whatever-it-takes" mantra.

Add it all up and it translates into faith. Faith from fans who know Edwards' history. Faith from the players who recognize his results-oriented approach.

In a game played by two teams with identical signatures right now - both recognized for performing solidly on defense and special teams while the offenses have been anemic - Edwards could very well be the difference.

Testaverde may get benched. Pennington, out all season with a broken left wrist, may be too rusty and unconditioned to have a significant impact. Curtis Martin may appear a bit old and worn in the backfield. There may be hope for the Eagles. Who knows, Brian Westbrook may save the Birds one more time.

"They are 3-3 and they are still in the hunt in their devision," Edwards said. "They are gaining confidence."

The Jets never lost theirs. Despite losing contributors such as receiver Laverneues Coles and kicker John Hall in the offseason and starting 2003 at 0-4, Edwards continues to prove that on the rare occasion, it's not purely about how schemes and personnel mesh.

Sometimes it is as simple as players meshing with the leader who assembled them.

Contact columnist Stephen A. Smith at 215-854-5846 or ssmith@phillynews.com.

title: Call search party: ground game MIA
content:
There is no such thing as a good loss, and although there are different levels of defeat -- none acceptable -- bad losses can be chronicled quite easily. And that was a bad loss.

"That one hurt," Chargers General Manager A.J. Smith says. "It really, really hurt."

Losing in Green Bay on Sunday was especially painful, because unlike the wood shedding in New England the week before, the Chargers -- despite their dumb mental mistakes, penalties, poor tackling, inability to finish and their failure once again to get tailback LaDainian Tomlinson off of his perplexing snide -- were in position to win the football game.

That they didn't win doesn't signal the end, a popular notion in scattered neighborhoods around here. They're 1-2, one game behind overrated Denver in the AFC West. To count them out pre-October would be foolish, although some folks don't mind being foolish when it comes to the Bolts. At least they don't have a coach tearing one of his own player's ACL.

"Would I be excited if we were 3-0?" Smith asks. "It would be nice, but what would it mean, except to say we're 3-0? I worry about everything, all the time, but I'm not (ticked). We'd all like perfection, but I feel we have everything in place.

"I'm extremely disappointed, sure. Last year, we were eight points from being undefeated. And what did we get out of it? Nothing. This is a new year, and I like the players and the coaches and the approach. I expect us to be competitive down the stretch. When the season ends, I'd like very much for the Chargers to be in the postseason."

Sounds good. But, if this team can't find a way to run the ball, it's not playing in January.

We all know coach Norv Turner made his bones as an offensive coordinator, but he loves the run, and his Chargers have run like me. In other words, poorly.

As one former NFL coach tells me: "Norv isn't just a good offensive coach, he's a great offensive coach. His offensive line has been horse(bleep). He's run the ball everywhere he's been."

Up to now. The same coaches run the same offensive line. There shouldn't be a problem there. LT, when asked if this team can win consistently without running the ball, says: "We haven't been able to run the ball in three games and we're 1-2. So there's the answer to your question."

As history professor Sam Kinison said to Rodney Dangerfield in "Back to School": "Good answer."

But, as we speak, there is no good answer to the running game. It sure doesn't appear as though Tomlinson has lost anything athletically. When he gets the ball in space, he's the same LT -- we saw it on that superbly designed screen for a score in Green Bay -- but space is hard to come by when you're running in a pantry.

It is true the Chargers have not tackled well, nor have they been able to pressure the passer, even when the QBs haven't tried the short stuff. It is true that, back-to-back, they've faced two great quarterbacks, Tom Brady, the best in the business, and Brett Favre, who once held the title and still isn't day-old bread. But, when you can control the football with the run, those passers will have fewer opportunities to force your defensive backs to make tackles.

Damn the eight-man fronts. Isaac Newton didn't invent them with apples. They're common. This isn't the first time we've seen teams try to gang up on LT.

"It's not going to stop; we're going to continue to see eight-man fronts," a frustrated Tomlinson, who is not in the best of moods these days, says. "We'll get it done. Yesterday (Sunday) it was close, to be honest with you. But we still didn't get it done."

Tomlinson has had times such as he's had the past three weeks, but they never should be strung together like this. Defenses aren't perfect. They make mistakes. The Chargers have been unable to find those mistakes.

"No question, we need to run the football," Turner says. "That's what this team has been built around."

Eight-man fronts can be had. The Chargers came close in Green Bay. Rivers was dead-on accurate most of the day and, as LT says, his side did score 24 points, which normally should be enough. But the NFL's greatest weapon is being eliminated, and the defense has allowed 69 points the past two weeks.

LT, who led the league in rushing in 2006 with 1,815 yards and set an NFL record with 31 touchdowns, has carried the ball 57 times for 130 yards, a 2.3 average. Stretched over a 16-game season, he would gain 693 yards. And the Chargers might be 5-11.

"Our offense starts with the run, and it will continue to be that way," Rivers says. "We have to keep plugging away at it. We're not going to surprise anyone with No. 21 back there."

No. 21 is LaDainian Tomlinson. And, if you put a decimal point between the 2 and the 1, that's about his yards per carry.

Totally, completely unacceptable.

title: At Texas, fortunes under Brown soar on, off field Longhorns coach keeps nice-guy image intact
content:
AUSTIN -- Four years ago today, as Texas football coach Mack Brown left a news conference, he was stopped by a fan wearing burnt orange. It was the day after the Longhorns won the school's first national title in 35 years.

"You have a free pass for the rest of your life at Texas," the fan told him. As Brown and his mentor, fabled coach Darrell Royal, walked away, Royal said, "Hell, that ain't true. You got six months before you better start winning again."

Soon, a sign went up in the Longhorns staff meeting room: "Complacency kills," it read.

Four years later and a few near misses along the way, Brown is back in the Bowl Championship Series title game as the No. 2 Longhorns face No. 1 Alabama on Thursday in Pasadena, Calif. (8 p.m. ET, ABC)

In an age when college coaches quickly are dubbed geniuses before the ink on their contracts dries, at a moment when coaches' misbehavior dominates the headlines, Brown has amassed the nation's best record (128-26) during his 12 years at Texas. All with little fanfare.

"Some of those guys get blown out of proportion, and he doesn't get the credit he deserves," says assistant head coach Major Applewhite, who played quarterback for Brown.

For nine consecutive years, Texas has won at least 10 games. Only Bobby Bowden, who won at least 10 games during a 14-year period at Florida State, has a longer streak.

Still, ask most college football fans to name the best three coaches in the game, and Brown's name might not make some lists.

"I think it's like anything," Applewhite says. "The longer you're around, people take you for granted. There needs to be an appreciation."

A turnaround at Texas

With Bowden retired, Brown's 20 consecutive winning seasons, including his stint at North Carolina, and 18 bowl games in a row are the longest streaks in the nation among active coaches.

Those with short memories forget how mediocre Texas was just before Brown's arrival. In the 12 seasons prior, three coaches amassed a 77-60-2 mark.

Last month, Texas amended Brown's contract and positioned him to become the nation's highest-paid coach. His guaranteed compensation will be $5.1 million in 2010. The school says Brown's pay comes from athletic revenue. Last month, Forbes ranked Texas the country's most valuable college football team at $119 million and said it made a profit of $59 million last season.

The school said football revenue has more than quadrupled under Brown, from $21.3 million in 1997 to $87.5 million in 2008, and season ticket sales rose from about 39,600 in 1997 to a school-record 84,000 this season.

In the coaching world, labels come easy. Alabama's Nick Saban: Intense. Southern California's Pete Carroll: Fun-loving. Ohio State's Jim Tressel: Conservative. Mack Brown? Ned Flanders nice.

"He is a nice guy," Longhorns quarterback Colt McCoy says. "What you see is what you get."

When McCoy visited Austin on his recruiting trip and met with Brown, the quarterback was swayed by this image: "His wife, Miss Sally, was sitting beside him. That made me feel that it is truly a family."

A story line heading into the title game has been the contrast between the head coaches' personalities. But Texas defensive coordinator Will Muschamp, who worked for Saban for five years, says perception isn't always reality. Saban isn't the killjoy he might appear to be, and Brown isn't all warm and fuzzy.

"Mack is very hard-nosed," Muschamp says. "He's tough, and he's hard on his coaching staff in a good way."

Sometimes, nice guys do finish first.

"Some people insinuated that I'm too nice to do this, but I always thought that was the greatest compliment anyone can give," Brown says. "I'd also like to think I'm tough and I'm disciplined."

Bowden said if he could play for any coach in the country right now, it would be Brown. "I think we are alike in a lot of ways in that we want to try to have as much fun as we can," Bowden told AOL FanHouse last month.

Brown used the word "enjoy" eight times this week in the first news conference, which took place, appropriately enough, at the happiest place on Earth.

"It's too hard to get here not to enjoy it, so we're going to enjoy the week, enjoy being here and try to play our best game. We still haven't played our best game," he said at Disneyland.

Says McCoy: "Coach Brown knows that when you're having fun and focused, you're at your best."

Learning to enjoy coaching

A few years ago, the opposite was true, as Brown and his team crumbled when the pressure rose. After the Longhorns lost to Washington State, finishing 10-3 in 2003, Brown was miserable.

"I think that after that loss, I felt that it was really cool to be the head coach at Texas and I wasn't enjoying it," he says. "I thought if you're going to work this hard for this long to get to this job and not enjoy it, then why did I do this?"

For Brown, this realization changed his perspective.

"We've played better since," he says. "I've had more fun and gotten my emotions under control."

Still, Brown entered the 2005 season unable to shake the "can't win the big one" label. That year, the Longhorns beat Oklahoma, ending a five-game skid in the series, and won the Big 12 title, Brown's first championship of any kind.

Once he loosened up, ignored his critics, plugged in his iPod and danced to his players' music, the Longhorns followed his lead. Most of the time.

"He's a horrible dancer," says Vince Young, whose MVP performance in the 2005 title game led Texas past USC 41-38. "But the fun that he has, that's the type of coach you want to play for."

Amid the pressure of this season, Brown has helped his players keep football in perspective.

"My job is to try to help these people grow and have a chance for a better life when they get out than they would have if they hadn't come," he says.

Before the Oklahoma game, Brown invited 16-year-old Corey Borner to speak to the team. Borner, paralyzed from the neck down after he was injured while making a tackle last spring in a scrimmage at DeSoto (Texas) High, told the Longhorns he was going to find a way to walk again, just like they were going to find a way to beat Oklahoma. After trailing at halftime, Texas won 16-13.

When Brown, 58, visited Borner last summer, Borner gave the coach a black wristband that read, "Find A Way." Brown wears it during games and sends Borner text messages before kickoffs.

"You hear our kids constantly say, 'Find a way,' " Brown says. "Corey's moving his arms now. He has some feeling in his legs. We got it pretty good, and if he's finding a way, we can find a way."

In the Big 12 championship against Nebraska, trailing 12-10 with 1:44 left and a spot in the national title game at stake, Brown says his team didn't blink.

"We didn't play well, but they thought they'd win," Brown says. The Longhorns won in the final second with a field goal. "After the game, the first thing Colt said was, 'Our job was to find a way.' "

The Longhorns had just played their worst game of the season. But in the locker room afterward they witnessed an even uglier scene. Surrounded by his players, moving to his own beat, Brown danced and danced.

"I can't dance a lick, but they're all laughing. It was ugly, it was bad, but they didn't care," Brown says. "They needed to laugh."

title: FAVRE'S CAST FAILING TO PROVIDE SUPPORT
content:
Brett Favre needs another cast, and not the type he wore on his badly sprained left foot two weeks ago.

In spite of all his injuries, including tendinitis in his throwing elbow and a perpetually swollen thumb, Favre has regained his form.

His supporting cast hasn't.

Last year, a bruised and swollen thumb contributed to a sub-par season in which Favre threw 23 interceptions and failed to lead Green Bay to the playoffs for the first time in seven seasons. This year, the trouble is his teammates.

Favre, who has started an NFL-record 137 consecutive games, is throwing the ball with as much zip as ever. On Monday night, he threw a bullet pass to Donald Driver that traveled 50 yards on a line.

Favre has even curtailed his gunslinger habits because he recognizes the Packers can no longer atone for his aggressive tendencies through superior talent and depth.

Departures and injuries have left the Packers a shell of the team that dominated in the late 1990s. The Packers (5-7) now enter games with little or no margin for error.

That's why, Favre said, he can't try to win games by himself anymore -except in the fourth quarter, when he regularly finds himself trying to rally his team from self-inflicted damage.

Nine of Favre's 13 interceptions have come in the fourth quarter.

Despair set in earlier than usual for the Packers in their 31-14 loss to Carolina on Monday night.

Green Bay found itself down, 14-0, early on to a makeshift Panthers lineup coming in at 4-7.

"At that point, it didn't seem like we could stop them," Favre said.

The Packers don't seem capable of stopping anybody once they fall behind.

Besides Favre, they have no playmakers. No Dorsey Levens, no Robert Brooks, no Mark Chmura, no Earl Dotson.

Favre has two rookie tackles protecting him in Chad Clifton and Mark Tauscher, and a rookie tight end (Bubba Franks) and first-time starter at halfback (Ahman Green) who are prone to drops and fumbles.

Coach Mike Sherman, who has preached a return to fundamental football since replacing Ray Rhodes last winter, is particularly piqued by the drops, which have come in bunches of up to a dozen a game.

Then, there's Antonio Freeman, a colossal disappointment since signing a $ 42 million deal 15 months ago that at the time made him the highest-paid receiver in the NFL.

Freeman entered this season intent on avoiding a repeat of last year, when he never got going after a long holdout and finished with 74 catches for 1,074 yards and six touchdowns -- a down year by his lofty standards.

But his numbers are down even more this season.

Although he has six touchdowns already, Freeman is averaging just 3.67 catches per game, down from 4.63 a year ago, and his per-game yardage is down from 67 to 54.

He committed a crucial fumble deep in Packers territory Monday night that led to a Carolina touchdown. Two weeks ago, he knocked the ball out of an official's hand for a 15-yard penalty that took the Packers out of field-goal range in a close game at Tampa and cost him a $ 10,000 fine.

And the defense hasn't been able to offset the offensive blunders.

The Packers' top two defenders, linebacker Brian Williams and cornerback Mike McKenzie, hardly have been heard from because of injuries.

They were counting on heart to carry them into the playoffs. But they somehow allowed a team that was hurting even worse to dismantle them in front of a national audience.

"This is a team loss," Sherman said. "There wasn't one part of our game that was good. Offense, defense, special teams."

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Cheers

Brett Favre can still school his old mentor once in a while.

Mike Holmgren led his unbeaten Seahawks into Green Bay with an overhauled defense that had proved opportunistic under new coordinator Ray Rhodes.

Instead, Favre led a turnover-free offense that converted six of nine third-down situations en route to a 35-13 rout.

"I just glanced at him and I could tell we were going to win the game," Packers G Mike Wahle said. "Brett played his butt off."

Favre had been intercepted seven times during Green Bay's 2-2 start, but he completed 19 of 25 passes for 185 yards and two TDs without a pick as the Packers remained two games behind Minnesota in the NFC North.

The Seahawks, who began the day with an NFL-best turnover ratio of plus-12, lost the ball twice without generating a takeaway.

"Unlike a lot of quarterbacks, Favre doesn't have to sit in the pocket and set his feet to throw the ball 400 miles an hour," Seattle LB Chad Brown said. "He's able to do it on one toe backing up."

His Chicago teammates call 380-pound RT Aaron Gibson "Eclipse" for a reason.

Gibson paved the way for much of the damage inflicted by RB Anthony Thomas as the Bears rallied for a 24-21 upset of the Raiders.

Following up on a 110-yard effort against Green Bay, Thomas rumbled for 123 yards on 22 carries Sunday.

"When you have a chance to give Anthony the ball 20 times, good things are bound to happen," Chicago coach Dick Jauron said.

Bears C Olin Kreutz said Gibson earned his nickname because opposing LBs "are going to get eclipsed." By the end of the game, the Raiders were tilting their defense to the left side in an effort to neutralize Thomas.

"The "A Train' was running hard today," Kreutz said. "And when he does that, the defense has to worry about him."

Jeers

Only two years removed from their status as the NFL's top-rated defense, the Steelers suddenly can't stop anyone.

The deterioration began late last season and Pittsburgh may have bottomed out during Sunday night's 33-13 home loss to Cleveland, which had been struggling offensively.

Two weeks ago, Tennessee's Steve McNair completed 15 of 16 pass attempts against the Steelers. Tim Couch of the Browns kept up the pace in the opening half, hitting on 16 of 17 passes while staking the Browns to a 23-10 advantage.

Pittsburgh fell to 2-3 and a beleaguered defense will be challenged by Denver, St. Louis and Seattle in upcoming weeks.

QB Tommy Maddox isn't helping the cause, either. He threw two picks as Cleveland limited the Steelers to 209 yards of offense.

"What do you think I'm going to do, jump off a cliff?" Maddox said. "It's five games into the season."

When Michael Vick returns from that fractured fibula, he may find himself on a fractured team.

It has all gone terribly wrong for the Falcons, who have dropped four consecutive games after opening with a win in Dallas. The defense, so opportunistic last season under coordinator Wade Phillips, can't make a play and the offensive line is porous.

The gravity of the situation is clear to everyone, including WR Peerless Price, who finally scored a TD with his new team.

"This is bad, four games in a row," Price said. "Now you're just trying to get a win, forget everything else. You've got to believe in what you're doing and you've got to believe in yourself."

Dunce Cap

The Broncos tempted fate one time too many and Scarface made them pay.

Denver led Kansas City 23-17 midway through the fourth quarter when Micah Knorr was brought on to punt from midfield.

Instead of angling for the sideline or booming his kick into the end zone, Knorr's 43-yard punt was fielded by Dante Hall.

"Where are you going to kick it?" was the weak excuse offered up by Denver coach Mike Shanahan after Hall's decisive 93-yard TD return, which was aided by at least one obvious clip.

After the game, Hall stood in front of an oversized poster of Al Pacino's title character and explained his attraction to the ultra-violent movie.

"I love "Scarface.' … He had a dream and he wasn't going to let anything stop him," Hall said.

DE Trevor Pryce was understandably frustrated after the Broncos outgained the Chiefs 469-261, only to lose on a late special teams breakdown.

"They're a good football team, but they're not world beaters," Pryce said, establishing an edgy tone for the Dec. 7 rematch at Invesco. "We played a lot harder than they did and we're probably a lot tougher team. But in the NFL, one play can turn the tide."

So can one bad decision.

Stat Of The Day

Teams with the edge in rushing attempts went 10-1 on Sunday, with the Broncos the lone exception.

Quote Of The Day

"We're underachieving right now. This team is not where we need to be." - Giants RB Tiki Barber on New York's 2-2 start.

(CHART) POWER RANKINGS
1. Colts. All questions have been answered.
2. Chiefs. Can a special teams player win league MVP honors?
3. Broncos. Pushed KC all over Arrowhead Stadium.
4. Vikings. Mike Tice looks like the NFL's Coach of the Year.
5. Panthers. Riding Stephen Davis into the playoffs.
6. Dolphins. Appears to be the class of the AFC East.
7. Bucs. What happened to homefield advantage?
8. Patriots. Running game starting to emerge.
9. Packers. Favre still has some magic left.
10. Seahawks. Defense exposed a bit in Lambeau.
11. Titans. This defense has been erratic.
12. Cowboys. Quincy Carter making all the right moves.
13. Redskins. Never count out Patrick Ramsey.
14. Bills. A healthy Travis Henry makes all the difference.
15. Giants. Continue to look poorly coached.
16. Rams. Could be primed for an October run.
17. Ravens. Kyle Boller needs to play well at Arizona.
18. Eagles. Suddenly finding ways to win.
19. Browns. Offense should subdue defenseless Raiders.
20. Steelers. Could give up 40 points at Denver.
21. 49ers. Don't be fooled by win vs. Detroit; something's missing.
22. Raiders. Won't recover from Windy City meltdown.
23. Texans. Coach Dom Capers building some confidence.
24. Jaguars. Byron Leftwich takes on stingy Miami defense.
25. Bengals. They're competitive in every game.
26. Falcons. They're not.
27. Bears. Anthony Thomas breaking tackles again.
28. Lions. Mariucci putting a young offense together.
29. Saints. Jim Haslett had better beat the Bears.
30. Jets. Perhaps a bye week will turn things around.
31. Cardinals. Looking at a lot of empty seats vs. Baltimore.
32. Chargers. Schottenheimer's second losing season as NFL coach.

title: T.O. & Garcia: Not too bad
content:
IT'S AN ITCH he can't seem to stop scratching. A scab he can't stop picking.

The more distance Terrell Owens puts in his rearview mirror between himself and former San Francisco 49ers teammate Jeff Garcia, the more obsessed he becomes with him.

Like a divorced couple that can't stop taking swipes at each other even after they've gone their separate ways, Owens can't seem to draw more than three or four breaths without complaining about his 5-year union with Garcia.

There he was again last week on HBO's "Inside the NFL,"

firing more below-the-belt shots at the 34-year-old quarterback. Said there's no telling what he could have accomplished the last 5 years if he had had a big, strong, cannon-armed quarterback like Donovan McNabb heaving the pigskin at him instead of the rag-armed Garcia. Said Randy Moss would have been eating his dust long ago if Garcia had been more accurate. "He threw the ball behind me, out of bounds; I left a lot of touchdowns on the field throughout the last 2 or 3 years," said Owens, whose Eagles meet Garcia's Browns Sunday in Cleveland.

In his recently released autobiography, "Catch This! Going Deep with the NFL's Sharpest Weapon," Owens claimed Garcia's lack of arm strength perpetually handcuffed the 49ers' offense.

"Our offense catered to his strengths," he wrote. "We couldn't do a lot of things the West Coast is designed for because of his weaknesses. I remember when Steve Young was there, Elvis Grbac, all those guys, we ran slants and square-outs religiously.

"When Jeff got there [in 1999], I think we were playing Atlanta and Jerry [Rice] was running a comeback or square or something. And the ball wasn't thrown on time. Jeff threw it and it got picked off for a touchdown. Ever since, we didn't run no more of those."

OK, enough already, T.O. Cease and desist with the Garcia bashing. Criticizing the Cleveland Browns quarterback for not having a McNabblike arm is like ripping Mare Winningham for not being as good-looking as Jessica Simpson.

I'm sure you have your reasons for continuing to stick pins in your Jeff Garcia voodoo doll and painting your years together as a hellish misadventure.

The only problem is it's not true. Until it all started to unravel last season, Owens and Garcia actually made some beautiful offensive music together.

In 67 regular-season games, they combined for 50 touchdowns, or 0.746 TDs per game. That's the second-best touchdowns-to-games ratio in the league, behind only the Colts' passing combination of Peyton Manning and Marvin Harrison, who came into this season with 68 TDs in 91 games, or 0.747 per game. The Vikings' Moss, who Owens is obsessed with almost as much as Garcia, came into this season with 40 touchdown catches from Daunte Culpepper in 60 games, or 0.666 per game.

The last four seasons, Owens had an NFL-best 51 touchdown catches. He was fourth in receptions during that period with 370, trailing only Harrison (448), Moss (376) and the Rams' Torry Holt (371). He also was fourth in receiving yards (5,265), behind Holt (5,996), Harrison (5,931) and Moss (5,649). What's he crying about?

He has convinced himself that he would be on Canton's doorstep right now if McNabb had been throwing home-run balls to him the last 5 years instead of the heavy dose of short-to-intermediate passes he was hauling in from Garcia. Makes for a nice fairy tale, but highly unlikely.

While I'm certainly not trying to suggest that Garcia is a better quarterback than the soon-to-be-28-year-old McNabb, the fact is, Garcia came into this season with better career numbers than McNabb in almost every pertinent statistical category, including completion percentage (61.4 to 57.0), passer rating (88.3 to 79.3), average yards per attempt (6.95 to 6.16) and touchdown percentage (one every 20.9 attempts to one every 24.3). McNabb held a slight edge in interception percentage (one every 43.2 attempts to Garcia's one every 42.1).

"Jeff never has been one of those guys who's going to beat you just horsing the ball down the field," one NFC scout said. "He's not going to stand in the pocket and throw, because he can't. He's a short guy. He has

to move to get lanes to throw the football. In San Francisco, he made a living throwing mostly short to intermediate routes, and was accurate doing it.

"Owens' comments about

Garcia's accuracy is revisionist history. Regardless of what T.O. says, most of [Garcia's] throws, except for last year when he really struggled, were on the money. He didn't make a receiver work nearly as hard for the football as McNabb did. But to his credit, McNabb is showing a lot more accuracy this year than I ever thought he would."

Garcia is what he is, which is an undersized guy (he's listed as 6-1, but is closer to 5-11) with an average arm, who is better at making plays on the run than in the pocket.

"When you look at him, he's certainly not physically impressive," an NFC personnel man said. "He looks like your fraternity brother. He looks like a guy you should be playing flag football with. There's nothing physically impressive about him other than he's got quick feet. He can be a little erratic, but that's his style. He's a little bit helter-skelter running around with the football. But that's his M.O. He's moving on every play. He's not standing there at all."

Garcia's preference for getting outside the pocket and making plays on the run apparently came as a complete shock to Browns coach Butch Davis, who must have gotten the scouting tape of Garcia mixed up with an ESPN Classic tape of Joe Namath before signing the quarterback in March. After the Browns' 34-23 loss to the Steelers 2 weeks ago, Davis suggested that Garcia was "skittish" in the pocket and needed to be more patient and let routes come open before he started scrambling.

"I can't believe he said that," an AFC pro personnel chief said. "If they watched the film of [Garcia] when he was with the 49ers, they should have known what they were getting.

"This is a guy who creates plays, who makes plays on the move. A guy that wants to find throwing lanes. A guy that uses his feet to get out of trouble or perceived trouble and find his primary or secondary receiver.

"Jeff is not a dropback passer. If that's what Butch Davis is looking for, then they made a huge mistake in getting this guy. He's always been about moving. He was able to be very successful

doing that in San Francisco. Then he gets to Cleveland and all of a sudden, they want him to be a different style quarterback. Well, guess what? He is what he is."

Garcia is struggling to find a comfort level in an offense that mistook him for somebody else. He's 22nd in the league in passing and has a 56.3 completion percentage for a 3-3 Browns team that has yet to win two games in a row.

Owens, meanwhile, has found bliss in Philadelphia with

McNabb and the 5-0 Eagles.

Yet, for some strange reason, Jeff Garcia still is never very

far from his mind.

Let it go, T.O. Let it go. *

Send e-mail to pdomo@aol.com

title: Rich Hofmann: McNabb looks to be on his way out
content:
THE WIDE RECEIVER boasts. The Web site director spits. The Eagles get ready for another shot at the Dallas Cowboys and that is the trivia of another week at the carnival. The real subtext, though, concerns the quarterback and the question: Is this the last go-round for Donovan McNabb, the man who has done everything a Philadelphia quarterback can do except for the one thing that everybody so desperately wants him to do?

Personally, I think this is it. I think the club made that clear without saying it by giving McNabb a raise during the offseason but declining to extend his contract past the 2010 season. If they win a Super Bowl, of course, things change. If they get to another NFC Championship Game, and McNabb plays great, things change. Short of that, though, I think it's over.

Eleven years is a long time for a quarterback in one city. Five years is a long time for a fan base to be locked in perpetual debate about a quarterback - and that is how long Eagles fans have been burying me in a digital avalanche every time I type the guy's name, pro or con. Ever since that achingly slow fourth-quarter drive in the Super Bowl, when McNabb either did or did not throw up in the huddle, the town has been thoroughly divided. During the flash points, you can read the exhaustion on everyone's face, including McNabb's. Another flash point is coming, maybe as soon as tomorrow night. When this season is over, there is nothing that anybody will say concerning McNabb that hasn't already been said a million times. The answer here is not obvious until you look at the contract. McNabb has trade value only as long as he is under contract - which means you trade him now or you have to extend him for X-number of years after the season, with a new signing bonus and plenty of color and pageantry (and a likely one-way ticket out the door for Kevin Kolb, whom you have previously identified as your future).

If the Eagles lose tomorrow, does anybody really think they are going to do that? Or that they should?

I have tried to be as objective as I could be over the years - admittedly, with varying success. Sometimes I hear the really asinine criticism and lean more toward being McNabb's defense attorney. Other times, when he's having one of those worm-burner days, I am too harsh. Overall, I think his body of work suggests McNabb is the best quarterback in the history of the franchise. But I also have seen this team lose four conference championship games, three as a favorite - and he hasn't played a great, complete game in any of them, or in the Super Bowl.

Sometimes it is all about the quarterback in the NFL. Other than an NHL goaltender, the quarterback has more to say about winning and losing than any single player in professional sports. He just does. (And he is paid accordingly.)

Here is a stat list. It is of 10 elite quarterbacks playing today. They all play well when they win - McNabb's rating of 92.3 is seventh out of 10 - and they all play somewhere between less-well and badly when they lose. This is a list of their passer ratings in the playoff games that they lose.

Kurt Warner . . . 91.6

Drew Brees . . . 91.5

Tom Brady . . . 79.0

Tony Romo . . . 75.8

Ben Roethlisberger . . . 74.2

Peyton Manning . . . 70.6

Philip Rivers . . . 69.0

Brett Favre . . . 68.0

Donovan McNabb . . . 66.4

Eli Manning . . . 48.4

It is the heart of the matter with McNabb - that is, that he is almost never one of their better players in defeat. (Before last year's NFC title game, his rating in playoff losses was 58.9.) And then there is the question of comebacks. The people at the great pro-football-reference.com have compiled the numbers for fourth-quarter playoff comeback wins by quarterbacks. We'll take the same 10 guys and line them up based upon how often they have brought their teams back in the postseason. Brady is otherworldly, with six comeback wins out of nine playoff games that he trailed in the fourth quarter.

Tom Brady . . . 6-for-9

Kurt Warner . . . 3-for-6

Eli Manning . . . 3-for-6

Ben Roethlisberger . . . 2-for-4

Brett Favre . . . 2-for-12

Philip Rivers . . . 1-for-3

Donovan McNabb . . . 1-for-7

Peyton Manning . . . 1-for-8

Drew Brees . . . 0-for-2

Tony Romo . . . 0-for-2

These are just the facts in one of sports' toughest proving grounds, the NFL postseason. McNabb has won 60 percent of his playoff starts, a good number. Those are all big games, not just the ones he loses. Of course, the point spread suggests he had what was perceived to be the better team in 67 percent of those games. And around it goes.

I wouldn't insult McNabb and say that Kolb is better today, or anything like that - but Kolb is intriguing. He would be a more natural stylistic fit for the offense, with not as big an arm as McNabb but with more of a willingness to get the ball out quickly. But, yes, you would be gambling on his development here. There is no denying that.

If he never gets a chance, though, you never find out. This is a very young Eagles team. If it were an old core, about to collapse, I could see the logic of McNabb staying to the end - but that isn't the reality. If you sense ambivalence here, that is because there is ambivalence here. It has been 11 years, after all. But if it ends quickly, it just feels like this is it. *

Send e-mail to hofmanr@phillynews.com,

or read his blog, The Idle Rich, at

http://go.philly.com/theidlerich.

For recent columns go to

http://go.philly.com/hofmann.

As public as their jobs are, it's rare to see Andy Reid and Donovan McNabb together off the football field.

The head coach and quarterback have the most important working relationship in the Eagles' hierarchy. The drafting of McNabb in April 1999 was Reid's first and most important major decision after being hired three months earlier. The development of McNabb is preeminent in everything Reid does in trying to turn the Eagles into a Super Bowl caliber team.

Yet their chance meeting in a hallway at the Eagles' complex Monday, in the presence of a couple of reporters, provided an unusual glimpse of them in a relaxed setting.

Reid was talking about McNabb when the 25-year-old quarterback came around the corner, strolling toward the nearby auditorium. McNabb realized he was the subject of the conversation and made one of his many silly faces. He looked both nonchalant and deeply curious about what was being said, then broke into a big smile when he caught Reid's eye.

As the quarterback disappeared into the auditorium, Reid chuckled appreciatively.

"He always seems to be laughing," Reid said, "but underneath that, he is such a great competitor."

And that's what Reid was trying to explain before McNabb walked in. A day earlier, McNabb had engineered two breathtaking, fourth-quarter drives to beat the rival New York Giants and claim the NFC East title.

It was a performance that fit what is fast becoming McNabb's modus operandi: No matter what happens in the early part of a game, he is capable of coming up with big plays at the end.

It's what Magic Johnson used to call "winning time," and it is more in keeping with the basketball player McNabb once was than a football player. But it's exciting, and it works.

Sunday's 24-21 win over the Giants, in a high-pressure, playoff-like game, bodes well for the Eagles as they prepare for the postseason. Playoff games tend to be tough and close, with one or two plays deciding them. A quarterback who is calm under pressure, and whose teammates believe in him, is invaluable in such games.

"If you can pinpoint one thing, it's that he keeps firing," Reid said. "Those are throwing situations, and he sees it and delivers without any hesitation. The tougher the situation, the less he hesitates, and that's a pretty good quality to have.

"A high percentage of that is innate."

But it's not all innate. Reid shed a little light on his approach to McNabb, and how that approach helps explain some of the play calling that has puzzled fans during the course of the season.

"He knows I trust him and I have confidence in him," Reid said. "You show a little bit of that with your play calling, too. If it's perceived to be a bad throw, and you come back with another throw, that's telling him right there that you still have confidence in him."

There are other reasons, of course. Reid's offense is based on throwing the ball, and he simply prefers to do that when he can. On top of that, it has become apparent that the running game is just not where the Eagles would like it to be, and Reid uses the short-passing game to compensate.

Duce Staley, coming off last year's foot injury and this year's shoulder problems, is simply more effective as a receiver right now. And rookie Correll Buckhalter, while looking lively at times, has fumbled enough for that to be an issue. He dropped a pitch from McNabb on Sunday and didn't carry the ball again.

Mostly, though, Reid is continuing the process of educating McNabb. The coach has said from the beginning that it's a four-year process. McNabb is near the end of his third season and second as a full-time starter.

"Teams have had a year to figure out how to rush him and how to put pressure on his throwing arm," Reid said. "That's what defensive coaches do. Now it's just a matter of learning to throw from all the different platforms - off-balance to the left, to the right. It's something you develop."

McNabb is well ahead of the gold standard - Green Bay's Brett Favre - at a comparable point in his development. In 1993, his second season as a full-time starter and third in the league, Favre's passer rating was 72.2. He threw 19 touchdowns and 24 interceptions that year.

McNabb's rating is 84.3. He has thrown 25 touchdown passes and just 12 interceptions.

More important, Favre's Packers were 9-7 in '93 and 9-7 in '94. They didn't reach the 11-win mark until 1995, Favre's fourth year as the starter. McNabb hit that mark last year and has a chance to equal it with a win Sunday in Tampa.

"For him to be considered one of the top quarterbacks in the National Football League at this point, that's a real compliment to him," Reid said.

Phil Sheridan's e-mail address is psheridan@phillynews.com.

title: FAULK AND LITTLE ARE MAKING IT EASY TO FIGURE THE ODDS
content:
NOW THAT THE NFL has officially released all the complicated formulas for figuring out the myriad NFL playoff possibilities, let me make this very easy for anyone who wants to calculate what needs to happen for the Rams to get into the postseason.

Charts? We don't need no stinkin' charts.

This is all you need to know about the Rams' possibilities for winning the NFC West and getting into Paul Tagliabue's Super Bowl Tournament.

Leonard Little is back.

Marshall Faulk is back.

Everything else just got a lot easier for the Rams after watching the two most talented men on this St. Louis roster prove on Sunday that they were both back at top speed and in scary Pro Bowl form.

"I'm tickled that we've got our guys, all of them," Mike Martz said on Monday afternoon. The Rams' boss had a crooked little smirk on his face that made him look a lot like a guy walking into an alley fight with two Rottweilers on a chain. "Marshall is back, Leonard is back. All our guys are back," he said, the smirk growing broader all the time.

Martz is walking into the December drive for the postseason with a team that is as healthy as it has been all year. The Rams' long and seemingly endless injury list is almost empty now. All the big-name, high-profile guys are healthy and ready to go. Jason Sehorn is as healthy as he's going to be. Adam Archuleta is back to playing like a human cannon ball. Damione Lewis is back in the middle of that defensive line, and as he rounds back into form, he ought to start plugging up a lot of those gaping running lanes. The Rams' season-long star-search for a legitimate bulldozer of a fullback has finally come up with a winner in Joey Goodspeed, and Marc Bulger seems to have broken out of his four-game funk.

While all of that is nice, none of this good news makes Martz grin as much as the sight of a healthy Faulk scooting through defenses, and a healthy Little exploding across the line of scrimmage on a Lawrence Taylor-like rampage.

The mark of superstars in any sport is whether they make everyone else around them better. When you look at the Rams, is there any question about how much more explosive Faulk makes this offense, or how much more cantankerous and aggressive Little allows this defense to be?

The Rams' offense becomes a lot more difficult to defend when Faulk is dashing through the line like a high-stepping drum major. On all three of his touchdowns against the Vikings, the closest contact any Minnesota defender had with him was a feeble snag at his jersey.

With Faulk running the way he is, safeties and linebackers have to account for him, which means that Torry Holt, Isaac Bruce, Dane Looker and Mike Furrey can raise hell in the secondary. The man even designs plays. Martz already told us that it was Faulk who came up with that Bruce-to-Looker pass play. "Every week he comes up to me on the practice field and says, 'Hey, what about this?'" said Martz. "They're all good plays, too."

Little hasn't yet come up with any new blitz packages for Lovie Smith, but he doesn't have to. How do you explain how this guy can miss a month of football and come back to the game even better than when he left?

Little makes things easier than ever for Smith, who called one of his most aggressive games ever, blitzing the daylights out of the Vikings. Little occupied so much of the blocking attention of the right side of Minnesota offense that it left gaping holes in the protection on the left side, which explains why Archuleta (two sacks) and Pisa Tinoisamoa (one sack) both rolled into Daunte Culpepper untouched.

I don't need a calculator to figure out the Rams' playoff possibilities. All I know is Little and Faulk are back. Go ahead, you do the math.

title: Cover-2
content:
How 'bout those Texans! The Houston Texans impressed people around the league on Sunday when they rallied from a 27-10 deficit on the road to beat the defenseless Washington Redskins and earn the first overtime win in franchise history.

"Once the old Texans got behind especially late in the game, they never seem to have the toughness, the resolve, the determination to keep playing," wrote John Clayton of ESPN.com. "But the Texans of 2010 are tough, they have the 'never give up' mentality and they now know they can win any game, anywhere, no matter what happens.

"The NFL's sleeping giant has always been the Texans, a talented team with questionable toughness and resolve to finish games. But the giant is awake and the rest of the league has taken notice."

Houston quarterback Matt Schaub, a graduate of West Chester East, passed for a franchise record 497 yards in the game and will get an immediate test this weekend when the Texans (2-0) host increasingly desperate Dallas (0-2).

Fast fact The unbeaten Miami Dolphins have gone through a stunning 13 starting quarterbacks since they last started 2-0 in 2002.

Playoff ponderings Only 57 of 120 playoff teams from 2000 to 2009 returned to the field the next season, and only 31 of the 76 division champions from the 2000s repeated the following season.

Given that history, an upheaval among the playoff field was to be expected. But this year's NFC crop is experiencing a very early shakeout.

Minnesota and Dallas are 0-2 and Arizona and the Eagles are 1-1. The other two playoff teams from a year ago were New Orleans and Green Bay, both 2-0. (The 2009 AFC playoff teams - Indianapolis, Cincinnati, San Diego, Baltimore, the Jets and New England - all are 1-1.)

Since 1990, only 22 of the 169 teams that started 0-2 made the playoffs. The Cowboys could still make it, as 9-7 might win the NFC East this year. But Minnesota is in big-time trouble.

In the Red Zone The Vikings lost to the Dolphins on Sunday because gray haired ole' Brett Favre, the league's best red-zone passer last season, went 1 for 5 in the red zone.

Dolphins quarterback Chad Henne, who had a touchdown on his one red-zone possession Sunday, was the league's worst last year.

Bengals frustrate Flacco In his last three games against the Cincinnati Bengals, Baltimore's Joe Flacco has thrown eight interceptions and the Ravens have lost all three. The graduate of Audubon High in Camden County threw a career-high four interceptions in Cincinnati's 15-10 victory on Sunday.

It's a stunning contrast to what Flacco's done against everyone else. He set a Ravens record with his 88.9 passer rating last year. But in the last three games against Cincinnati, his passer ratings have averaged 44.1.

Contact staff writer Don McKee at dmckee@phillynews.com.

This article contains information from the Associated Press.

title: Morris Daily Herald, Ill., Mark Johnson column
content:
Nov. 16--While winning five of their first eight games, the Bears rarely were impressive. Soft opposition, not strong play, was cited as the main reason for their solid start.

Three of Chicago's early wins -- over Detroit, Carolina and Buffalo -- were grind-it-out victories over bad teams. We thought the Bears had beaten a good team in Week 2, but the Cowboys went on to lose seven of their first eight games. Even the Bears' one victory over a team with a winning record -- Week 3 over the Packers -- was due more to the 392 penalties (that's an approximation) called on Green Bay than the play of the Bears.

It's tough to call a team with an inconsistent-at-best offense good for merely keeping its head above water against a cake schedule. For the Bears to earn that distinction, they needed some sort of a signature victory. Sunday they may have gotten one when they pummeled the Vikings 27-13 at Soldier Field.

If you're looking to be cynical about the win, you have plenty of ammunition available. You could question how hard the Vikings were playing for a coach (Brad Childress) many of them loathe. You could point out that, due to injuries and the absence of Randy Moss, Brett Favre was stuck throwing to a group of receivers that make Johnny Knox and Earl Bennett look like Jerry Rice and John Taylor. Oh, and you could also point out that the Vikings are 3-6 with the loss.

I'll grant you that the Vikings are playing mediocre football, but there aren't five teams in the NFC with more talent. Much as they dislike Childress, their dramatic comeback win over Arizona two weekends ago was proof they haven't quit. They're a veteran group that needed to win Sunday to have any real hope of capping Favre's supposed last season with a playoff berth. And the Bears kicked the snot out of them.

It wasn't a blowout, and most of the game was closer than the Bears' 14-point margin of victory suggests. But it's telling to just how good the Bears' defense was that I felt very comfortable most of the way, even though the lead didn't reach double digits until the fourth quarter. And even though the Bears were playing a Hall of Fame quarterback and a superstar running back (Adrian Peterson) who has destroyed them before.

Schematically the Bears did nothing dazzling. Favre commented afterward how the Bears basically stuck with their Cover-2 and dared Peterson to beat them. That the Bears still held Peterson to 3 yards a carry on the nose is a testament to how well Lance Briggs, Julius Peppers and Brian Urlacher played against the run.

What was especially encouraging about Sunday, however, had nothing to do with the defense. It was the fact that, for the first time in a loooooong time, the offense was almost as good. Aside from the one Bad Jay (Cutler) interception that the Bears' quarterback forced into all kinds of end zone, it was a good day for him and the offense.

Most encouraging above all else -- the overall strong play of Cutler, the production of the tight ends, Knox continuing his 1,000-yard pace -- was the play of, believe it or not, the offensive line. This marks two straight weeks where, with relatively rare exceptions, Cutler has had time to throw. The unit needs to run block better and cut down, big time, on its penalties (where was your head, Olin Kreutz?) but is actually making some progress.

What this all adds up to is a real basis for cautious optimism about the Bears' playoff chances. At 6-3, they're certainly on pace to get a bid, if not a first-round bye, in a conference that lacks dominant teams.

Of course the schedule gets a whole lot tougher from here on out. It starts with a difficult but winnable game against 5-4 Miami on Thursday night football. Four games against teams with winning records remain after that, and the only games left against losing teams are on the road. Making the playoffs remains tough for the Bears, but Sunday's win made it a lot more realistic a task.
