title: Some fans expect Bulger to be what Warner was
content:
So many things changed when Marc Bulger's last pass of a topsy-turvy afternoon got snatched by a San Francisco defensive back. The Rams' miracle march to victory was stopped, and the shock waves could be felt all the way in St. Louis, where Rams fans raged the rest of the night and into Monday.

The loss immediately became Marc Bulger's fault.

We heard the usual catcall: Bulger is no Kurt Warner.

Never mind that Bulger was attacked on all sides by a furious San Francisco pass rush and managed to complete 19 of 30 passes for 181 yards and two touchdowns after the 49ers jumped the Rams for a 28-9 lead. True, Bulger's last pass was behind Isaac Bruce, and it wasn't a good throw, but Bruce has made those type of catches many times before. And it's true that Bulger needs to have more cool-handed efficiency in the red zone; the Rams are letting too many TDs dissolve into field goals.

But the reaction to Sunday's 28-25 loss offered the latest look into Bulger's professional life in St. Louis. Unless Bulger wins the Super Bowl, unless he wins a couple of MVP awards, unless he plays at the extreme level of excellence achieved by Warner from 1999 through 2001, he'll never be fully accepted in this town.

And if Bulger and the Rams go to Arizona and lose to Warner and the Cardinals on Sunday, Bulger will lose even more status in St. Louis. Bulger's Warner burden will grow heavier.

Bulger needs to have a beer with Tony La Russa and soak in some friendly advice about what it's like to follow a legend. La Russa still lives with the ghost of Whitey Herzog hovering at Busch Stadium. And Warner's specter never drifts far from Bulger's place in the huddle.

"I could be in a situation where I'm not playing or the expectations aren't high," Bulger said. "But you've got to win a Super Bowl here. It's obvious if you want to be compared to that 1999 through 2001 team, you know what the goal is. You're going to have to win the Super Bowl, and anything besides that is not going to cut it. I can handle it. Really, it doesn't bother me."

Bulger is a very good quarterback who ranks third in yards per attempt and seventh in the passer efficiency rating among all NFL QBs since the start of the 2002 season. But that's not satisfactory to some who saw Warner average 292 yards and throw 98 touchdown passes from 1999-2001, with the Rams scoring 32 points a game.

This was spectacular, and abnormal. It may have been the greatest three-year run by a QB in NFL history. And no quarterback has come close to repeating Warner's three-year peak performance.

Not even Warner.

"Everybody expects me to be that quarterback, too," Warner said. "You don't get rid of it. Thank God you have those years that you do. It's the same way in my situation, and I know Marc is dealing with it. You have that standard even in my situation -- every year, every time you step on that football field, they expect you to perform at that type of level.

"And it's hard to do. It was a great run that we had. We had everything going, we had everything clicking, we were together for a long period of time. And it was one of those special, special periods.

"Yeah, I feel for Marc. But at the same time, I don't feel too bad for him because the guy's had nothing but success in the league. You can't get caught up with what the media says or what the fans want. The guy's played good football. That's why he's the starting quarterback there, that's why he's been to the Pro Bowl. He's an excellent quarterback, and has proven it year in and year out."

Here's what I don't understand: Why can't we appreciate Bulger and Warner? Both are classy guys, and there's no villain here. The Warner of 1999-2001 was a once-in-a-lifetime quarterback, but that time has passed. It's true, Bulger is no Warner. But Kurt Warner isn't Kurt Warner, either.

title: ACTION AVOIDS HAWK OPPORTUNITIES FOR BIG PLAYS HAVE BEEN RARE FOR THE LINEBACKER, BUT COACHES REMAIN PLEASED.
content:
A.J. Hawk knows it comes with the territory.

When you enter the NFL as the fifth pick in the draft, when you're viewed as the next face of the Green Bay Packers when (if?) Brett Favre finally retires, folks are going to expect a lot. And rightfully so.

"That's good. People had better have high expectations for me," the Packers' second-year will linebacker said. "Because whatever they expect of me, believe me, I expect a lot more. But I also understand my job. I understand what I'm doing here."

And what he's done through the Packers' first five games has been, well, uneventful. While he hasn't made any game-changing, glaring mistakes, he hasn't made any game-altering, great plays, either.

He heads into Sunday's game against the Washington Redskins at Lambeau Field with 41 tackles - third on the team behind middle linebacker Nick Barnett (51) and safety Atari Bigby (45) - and without a sack, interception, pass breakup or forced or recovered fumble.

Yet while those outside the building might wonder why a player coach Mike McCarthy called "a prime-time player in the National Football League" this offseason has had such a quiet start, Hawk's coaches are unconcerned.

"Pretty solid," linebackers coach Winston Moss said when asked to assess Hawk's season so far. "A lot more consistent than (his play at the beginning of) last year. He's off to a solid start. I don't think the opportunity has presented itself as much as probably he wants it to or I want it to, so you just have to be patient. Reporters or fans might expect more from him, but I'm fine."

The lack of opportunity, according to defensive coordinator Bob Sanders, is a function of what he's asked Hawk to do.

In the first three games - against the Philadelphia Eagles, New York Giants and San Diego Chargers - Hawk spent most of his time in pass coverage, against tight ends L.J. Smith, Jeremy Shockey and Antonio Gates, and saw Shockey and Gates get the best of him at times.

In Sunday night's loss to Chicago, Hawk made six tackles and was solid against the run, but once again, he didn't do anything extraordinary. He did make a savvy play by covering fullback Jason McKie in the flat late in the game, but it was for naught because safety Nick Collins incorrectly headed in that direction, too - allowing tight end Desmond Clark to get behind Collins and sam linebacker Brady Poppinga for the decisive touchdown.

"We tell everybody on the defense, 'We don't need you to do anything more than what your job is. If everybody does their job, we're in good shape.' And for the most part, A.J.'s done that," Sanders said. "He played physical, he was in pretty good position most of the night on all his run fits. The plays have to be at your spot for you to make them.

"Big plays, just like turnovers or sacks, they seem to come in droves. I'm sure there's certain plays he'd like to do over or have back. But he's done a nice job. And I think he'll continue to do that.

"I think his plays will come. If the plays are going somewhere else, it's hard to make them."

For his part, Hawk doesn't feel he's played poorly and believes Sanders is right about his opportunities coming. Last season, when Hawk led the team with 155 tackles while also registering 3 1/2 sacks, two interceptions and nine pass breakups, he had 42 tackles through five games, though he did have 1 1/2 sacks.

"I actually feel pretty good about where I'm at," said Hawk, who had seven games of double-digit tackles last season, five of which came after Week 5, including a 20-tackle game (16 solo) against Seattle.

"Different games are set up for different type things, and these first few games, I'd been asked to be in coverage a lot. And so I understand when I'm in coverage I don't want to be making tackles, because that means they're throwing the ball to my guy.

"It's not frustrating to me. It's my job, and I take pride in that. It's something where I understand our scheme, I understand the defense, and I understand that different things will come to me. I can't be out there pressing, trying to do things outside the scheme.

"Obviously I'd like to be in on every play. But I know that realistically, with what we're doing and how our scheme is (structured), especially with the tight ends we faced early in the year, that I'm going to be away from the ball, I'm going to be downfield in coverage. It's a long season. I'm not worried at all about where I'm at or where this defense is at."

Barnett charged

Linebacker Nick Barnett was charged Tuesday with two counts of disorderly conduct stemming from an altercation with two women in a bar this summer. Barnett, 26, was summoned to appear in Outagamie County Circuit Court on Dec. 11, according to the criminal complaint filed by special prosecutor Raymond Pelrine.

title: Fantasy market analysis could help in crazy year
content:
For those of you who follow the stock market on a daily basis, you know it's been a crazy year.

The housing market's in the tank, yet the Dow's up 200!

No, wait, oil's spiking and we're down 550 for the week!

The fed's lowering rates, and the Dow is now at an all-time high!

Sell! Buy! Sell, buy! Sell, sell, sell!

I don't know if it's just me, but the start of the fantasy football season seems to be following a similar path.

One week, a player or team looks great; the next, you wonder why he's on your team.

So in this column, I'm playing fantasy football broker (at no charge!), and will attempt to guide your team to fame and fortune.

Or at least a winning record.

Detroit RB Kevin Jones: $3 stock whose stock may be ready to explode. Let's face it: The Lions have almost no running game. Tatum Bell has averaged 27 rushing yards the past three weeks and doesn't seem to be a good fit in Mike Martz's offense. Jones, who was in jeopardy of missing the entire season because of injury, has yet to reach his potential as a fourth-year pro. But if he stays healthy in this offense, he could help a ton of fantasy teams the rest of the way.

Bucs RB Earnest Graham: $1 stock also ready to explode. Regular readers of this space know Graham was in last week's column. Now that Cadillac Williams is out for the season, Graham will split carries with Michael Pittman. But Graham will be the man on the goal line, meaning he could easily score 6-9 TDs the rest of the way.

The entire St. Louis and New Orleans offenses: Former $200 stocks in AOL-like tailspins. We all loved Marc Bulger and Drew Breeson draft day. Same with Steven Jackson, Reggie Bush, Torry Holt and Marques Colston. Now they're all bankrupting fantasy seasons. We can put a "sell" tag on them, but what are we going to get in return? A kicker or a defense, if we're lucky. If you can get something better, pull the trigger. Otherwise, get some replacements in a hurry because most of these guys aren't going to improve.

Giants defense: $0.50 stock that suddenly discovered a pass rush (and invented the Internet, apparently). Two weeks ago, the Giants couldn't stop a flag football team. Then, after holding the Redskins at bay, they sacked Donovan McNabb 12 times last week. Go figure. Give them another week or two - if the trend continues, feel free to pick up the Giants ... and be worried when your players face this unit.

Packers QB Brett Favre: Blue-chip company that was in the dumps, but new employees making it viable again. Favre never really went away. His team just did. Now he's surrounded by an incredible defense and a great group of receivers and tight ends. The 37-year- old wonder boy has 8 TD passes with 999 yards the past three games. He's become a viable fantasy starter once again.

Eagles QB Donovan McNabb: Speculative stock that figures to continue its wild swings. After getting burned by McNabb in the opening two weeks, his owners figured they'd be OK after a 4- touchdown, 381-yard day against the Lions. But then came last week when the Eagles managed a whopping 3 points against - of all teams - the Giants. McNabb's a tough one to figure the rest of the way, but I think you've got to keep playing him for a while and hope for the best.

Dolphins RB Ronnie Brown: Stock soaring. Where did this guy come from? The last two weeks, he has 246 rushing yards, 172 receiving and 4 TDs. That's vaulted him to No. 1 status in fantasy points among running backs in many leagues. Congrats if you own him.

Ravens and Bears defenses: Stocks plummeting. The Bears can improve their value if they get their core players back quickly. And, of course, they do still have Devin Hester. But it's Baltimore that's been the real disappointment. Through four games, the Ravens have just 6 sacks, 3 interceptions and 3 fumble recoveries. Worse still, they're allowing 22.5 points per game. It's still a unit to play, but when your players face the Ravens, you don't have to be as worried about a really bad matchup.

My teams: Despite a 30-point explosion by Tony Romo, the rest of my unit-league team decided to take day-long siestas and fell to 3- 1 after a 72-50 loss. In the regular league, I scored the most points among the 10 teams thanks to big days by Matt Hasselbeck, Joseph Addai and Santonio Holmes. I won that game 129-81 and improved to 3-1.

Good bets

- Packers QB Brett Favre vs. the Bears. Seventh among QBs in fantasy points - and tied for second since Week 2 - Favre has once again become an appealing option. He figures to have a field day against a franchise he seems to get a kick out of torturing.

- Redskins RB Clinton Portis vs. Detroit. If Portis (knee) doesn't play, just plug Ladell Betts' name in this good bet. The Lions can be had on the ground or through the air, but the Redskins are much better at pounding it out.

- Cardinals RB Edgerrin James vs. St. Louis. After running the ball just 31 times for 134 yards the past two weeks, James figures to go wild against a Rams defense that has allowed the Niners' Frank Gore and the Bucs' Earnest Graham to run for 2 TDs each.

- Browns QB Derek Anderson and WR Braylon Edwards vs. New England. Cleveland is going to fall behind. Way behind. And Anderson will throw, throw and throw some more.

- Texans RB Ahman Green vs. Miami. Boy, have the Dolphins gotten old in a hurry. This team can't stop anybody, as evidenced by the nearly 200 yards a game they are giving up on the ground.

- Panthers QB David Carr vs. New Orleans. Sleeper alert. Against a woeful Saints secondary, Carr will hit Steve Smith over and over ... and over again.

- Bills WR Lee Evans vs. Dallas. Ever think Lee Evans would be a super-sleeper? That's pretty sad, but I say he has a big game at home on Monday Night Football.

Bad Bets

- Bears RB Cedric Benson vs. Green Bay. The Packers are one of two teams (Minnesota is the other) that hasn't allowed a rushing touchdown this season.

- Seahawks RB Shaun Alexander and QB Matt Hasselbeck vs. Pittsburgh. Full disclosure: I hope I'm dead wrong with this prediction because I'm starting both of these guys in two separate leagues. But the Steelers are tough, and I'll take any points I can get.

- Steelers QB Ben Roetlisberger vs. Seattle. This game just reeks of a 13-10 final. The Seahawks have allowed just 2 passing TDs - and that includes a game against Carson Palmer and the Bengals. This defense is good.

- Rams WRs Isaac Bruce and Drew Bennett vs. Arizona. Bruce (hamstring) may not even play, so watch that.

But even if he does, most Rams are risky plays until further notice.

- Chargers QB Philip Rivers and WR Vincent Jackson vs. Denver. No quarterback has thrown for over 200 yards against the Broncos so far. Of course, three of them were J.P. Losman, Luke McCown and David Garrard (the other was Peyton Manning). Expect the Chargers' struggles to continue.

- Bills RB Marshawn Lynch vs. Dallas. The rookie will have a tough time of it against a tough Cowboys' defense.

jdietz@dailyherald.com

title: Garza expected back vs. Bills
content:
After playing hurt for three weeks and then undergoing arthroscopic knee surgery to remove torn cartilage and missing the past two games, 10-year veteran guard Roberto Garza is expected back for the next game, against the Bills on Nov. 7 in Toronto.

But coach Lovie Smith wouldn't say whether the five-year starter would get his job back at left guard when he returns to an offensive line desperate for some cohesion.

"Having another veteran guy in the mix, of course will help our football team," Smith said. "When Roberto gets back healthy, then we'll put him into the mix.

"Against Buffalo, the group we think gives us the best chance to win, that's the group that will be out there."

Former left tackle Chris Williams has started in Garza's spot the past two games, and he could move back to left tackle. That would allow Frank Omiyale to move back to right tackle, where J'Marcus Webb has started the past three games.

Edwin Williams, who has started the past three games at right guard, left Sunday's game with back stiffness. He was replaced by Lance Louis, who started the first four games at right guard before being sidelined with a knee injury.

Edwin Williams is expected to be fine by the Bills game.

OK with Jay:

Is Jay Cutler in danger of losing the team after he turned the ball over five times in the second half against the Redskins?

Coach Lovie Smith doesn't think so.

"I can understand why you would ask that question," Smith said. "You guys (the media) had a chance to talk to our team. I don't necessarily have to answer that, but what I would say that they would say is, ‘No.'

"Jay, of course, would like to have some of those throws back. There are so many things that all of us would like to do differently. Our team is just that, a team. We lost as a team.

"Everybody had a chance to make some plays. Maybe some had more opportunities. But we'll just tighten those things up and everyone involved will do a little bit more to correct some of those things."

Immovable object:

Those wondering why the quarterback sneak by Jay Cutler that resulted in his fumble was run at the Redskins' 350-pound Pro Bowl tackle Albert Haynesworth are still wondering.

"We didn't execute the way we needed to on that play," coach Lovie Smith said, "just say that."

Injury update:

Linebacker Lance Briggs (ankle), safety Major Wright (hamstring) and cornerback Zack Bowman (foot) are expected back after the bye week.

Briggs started against the Redskins but was removed after one series.

title: craig's list Mark makes his picks: No such thing as must-win in NFC
content:
Memo to all Vikings players, fans and hyperventilating radio personalities who insist the Vikings are playing the Packers in a "MUST-WIN!!" game on Sunday:

Um, your squad plays in the NFC. You know, the one that sent the 8-8 Giants to the playoffs last season. The one that crowned the 9-7 Seahawks champions of the West Division. The one whose 2006 playoff teams now sport a combined 8-10 record, including a 1-5 mark for the reigning conference finalists (Bears 1-2; Saints 0-3).

So relax. If the Vikings lose, yes, they'll officially stink. But cheer up. Not stinking isn't a requirement to make the postseason in the NFC.

GB -1 1/2 at MN: Packers by 7.

I mailed my apology to Brett Favre, but it was intercepted. (Just kidding. He's my MVP frontrunner, so step away from your e-mail, and nobody gets hurt.)

OAK +4 at MIA: Dolphins by 3.

I know it's only Friday. But the Dolphins have already sacked Daunte four times.

HOU -3 at ATL: Texans by 6.

To the person who keeps e-mailing me defending Joey Harrington: Mrs. Harrington, your little boy is still 0-3.

BAL -4 at CLE: Ravens by 3.

Even Brady Quinn now thinks the Browns can beat Notre Dame.

CHI -3 at DET: Bears by 4.

Anyone know of a good realtor in Chicago? Contact R. Grossman, Halas Hall, 1000 Football Dr., Lake Forest, IL, 60045.

STL +13 at DAL: Cowboys by 9.

T.O. is behaving so darn well, ESPN might have to become the 12-hour all-sports network.

NYJ -3 1/2 at BUF: Jets by 6.

Want to make Vikings fans feel better about their QB situation? Try these six words: Trent Edwards, starting quarterback, Buffalo Bills.

TB +3 at CAR: Panthers by 4.

Want to make Vikings fans feel worse about their QB situation? Try these seven words: Jeff Garcia, starting quarterback, Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

SEA -2 at SF: 49ers by 3.

Perhaps that Nate Burleson poison pill wouldn't have tasted so bad after all.

KC +11 1/2 at SD: Chargers by 14.

The loser has agreed to rehire Marty Schottenheimer for the remainder of the regular season.

DEN +9 1/2 at IND: Colts by 6.

Broncos coach Mike Shanahan is 3-0 vs. reigning Super Bowl champions. But you're right. He'll have to win this one without Brewster.

PHI -3 at NYG: Eagles by 6.

Yeah, Donovan McNabb had a perfect passer rating last week. But he did have those five incompletions. And I haven't seen a good soup commercial in, like, forever.

NE -7 at CIN: Patriots by 10.

The over/under for points: 52 1/2. The over/under for number of times Suzy Kolber will say "overcoming adversity" and/or "no one gave them a chance": 53 1/2.

UPSET SPECIAL

PIT -6 at ARI: Cardinals by 3.

Mike Tomlin is trying to be the first Steelers rookie coach to start 4-0. Cards coach and former Bill Cowher heir apparent Ken Whisenhunt is trying to go 2-2 without bending Steelers owner Dan Rooney into one of those balloon animals.

Last week's prediction: Vikings 13, Chiefs 10. Final: Chiefs 13, Vikings 10. Season record: 1-2

title: Injury bug strikes our f irst-half MVP
content:
It's that time of year again — time to dole out the midseason fantasy football awards.

Time to heap praise upon our studs and blast the duds.

So without further adieu ...

My first-half MVP award goes to none other than ...

(envelope, please) ...

ANTONIO GATES!

You could argue for Philip Rivers, Peyton Manningor Arian Foster, but Gates is blowing his peers away at his position and it's not even close. In standard scoring leagues, Gates has posted double-digit fantasy points EVERY WEEK but one. He's 12th and 15th overall in fantasy points in my two leagues.

That's unheard of for a tight end.

But now the bad news: Gates popped the plantar fascia in his right foot last week against the Titans. A foot specialist told the San Diego Union-Tribune that "it's going to be really tough to play with any proficiency this Sunday, unless they do a remarkable job anesthetizing him. ... This injury will make it difficult for him to push off and be explosive."

So pay attention this week to practice reports. Hopefully this won't be an issue that hinders Gates' production because to this point — with 663 yards and 9 TDs — it's been epic.

Surprise RB:

It would be easy to say Houston's Arian Foster (980 yards from scimmage, 8 TDs) should win this award. But it would also be inaccurate.

By draft day, Foster was going in the fourth, maybe fifth round as it became clear he had the talent to be a lead back on a high-scoring offense.

No, the award goes to Oakland's Darren McFadden, a mere afterthought on draft day who was generally taken in the 10th round or later. McFadden is fourth among RBs in fantasy points in just six games. He has 6 TDs and 685 yards from scrimmage.

And for his owners, dream matchups await in the fantasy playoffs when McFadden will destroy the Jaguars, Broncos and Colts in Weeks 14-16.

Biggest bust RB:

This is a dead heat between Carolina's DeAngelo Williams and San Diego's Ryan Mathews. I ranked them eighth and ninth, respectively, in August and they now are tied for 35th in one of my leagues. I still believe Mathews can produce RB2-like numbers in the second half, but that's not what you drafted him for in the second round.

Surprise QB:

Any chance the Bears can rescind the Cutler trade? With no running game, Denver's Kyle Orton has gone from a fantasy afterthought to must-start material.

With a stunning five 300-yard games and 12 TD passes, Orton ranks third — THIRD! — among fantasy quarterbacks. His owners will especially enjoy upcoming games against St. Louis (Week 12), Arizona (Week 14) and Houston (Week 16).

Biggest bust QBs:

Like RB Foster above, it would be easy to just give this to Brett Favre (ranked ninth in preseason; in 29th now). But I believe you have to look at where players were drafted and go from there. While owners hoped Favre could continue to post starter-worthy numbers, it was certainly no slam dunk.

But when it came to Matt Schaub (fourth in preseason) and Tom Brady (fifth), owners knew they'd be getting huge numbers every week. Only it hasn't worked out that way. Schaub is a pathetic 15th among QBs in fantasy points, while Brady — with 1 TD pass in each of the last four weeks — is 11th.

Brady figures to struggle the rest of the way, and Schaub may too if Andre Johnson's ankle doesn't heal and Houston's O-line continues to pass block like the Bears' does.

Surprise WR:

He had 660 yards and 3 TDs over the past four seasons. He was owned in 1 percent of cbssportsline.com leagues in Week 1. And he's a former Bear who has never sniffed a 1,000-yard season.

But somehow, Brandon Lloyd has exploded this year to lead the league in receiving yards (878) and 100-yard games (5) while hauling in 4 touchdowns. In one of my leagues, he is 40 points ahead of Miles Austin, 44 ahead of Andre Johnson, 49 ahead of Reggie Wayne and has doubled Marques Colston's point total.

Stunning, indeed.

Biggest bust WR:

No contest here. Randy Moss (second in preseason, 33rd now) has just 22 catches for 313 yards and 5 TDs.

And now he's on the run-first Titans, which should be good for Chris Johnson but not necessarily for Moss owners.

It's all truly pathetic stuff, Randy. Truly.

My teams:

Terrell Owens' second TD — the one that bounced off a DB's hands — saved my day in the unit league (6-2) where I won a nail-biter, 44 1/2-42. In the regular league, I improved to 5-3 with a 123-113 victory thanks to big days by Adrian Peterson, Darren McFadden and Antonio Gates.

Good bets

* Colts TE Jacob Tamme at Philadelphia. More than his 6 catches for 84 yards last week, I was particularly impressed with how Peyton Manningkept looking Tamme's way. Manning uses the tight end — and he'll make Tamme a household fantasy name before long.

* Texans WR Jacoby Jones vs. San Diego. Sleeper pick here with Andre Johnson banged up. If you are weak at your third receiver slot or have a flex play, consider Jones, who I think scores this week.

* Bears RB Matt Forte vs. Buffalo. Mike Martz says the Bears will run more. Really. Trust him. Call me crazy, but I do, especially against the Bills. Forte will get over 110 yards from scrimmage.

* Packers WR James Jones vs. Dallas. Donald Driver is hurt, which means Jones is set to torch the pathetic Cowboys secondary.

* Chargers RBs Ryan Mathews and Mike Tolbert at Houston. Did you see what Mike Hart (12-84) did to the Texans on Monday night Football? I did and it compelled me to start Mathews and Tolbert in one of my leagues.

* New England's defense at Cleveland. Here's one of those "spot starts" you can give the Pats D.

* Steelers QB Ben Roethlisberger at Cincinnati. Big Ben was a disappointment in New Orleans, but he'll get well against a Bengals squad that's allowed 61 points the last two weeks.

Bad bets

* Bills QB Ryan Fitzpatrick vs. the Bears. Rod Marinelli's defense has allowed a scant 4 passing TDs ... and it's getting Lance Briggs back. Sit Fitz if you can.

* Texans QB Matt Schaub vs. San Diego. Until further notice, I don't trust Schaub.

* Giants RB Ahmad Bradshaw at Seattle. OK, he's not a truly awful bet, but if I'm in a TD-heavy league, I'm looking elsewhere. Prediction: Brandon Jacobs outscores Bradshaw this week.

* Seahawks RB Marshawn Lynch vs. the Giants. Nine carries for 7 yards last week, Marshawn? And now you get the third-best run defense? Welcome to the bench, bud.

* Bengals WR Terrell Owens and RB Cedric Benson vs. Pittsburgh. The Steelers give up like 9 yards a game rushing. Seriously. Nine. OK, really it's 59 and that's a huge reason Benson's sitting for me, and Tolbert and Mathews are in.

* Dolphins WR Davone Bess at Baltimore. After giving up 505 yards in their last game, I say the Ravens come out possessed.

jdietz@dailyherald.com

title: Culpepper progresses by dealing with errors
content:
The timing of the call might be questioned, but not the outcome _ or what the outcome revealed _ about Daunte Culpepper.

     There were two minutes left in a game. The Vikings led 6-0 and had the ball, first-and-10 at the Miami 15-yard line. As expected, a running play came in from the sidelines.

   But that changed.

     "Two minutes left and we need one more first down and the game's over with," receiver Cris Carter said. "And they put nine people at the line of scrimmage, so he audibled to a pass."

     Carter smiled at the memory.

     "That's more like [Brian] Billick," Carter said, referring to the team's former offensive coordinator. "That's the way Brian thought. They could have brought seven and left the safety back. But they didn't. And Daunte audibled."

     The result? Touchdown, Randy Moss. The lesson? A few mistakes aren't going to keep Culpepper down. It was, perhaps, another in a line of moments helping to define Culpepper.

     Back in training camp, on the Vikings' day trip to River Falls to scrimmage Kansas City, the well-traveled Warren Moon was asked about Culpepper.

     Moon, the former Viking who at that point had passed for nearly 28 more miles in the NFL than Culpepper, said all the right things. But then he finished with this observation: You'd learn more about him after something went wrong, Moon said, by how he faced adversity.

     So far, quite good.

     In Sunday's victory over Miami, Culpepper threw three interceptions, two of which were probably his fault. Another drive in Miami territory ended when the ball was stripped from his hands near the end of the first half, during which he threw a pick, fumbled and was sacked three times.

     Here's how he responded: by completing 14 of 23 passes for 210 yards and a touchdown in the second half. Since training camp began, people have affixed names like big, fast, strong or talented to Culpepper. Here's another:

     Unflappable.

     "One of the great things Daunte did was that he was never deterred," Vikings coach Dennis Green said. "If he didn't have a good play one time, he'd come back and have another good play. He never backs down."

     That's one of the most important things a quarterback can have, perhaps more than knowing the playbook: The ability to make a mistake without bruising his confidence.

     In that way, Culpepper reminds Vikings offensive coordinator Sherman Lewis of another second-year, first-time starting quarterback he worked with a few years ago. A guy named Brett Favre, in Green Bay. Back in 1992, Favre was thrust into the starting role early in the season. He had his ups and downs, but he never lost his nerve.

     "That's just confidence, all confidence," Lewis said. "[Culpepper has] got great confidence in himself, and he has great confidence in the guys around him. I believe he can make the great throw, or that he has great receivers out there, and if he puts it near them they're going to make the play. That's great. That's very unusual for a young quarterback."

     That attitude will be crucial Sunday, when Culpepper makes his first road start and plays his first regular-season game in front of a hostile crowd.

     "I know going into the game, throughout the course of the game, some things may go bad," Culpepper said. "Throughout the history of the game, the good ones can make good things happen after something bad happens. That's just how I want to be. . . . Be able to bounce back."

     This is not to say mistakes don't bother him. They do. But, perhaps with a little help from his friends, he is able to learn from them rather than linger on them.

     Easy to say, harder to do. Young quarterbacks often go into a shell after having a pass or two picked off. That Culpepper won't back down is encouraging.

     "Yeah, but most of the quarterbacks who do that are marginal anyway," Carter said. "I mean, Daunte is a phenomenal athlete. He's not . . . he's not like everybody. I mean, Elway, Marino, all those guys struggled early in their career. Brett Favre, all of them at some point. But they had something about them that was different."

     Part of the difference, for Culpepper, is his surrounding cast.

     "We have a very mature team," he said. "It has helped me out, having Cris and Randy, and Robert Smith. I know I have to be like them, because I'm running the offense. I have to worry about the next play, not what happened on the last one. I do that and I won't go into a shell."

     Carter even helps Culpepper change plays at the line of scrimmage, something Dolphins linebacker Zach Thomas noted after Sunday's game.

     "I know the coverages, and I know when we have certain things on the game plan, and I watch so much film," Carter said. "I mean, I just knew what they were going to do. And I would call out and tell him. He's looking at it this way, I'm looking at it from a different angle, we just have two perspectives on it. The more information you can get, the better off you're going to be."

.

     Staff writer Kent Youngblood can be contacted at kyoungblood@startribune.com

.

The class of '99

    Daunte Culpepper is one of six second-year quarterbacks starting in the NFL this season.

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Times staff writer Tom Jones looks back at the weekend in the NFL and ahead to this weekend.

Three best performances
RB Brian Westbrook, Eagles He looked like an All-Pro and, in turn, the Eagles looked like a contender again. Westbrook rushed for 167 yards and two touchdowns on 22 carries and added another 42 yards on six receptions as the Eagles kept pace in the ultracompetitive NFC East. QB Chad Pennington, Dolphins Who would you rather have right now, Pennington or Brett Favre? The Jets let go of Pennington to make room for Favre, but Pennington has found his career again in Miami. On Sunday, while Favre was throwing three picks in a much-too-close victory against Kansas City, Pennington was throwing for 314 yards and a entry in Miami's victory against a Bills team that was 5-1 coming in. WR Santana Moss, Redskins Maybe the best "Moss'' in the NFL right now, and that's saying something. The wide receiver out of Miami - and not the one named "Randy'' - caught nine passes for 140 yards, including a 50-yard touchdown. But his most electrifying moment came on an 80-yard punt return for a score as the Redskins knocked off the winless Lions.
Three worst performances
The Bucs offense In the previous two weeks, the Cowboys had given up 64 points. Yet the Bucs offense, even with Joey Galloway back, could scratch together only nine points and really didn't come close to scoring a touchdown. You allow 13 points on the road to any team and your offense must find a way to win that game. LB James Harrison, Steelers You hate to pick on the guy because he was thrust into a tough spot. But when the Steelers lost long snapper Greg Warren with a season-ending knee injury in the third quarter, Harrison became Pittsburgh's long snapper. With the Steelers leading 14-12 in the fourth quarter, Harrison hiked the ball over punter Mitch Berger's head and out of the end zone. The result was a safety, which tied the score. The Giants drove for the winning entry after the ensuing free kick. The Bengals It's one thing to be winless and still fighting. The Lions, for instance, are playing their guts out, coming close, but just coming up short. But the Bengals? This team isn't even showing up. They fell to 0-8 for the fourth time since 1991 and this loss was ugly at it gets - 35-6 to the Texans.
THREE THINGS THAT STRUCK US
Singletary isn't going to make it Geez. One game into being a head coach and new 49ers boss Mike Singletary is already sending players to shower in the fourth quarter and wigging out after games. You should at least be in the league a year or two before having complete Dennis Green meltdowns, don't you think? There is no Super Bowl favorite Usually by this point in the season, one team out there looks unstoppable. The Giants are the closest thing to that for now, but it's because the rest of the league looks so ordinary. Even the lone undefeated team (the Titans, 7-0 after Monday night's victory over the Colts) doesn't really look all that scary. These Europe games are dumb New Orleans and San Diego played in Wembley over the weekend, and the Londoners loved it with more than 83,000 showing up. But is the league really considering expanding to Europe at some point? We just don't understand the point of these games, especially because it's such a hardship on the teams who have to fly over there and play.
THREE TEAMS ON THE RISE
New York Giants What an impressive victory. On the road. Against a really good Steelers team. Down in the fourth quarter. And the Giants rallied to win and showed that they might not only be the team to beat in the NFC, but the team to beat (again) in all of football. Very impressive. Houston Texans On Oct. 5, the Texans blew a 17-point lead in the fourth quarter to the Colts. Since then? The Texans have reeled off three consecutive victories. True, the victories haven't come against great competition. In fact, two have come against winless teams (Lions and Bengals) and the other came against the Dolphins. Still, victories are victories. This is the NFL. It's not how, but how many. New England Patriots The moment Tom Brady went down, most crossed this team off their Super Bowl contenders list. But quietly, the Pats have won two in a row and sit at 5-2 and, along with Buffalo, atop the AFC East. They aren't necessarily impressive each week (see Sunday's 23-16 victory against St. Louis), but once again, it's now how, it's how many.
THREE TEAMS ON THE DECLINE
San Francisco 49ers Three weeks into the season, the Niners were 2-1 and figured to be a seasonlong contender in the mediocre NFC West. Turns out, the NFC West is the epitome of mediocre and the Niners are out of the race. They've lost five in a row, including an embarrassing 34-13 loss at home Sunday to a Seahawks team that now only has two wins. Not only has San Fran fired one coach, it is already driving another crazy. Jacksonville Jaguars Just when it seems the Jags are about to shake off whatever is wrong with them, they take another step back. For example, Sunday. Two weeks after going into Denver and gutting out a 24-17 victory against the Broncos, the Jags returned home following a bye and fell to Cleveland, 23-17. Teams with playoff hopes just can't lose games like that. Tampa Bay Bucs Look, the Bucs are going to be fine, mostly because of a soft schedule over the next month. But Sunday was a depressing loss. The Bucs couldn't get a thing going against a good team that is struggling on both sides of the ball. The Bucs are probably going to get into the playoffs, but based on Sunday's game, you wonder how much damage they can do when they get there.
Three big games this weekend
Patriots at Colts For the first time in years, this is not the regular-season game of the year in the NFL. Still, not a bad matchup between two teams trying to prove they're better than most people think they are. Cowboys at Giants A classic NFC East showdown. More important to the 5-3 Cowboys than the 6-1 Giants. The only thing missing is Pat Summerall and John Madden calling the game. Steelers at Redskins A Monday night showdown between a pair of two-loss teams. The big question is whether the Steelers are just too banged up to keep playing good teams and surviving.

title: Amid 'questionable calls,' NFL needs to overhaul officiating
content:
Hey, from a Seahawks fan: Congratulations to Pittsburgh on a Super Bowl victory. Now, let's agree that NFL officiating needs a major overhaul ("Steelers record Motown hit," Cover story, Sports, Monday).

Pittsburgh linebacker Joey Porter vocalized the officiating problem earlier in the playoffs. And now the title of Super Bowl champion has been cheapened by the many questionable calls going against the Seahawks during Super Bowl XL.

Consider: The reversal of Darrell Jackson's touchdown because of "offensive pass interference." Seahawks quarterback Matt Hasselbeck being called for an illegal block, an impossible call to make because he was tackling, not blocking. The phantom "holding" of Sean Locklear on a monster Seahawk pass play to Jerramy Stevens on the 1-yard line. "Big Ben" Roethlisberger getting a timeout, even though the play clock clearly said zero and it should have been delay of game.

Add in the late determination of the initial call on Big Ben's first "touchdown" and the missed calls, and the impact of shoddy officiating looms huge. How can a team lose that has more total yards, more first downs, fewer turnovers and longer time of possession?

It's incumbent upon the NFL not to let "biased officiating" be even considered as an answer to that question.

Randolph Kaech

Ferndale, Wash.

'Lighten up, NFL'

As I watched the latest Super Bowl and its blown calls, I had a revelation: It's not the referees, it's the micro-management aspect of the NFL rules. Calls will always be subjective by referee, but I take issue with the parameters the refs are given to be subjective in.

Whether it's the tuck rule, the pity-pat push-offs or the is-it-or-is-it-not-a-touchdown, the NFL rules committee has gotten too granular in defining what constitutes a particular penalty.

I think it's a response to the intense news media coverage, where bad calls are replayed and overanalyzed during and after the game on the Internet, talk radio, TV shows and editorials.

The rules committee is preoccupied with perceived fallout so, in turn, it overreacts by trying to accommodate every scenario, confusing definitions.

Keep it simple, stupid: a fumble a fumble, a touchdown a touchdown. Barring the egregious foul, lighten up, NFL!

John Coates

Atlanta

'Final straw' with NFL

With all the problems in major pro sports, I am sad to see the Super Bowl take more hits:

*First, a bad performance of the national anthem. As a soldier in the U.S. Army, I believe in freedom but I also believe in respect. As much as I like Aretha Franklin, that performance seemed to be disheartening.

*Second, lousy officiating. This leaves a bad taste in my mouth.

*Third, the labor dispute between players and the NFL. Players and owners need to get a grip and fast. The NFL will lose more fans, and everyone associated with the league will be to blame. This is my final straw with the NFL.

One less fan to deal with.

Larry Dobra

Greenville, N.C.

Can't sing to anthem

We were totally disgusted as we watched the singing of our national anthem at the Super Bowl. We have never heard such a ridiculous version of this beautiful anthem in all our years. How could anyone sing along with that garbage? This is a total embarrassment to our country.

We take pride in hearing this anthem or singing along whenever it is played. There was no way anyone in the stadium could have joined in with the performance at the Super Bowl. In the future, we hope the NFL will give some direction to these misguided musicians. Perhaps the NFL should use a recorded version of our national anthem, similar to what is used in the Olympics.

Do what you want with the halftime entertainment, but leave our national anthem to those who appreciate it, love it and can sing it the way it was written.

Ralph and Susan Wehnes

Huntley, Ill.

'Pathetic' Super Bowl

I hope the NFL is proud of itself. From start to finish, that was the most pathetic Super Bowl in the history of the NFL. The national anthem should be sung as it's supposed to sound, not have an individual's stamp on it. Both teams looked as if they were playing for 10th place in the league, the officiating was at an all-time low and the halftime "entertainment" was anything but. (The Stones should bow out now to keep their legacy intact, but that's another story.)

We saw how bad the officiating was in the playoffs in Denver and Indianapolis, and the NFL even admitted it. You'd think they'd have made an effort to get it right for the "big game," but it was still obvious -- not only to viewers but to analysts, too -- that some calls were just atrocious.

Thanks for a stinker, NFL; better luck next year.

Tom Mowbray

Warwick, R.I.

title:
BOWL: REX GROSSMAN;
 LVP END ZONE
content:
IN End Zone last week, we suggested that the underpressure Chicago quarterback would have a shot at the MVP award if he proved anything better than useless in the big game.

Suffice to say he didn't trouble the judges. Grossman had trouble keeping hold of the ball in the incessant rain, let alone seeking out his receivers with it and the two interceptions he threw were both poorly thrown passes.

The Bears got to the Super Bowl thanks to their special teams and defence, and in spite of the hapless passer who let them down again when it mattered most.

title: Ringing similarities
content:
Redskins' QB Campbell shares some traits with Favre

ASHBURN, Va. - Brett Favre and Jason Campbell grew up 100 miles apart in rural Mississippi. The combined population of their hometowns - Kiln and Taylorsville - is less than 4,000.

"You have nothing else to do but sit outside and throw footballs at trees," Campbell said yesterday. "That's how you work on your accuracy. Knock some bark off a tree."

Small-town people not only have their own set of jokes, they are also quiet neighborly, so during the offseason Campbell figured he would pay a visit to the Green Bay Packers legend, who now lives in Hattiesburg. The Washington Redskins quarterback walked up - unannounced - to the call box outside Favre's gated home.

No answer. Campbell waited five minutes and went away.

"I didn't want a helicopter to come by," Campbell said.

Told yesterday that Campbell had come calling, Favre seemed bemused.

"I didn't know he came by," Favre said. "What did he say he was coming by for?"

Why, to say hello and catch up on old times, of course. Being a quarterback from the Magnolia State, Campbell has been a Favre fan practically since birth. Campbell was a rising senior in high school when he met Favre for the first time, when they happened to share the same trainer.

Campbell was amazed at how hard Favre threw the ball to the trainer, enough "to make his hand almost bleed," according to Campbell. Favre then greeted Campbell and offered advice to the young kid, mainly to stay focused amid the many distractions that lay ahead in college.

Campbell is now 26. Favre turned 38 yesterday. They are a football generation apart. Campbell's father, a substitute high school teacher, actually has Favre's daughter in his class from time to time. Yet, here they are, about to face either in the NFL for the first time when Campbell and Redskins come calling - no waiting at the gate this time - to face the Packers on Sunday.

"The years go by so fast," Campbell said. "Now to be on the same field with him ..."

Favre is setting records right and left in his 16th season, while Campbell has started only 11 NFL games and is just reaching his first milestones. He had a 100 passer rating in a game for the first time in Sunday's 34-3 win over Detroit and was named NFC offensive player of the week. He had a 79 percent completion percentage (23-for-29), by far his best as a pro. His 248 yards passing were a career-high, as were his 23 completions.

Those are tiddlywinks numbers for Favre. He's completed at least 23 passes in every game this season and is averaging more than 300 yards a game. He's had a 100 rating three times in Green Bay's five games.

What the two do have in common - other than their strong arms - is the ability to shake off the negative. Favre keeps firing no matter how many interceptions he's thrown, and Campbell has emulated that quality, earning praise from teammates and coaches for keeping an even keel.

"He doesn't look back," Campbell said. "He makes a mistake and moves on to the next play. There's where I am in my career. You make a mistake and try to move on."

That wasn't so easy two weeks ago, after Washington blew a big second-half lead and lost 24-17 to the New York Giants. The Redskins had four chances to score from the 1-yard line in the final minute, and Campbell didn't help things by rushing the final two plays.

Campbell said the loss stuck with him longer than usual. It was fortunate the bye week followed, giving him a chance to get over it.

"Any time you lose the game on the 1-yard line, it hurts," Campbell said. "But one thing it did was teach us a lesson."

Campbell rebounded with a game that was almost too easy. The Lions barely touched him and allowed him to complete short-to-medium passes all game. The diciest moment came when Campbell felt a bit dizzy and had his face mask bent while recovering his own fumble on a third-down play. He spit out some grass while coach Joe Gibbs called a timeout, then completed a fourth-and-2 pass to Antwaan Randle El that set up a touchdown.

"Jason has his best game for us," Gibbs said. "It was all the things you picture in a quarterback."

NOTES: WR Antwaan Randle El (hamstring), LB Marcus Washington (hamstring), DE Phillip Daniels (shoulder) and FB Mike Sellers (heel) did not practice. "Hopefully there's a miracle there," Gibbs said of Randle El's chances of playing Sunday. WR Santana Moss (groin) took part in the full practice.

title: Faulk runaway choice by AP as league MVP
content:
NEW YORK -- Marshall Faulk's record-setting ride for the St. Louis Rams earned him the NFL's Most Valuable Player award in balloting conducted by The Associated Press and announced yesterday.

Faulk, whose 26 touchdowns eclipsed Emmitt Smith's league mark by one, carried the defending Super Bowl champion Rams into the playoffs with his late-season heroics.He scored 11 of those touchdowns in the last three weeks and had three four-TD games overall.

The running back out of San Diego State also had 220 yards rushing in Sunday's victory at New Orleans that helped St. Louis squeeze into the postseason.

"This season was pretty high up there, a very special one just because of all the things we faced," said Faulk, who finished with 1,359 yards and 18 touchdowns rushing, plus 81 receptions for 830 yards and eight TDs.

Faulk, 27 and in his seventh NFL season, received 24 votes from a nationwide panel of 50 sportswriters and broadcasters. He beat Philadelphia quarterback Donovan McNabb (11), Tennessee running back Eddie George (8), Oakland quarterback Rich Gannon (5), Indianapolis quarterback Peyton Manning (1) and Baltimore linebacker Ray Lewis (1).

"No question he's deserving of the MVP," said Rams quarterback Kurt Warner, who won the honor last year. "Look at what he did in 14 games. I don't think there's any question about what he's meant to the team."

Faulk missed two games and was hobbled in two others by a knee injury that required midseason arthroscopic surgery.

Faulk is the second successive Ram to win it and the third overall -- Roman Gabriel was the 1969 MVP. Faulk is the 14th running back to win the award.

title: TACKLING COWART'S CONTRACT
content:
When the Jets lose, it costs Sam Cowart money. When Cowart sits, it also costs him money.

At the same time, it could save him from a one-and-done career in New York.

It's a little confusing, but such is the nature of Cowart's unusual contract, obtained by the Daily News. Right now, the way things are going, his earning potential is shrinking by the week.

The former Pro Bowl linebacker, the jewel of the Jets' free-agent class, signed a one-year, $3 million contract, with a five-year, $27 million option. The option was a smart move by the Jets, considering Cowart's injury history.

To trigger the option, the Jets must pay a bonus next March, ranging from $3 million to $6 million. The amount is determined by various incentives - wins, playing time and the team's final ranking in total defense.

To hit the $6 million mark, Cowart must appear in 90% of the defensive plays and the team must win 10 games or finish in the top 12 in defense. At the start of the season, he was confident he'd hit the jackpot.

Sure, why not? The Jets figured to be a playoff contender and Cowart, excluded from the nickel and dime packages, told former teammates in Buffalo that he expected to work his way into an every-down role.

That hasn't happened. After six games, it has become mathematically impossible for him to hit the 90% mark. That kills the $6 million bonus.

Cowart's bonus is based on a sliding scale, (with descending playing-time levels) and he could wind up sliding down to $3 million, according to a person familiar with the contract. That may not be what he had in mind, but it would be more palpable for the Jets' salary-cap situation.

These dollar figures may not seem important in late October, but they will figure heavily into the team's decision on whether to retain Cowart. Right now, it's too close to call.

Cowart, struggling in a new system and still rusty after missing virtually the entire 2001 season with a torn Achilles tendon, has been solid, if not impressive. His 54 tackles, tied with MLB Marvin Jones for the team lead, project to 144 over a full season - 37 less than James Farrior's total last season in the same position.

In Sunday's win over the Vikings, Cowart recorded 10 tackles in limited action (he sat almost half the game, coming out on passing downs), but take a closer look at the quality of the tackles. At least six of those occurred on six-yard gains or more, including four pass completions.

Defensive coordinator Ted Cottrell, hoping to free Cowart from having to play on the line of scrimmage, called more "Under" fronts than usual. Results? Cottrell said Cowart played "pretty well."

Yeah, it was a nice game, but it wasn't the Cowart of 2000, the guy who was compared to Ray Lewis. He has 10 games to convince the hierarchy he's worth another $3 million (or more) investment.QUIETING RACKET ON HACKETTHave you noticed that Paul Hackett's name is shorter these days? The Jets' offensive coordinator no longer appears in print as the "Embattled Paul Hackett."

"Right now, no one's on Paul," Herman Edwards said with a laugh.

The temperature of Hackett's seat is lower than usual because of Chad Pennington, who, in two starts, has executed his West Coast offense better than Vinny Testaverde did in 20 starts.

Suddenly, Hackett looks smarter. If there was any doubt about his job security, it's probably gone now.

No, Hackett can't stand on his record - only five teams have scored fewer points than the Jets over the last 1 1/2 seasons - but his case is bolstered by the recent improvement and the amount of time he has invested in Pennington. Barring a collapse, Edwards won't break up that chemistry and start over.

Bad news for Hackett haters.

Faking it: It's too early to draw many conclusions on Pennington, but there's one aspect of his game that already is top shelf - his play-faking talent.

The Jets' philosophy on play-faking is different than many teams. Hackett insists on faking with the ball, not the hands.

"He's a natural at it," Hackett said. "He's at a very sophisticated level."

Hackett believes some of the best-known play-action quarterbacks, namely the Colts' Peyton Manning, tend to "over-exaggerate" the fake. Hackett, including former Jets QB Boomer Esiason in that category, wants his guys to use as little motion as possible.JETS ARE THROUGH SPREADING SELVES TOO THIN ON 'O'Funny, but the Jets are back to running the same offense that was ripped last season for being too conservative. They dropped the spread formations, with wide receivers all over the field, and they're using more two-back sets.

Even though the spread attack isn't part of the West Coast philosophy, the Jets made the switch late last season to appease Vinny Testaverde, who wanted to open up the offense. The plan this season was to continue that style, putting the ball in Testaverde's hands. It took three games to admit that was a mistake.

Paul Hackett won't admit it publicly, but he has to be more comfortable with Chad Pennington, a player he can mold.

"Maybe it's a year early, but this is where we're headed as the New York Jets, and it's real exciting," Hackett said.

The Jets also believe Pennington is quicker on the trigger than Testaverde, who has a tendency of dumping off to his second and third options. Compare his tendencies versus those of Pennington.

Testaverde: 60 of his 82 attempts (73%) traveled less than 11 yards in the air, according to STATS, Inc.

Pennington: Excluding the last game, yet to be tabulated by STATS, Inc., his numbers were 38 of 76 attempts (50%) under 11 yards.

Backup plan: Testaverde might be on the bench, but he's not completely out of the Jets' plans. They would like to bring him back next season as a backup. His salary drops to $1 million in 2003, so it's feasible cap-wise.

Basically, the Jets can't afford not to keep Testaverde, who is signed through 2006. If they released him next June, the cap hit would be crippling. Waiting until June 2004 is no bargain, either - a $3.5 million cap charge in '04 and another $4 million hit in '05.

Shoulders load: Rookie FS Jon McGraw had an MRI yesterday on his left shoulder.

The results were negative and he's expected to practice today

title: For Pack, time to regroup
content:
orton picked off as broncos lose again DENVER -- Tyrone Carter started in place of safety Ryan Clark for Pittsburgh as a precaution Monday night and made the big play that sparked the Steelers to their fifth straight win, taking the first of his two interceptions 48 yards for a touchdown in Pittsburgh's 28-10 win over the Denver Broncos. Clark nearly died following a game in Denver two years ago because of a rare blood disorder that is aggravated by playing in high altitude, so Steelers coach Mike Tomlin decided to hold him out of the game. Broncos quarterback and Iowa native Kyle Orton threw three interceptions, his first of the season. The Associated Press GREEN BAY, Wis.

- Despite two losses to Brett Favre and the division rival Minnesota Vikings in the first half of the season, at least the Green Bay Packers were beating the teams they were supposed to. Until now. Green Bay was reeling Monday after blowing a fourth-quarter lead to a previously winless Tampa Bay team starting a rookie quarterback. Now 4-4 at the season's halfway mark, a team expected to make a serious playoff push now finds itself on the verge of a free fall going into Sunday's home game against Dallas. The problems are the same: Too many sacks allowed, not enough pass rush and costly special teams lapses. But as fans holler for change on barstools and sports radio call-in shows across Wisconsin, they probably won't be happy to hear that Packers coach Mike McCarthy is calmly staying the course. McCarthy maintains that the mistakes are correctable and insists the season isn't a lost cause. "We're disappointed and we're 4-4," McCarthy said. "We're at the halfway point of our season. I'm very disappointed, but that's our work to this point. I take full responsibility. I'm at the point of this football team. I have all the confidence in this team that we'll get ready and we'll move on and win a big game here at home against Dallas." But the Packers might have to face the Cowboys without outside linebacker Aaron Kampman and right tackle Mark Tauscher. Kampman said he sustained a concussion after taking a blow to the head on the fourth play of the game, but wasn't taken out of the game until the fourth quarter. Speaking briefly to reporters Monday afternoon, Kampman said he still wasn't feeling quite right but just needed rest. His availability this week remains unclear. Kampman played into the fourth quarter before coaches realized he wasn't OK. Even amid increased awareness about the dangers of head injuries in football, defensive coordinator Dom Capers said coaches trust players to tell them when they're not feeling right. "You have to kind of depend on them, you know," Capers said. "And most guys that play this game, they think they can just shake it off. It's part of the game, you know. To a certain degree they can shake it off, it's no problem. And I think that's probably the approach that Aaron took until maybe in the second half, he saw that things weren't right." Tauscher, meanwhile, sprained his left knee - the same knee that sustained a major injury in December. Tauscher tore his left anterior cruciate ligament and was out of football until re-signing with the Packers last month. McCarthy said Tauscher has a "slight" chance of playing this week. The blame for the Packers' sack total goes beyond the line. Quarterback Aaron Rodgers still needs to get rid of the ball more quickly in certain situations, and McCarthy hinted that the wide receivers weren't getting open on Sunday. The Green Bay defense, meanwhile, continued to stop the run but didn't get enough pressure on rookie Josh Freeman and struggled in the fourth quarter. But for the second week in a row, the Packers' biggest problems were poorly timed lapses on special teams. A week after giving up two long kickoff returns to Minnesota's Percy Harvin, the Packers gave up a 83-yarder to Tampa Bay's Clifton Smith to set up an early fourth-quarter touchdown and swing the game's momentum in Tampa's favor. Despite talking about all-too-familiar problems Monday, McCarthy said the Packers don't need major changes. "To have a new message or a new messenger, I'm confident that's not what our football team needs right now," McCarthy said. "They have a very loud, direct, clear message in the team meetings day in and day out. So there is no question or uncertainty of what we are asking everybody to do, coaches and players, and the accountability of what needs to be done."

title: COACH SPEAKS ON FAVRE, THE FUTURE
content:
Green Bay Packers coach Mike McCarthy, left, touched on a variety of topics at Wednesday's end-of-the-season news conference at Lambeau Field. Among the highlights: Favre watch: McCarthy said he spoke with quarterback Brett Favre "for quite some time" Tuesday, and Favre and his family "headed back to Mississippi (Wednesday)." According to the coach, Favre's approach to making a decision about whether to return in 2008 will be "very similar to last year: Take a couple weeks, take the emotion out of everything that's gone on . . . and make the best decision that's in the best interest of him and his family." McCarthy said the two will talk "in the next seven to 10 days."

Off to Hawaii: McCarthy said he's "not sure" whether Favre will play in the Pro Bowl. The Packers' coaching staff will coach the NFC Feb. 10 in Honolulu.

More late games: On expectations for next season: "Our challenge to improve on 13-3 is much bigger than the challenge we encountered this year, in my opinion." He also said he expects fewer noon starts and more Sunday and Monday night games for the Packers.

title: Garcia finally finds right place, right time
content:
On this side of the field, The Natural. Peyton Manning.

On the other side, the guy nobody knew or wanted. Jeff Garcia, who now, incidentally, holds the second-highest rating among NFL passers and leads the league with 20 touchdown passes.

Manning won't make any plays against Garcia when the Indianapolis Colts welcome the San Francisco 49ers to their dome home Sunday. Nor will Garcia be looking to exploit Manning. If there's a head-to-head aspect, it lies in the sub-story of the NFL mining endlessly for quarterback gems even as it skips over diamonds in the rough and celebrates fool's gold.

Manning, the son of former New Orleans great Archie Manning, was the first player selected in the 1998 draft. Groomed and bred for success, he quickly helped the Colts become a playoff contender.

Garcia, the son of a former junior college coach, grew up with similar instincts born of blood but far less publicity and no predictions of imminent greatness. Now he has arrived, and with him the 49ers' chances of regaining their former greatness.

"It's difficult to scout every player. There are going to be misses. There are going to continue to be kids who go undrafted and kids who get drafted who maybe shouldn't be," Garcia says, sipping bottled water in a meeting room at the 49ers practice facility in Santa Clara, Calif. "I think when people doubted me, or when the doubts were out there, that added fuel to my desire to be the kind of player that I imagined I could be. I won't be satisfied until I experience that perfect game, which I know may never happen."

He should know never to say never. Already the three come-from-behind, overtime wins that elevated the 49ers to a 7-2 record evoke the mention of Joe Montana, the Hall of Famer, three-time Super Bowl MVP and master of late-game heroics. Once unloved, Garcia, 31, must swim mightily to keep his head above a tidal wave of accolades.

"He's the ultimate quarterback. He ranks right with Montana and (Steve) Young in his performances. What he has to do to reach the levels they did is win a Super Bowl," says former 49ers general manager and coach Bill Walsh, who made the first comparison of Garcia to Montana long ago.

Comparisons inevitable

Young and Montana. Always the measuring stick in the Bay Area. Even back then.

Go back to late 1993. Garcia and a middling San Jose State team lose a shootout to Stanford's Walsh, who comments about Garcia's Montana-like skills.

Jump ahead to the East-West Shrine Game a few months later. Walsh is watching practice. So is Bob Garcia, Jeff's dad and his one-time coach at Gavilan College, a 2-year school in Gilroy, Calif., about 80 miles south of San Francisco. The father suggests the son thank Walsh for his kind words.

"So I introduced myself and said I appreciated what he said, and he said I was deserving of it and that I was going to be the MVP of the Shrine Game," Garcia says, his face taking on the same stunned look it must have had that afternoon.

All he can offer in return is, " 'Oh. Thanks. Nice talking to you.' "

The West team trailed 28-7 when Garcia entered late in the fourth quarter. Three touchdown passes and a two-point conversion later, the West owned a 29-28 victory. The MVP? Well, the votes already had been cast for the East quarterback but a recount produced co-MVPs -- Garcia and Jay Fiedler.

That's the Fiedler who went undrafted. The Fiedler who signed with Philadelphia and didn't take a snap in two seasons. The Fiedler who fell out of the league and spent a year as an assistant coach at Hofstra University, who played in NFL Europe and found his way to the Miami Dolphins, where he now starts.

Fiedler says he doesn't remember Garcia from the Shrine Game, and apparently no one else did either. Garcia wasn't invited to the scouting combine, got no tryouts, went undrafted. Even Walsh's extensive network of contacts failed to net him a workout or any encouragement.

Almost.

The journey begins

Spring 1994. One NFL assistant coach phones Garcia out of respect for Walsh. It was the quarterbacks coach of the Green Bay Packers. He tells Garcia that the Packers are pretty much set with Brett Favre, Mark Brunell and Ty Detmer and offers this advice: "If you have an opportunity in Canada, go for it. You'll get great experience up there. Take advantage of it."

The coach was Steve Mariucci, now benefiting from Garcia's expatriate days. But there's more. The Packers ultimately did sign another undrafted quarterback to serve as an extra training camp arm. He didn't last, bounced around NFL Europe and the Arena League and then found a home in the NFL . . . with St. Louis. You've probably heard of Kurt Warner.

When Garcia scanned the '94 draft, the real frustration set in.

"The two top quarterbacks were Heath Shuler and Trent Dilfer. Dilfer has done a lot of positive things," Garcia says. "The other kid on the West in the Shrine Game was Doug Nussmeier, who was like a fourth-round draft pick (of New Orleans), and I was thinking, 'He's not any better than me. Why are they overlooking me?' But I can't make the rules. I had to find a place for myself and do what I do best."

Shuler flopped with Washington and New Orleans, and a foot injury terminated his short, sad career. Dilfer ultimately failed in Tampa Bay, lent Baltimore a firm and steady hand in its drive to the NFL title last season but was not retained as the Ravens wooed Elvis Grbac. Dilfer now is the backup in Seattle.

Nussmeier? Well, one day after practice with the Saints, he called his agent to chat and mentioned offhandedly that the best quarterback on the field was this local kid in for a visit -- Peyton Manning.

So Garcia trundles up to Calgary to play for the Stampeders. He earns the right to back up the league's biggest (and smallest) star, a fellow American for whom the NFL could find no job -- Doug Flutie. Need we recount his travels and travails?

After a year and a half on the bench, Garcia replaces the injured starter and begins to work similar magic. The Stampeders let Flutie go and win the 1998 Grey Cup behind Garcia, who is named MVP.

Then, finally, with Walsh back running the 49ers, Garcia gets the call to come home. But it's the same old story at first.

"We did not bring him in with the idea of him being the starter," general manager Terry Donahue says.

Timing is finally right

Timing, as Garcia well knew, is everything. Young's career-ending concussion four games into the 1999 season put Garcia in the lineup as a 28-year-old rookie; he made the Pro Bowl in 2000 after throwing 31 touchdown passes and only 10 interceptions. No one saw this in the making?

"Players blossom at different times," Donahue says.

He can recite all the reasons teams bypassed Garcia -- height (6-1), arm (good but not a cannon), excellent quickness but not flat-out speed. Garcia says "the ever-shifting mold at quarterback" worked against him at a time when everyone wanted "the next Troy Aikman."

Walsh says scouting often fails to reveal what the player truly offers and teams need most: "Intuition, instinct and the natural competitive zeal that just a few players have. They missed it with Flutie, Joe Theismann, Warren Moon."

Perhaps it's because they can examine the outside of the body but not the inner core.

"People can't measure your heart," Garcia says. "They wanted to look at my size, my arm strength or the physical aspects and knock me for that. I always looked at it like, 'What do I do on the football field, not in a one-on-one workout?' I'm not a sprinter. I'm not going to electrify you in that way.

"But what do I do on the football field, with 21 other people around me? When accurate decisions, timely decisions, have to be made, do I do the right thing? And I always believed that I had. But I didn't fit the mold, and I was discounted for that. Whatever. Life goes on."

Sometimes, despite the odds, it goes on pretty well.

title: Sports briefs
content:
College football

IRISH: DEAL NOT FACTOR: Notre Dame's new five-year television contract with NBC will not play a significant role in the school's decision whether to keep its football program independent.

"There's not an immediate correlation of any kind between those two things," athletic director Kevin White said Sunday in an interview with The Associated Press. "We made a decision to extend our deal with NBC. We're going to continue to monitor our choice. We haven't made any decision."

There has been speculation since Notre Dame and NBC announced the five-year extension that it was a sign the school, a Big East member in basketball and other sports, would continue as an independent in football. The contract with NBC is worth a reported $9 million a year through 2010.

White has avoided talking about Notre Dame's conference future except to issue a statement saying the school would "monitor the landscape."

White said Sunday nothing has changed.

"We're looking at everything and we're looking at it all the time," he said. "We're not rushing to a decision."

RARE LOSS: Larry Kehres hasn't had much practice consoling his Mount Union team after a loss. In his 18 seasons as coach, the Purple Raiders have won 205 games and lost just 18. The latest loss, 24-6 to St. John's in the Division III national championship Saturday, ended the Purple Raiders' NCAA-record 55-game winning streak and three-year hold on the Amos Alonzo Stagg Bowl. But losing, Kehres said, can sometimes be good.

"It's a real springboard for the young guys," he said. "Don't assume you're going to be successful. There's certain steps you have to take to be successful. Start taking them. It's like a wake-up call, a slap in the face, and that's how we'll use it. That's how we used it last time."

Last time was 1999 when Mount Union lost 24-17 to Rowan in the Div. III semifinals, ending a then-record 54-game winning streak. They hadn't lost since, winning the next three Stagg Bowls to make it seven in 10 years and breaking the winning streak record with a 66-0 victory against Bridgewater to earn a chance at a fourth straight title.

St. John's, where steady success in football allowed coach John Gagliardi this year become the career victories leader among college coaches, ended those hopes Saturday.

VAN PELT THROWING: Colorado State quarterback Bradlee Van Pelt threw hard again Sunday, a sign his broken throwing hand might be healed enough for him to play in the San Francisco Bowl against Boston College on Dec. 31.

"I'm getting close. Everyday I'm getting closer and closer," Van Pelt said Sunday. "Sometimes it's a little uncomfortable throwing a ball, but it's on track."

Van Pelt broke a bone in his right hand during the Rams' 24-23 victory Nov. 22 over UNLV. He had surgery the following week.

Pro football

NAMATH CUT OFF: Joe Namath's interview on ESPN was cut short when the Hall of Fame quarterback gave curious answers to Suzy Kolber's questions. Namath was at the Patriots-Jets game Saturday night as the Jets celebrated their 40th anniversary team. Asked by Kolber, ESPN's sideline reporter, about what the team's struggles meant to him, Namath replied:

"I want to kiss you," as he leaned toward her.

He added that he believed the team would come back next season with Chad Pennington available to play quarterback all year. But those at ESPN had heard enough.

"Based on Joe's response to the second question, we concluded the interview expeditiously," the network said in a statement Sunday. "While Joe made some relevant football points, had we known what was to come, of course, we would not have conducted the interview."

Kolber responded to Namath's comments by saying "Thanks, Joe. A huge compliment."

Namath capped off the interview by repeating: "I want to kiss you." before Kolber turned things back over to the announcers in the booth.

The Jets said they would have no comment.

TEXANS MIMIC HORN: David Carr called the play in the huddle. Then he and center Steve McKinney cooked up a little something extra. After Carr's 20-yard touchdown pass to Corey Bradford in the third quarter of the Texans' 27-24 loss to Tennessee, he and McKinney ran to the goal post padding. McKinney pretended to search the bottom of the post for a cell phone.

"We talked about it in the huddle right before that," McKinney said. "We knew we were going to score."

The comedy bit was aimed at Saints receiver Joe Horn, who hid a cell phone underneath the goal post a week ago and made an end zone call to celebrate the second of his four touchdowns against the New York Giants. Horn was fined $30,000 for his antics.

No one in the Texans locker room was laughing after the team lost on a late touchdown strike by Tennessee quarterback Steve McNair.

"It would have been a lot funnier if we'd have won," McKinney said.

title: Verdict is in: Jauron unlikely to get job done
content:
OK, who do you think the next Bears head coach will be?

Just two weeks ago I reserved judgment on Dick Jauron. Not because I thought he was doing a good job but because I couldn't figure out what he was doing.

Jauron isn't like any other NFL coach I've seen, except maybe Neill Armstrong, another really decent human being, and we all know what happened to him after four seasons with the Bears.

I wanted to wait until season's end to evaluate Jauron. But two weeks later - and two months before season's end - it's impossible to imagine this being written about him in the future, much less as soon as next season:

"He seemed shambling and bumbling and overmatched, and he stubbornly insisted on staying with a quarterback …

" … fair is only fair, and so those of us, ahem, who dared dart (fill in head coach's name) as inflexible and constricted before, now owe him. Here is a bouquet his hefty way. All due respect to a man who has the courage of his convictions and the will to stand by them, no matter what."

That's what Bill Lyon wrote about Eagles coach Andy Reid in Sunday's Philadelphia Inquirer. Reid's team is 5-3 now - matching 1999's victory total - and last year's criticism has turned into this year's compliments, albeit prematurely since the Eagles can still collapse.

Can even the hint of a turnaround like that happen concerning Jauron? I mean, these really are the early stages of his adminstration. He still doesn't have very many good players. His team is a bad combination of too young and too incompetent.

But I'm ready to say it can't happen here.

Jauron and his entire coaching staff look overmatched. If he's learning on the fly and Gary Crowton is transitioning from college to the pros and Greg Blache is … well, this is not the place to get job training.

Monday afternoon I ran across an Associated Press story. I'm not making this up. This really was the final paragraph:

"The Bears (1-7) are off on Sunday, and Jauron said the team will use its bye week to try to figure out what went wrong during the first half of the season."

How would you like to be a fly on that wall? You certainly wouldn't be ointment for a fly to get into.

The scary thing for me, and it should be for Jauron too, is I'm actually starting to decipher what he's saying. Like, after Sunday's loss at Philly, Jauron tried to explain why Jim Miller hadn't taken any snaps with the first offense since training camp.

The explanation, which really wasn't one, was that in retrospect if you knew Cade McNown would be injured, then Miller would have been prepared better.

Listen, I'm no genius but I know it's likely any NFL starting quarterback is going to go down at some time during the season. McNown, Kurt Warner, Elvis Grbac and Akili Smith all were injured on Sunday alone.

Jauron also was questioned about why the Bears didn't try a Hail Mary pass at the end of the first half. I think he said he wanted his team to get into the locker room to regroup, which was a better explanation than Crowton had, which was none.

I've heard enough already. I know Jauron probably won't be fired after this season. I also acknowledge that it's remotely possible that when the Bears have good players he'll coach better.

I've decided to believe it won't happen. I'm keeping those two paragraphs written by Bill Lyon just in case, but I've pretty much decided in less than half the time that Jauron is the current Neill Armstrong, a wonderful guy and inadequate head coach.

Sooner or later the Bears are going to have to replace him, so with whom?
