Randy Walker isn't dumb enough to take the job. Ron Turner is taking himself out of the running. Denzel Washington probably doesn't want to move down from the high-school level to such a disorganized, disoriented, dismayed team as the Bears.

So who do you think the next Bears head coach will be?

title: Gruden picks Johnson
content:
Late Sunday night, Jon Gruden whispered another call over the headset to Brad Johnson.

It was the old quarterback reverse.

Hours after benching Johnson in favor of Chris Simms in the team's 10-6 loss to Seattle, the Bucs coach telephoned the 13-year veteran to inform him he would start Sunday night's nationally televised game against the Raiders.

"All I can say about Brad Johnson is that he is a class act," Gruden said at his news conference Monday. "He has been around a long time. He understands why we made the change. I don't believe he'll agree with all of the reasons for us making the change. But I can only say he handled it with great class, and I truly expect him to come back this week, have a hell of a week of practice and find a way to get this ship right."

Neither Johnson nor Simms were available for comment.

Gruden said he went to Simms to provide the offense with a spark but he never intended to fire Johnson as the starting quarterback.

Simms, 24, led the Bucs to two field goals and generally made a successful debut. But his inexperience resulted in several mistakes, including two fumbles, one lost, and throwing a game-clinching interception with just more than a minute remaining in the game.

Simms will remain the No. 2 quarterback against the Raiders. But Gruden said he does not plan to have a quick hook with Johnson if the offense struggles. Tampa Bay, which has not scored an offensive touchdown this season, will try to avoid falling to 0-3 for the first time since 1996.

"Brad Johnson is a heck of a football player and a team man here. He's been victimized, I think, by a lot of circumstances," Gruden said. "The lineup has been a revolving door, and it's been difficult on him. His style and the way in which he plays, he's a very system-oriented quarterback. Hopefully, the experience some of these new players have gotten in the last couple of games will help them improve their performances as well."

Only three quarterbacks - the Browns' Jeff Garcia, the Chiefs' Trent Green and the Bears' Rex Grossman - have a lower passer-efficiency rating than Johnson's 55.4.

However, Gruden said, the Bucs' other offensive players need to rally around their Super Bowl-winning quarterback.

"The big thing is we've got to find a way to get something going," Gruden said. "Running, receiving, whatever it may be. A lot of times the quarterback gets too much credit, just like the head coaches do when you win. And sometimes you get too much credit when you don't. We made a change (Sunday) in a game to try and ignite our football team and maybe use a couple different types of plays, feature a little different series of offense.

"But Brad Johnson is Brad Johnson. He's got to play like Brad Johnson, very efficient with the football and make some plays along the way, which we think he can."

So why didn't Gruden want to continue riding the Bull after 15 plays Sunday? Call it a hunch.

Johnson managed to produce just two first downs and an interception that set up the Seahawks' only touchdown before being benched.

Simms, who had never taken a snap in a regular-season NFL game, fared a little better at times. He completed 21 of 32 passes for 175 yards. But he was sacked four times, fumbled a center exchange near the goal line, lost another fumble and was intercepted by rookie Michael Boulware at the Seahawks 15.

"I thought he showed great poise, and he shows great promise as a future player for our organization," Gruden said of Simms. "I thought he did show great confidence and delivered some sensational throws. But he made some mistakes, which I'm sure he'll address.

"Sometimes you're going to do things by feel, (by) the pulse of your team, (when) you're standing down there in the heat of the battle. Like all of us, we have to perform and get results. (Johnson) understands that. He's going to do a good job for us, and we just have to get the battle cry out to everybody on offense to help out and I'm confident we can do that."

What gives Gruden confidence the Bucs can still turn the season around? It's Tampa Bay's defense. Its 26 points allowed are tied for fourth fewest in the league.

It also limited the Seahawks to 1-of-14 on third-down conversions.

"If we can get it going on offense and I'm not the first coach to stand before you and say, "If we can get it going offensively, "' he said. "I'm not trying to have any fun with this at all. I have great confidence in this football team.

"It's unfortunate right now that we've gotten off to such a sluggish start offensively, but I'm counting on our defense to be outstanding. It might need to score for us. If that's what it takes for us to win because they sure have the potential to do it."

IN THE SPOTLIGHT

* Dolphins (9-4) at Patriots (9-5), 1:30 p.m. Saturday, Foxboro Stadium, Foxboro, Mass. TV: KDKA.

IN THE CENTRAL

* Browns RB James Jackson was placed on injured reserve yesterday and will miss the remainder of the season. He aggravated a left ankle sprain in the first quarter of a loss to the Jaguars Sunday in Cleveland. Jackson led the Browns with 554 yards rushing. In other moves, the Browns activated RB Carl Fair off the practice squad and signed DL Derrick Ham to the practice squad.

* Titans QB Steve McNair missed practice for the third consecutive day and remained questionable for tomorrow's game.

NEWS & NOTES

* Three current players and a former NFL player were accused in a lawsuit yesterday of drugging and raping a woman at a Las Vegas apartment and hotel room in May 2000. Lions QB Charlie Batch, S Ron Rice and Dolphins WR Dedric Ward are named in the lawsuit along with former Lions DB Tyree Talton. "I vehemently deny the allegations," Batch said in a statement. "The police investigated in May 2000 and found nothing illegal took place. These accusations are just another example of how success in sports makes someone a target."

* Vikings QB Daunte Culpepper had arthroscopic surgery to repair a sprained ligament in his left knee. Coach Dennis Green said there were no complications with the surgery.

* Raiders WR Jerry Rice said that if his team were to win the Super Bowl, he might retire after this season. Rice, 39, signed a four-year contract with the Raiders in the off-season, but his agent said Rice's intention was only to play two more seasons. Rice is in his 17th season. He spent the previous 16 with the 49ers.

HE SAID IT

* Takeo Spikes, Bengals LB, on his team's opponent Sunday, the Ravens: "They must think we don't belong on the same field with them. Hello? We're 1-0 against them."

title: JETS FAVRE FROM A QUARTERBACK FIX. OPTIONS LOOK LIMITED IF BRETT CALLS IT QUITS
content:
BRETT FAVRE IS on a hunting trip in Louisiana, where he won't have much luck if he's misfiring as badly as he did last month on the field.

Soon enough, the Jets will be hunting, too - for Favre's potential replacement. And that could be a dangerous game.

As the future Hall of Famer mulls retirement after a physically and mentally taxing season, the Jets are preparing to formulate a contingency game plan in case Favre decides to quit, which appears likely. Ideally, they would like his answer by Feb. 27, the start of free agency.

Team officials have said they want Favre, 39, to return - owner Woody Johnson reiterated that stance yesterday in separate radio interviews - but the issue has stirred debate in the NFL community.

"I think it's time to move on," ESPN analyst Ron Jaworski, a former quarterback, said yesterday in a phone interview. "The time is right to develop a new quarterback under Rex Ryan. No disrespect toward Brett Favre, but the guy is up there in years. Don't build around him. Find the guy you want to be your quarterback for the next 10 years."

An NFC scout, speaking on the condition of anonymity, offered a dissenting opinion: "There's nobody out there. I'd keep Favre for another year until I get somebody."

This will be the first major decision of the Ryan regime. GM Mike Tannenbaum will make the final call, according to Johnson, who said in an interview on WFAN, "I like (Favre) and I think we can win with him . . . but I don't make the final decision."

There's also the matter of Favre's cap-busting salary - $13 million, which the Jets almost certainly would want to renegotiate if he comes back.

Basically, the Jets would have four quarterback avenues if Favre doesn't return: The trade market, free agency, the draft and internal options, which would mean letting Kellen Clemens and untested Brett Ratliff battle for the starting job.

The organization is high on the strong-armed Ratliff (6-4, 225), who wowed team scouts after spending 2007 on the Jets' practice squad as a non-drafted free agent out of Utah and performing well in the 2008 preseason. Three of the nine highest-rated passers in the league this season weren't drafted - Kurt Warner, Tony Romo and Jeff Garcia - so there are examples of Cinderella stories. But do the Jets want to entrust a veteran team to a quarterback with no regular-season experience?

They could return to Clemens, who went 3-5 behind the porous line in 2007, but he struggled last summer before Favre's arrival and his star has dimmed. When the season ended, the former second-round pick said, "I feel ready to become the player I was drafted to be" - but the organization doesn't share that same confidence.

Jaworski, who gushed about Clemens before the '06 draft, isn't ready to jump off the bandwagon, saying, "I'm still a believer in Kellen Clemens. Some work needs to be done, but at least there's experience under his belt."

The most intriguing option is the Browns' Derek Anderson, who has a big arm - and a bloated interception total. He parlayed a breakout '07 season (29 touchdowns, 18 interceptions) into a three-year, $24 million contract, but he lost his job this past season to Brady Quinn and finished as the league's lowest-rated passer.

New Browns coach Eric Mangini could decide to trade Anderson, but he wouldn't do any favors for the Jets, the team that fired him. It probably would cost at least a third-round pick to acquire Anderson, the same price it cost the Jets for Favre.

There's always free agency, but the biggest name - the Patriots' Matt Cassel - probably will be out of reach. Chances are, the Patriots will use the franchise tag on Cassel, protecting themselves if Tom Brady isn't ready to return from his devastating knee injury. They could trade Cassel with the tag, but there's virtually no chance they would deal him to the Jets.

Others eligible for free agency are Warner (Cardinals), Garcia (Buccaneers), Kerry Collins (Titans), Byron Leftwich (Steelers) and Rex Grossman (Bears). The Cards won't let Warner get away, not after his magical Super Bowl run. Collins is said to be mulling only two options - returning to the Titans as the starter or retiring. Garcia will be looking for a new home, but he's 38.

Former starters Grossman and Leftwich carry plenty of baggage, but at least they're on the right side of 30. Leftwich is injury prone, but he has played well in limited backup duty and is looking for a fresh start. If Ryan made Grossman his quarterback, the Jets would be a Two-Rex team.

The draft is thin, with only two quarterbacks considered certain first-rounders - Mark Sanchez (Southern Cal) and Matthew Stafford (Georgia). Trouble is, the Jets are picking 17th, and both are projected as top-10 picks. Things can change, of course. A year ago, Joe Flacco wasn't on anybody's radar until the scouting combine, where he blew away everybody with his arm and wound up being selected by the Ravens in the first round.

Ryan, formerly the Ravens' defensive coordinator, got an up-close view of the unflappable Flacco, who became the first rookie quarterback in NFL history to win two playoff games. Ryan recognizes that a team can win without a prolific passer as long as the defense and running game are strong.

Maybe that explains why he hasn't given an unconditional vote of confidence to Favre. Asked yesterday on WFAN to pick a starting quarterback, Ryan replied: "Joe Namath."

title: Presto, Jones no longer the invisible tight end
content:
It was a disappearing act worthy of a Las Vegas stage. Chargers tight end Freddie Jones goes 6-foot-5 and 275 pounds, and yet there have been times the past two seasons when he was invisible on the football field.

Quarterbacks would talk about his Pro Bowl potential during the week, then look in other directions on Sundays. The low point came last season, when he was held to two or fewer receptions in four of the final six games.

Things have been different this season. In the past four-plus games, Jones has caught 26 passes for 337 yards and three touchdowns. He ranks third among tight ends with 40 receptions and is on pace to shatter his season high of 57 catches, set two years ago in his second year in the league.

Jones credits his emergence with being at peace and turning his life over to God. But others say the difference has been the return of quarterback Jim Harbaugh to the starting lineup.

Harbaugh does not have the arm strength to throw the ball down the field consistently, so he often settles for throwing short to Jones, whose 10 receptions for 111 yards and two touchdowns last week against the Raiders were all career highs.

When coach Mike Riley was asked if Harbaugh has used Jones as a crutch since becoming the starter Oct. 1 against Seattle, he paused and then nodded.

"There's no question that's a valid point," he said. "Sometimes it's just easiest, I suppose."

Don't get Riley wrong. He's pleased with Jones' production. He just wants to find a way to balance the outside passing game with Jones' work in the seams and flats.

The Chargers tried to involve Jones in the passing game in previous seasons, but at times he was his worst enemy because he suffered from a case of the drops. Passes that seemed so simple to grab would wind up on the turf with disturbing frequency.

But Jones said he came into this season with a goal of becoming the player that coaches and teammates envisioned when he came into the league in 1997 after being drafted in the second round out of North Carolina. And while hard work has helped him improve, he said a higher power is also at work.

"I gave my life to God this year and I think that's been the major difference in my life as far as my focus," he said. "Things were just made simple once I did that. I don't have to worry about any problems, I don't have to worry about any bills, I don't have to worry about my mom, I don't have to worry about what the coaches think. That alleviated a lot of the stress in my world."

It also helped that in the offseason Jones signed a new five-year contract that averages $3 million a year. The deal made him one of the league's highest-paid tight ends and added the weight of expectation on his shoulders.

Thus far, it has not been a problem. Kansas City's Tony Gonzalez and Jacksonville's Kyle Brady are the only tight ends with more receptions than Jones, and each has 41.

Part of Jones' success has to do with wideouts Jeff Graham and Curtis Conway. Despite their limited numbers this season -- which has as much to do with the problems at quarterback as it does with their earlier injuries -- the two have commanded double-teams at different points in different games, which has allowed Jones to see a lot of man coverage.

"Their presence on the field just makes it seem like my routes are easier," Jones said. "It just seems like I'm not double-teamed as much. I definitely see a difference in the coverages."

Jones said he doesn't grab the game summaries each Monday to see what Gonzalez and Brady did the previous day, but he acknowledges part of his success can be attributed to Gonzalez. The two play in the same division, and when Jones studies AFC West defenses he often grabs tapes of Chiefs games to see how they coped with Gonzalez.

"It's good for me to look at how they try to cover him that week, because he's a key player for that team," he said. "It's good to see how he reacts. You learn a lot."

It's possible that Gonzalez also grabs tapes of Chargers games to see how defenses cover Jones. These days, no one can accuse him of not showing up.

title: Culpepper just had a bad day, that's all
content:
RSEC:             The Vikings were trailing Carolina 24-13 with 5 1/2 minutes remaining in Sunday's NFL opener at the Metrodome. They had second-and-5 at Carolina's 43.

     The Vikings sent in a play with a formation they had used several times in the exhibition games. The Vikings had run on all those occasions, and the idea here was to get the Panthers' defense to anticipate another run.

   The best way to get a cautious secondary to move forward was with a strong fake to the running back. Quarterback Daunte Culpepper decided to go from a shotgun formation, rather than from under center. That meant there was no run fake for the Panthers to consider.

     Even without the hard sell, Randy Moss was able to run past the cornerback and get some room along the left sideline. Culpepper ignored Moss and tried to throw long down the middle to tight end Byron Chamberlain.

     "Daunte comes out in the shotgun . . . doesn't throw to Moss," a Vikings assistant coach said. "That tells me it was just a day when he was off all afternoon."

     Moss might have scored if Culpepper had gone in his direction. Instead, Moss walked back slowly along the sideline and the Vikings put in another receiver for third down. It took two plays and a timeout for the Vikings to earn a first down at the Carolina 38.

     There was less than four minutes remaining when the Vikings made it inside the 10, then Culpepper fumbled through the end zone and that made it official. The Vikings _ the largest favorites on the NFL's opening weekend at 10 points _ had lost for the fifth time in their past 30 regular-season and playoff games inside the Dome.

     The Vikings' brain trust was amazed Culpepper was not able to find receivers against a defense that was as "vanilla as it can get," the assistant said.

     Coach Dennis Green said at Sunday's postgame interview session: "We missed opportunities all over the field. We could have done more."

     It sounded as though a frustrated coach was lamenting Culpepper's inability to locate available receivers. Green and his coaches reviewed the missed opportunities on videotape Monday morning.

     Then, Green came to Monday's weekly news conference and talked more about five turnovers than Culpepper's inability to hit the Panthers defense with a several long strikes to Randy Moss, the Vikings' $75 million weapon.

     "Daunte is one of the best quarterbacks in the National Football League," Green said. "We don't operate in the blame aspect of the game."

     This put Green in a different category than the fans, a few of whom were so overwrought by Culpepper's opening performance that they e-mailed newspapers and called radio shows to suggest it was time to turn to Todd Bouman.

     There's no great mystery as to what happened on Sunday, folks. Culpepper had an off-day.

     A pro football team with a quarterback having a terrible game is like a basketball team having a terrible shooting night: It can lose to anyone.

     The Panthers put no pressure on Culpepper over the final 2 1/2 quarters. They rushed with four linemen and put seven defenders in pass coverage. Culpepper looked away quickly when he saw a safety rolled toward Moss and settled for the underneath throw to Chamberlain.

     Moss had one catch for 28 yards. Chamberlain had seven catches for 83 yards. What do you think the coaching staffs that will face the Vikings were saying when they saw those statistics Monday morning?

     One, we're going to have to come up with something special to stop Chamberlain; or two, if we double-cover Moss on every play, Culpepper will keep throwing the ball short to the tight end . . . hallelujah!

     My guess is the winner among rival coaches was answer No. 2.

     "Daunte knows he can play better, and that's a good start," Green said. "I think he'll do just fine. We're confident Daunte will play better than he did last year."

     It's almost a guarantee Culpepper will play better in all of the 15 games that remain on the Vikings' schedule than he did Sunday.

     This was Greg Maddux giving up seven runs in 3 1/3 innings. This was Kobe Bryant going 4-for-21 from the field. This was the rare instance of a great athlete being off physically and instinctively all afternoon . . . nothing more.

    _ Patrick Reusse is at preusse@startribune.com.

title: Surprise players light up scoreboards
content:
Just when you think you have fantasy football all figured out, just as Priest Holmes returns to his dominant form and Kurt Warner is back to throwing for 300 yards and Stephen Davis is back to scoring touchdowns for Washington, meet your Week 12 All-Star Fantasy team:

Matt Hasselbeck, Marcel Shipp, Joey Galloway and the Atlanta Falcons defense.

Yes, sir, this would have been the winning combination, but chances are only the Falcons defense benefited fantasy players.

Hasselbeck has been a perpetual disappointment for Seattle since leaving Green Bay, where the backup established himself as the greatest quarterback in preseason history.

Sunday, he threw for 362 yards and three touchdowns.

Okay, so it was against Kansas City. But Hasselback has thrown for 250-plus yards in three of his past four games, with four touchdowns and no picks.

As for Arizona's Shipp, he is everything Thomas Jones was not, proving it with another good outing Sunday: 135 yards and a touchdown.

And Galloway, who was a weekly play until Chad Hutchinson took over as Dallas' quarterback (62, 56 and 49 yards), finally found some synergy with the new signal-caller by catching seven passes for 144 yards and two scores.

Maybe this week, some owner will actually start these guys.

FORECAST - CLOUDY: Forecasting whom to play is always risky business, because it involves going beyond the Brett Favres and Ricky Williamses of the world.

Case in point: one generally reliable fantasy Web site had a rough week with its hot and cold picks.

For hot picks, it suggested New England WR David Patten (5 catches, 62 yards), Tennessee QB Steve McNair (21 of 43, 3 INTs, 0 TDs), Minnesota RB Michael Bennett (14 carries, 55 yards, 0 TDs), Baltimore TE Todd Heap (4 catches, 42 yards) and New Orleans quarterback Aaron Brooks (23 of 40, 318 yards but 3 INTs and 0 TDs).

That's a losing lineup for sure.

A winning lineup would likely include these players from the "cold" list: Pittsburgh WR Hines Ward (5 catches, 125 yards, 1 TD), Green Bay's Ahman Green (56 yards rushing, 40 receiving) and Shipp.

Which is why I generally stay away from forecasting and concentrate my energies on ripping Jones instead.

WHITE BLUE THANKS TO GREEN: William Green's first 100-yard game for Cleveland, on the heels of a 96-yard performance, probably means owners of Jamel White can officially let go. Of course, dropping the once productive White is not nearly as enjoyable as finally cutting Jones.

YUCK!: In one of the leagues on Yahoo where quarterbacks are penalized for interceptions and fumbles, four starting quarterbacks had 1 point or less, and all big names: Favre (1), McNair (minus-1), Brooks (minus-2) and Drew Bledsoe (minus-3).

HEARST SO GOOD: Owners of San Francisco's Kevan Barlow have been praying for an injury to Garrison Hearst for two seasons. Owners of Hearst now get the last laugh as Barlow is expected to miss Sunday's game with a knee sprain.

STAT OF NOTE: New England TE Christain Fauria has 84 yards his past five games but of his 10 catches in that span, four were for touchdowns.

title: RUNNING BACKS' STATUS MURKY
content:
Bill Cowher was evasive yesterday about the health of his running backs, but at least this much is known about what will likely transpire Sunday in Cleveland:

* Jerome Bettis (calf), who practiced for the second day in a row, will play against the Browns.

* FB Dan Kreider (hip flexor), who did not practice for the second day in a row, likely will play.

* RB Willie Parker, the only healthy running back on the 53-man roster, will be the third-down back.

The only remaining question is Duce Staley (hamstring), who did not play in the 27-3 victory against the Philadelphia Eagles Sunday and did not practice for the second day in a row.

When asked if Staley will practice today -- the true determination of whether he will play in Cleveland -- Cowher said, "We'll see."

Staley remains questionable on the team's official injury report, meaning there is a 50 percent chance he will play. The Steelers may elect to give him another week's rest because Bettis, who rushed for 149 yards on 33 carries against the Eagles, does not appear to be bothered by his injury.

"It's not much different," Cowher said when asked to assess his injury situation. "We'll utilize the whole week. We might have to make some game-time decisions."

The Steelers added FB J.T. Wall to the practice squad this week and have until 4 p.m. tomorrow to add him to the 53-man roster. But Cowher said he is "hopeful" Kreider will practice today, reducing the possibility Wall will be signed for the game.

Verron Haynes (turf toe), the team's third-down back, is listed as doubtful and will not play against the Browns.

"We'll see how we are [today]," Cowher said. "Injuries are part of the game. We're dealing with it. That's why you have a 53-man roster."

%% More injury updates %%

Two players returned to practice -- C Jeff Hartings (knee/probable) and DE Travis Kirschke (back/probable). WR Lee Mays (hamstring/probable) did not practice for the second day in a row.

Browns WRs Andre Davis (toe/doubtful), who caught a 7-yard touchdown in the first meeting, and Andre King (ankle/doubtful) did not practice for the second day in a row.

%% A first for Gardocki, too %%

Punter Chris Gardocki had an easy day against the Eagles: The Steelers were so efficient on offense they never had to punt -- the first time that has happened in franchise history.

It was also a first for Gardocki, who said he can't remember going an entire game without at least punting once.

"If I would have known in pregame I didn't have to punt, I wouldn't have gotten loose," Gardocki said, smiling.

Gardocki did get into the game, though: He's the holder for kicker Jeff Reed.

This will be Gardocki's first game in Cleveland since leaving the Browns and signing in free agency with the Steelers. He ranks fifth in the AFC with a 43.6-yard average and hasn't had a bad game since Week 2 in Baltimore, when he averaged just 40.9 yards and had punts of 32, 33 and 39 yards.

"The bad ones you get out of your mind, and you go forward," Gardocki said. "That's part of football, no matter what position you are.

"Things like that happen, and those things you got to forget about and move on. That's part of being a professional -- consistency. That's the name of the game when you become an NFL player, just being consistent and bouncing back."

Gardocki has been a model of consistency for 14 seasons: He has punted 1,015 times and never had a kick blocked, an NFL record.

%% Award winners %%

Quarterback Ben Roethlisberger was named the NFL's Rookie of the Week in a voting by fans on NFL.com.

It was the sixth consecutive game Roethlisberger has won the award.

Running back Jerome Bettis was named the FedX Ground Player of the Week for rushing for 149 yards in the victory last week against the Eagles.

title: Jaguars' Smith revered his dad's friend Payton
content:
Jacksonville Jaguars star Jimmy Smith makes a strong case for
being the NFL's best wide receiver. Smith led the league with
116 catches last season to cap his third consecutive Pro Bowl
campaign. He has picked up exactly where he left off this season.After two weeks, Smith leads the league in receptions (21) and
yards from scrimmage (343). Smith chatted recently with USA TODAY's
Jarrett Bell:

Q: If you didn't play football, what might you be doing to
make a living?

A: I don't have a clue. I hate these questions. I look at football
as a way to get a head start on life. I was a business major (at
Jackson State) and got my degree, but I really didn't want to
have to use that. So, if I didn't have football, I'd have a decent
job. Probably in finance. Maybe trading stocks. That's something
I enjoy doing.

Q: What was your first job?

A: Bagging groceries.

Q: If you were stranded on an island, who would you want there
with you?

A: I'd get in trouble with my wife if I said, Halle Berry, Tyra
Banks . . . But really, I'd want to have my immediate
family around. My wife, Sandra; my son, my mom, my dad and my
sister.

Q: Who's your favorite all-time sports figure?

A: Walter Payton. I had a relationship with him, going to Jackson
State. He was a friend of my father. And we're still good friends
with Eddie Payton, who coaches golf over there. Walter was the
person I looked up to the most, besides my father."

Q: OK, the Paul Tagliabue question. If you were NFL commissioner
 . . .

A: I would get rid of the salary cap.

Q: What if your house is burning down, and your family is safe.
What material things would you go back and get?

A: I guess, even though they're insured, my two Super Bowl rings
from Dallas. My wife would be getting the photo albums. And I'd
make sure I got my wallet because I hate going to the DMV to get
driver's licenses.

Q: Tell me something that annoys you?

A: Arrogance. Arrogant people in general.

Q: Who's had the biggest impact on your career?

A: My mother and father, Etta and Jimmy Sr. When I was at the
lowest point in my career in Dallas, they were there for me every
day. My mom kept preaching, "a setback is a setup for a comeback."
She'd always say that. It went in one ear and out the other. I
heard it a thousand times. But she planted it in my brain, and
I used that for motivation to get back on the right track. It's
so important for parents to invest in their kids, and my parents
definitely invested in me.

Q: What CDs would we find in the Jimmy Smith ride right now?

A: You'd find Donnel Jones, Busta Rhymes and The Lox.

Q: Do you have a favorite movie?

A: I've got a couple. Young Guns, I and II. And
Braveheart.

Q: And what will you NOT watch on TV?

A: Soaps. And those real silly sitcoms.

title: 2DAY
content:
11.26.09 THE GAME PLAN

THE BUZZ You can watch close to 10 hours of NFL games. You can eat and drink in copious amounts. You can nap at will. Yes, there are many reasons to give thanks.

REMOTE PATROL That said, the best single thing on TV today might be the Gophers men's basketball game against No. 12 Butler (7:30 p.m, ESPN2).

Click pick If there's anything about the history of this grand day you've ever wondered about, chances are you'll find it at www.history.com/thanksgiving.

STAR GAZING Recently signed offensive lineman Jon Runyan, 35, of the Chargers is retiring at the end of the year to run for Congress in New Jersey.

FAST FACT Brady Quinn said it would be a "horrible decision" for Notre Dame to fire Charlie Weis. Quinn, who QBs the Browns, must know a lot about bad decisions.

WHAT THEY MIGHT BE DOING TODAY ...

Daunte Culpepper might be less mobile today than a one-legged turkey.

NFL fans might wish the Giants vs. Denver game was earlier and on network TV.

The Cowboys might be wary of the upset-pulling Raiders, especially with Dallas struggling to score points.

THE LINE

-Jason Snelling -4 over Rock Cartwright: The Rock has gone from No. 3 to No. 1 in the injury-riddled Redskins backfield, but take Snelling's pumpkin pie-soft matchup vs. Tampa.

-Knowshon Moreno -6 over Mike Bell: We smell a lot of points coming in tonight's game, and Denver would be smart to lean on young Moreno.

-Alex Smith -3 over Vince Young: If you do have a concussed QB, Smith is our favorite waiver-wire grab this week. We're bold enough to say he'll be a top-10 QB in Week 12.

Last week: 1-1

Season: 17-14-1

Medcalf: Developments with Mbakwe

Last week, a Miami court gave Gophers power forward Trevor Mbakwe a Jan. 7 pre-trial date, when his lawyer and prosecutors will give a judge an update on the status of his case. That wipes out the previous Dec. 14 trial date. Mbakwe is charged with felony battery after an alleged assault on a woman in April in Miami. Following a victory over Utah Valley, Tubby Smith said Mbakwe wasn't on the team's bench because of a "change" in his case. New court documents indicate that a number of factors are pushing Mbakwe's trial back. Mbakwe's roommate at Miami Dade Community College, Kentucky guard Darnell Dodson, is a key witness. He's averaging 12.0 points per game for the Wildcats. Mbakwe's lawyer, Gregory Samms, encountered challenges when he tried to secure Dodson's testimony earlier this month. That was when the trial was still scheduled for Dec. 14. But in another development that could end up helping Mbakwe get back on the court faster, his lawyer also has had trouble tracking down the alleged victim for her testimony. From court docs: "The alleged victim ... was attempted to be served on this Tuesday, November 10, and the undersigned was informed by his process server that [the alleged victim] no longer lived at that apartment." Samms requested the state's assistance in tracking down the central person in Mbakwe's case.

Gold in the Barn: www.startribune.com/gophers

title: Overhaul unlikely after another playoff flop
content:
Zach Thomas headed out of the Dolphins locker room and into the offseason Monday carrying a book titled Dare to Succeed.

The paperback had been gathering dust in his locker.

"Somebody sent it in the mail, and it's been in there about a month," Thomas said with a wry smile. "From the way I played Sunday, you can tell I didn't read it."Compiled from Wire ReportsDOLPHINS.15

Perhaps the book should be required offseason reading for the Dolphins, who staged another postseason collapse by losing to the Ravens 20-3 Sunday.

The Dolphins are the only team to reach the playoffs each of the past five seasons, but they've made a quick exit every time by a combined score of 164-16. In those games, Miami has one touchdown.

"After five years of doing the same thing, something needs to change," defensive end Jason Taylor said.

But despite the repeated playoff drubbings, tweaks are more likely than an overhaul. Coach Dave Wannstedt will return for his third season, and the coaching staff, defensive personnel and offensive playbook will stay mostly the same.

"We have a good nucleus here," Wannstedt said. "We're a lot closer to the top than some people would like to believe."

Wannstedt must choose an offensive coordinator to replace Chan Gailey, recently hired as Georgia Tech coach. Quarterback coach Mike Shula and Chargers offensive coordinator Norv Turner are the leading candidates.

Changes are also possible at running back and even quarterback, where Jay Fiedler, who has played for two years in the shadow of Dan Marino's legacy, now must endure comparisons with another likely Hall of Famer: Troy Aikman.

The offseason commenced with a quarterback controversy thanks to broadcast reports that Aikman might come out of retirement and join the team.

Wannstedt said he wants Fiedler back and said the Dolphins have not talked with Aikman. But Wannstedt, an assistant in Dallas during Aikman's prime, declined to say whether Miami would be interested in Aikman if he wants to play next season.

Aikman, 35, led the Cowboys to three Super Bowl titles in 12 seasons before retiring in April. Ten concussions and a bad back contributed to his decision, and Jan. 6 he said it was "extremely doubtful" he would play next season.

Aikman finishes the first season of his two-year Fox deal with Saturday's Eagles-Bears playoff game. The contract has no escape clause, network spokesman Dan Bell said Monday.

Still, Fox would allow Aikman to leave, Bell said, just as it did Bob Brenly to manage the Diamondbacks last season and Matt Millen to preside over the Lions.

Whatever happens, the Dolphins will remain a team built on defense and ball control, which means they must find a ground game.

"That has to improve," Wannstedt said. "We struggled with that all year."

Six starters will be free agents, and the most likely to depart are running back Lamar Smith, safety Brock Marion and tackle Spencer Folau.

The Dolphins gained 3.5 yards a carry, third-worst in the NFL, and were outgained 226-46 by Baltimore.

Shoring up the defensive front seven is another goal. The run defense turned soft when tackle Daryl Gardener was sidelined at midseason, and his future is uncertain because of chronic back trouble.

"We definitely missed Daryl," Thomas said. "But we've had everybody healthy and gotten run all over, even with Daryl in there, so that's not the excuse."

The Dolphins finished 11-6 for the second season in a row but were 2-6 against teams that made the playoffs.

title: Cowboy is warned after talk of revenge
content:
Dallas Cowboys cornerback Terence Newman has been put on notice by the NFL after indicating his desire for revenge on Detroit quarterback Jon Kitna.

Posted on Newman's locker Thursday was a letter he received from the NFL after making comments about Kitna during a satellite radio interview. Newman refused comment to reporters when he walked through the locker room.

"Be advised that your comments will now compel us to carefully monitor your activities in this weekend's Cowboys-Lions game," read part of the letter signed by Ray Anderson, NFL executive vice president for football operations. "As you know, flagrant fouls may subject a player not only to fines but to suspension as well. So conduct yourself accordingly."

Kitna threw four touchdown passes in the regular-season finale in Dallas last year to end a 3-13 season for Detroit. In an interview the following week with a radio station in Seattle, where the Cowboys had to go for the playoffs, Kitna called out the Dallas defense, notably Newman and linebacker Bradie James.

The Cowboys (11-1), who have already clinched another playoff spot this season, play at Detroit on Sunday. Kitna's bulletin-board remarks from January have been widely discussed.

While Newman hasn't spoken to local media this week, he issued what amounted to a warning for Kitna during an interview Tuesday on Sirius NFL Radio.

"Basically what it boils down to is you've got to watch what you say. Your mouth can't write checks that your (expletive) can't cash. That's what it comes down to," Newman said. "Everybody's going to see those quotes. He better just hope I don't blitz off the edge, because I've got 15, 25, 30 (thousand dollars), however much it would be for a fine. I've got that much for one fine. Revenge will be sweet definitely."

TAYLOR KILLING - At least one suspect charged in the killing of Washington Redskins star Sean Taylor was part of an earlier break-in at the player's home, according to newspaper reports.

Investigators believe they have connected a single suspect to the Nov. 18 break-in at Taylor's suburban Miami home, the Miami Herald reported. The Herald cited unnamed law enforcement sources who were familiar with the Taylor case.

STEELERS - Despite all the clamor, Pittsburgh safety Anthony Smith isn't taking back his words. He guaranteed a victory over the New England Patriots and, on Thursday, said he's not backing down.

Smith, a second-year safety from Syracuse, insists he's not trying to make a name for himself. "I just felt we're going to go out there, we're going to have a good week of practice and everybody's focused, and we're going to get a victory," Smith said.

Defensive back Tyrone Carter pulled Smith aside when he arrived at the Steelers' practice complex Thursday. "I said, 'You can't really do stuff like that,'" Carter said. "You've got to really think about it, the stuff that you say. I don't care how you feel about it, you've still got to think about it."

The Patriots, meanwhile, have their own guarantee: They'd never guarantee a victory like Smith did. Some of them can't even guarantee they'd recognize him.

"I didn't know who he was until we started preparing for them," Pats quarterback Tom Brady said.

TITANS - Suspended NFL player Adam "Pacman" Jones pleaded no contest in Las Vegas to a reduced charge in a strip club melee preceding a triple shooting that left a man paralyzed.

title: Injury won't stop me, says delighted Graham Bowl and beautiful
content:
`If someone asked me six or seven weeks ago if I would be in this position, I would have paid them a million dollars.' -- BEN GRAHAM

FORMER Geelong football star Ben Graham vows injury won't prevent a fairytale end to his tumultuous American football season in the National Football League's Super Bowl.

The former Cats captain's Arizona Cardinals won in a thrilling 32-25 comeback victory against the Philadelphia Eagles yesterday to book a berth in the biggest event on the American sports calendar.

But Graham may have aggravated a groin injury as the Cardinals collected the National Football Conference (NFC) title and gave their delirious fans hope of only their second NFL crown and first since 1947.

If the 35-year-old, who left the AFL in 2004 to pursue a career as a punter in the NFL, can overcome the injury he will make history as the first Australian to play in the Super Bowl when the Cardinals meet the Pittsburgh Steelers in the decider in Tampa Bay on February 1.

Pittsburgh downed Baltimore 23-14 to win the American Football Conference (AFC) crown.

``I just cannot believe how it's turned out,'' said Graham, standing in midfield as confetti cascaded from the roof of the Cardinals' massive indoor stadium.

``If someone asked me six or seven weeks ago if I would be in this position I would have paid them a million dollars.''

While Graham celebrated on the field with his wife Katie and daughters Sophie, 9, and Rosie, 7, fellow Aussie punter and former AFL star Sav Rocca was devastated.

Rocca's Eagles led 25-24 late in the final quarter.

But the Cardinals snatched the Super Bowl berth when veteran quarterback Kurt Warner capped a brilliant passing game with an eight-yard touchdown pass to running back Tim Hightower to claim the lead with just under three minutes to go.

``It's obviously very disappointing for the team to come this far,'' an upset Rocca said.

``We almost pulled it off at the end, but it didn't quite work out.

``I think we let them get too far ahead at half-time.''

As devastated as former Collingwood and North Melbourne star Rocca was, he congratulated his old sparring partner Graham.

``Ben deserves it,'' Rocca said. ``He's had a tough year and played great today.''

Graham was hoping for a quiet day, but was called on to punt five times, testing the groin each time.

His first punt sprayed off the left side of his foot and went out of bounds, picking up just 34 yards, while he grimaced on at least two others that did not have his usual power.

Graham averaged just 37.2 yards for his five punts.

Rocca was called on just once, but booted a huge 58-yard effort in the second half to pin the Cardinals deep in their own territory.

Graham said his trainers had worked on the groin the past week and there was no way it would keep him out of the Super Bowl.

``Hell no,'' he said.

The story of how Graham ended up in the finals is like the script from a Hollywood movie, having started the season with the New York Jets, his employers since 2005, and then being sacked, re-signed and sacked again by the Jets before being picked up by the New Orleans Saints.

After just one game with the Saints, Graham's contract was waived and after eight weeks without a job he was picked up by Arizona.

title: PATRIOTS TRY TO REBUILD AFTER 3-GAME SKID
content:
For the New England Patriots, the three consecutive wins that started the season are a distant memory -- almost as irrelevant as their long-ago Super Bowl victory.

We re 3-3, tight end Christian Fauria said Monday. Six games, and we have nothing to show for it.

After starting the season with seemingly impressive victories that ran their overall winning streak to 13 games, the Patriots have completely collapsed.

Sunday s 28-10 loss to the Green Bay Packers was a collection of penalties, turnovers and mental mistakes that kept New England from putting any drives together and prompted coach Bill Belichick to say he was ready to start over.

I think we re at the point where we need to rebuild this a little bit. That s what we re going to start to do, he said Monday before the start of the team s well-timed bye week. We can't play like we played yesterday or it ll be a long year.

Even the Patriots three wins don t look as good any more now that the teams they beat -- the Steelers, Jets, and Chiefs, all of whom were expected to be contenders -- have stumbled to a combined record of 6-10. Losses to San Diego and Miami hinted at a problem, but the Patriots thought they could make it go away with minor adjustments.

I don t think anybody expected this, defensive lineman Steve Martin said. We didn t hit it on the head. We ve got to get to the core of what it is. We ve got to take the bye week and evaluate everything.

Belichick was short on specifics, other than to say everything will be considered. Wide receiver Troy Brown s list of improvements included running better routes, protecting the quarterback better and making better decisions.

I think everybody s disappointed, said Brown, who missed the first two losses with a sore knee. It s surprising, but for the most part it s just disappointing that we let ourselves get to this point."

One thing that won t happen is wholesale roster moves. It s not that Belichick wouldn t like to make them, but there isn t much available and the Patriots don t have salary cap space to add someone who could make a big difference.

There aren t a lot of options there, Belichick said. I m not ruling it out 100 percent. I m not saying that it couldn t happen, but there are very few options.

The play that spelled doom for the Patriots on Sunday also showed why they re struggling -- a combination of physical and mental mistakes and bad breaks at the worst possible time.

Trailing 7-3 late in the second quarter, Tom Brady spun to his left to throw a screen to Kevin Faulk. The ball went through Faulk s hands and hit the ground, but the officials correctly saw that it was a backward pass and never blew the whistle.

Even so, both teams stopped playing. Eventually, Green Bay lineman Kabeer Gbaja-Biamila outraced Patriots lineman Stephen Neal for the ball, giving the Packers a first-and-goal from the New England's 8 On the next play, Brett Favre threw to Ahman Green for a touchdown, and it was never close again. Neal hurt his knee on the play and did not return.

It was a total, total breakdown of alertness and being aware of the situation, Belichick said. You don t hear a whistle, you keep playing and we didn t. And it cost us.

title: Dolphins drowning ahead of London trip
content:
Sunday October 28 promises to be a momentous day in the history of the NFL. For the first time ever, two teams will play in a regular-season game outside North America when the Miami Dolphins "host" the New York Giants at Wembley Stadium. To give an indication of how eagerly anticipated the game is on this side of the Atlantic, there were requests for more than 500,000 tickets within 72 hours of its announcement, and all tickets were sold within two hours of their release. Everyone from Chelsea's Didier Drogba and John Terry - honorary Giants captains for the day - to London mayor Ken Livingstone has been trying to grab a bit of positive publicity from the game, and the NFL has launched several PR events to ensure it maximises its commercial potential in Europe. All well and good then. There's just one problem: to use the American parlance, the two teams involved stink.

A friend of mine - a staunch Dolphins fan - sent me a depressing email this morning, suggesting that flying New York and Miami to the UK was like the Premier League staging a game between Aston Villa and Derby County in the US. Harsh, perhaps, on the Giants, who after losing to the NFC's two best teams - the Green Bay Packers and the Dallas Cowboys - are showing signs of a recovery, sparked by London-born Osi Umenyiora's remarkable club-record six sacks against the Philadelphia Eagles last Sunday. And harsh, too, on Derby, who have at least won a game this season.

The Dolphins have a 100% losing record after four games, and should they lose again this Sunday to the Houston Texans it will be their eighth successive defeat dating back to last season, equalling the longest losing streak in the franchise's history. From top to bottom Miami have been one of the worst-run teams in the league this decade, and there is little sign of an upturn of fortunes in the near future.

Their poor management on draft day is symptomatic of their wider problems. In six of the last 11 drafts the Dolphins didn't have a first-round selection who ever played for the team, and only three players in their current roster were taken from the first round - one of whom is an average left tackle, Vernon Carey, and another is this year's pick, wide receiver Ted Ginn Jr. Ginn was controversially taken ahead of, among others, Brady Quinn, and his contribution to the cause so far reads several unexceptional kick-off and punt returns, and one reception for 15 yards. Compare that to, for example, Dwayne Bowe, the Kansas City Chiefs' choice at No23, who has 18 receptions for 299 yards and three touchdowns, and you begin to understand the Dolphins' fans disillusionment.

Miami's dealings in other rounds of the draft have been equally fruitless, in part because they have regularly frittered away picks in a desperate, and unsuccessful, search for a franchise quarterback, someone to replace the great Dan Marino. The current incumbent is Trent Green, a favourite of coach Cam Cameron. Green has had the sort of ordinary season widely expected of a 37-year-old, with five touchdowns and seven interceptions thrown in the four games. To compound matters, the two quarterbacks ditched by the Dolphins in the off-season have already had a measure of success. An impressive performance from Joey Harrington last weekend gave him his first victory with the beleaguered Atlanta Falcons, while Daunte Culpepper - a complete flop at Dolphin Stadium 12 months ago - had a hand in all five of the Oakland Raiders' touchdowns in their defeat of Miami on Sunday.

With no structured method in place to produce players of the requisite standard, the Dolphins have resorted to signing over-the-hill free agents. Whereas the New England Patriots have a Moneyball-esque knack of bringing in undervalued players and improving them through their outstanding coaching system, Miami prefer to splash out $20m on Joey Porter, a linebacker with dodgy knees who was released by the Pittsburgh Steelers.

It is no wonder, then, that Jason Taylor, their star man who considered retirement last year, is rumoured to be mulling over a move away before the October 16 trade deadline. The NFL will hope for its sake that he doesn't - Taylor has been the lone Dolphin around whom it has based its entire marketing campaign for the London game. If he leaves, so does any hope of a Miami victory.

Adding to the Dolphins' pain this week was Brett Favre breaking Marino's all-time touchdown record. Packers quarterback Favre threw for his 421st score against the Minnesota Vikings, with the remarkable reception that the fans of the Packers' great rivals gave him proving just what an achievement that is. Despite the Packers' almost non-existent running game, Favre, 38 next Friday, continues to defy the odds in maintaining Green Bay's perfect start to the season.

Adding to the Dolphins' pain this week, part II: the Indianapolis Colts and the Cowboys may also be yet to taste defeat, but there has been little in the Patriots' performances so far to counter the growing belief that they could become only the second team to record a perfect season after the 1972 Dolphins. New England have now won their opening four games by more than 20 points, the first team to do so since the Buffalo All-Americans in 1920. The biggest threat to the Patriots' perfection could come in Week 15, if they choose to rest players ahead of the play-offs when their visitors are ... Miami.

Why the NFL is so difficult to predict: only three of last year's eight division winners have a record above .500. Indeed, the combined tally of the San Diego Chargers - who had the league's best record last year - and the two teams who contested the NFC Championship game in January, the Chicago Bears and the New Orleans Saints, is 2-9.

Why the NFL is so difficult to predict, part II: the Tampa Bay Buccaneers finished rock bottom of the NFC South last year, yet are top this time around having started 3-1. But hold on: the 2006 NFC South winners, the Saints, had finished last in 2005; the 2005 winners, the Buccaneers, had finished last in 2004; the 2004 winners, the Falcons, had finished last in 2003; and the 2003 winners, the Carolina Panthers, had finished last in 2002. Perhaps the NFL isn't so difficult to predict after all.

title: FANTASY REALITY EDITION
content:
Before we put a bow on another successful Fantasy Reality season and offer up a special edition year-end guide, a word from our special guest, former NFL coach Jim Mora, on the upcoming fantasy playoffs.

Jim Mora: PLAYOFFS??!!!!!

Chris Carr: Well put, coach. Thanks for stoping by. RandBall, we have just one shot to draw up a game plan for our loyal readers who have stuck with us through the my best of times and your worst of times. We need to map out a plan for Weeks 14 through 16. What does the RandBall crystal ball tell us today?

Michael Rand: Week 14 is for suckers. But for those who don't have a first-round bye, I'm willing to go the distance. I've talked about this before, but I cannot stress the KISS method enough. (Go ahead and make your requisite Tiger Woods joke right there.) But really, it means Keep It Simple, Stupid. For instance: Don't bench Drew Brees,like one guy I know did last week.

Carr: Anyone benching Brees puts his or her right to play fantasy football at risk. RandBall and I have special media access to this type of information and we will not hesitate to yank your FTOL (Fantasy Team Owner License) if you bench Brees. Not benching Brees is one tip, here are many more that will help you bring home a league title:

THIS WEEK: WEEK 14

The good news Two guys that you're thinking about benching but shouldn't: Ryan Grant and Ray Rice. Both had rough MNF games, but they have juicy matchups on this short week (short weeks often mean more running, less passing). Definitely start Rice vs. Detroit, and Grant's matchup against struggling Chicago ain't too shabby either. ... Alex Smith is now a startable fantasy quarterback. Stop making us repeat ourselves. It's true. Pick him up and start him and stop being tempted by Brady Quinn and Bruce Gradkowski. ... Your Saints should go marching in (sorry ... really ... so sorry) to the end zone against a bad and discouraged Atlanta D.

The bad news One word: Questionable. Lots of injuries out there, so keep a close eye on your favorite sports section and the internets. Michael Turner, Hines Ward, Rashard Mendenhall, any Eagle --all valuable, and all dinged up. But where there's an injury (Ward?), there's a potential super-sub (Mike Wallace!). ... Two guys not questionable: Clinton Portis and Steve Slaton, both done for the year. We'd stay away from any Texans or Redskins replacements. ... The Bengals have been playing grind-it-out games lately. Don't count on huge numbers from your homegrown Vikings players.

NEXT WEEK: WEEK 15

The good news Knowshon Moreno owners should be savoring his productivity, his red-zone carries and his matchup against Oakland's 30th-ranked run defense. You watched Kurt Warner shred the Vikings; imagine what he and his receivers will do against the Lions.

The bad news We quote a fellow Strib Fantasy owner: "Anyone who picked Matt Forte this year will never pick him again." Tough to argue, and with Lovie Smith saying he'd prefer to spread the carries around Forte belongs on your bench. Definitely sit him in Week 15 vs. Baltimore.

CHAMPIONSHIP WEEK: WEEK 16

The good news The word from Sean Payton, via SI, that he will not take his foot off the gas down the stretch is simply great news for owners of any of the high-flying Saints, and for anyone who's ever cringed when an owner loses a fantasy playoff game because a star resting for real playoff game takes a seat. The Saints host Tampa Bay here, so expect both fireworks and late-game rests.

The bad news Two games that have low-scoring, must-win, dogfight written all over them: Pittsburgh-Baltimore and Dallas-Washington.

FANTASY PLAYOFFS MVP

Carr's pick: Tom Brady, QB, Patriots

"What's wrong with the Patriots?! What's wrong with Brady?! Ahhh!" Shhhhh! New papa Brady, surrounded by weapons, will shake off sleep deprivation and rally against the Panthers, Bills and Jaguars. Three huge games.

Rand's pick: Drew Brees,QB, Saints

He's been great all year, and as long as the Saints keep pressing forward, what's not to love about matchups against Atlanta, Dallas and Tampa Bay?

FANTASY PLAYOFFS BUST

Carr's pick: DeAngelo Williams, RB, Panthers

Last year's playoff hero is headed for a letdown. He gets a lot of his points off big plays, which will be harder to come by now that he's banged-up. Add a brutal schedule on top of that and you have one big pile of disappointment.

Rand's pick: Adrian Peterson, RB, Vikings

Bengals (Week 14) are No. 2 vs. run and Bears (16) sold out to stop it last time and figure to do the same in cold weather. (Note: A determined AD could prove everyone wrong, which would be delightful.)

FINAL (VERY RANDOM) THOUGHT

Carr: Guys you can pick up now who might bring you a title and win the annual Billy Volek Memorial Award: Alex Smith, Mike Wallace, Fred Jackson, Jason Snelling, Robert Meachem, Kenny Britt and Fred Davis.

Rand: Write down everything that went wrong this year and e-mail it to yourself. Then throw it away in August because it's all luck, anyway.

title: White wins Big East honor
content:
West Virginia quarterback Pat White was named the Big East offensive player of the week after having a hand in five touchdowns to lead the Mountaineers to a 35-21 win against Louisville. He ran for 200 yards to become the NCAA career leader for rushing yards by a quarterback. He also threw for two scores. With 98 career touchdowns, White broke Donovan McNabb's record for Big East career touchdowns.

West Virginia kicker Pat McAfee, a Plum graduate, is a finalist for the Ray Guy award, which is presented to college football's best punter. Oklahoma State's Matt Fodge and Utah's Louie Sakoda are also finalists.

title: T.O. to play Super Sunday? No one is saying he won't
content:
He leaps and he lands and he leads cheers like a kid with pompoms, you should excuse the imagery. But can Terrell Owens play football in Jacksonville on Feb. 6?

It sure sounds that way, even as Eagles coach Andy Reid attempts to dampen expectations that he fueled himself on the victory podium Sunday night after the NFC Championship Game.

"He's going to continue his rehab," Reid said, after T.O. had spent Sunday garnering a ridiculous amount of pretty energetic airtime on the telecast of the Eagles' win over Atlanta.

"Somewhere in the next few days he's going to try to run on it and jog on it and see what he can do," Reid said. "We'll progress from there. He's coming in every day for treatment and he's been working in the pool and running in the pool, it's just a matter of him getting out and trying to land without the water."

Asked if there would need to be another X-ray and a doctor's final approval before Owens might play, Reid said: "Yeah, he'll be in touch with the doctor and we'll see what happens there. Then it comes down to, can he do it? He has a couple of different things in there. He has the pain from the broken part of the leg - he really doesn't have much with the ankle. He has to run on it. We have to see how that holds up when he runs."

Reid said Owens wanted to play against Atlanta, and uses terms like "champing at the bit." But even if T.O. did play in the Super Bowl, you'd have to wonder about his effectiveness. And when somebody asked quarterback Donovan McNabb if Owens might be valuable even as a decoy, McNabb bristled.

"Believe me, if T.O is out there, he won't be a decoy," McNabb said. "If you asked him that, that is kind of a disrespectful thing to say for him. I mean, 'You would just be out there to run dummy routes.' We brought T.O in here for a reason and T.O has done a wonderful job for us. If T.O. is out there, T.O. will catch some balls. I mean, not to take anything away from the rest of the guys, because they will catch balls as well and they are the reason why we made it this far and we are looking forward to competing together.

"T.O. has told me that he is going to do everything he can to get back. We all know with injuries and all, the mind-set is that you are going to be back, but obviously your body [can] tell you a totally different thing."

And, with that, we wait.

As you might expect, updates to follow. *

title: AMERICAN FOOTBALL: STEELERS CUT AFTER STROLL AGAINST INDY
content:
PITTSBURGH continued their steady march back to the top of the Super Bowl betting with a convincing 28-10 home victory over Indianapolis on Monday night, writes Phil Agius.

The pre-season favourites to win the AFC looked all at sea when heavily beaten by New England and Oakland in their first two matches, but have since won three of their last four.

Hines Ward picked up Pittsburgh's first and fourth touchdowns on 17 and 14-yard tosses from veteran quarterback Tommy Maddox, who is helping the fans forget Kordell Stewart, the No. 1 at the start of the season. Coach Bill Cowher's side are now in a three-way battle for AFC North honours with Baltimore and Cleveland. Cowher said: "I'm happy with where the team is right now. We have to sustain this play."

The Steelers were cut from 14-1 to 10-1 for the Super Bowl by both Bet365 and Ladbrokes which is only two points bigger than their pre-season quote.

Elsewhere in the league,

Miami completed the signing of receiver Cris Carter and Dallas announced that quarterback Quincy Carter would be replaced by former baseball ace Chad Hutchinson when they host Seattle on Sunday.

title: Sports briefs
content:
Football

NEBRASKA A.D. LEAVES: Nebraska athletic director Bill Byrne will leave the school to take the same position at Texas A&M, he said Tuesday night.

"I thought I could try my hand one more time at building a program," said Byrne, who came to Nebraska in 1992 from Oregon. "It's going to be extremely hard to leave, but I look forward to the challenges."

Among Byrne's first duties with the Aggies will be hiring a football coach to replace R.C. Slocum, who was fired Monday.

MADDOX TO START: Steelers coach Bill Cowher didn't hesitate Tuesday to announce Tommy Maddox will start against Houston, mostly so it wouldn't distract his team. Last week, Cowher waited to pick Kordell Stewart as his quarterback, mostly to distract their opponent.

Cowher, determined not to let a quarterback debate divide a team that is 6-1-1 in its last eight games, said Maddox was playing too well before getting hurt Nov. 17 to lose his job to an injury.

The decision was difficult, mostly because Cowher will sit down a hot quarterback -- something NFL coaches rarely do. Stewart played virtually error-free in the two-plus games Maddox was out, completing 51 of 69 passes for 562 yards, three touchdowns and one interception and running for 135 yards. --

Golf

WOMAN TO PLAY PGA EVENT: Suzy Whaley, a 36-year-old Connecticut golf pro, has decided to play in next year's Greater Hartford Open, which would make her the first woman to qualify for a PGA Tour event and play in it.

Whaley earned an automatic spot in the tournament when she won the Connecticut PGA section championship in September.

AUGUSTA SLAMMED: Augusta National Golf Club showed no signs Tuesday of bowing to pressure to allow women to join, even after a former television executive became the first member to defect from the club over the issue.

Former CBS chief executive Thomas H. Wyman leveled a parting shot while resigning from the club where he has been a member for 25 years, calling Augusta National's stand on female members "pigheaded" and saying up to a quarter of the club's 300 members feel the same way he does.

Augusta National officials took the resignation in stride and said it would not change the club's position that it will decide when to admit women members on its own and that there will be none by the Masters tournament in April.

"We are disappointed that Mr. Wyman has chosen to publicize a private matter," club spokesman Glenn Greenspan said. "While we respect the fact that there are differences of opinion on this issue, we intend to stand firm behind our right to make what are both appropriate and private membership choices." --

Hockey

ROOKIE SUSPENDED: The New Jersey Devils suspended rookie center Mike Danton on Tuesday for failing to report to the minors.

Danton was assigned to the AHL's Albany River Rats on Friday after complaining about playing time.

This is the second straight year that Danton and Devils general manager Lou Lamoriello have had problems. Danton sat out most of last season after disagreeing with Lamoriello about a medical diagnosis. The player failed to report to Albany then, too.

Baseball

DODGER MOVES: The Los Angeles Dodgers reached a tentative agreement with the Cubs on Tuesday to trade first baseman Eric Karros and second baseman Mark Grudzielanek to Chicago for catcher Todd Hundley.

The Cubs, retooling their roster under new manager Dusty Baker, and Dodgers were given 72 hours to finalize the deal. To make it happen, Hundley and Grudzielanek must agree to have their contracts restructured.

Basketball

NETS INJURED: The New Jersey Nets placed guards Kerry Kittles and Chris Childs on the injured list Tuesday and activated guard Brandon Armstrong. Kittles sprained his left knee in Saturday's game at Portland. He missed the 2000-01 season with a right knee injury.

Kittles started this season's first 17 games and is averaging 12.6 points.

Childs, signed as a free agent in the offseason, was suspended in October for failing to stay in shape while sidelined with an Achilles' tendon injury. He recently had treatment at a weight loss center.

Armstrong has been on the injured list since the start of the season with a lower back strain. --

Tennis

HEWITT, WILLIAMS HONORED: Lleyton Hewitt and Serena Williams were honored as world champions for 2002 by the International Tennis Federation on Tuesday.

Hewitt won the award for the second straight year, and Williams was cited for the first time.

Hewitt captured his first Wimbledon title and earned the season-ending No. 1 ranking for the second straight year by retaining his Masters Cup title in China last month.

The men's doubles world champions are Mark Knowles of the Bahamas and Canada's Daniel Nestor. The women's doubles award went to Argentina's Paola Suarez and Spain's Virginia Ruano Pascual. --

Olympics

WRESTLER SUSPENDED: Greco-Roman wrestler Tipton Peterson of Quantico, Va., was suspended from competition for two years after testing positive for steroids.

Peterson, 28, initially tested positive at the World Team Trials on June 23 in St. Paul, Minn., for an elevated testosterone/epitestosterone ratio, and subsequently was positive in three out-of-competition tests in July, August and September for methandienone and stanzolol, U.S. Anti-Doping Agency officials said Tuesday.

The suspension was effective Aug. 8 and will be carried out by USA Wrestling.

title:
Humbled Vikings pin hopes on home;
Defeat at St. Louis exposes weakness
content:
The way the Vikings were couching it Monday, their 40-29 loss at St. Louis the day before came about as a perfect fusion of two huge advantages for the Rams: They were playing on their home artificial turf, and the health of their starting quarterback and running back had improved dramatically during the week.

     With the atmosphere in the Trans World Dome reminiscent of last season's playoff mayhem, Kurt Warner once again scattering laser passes around the field and Marshall Faulk's surgically-repaired knee looking better than ever, the Rams were at their best Sunday. With their game against Green Bay scheduled for Sunday at the Metrodome, the Vikings hope they can duplicate the Rams' success. The Vikings have won 13 consecutive games at the Metrodome dating to last season, and they can clinch home-field advantage throughout NFC the playoffs with a victory Sunday.

   "Playing at home is the name of the game," Vikings coach Dennis Green said. "We were on the road [Sunday]. I think everybody would prefer playing at home than on the road. . . . If [the Rams] are healthy, they are legitimate defending world champions. I think there was no fluke that they won it last year. If they're healthy, they bring the type of team they brought last year."

     Despite the decisive loss Sunday afternoon, however, the Vikings remain in the driver's seat in the NFC playoff race. They can clinch the NFC Central even if they lose to the Packers, provided the Rams defeat Tampa Bay on Monday night, and there are scenarios possible that would give the Vikings home-field advantage throughout the playoffs even if they lose to the Packers and in the season finale at Indianapolis.

     The Vikings will take a bruised team into the game against the Packers. Special teams ace Chris Walsh broke a cheek bone and could be out until the playoffs, while fellow receiver Matthew Hatchette has a strained oblique muscle and likely will be listed as questionable for Sunday's game.

     The return of injured starters Dwayne Rudd and Orlando Thomas is still in question, and starting cornerbacks Robert Tate (knee) and Kenny Wright (ankle) also were hobbling after the loss to the Rams.

     So thorough was that defeat, in fact, that most Vikings were more than willing to put it out of their minds in its aftermath.

     "Our focus is now completely on Green Bay and winning home-field advantage so we don't have to go on the road and play," defensive lineman John Burrough said. "[The Trans World Dome] is a tough place to play."

     The Vikings have sustained all three of their losses on the road this season. Two have been one-sided affairs: a 41-13 loss to the Buccaneers along with Sunday's 11-point defeat at St. Louis. Green called the Vikings' 4-3 road record "average," and it is clear now more than ever that the Vikings' Super Bowl hopes might be contingent on securing home-field advantage.

     Their other loss, an overtime defeat at Green Bay, came when Packers receiver Antonio Freeman caught a pass tipped by former Vikings cornerback Cris Dishman. Played in a cold rain, the game went to overtime when the Vikings botched a potential winning field-goal attempt at the end of regulation.

     That play, along with Freeman's now-infamous immaculate deflection reception, illustrate how games can go awry for road teams in the NFL.

     Green made it a point at his weekly news conference Monday to establish the Vikings' confidence in playing at the Metrodome, where they last lost in October 1999. Asked to explain why their comfort level is so high there, Green said, "It's sports. It's part of sports. I really couldn't explain it to you in this period of time. Baseball, football, basketball, hockey. It's sports."

.

     Kevin Seifert can be contacted at kseifert@startribune.com

THE EAGLES insisted that they were more than just Terrell Owens' supporting cast, in the wake of the ankle injury that probably ended Owens' season. To some ears, those protests sounded thin. Their tone gained resonance yesterday, when the Eagles announced they had secured an NFL-high nine Pro Bowl berths, one for Owens and eight for that supporting cast. It was the most players from one team named in the initial selections since 1998.

There were players on the Eagles' list everyone expected to see - quarterback Donovan McNabb and T.O., each for the fifth time, and kicker David Akers, for the third. There were emerging players, such as cornerback Lito Sheppard and strong safety Mike Lewis, who were honored for the first time. There were a couple of guys who'd worked hard to get back to leaguewide accolades after missing out last season - free safety Brian Dawkins (going for the fourth time) and left offensive tackle Tra Thomas (third). There was a steady, veteran warrior who finally got his reward - linebacker Ike Reese, the Eagles' special-teams captain, was named the NFC special-teams player.

And there was a pleasant surprise, one that hints at why the Eagles still think they can make it to the Super Bowl without T.O.: middle linebacker Jeremiah Trotter didn't become the starter until Nov. 15, so he pretty much made the Pro Bowl on the basis of the last six games. Since then, he recorded 37 solo tackles, as the Eagles' defense soared from 24th overall to 10th, and their run defense improved from 27th to 11th.

"That's something you don't see a lot of, to see a guy start six games and play in such a way to get that much respect that fast. To me, that speaks volumes," said Dawkins. "He is a leader. In the huddle, there is a certain way he carries himself. He carries himself as a middle linebacker. There's a mentality that he plays with and tries to let that rub off on other guys . . . He's making plays, and that's what matters, in the end."

The Eagles never have had nine players selected for the Pro Bowl (2 years ago, seven were named initially, then 10 eventually went to Hawaii, after all the injury substitutions). But the Eagles have never been 13-1, either. There is an excellent chance this season's Pro Bowl count will hit double digits; a source close to the situation said defensive end Jevon Kearse is a first alternate and center Hank Fraley is a second alternate.

Running back Brian Westbrook, whose 1,515 yards from scrimmage rank fourth in the NFC, apparently is not in the picture. That seems strange, in that Westbrook is such an important weapon for the team that has dominated the NFC this season. But running backs tend to get Pro Bowl berths on their rushing yardage, and Westbrook is most dangerous as a receiver. He leads NFL running backs in receptions (73) and receiving yards (703). His 812 rushing yards on 177 carries rank ninth in the NFC.

The Eagles also could have hoped for a Pro Bowl berth for cornerback Sheldon Brown, a first-year starter who yesterday got the consolation prize of being named NFC defensive player of the week. Lewis said Brown was having the best year of anyone in the secondary. But it would have been quite a lot for the Eagles to get Brown on the team as well, since they already were sending three-fourths of their secondary to Hawaii.

Two years ago, Dawkins was part of an Eagles secondary that sent three of its four members to the Pro Bowl. When the team let free-agent corners Troy Vincent and Bobby Taylor walk after last season, many experts foresaw a dramatically downgraded unit.

"We've come a long way in a short period of time," Dawkins said. "The most important thing on a football field, and especially in a secondary, is trust. You have to trust that each guy can do his job and have a good time doing it and not have to worry about a guy. We have come a long way in building that trust together."

Lewis said Sheppard and Brown "were so ready to go out and prove the critics wrong."

Sheppard acknowledged that all the offseason doubts bothered him.

"There were a lot of things going through my mind, with the team, me stepping in, and all the critics and everything," said Sheppard, the Eagles' top draft pick in 2002. "It was a lot of pressure, and coming into this year, I just wanted to prove that I belong on this level - that I can play . . . One of my goals coming into this year was to prove people wrong."

That also was one of Trotter's goals, after he was released by the Washington Redskins and didn't attract much interest across the league. He'd made two Pro Bowls with the Eagles, then left in a dispute over money. His 2 years in Washington weren't what either Trotter or the Redskins had envisioned. He returned last July for 1 year at the veteran minimum - a price that is sure to go up this offseason. Then he played more than half the year getting most of his reps on special teams. It took the embarrassment of a 27-3 loss Nov. 7 in Pittsburgh to get defensive coordinator Jim Johnson to make Trotter a starter.

The impact, both physical and emotional, has been extraordinary.

"Trotter came into the huddle and he was that voice, that voice that we were missing, that leader that we needed in there," Lewis said yesterday.

Trotter was asked what he has done to change the defense so dramatically.

"If I could summarize it in one word, I would say attitude, the attitude of the defense," he said. "When I was just playing special teams, the attitude [of the defense] was, 'We're not that good against the run, and teams are going to come in every week and pound the ball on us, it's going to be a long day.' But I think the attitude now is, 'Bring your best, because we're going to bring our best . . . You're not going to just run the ball down our throat.' We still have the same people on the field, except for myself. That goes to show it's all about attitude, the mentality of different guys, coming to play, knowing it's going to be a physical game. Just playing with a lot of intensity, and then, every now and then, trying to make a play here or there." *

*

1. David Akers: The Eagles' kicker makes his third Pro Bowl appearance, on the strength of tying an NFL record with 16 field goals of 40 yards or longer this season. He leads the NFC in scoring with 117 points. Akers noted that Green Bay's Ryan Longwell and Seattle's Josh Brown also had fine years.

"We've attempted so many long field goals - I think we have 19 [attempts] over 40 yards, which is more than I have underneath 40 [11 attempts, 10 successful]," Akers said. "I feel really honored."

2. Brian Dawkins: The free safety returns for a fourth Pro Bowl after missing out last year, when a foot injury cost him nine games. Dawkins has four interceptions, three sacks and 92 tackles. Dawkins' dark-visored visage is the face of the Eagles' secondary.

"All you got to do is look into that shield and you'll know that he's going to give you 110 percent," strong safety Mike Lewis said of Dawkins yesterday.

3. Mike Lewis: The strong safety makes his first Pro Bowl in his second year as a starter. He has just one interception but leads the Eagles with 121 tackles.

"I know how much he wanted this," said Dawkins, contending the honor provided needed validation to the 2002 second-round pick.

"You look at the numbers, I would say no. Other guys are having better statistical years than I'm having," Lewis said, when asked if he'd thought his stats would get him to Hawaii.
