"At the same time, we're winning, and my presence is felt out there on the field, and my peers respect the way I play the game."

4. Donovan McNabb: This is McNabb's fifth Pro Bowl, but his first three, he was added late after Brett Favre pulled out. Last season, some observers were surprised McNabb was voted in, since he'd had a horrible first half of the season. This year, there is no debate, as the Birds' QB puts the finishing touches on his finest season. His 103.9 passer rating is second in the NFC only to the Vikings' Daunte Culpepper (110.1). McNabb's 63.7 completion percentage, 30 touchdown passes and 3,839 passing yards are career highs.

"It's just not individual stats for me," he said. "You can easily look at how many yards passing, touchdowns and all that and say, 'He's had a remarkable year,' but offensively, I think we've had a remarkable year."

Middle linebacker Jeremiah Trotter said people who write the Eagles off because they've lost Terrell Owens "are forgetting about that guy that wears No. 5. He's Superman."

5. Terrell Owens: While he was having ankle surgery in Baltimore yesterday, the wideout was named to his fifth Pro Bowl, in which he almost certainly won't be able to participate. With 77 catches for 1,200 yards, he is the first 1,000-yard receiver of the Andy Reid era. His 14 TD catches are a team record.

"When you bring in a guy who brings a different type of work ethic, a different attitude, a different passion to the game, I think it rubs off on the rest of the guys," Donovan McNabb said of Owens.

6. Ike Reese: The Birds' special-teams captain thought he had a better special-teams year last season, when he might not have had quite as strong a supporting cast. But he's been building a reputation for several years, and grabbing the NFC's lone Pro Bowl special-teams berth can be seen as the culmination of that work.

"It means that at least for 1 year, I was the best at my position on this level," Reese said. "To say that, it's something that can never be taken away from me. That's something I'll be able to brag about to my grandkids, my kids, for years from now."

7. Lito Sheppard: It's been quite a ride for the Eagles' 2002 first-round pick, who was widely derided when he had trouble getting on the field as a rookie, and whose ability to replace Troy Vincent as the No. 1 corner was openly questioned going into this season. Needless to say, this is his first Pro Bowl, in his first season as a full-time starter. His five interceptions are tied for second in the NFC.

"I'm only 23 years old. Hopefully, people see my hunger, my motivation," Sheppard said yesterday. He has consistently acknowledged that he has a lot more to learn. "I've been doubted pretty much all my life."

8. Tra Thomas: The left offensive tackle makes his third appearance, which might be his most satisfying after he was not chosen last season and endured the quiet dissatisfaction of Eagles coaches. Thomas slimmed down and played much more consistently this season. He was not available to talk to reporters yesterday.

9. Jeremiah Trotter: The middle linebacker's third Pro Bowl appearance was the biggest surprise among the Eagles' selections, since he didn't become the starter until Nov. 15. Remember, Trotter signed with the Eagles last summer only when other teams failed to make offers, following his release by the Redskins. But Trotter's muscle and attitude have transformed the Birds' defense, which has gone from 24th overall to 10th over the past 6 weeks, and from 27th to 11th against the run.

"I think it's a little bit sweeter, considering the road I had to travel from the beginning of the season and what I went through this offseason," Trotter said.

title: AMERICAN FOOTBALL: WHAT A SCALP, REDSKINS
content: SANTANA Moss converted two late touchdown passes from Mark Brunell to give the Washington Redskins an improbable 14-13 comeback win over the Dallas Cowboys in the NFL. "We just didn't quit," said Brunell.

title: Sam Donnellon: Vick taking the lead on being Eagles team leader
content:
ON A DAY when DeSean Jackson again pressed the mute button and most of the Eagles disappeared from the locker room before the media were allowed in, Michael Vick again blamed himself for Sunday's loss to the Bears.

"We had that game in the palm of my hands," he said at one point, adding moments later, "I committed a turnover that was the turning point of the game."

Vick also threw for 333 yards and two touchdowns, again took some vicious hits, and rallied the Eagles from an 18-point hole to make the game interesting until the end. But he was correct in saying that the end-zone interception turned the game upside down.

The odd thing was hearing him blame himself so forcefully, so without qualifiers.

After more than a decade of not hearing much of that from this position.

This is bound to incite again those who see a Donovan McNabb slight in any comparison, to reignite all those Charlie Brown-like charges of being unjustly picked on. So for the record, once again: McNabb was one of the best NFL quarterbacks for most of his career here. And in a more run-oriented offense that relied less on his accuracy and autonomy, he might have even won a championship.

But his admissions of culpability - when there even were admissions - were so muted, diluted and deflective that they had to annoy teammates as much as they did those seeking answers.

You know them by heart: It wasn't just his fault, it was everybody's fault. Everybody has to get on the same page.

That was him at his best.

At his less than best, he might have mentioned something about not enough weapons, about plays called, about running the ball more, refocusing for next week, about execution, about everybody doing his part, and things of that nature.

And/or he might have implied that he was being held to an unrealistic and unfair standard, as if culpability and credibility meant the same thing.

Vick still has much to prove to us, not the least of which is whether his apparent improved citizenship is a fleeting or lasting characteristic. But give him credit for yesterday, and for the way he has conducted his brief but productive stint as the Eagles quarterback. Coming off that tough loss and a late flight, with even part-time players hiding out yesterday, it would have been a perfect opportunity for him to play the quarterback card and be unavailable for comment.

Especially after coach Andy Reid reportedly tore into the team after Sunday's loss, especially after the terse and well-reported conversation Vick had with the mercurial Jackson, who was held to two catches, one for 21 yards, the other for 5.

Instead, Vick stood in front of a big, white message board and answered questions for 7 minutes, 15 seconds, owning the loss a few times for those who came in late.

The day was dominated with talk of Jackson, of course, especially after he again refused to speak with the media, disappearing quickly once the locker room was opened. He has faced double teams repeatedly this season, and his stats are way down, and it might be affecting his focus and performance. Jackson dropped a potential touchdown pass in each of the two games preceding Sunday's game, and at least one report had Reid singling him out during an uncharacteristic team tongue-lashing following Sunday's messy 31-26 loss.

Asked about it on his weekly Monday night show on WIP, Reid was in full defuse mode, telling Howard Eskin at one point, "You're reading into things."

Reid went on to say: "He's a phenomenal player, and defensive coordinators pay attention to him. If they're going to double him, it's going to give Jeremy [Maclin] a chance to make plays. It's going to give Brent Celek a chance to make plays."

All of that hinges on Vick making plays. He didn't do enough of that Sunday, he said, which played a part in the two men's, um, discussion after the loss.

As for Jackson? Vick said: "He took the loss very tough. Obviously, there were things he wanted to do better. But like I told him, there are things I wish I could have done better. So you can't get down on yourself."

Vick said the gist of Jackson's words to him were about "things . . . that we needed to correct," that the wide receiver was "my righthand man." The quarterback's tone, as was the case Sunday, was not adversarial, but rather empathetic. It's a subtle part of the job, playing shrink sometimes.

"He understands that it's not going to be a picnic," Vick said of Jackson at one point.

If he didn't before, those words should send a message.

So should this: "Sometimes things are going to happen, and you just have to bounce back from [them]. It was just me giving him a boost. And trying to get refocused."

It's his team. If that wasn't already clear, it is now. Vick is shouldering the position, extending himself in ways that have nothing to do with playcalling or execution. He takes blame. He puts out fires.

The best ones do it all the time. And it never ceases to impress. *

Send e-mail to

donnels@phillynews.com.

For recent columns, go to

http://go.philly.com/donnellon.

title:
PRO BOWL VOTERS PASS ON QB MCNABB;
RUNNING BACK ALSTOTT GETS PICKED AS FULLBACK
content:
Donovan McNabb may be one of the leading candidates for the NFL's MVP award. But the fans, players and coaches who vote for the Pro Bowl consider him only the fourth-best quarterback in the NFC.

The Philadelphia QB, who has been a one-man offense for a team that is 10-5 and has clinched a playoff berth after going 8-24 the past two seasons, was the most conspicuous absentee in the Pro Bowl voting, announced Thursday.

He finished as the first alternate behind Minnesota's Daunte Culpepper San Francisco's Jeff Garcia and the Rams' Kurt Warner. Warner missed 5 1/2 games with an injury and threw four interceptions in his first game back, while Garcia, who's had a breakout year, plays for a team that is 5-9.

"I think Donovan is the best quarterback in the league," said Chad Lewis, the Eagles' little-known tight end, who made the NFC team. "There's nobody I'd rather have playing. The other quarterbacks had big numbers so I guess that held Donovan back, but there's no doubt in my mind Donovan deserves to go."

McNabb's absence was one of several quirks in the Pro Bowl voting, which annually has strange selections and absentees.
 
This time:

* Mike Alstott, among an NFC-high eight Tampa Bay Buccaneers selected (Tennessee led the AFC with eight), was chosen as the fullback, even though he plays running back. Alstott also was benched after a series of fumbles and missed the last three games with a knee injury.

* Carolina's Wesley Walls was voted the first alternate at tight end in the NFC. He has missed most of the second half of the season after undergoing major knee surgery.

* Brian Griese of Denver was selected as one of the AFC quarterbacks, along with Oakland's Rich Gannon, who will start, and Indianapolis' Peyton Manning. Griese, who was having an outstanding year, has been out since Nov. 13 with a shoulder separation and the Broncos have won four in a row with Gus Frerotte at quarterback.

There was one milestone: Guard Bruce Matthews, 39, of Tennessee made it for the 13th consecutive time, tying Reggie White for the most appearances.

Another veteran, 35-year-old defensive end Trace Armstrong of Miami, made it for the first time in his 12th NFL season. Armstrong, who leads the NFL with 16 1/2 sacks, will start for the AFC even though he's been a third-down specialist for several years.

"I'm slowing down. You just learn to hide it," he said. "If anything, this shows hard work and persistence pay off. That's been my credo. I work really hard in the offseason and go into each year thinking I can get better. I think that's the way you've got to be."

Durability helps, too. In six seasons with Miami, Armstrong has missed one game.

This year he has teamed with Jason Taylor to give the Dolphins perhaps the best pass-rushing tandem in the NFL. They're part of a defense that ranks second only to Baltimore in points allowed.

"This game thrills you every Sunday," said Armstrong, who is president of the NFL Players Association. "This is the great addiction. And then to get something like this is obviously a tremendous feeling."
 
But there was a changing of the guard in other places.

Second-year-man Champ Bailey made the NFC team at cornerback ahead of teammates Deion Sanders and Darrell Green, each of whom has made it seven times. Dallas' Emmitt Smith, who last week became the third running back ever go rush for more than 15,000 yards, also missed out to Minnesota's Robert Smith, the Rams' Marshall Faulk, and San Francisco's Charlie Garner.

Five teams do not have players in the game, to be played Feb. 4 in Honolulu: Atlanta and Chicago in the NFC; Cleveland, Seattle and New England in the AFC.

title: Childress is extended
content:
EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn. - The man who brought Brett Favre to the Minnesota Vikings will be sticking around for a while.

The Vikings signed Brad Childress to a contract extension on Thursday, nine games into what is shaping up as a promising season for the men in purple. Terms of the deal were not disclosed. ESPN.com cited anonymous sources in reporting that the extension will carry through the 2013 season.

"Brad has done a tremendous job leading this football team and we value the positive environment he has created for the Minnesota Vikings on and off the field," Vikings owner Zygi Wilf said in a statement issued by the team. "He has continued to positively impact this team and create a strong foundation for future success."

Childress, in the fourth year of his original five-year, $10 million deal, was hired in 2006 to take over a team in turmoil. The Vikings had become a national punchline for a string of incidents under the loose regime of Mike Tice, including the infamous "Love Boat" scandal and Tice's admission to scalping Super Bowl tickets that prompted Wilf to institute a code of conduct for the organization.

Childress has restored order and dignity to the franchise, and has done plenty of winning of late, as well. The Vikings have improved each year under his watch. They went 6-10 in 2006, 8-8 in 2007 and 10-6 with an NFC North title last season.

This season, he convinced Favre to come out of retirement one more time to join the Vikings, even going so far as to pick up the quarterback at the airport on the day of his arrival in August.

Favre leads the NFL in quarterback rating, has thrown 17 touchdowns and just three interceptions, and has helped put the Vikings (8-1) in position to compete for the top seed in the NFC.

"I am grateful for the opportunity I was given," Childress said. "I've got a good group of guys in the locker room, which I've said over and over. I've got a great ownership group. Not only am I grateful for them, they've been good owners from a standpoint of empowering us with players. They listen. There's a good dialogue."

The Wilf family has broken the bank to sign big-money players like defensive end Jared Allen, who leads the league in sacks, All-Pro guard Steve Hutchinson and receiver Bernard Berrian.

They have also poured money into upgrading the team's headquarters in Eden Prairie and steadfastly supported Childress even when he was the subject of criticism by fans early in his tenure.

"They have to buy into the vision of how you see the Minnesota Vikings going forward and we've been good enough to be able to fulfill part of that vision," Childress said.

The next step is to do some damage in the playoffs.

title: Mike Bruton: Will Couch enable the Browns to discard that expansion label?
content:
This was supposed to be the breakout year for the Cleveland Browns. It still could be, but for a few more weeks the faithful in Ohio are going to have to hold their collective breath.

There was reason for lofty expectations in August. Head coach Butch Davis was going into his second year, quarterback Tim Couch into his fourth. The expansion-team label had been peeled off and discarded.

Browns president and CEO Carmen Policy declared that Cleveland was a playoff-caliber team.

It had to be true. Couch was ready. With three seasons of NFL warfare under his belt, he was ready to blossom into the franchise-maker the Browns believed him to be when they took him first overall in the 1999 draft.

Besides, why would Davis leave Miami, a team on the verge of a national championship, if he didn't see potential - huge potential?

Then Couch injured his right forearm late in the exhibition season and missed the first two games. Backup Kelly Holcomb shined like a new penny, although the Browns lost their opener, 40-39, because of linebacker Dwayne Rudd's mental lapse on the last play of the game. He took his helmet off.

Rudd may as well have opened Pandora's box.

Couch came back and looked good in a 31-28 victory over Tennessee, throwing for 326 yards and three touchdowns, then fizzled in a 16-13 loss to the Pittsburgh Steelers, throwing for just 144 yards with no TDs and two interceptions.

The fans started calling for Holcomb, and Couch descended to the lowest point of his career on Oct. 6 when Browns fans cheered as he left the field against Baltimore after suffering a concussion. Before leaving the game, the young quarterback had thrown for 216 yards, with one TD and two interceptions.

Couch was in tears later in the locker room. Not Elvis Grbac tears, tears of hurt. Tears that come with intense anger.

Then, the next week against Tampa Bay, the Browns lost, 17-3, and Couch threw for just 151 yards.

This three-game losing streak prompted Davis to pull out the expansion-team label again.

Who can blame a coach for trying to protect his players? He was trying to tamp down the high expectations that, at that point, his team was choking on.

Flashbacks of last season had to be dogging the Browns. They were 2-4 with vivid memories of the 2-1 start in 2001, when they finished 3-13.

Then Couch threw for 148 yards in a 34-17 victory over a real expansion team, the Houston Texans. No big deal, right?

But last week against the New York Jets, just days after team owner Al Lerner died, the Browns overcame a 21-3 deficit to win, 24-21, with Couch throwing for 307 yards and two TDs with no picks.

That marked the eighth time in Couch's career that he led the Browns to a fourth-quarter victory. Cleveland is second behind Kansas City (109) in the NFL with 73 fourth-quarter points and fourth with 122 second-half points.

Couch ranks fifth in the AFC and ninth in the league with a fourth-quarter passing rating of 93.5.

Is it time to pull out the playoff team label again?

"We talked two weeks ago, before the Houston game," Davis said. "[We said] it was a 10-game playoff schedule. I'm saying it's not single elimination, but we're getting to that point in the season when you need to start playing well. Hopefully, we've found a little bit of a rhythm."

The Browns are 4-4 and playing for the AFC North lead against Pittsburgh (4-3) on Sunday at Browns Stadium.

This could be the game that determines whether they are an expansion or a playoff team.

The comeback against the Jets at Giants Stadium on Sunday restored confidence in the Browns and, more important, in Couch.

"They run a lot of crossing patterns and things of that nature," Jets head coach Herman Edwards said. "The crossing patterns develop when the QB has a lot of time. He wasn't hitting the first option. He was looking downfield for the deeper throws, then he would throw to the short guy."

In other words, Couch was getting pass protection and using it well and with confidence.

After weeks of turbulence, Couch finally seems unconstrained. There's a difference.

"I know for a fact and there's no question in my mind that he's healthier," Davis said.

Browns wide receiver Dennis Northcutt added: "He's confident. He believes in us. He believes in this team and the offense. He keeps his poise."

The Browns have a bye week after Pittsburgh. After that they have Cincinnati, Baltimore, Jacksonville and New Orleans on the road and face Carolina, Indianapolis and Atlanta at home.

The toughest matchup will be the Saints. The Falcons won't be easy, but the rest of the teams on their schedule arguably are beatable.

It just depends on how much the victory against the Jets carries over.

"It's a sign, I think," Browns defensive tackle Gerard Warren said of Sunday's comeback. "Now it's coming around. We've got goals and ambitions that we are starting to achieve this year.

"We got sidetracked for some part of the season, but now guys realize what we want to do, how we've got to do it and where we want to go."

Of the comeback, Couch said, "I think it says a lot about our team."

In the second half against the Jets, Couch was in a groove. He took what the Jets left him. New York's Cover 2 scheme takes away deep routes and leaves openings underneath.

Couch completed all but eight of the 27 passes he threw in the second half, accounting for 134 yards and two TDs.

"Tim's performance was typical Tim Couch," Davis said. "He has the ability to make plays in the pocket as well as scramble to keep other plays alive. Without some of the plays he made, we couldn't have won. I'm really proud of Tim."

The Browns' fortunes, expansion team or playoff team, probably will be forged in their next four games, against the Steelers, Bengals, Panthers and Saints.

"You'd like to think that everything positive you do will carry over," Davis said. "I think the team's growing. I'm proud, and no one will say that this team doesn't have heart and character. We fight and compete to the end."

Big hurts and other stuff. I'd say Brett Favre's streak of 155 straight starts, the longest such string for any NFL player, is not in jeopardy.

The Green Bay quarterback was listed as questionable yesterday after suffering a sprained knee Oct. 20 against Washington. The Packers did not play Sunday and will face Miami on Monday night.

Questionable usually means a player has a 50 percent chance of playing. That doesn't apply to Favre. Pencil him in at 80 percent.

Shaun King, though he played well after quarterback Rob Johnson was hurt Sunday in Tampa Bay's 12-9 victory over Carolina, probably won't start this Sunday against Minnesota.

King went in cold and completed a key 7-yard pass to set up the Buccaneers' winning field goal. Johnson, who has a strained gluteal muscle, is listed as questionable, and coach Jon Gruden expects him to be ready.

"It was an amazing thing," Gruden told reporters about King's quick insertion into the game. "Shaun King is a guy standing there on the sideline with his helmet on, ready to go. We did not want to use a time-out. Rob got hurt on that last scramble, and no one really knew it. We called a play and had no quarterback. We couldn't find Rob. He got out of bounds and never surfaced. Shaun was standing right next to me. We threw him out there and he makes a key play to Karl Williams to set up the field goal."

Gruden was satisfied with Rob Johnson's play. Though the coach didn't say otherwise, one has to wonder whether Brad Johnson, the starting QB when the season began, will get his job back.

"I thought for his first start, to get away from North Carolina with a win is a good beginning," Gruden said of Rob Johnson. "I expect him to become a great quarterback. That's the only expectation we have for him, and, hopefully, he understands that."

Brad Johnson suffered broken ribs a little more than a week ago and also is very ill with the flu.

Contact Mike Bruton at 215-854-2739 or mbruton@phillynews.com.

title: NFC notes
content:
Arizona: The defense allowed a 10-play, 81-yard touchdown drive on the first possession of its season opener, and a seven-play, 80-yard TD drive to start Sunday's loss to St. Louis. "I think we're relying too much on scheme," coach Dennis Green said. "They think the scheme is going to stop it. It's man against man. Hopefully, two games are enough for us to figure it out."

Atlanta: Backup QB Matt Schaub ran the first-team offense in practice Wednesday with Michael Vick held out with a sore left hamstring. Vick, who did some light running on the side, is listed as questionable for Sunday. "As long as I'm getting mental reps, I think I'll be able to go out there and execute the game plan," Vick said. "It's possible I may not practice all week. … I just have to be able to be me."

Carolina: RB Stephen Davis has carried the ball 38 times in two games, including 25 times Sunday, and his surgically repaired knee is holding up to the pounding. Coach John Fox said the Panthers will continue to monitor Davis' number of carries but indicated the Panthers will ride Davis as long as he can carry them.

Chicago: The Bears started CB Jerry Azumah at nickel back Sunday, partly because they don't want to rush him back from hip surgery and partly because second-year CB Nathan Vasher is playing so well. In fact, Azumah may not be able to regain his starting spot from Vasher, who has three interceptions in two games.

Dallas: Coach Bill Parcells was asked if he had any regrets about the perception he became too conservative with the play-calling after the Cowboys built a 13-point lead against Washington on Monday night. "I think we had our team in position to win the game on seven plays," Parcells said. "If we make any one of the seven plays, we win the game."

Detroit: QB Joey Harrington and WR Roy Williams had a heated exchange coming off the field following a miscommunication that led to a Chicago interception Sunday. The conversation quickly cooled, and coach Steve Mariucci said he took the exchange as a good sign of being passionate. "Yeah, because they care so much," Mariucci said.

Green Bay: TE Bubba Franks, who has played in all of the Packers' 88 games since he entered the NFL in 2000, could miss Sunday's game vs. Tampa Bay with a bruised left knee. "I still haven't come to grips with the fact that he would be unavailable, because he's always been there," said coach Mike Sherman, who has never coached a game for Green Bay without the three-time Pro Bowler in the lineup.

Minnesota: QB Daunte Culpepper has 10 turnovers, including eight interceptions, and a 41.6 quarterback rating through two games. He also hasn't been running the football as much. Part of the reason could be an injury to his right knee that the Vikings haven't discussed. Culpepper wore a tight elastic brace around the knee on Sunday.

New Orleans: The Saints, who made it clear they didn't consider Monday's game at Giants Stadium a "home" contest, will play their third consecutive game in front of the opposing team's crowd. And it will come against a 0-2 Minnesota team with its back against the wall. "I don't think their record is indicative of the kind of team they have," coach Jim Haslett said.

N.Y. Giants: QB Eli Manning said he expects to be booed during his first game in San Diego, the team for which he said he would not play if selected with the No.1 overall pick in 2004. The Chargers selected Manning and then traded him to the Giants. "I wasn't trying to manipulate the system or do anything," Manning said. "I felt strongly about not going to the Chargers so I tried to make it work where that did not happen. I knew I'd get criticized and harassed."

Philadelphia: QB Donovan McNabb's bruised chest continues to improve, although he also has a groin/abdomen injury and is officially listed as questionable. He'll likely wear a protective jacket underneath his jersey again this week as he did against the 49ers.

St. Louis: The Rams hope to continue to get consistent punting like they received from rookie P Reggie Hodges last week. Hodges got off to a rocky start in the season opener, averaging 33 yards a punt with a net of 14.3, leading to poor special teams play. Sunday against Arizona, Hodges averaged 42.9 yards on seven punts with a net of 40.1.

San Francisco: LB Julian Peterson promised the team would rebound from the 42-3 loss in Philadelphia and play better vs. Dallas. Peterson went so far as to guarantee a victory Sunday against the Cowboys. "I'm positive we're going to play well," Peterson said. "I'm positive we're going to win." Asked if he was guaranteeing a victory, Peterson said, "Yes, you can say I guarantee it."

Seattle: QB Matt Hasselbeck said his sore arm, which took a hard shot in Week 1 and for a while put his status for last Sunday's game in doubt, shouldn't be a long-term hindrance. "I just needed a couple of days," he said. "It is a balance. You give up reps in practice for rest."

Tampa Bay: Speakers are being used at practice this week in an attempt to be prepared for a sellout crowd at Lambeau Field against Green Bay on Sunday. "We got some young players starting, and they need to get acclimated to the noise," coach Jon Gruden said.

Washington: Despite a 2-0 record coming off a dramatic 13-point come-from-behind win in Dallas, coach Joe Gibbs admits the Redskins have many areas to improve, starting with a minus-3 turnover margin. "If you keep putting yourself in that box, we're going to get in trouble. We have to hammer that home," he said.

From The Sports Xchange and wire reports

title: NFL Scouting Report By Marc Narducci
content:
Minnesota (6-8) at Green Bay (6-8)



When: Tonight at 8.

TV:NFL Network

Prediction: Packers 24, Vikings 20

Line: Packers by 3

Over-under: 36½

Green Bay has won two in a row, and, in the wacky NFC, both the Packers and Vikings are still alive in the playoff race. The Packers won, 23-17, earlier this season at Minnesota. Green Bay was able to beat Detroit last week, 17-9, despite a 32.8 passer rating from Brett Favre, who threw three interceptions and no touchdowns. Vikings coach Brad Childress announced that rookie Tarvaris Jackson, a second-round pick from Alabama State, will start the final two games. Jackson has completed 17 of 27 passes for 212 yards, one TD and one interception this year. This marks the third time that Childress has benched starter Brad Johnson. Led by rookie linebacker A.J. Hawk (109 tackles), Green Bay's defense could pose problems for Jackson.

title: Fantasy football
content:
STUDS

Tony Romo, QB,Cowboys (at Bills): The Buffalo defense is ranked last in the NFL against the pass. Seeing that Romo is the hottest quarterback this side of Tom Bradyand the weather in Buffalo hasn't turned nasty yet, we expect another boffo performance on Monday night.

Edgerrin James, RB, Cardinals (at Rams): James has faced tough matchups the past two weeks but gets a major break here against a Rams defense that is ranked near the bottom of the league against the run. He is on pace for 12 TDs and more than 1,400 yards rushing in his second season in Arizona, as new offensive line coach Russ Grimm has helped make vast improvements. He rushed for 209 yards and a TD in two games against the Rams during his disappointing 2006 season.

LaDainian Tomlinson, RB, Chargers (at Broncos): He finally got untracked last week against the Chiefs, rushing for 116 yards and a touchdown in the first half. He should keep rolling Sunday against a Broncos team that hasn't stopped anybody on the ground. Last season against the Broncos, Tomlinson rolled up 291 total yards and scored seven touchdowns (six rushing, one receiving).

Steve Smith, WR, Panthers (at Saints): The last two seasons against the Saints, Smith has averaged 99 yards receiving and scored at least one touchdown in each contest. The Saints haven't done a good job against the pass, allowing nearly 10 yards per attempt, easily the worst in the NFL. Even without Jake Delhomme, Smith should rebound from two subpar games to post big numbers.

---

DUDS

Darrell Jackson, WR, 49ers (vs. Ravens): Carson Palmer,Kurt Warner and even Derek Anderson have had solid games against the Ravens, leading to their pass defense being ranked 22nd in the NFL. Still, we can't recommend Jackson with former Raven Trent Dilfer starting at QB.Dilfer looked a lot like Chris Chandler did with the Rams in 2004 after replacing the injured Alex Smith last week.

Derrick Ward, RB, Giants (vs. Jets): It's not that the Jets have a great run defense, it's that Brandon Jacobs is expected to return from the knee injury he suffered in Week 1, which will prevent Ward from getting the 20 carries he has averaged the past three weeks.

Vincent Jackson, WR, Chargers (at Broncos): The Broncos rank second to last in the NFL against the run and first against the pass, so you don't have to be John Madden to figure out how to attack them. While LaDainian Tomlinson is likely to have a big day, Jackson's disappointing season is likely to continue at least another week.

Warrick Dunn, RB, Falcons (at Titans): Dunn is nothing more than a flex play at this point, but if you have other viable options this week you should give them strong consideration. The Titans are allowing just 3.5 yards a carry, and that's exactly what Dunn is averaging over his 64 carries. Since Jerious Norwood prevents Dunn from seeing a huge workload, he probably won't put up more than a handful of points without breaking a big play or scoring a touchdown.

---

INJURIES

Joseph Addai, RB, Colts: He left last week's game with a bruised shoulder. Addai didn't practice Wednesday and might not be able to play against the Buccaneers on Sunday. His owners should attempt to grab backup Kenton Keith immediately.

Javon Walker, WR, Broncos: Although he says that the knee injury that sidelined him last week is not a long-term thing, Walker's status for this week's game against the Chargers is in question.

Anquan Boldin, WR, Cardinals: Boldin didn't practice Wednesday, and it's unclear if he'll return from his hip injury against the Rams, a team he has topped the 100-yard mark against three times in the last two years.

Clinton Portis, RB, Redskins: He bruised and sprained his right knee in Washington's Week 3 loss to the Giants but expects to play against the Lions after last week's bye gave him time to heal.

Marvin Harrison, WR, Colts: He's almost never injured, but a bruised knee that knocked him out of last week's game in the second quarter and prevented him from practicing on Wednesday could force him to the sidelines this weekend.

---

RUMBLINGS

Now that Buccaneers running back Cadillac Williams is out for the season with a torn patellar tendon that could threaten his career, two players will see a huge spike in fantasy value. Michael Pittman can be viewed as a poor man's Brian Westbrook because of his pass-catching ability and the fact that he has averaged 4.5 yards a carry since 2003. Earnest Graham might wind up holding even more value because he figures to continue getting the ball near the goal line. Graham ran all over the Rams during the fourth quarter in Week 3, and he could be viewed as a Marion Barber clone. Neither of these players will be more than a decent flex option, and their limited value would lessen if the Bucs acquire another player, which is a possibility - rumors have had them trying to lure Corey Dillon out of retirement and attempting to trade for Minnesota's Chester Taylor and Mewelde Moore. ... Washington's Chris Cooley entered this season as one of the six or seven better tight ends in fantasy, and his owners are concerned that he has caught just six passes through the first three games. The reason Cooley's production has been limited is because injuries to offensive linemen Jon Jansen and Randy Thomas have forced him to spend more time blocking than running pass routes. "The one thing about him is that he's unselfish," Redskins quarterback Jason Campbell told the Washington Post. "He understands the situation; we've had a lot of linemen hurt. Right now, we have to use him to block more." ... So far, Cedric Benson has been a bust as a feature back in Chicago. Not only is he averaging just 3.2 yards, but he's scored just one touchdown and has made little impact in the passing game (eight receptions, 52 yards). Bears coach Lovie Smith hopes that a larger workload will help jump-start the third-year running back, and he plans to give Benson just that on Sunday in Green Bay after attempting 52 passes at Detroit last week. "Cedric is the type of back who needs a lot of carries, so we're going to try to get him more carries," Smith said. The Packers' defense is allowing 4.5 yards a carryYPC so far this season, so Benson is a decent flex play this week. ... Reggie Brown and Donovan McNabb have about as much chemistry as John Fogerty has with his former Creedence Clearwater Revival bandmates. Brown, who qualifies as one of the bigger busts in fantasy football (eight rec., 81 yards, no TDs) hasn't delivered even three points for his fantasy owners in any game thus far. The Eagles have a bye this week, so his owners won't even have to consider playing him. We'd relegate the 26-year-old to the bench until he has something resembling a productive performance. ... Need a tight end to replace a disappointing starter or to serve as a bye-week or injury fill-in? Look no further than Green Bay's Donald Lee, who is on pace for 64 catches and is available in many leagues.

---

More
