title: craig's list Mark makes his picks: Read it here first - as usual
content:
Don't you just love the mad dash by some of the national insiders to be the first to report something ... anything ... whatever it takes, right or wrong, just so they can slam their pencil on the desk and declare, "I'M DONE! I'M DONE! LOOK AT ME!"?

If you followed that rat race this week, then you know injured Giants quarterback Eli Manning is:

A. Day-to-day.

B. Out two weeks.

C. Out a month.

D. Out for the season.

E. Had his right arm removed and cryogenically frozen next to Ted Williams' head.

Here's what we think: The Giants are up a creek without a Tiki either way.

MIN +3 at DET: Lions by 4. Tickets still available? Huh. Must be road construction in Detroit, too.

IND -7 at TEN: Colts by 4. The Colts have 16 rookies or first-year players, a record for a defending Super Bowl champion. Meanwhile, Peyton Manning took his part of the team's salary cap and purchased the planet Earth.

HOU +6 1/2 at CAR: Panthers by 3. The Texans have a three-game winning streak for the first time. How sad is that?

GB pick at NYG: Packers by 3. With the win, Brett Favre reaches an NFL-record 149 victories. With the loss, Tom Coughlin ages another 10 years.

BUF +10 at PIT: Steelers by 7. The Steelers can become the first AFC team to win 500 games. But 490 came vs. Cleveland.

CIN -6 1/2 at CLE: Bengals by 9. Carson Palmer is 5-1 against the Browns. For his next amazing feat, he will try to get Chad Johnson to open up to the media.

ATL +10 at JAC: Jaguars by 7. David Garrard is 4-1 when throwing two or more TDs. Joey Harrington is 0-1 when throwing an INT right smack dab into a defensive tackle and then calling it a "bad-luck" play.

SF +3 at STL: Cardinals by 6. Remember that gut feeling I had about the Rams being a surprise team? Might have been gas.

NO -3 1/2 at TB: Saints by 6. Drew Brees is 3-0 against Tampa Bay, officially making the Bucs his second favorite opponent behind the Gophers.

SEA -3 at ARI: Seahawks by 6. Coach Ken Whisenhunt's home debut is Sunday. That's three years before his successor's home debut, six years before his successor's successor's home debut, nine years before his ... .

NYJ +11 at BAL: Ravens by 7. The Jets are 4-1 after a loss under coach Eric Mangini. Not sure what their record is the week after losing to a dirty, rotten, no-good cheating son of a ...

OAK +10 at DEN: Broncos by 7. The Raiders need four victories to reach 400. Tiger needs five majors to tie Jack. Let the race begin.

KC +12 at CHI: Bears by 14. The Bears have an NFL-record 59 home-opening victories. "@#$%, ^%$#," said Chiefs defensive coordinator Gunther Cunningham.

SD +3 1/2 at NE: Patriots by 6. Bill Belichick has a sideline video from last year's divisional playoff game at San Diego. It captures Marty Schottenheimer signaling that it's January and he has absolutely no clue what defense to call.

WAS +7 at PHI: Eagles by 3. In an effort to gain a competitive advantage, Eagles coach Andy Reid spent the week looking for someone who can catch a punt.

UPSET SPECIAL

DAL - 3 1/2 at MIA: Dolphins by 6. Miami has won 10 of its past 13 home openers. So you know what that means? South Florida in September is less desirable than a fourth-quarter bear hug by sideline reporter Tony Siragusa.

Last week: Predicted Lions over Raiders. Season: 1-0

SEASON TRACKER

Last week: 12-3

Vs. spread: 7-6-2

title: Dilfer to start for Ravens
content:
Desperate to revive an offense that hasn't produced a touchdown in four games, Baltimore Ravens coach Brian Billick said former Bucs quarterback Trent Dilfer will start Sunday against the Pittsburgh Steelers.

After the Ravens stumbled through a 15th straight quarter without a touchdown with Tony Banks running the offense, Billick turned to Dilfer in the fourth quarter of Sunday's 14-6 loss to Tennessee.

Dilfer went 7-of-13 for 58 yards and failed to produce a score, but he nevertheless showed Billick enough to gain the starting job.

"Clearly we have got to do something to change the impetus," Billick said Monday. "Trent brings certain aspects to the offense: his experience, a certain tempo that we can benefit by. And he's been down this road before."Compiled from Wire ReportsNFLNEWS.24

Dilfer started 76 games for Tampa Bay before signing with Baltimore as a free agent in March. He can well understand what Banks is going through; Dilfer was benched Oct. 31, 1999, by the struggling Bucs and returned only after his replacement, former Ravens backup Eric Zeier, sustained bruised ribs.

"One of the biggest things about this position is you have to prepare to deal with failure," Dilfer said. "Tony has handled it like a total pro. Tony is going to be a player in this league for seven, eight, nine more years and be very successful. He can't let this setback affect him too negatively, the same way I didn't let my setback last year affect me."

Cards fire coach Tobin

TEMPE, Ariz. - Vince Tobin, who coached Arizona to its first playoff victory in 51 years two seasons ago, was fired a day after the Cardinals lost to the Cowboys 48-7.

Defensive coordinator Dave McGinnis was named interim coach.

"I felt it was time," owner Bill Bidwill said at a news conference. "Vince is a very fine man. He's worked very, very hard here. I wish him well in the future."

Bidwill said McGinnis will be "a prime candidate" for the job after this season.

McGinnis said his top priority will be to instill some enthusiasm in the team.

"The last three weeks we haven't been playing, we've been pressing," McGinnis said. "I want the guys to play instead of press."

Tobin's firing came with the Cardinals in last place in the NFC East and two weeks before a vote in Maricopa County on a proposed $ 331-million stadium for the team.

WARNER OUT, FAULK OKAY: Rams running back Marshall Faulk should be ready for Sunday's game at San Francisco, but quarterback Kurt Warner will miss at least three weeks with a broken little finger on his throwing hand. The team's top offensive players were injured in Sunday's loss to Kansas City. Coach Mike Martz said Faulk has a Grade 1 bruise on his left shoulder but no separation of the clavicle.

MCNOWN OUT A MONTH: Bears quarterback Cade McNown could be sidelined for up to a month after separating his throwing shoulder. Veteran Jim Miller is the likely starter for the next game Nov. 5 against the Colts.

GEORGE DAY-TO-DAY: Pro Bowl running back Eddie George has a sprained ligament in his right knee, and the Titans aren't sure whether he'll be able to play Sunday at Washington. George, 27, was injured on his first carry against the Ravens. His status is day-to-day.

CARRUTH TRIAL: Jury selection began in the Charlotte, N.C., murder trial of former Panthers receiver Rae Carruth, accused of masterminding the shooting death of his pregnant girlfriend to avoid paying child support. No jurors were selected in the first day. If convicted of first-degree murder, Carruth could be sentenced to death.

CHIEFS: Quarterback Elvis Grbac is expected to play Sunday despite an elbow injury that knocked him out of the victory over the Rams.

COLTS: Linebacker Rob Morris, the team's first-round draft pick, is out for the season with a ruptured patella tendon.

FALCONS: Linebacker Jessie Tuggle will miss at least the next 4-6 weeks after tearing the medial collateral ligament in his left knee.

49ERS: After another emotional outburst, Terrell Owens spent the day apologizing to his teammates. After Sunday's loss at Carolina, Owens blasted his teammates in a postgame rant, accusing them of quitting and playing only for their paychecks. The next day, Owens apologized before the defensive players' meeting.

JAGUARS: Right tackle Zach Wiegert is out for the season with a torn ligament in his left knee. Wiegert had replaced Leon Searcy, who was injured in July and is out until at least November.

PANTHERS: Defensive end Chuck Smith was placed on injured reserve because of an ailing right knee and is out for the season. Doctors found a stress fracture in Clarence Jones' leg that will sideline the left tackle for at least four weeks.

SEAHAWKS: Quarterback Brock Huard, who has started the past two games, might miss Sunday's game against the Chiefs with a concussion. Jon Kitna, who also sustained a concussion, was much better and is expected to take the snaps as the No. 1 quarterback this week.

title: If it's L.A., let's hate it
content:
TRUE GIANTS FANS have come to this conclusion, according to Steve
 Rubenstein of the San Francisco Chronicle: ''There are just as many reasons to
 hate the Angels as there are to hate the Dodgers: Probably more.

''The Anaheim Angels, after all, are represented by a monkey, are owned by
 a company that got its start making cartoons and can be found in the Los
 Angeles basin.

''The Los Angeles basin, Giants fans know, is the same basin where Los
 Angeles is located."

Flag on the play

Mitch Albom in the Detroit Free Press on San Francisco 49er Terrell Owens
 pulling out a pen and autographing a ball after scoring a touchdown:

''You can call me square, flat, unhip, old-fashioned, old-school, no cool
 or dull. When writing utensils become part of your football gear, things have
 gone too far."



A better target

Newsday columnist Shaun Powell thinks attorney Johnnie Cochran should make
 a case for more African American frontoffice hires in the NFL instead of head
 coaches.

''Forget coaches. There are only two black men holding those jobs, and I
 really couldn't care less.

''Coaches have nothing except a weekly radio show, a bottle of Pepto and a
 four-year contract they'll fulfill only if they're lucky."



Fuhgedaboudit

Bob Sansevere of the St. Paul Pioneer Press doesn't think New York Jets
 quarterback Chad Pennington should celebrate his big game against the reeling
 Minnesota Vikings.

''Joe Namath never had as fine a day completing passes," he wrote. ''Then
 again, ol' Joe Willie never got to face this Vikings defense, which has more
 holes in it than Pussy Bonpensiero's body after it was pumped full of lead at
 the end of the second season of 'The Sopranos."'



One too many

Don't tell Carolina goalie Kevin Weekes he gave up a meaningless goal with
 52.3 seconds left in the Hurricanes' 4-1 victory over New Jersey. It would
 have been his first shutout for Carolina, his fifth NHL team.

''There's no sense in swimming 95 percent across the Atlantic Ocean and
 then getting close to the shoreline and saying, 'Forget it,"' Weekes said.



The last word

''The Huskers are in uncharted waters, lost at sea, with a boat full of
 holes, no compass, no idea where they're going or how to get there. They are
 impostors with amnesia who woke up with nobody to tell them where they are."

- Tom Shatel of the Omaha World-Herald, after Nebraska's first football
 loss to Oklahoma State in 41 years.

title: And returning to the lineup, an oft-wounded QB The Purple still sees good things from Tarvaris Jackson, but he has to last longer in games to do them.
content:
There are a couple of things to make clear here, in regards to what Vikings coach Brad Childress had to say about quarterback Tarvaris Jackson during his Monday media session.

First, Childress did not say Jackson was a China doll. He merely raised it as a possibility.

Second, this was a reference to the historic fragility of China dolls, and not to that country's ongoing efforts to poison the youth of America with imported toys.

Jackson will return to the lineup Sunday against Oakland. That means he has recovered from a concussion, Jackson's fourth injury in 1 1/2 seasons with the Purple.

Jackson suffered this latest injury Nov. 4 against San Diego.

Childress was asked this question Monday: "How much do you factor in that he may be injury-prone when you make a decision if he's your guy?"

The coach formulated his answer for a moment, then said: "You ask yourself, `Is this just one particularly bad year? Is it the fact he is a China doll?' Somewhere you have to have some durability and resilience."

Jackson injured a knee in practice before ever playing a game as a rookie in 2006. He underwent arthroscopic surgery. He opened this season as the No. 1 quarterback and has made five of nine starts. He's completed only three of those five starts.

He missed two starts because of a groin injury, one because of a finger injury and Sunday's game in Green Bay because of a concussion.

Monday's headline was that Adrian Peterson had a tear in a ligament that will not require surgery. The subhead was Childress planned to go back to Jackson as his quarterback.

"I kind of erred on the side of caution with Tarvaris," Childress said. "It wasn't that he couldn't practice last week, but we started Brooks [Bollinger].

"It's still my commitment to want to continue to develop Tarvaris. I do feel like coming out of training camp, he was the best quarterback. So, we probably would end up being back with Tarvaris."

The only item that could change that would be if, say, Jackson goes to the barber shop this week and suffers an excruciating nick.

A few minutes after putting Jackson back in the lineup, Childress uttered "China doll," causing his media audience to perk up.

Question: "Is there a growing concern that maybe Jackson is a China doll?"

The coach answered quickly: "Not from my standpoint, no."

Jackson has been overmatched during most of his playing time this season. This has caused many of us on the outside to declare: "That young man is never going to be a capable NFL quarterback."

That's opinion, not fact. What we know for sure is Jackson suffers from the accelerated timeline in this salary-cap age.

For instance: Brad Johnson came to the Vikings in 1992 and didn't start until 1996. Rich Gannon arrived in 1987 and didn't play significantly until 1990.

The Vikings decided not to trade high draft choices for Atlanta's Matt Schaub, who went to Houston. The Vikings decided not to make a sizable bid for free agent Jeff Garcia, who went to Tampa Bay.

The Vikings brought in Kelly Holcomb as a backup to go with Bollinger - two quarterbacks with no history as No. 1 guys - and hitched themselves to Jackson.

"Gannon came out and talked to us," Childress said. "He said he was totally unprepared to play in his first season. He told Tarvaris, `As a young quarterback, you don't know what you don't know.'-"

As outsiders, we feel like we know this after watching Jackson play three full games this season: Can't play now, won't be able to play later.

Are Childress and the Vikings seeing enough from Jackson - behind the closed doors at Winter Park - to believe in his future as the franchise's quarterback?

"I see him do good things in practice," Childress said. "Talk to our defensive players. They have been around. They see his talents. They talk about Tarvaris' ability all the time. They have no agenda in saying those things.

"We put a lot of defensive pressure on him in practice. We replicate what he will see in a game pretty well. Tarvaris responds. He makes plays.

"Obviously, he has to start making those plays on Sunday and ..."

And? "Stay healthy," Childress said.

Patrick Reusse can be heard weekdays on AM-1500 KSTP at 6:45 and 7:45 a.m. and 4:40 p.m. - preusse@startribune.com

title: Sanchez, Jets take next step
content:
With a playoff win under his belt as a rookie quarterback, Mark Sanchez of the New York Jets already is a member of a small club. The test now is whether he can do it again.

Though his numbers weren't glitzy, the first-round draft pick from Southern California picked the right time to have his best passing performance in an up-and-down season as he helped the Jets to a weekend victory at the Cincinnati Bengals in the wild-card round.

Next up: an AFC divisional-round game Sunday at the San Diego Chargers.

"Hopefully, we're not done. We have another week," says Sanchez, who joined Joe Flacco of the BaltimoreRavens (2008), Ben Roethlisberger of the PittsburghSteelers (2004) and Shaun King of the Tampa Bay Buccaneers (1999) as the only rookie quarterbacks to win postseason starts.

During Saturday's 24-14 victory at Cincinnati, Sanchez found time during timeouts to savor the moment.

"You just think, wow, the entire country is watching this. And I get to say go. I get to say hut. That's crazy. It blows your minds. It's unbelievable," Sanchez says.

Obviously, he was not overcome by the playoff atmosphere.

The Jets benefited from a strong running game and a defense that limited the passing of Cincinnati's Carson Palmer,but Sanchez was no bystander.

He completed 12 of 15 passes for 182 yards and a touchdown with no interceptions. His 80% completion rate was a Jets postseason record.

"He did a wonderful job. He managed the game very well. He didn't make any mistakes," says Jets rookie running back Shonn Greene, who rushed for 135 yards and a touchdown. "That's just a peek of what you're going to see from him in the future."

Sanchez was intercepted 20 times during the regular season (five times by the Buffalo Bills in an October game and four times by the New England Patriots in a November game). He passed for 12 touchdowns in 15 regular-season starts.

He downplays his role in Saturday's victory.

"I think we capitalized on offense and defense," he says. "It was an unbelievable effort by out defense. ... It is pretty special. It's got nothing to do with me," he says. "Maybe I lucked out. ... We'll try to put another of these games together and play smart."

In San Diego, the Jets' top-ranked defense will face a Chargers passing game led by quarterback Philip Rivers.

Jets coach Rex Ryan says that in Cincinnati his offense showed that while it relies on the run, it is not one-dimensional,

"We can have an efficient passing attack," Ryan says. "(Sanchez) had the eye of the tiger ... and he was ready to get out there and throw it around."

Ryan says Sanchez's increased command of the offense was evident against the Bengals.

"He was all over me one time for calling a timeout," Ryan says. "And I was like, 'Slow down, kid.' And he said, 'I know the coverage,' "

Ryan's bottom line on Sanchez: "What a job he's done. I think he's tired of hearing he's the weak link on this football team, instead of being just part of the success."

title: SAFETY FIRST AS BIG BLUE SECONDARY BRACES TO FACE MOSS
content:
SAM GARNES, who grew up on Penfield St. in the Bronx, right around the corner from Wakefield Grace United Methodist Church, who made it from there to Giants Stadium, was asked yesterday who the fastest kid in the neighborhood was when he was growing up, and the game was in the street sometimes and not in a championship game.

Garnes is the Giants safety who used to take the No. 41 bus and switch to the No. 16, which let him off near Montefiore Hospital. He'd walk the rest of the way to DeWitt Clinton High and sometimes he would run, pretending he was with the Giants already.

He thought about the question, who was fastest, but not for too long, and finally he said, "I was the fastest kid in the neighborhood."

The fastest kid in the neighborhood on Sunday afternoon, with the Super Bowl waiting at 4 o'clock for either Garnes' team or the Vikings, is Randy Moss. Garnes talked about Moss yesterday because everybody on the Giant defense was talking about him, and has been since the Giants beat the Eagles last Sunday.

As Garnes kept answering questions about Moss, trying to describe what he does on a football field and how fast he does it, it was as if Moss had had shown up at Giants Stadium already, as if he were outside on a bright cold afternoon, lined up on the opposite side from Cris Carter, looking to break a big play, looking to make Jason Sehorn bad on the corner, looking to make Sam Garnes of DeWitt Clinton and Shaun Williams, the free safety, look just as bad behind Sehorn.

"You don't want to be watching 'SportsCenter' the next day," Shaun Williams said yesterday, two lockers down from Garnes, "and see (Moss) catching one over you. Or behind you."

All the Giants keep saying that one man can't beat them out of the Super Bowl on Sunday and they are probably right about that. If the Giants get after Daunte Culpepper the way they did Donovan McNabb of the Eagles, then Culpepper never gets to have the game of catch he has with Moss sometimes, and Carter. He is on his back or running for the sidelines and the Giants win a close, low-scoring game.

The best way for the Giants to cover Randy Moss is to stop Culpepper and stop Robert Smith, the gifted Minnesota running back. If they can do that, then maybe Culpepper and Moss don't get to make it look as easy as they did against the Saints, when Moss caught two passes for two long touchdowns, when he looked like a kid running free from Garnes' house on Penfield to Wakefield Grace United.

Yesterday Garnes was asked about that little pass Moss caught early against the Saints last Saturday, one that became a touchdown so fast it seemed to have been done with a television remote.

"He got it, he took off, he showed you why he's 4.1 (in the 40-yard dash) or whatever he is," Garnes said.

Then Sam Garnes said, "You want to think if you're in the same position, you make that play . . ." The words disappeared as easily as Moss does sometimes.

We talk a lot about the young quarterbacks in the NFL right now, starting with McNabb and Culpepper. There are so many running backs to watch. Moss, more than anybody, is the player to watch in the whole league. He is big and fast as the wind the Giants hope will be blowing through the stadium and makes catches sometimes that make you think he has hands as big as outfielders' gloves. People worried about his attitude when he came out of college and he still does things, with the refs especially, that make you wonder about him sometimes.

It doesn't change that he is the most dangerous player in the sport, the one who most easily can take your breath away, make catches and plays that you are sure no one else playing can make. The defense is aware of him on every snap of the ball the way offenses were once aware of where Lawrence Taylor was.

More than any other football player, Randy Moss makes you afraid to miss one single play.

"You want to play against the best," Shaun Williams said yesterday. He is a No. 1 draft choice who has become one of the top free safeties in the league. "Well, we get the best on Sunday."

Carter is on the other side of the field. Carter is in the Hall of Fame if he never catches another ball. The two of them are as much fun for a defense as Jordan and Pippen were. Still everybody talks about Moss, and what Sehorn can do with him on Sunday, how the Giants have to find a way to keep him in the ballpark.

"You just hope it's not you," Shaun Williams said, meaning you being the one chasing him, or coming down without the ball.

"He's got a presence," Sam Garnes said of Moss.

He comes with help on Sunday. He comes with Culpepper and Carter and Robert Smith and a defense that finally showed up against the Saints. Randy Moss shows up with Dennis Green, a great football coach, one who seems to make it this far no matter who is quarterbacking the Vikings. Green has to think it is his time to finally make the Super Bowl, after coming so close two years ago.

MOSS IS THE PLAYER they will want to see on Sunday at Giants Stadium. He is the attraction. In the old days it was Jerry Rice the Giants had to stop in a big game. Now it is Randy Moss. He is the fastest kid in the neighborhood.

title: Bears safety Brown tough encore to follow; Again?: Chicago star sees close game vs. Packers
content:
Mike Brown can't imagine how it could happen a third time in three weeks, how he might pick off another overtime pass and return it for a game-winning TD.

"I doubt that situation that has happened in the last two weeks will ever occur again in any game I'm a part of," Brown, the Chicago Bears' second-year safety, said Wednesday.

"It's so unbelievable what happened."

Two weeks ago, a pass bounced off the 49ers' Terrell Owens, went up in the air and landed in the hands of Brown, who ran 33 yards for a TD. That gave the Bears a 37-31 win 16 seconds into overtime.

The Bears rallied from a 19-point deficit in that game, scoring two touchdowns and a 2-point conversion in the final four minutes to tie it.

If that was a head-shaking finish, last week's victory over the Browns was ever more amazing.

The Bears scored two touchdowns in the last 28 seconds of regulation, tying the game on a final-play desperation pass from Shane Matthews to James Allen.

In overtime, the Bears had to punt and, on Cleveland's third play, Bryan Robinson tipped a pass. Brown was there again, grabbing the ball and racing 16 yards for a 27-21 win.

"It's really an amazing thing," Bears coach Dick Jauron said.

Brown was named NFC Defensive Player of the Week, even though earlier in the game he let an interception sail through his hands and the Browns' Kevin Johnson went for a touchdown.

At Nebraska, where he was a teammate of Packers running back Ahman Green, Brown led the Cornhuskers in tackles three straight years. As a senior, he showed his big-play ability with five interceptions, a fumble recovery and six forced fumbles.

Told that if Sunday's game with the Packers should go to overtime, Green Bay quarterback Brett Favre said he plans to keep the ball away from him, Brown laughed.

"Last week the pass was on the other side of the field and it still bounced my way," Brown said.

With a 6-1 record, the Bears are one of the biggest surprises in the NFL. In addition to playing better football, they've certainly gotten some breaks.

"What has happened in the last couple of weeks, you start feeling better about yourself because you are getting the bounces and things are going your way," Brown said.

Brown said the Bears must first stop the Packers' running game. Green has 681 yards rushing in seven games and is averaging 5.2 yards per carry.

"It's always fun going against ex-teammates, especially college teammates. It will be a little friendly rivalry going on. We'll have some fun," Brown said.

"On game day it will be different. I won't be his friend on game day, so he doesn't have to worry about me saying 'good job.' I'm going to be in his face.

"He's a great back running with a lot of authority and power and he's running over guys. He's got great speed and vision, what you want in a back."

Something tells Brown that Sunday's game is also going to be decided late. Maybe he'll be involved again, as he has been the last two weeks.

"It's going to be one of those games I think that will come down again to the fourth quarter and whoever can make the biggest play down the stretch will win," he said.

Notes: Coach Dick Jauron made it official Thursday, saying Jim Miller, who suffered a hip pointer two weeks ago, will start at quarterback against Green Bay. Left tackle Blake Brockermeyer (knee) and right guard Chris Villarial (hip) are also expected to play. They were listed as questionable.

title: ABC EXPECTS TO KEEP MILLER IN THE BOOTH
content:
Comedian Dennis Miller probably will return for a second season with ABC's Monday Night Football even though the program is still losing viewers after hiring him, said Don Ohlmeyer, the show's producer.

The Walt Disney Co. network hired Miller in June as part of its effort to overhaul its prime-time National Football League broadcast, once the network's most-watched prime-time show.

Neither Miller, who was signed to a multiyear contract, nor his agent could be reached to comment.

Through the first 10 weeks of the NFL season, Monday Night Football has been watched in an average of 12.9 percent of the 100.8 million U.S. homes with televisions. A year ago, the show was watched in an average of 13.9 percent of homes with televisions, according to Nielsen Media Research Inc.

Broncos. Denver quarterback Brian Griese will miss as much as a month because of a separated shoulder on his throwing arm suffered in Monday night's win over Oakland.

Also, guard Mark Schlereth's injured left knee will force him to miss the rest of the season and could end his 12-year NFL career.

Raiders. Kicker Sebastian Janikowski returned to practice after missing two games because of a bacterial infection in his foot, and Oakland coach Jon Gruden said the rookie should be ready to play Sunday in New Orleans.

Saints. New Orleans running back Ricky Williams had two screws and a small plate inserted into his broken left ankle and will need six to eight weeks to recover.

Patriots. Drew Bledsoe said his jammed right thumb would not keep him from starting Sunday, but one of his veteran receivers will be missing. Tight end Eric Bjornson was cut.

Bengals. Cincinnati coach Dick LeBeau said backup quarterback Scott Mitchell would start Sunday at New England, with second-year man Akili Smith going to the bench.

The Bengals (2-8) have the worst passing attack in the NFL.

Giants. Starting wideout Amani Toomer and Joe Jurevicius, his backup, both sustained concussions in Sunday's loss to the St. Louis Rams, and their status for New York's game this weekend's against Detroit is questionable.

Rookie receiver Ron Dixon has missed the last two games with a foot injury and did not practice yesterday. Ike Hilliard and Thabiti Davis, however, are healthy.

Cowboys. Dallas signed free-agent punter Barry Cantrell to replace Micah Knorr, who will be out three to six weeks with a fractured lower left leg.

Vikings. Fullback Jim Kleinsasser will miss at least one game and possibly another because of a hamstring injury, Minnesota coach Dennis Green said.

Also, receiver Randy Moss will appeal the $40,000 fine he was assessed for touching an official's arm in a game against Tampa Bay on Oct. 29.

Redskins. Washington defensive backs Darrell Green and Shawn Barber will return to the lineup for Monday night's game at St. Louis, but quarterback Brad Johnson will miss his second consecutive game.

Buccaneers. Defensive end Marcus Jones signed a seven-year contract extension. Jones, 27, is second on the Tampa Bay defense with 10 sacks this season, trailing only Warren Sapp, who has 111/2. Jones is tied for fifth in the league with Miami's Jason Taylor. He has recorded 24 tackles and one fumble recovery in 10 games.

Cardinals. Arizona center Mike Gruttadauria was hospitalized in Tempe, Ariz., because of an injury to a cervical disk, coach Dave McGinnis said. Gruttadauria will not play against the Eagles on Sunday.

title: DIRTY RATS, RING ENVY, AND YADA, YADA, YADA
content:
As a service to its tabloid readers, the New York Post ranks its top stories in order of importance. The No. 3 entry Thursday focused on a "Medical Miracle" relating to a woman who had seizures when she heard Sean Paul, a reggae and hip hop artist. She was cured after brain surgery (and you've probably already concluded that writing this column doesn't qualify as such). The No. 2 entry was about "Economic Woes" and updated the gloom and doom on Wall Street after the Dow Jones industrials tumbled over 300 points while the housing market took another hit.

The No. 1 story?

Well, according to the New York Post, "Shaun says 'We're not dirty' - Hits back at Packers' claims of foul play."

That would be New York Giants center Shaun O'Hara, the "Dirty Rat" who mixed it up with Green Bay defensive end Aaron Kampman during the teams' first confrontation on Sept. 16. Kampman was not pleased with some things that took place after the whistle. "I have never intentionally tried to hurt someone or do anything of that nature," O'Hara pleaded to the New York press corps. "But you play to the whistle. As an offensive lineman, especially being a limited athlete like myself, I have to play extremely hard and I kind of pride myself on that. But I don't necessarily consider myself a dirty player."

Are the Giants and Packers ready to get "down and dirty" for their Sunday rematch at Lambeau Field? Or is this much ado about nothing? Did someone say nothing? In the words of New York quarterback Eli Manning'spatron saint, Jerry Seinfeld,"I am so busy doing nothing that the idea of doing anything - which as you know, always leads to something - cuts into the nothing and then forces me to have to drop everything."

Yada, yada, yada.

Or Hail-Mary Jerry?

That was how the New York Post packaged its No. 1 story today, which dealt with Seinfeld and the decision of a Green Bay television station's general manager to pull the regularly scheduled "Seinfeld" rerun from its Saturday night lineup on the Fox affiliate, in an attempt to rattle Manning. Seinfeld told the Post, "I'm going to send Eli a complete collection of 'Seinfeld' DVDs and a partial collection of 'Hogan's Heroes' for inspiration. You think I'm going to take that sitting down? I'm dispatching George Costanza to be the new traveling secretary for the Packers."

Given the potential for sub-zero windchills Sunday afternoon, George might want to engage Elaine on that shrinkage issue again. Not that Michael Strahanhas ever been accused of being a shrinking violet. While professing his admiration and respect for a friend, Green Bay quarterback Brett Favre, the Giants veteran defensive end acknowledged that he has grown tired of the Favre lovefest, perpetuated by the national media. Stressing that Favre already has already earned a Super Bowl ring, Strahan whined to the Post, "He can hang it up and say, 'I was a Super Bowl champion at some point.' But all of us don't have that, and some of us are still pressing to get it."

Strahan, a 15-year pro, cited two teammates - wide receiver Amani Toomer and punter Jeff Feagles - who have played in the league for 12 and 20 years, respectively. Neither has a ring, neither has won a Super Bowl. And it's possible that Feagles might have a bigger impact on the outcome of Sunday's NFC title game than either Toomer or Strahan. Especially if the Giants and the Packers get involved in a field position tug-of-war because of the weather conditions.

That would put both punters - Feagles and Jon Ryan, who had a nightmarish experience against the Chicago Bears - into play at Lambeau Field.

"You're not going to go out there and hit the 65-, 70-yard punt that you get on a nice, balmy day," Feagles said during a news conference this week. "It's crucial that you concentrate on the football. Your hands are cold and the ball is rock hard. You try to focus on those things and hopefully you get a big play somewhere." How will he keep warm between punts? "Those heated benches and those heaters are my friends," he said. "I just have to keep all the guys that aren't playing in the game away from them."

Feagles will be taking snaps from a rookie, Zack DeOssie, a backup linebacker from Brown. Another rookie, Jay Alford, a backup defensive tackle from Penn State, will handle the snaps on placements. "It's going to be like a rock," New York special teams coach Tom Quinn said of the football. "But I like their makeup as far as how they handle the pressure and different things. Jay is playing a little of defensive line, so that helps keep him within the flow of the game. And Zach is playing on special teams, so they're not just snapping. They're doing other things."

If the game is close, it could come down to the field goal accuracy of Green Bay's rookie placekicker Mason Crosby and New York's Lawrence Tynes, who has bounced around from the Scottish Claymores (he was born in Scotland) to the Ottawa Renegades of the Canadian Football League to the Kansas City Chiefs to the Giants. Tynes has a degree in criminal justice from Troy State. And if there is any justice, this pregame rhetoric will finally cease and a clean and fair fight will break out Sunday. At that, there's plenty of fight in Tynes.

While a member of the Chiefs, who held their preseason training camp in River Falls, he was charged with breaking a bouncer's nose in a bar fight, the perfect backdrop for these teams and why the Giants have a puncher's chance of pulling the upset.

mlucas@madison.com

title: From LJ to Benson, keep an eye on Soldier Field this week
content:
jdietz@dailyherald.com

What a crazy opening week in the NFL, huh?

Thanks to everything from Tony Romo's5-TD game to Randy Moss' apparent reemergence, plenty of fantasy football teams posted monster point totals.

But then there's the other side of the coin, one that isn't getting as much ink this week: the disappointing showing of many leagues' second and third draft picks - Kansas City's Larry Johnson and St. Louis' Steven Jackson.

Jackson lost 2 fumbles and managed just 58 rushing yards, and now offensive tackle Orlando Pace is out for the season. While I don't think this bodes well for Jackson owners, I still believe he'll have a tremendous season.

It's Johnson who worries me. Unlike Jackson's Rams, whose offense has plenty of weapons, Johnson's Chiefs have as much energy as a 20-watt light bulb.

Quarterback Damon Huard, aging tight end Tony Gonzalez and a middling (at best) receiving corps that includes Chicago Rush standout Bobby Sippio isn't striking any fear into the hearts of opponents.

Johnson vowed after Sunday's 20-3 embarrassing loss to Houston things will get better.

"I'm not going to let what happened today happen next week," said Johnson, referring to the Chiefs' game against the Bears on Sunday at Soldier Field. "I'm going to get back to my old ways."

Uh, huh. Against the Bears? The same team that held LaDainian Tomlinson to 25 rushing yards last week?

Good luck with that.

While I don't think Johnson will necessarily be a bust this season, his fantasy owners better have a solid team built around him.

The Chiefs' schedule is daunting. After this week, they play Minnesota at home, travel to San Diego, then host Jacksonville. All of those teams sport top-notch run defenses and will shut down a one-dimensional attack like Kansas City's.

It wouldn't shock me at all if Johnson averages 65 rushing yards with just 2-3 TDs after the first five weeks.

More to watch: Soldier Field is going to be a great place to get a feel for where two other fantasy football entities are at early in the season.

One is Cedric Benson. How will he rebound after being called "soft" by Chargers linebacker Shaun Phillips? I'm guessing he'll come out like a man possessed.

The other is the Bears' defense. Here's a unit that lost its heart and soul in Mike Brown last week, as well as defensive lineman Dusty Dvoracek.

Against the Chiefs, we should see another strong outing by Brian Urlacher and Co.

But what worries me is Brown's absence long term. Before he was lost last season, the Bears racked up 19 sacks in their first six games and held four of those opponents to 10 points or less.

After the sixth game, the Bears managed just 17 more sacks through Week 16, held just two foes to under 10 points and allowed 30 or more twice.

That's a big deal in leagues that give bonus points for low- scoring games, or take them away if the other team scores 30-plus.

The Bears' defense is supposed to be better all-around this year, and certainly with a healthy Tommie Harris, it should be.

But Brown's injury will still hurt - and if another major player is lost - you can count on the Bears not producing as many fantasy points as you'd hoped for in '07.

Alexander dinged up: Seahawks RB Shaun Alexander was wearing a cast or brace on his left hand at practice this week. He is still supposed to start, but other running backs are getting more reps.

When asked what the device was on his wrist, Alexander replied, "It's just a thing we made up. A boo-boo with a boo-boo protector."

Hopefully Alexander isn't about to give fantasy teams any boo- boos - as in goose eggs for points.

My teams: The Cowboys propelled both of my squads to league-high totals. In the unit league, Tony Romo(34 points), Terrell Owens (16), Antonio Gates (13) and the Vikings' defense (20) led the way to a 108-52 victory. And that was with Plaxico Burress (3 TDs) on the bench! In the regular league, Owens (22), Jason Witten (20) and Joseph Addai (22) spearheaded a 129- 88 victory.

Good bets

- Bears RB Cedric Benson vs. Kansas City. The Bears want to wash out the sour taste in their mouths from the opener, and Benson has plenty to prove after being called "soft" by Chargers linebacker Shaun Phillips. I'm expecting at least 80 yards and a touchdown.

- Bucs WR Joey Galloway vs. New Orleans. Since arriving in Tampa, Galloway has 7 TD receptions in five games against the Saints.

- Seahawks QB Matt Hasselbeck vs. Arizona. Seattle usually has a field day against the Cardinals.

- Vikings RB Adrian Peterson vs. Detroit. Even if Chester Taylor returns, which seems unlikely, Peterson is a great play this week.

- Eagles QB Donovan McNabb and WR Reggie Brown vs. Washington. Dolphins WR Chris Chambers managed 6 catches for 92 yards last week against the Redskins. Watch for McNabb to target Brown after both got off to abysmal starts in Green Bay.

- Packers RB Brandon Jackson vs. New York Giants. OK, my Week 1 sleeper (Chester Taylor) got injured, so let's hope I'm not starting a trend here. Jackson may not be a great play most weeks, but the Cowboys' two top backs amassed a quiet 131 rushing yards Sunday as Tony Romowas shredding a weak Giants defense.

Bad bets

- Chiefs RB Larry Johnson vs. the Bears. After LaDainian Tomlinson ran for 25 yards against Brian Urlacher and Co., what is Johnson going to accomplish coming into Soldier Field after managing just 43 yards against Houston? Anything over 70 yards with a score seems optimistic. Still, most of you wouldn't dream of sitting him.

- Giants WR Plaxico Burress vs. Green Bay. Even if Eli Manning somehow plays, the Giants won't be throwing the ball all over the place on the Packers. And if backup Jared Lorenzen is in there, I'd expect about 4 catches for 45 yards from Burress.

- Falcons RBs Warrick Dunn and Jerious Norwood vs. Jacksonville. Just because the Titans shredded the Jags doesn't mean Atlanta will. Resist the urge to start Dunn or Norwood.

- Saints RB Deuce McAllister vs. Tampa Bay. I'd take a wait-and- see approach with McAllister for now.

- Titans RB Chris Brown vs. Indianapolis. So, you're itching to get the newly acquired Brown into your lineup after his 175-yard day? If your other options are weak, feel free. But I'm saying the Colts keep Brown in check.

- Cardinals QB Matt Leinart vs. Seattle. The Seahawks' defense is a nasty bunch, and Arizona looked awful against San Francisco.

- Lions RB Tatum Bell vs. Minnesota. Congratulations to Bell owners who had the foresight to plug him in there against Oakland. Now park him on the bench for a week.

jdietz@dailyherald.com

- Catch John on WSCR 670-AM's "Fantasy Focus" on Sunday at 8 a.m. and also with Mike Murphy on most Thursdays in the 1 p.m. hour.

title: NOTEBOOK
content:
Smith Finishes With A Flourish In Music City

NASHVILLE, Tenn. - Junior Antone Smith's first season as FSU's featured back didn't exactly go as planned, but that didn't stop Smith from finishing with a bang.

Smith rushed for a career-high 156 yards in FSU's 35-28 loss to Kentucky on Monday in the Music City Bowl, capping a roller-coaster season with the best game of his career.

After playing behind Leon Washington and Lorenzo Booker his first two seasons, Smith envisioned becoming the first FSU back since Warrick Dunn in 1996 to rush for 1,000. Instead, he finished with 819 yards in a season in which he battled turf toe, a concussion and a shoulder injury that forced him to miss the Maryland game and play sparingly in FSU's loss at Florida.

Smith returned to the starting lineup on Monday and didn't disappoint.

"When you have been away from something so long, and you can't really go out there and help your team, you kind of doubt yourself at times," Smith said. "At the same time, I knew I could do a lot more if I got a chance to do that.

"This game here gave me a chance to do that. I had lanes like I probably haven't seen in a long time."

BOBBY GETS HOT: A Big East Conference officiating crew made three calls against FSU in the first half that TV replays showed were questionable at best.

The first was an offensive pass interference call against WR Greg Carr that nullified an apparent 33-yard TD catch. The second call was an intentional grounding penalty on QB Drew Weatherford when Smith was clearly the intended receiver, and the third strange call was an illegal jumping infraction against Kenny Ingram on a field-goal attempt. Finally, when FSU CB Tony Carter returned an interception 24 yards for a TD to tie the game 14-all late in the second quarter, he flipped into the end zone, drawing a 15-yard flag.

As Bowden left the field at halftime, he shared his displeasure with ESPN sideline reporter Jack Arute, especially the call against Carter.

"That irritates the crap out of me," Bowden said.

GENO LEAVING? Junior LB Geno Hayes is considered the most likely FSU player to leave school early and declare for the NFL draft. He didn't commit either way following Monday's game.

"Right now, [I will] just sit back and evaluate all my options," Hayes said when asked about his immediate plans. "That's all I'm going to do."

NO PICKS ALLOWED: Weatherford broke Chris Weinke's school and ACC record for most consecutive passes without throwing an interception. Weatherford ran his streak to 270 until throwing a pick in the fourth quarter, easily eclipsing Weinke's record of 237.

BOWL BITS: FSU senior WR De'Cody Fagg caught five passes, closing his career with a 26-game reception streak ? The crowd of 68,611 set a Music City Bowl record ? Kentucky QB Andre Woodson's two first-half TD passes gave him 38 for the season, breaking former Wildcats star Tim Couch's single-season record (37 in 1998) ... FSU dropped to 2-4-1 all-time in bowl games against SEC schools.

Scott Carter

title: Getting ready for the big game
content:
Nine days and counting.

And the city is already in a frenzy.

It's all Eagles, all the time. On television, in the streets, on the cars, on city buildings. Philadelphia is getting ready for the first Eagles Super Bowl showdown in 24 years, and the Daily News has the guide you need to navigate your way through the week day-by-day, from what to watch to where to party.

We even offer suggestions for football movies to rent this week to get you psyched for game day.

If you're planning to invite the whole neighborhood to your Super Bowl bash, you'd better get busy today, since everyone from local caterers to liquor stores are expecting the biggest Super Bowl party rush for food, decorations, and booze in years.

Here's what's happening:

TODAY/TONIGHT

AT HOME

* Head to the local grocery store for a first wave of food shopping. No, it's not too early to make it and freeze it, according to Super Bowl food guru Jack McDavid, owner of Jack's Firehouse, at 23rd and Fairmount, and chef at the "Taste of the NFL" dinners held each year in Super Bowl cities the night before the big game.

Some of McDavid's suggestions for freezing and thawing the day before the game: individual macaroni and cheese servings heaped into paper cupcake cups, a pot of chili, and fully cooked calzones and lasagna.

Remember to snag all your sodas, chips and bagged snacks today and stow them for the week.

MOVIE TO RENT

"Remember the Titans" (2000). Denzel Washington helps fight prejudice via his Virginia high school football team.
