title: Rams question Patriots' 'perfection'
content:
The New England Patriots committed 22 penalties in their first six games this season. The Pats' total still is 22 - a league low - after they pitched a perfect game Sunday in turning aside the Rams 23-16 in Foxborough, Mass.

That's right: New England was assessed zero penalty yards. "Never ever, ever, ever played a perfect team before," Rams guard Richie Incognito quipped. "That's all I am going to say on that one."

The one penalty that was called against the Pats - offensive holding in the fourth quarter - was declined by the Rams because quarterback Matt Cassel was sacked for an 8-yard loss on the play.

The Rams were flagged 12 times (three were declined) and set back 63 yards. "We did commit some penalties," coach Jim Haslett conceded Monday after watching the game tape. "I thought there should have been some calls on the other side."

Haslett noted three instances in which he felt New England should've been hit with a personal foul: a facemask on quarterback Marc Bulger, a late hit on Bulger and an out-of-bounds hit on cornerback Fakhir Brown after an interception.

Haslett also maintained that the Rams got a bad placement - at the Pats' 48-yard line - on a punt that Donnie Jones shanked out of bounds midway through the fourth quarter. New England tied the score 16-16 six plays later with a field goal.

"I thought we should've had at least another 10 yards," Haslett said.

The Rams will send tapes to the league office, citing plays in which they think calls were missed. "We've got a few today," Haslett said, smiling. "It doesn't help. It just lets them know you know."

Still, Haslett emphasized that calls made, or not made, in the heat of the game can be seen in more ways than one. "They're all judgmental calls," he said. "Your eyes or my eyes might see two different things. They're subjective. That's kind of how that works."

Injury Update

A reinjured right hamstring by defensive end Leonard Little was the Rams' only significant injury Sunday. Little, who missed two games in September after pulling his hamstrung, went out early vs. the Pats.

"We'll see how he goes in practice this week," Haslett said. "He seems upbeat, and he worked rehab pretty good today."

Running back Steven Jackson (thigh) "should be ready to go this week," Haslett said. The availability of defensive tackle Adam Carriker (ankle) for Sunday's game against NFC West-leading Arizona is questionable, Haslett said.

Haslett also reported that tight end Joe Klopfenstein has a torn ligament in his wrist and will be fitted with a cast or a brace. Cornerback Tye Hill (knee) will return to practice this week, and wideout Drew Bennett (foot) will start running for the first time since he was hurt in the season opener.

Defending Bulger

Those placing the lion's share of the blame for the loss on Bulger are off the mark, Haslett insisted. "I thought Marc played OK," Haslett said. "He didn't have a lot of time, and it's hard to step up (in the pocket) sometimes. He's got a young group of receivers out there, and they're kind of learning each other."

Bulger, sacked four times and pressured often, threw for a season-high 301 yards, including a 69-yard touchdown to rookie wide receiver Donnie Avery.

But Bulger struggled down the stretch, connecting on only six of 16 passes in the final period. He was intercepted with 1:08 to go, ending the Rams' final threat.

RAM-BLINGS

Nickel back Jason Craft collected a team-high nine tackles, according to the coaches' film review. ... With two sacks, rookie defensive end Chris Long boosted his season total to four. Little is the team leader, with 4 1/2.

title: So far, predictability is in short supply
content:
The great god Parity rules in the NFL this season. After just three weeks, there are only three undefeated teams in the standings, and that may make for entertaining watching, but those results are a headache for bettors who are looking for some predictability.

Only two teams are 3-0 against the spread, and both are surprises. Pittsburgh, without suspended starting quarterback Ben Roethlisberger,and Kansas City, with a largely anonymous roster, are perfect on the betting boards.

If you're looking for trends, it is that the underdogs have been carrying the day with an overall record of 28-20. And at home, the dogs are 13-8 (nearly 62 percent).

Baltimore at Pittsburgh (minus-1). Now that the Steelers have proved that they can win with just about anyone playing quarterback, the notion of trying to handle Baltimore with Roethlisberger still on suspension seems a lot less daunting. In this bitter rivalry, the record against the spread could not be closer. In the last nine games through 2006, these two are 4-4-1. If I had to give any more than one point, I would not pick this game. Pick: Steelers, giving 1.

New England at Miami (plus-1). It's hard to believe that this Patriots defense is a Bill Belichick production. New England is No. 28 in the NFL in points allowed and No. 27 in yards allowed. Meanwhile, the Dolphins have matured as an offense behind quarterback Chad Henne, and the Miami defense can be stout, although it faltered last week against the Jets. For the second week in a row, Miami is a home underdog against a division rival, and it's hard seeing a Bill Parcells-assembled team being embarrassed two straight times in its own house. Pick: Dolphins, getting 1.

Houston at Oakland (plus-3). Texans wide receiver Andre Johnson has an ankle injury and has been listed as questionable, but Houston should be in bounce-back mode after a disappointing loss to Dallas. I have to admit that head coach Tom Cable has kept the Raiders competitive the last couple of weeks, especially against the spread, but being at home has been of no advantage to Oakland over the last several years. In their last 57 at home, the Raiders are a wretched 17-39-1 against the spread. Pick: Texans, giving 3.

Seattle at St. Louis (plus-1). Yes, the Seahawks are coming off a monster win over San Francisco, but that was at home where Seattle is 2-0 both straight up and against the spread. The Rams, despite being 1-2 straight up, have been competitive in every game and have been quietly playing excellent defense, giving up less than 17 points a game. And they're a home dog. Pick: Rams, getting 1.

Carolina at New Orleans (minus-131/2). The Panthers are going with a rookie quarterback, Jimmy Clausen, and the 'Dome is going to be a tough place for a guy who's just getting his bearings - and already has his star wide receiver screaming in his ear. Pick: New Orleans, giving 131/2.

I like but don't love . . .Indianapolis at Jacksonville (plus-71/2). I'm probably being influenced by last week's Eagles' rout in Jacksonville. Pick: Colts, giving 71/2.

If you held a gun to my head . . .Washington at Philadelphia (minus-6). Pick: Redskins; Denver at Tennessee (minus-61/2). Pick: Titans; Cincinnati at Cleveland (plus-3). Pick: Bengals; San Francisco at Atlanta (minus-7). Pick: 49ers; Arizona at San Diego (minus-8). Pick: Cardinals; Detroit at Green Bay (minus 141/2). Pick: Lions; Chicago at New York Giants (minus-4). Pick: Bears.

Pick of the Week

New York Jets at Buffalo (Plus-5).

If the Jets continue to play offense they way they have the last two weeks, they'll turn Rex Ryan from blowhard into prophet. Quarterback Mark Sanchez has six touchdown passes and zero interceptions so far this season, and running back LaDainian Tomlinson had a productive if less than Canton-like day in a win over Miami in which the Jets covered as road favorites. The Bills rolled up 30 points against New England last week in a losing effort, but that says more about the Patriots' defense than Buffalo's offense. The switch to Ryan Fitzpatrick at QB may help a bit, but the Bills are still a fairly dysfunctional outfit, and the Jets are perfectly capable of creating at least three turnovers and holding the Bills to 10 points. Pick: Jets, giving 5.

Contact Bill Ordine at 215-854-2939 or wordine@phillynews.com.

title: HERM NO. 1 JET FIGHTER       PLAYERS CREDIT COACH FOR PLAYOFF MOTIVATION
content:
Herman Edwards said the turning point in the season occurred Nov. 2, the day before the Jets faced the Chargers in San Diego. He summoned his coaching staff to a meeting at the team hotel in LaJolla, Calif., where he reaffirmed his marching orders with a passionate, one-hour talk to his lieutenants.

Since then, the Jets are 7-2.

Edwards' wife, Lia, believes the defining moment occurred three weeks earlier, the bye weekend. The couple had made plans to visit their son, Marcus, in Tampa, where he plays football for the University of South Florida. It was a rare chance to see his son play, but Edwards canceled the trip.

It hurt him deeply, but the Jets were mired in a four-game losing streak, and Edwards felt he had to stay in New York.

"He had to do what was in the best interest of the football team, so he decided to stay home and reevaluate some things," Lia said yesterday by phone. "He went into his office for two days. It was just him, figuring out what to do, how to motivate the team.

"That was a good turning point, that bye week," she added. "Everything changed after that."

It changed, all right. The Jets, left for road-kill, rebounded to capture the AFC East title and they face the Colts in a wild-card game Saturday at Giants Stadium.

Obviously, many factors contributed to the turnaround - namely, the emergence of Chad Pennington - but the No. 1 reason, according to those in the locker room, was Edwards' leadership.

"He's been the solid rock that we've leaned upon throughout this whole season," Pennington said. "He's been the guy who never went to the extremes, whether it was way down low with his emotions or way up high."

Edwards never showed it publicly, but there were times when he was down, according to his wife.

"He was discouraged because he knew how talented the team was," Lia said. "It's hard. You start to question yourself when you're 2-5, but he didn't change his values. He stayed with his beliefs."

Edwards became totally focused on rescuing the season, Lia said. That meant a slight change in lifestyle. Instead of going out to dinner on Friday nights, their usual weekly getaway, they stayed at home and watched movies. That hasn't changed.

It can be a lonely existence, coaching a struggling team. Your assistant coaches are locked in their offices, searching for solutions. Your head-coaching friends around the NFL are busy with their own problems. Amid the turmoil, Edwards drew inspiration from his wife.

"The only person who was kind of wearing me out was my wife," he said. "She would look at me and shake her head and say, 'You're not talking to them right.' Then we'd lose another game and she'd say, 'You're really not talking to them right.' It got down to the third game and she said you better figure it out.

"She kept giving me confidence by telling me, 'You're a good coach. You know you're a good coach, so just keep coaching. She kept saying to make them listen."

Edwards didn't turn into venom-spewing tyrant - that's not his personality - but he did convey a greater sense of urgency, according to several players. Then came the big pow-wow with his coaches.

"At that point, he felt it was important to bring the whole crew together and discuss his philosophy and vision for the football team," offensive coordinator Paul Hackett said. "That was probably the single-most important thing that happened this year, when we shifted in San Diego."

It culminated on Sunday with a division-clinching win over the Packers. Afterward, Lia met her husband on the field and gave him a congratulatory kiss. She choked up yesterday as she recalled the moment. She also remembered his first words:

"We did it!"

title: FOOTBALL FRIDAY: THE NFL GAMES OF THE WEEK
content:
GIANTS (11-2) AT COWBOYS (8-5)

Romo needs to prove himself

Cowboys QB Tony Romo only added to his reputation as a late-season flop in Pittsburgh. He's back home against a Giants defense that ranks fifth in sacks per pass play.

BRONCOS (8-5) AT PANTHERS (10-3)

Panthers aiming for No. 1 seed

Carolina should be able to physically dominate the AFC West leaders this week en route to playing the Giants next week in a game that could decide the NFC's No. 1 seed.

STEELERS (10-3) AT RAVENS (9-4)

Classic AFC North fight

Because Pittsburgh beat the Ravens 23-20 in overtime earlier this season, another victory here would make the Steelers 2-for-2 in AFC North titles under Mike Tomlin.

title: Eagles' Taylor, Titans' Wycheck receive players of the week honors
content:
Eagles cornerback Bobby Taylor, Tennessee Titans tight end Frank Wycheck, an Archbishop Ryan High School product, and special teamer Tim Johnson of the Oakland Raiders, were named the NFL offensive, defensive and special teams players of the week for the divisional playoffs, the league announced yesterday.

Taylor played a vital role in helping the Eagles to a 20-6 win over the Atlanta Falcons on Saturday at Veterans Stadium. The Pro Bowl cornerback made his first career playoff interception a memorable one, returning the pickoff 39 yards for the game's first touchdown, giving the Eagles a 7-0 first-quarter lead.

Wycheck had a career-high 123 receiving yards and matched a personal best with 10 receptions in Tennessee's 34-31 overtime victory over Pittsburgh.

Johnson was honored for his play in Oakland's 30-10 win over the New York Jets. The first-year linebacker from Youngstown State led the Raiders with four special teams tackles and helped keep the Jets' AFC-best kick-return unit (23.8-yard average) in check.

* Tennessee quarterback Steve McNair did not practice yesterday, and defensive end Jevon Kearse probably will not practice this week as the Titans prepare for the AFC Championship Game at Oakland on Sunday.

Kearse has pain in his left foot, which he broke in the opener. McNair threw some passes and was able to grip the ball even though his right thumb still is sore after having skin trimmed from the first knuckle in last week's 34-31 overtime victory over Pittsburgh. Both are expected to play Sunday.

* Dallas police considered more charges against Cowboys reserve defensive back Dwayne Goodrich, who's accused in a hit-and-run accident that killed two people. Goodrich, 24, was released from jail early Wednesday after posting $50,000 bond on two felony counts of manslaughter. Authorities said he also could be charged with failure to stop and render aid.

Police say Goodrich was the driver of a car that slammed into the scene of an accident on North Dallas freeway early Tuesday. Three people were trying to help a man escape a burning car when the speeding car hit three people, killing two of them.

* Dallas Cowboys coach Bill Parcells hired three assistants, including offensive line coach George Warhop. He also hired Tony Sparano as the tight ends coach, and Mike MacIntyre as an assistant secondary coach.

* Oakland Raiders defensive end Trace Armstrong was placed on injured reserve with a ruptured groin, ending his successful comeback season.

* The San Diego Chargers are offering to pay for half of a $400 million stadium they want built to replace 36-year-old Qualcomm Stadium. Under a proposal that the team will present to a citizen's task force tonight, the public's contribution of $200 million would be paid off in part by taxes generated by a mixed-use "urban village" that would include housing, shops, restaurants and a hotel on approximately 70 acres of the 166-acre Qualcomm site.

title: Favre hits home with magic game
content:
There is no manual for this, no textbook to study, no playbook to memorize.

There is no right way to grieve, no wrong way to mourn.

There is only what works for you.

So when we lose a loved one, we stumble about until we find what seems to fit, what seems to feel right for us, what we can draw around ourselves for warmth and comfort.

For Brett Favre, playing a football game the night after his father died is what felt right.

He ended up playing the game of his life.

And as you watched it, and him, every moment you have ever spent with your children, and your grandchildren, came flooding back. Every game of catch. Every bedtime story read. Every soccer game driven to. Every bit of homework agonized over. All of it, all of it that binds us and bonds us forever . . . mothers and sons, fathers and daughters, all the things we fumble to find the words for, all the things that, in the end, make everything else matter less than one minuscule grain of sand on a very large beach.

This all came in the midst of what is always such an emotional holiday anyway, when the ache of loss feels especially sharp and keen, when those who are no longer with us seem close by somehow, and here was this quarterback honoring the memory of the man who had been both his father and his coach by doing what he was sure the old man wanted.

Indeed, at one point in the game, one of Brett Favre's receivers, Javon Walker, said to him: "You know, your dad is having a hell of a time watching the way you're playing."

Against this, on Monday Night Football, the Oakland Raiders had no chance, no chance at all. Brett Favre was a man possessed. You swore you saw a bolt of blue flame arc from him to the rest of the Green Bay Packers, and they, too, played with an inspired, unstoppable fury.

By intermission, Brett Favre had played a statistically perfect game. The NFL has no higher quarterback rating than what he compiled in the first half, as he passed for 311 yards and four touchdowns.

But even more electric was the way he played, with a sandlot joy, turned into a kid again, running free, laughing in the sun, rearing back and flinging the ball a country mile, certain that his receiver would outleap the defenders, sometimes as many as three of them, because this was a thing meant to be, a night on which he could do no wrong, in a game that would take on a life of its own and pass into lore and legend.

For one night, the field became Brett Favre's refuge.

And then, yesterday, in the lyrically named Mississippi town of Pass Christian, on Christmas Eve, Irvin Favre, who had died at the age of 58, was buried.

The father of Brett Favre had suffered a heart attack while driving along the dusty Mississippi back roads, his pickup truck ending up in a ditch.

It fell to Deanna, Brett Favre's wife, the wrenching duty of making the phone call to her husband. She couldn't.

Instead, she called Doug Pederson, Favre's backup, on Pederson's cell phone. The same Doug Pederson so many of us roasted the first year of Andy Reid's coaching tenure with the Eagles. All you need to know about Doug Pederson is that he was the one Deanna Favre trusted and turned to in a terrible time, and it was Doug and Jeannie Pederson whom the Favres asked to fly home with them.

"Every game since the fifth grade," Favre said, referring to how often his father had seen him play.

And his father was his high school coach, too, and, yes, he was always harder on his own son than on the rest of them, which is almost always the way it is with fathers and sons, especially in such situations.

All that you need to know about Irvin Favre is that, even though he had a son who could throw a football through a car wash without it getting wet, he had other kids, too, other players' well-being and development to tend to, and some of them were very good at running the ball.

So Irvin Favre installed the wishbone.

His son remembers telling him: "I'll never get to the pros this way, Dad."

But of course, he did.

And when Brett Favre finally does retire, his next stop will be Canton, Ohio, and the Hall of Fame, and on that hot July day when he is inducted, he will feel, again, his father close by.

That game Monday night was No. 205 in a row for Brett Favre, which is, by several city blocks, a record for a position fraught with peril. For 14 years, they have tried to hurt him enough so that he cannot play. No matter what they have broken, he has always played.

This time, what was broken cannot be splinted or casted.

He played not for the record, but for his team, which is all-out trying to reach the playoffs, and for the man who coached him and loved him and drove him and hugged him.

Wesley Walls, one of the Packers who catches Brett Favre's hissing spirals, said: "He played an amazing game for us, and we all felt we had to do the same for him."

The Packers' coach, Mike Sherman, said: "We were close as a team before, but now we're even closer. We can't change the events of the last 24 hours, but those events have changed us."

Some of our games are silly and many are irrelevant, and a lot of what we fret over is foolish. But every entrancing once in a while, one of them brings us a moment for reminding and for remembering.

Every once in a while, it's Christmas.

Contact columnist Bill Lyon at 215-854-5508 or blyon@phillynews.com.

title: Eagles - ALL PLANS LEAD TO ROMO
content:
IF THE EAGLES get good pressure on the opposing quarterback, Jim Johnson's defenders expect to wreak havoc.

And sure enough, the last time the Birds faced Dallas quarterback Tony Romo,back on Nov. 4, havoc was wreaked. But the Eagles were wreakees, not wreakers.

Time and time again, Romo danced away from pressure and found receivers open in the middle of the field - often Terrell Owens, who romped through his former teammates for 10 catches and 174 yards.

"We added pressure," Eagles weakside linebacker Takeo Spikes recalled yesterday, as the 5-8 Birds prepared for this weekend's visit to the 12-1 Cowboys. "When there was pressure added, he dumped the ball off. We had some busts; [but] at times, it wasn't busts, guys were out there playing hard, trying to make plays, and failed to cover up the back. We're pretty much sure, [this time] they won't be open for that reason."

Largely lost amid the general disappointment in the Eagles' fortunes this season has been the fact that the defense has been decent; last week the Giants managed one touchdown, for which they drove all of 37 yards after a Brian Westbrook fumble. But the Cowboys pounded the Birds, winning 38-17, Romo completing 20 of 25 passes for 324 yards and three touchdowns. No other opponent has scored so many points.

"It was probably our worst defensive game, to tell you the truth," Johnson said yesterday.

Johnson is in his ninth season here, facing a challenge in Romo unlike anything he has seen in the NFC East until now. For much of the Andy Reid era, Donovan McNabb was the only emerging star quarterback in the division. Johnson was able to blitz and befuddle a long progression of Quincy Carters, Patrick Ramseys and Eli Mannings. Romo, 27, in his second season as a starter, is something different.

"Right now, he's playing as high-performance as I've seen since I've been here," Johnson acknowledged. "I can't think of anyone else in the division who has played like him."

Almost exactly a year ago, on Christmas Day, the Eagles went to Texas and handled Romo well, holding him to 14-for-29 for 142 yards, his lowest total of the 11 games he started in 2006. Jeff Garcia led the Birds to a 23-7 victory that might have been that team's best performance.

"He's probably a guy who has much better vision this year than last year," Johnson said. "If the first receiver's not there, he goes to the second. If the second's not there, he goes to the third. He's been very effective on checkdowns when people take away their main receiver . . . He's doing a great job with that, probably as good as anybody in the league right now."

It could be that the way to defense Romo - not that there is a great way, given the Cowboys' extensive weapons - is not to blitz but to cover, hoping he'll throw into coverage, the way a quarterback he has been compared to sometimes does.

"A lot of guys compare his game play to Brett Favre," Spikes said. "He's a guy where, he may not throw off the front foot, he may not step into every throw, but he's very football savvy. He understands the game, and he will do whatever it takes to make plays. A guy who has great escapability, and at the same time, at the last minute, he's going to make a play happen. He's a playmaker."

Corner Lito Sheppard agreed, and cited an example from the November game.

"On one play, Romo did a great job of sidearming a pass - 'QI' [safety Quintin Mikell] was right in his face, he sidearmed it around him, they were able to get 30 or 40 yards on that one," Sheppard said. "He does a great job of moving around."

Though that Nov. 4 game was a blowout, there was a point where the Eagles had a real chance to make a game of it. They trailed just 14-7, time ticking down in the first half, when Sheppard stepped in front of Romo's receiver, Patrick Crayton, and pilfered his seventh interception against the Cowboys since 2003. But Dallas safety Ken Hamlin's pick of McNabb gave the ball right back, and the Cowboys ended up taking a 21-7 halftime lead anyway.

Romo has thrown 14 picks this year, which is a lot, but five of them came in one game, against Buffalo.

"He's a very accurate quarterback," Sheppard said. "If you're close to the receiver, you definitely have a chance to make plays . . . I wouldn't necessarily say he has the strongest arm, but he does a great job of timing it up, and putting the ball where the receiver has a better chance of making the play than the DB. If you take some chances, you can probably steal one, but if you don't steal it, you just know you can get burned."

If the Eagles' defense has a chance on Sunday, it might lie in approaching the Cowboys unconventionally, the way they approached the Patriots back on Thanksgiving weekend. The Pats scored three offensive touchdowns in a 31-28 victory, but compared to some of the performances New England has managed this season, it almost seemed the Pats were struggling. The Eagles sacked Tom Brady three times, using a three-man front quite a bit and blitzing linebacker Chris Gocong from unpredictable angles. One of the main things they did was limit Randy Moss (five catches, 43 yards, all in the first half). Moss didn't catch a pass after taking a big-time hit from safety J.R. Reed in the third quarter.

"That's the same type of character we're going against, literally," Johnson said, drawing chuckles from his audience. "That's the same type of person you're going against. We didn't do a good job against T.O. last time. I know our guys are up to it. We'll see how it goes."

Sheppard said the receivers are similar, but he wouldn't bet on Owens reacting similarly to getting laid out.

"That'd get T.O. a little 'crunk.' You give him a good hit, he's coming back for more, I guarantee that," Sheppard said.

Johnson noted that first-year Cowboys offensive coordinator Jason Garrett moves Owens around more than the previous coaches did.

"We have to make sure we're in the right combination coverage, depending on where he is," Johnson said.

Johnson said he feels Sheppard is finally healthy, and he wants to see better play from his secondary, really the defense's only disappointing unit this season. The Birds rank ninth in the NFL against the rush, but just 18th against the pass. *

title: BRETT'S FAVRE AND AWAY A SPORTING HERO
content:
TEN days ago, Green Bay Packers quarterback Brett Favre dislocated his left shoulder and sprained his right elbow.

Mere inconveniences for Favre. On Sunday, he ignored the injuries and started his 250th consecutive game for the Packers, leading them to a 38-7 win over the Oakland Raiders.

That's more than 15 years without missing a game in one of the most brutal sports you can play.

Favre (left) who broke Dan Marino's all-time record for touchdown passes earlier this season, is an inspirational figure, a hero for what he does on the field and the way he gives his time to good causes off it. No wonder Sports Illustrated magazine just named him their Sportsman of the Year.

title: Searching near and Favre for model for McNabb
content:
TALK TO ANDY REID about the development of Donovan McNabb and it's a

pretty good bet he'll eventually work Brett Favre's name into the conversation.

As everyone knows, Reid was an assistant coach on Mike Holmgren's staff in Green Bay from 1992 through '98.

He watched Favre grow up as an NFL quarterback. Watched him develop from a cannon-armed project to a three-time league MVP. Watched him guide the Packers to three consecutive division

titles, two

conference championships and a Super Bowl victory.

Favre's career has served as a "how-to" guide for Reid in McNabb's development. The Eagles coach frequently compares McNabb to Favre at similar points in their careers.

And while I'm not a mind reader, I'd bet my kids' dwindling college education fund that Reid has mentioned Favre to McNabb this week in trying to help his struggling quarterback deal with the aftershock of his godawful play against New England and Tampa Bay the last 2 weeks.

I'd bet he's run down Favre's career with McNabb and pointed out that even Hall of Fame-bound quarterbacks occasionally stink up the joint.

Probably mentioned those back-to-back games against the Vikings and the Bucs last season in which Favre threw seven interceptions.

Probably mentioned the Packers' 2001 playoff loss to the Rams in which Favre threw six interceptions.

Probably pointed out the 5-week middle-of-the-season stretch in '99 when Favre threw 11 interceptions and completed just 50.6 percent of his passes. And that four-game October-into-November dry spell in '98 when he heaved 11 interceptions and completed just 72 of 135 passes, yet bounced back to lead the Packers to an 11-win season and a playoff berth. Probably pointed out Favre's ugly 19-for-41 performance in Week 2 of the '97 season against an Eagles team that wound up 6-9-1, as well as his 28-for-61 game against the 49ers in '96. The Packers wound up

going to the Super Bowl in both those years, and won it all in '96.

Whether McNabb ever will be as good as Favre remains to be seen. As I pointed out a few weeks ago, the only two things we knew with absolute certainty about him going into the season were that he can run and doesn't throw many interceptions. The first two games have done nothing to change that.

McNabb's supporters will tell you that's baloney. They'll tell you he is, without a doubt, one of the game's top four or five quarterbacks. Yet last year he was 22nd in the league in completion percentage (.584) and 23rd in yards per attempt (6.34).

Last year, before he got hurt, he had poor back-to-back-to-back performances against the Bucs, the Giants and the Bears that were almost as ugly as the last 2 weeks. Completed just 50 percent of his passes. Threw just one touchdown. Averaged just 5.37 yards per attempt.

The difference last year was that the Eagles managed to win all three of those games because they had a stingy defense that could carry them when their quarterback was in a funk. Without Brian Dawkins and Bobby Taylor, that's no longer the case.

Now they need McNabb to carry them. But that might be a tall order considering what we've seen from him in Weeks 1 and 2.

"To be a consistent quarterback, you must be fundamentally sound," ex-Eagle quarterback and current ESPN analyst Ron Jaworski said yesterday after breaking down the tape of the Eagles' loss to New England. "Right now, Donovan isn't. He's throwing off his back foot . . . there's been an incredible

regression in his mechanics."

Jaworski said the Patriots rattled McNabb by putting early pressure on him and using tight man-to-man coverage that forced him to make tough throws. "You can read a quarterback's mind by watching his feet," Jaworski said. "And Donovan's feet were constantly moving.

"I know he was sacked seven times. But in watching the tape, the protection, for the most part, was pretty good. He had people open. He just wasn't seeing them. He would speed through his read progressions too fast and then go to checkdowns. There would be times when he'd seem to be staring straight at an open receiver and then check down to the back instead."

For the most part, it has been a 4-year honeymoon for McNabb in Philadelphia. With the exception of the draft-day booing that seems to make its way into every national story about the guy, there seldom has been heard a discouraging word. Until Sunday. The boos were constant in the second half. At one point, a "We want A.J." chant started up. As he walked off the field after the game, somebody even heaved a water bottle at McNabb.

The good news is, unlike the guy who was picked just ahead of him in the '99 draft, Tim Couch, he didn't cry. Or at least not that we saw. But his resiliency will be tested if his play doesn't quickly improve.

"It's the first time he's faced this kind of adversity and been really attacked by the fans and media," Jaworski said. "He's got to work through it. I remember the first time it happened to me. It's not an easy thing to deal with. You almost have to take a foxhole mentality."

Which begs the question, what would Brett do? *

Send e-mail to pdomo@aol.com

title: NFL notebook
content:
Giants are wary of Vick

Watching Michael Vick put on a show on national television caused a variety of reactions from the New York Giants.

Coach Tom Coughlin got indigestion. Defensive tackle Barry Cofield had the feeling he was seeing one of the most unique players in the game. Former Falcons teammate and linebacker Michael Boley felt the dynamic Philadelphia Eagles quarterback was finally living up to his potential.

There was another general feeling among the Giants (6-3) on Wednesday. They either must be at their best Sunday night in Philadelphia or the Eagles (6-3) are going to make them look foolish in what has become the battle for first place in the NFC East.

The normally straight-laced Coughlin even wondered whether his team would be allowed to use 12 players on defense for a couple of plays.

"I think the officials can give us one of those based on last week, maybe," Coughlin said, referring to the Eagles' 59-28 win over the Washington Redskins, in which Vick threw for four touchdowns and ran for two.

Vick rushed 10 times for 80 yards, threw for 333 more and led the Eagles to a franchise-record 592 yards in total offense.

Stafford decides against surgery - Detroit Lions quarterback Matthew Stafford said he will not have surgery on his throwing shoulder. He said orthopedist Dr. James Andrews concurred with the team's evaluation that surgery isn't necessary at this time and he will focus instead on rehabilitation.

Stafford's separated right shoulder will keep him out of a second straight game Sunday and his seventh overall this season.

Henne doubtful for tonight - Quarterback Chad Henne is listed as doubtful for tonight's game against Chicago, while two other Miami Dolphins starters are out because of injuries.

Center Joe Berger (knee) and safety Chris Clemons (hamstring) will miss the game. Henne might be available as an emergency quarterback, but Tyler Thigpen is expected to make his first NFL start since 2008 with Kansas City.

For the Bears, linebacker Pisa Tinoisamoa is out because of a knee problem.

Elsewhere - The Denver Broncos released former first-round pick Jarvis Moss, who had just 3½ sacks in 3½ seasons. He moved from defensive end to linebacker last year. ... Arizona running back Beanie Wells says he is finally healthy after being slowed by a knee injury. ... Pittsburgh receiver Hines Ward cleared his post-concussion tests and practiced in full pads. His streak of catching a pass in 186 consecutive games ended when he sustained a concussion Sunday night. ... Houston quarterback Matt Schaub spent the night in the hospital and missed Wednesday's practice because of a bursa sac injury in his right knee. Coach Gary Kubiak expects Schaub to play Sunday vs. the Jets in New York.

title: Sunday’s games
content:
Sunday’s games

Minnesota (3-3)

at BEARS (3-3)

Time: noon, Channel 32

Line: Bears by 3

Series record: Vikings lead 50-42-2.

Last meeting: Vikings 20, Bears 13, Dec. 17, 2007.

Last week: Vikings beat Lions 12-10; Bears lost to Falcons 22-20.

Streaks, stats and notes: Vikings beat Bears 34-31 at Soldier Field last Oct. 14 as Adrian Peterson ran wild and Ryan Longwell kicked 55-yard field goal on final play. Peterson ran for 224 yards on 20 carries and scored on runs of 67, 73 and 35 yards. title: BOOK LOOKS AT THREADS IN DYNASTIES
content:
The book's title "Gridiron Dynasties" is something of a misdirection play.

After all, Central Catholic High School's football program has been good in recent years, but would hardly qualify as a dynasty.

That's a word reserved more, in Western Pennsylvania, for the Braddock Tigers and their 55-game winning streak in 1950s, Penn Hills and its WPIAL championship runs in the 1970s and Westinghouse and its 12 Pittsburgh City League titles in 13 season from 1954-66.

Still, the Central Catholic Vikings' football program is one of 12 from across the country profiled in the book.

"It's nice that we're included in something that's nationwide," Central Catholic coach Terry Totten said. "The book just came out recently and not a lot of people know about it yet, so I haven't received a lot of feedback."

Some of the other high school football programs profiled in "Gridiron Dynasties" are Colerain in Cincinnati, Ohio; Lowndes High from Valdosta, Ga.; Jenks High in Jenks, Okla.; and Katy High from Katy, Texas.

What's surprising is the author, Gavin Kralik, is the head coach at Bethel High School in Spanaway, Wash. He is quick to point out that he was not an English major at Liberty University.

"A motivating force for my doing the book was that I would go to coaching clinics and, in talking to other coaches, it became apparent that it was so much about X's and O's as it was about program building," said Kralik, 31. "I just felt that coaching is as much about building a program as it is anything else and I tried to pick out schools [for the book] that have outstanding programs."

He realizes that the term dynasty doesn't fit Central Catholic even though the Vikings have won PIAA Class AAAA titles in 1988 -- the first year for state-wide football playoffs -- and in 2004 and '07. But Kralik wasn't after teams that have a long string of state titles or used to be great.

"The game keeps evolving, so I decided to look at teams that have been outstanding in the past 10 years," he said. "But really, I focused more on the past four or five years and that's what led me to Central Catholic since it has won two state titles in four years."

He didn't interview coaches from afar. Kralik made it a point to visit each of the 12 schools in his book and spent a couple of days in Pittsburgh.

"He came in for a day last March," Totten said. "He asked a lot about the philosophy we have here and about the tradition of the school."

In the book, Kralik states that Central Catholic has 20 coaches on its staff, which is large by today's standards, and that most work for next to nothing. A fumble is that Central Catholic's pedigree includes Dan Marino and "Trent" Bulger. The reference was to St. Louis Rams quarterback Marc Bulger.

"Yeah, that's something we're going to have to correct," Kralik said.

Chapters on each school include an overview, something about the football program, the coaching staff's philosophy, information on the facilities and something unique to that team or state. For example, one of the sections for Central Catholic was about the Big 33 Classic game played in June in Hershey and its tradition. Under the chapter on Lakeland High in Florida was a section on spring practice in the state.

There are also chapters on the offensive systems each school uses, the common denominators in the 12 programs and program comparisons. The appendix has a diagram of a favorite offensive play from 11 of the 12 teams.

Most coaches don't like to open their playbook to outsiders, but Kralik said he didn't have many problems.

"One guy didn't want to give out a play," he said. "I think the philosophy for the rest of them was that they run their plays so well that it doesn't matter if you know what they're doing."

Kralik started working on the book in December and finished it early this summer. He said he might try his hand at another book or two before getting out of coaching.

Totten, meanwhile, has seen a copy of the book.

"I thought it was well done ... he did a good job," he said. "Of course, it's great for the school and our program to get the attention."

"I didn't want it to be an X's and O's kind of thing," Kralik said. "I think it's something everybody who likes high school football will enjoy."

title: Sports in brief
content:
Vikings face uncertain future after 2011 EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn. - The Minnesota Vikings are nearing the end of the season amid uncertainty about where they will play after 2011. Next year's session of the Minnesota Legislature will play a big part in the discussion. The Vikings are pushing for passage of a bill to help fund a new stadium that would replace the Metrodome, now known as Mall of America Field. Meanwhile, two separate groups in Los Angeles are trying to lure a team back to the nation's second-largest market.

Los Angeles hasn't had an NFL team since 1995. The Vikings say they've heard from both of those groups but that they're still committed to getting a new facility and staying in Minnesota for good. Colts retrack behind Manning NASHVILLE, Tenn.- Peyton Manning threw for 319 yards and two touchdowns, and the Indianapolis Colts snapped their three-game skid by beating the Tennessee Titans 30-28 on Thursday night to stay just a half-game back in the AFC South. Manning broke out of the worst slump of his career with a crisp performance against a defense that now hasn't intercepted a pass in 14 straight quarters. The Colts (7-6) haven't lost four straight since 2001 when their five-game slide prompted Jim Mora's famous rant about the playoffs. Tennessee (5-8) now has lost six straight and dropped 2½ games behind Jacksonville (7-5) in the AFC South with three to play. Favre watches practice again EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn. - Brett Favre is watching Minnesota Vikings practice again. Favre was on the field Thursday during the portion of practice open to the media, but was not throwing because of a sprained throwing shoulder. Offensive coordinator Darrell Bevell said earlier in the day that it will be a game-time decision for Favre on Sunday against the New York Giants. The 41-year-old quarterback has started an NFL record 297 straight games. Sterger: No lawsuit if NFL acts EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn.- A spokesman for the former New York Jets game hostess who allegedly received inappropriate photos and phone messages from Brett Favre says she won't sue the Vikings quarterback if he's punished by the NFL. Jenn Sterger's manager, Phil Reese, said Thursday that his client would like the league to "implement a program" to prevent unwanted advances - similar to those she's alleging Favre made. The alleged messages from Favre to Sterger were sent in 2008, the QB's only season with the Jets. Reese said Sterger wants the league to discipline Favre before his career ends. "It's not a money story," Reese said, adding his client's goal is some acknowledgment that Favre was in the wrong. Cecil Newton won't attend Heisman ceremony The father of Auburn quarterback Cam Newton said Thursday he will not attend the Heisman Trophy award ceremony because his presence might "rob Cam and the event of a sacred moment." Cecil Newton, who was invited to attend Saturday by the Heisman Trust, released the statement Thursday through George Lawson, the Atlanta-based Newton family attorney. Cam Newton is the favorite to receive the Heisman Trophy Saturday in New York. BASKETBALL Marquette weighs waiver for Fulce MILWAUKEE - Marquette will decide before next month's game at Pittsburgh whether forward Joe Fulce will return or pursue a redshirt after injuring his troublesome left knee again. Coach Buzz Williams says he's not convinced that Fulce will ever play again after sustaining a severe bone bruise on Tuesday night, despite a doctor's diagnosis that the injury wasn't as severe as initially feared. If Fulce doesn't play after the Golden Eagles' game against Pittsburgh on Jan. 8, the senior forward would be eligible for an NCAA medical hardship. NBA roundup: Celtics, Mavericks win Kevin Garnett banked in a shot off an alley-oop pass with 1.4 seconds left to give the Boston Celtics their ninth straight victory, 102-101 over the Philadelphia 76ers on Thursday. After Philadelphia's Andre Iguodala drove for a hook shot with 6.6 seconds left to give Philadelphia a 101-100 lead, Rajon Rondo flipped a long pass to Garnett for the winner. Garnett then raced to midcourt to intercept the 76ers' inbounds pass. * In Dallas, Dirk Nowitzki had 21 points and 10 rebounds, Shawn Marion added 18 points, and Dallas stretched its winning streak to 11 games, spoiling former Mavericks coach Avery Johnson's return. TH wire services

title: Hall of Fame quarterback Blanda dies Veteran of 26 seasons in NFL and AFL held pro scoring record when he retired in '76.
content:
ALAMEDA, Calif. - George Blanda, who played longer than anyone in pro football history and racked up the most points in a career that spanned four decades, mostly with the Chicago Bears and Oakland Raiders, died Monday after a brief illness. He was 83.

Blanda retired a month shy of his 49th birthday before the 1976 season. He spent 10 seasons with the Bears, part of one with the Baltimore Colts, seven with the Houston Oilers and his final nine with the Raiders. He was elected into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1981 in his first year of eligibility.

He held the pro scoring record when he retired, with 2,002 points. He kicked 335 field goals and 943 extra points, running for nine touchdowns and throwing for 236 more.

He also threw for 26,920 yards in his career and held the pro football record with 277 interceptions until Brett Favre passed him in 2007. His points record stood until it was topped by several players in recent years.

"It certainly doesn't bother me," Blanda said about losing the scoring record. "The one record I was happy to get rid of was the one for the most interceptions, when Brett Favre got that one."

It was a five-game stretch for Oakland in 1970 that is the lasting imprint of his career. At 43, Blanda led the Raiders to four wins and one tie with late touchdown passes or field goals.

Later that season, he became the oldest quarterback to play in a championship game, throwing two touchdown passes and kicking a field goal in Oakland's 27-17 loss to Baltimore in the AFC title game. His performance that season earned him The Associated Press Male Athlete of the Year honors.

Blanda joined the Oilers of the new American Football League in 1960 and played 16 seasons before hanging it up for good after the 1975 campaign. He led the Oilers to the first two AFL titles, defeating the Chargers for the championship after the 1960 and '61 seasons.

Blanda was the AFL Player of the year in 1961, holds AFL single-game passing record of 464 yards on Oct. 29, 1961, against Buffalo, and was chosen the league's all-time kicker.

title: THE NEW NO. 1 QUARTERBACK YOU CAN HAVE PEYTON, AND ROMO, TOO. WITH NO BRADY AROUND, BIG BEN IS NFL'S BRIGHTEST STAR
content:
Everything changed at 1:25 p.m. Sunday. When New England Patriots quarterback Tom Bradycrashed to the Gillette Stadium turf, clutching his left knee, the impact was felt all the way to Pittsburgh. Instantly, the Steelers became a more serious threat to make the Super Bowl. Instantly, Ben Roethlisbergerbecame the NFL's best quarterback.

What?

There's another quarterback you'd want more now that Brady is out for the season?

You can have Peyton Manningor Tony Romo.

I'll take No. 7.

I don't want anyone else trying to lead the Steelers through the AFC, which suddenly seems so much less formidable now that the powerful Patriots had a leg cut out from under them.

No one at Steelers headquarters yesterday was celebrating Brady's bad luck even if his injury significantly lessens the Patriots' Super Bowl hopes. NFL players are card-carrying union brothers. "You never want to see anyone get hurt like that," said veteran quarterback Charlie Batch, who has had his share of injuries, most recently a broken collarbone in the preseason that put him out for the season.

If anything had the Steelers in a mood to party one day after their 38-17 smack-down of the Houston Texans, it was seeing Roethlisberger breeze through the team's South Side complex without so much as a limp. He, just as easily as the great Brady, could be finished for the season. Within an hour after Brady went down after taking a hit from Kansas City Chiefs safety Bernard Pollard, Roethlisberger took a pop on his right knee from Texans defensive tackle Amobi Okoye on the next-to-last play of the first half.

At first, the hit looked bad. Roethlisberger tried to bury his face in the Heinz Field grass, clearly in pain. Who knows what offensive tackle Willie Colon was thinking when he rushed over to help him up? Colon and guard Kendall Simmons had tried to steer Okoye clear of Roethlisberger and were sickened when they saw him crash into the franchise. I know what I was thinking: The season could be over.

I'm guessing I wasn't alone.

Batch said Roethlisberger was pretty shook up at halftime, at least until the docs checked him out.

That made several hundred thousand of us, the 64,000-plus at the stadium and the countless Steelers fans watching on television.

Who really could relax until Roethlisberger jogged onto the field for the first series of the third quarter and promptly lead the Steelers on another touchdown drive?

"After seeing it on film, it could have been another Tom Bradything," Simmons said after an afternoon team meeting. "Ben said he was all right today. I'm just thankful he was able to pull his leg back enough in time."

Roethlisberger isn't just very good.

He's very lucky.

"That's a quarterback's worst nightmare, someone rolling up your leg like that," Batch said. "That and when someone hits your arm as you're throwing. There are times you know you're going to have to take a hit and you can kind of prepare for it. But there's nothing you can do when someone gets your leg like that."

Even if Roethlisberger's knee did ache a bit yesterday, it's safe to say he felt better than he did after a lot of games the previous two seasons when he was sacked 93 times, more than any AFC quarterback. In addition to the Okoye hit, he was sacked twice by defensive end Mario Williams, once landing on his throwing shoulder, which is never a good thing for a quarterback. Other than those plays, though, his offensive line did a good job keeping him clean.

Just as he predicted it would.

"I'm excited for those guys so they can silence the critics who are talking bad about them," Roethlisberger said during training camp. "I have all the faith in the world that they'll protect me."

It's fun to think about how good Roethlisberger will be this season if he stays healthy. He was just about perfect against the Texans, completing 13 of 14 passes for 137 yards and two touchdowns, both to wide receiver Hines Ward. He also wisely picked his spots scrambling, running just twice, the first time for 17 yards on a third-and-4 play on the Steelers' first possession.

"He looks to me to be all the way back to where he was in our workouts after the Super Bowl year, before the [motorcycle] accident," Batch said. "He was on fire then. Every pass was right there. Every decision was the right decision. It's the same way now, and he's just going to keep getting better and better."

Think about that for a second.

Roethlisberger became the first quarterback in NFL history to go 13-0 in the regular season as a rookie in 2004.

He became the youngest quarterback to win a Super Bowl in '05.

He made the Pro Bowl and set Steelers records for touchdown passes (32) and passer-rating (104.1) last season.

And he's going to keep getting better?

Why not?

I ask you:

Why can't Roethlisberger take over for Brady as the NFL's brightest star?

title: REID'S STYLE HEAVY ON SUBSTANCE; THE FORMER PACKERS ASSISTANT COACH EMPLOYS AN ''OFFENSIVE LINEMAN; MENTALITY'' AS EAGLES COACH.
content:
Everything anyone could ever want to know about Philadelphia Eagles coach Andy Reid is illustrated by his parking spot outside of Veterans Stadium -- and the vehicle parked there.

''It is the prime-time parking spot in the whole place,'' said Packers quarterback Matt Hasselbeck, Reid's former pupil in Green Bay who was in the lot while visiting the Philadelphia area during the off-season. ''It's like, right up in front. There's a Lexus RX-300, there's a Mercedes Benz. All these nice cars.''A similar story appeared in the first edition on page 4C.  The headline was the same.

And then there is Reid's mode of transportation. ''A 1988 Chevy Suburban,'' Hasselbeck said, chuckling. ''I think it's white with hunter green. It might even have the dark brown wood paneling on the side.''

''Actually, it's a '90,'' Reid said later.

That's Reid, the definition of substance over style -- whether it's his old Suburban or two unflattering pictures of him in GQ magazine this month mocking his wardrobe and girth.

''Andy probably won't care, and his wife will say, 'Aw, he looks cute,' '' Hasselbeck said, thumbing though the article, 'How GQ is the NFL coaching corps?' while sitting at his locker. ''That's just him, a hard-working, blue-collar type of guy.''

And as Reid returns to Green Bay for the first time as an opposing coach Sunday after five years as Packers tight ends coach and two as quarterbacks coach under Mike Holmgren, his former players are sure he hasn't changed a bit. Reid will still be down-to-earth, still be shabbily dressed and still be on top of his coaching game.

''I remember when Andy got the (quarterbacks) job (in 1997). He had been tight ends coach here for quite some time,'' quarterback Brett Favre said. ''He called me and said, 'Hey, I'm you're new quarterback coach.' I said, 'Oh, no.'

''Here's a former offensive lineman. (Reid played tackle and guard in college at Brigham Young). I'm thinking, 'What is he going to teach me about being a quarterback?' I was apprehensive about it. But I couldn't have been more wrong. He never played the position but he coached it as if he did. I was amazed.''

It was that brilliance, not to mention having Holmgren as a reference, that landed Reid, 42, the Eagles' job after the 1998 season. Although he might have had a shot at the Packers' job that went to Ray Rhodes -- Holmgren left Green Bay for Seattle on Jan. 8, 1999 and Reid became the Eagles' coach three days later -- Reid has no regrets. He started talking with the Eagles shortly after the Packers' playoff loss to San Francisco and hasn't looked back.

''Right after the San Francisco game, I came back here (to Philadelphia) and interviewed and committed to them, and really before anything was done in Green Bay,'' Reid said. ''So, I never got into that.''

He looks forward to coaching against Sherman, his good friend who became tight ends coach when Reid was promoted to quarterbacks coach in 1997 (and who replaced Rhodes as Packers head coach). Although they consider themselves close friends and have kept in contact since going their separate ways, neither plans on letting personal feelings affect them Sunday.

''I remember a few Thursday nights we fought over the last piece of pizza upstairs,'' Sherman said. ''But that element of it really isn't a factor for me at this present time. . . . At this juncture I just want to win.''

Reid feels the same way.

''As I mentioned to the players, this is not 'Win one for the Gipper' or 'Win one for Andy going back to Green Bay.' That becomes a distraction. They're here to win one for the Eagles,'' Reid said. ''I have an offensive lineman mentality, so I'm very narrow-minded. We're going there to play the football game. Maybe I'll come back in the summer and enjoy a few bratwursts.''

title: Bucs Notebook
content:
Faine is eager to make his return

TAMPA - Jeff Faine wants it known up front - he's no savior.

Tampa Bay's veteran center is eager to help a winless club when he returns Sunday from a strained triceps that has sidelined Faine since the season opener against Dallas.

It's been pretty frustrating," Faine said Monday after the Bucs fell to 0-5 with a 33-14 loss at Philadelphia. "You feel handcuffed not being able to help the way you want to. I think the guys up front have done pretty well, but it's tough when you take out a piece that makes all the line calls. It's tough to be cohesive."

Tampa Bay's running game has struggled since rolling up 174 yards against the Cowboys and second-year QB Josh Johnson faced a dizzying array of blitzes Sunday at noisy Lincoln Financial Field.

"That's (Philadelphia's) M.O.," Faine said, referring to the Eagles' reputation as heavy blitzers under longtime defensive coordinator Jim Johnson, who died earlier this year. "We weren't able to pick it up the way we should have. I expected a lot of pressure with a new quarterback in there, trying to test Josh and see how he responds."

As Sean Mahan returns to his reserve role, Coach Raheem Morris can't wait to see one of his team captains back on the field.

"He definitely helps you with all the protection issues you may have," Morris said. "He makes the call ? Jeff's the leader."

Faine said he isn't worried about coming back too soon.

"There's a risk just going out there, period," he said. "I'm really not too concerned with it."

Empty seats

With the 1-3 Panthers coming to town Sunday, there is a possibility of the first NFL blackout in Tampa since Raymond James Stadium opened in 1998.

Bucs spokesman Jeff Kamis said Monday that the club does not anticipate a blackout, although tickets remain available for Tampa Bay's initial matchup against an NFC South rival.

With the Bucs in the throes of a nine-game losing streak, MLB Barrett Ruud isn't quite sure what to expect from Sunday's crowd.

"There's always a mix - there are going to be fans that kind of quit on you, but there are others that stick with you," the former Nebraska Cornhuskers standout said. "I'm a fan, too. When Nebraska loses, I'm down. When they win, I'm up. Nobody wants to hear excuses. They want to see results and we do, too. It's with you all the time and honestly, it's with you a lot more than when you win. At least for me, the highs of winning don't equal the lows of losing."

Start me up

A surprising name has emerged among the league's sack leaders.

Following his three takedowns of Donovan McNabb on Sunday, Bucs DE Jimmy Wilkerson ranks second in the NFC behind Minnesota's Jared Allen (6.5) with 5 sacks.

That matches Wilkerson's 2008 total with Tampa Bay and dwarfs the one sack Wilkerson accumulated during five years as a spare part in Kansas City.

"Being a starter helps open your mind a little more," said Wilkerson, who was elevated to the top of the depth chart at left end this spring when veteran Kevin Carter was not re-signed. "All the years I've been playing as a backup, you try to go off what you see the starters do. You try to pick their brains and emulate them. Now, I have that role, knowing there's someone behind me that can easily take my job. I don't want to be back in that backup position."

No change at quarterback

Josh Johnson made strides in his second NFL start, and Morris has no intention of unseating him for rookie first-round pick Josh Freeman.

"Josh Johnson's the starting quarterback," Morris said. "I'm not going to think about putting Freeman in there when I've got Josh Johnson as my starter. We've got to get Josh Johnson better right now. Freeman's got to be in the lab in his mind saying he's the starter, preparing like a starter.

"When he has an opportunity, that's when he'll be in there."

Extra points

The Bucs signed rookie DE Michael Bennett off waivers from Seattle. To make room for the undrafted free agent out of Texas A&M, Tampa Bay waived rookie G Marc Dile of the University of South Florida ? Ruud is tied for third in the league with 49 tackles ? While the Panthers are one of five teams that haven't allowed a completion of 40 yards or more, the Bucs have yielded an NFL-high eight.

Ira Kaufman

title: Packers ink Westbrook to help patch defense; Cornerback: 6-year vet was cut by Dallas
content:
The Green Bay Packers began patching up their decimated defense Wednesday by signing veteran cornerback Bryant Westbrook.

The sixth-year veteran, who played five seasons in Detroit, was cut by Dallas on Sept. 18 after he committed two costly penalties in the Cowboys' embarrassing season-opening loss to expansion Houston.

Westbrook (6 feet, 201 pounds) takes the roster spot of defensive end Joe Johnson, who was placed on injured reserve Wednesday with a torn left triceps tendon.

Coach Mike Sherman also said he was leaning toward putting second-year defensive back Bhawoh Jue on IR this week because of nagging groin and hamstring injuries that have bothered him for months.

"I'm frustrated by the injuries, obviously, and his lack of availability to our special teams and to our team," Sherman said. "They have affected his progress because he's not out there. So, that's tough."

Jue was one of four defensive backs injured Monday night when the Packers (4-1) beat the Chicago Bears 34-21.

Starting cornerback Mike McKenzie aggravated a strained groin muscle in pregame warmups and was joined on the sideline by Jue and two other starters, cornerback Tyrone Williams and safety Darren Sharper, with hamstring injuries.

Tod McBride, who filled in admirably for McKenzie, also injured a knee.

The Packers, who already have been without starting safety Antuan Edwards (arm), listed McKenzie and Sharper as questionable and McBride and Williams as probable for New England on Sunday.

Sherman added cornerbacks Todd Franz and Brandon Jennings to the practice squad so he could have enough defensive backs this week.

He also closed practice for the first time in his three-year tenure as coach and put his assistants off limits to reporters.

"I don't anticipate it to be a long-term deal," Sherman said. "It's just while we sort through some things and make some personnel moves and changes."

Westbrook signed an $$ @875,000 contract with the Cowboys last summer but was released after their 19-10 loss to the Texans. At one point during that game, he was yanked off the field so coaches could talk to him.

"He had a good workout with us," Sherman said. "He showed a good burst, corner skills. He's a heavy hitter. He possibly could play safety at some point for us."

The fifth overall selection in the 1997 draft, Westbrook had an Achille's injury while playing for the Lions, but Sherman said that's no longer a concern.

"He has, according to our doctors, recovered totally from that," Sherman said. "He had Achille's tendinitis (in his other leg) in the preseason when he was at Dallas. We actually tried to bring him in sooner than this, but he hadn't fully recovered from that.

"Based upon my observation in his workout he has recovered from that and the doctors concurred that he has. So, I think he can help us," Sherman added. "He has a tremendous amount of experience in the league."

Sherman said he'd consider adding a defensive lineman to take Jue's roster spot.

With Johnson down, the Packers are without both of their starting ends. Vonnie Holliday has a torn pectoral muscle that will keep him out another month.

The Packers plan to replace Johnson, who signed a six-year, $$ @33 million free agent contract over the winter, by committee. One of those players is Rod Walker, whom Sherman tried to trade in August.

"I think sometimes your best personnel moves are the ones you don't make and hanging onto an extra defensive lineman really helped us," Sherman said.

Conversely, Sherman's drafting of pass-rusher Jamal Reynolds over linebacker Dan Morgan with the 10th overall selection in the 2000 draft is coming back to haunt him now.

Reynolds has been a bust in Green Bay and Morgan a force in Carolina.

"That is frustrating," Sherman said. "Obviously, that's a spot that you would have anticipated … could solve a problem, and it hasn't worked out that way."

Sherman said there might be a silver lining to the rash of injuries.

"When you have injuries it can either be divisive or unite your team," he said. "I think it can unite your football team and guys play at a higher level. The ones that are called upon play better than you thought they would and other players assume bigger responsibilities.

"So, life goes on."

Notes: Brett Favre, who has started 177 straight games, including playoffs, a record for NFL quarterbacks, is listed as probable on the injury report with a sore left (non-throwing) elbow. Favre was named the NFC's player of the week for the seventh time Wednesday after throwing for 359 yards and three TDs against the Bears and surpassing 40,000 yards passing for his career.

title: MCNABB'S RACE IS NOT THE REASON THAT HE'S OVERRATED
content:
RUSH LIMBAUGH was right on two points:

* Philadelphia Eagles quarterback Donovan McNabb IS overrated.

* The Eagles reached two NFC championship games on the strength of their blitzing, disruptive defense.

But when Limbaugh brought race into his discussion of McNabb's performance, he set off a wave of indignation that led to his resignation from ESPN. Despite what Limbaugh thinks, McNabb wasn't propped up by journalists sympathetic to McNabb's status as an African-American quarterback.

Before Philadelphia's 23-13 victory at Buffalo on Sunday, Limbaugh said this on ESPN's pre-game show: "I think what we've had here is a little social concern in the NFL. The media has been very desirous that a black quarterback do well. There is a little hope invested in McNabb, and he got a lot of credit for the performance of this team that he didn't deserve."

If McNabb is overrated, it's for one primary reason: The Eagles made him the second overall choice in the 1999 NFL draft. With that entry comes intense hype, great expectations, high standards. And to fulfill the hopes cast for him, McNabb would have to play brilliantly and take his team to the Super Bowl, and maybe even win the Super Bowl. The white John Elway was deemed overrated by some until he led the Denver Broncos to two consecutive Super Bowl championships late in his career.

If you're the second pick in the draft, and heralded as a franchise savior, you'd better deliver sensational results, and that's true of any quarterback, black or white. McNabb is no more or less overrated than Cleveland's Tim Couch, or San Diego's Drew Brees -- two acclaimed white quarterback draftees who haven't made a major impact on their teams.

McNabb has been a good, not great, quarterback in his four-plus NFL seasons. His highest QB rating is 86.0. He didn't play well in the biggest game of his career, a 27-10 loss at home to Tampa Bay in last season's NFC championship. He's regressed -- at least so far -- this season, completing 49.5 percent of his passes with no touchdowns and three interceptions for a terrible QB rating of 51.1. He's near the bottom of NFL QB rankings. It's also true that McNabb has never been surrounded by a tremendous su pporting cast.

What Limbaugh apparently overlooked is the diminishing relevance of race at the QB position. It used to be an issue, a controversy. And once upon a time, the sports media did campaign for black quarterbacks, raising the legitimate question of why more African-Americans weren't receiving the opportunity to play the position in the NFL.

In recent years, the emphasis has shifted to the frustration of African-Americans who desire to become NFL head coaches. Limbaugh is behind the times; black QBs are visible and popular. When Michael Vick returns from a broken leg in Atlanta, he'll be the 10th African-American quarterback to start an NFL game this season. The appearance of black quarterbacks has become such routine business in the NFL that they're recycled -- Jeff Blake, Rodney Peete and Tony Banks are examples -- just like the white journeymen QBs who bounce from team to team.

Limbaugh was just playing the role that ESPN created for him to boost ratings on the pregame "Countdown" show, which were up 10 percent. He was delivering controversy, but he gave the folks at ESPN more than they bargained for.

Still, I'm surprised Limbaugh didn't tough it out and instead resigned. After all, this is America, and he has the right to say stupid things.
