title: FANTASY FOOTBALL: WEEK FOUR!
content:
Congratulations. You are 3-0, and it looks like there is no possible way your potent team will ever be outscored. Before you start placing bets on your own team (I don't know if you can actually do this, but it wouldn't surprise me if there were websites for this), you might want to take a look at the next couple weeks as teams start having their seasonal bye weeks beginning this Sunday.

Since I started playing fantasy football back in 2004, a few things have held constant through each season: Donovan McNabb was most likely my quarterback (this is only my second season without the Syracuse alum), bye weeks usually accounted for two of my losses and one of my wins and my leading receiver at the end of the year is always someone I picked up off waivers. I am the Al Davis of drafting wide receivers, so I apologize to all of you who drafted Calvin Johnson this season. Matt Stafford's shoulder injury and the resulting mediocre stats from Johnson are probably my fault for taking him in the second round.

This is the time of the season where the waiver wire becomes your best friend. Whether you are looking for a one-week wonder to fill in for a bye or a minor injury, a player who is just about to start his season due to suspension or holdout or just a sleeper you want to take a flyer on because who you drafted was more Ryan Leaf than Peyton Manning, here are some common free agents to keep an eye on:

Waiver Wire Wonders

Sam Bradford, QB St. Louis vs. Seattle: Bradford has probably done as well as fans could possibly expect him to do for the Rams so far. The rookie may not be a good long-term solution if you need a quarterback, but this week he is playing a pass defense that allows over 300 yards per game.

Charlie Batch, QB Pittsburgh vs. Baltimore: Speaking of incredibly short-term solutions, Batch's starting gig has an expiration date of week six when Ben Roethlisberger returns from his four-game suspension. He's got a tougher test this week with the Ravens coming to the Steel City, but he has a much better supporting cast around him than Bradford.

Peyton Hillis, RB Cleveland vs. Cincinnati: In the shock of the season, the Browns have gotten off to a bit of a slow start. Due to Jerome Harrison's thigh injury along with quarterbacks Jake Delhomme and Seneca Wallace fighting their inability to play quarterback, Hillis has been forced to carry the load more and more over the past few weeks and should rack up another good statistical day.

Jacoby Jones, WR Houston @ Oakland: Matt Schaub has become one of the powerhouse quarterbacks in the league, largely thanks to a loaded receiving corps. Andre Johnson is still hobbled with an ankle injury, so Jones and Kevin Walter may get some more opportunities to impress. If Johnson plays through injury, he will have to face shutdown corner Nnamdi Asomugha. This could allow Jones to have a big day against the Raiders.

Aaron Hernandez, TE New England @ Miami: After giving up two touchdowns to Dustin Keller on Sunday night, the Dolphins defense has a lot of other things to worry about with the Patriots coming to town. New England has continued to stay in the air throughout the first few games without a proven running back, so look for Hernandez to get some opportunities in the red zone for his first NFL touchdown.

Dustin Keller, TE NY Jets @ Buffalo: Despite some great performances in last year's playoffs and the beginning of this year, Keller is available in about one out of every four leagues. His value might take a hit in the future with the return of Santonio Holmes. Nevertheless, Mark Sanchez calls his number on third down or in the red zone time after time, and that will be no different in Buffalo.

Start 'em

Greg Jennings, WR Green Bay @ Detroit: The blanket statement "Start any offensive player against Detroit" isn't too bad of an idea, but it especially isn't for Jennings this week. No. 85 is coming off a down week against the Bears, but a bad Lions defense and no ground attack from Green Bay will lead to some big numbers for Jennings and Aaron Rodgers.

Michael Vick, QB Philadelphia vs. Washington: It's really tough to believe that Vick is going to continue this torrid stretch, but I just don't think the Redskins are going to be the ones to stop it. With McNabb returning to Philly, this game has a chance of turning into a shootout, and Vick has a lot of weapons to throw to in the event of a firefight.

Maurice Jones-Drew, RB Jacksonville vs. Indianapolis: MJD remains the focal point of the Jaguar offense, and he always seems to shine brightest against the AFC rival Colts. In eight career games, Jones-Drew has scored nine touchdowns (seven rushing) and averages just under 100 yards per game.

Matt Ryan, QB Atlanta vs. San Francisco: After a massive overtime win in the Superdome last week, Ryan and the Falcons are flying high back home to face the Niners. Matty Ice looked really solid at home during a week two matchup against the Cardinals. Prime targets Tony Gonzalez and Roddy White have to like the look of their counterparts, who have given up seven touchdowns through the air this season thanks to some less-than-stellar quarterbacks.

Zach Miller, TE Oakland vs. Houston: The Texans may be a sexy pick in the AFC South, but there is no denying that they need to fix their pass defense. With limited options from his receiving corps, Bruce Gradkowski will be looking for his big tight end to attack the middle of the Texan defense.

DeAngelo Williams, RB Carolina @ New Orleans: The Saints haven't been very good against the run this year, granted against road-grating backs like Peterson, Gore and Turner. Carolina will have Jimmy Clausen under center again, so their only hope may be to run like the police are on their tail.

Ochocinco and T.O., WR Cincinnati @ Cleveland: Batman and Robin, or whatever the hell they call themselves, haven't really been tearing up Gotham yet this year. Facing the Cleveland Browns can solve a lot of their woes. Hopefully they don't get a call from Commissioner Gordon for excessive celebration.

Sit 'em

Jay Cutler, QB Chicago @ NY Giants: The Bears are the lone NFC team without a blemish in their loss column, but color me skeptical that Cutler has turned the corner as a premier quarterback. I'm still not convinced he has a clear concept of colors, because he throws a lot of critical passes seemingly straight at defenders. Don't expect the Giants to waste as many opportunities as the Packers did.

Pittsburgh, D/ST vs. Baltimore: On the off chance you have multiple defenses, steer clear of the Steel Curtain this week. Joe Flacco looks like he's finally starting to heat up with Todd Heap and Anquan Boldin. The Steelers' offense won't be able to sustain drives and give the defense the breather they'll need against a physical runner like Ray Rice.

Eddie Royal, WR Denver @ Tennessee: An overall bad matchup for the Broncos, it's especially ominous for the Denver receivers. The Titans still haven't given up a touchdown to a receiver this year. Royal, who gains most of his value from being a deep threat, may be in for a long week in the Volunteer State.

LaDainian Tomlinson, RB NY Jets @ Buffalo: Speaking of players I'm not sold on, I just don't get the feeling that LT is a safe bet any week unless you need an emergency flex player. Buffalo may be a lot of things, but they aren't bad against the run. I'd steer clear of Tomlinson and Shonn Green this week.

Lance Moore, WR New Orleans vs. Carolina: Classic buy low, sell high. I just don't think last week was a breakout game for Lance. The Saints have a lot of good receivers that sporadically post great numbers. But none of them, aside from Colston, do it with any consistency.

Rashard Mendenhall, RB Pittsburgh vs. Baltimore: With Charlie Batch still under center, everybody knows that Mendenhall will be ridden like a horse come Sunday. That includes the Ravens defense. Historically

title: Dramatic turnaround sees Dynamic Vick playing like an MVP
content:
Associated Press

Philadelphia

Michael Vick had just scored after another dazzling run against the Washington Redskins when Philadelphia Eagles fans at a local sports bar began chanting: "M-V-P! M-V-P! M-V-P!"

It was a far cry from what Vick was being called just over a year ago.

Once one of the NFL's biggest and highest paid stars, his career was in ruins and his life in shambles. He was broke, reviled and relegated to being a situational No 3 quarterback after serving 18 months in federal prison for running a dogfighting ring.

Now he's on top again, playing maybe better than ever, and just in time to become perhaps the biggest free agent on the market next year.

Vick may have had the best all-round game by a quarterback in NFL history on Monday against Washington. He threw for 333 yards and four touchdowns, and ran for 80 yards and two more touchdowns in Philadelphia's 59-28 victory.

So much for everyone who protested his arrival in Philadelphia and threatened to boycott games.

"I thought what he did was disgusting, but the guy paid his dues and deserves a second chance," said Shaun Young, an 18-year Eagles season ticket-holder. "He's playing incredible. I respect people's opinions that don't like him, but isn't this world based on second chances?"

Since signing with the Eagles in August 2009, he's been a model citizen off the field, working with the Humane Society of the United States and speaking to school and community groups about the cruelty of dogfighting.

On the field, Vick has been downright spectacular, even more so than when he was a superstar with the Atlanta Falcons.

"I feel like I'm playing the best football only because of the coaches that I have, the guys I'm playing with. The offensive line is playing great," Vick said. "I have a great group of guys around me. Just as I'm doing a great job, those guys are doing a great job as well."

Fickle fans who were outraged when the Eagles signed him to a two-year deal are now demanding the team give him a new contract. Vick, who filed for bankruptcy protection in July 2008, could be a top free agent on the market next year if Philadelphia let him get that far. Teams who weren't willing to trade a high draft pick for him when the Eagles made him available last off-season just may be lining up to throw money at him.

"The other 31 teams out there need to save their money and vote for Michael Vick or try to get a bid for him because the way he was looking out there, he was looking awesome," Redskins defensive tackle Albert Haynesworth said.

Some would argue that if Redskins quarterback Donovan McNabb can get a 78 million, five-year deal from the Redskins, the 30-year-old Vick certainly should be worth more right now. He's younger and arguably playing better than McNabb did when he led the Eagles to the Super Bowl in 2004. McNabb was traded by the Eagles to Washington in April and when Kevin Kolb was concussed in the season-opener, Vick became the starting quarterback.

"I could have never envisioned this," Vick said. "Signing here, I didn't even think I'd be starting as the quarterback this year. So, all of this is paramount for me, but at the same time it's somewhat surprising."

Indeed, Vick has made the Eagles instant play-offs contenders at 6-3 in what was to be a rebuilding season. They're 4-0 in games that he's started and finished, and are tied with the New York Giants for first place in the NFC East division.

Despite missing three games with a rib injury, he has passed for 1,350 yards and 11 touchdowns and rushed for 341 yards and four touchdowns. He hasn't thrown an interception or lost a fumble and is the current highest-rated passer in the NFL.

Hard to believe that Vick's No 7 jersey, which was being sold at clearance prices last December, is a hot item nowadays.

"He's my favourite player," said Ashley Walton, one of many fans wearing a Vick jersey at a sports bar in South Philadelphia.

"I know what he did was wrong and it's something some people will never forgive him for, but he went to jail and he did his time. People can't judge him for one mistake forever. Everyone makes mistakes."

title: Brady, Favre among best midway through season
content:
It has been a year of rediscovery for many of our midseason award winners.

Start with Randy Moss, who is enjoying a renaissance, leading the league with 12 touchdown receptions, 924 receiving yards and demoralizing, jump-ball catches for the 9-0 New England Patriots after two lost seasons in Oakland.

"He's gone from the ultimate Black Hole of football in Oakland to the ultimate football nirvana with Tom Bradyin New England," CBS analyst Boomer Esiason says. "This is the Randy Moss we remember fondly from Minnesota."

Rejuvenated Brett Favre looks 10 years younger, throwing game-winning touchdowns the last two weeks for the run-challenged, 7-1 Green Bay Packers.

Quarterback Jon Kitna and coach Rod Marinelli have the long-lost Detroit Lions at 6-2, bidding for their first playoff appearance since 1999. The 5-3 Cleveland Browns and 4-4 Buffalo Bills have made unexpected turnarounds.

Following Sunday's mid-foot sprain suffered by Larry Johnson, Priest Holmes, 34, figures to make his first start for the Kansas City Chiefs since suffering a neck injury Oct.30, 2005.

Then, there's three 2006 playoff teams, the 3-5 Chicago Bears, 3-5 Philadelphia Eagles and 1-8 New York Jets, who have lost their mojo.

"Welcome to the ever unpredictable always entertaining NFL," Esiason says.

Consider the leading awards candidates at the season's midpoint:

Coach of the Year: Mike McCarthy.

"Mike has done a great job meshing with Brett and Brett's taken that team on his shoulders," ESPN analyst Floyd Reese says.

Esiason gives the nod to New England's Bill Belichick, but also acknowledges McCarthy.

"I don't care how much talent Bill Belichick has," Esiason says. "I don't care about Spygate or complaints about running up the score. Right now, there isn't a better coach in all of football.

"Second is Mike McCarthy. Nobody expected the Packers to be where they are. Nobody knew how good their defense was. And they don't need a running game. They're just giving the ball to their 38-year-old quarterback."

CBS analyst Charley Casserly cites McCarthy, Marinelli, 6-2 Tennessee coach Jeff Fisher, Cleveland's Romeo Crennel and Buffalo's Dick Jauron. At 7-1, Dallas' Wade Phillips; 7-1 Indianapolis coach Tony Dungy; 6-2 New York Giants coach Tom Coughlin; 6-2 Pittsburgh coach Mike Tomlin and 4-4 Chiefs coach Herm Edwards also merit consideration.

MVP: Tom Brady.

The two-time Super Bowl MVP quarterback has 33 touchdowns, four interceptions, and a league-best 2,686 passing yards.

"His numbers are staggering," Esiason says. "When Peyton Manninghad his 49-touchdown season in 2004, I said, 'We are witnessing the greatest single season a quarterback has ever had.' I'm repeating that line for Tom Brady."

Offensive MVP: Moss and Favre.

Esiason cites Moss's Nerf ball-like, one-handed grabs. Reese makes a strong case for Favre, who leads the league with 10 passes of 40-plus yards and is completing 67% of his passes despite a 32nd-ranked running game.

"He might be doing the most for his team of any player in the league," Reese says. "Brett's playing as well as he's ever played."

Defensive MVP: Bob Sanders.

"When he's healthy, they're a completely different team," Esiason says of the Colts safety. "They have some of the better tackling defensive backs simply because he inspires them with his tackling."

Reese likes Patriots linebacker Mike Vrabel, who has 81/2 sacks and two touchdowns as a red-zone tight end.

"He's making a ton of plays," Reese says. "He's not only preventing points, he's scoring them."

Casserly cites Titans defensive tackle Albert Haynesworth.

Rookie of the Year: Adrian Peterson.

The Minnesota Vikings running back leads the league with 1,036 rushing yards, nine touchdowns and might have been the No.1 pick in April's draft if not for injury concerns, according to Casserly.

"I played with a young Corey Dillon in Cincinnati," Esiason says. "Other people say he reminds them of Eric Dickerson and Jim Brown.

"(Buffalo's) Marshawn Lynch is also playing great."

Defensive Rookie: Patrick Willis.

The San Francisco 49ers middle linebacker has a league-best 83 tackles. "If there's one silver lining for the 49ers, it's Patrick Willis," Esiason says.

Comeback Player: Amani Toomer

"It has to be Amani Toomer, how he's returned from a serious knee injury at 33," Esiason says of the veteran Giants receiver.

Honorable mention goes to the Chiefs' Holmes, who made his way back onto the field after nearly a two-year absence.

title: GRIDIRON: SIMS FACES TOUGH TEST
content:
FORMER Scottish Claymores star Barry Sims faces the biggest challenge of his career on Sunday as he tries to book himself a place in the 2001 Super Bowl.

The Oakland Raiders' offensive lineman, who played for the Claymores in 1999, must protect quarterback Rich Gannon from the attentions of the much-vaunted Baltimore Ravens defence in Sunday's AFC Championship Game.

And the 27-year-old is desperate to book a place in the January 28 gala game in Tampa and said: "To get to the Super Bowl would be a fantastic achievement considering the fact that only two years ago I was playing in Europe for the Claymores."

title: Here's the painful lowdown on Levens: It's time to let him go
content:
Upon returning from a wedding last Saturday night, I logged onto my computer and went to www.tennessean.com.

What I read disturbed me: Eddie George 50-50 for Monday night.

With George as one of my primary point producers, it was decision time with 12 hours before Sunday kickoff. Do I go with George and Ricky Watters; or Watters and Dorsey Levens?

I bit the bullet and went with Levens, a five-year veteran on my fantasy team. To my relief, he came up huge against the Dolphins and found the end zone twice in the first 18 minutes.

Of course, he soon left the field with a "bruised knee" and never returned.

But so what? He did his job for me.

But the injury is a big deal and continues a disturbing trend in Levens' career. The guy just can't stay healthy.

The league I have him in is a keeper league, but it's also a unit league in which we bid on every player. Five years ago, when Levens was an unknown nobody, I had him at 1 unit. Every year if we want to protect a player for another year, we up his unit value 5 units. So Levens went from 1 to 6 to 11 to 16 and now sits at 21. And just to give you an idea of how much some premier backs go for, Eddie George went for 57 (out of a possible 200); Charlie Garner 41; Mike Alstott 45; Terrell Davis 40.

So, as you can see, Levens is basically a steal.

Or is he?

Look at his production over the past few years:

- 1996: 566 yards, 10 total TDs.

- 1997: 1,435 yards, 12 TDs

- 1998: 378 yards, 1 TD

- 1999: 1,034 yards, 10 TDs@*

- 2000: 224 yards, 3 TDs

There's a reason I put an asterisk next to 1999's numbers. Levens scored 4 touchdowns in Green Bay's final game, when nearly every fantasy football league has shut down. So if you take that week out, Levens has just 9 TDs over the past 24 weeks.

I'll have a decision to make before the 2001 season starts: Keep Levens at 21 units for one more year, up him to 26 and possibly keep him for two more, or just drop him.

It's a tough call but right now I'm leaning toward sending him back into the pack and letting somebody overpay for him. My advice to you: find a better alternative.

I've picked up Levens' teammate, Ahman Green, in both of my leagues and he looks to be at least a good short-term solution.

- One little piece of advice for die-hard fantasy football players. If you have a chance to join or start up a unit league, I highly recommend it. These leagues separate the contenders from the pretenders. And think about it: everybody has the exact same chance to get any player in the entire NFL as long as you're willing to pay the price. It's a lot of fun.

Good bets

- The Bears' Jim Miller and Marcus Robinson vs. the Colts. It goes without saying that Indianapolis' Terrific Trio of Marvin Harrison, Peyton Manning and Edgerrin James are good bets this week at Soldier Field. But with a week off and a rejuvenated offense, watch for Miller to throw for over 250 yards and 2 or 3 TDs, while Robinson grabs at least 1 and goes over 100 yards.

- The Giants' Amani Toomer and Ron Dayne vs. Cleveland. From what I've read, Toomer seems to be back and Dayne should find the end zone once against the Browns.

- New Orleans' Ricky Williams vs. San Francisco. He'll bounce back against a weak defense.

- Washington's Stephen Davis vs. Arizona. With a depleted receiving corps, Davis should carry the ball 30-plus times.

- Seattle's Ricky Watters vs. San Diego. Watters has been a horse all year.

- The Jets' Curtis Martin vs. Denver. Martin's been quiet. He'll kick up some dust against the Broncos.

- Oakland's Rich Gannon vs. Kansas City … Could be a shootout.

- Minnesota's Randy Moss vs. Green Bay. Moss is always a no- brainer start, but he's been unreal on Monday nights.

Bad bets

- San Francisco's Jeff Garcia and Terrell Owens vs. New Orleans. Could be a bit of a stretch, but these guys have got to cool down eventually. Saints defense may be up to the task.

- Cincinnati's Corey Dillon vs. Baltimore. Dillon's due to hit a wall.

- New England's Drew Bledsoe and Terry Glenn vs. Buffalo. The Bills are still fighting for the playoffs.

Bledsoe and Glenn will pay the price.

- Detroit's James Stewart and Charlie Batch vs. Miami. With no passing game, watch for the Dolphins to put eight men up front. When the Lions are forced to pass, Batch could get knocked out of this game.

- Atlanta's Jamal Anderson vs. Tampa Bay. The Falcons are going nowhere and the Bucs are finding their way again.

- Cleveland's Travis Prentice vs. the Giants. With a rough schedule looming, don't expect much more from Prentice the rest of the year.

John Dietz's column appears every Friday in SportsWeekend. You can e-mail him at jdietz@@dailyherald.com.

title: THE LAPS UPON WHICH THEY LEAP 15 YEARS OF MEMORIES FROM THE FRONT ROW AT LAMBEAU
content:
Fifteen years ago this week, Leroy Butler took one small step in the evolution of touchdown celebrations and one giant leap in the value of front row end zone seats.

It was on Dec. 26, 1993, that Green Bay Packers legendary defensive end Reggie White scooped up a fumble by Los Angeles Raiders running back Randy Jordan and lateraled the ball to Butler, who ran the final 25 yards to the end zone to give the Packers a three-touchdown lead early in the fourth quarter.

Then, on a sudden impulse, Butler pointed to the stands and took off toward the wall behind the end zone. Blame it on the numbness caused by the third-coldest game day ever at Lambeau Field, with the temperature at zero and the wind chill at minus-22. Or the jubilation of putting the nail in the coffin of the shivering Californians for a victory that clinched Green Bay's first playoff berth in 11 seasons, paving the way for a new era of greatness.

Butler described the football-changing moment in his autobiography, "The Leroy Butler Story: From Wheelchair to the Lambeau Leap": "Because I had pointed, the fans knew what they had to do," wrote Butler, the Packers' former All-Pro safety. "I go up into the green padding ... and when I'm halfway up, a guy starts pulling me up the rest of the way. Everyone right behind him grabs on. Everyone is screaming and yelling. Some are complimenting me with 'Awesome' or 'Good job.' It only lasts 2 or 3 seconds, and I'm back down.

"It's an incredible feeling as I run back to the bench. Dorsey Levens and a lot of other teammates keep telling me how cool that moment was. It took off from there. Ever since, every time we make a big play, there's the Lambeau Leap."

And every time there's a Lambeau Leap, there's a giddy group of fans with a special memory and a story to tell.It's a tradition that seems particularly fitting in Green Bay, where as the collective owners of the franchise, Packers fans have a relationship with the players unique in professional sports.

Terry and Marlene Garrits of Sun Prairie have been sitting for 22 years in two of the six seats that have been in Marlene's family ever since her parents bought them when the stadium opened in 1957. While their seats in the north end zone may not have been the most coveted for those first 36 years, that is no longer the case.

"A lot of people say the 50-yard line is the place to be," Terry said. "I disagree. I wouldn't swap these seats for first row on the 50. I wouldn't trade them for any seat in the house."

Unlike many of the front row ticket holders, who can recount each and every player that has leaped into their midst, the Garritses have lost count of visitors who have hopped into their stretch of seats. Their best estimate is between eight and 10.

"This year we've had two - Greg Jennings and Jason Hunter," Terry said.

The Jennings leap was particularly memorable to Marlene.

"There are certain players we don't want to give back when they jump up here," she said. "Greg Jennings was really good. In the past, when Dorsey Levens jumped up here, I didn't want to give him back, either."

Hal and Rainy Knutson got their front row seats after spending 32 years on the waiting list. "We signed up in 1965 and we got these right after the Super Bowl year," Hal said, referencing Green Bay's 12th NFL title, achieved in 1997. "I guess these didn't used to be such good seats, but now with the Leap that's all changed."

Rainy's most memorable Leap experience came courtesy of wide receiver Donald Driver.

"Donald Driver jumped right here and I had ahold of his leg," she recalled. "Then the next day there was a picture of us on the front page of the paper. Donald was in Madison that Monday signing autographs at the Capitol, so I took the paper there and had him autograph it. He said, 'I wondered who was hanging onto my leg.' That was really fun."

Of course, there are some risks to the Lambeau Leap.

"Our son came to a game a couple of years ago with his girlfriend," Rainy said. "Ahman Green jumped up here and his helmet hit her in the lip. She had to go to work the next day with a fat lip."

"They call it the Lambeau Lip," added Hal.

Pat and Ron Steers of Green Bay encountered a different kind of physical risk in the early years of the Leap. William Henderson leaped into the stands right in front of them and the fans in the next several rows poured down to toast their favorite fullback.

"When Henderson made his leap here, he said he came in smelling like an athlete and went out smelling like a brewery," Ron said.

"In those days people would just pile on," he said. "As soon as there was a touchdown, they'd just pile on and it was like dominoes - boom, boom, boom. But after that episode with Henderson (the ushers) started handing out sheets that warned people that they could be ejected if they piled on. They did a good job of controlling it. Now people beyond the second row usually just sit and watch."

The Steerses were in Lambeau when Butler made his first Leap. After that, they eagerly awaited their first leaper, who turned out to be return man Desmond Howard.

"He wasn't going to jump," Ron recalled, "but then Eugene Robinson pushed him and he jumped up. But he didn't quite make it all the way, so I grabbed him by the shoulder pads to help him up. He came up a little faster than I thought he would and the crossbar on his helmet hit me right in the nose. It knocked me back, but I just said, 'I love you man.' It was great."

The front row residents say there's no real way to entice a player to jump in their vicinity. Of course, you need a touchdown to be scored on your end of the field, but it's almost as important that the score comes on the right side of the goalpost.

For Packers players, the Leap remains a coveted reward for a touchdown. It was an experience that quarterback Aaron Rodgers yearned for while watching Brett Favre from the sidelines since being drafted in 2005. His wait ended in this year's season opener against the Minnesota Vikings, when he scored on a one-yard quarterback sneak.

"I've been dreaming about that for four (seasons), to be honest," Rodgers said after the game. "I was hoping my first Leap would be a little more flashy - a 10-yard, 15-yard run or something - but at that point in the game I just said, 'What the heck, I'm going to go for it.'"

As the father of the Lambeau Leap, Butler understands and appreciates that emotion.

As he wrote in his book, "Scoring a touchdown is exciting, but the anticipation of all those fans ready to thank you for what you have done gives you chills like nothing else."

dpunzel@madison.com

title: VIKINGS INSIDER Vikings have little choice with Jackson The second-year quarterback is overmatched, but benching him leaves the team with issues that would drag into the 2008 season.
content:
This is the worst-case scenario. It's Armageddon. It's choosing between Door A and Door B, knowing one leads to arsenic and the other to a 500-foot cliff. You want to stand still, dig in and hope a better choice emerges - even as hurricane-force winds force you toward a decision.

Six games into their Tarvaris Jackson experiment, the Vikings are facing the crossroad of their nightmares. Jackson's fractured finger notwithstanding, do they pull the plug and try saving their season with backup Kelly Holcomb? Or do they continue to give Jackson the experience he needs to develop?

Either way, it's a lose-lose situation. Turn to Holcomb and Jackson's evaluation period flows undesirably into 2008. Stay with Jackson and risk a disastrous season if he does not improve.

One point is clear: Jackson, less than two years removed from Division I-AA Alabama State, was not ready to take over an offense that had few proven receivers to ease his transition. At his best, he has been a below-average NFL quarterback. At worst, Jackson has appeared to be in completely over his head.

He is completing 45.9 percent of his passes and has a 48.7 passer rating. The Vikings are 2-2 in his starts, but his most recent performance in a 24-14 loss to Dallas suggests that routine competency is a long way away.

Even if it was just offseason dreaming, the Vikings believed they would avoid this circumstance.

They bet Jackson could perform well enough to not detract from a potentially dominant running game, along with a defense that makes big plays and generally keeps opponents within striking distance.

Instead, his performance has followed an uglier - but not altogether unpredictable - path that has jeopardized the Vikings' season before Halloween.

Monday, coach Brad Childress acknowledged the line he is straddling: Getting Jackson the playing time he needs while still trying to win games. So far, those twin goals appear mutually exclusive.

So perhaps as early as today, Childress must decide whether to reinforce his commitment to Jackson while tacitly subordinating his team's competitiveness.

The finger injury provides a short-term excuse. Doctors have told Jackson he can play with the condition, but it would be easy to sit him for a week or two under the guise of letting the finger heal. Eventually, however, Childress will have to make the choice.

All of which leads to a central question: Will the Vikings know whether Jackson is their long-term answer by the end of this season? If not, will they risk 2008 to find out, or will they pursue another option during the offseason? (Their opponent this week, Philadelphia's Donovan McNabb, could be available.)

Childress previewed his thought process Monday, making clear he wants Jackson to play as often as possible in 2007.

"If you are going to develop somebody," he said, "the word kind of speaks for itself. We are currently in that process. I believe we'll know a lot more about Tarvaris at the end of this year than we do right now."

Four starts is not enough time to evaluate a young quarterback, but the Vikings never gave themselves an avenue for impatience. In truth, they locked themselves into this position over the winter, when their faith in Jackson precluded pursuit of a veteran starter.

Unless they already have given up on Jackson internally - an assertion made this week by the website profootballtalk.com - the Vikings have no choice but to stay with him this season in hope they can make a clear decision for the position in 2008.

That tack could scuttle their season, but that is the risk they took last offseason.

Monday, Childress was asked to gauge the status of Jackson's development, and whether it is infringing on the Vikings' competitiveness.

"[He is] five games into his career," Childress said. (Technically, Jackson has six NFL starts and has played in eight games.)

"That's the easiest thing I would say to you," Childress added. "If you think about five games into Peyton Manning's career, and where he was at from a touchdown to interception ratio, if you were doing the logic, you would say, `Whoa, not very good.' It's a curve."

For an equal match, consider the first four starts of Manning's career. As a rookie in 1998, Manning had a 52.1 passer rating after four games. He had thrown three touchdowns and 11 interceptions. (Tossing away his two rookie starts last season, Jackson has two touchdowns and five interceptions in four 2007 starts.)

In the final 12 games of 1998, Manning threw 23 touchdowns and 17 interceptions. The Colts finished 3-13, but they were 13-3 the following season as Manning earned his first Pro Bowl berth.

Yes, Manning deserved the benefit of the doubt after four starts. There are no good choices, but Jackson probably gets the same treatment - no matter what the cost.

Kevin Seifert - kseifert@startribune.com

title: Pro Bowl snubs Eagles' MVP candidate McNabb
content:
NEW YORK -- Donovan McNabb may be one of the leading candidates for the NFL's MVP award. But the fans, players and coaches who vote for the Pro Bowl consider him only the fourth-best quarterback in the NFC.

The Philadelphia QB, who has been a one-man offense for a team that is 10-5 and has clinched a playoff berth after going 8-24 the past two seasons, was the most conspicuous absentee in the Pro Bowl voting, announced Thursday.

He finished as the first alternate behind Minnesota's Daunte Culpepper San Francisco's Jeff Garcia and St. Louis' Kurt Warner. Warner missed 5 1/2 games with an injury and threw four interceptions in his first game back, while Garcia, who's had a breakout year, plays for a team that is 5-9.

"I think Donovan is the best quarterback in the league," said Chad Lewis, the Eagles' little-known tight end from BYU, who made the NFC team. "There's nobody I'd rather have playing. The other quarterbacks had big numbers so I guess that held Donovan back, but there's no doubt in my mind Donovan deserves to go."

Warner, the league and Super Bowl MVP last season, didn't seem to have strong feelings.

"It was one of those things where I felt I played well when I played," he said. "But if I didn't make it, I could understand that as well."

Lewis wasn't the only former Utah college player to be voted in. Corey Dillon of the Cincinnati Bengals made the AFC team as a running back. Dillon is a former Dixie College star.

McNabb's absence was one of several quirks in the Pro Bowl voting, which annually has strange selections and absentees.

This time:

Mike Alstott, among an NFC-high eight Tampa Bay Buccaneers selected (Tennessee led the AFC with eight), was chosen as the fullback, even though he plays running back. Alstott also was benched after a series of fumbles and missed the last three games with a knee injury.

Carolina's Wesley Walls was voted the first alternate at tight end in the NFC. He has missed most of the second half of the season after undergoing major knee surgery.

Brian Griese of Denver was selected as one of the AFC quarterbacks, along with Oakland's Rich Gannon, who will start, and Indianapolis' Peyton Manning. Griese, who was having an outstanding year, has been out since Nov. 13 with a shoulder separation and the Broncos have won four straight with Gus Frerotte at quarterback.

There was one milestone: 39-year-old guard Bruce Matthews of Tennessee made it for the 13th straight time, tying Reggie White for the most appearances.

Another veteran, 35-year-old defensive end Trace Armstrong of Miami, made it for the first time in his 12th NFL season. Armstrong, who leads the NFL with 16 1/2 sacks, will start for the AFC even though he's been a third-down specialist for several years.

"This game thrills you every Sunday," said Armstrong, who is president of the NFL Players Association. "This is the great addiction. And then to get something like this is obviously a tremendous feeling."

But there was a changing of the guard in other places.

Second-year-man Champ Bailey made the NFC team at cornerback ahead of teammates Deion Sanders and Darrell Green, each of whom has made it seven times. Dallas' Emmitt Smith, who last week became the third running back ever go rush for more than 15,000 yards, also missed out to Minnesota's Robert Smith, St. Louis' Marshall Faulk, and San Francisco's Charlie Garner.

Five teams do not have players in the game, to be played Feb. 4 in Honolulu: Atlanta and Chicago in the NFC; Cleveland, Seattle and New England in the AFC.

title: Bengals RB Benson surprisingly productive
content:
"Workhorse" and "Cedric Benson" would hardly have been associated together before this season.

After all, this is Cedric Benson — the same guy who didn't surpass 196 carries or 674 yards in three seasons after being a first-round draft pick. The same guy who was jettisoned by a Bears team that went to a Super Bowl with Thomas Jones as the featured back. The same guy who only once in four years played in more than 12 games.

It's the same guy who leads the NFL in carries and rushing yards while sporting a tidy 4.4 yards-per-carry average that would mark the best of his career.

Benson has been one of the biggest beneficiaries of his team's fortunes this season. The Cincinnati Bengals have consciously slowed down the clock and kept games close in compiling their 4-1 record.

It has worked wonders for a team known for underachieving in recent seasons and that was expected to be mediocre this season. And for the Bengals to continue with their surprising ways, they will have to hope both that quarterback Carson Palmerplays like a Pro Bowler in the first three quarters and Benson can keep up his pace.

There is at least one positive sign in Benson's favor: Unlike running backs such as Chris Johnson, Steven Jackson, Maurice Jones-Drew and Julius Jones, Benson has gained his yards through consistency rather than the big run. Benson's longest run has been only 28 yards, but he is still tied for second in the NFL with four runs of 20 or more yards.

As long as the Bengals' offensive line keeps playing adequately, it's only a matter of time before Benson breaks off a big run or two. And if he can stay healthy, those big runs may be enough to make him fantasy football's most valuable pickup.

Benson with the best

Bengals RB Cedric Benson is among the league's top rushers through five weeks:

Name, team: Yds./TD

Benson, Cin: 487/3

Adrian Peterson, Min: 481/7

Chris Johnson, Ten: 468/2

Steven Jackson, Stl: 451/0

Ronnie Brown, Mia: 443/6

Henne not making difference for WRs

The Dolphins are surging, and Chad Henne may turn out to be the best quarterback the team has had since Dan Marino.

So far, it isn't meaning a thing for Dolphins receivers.

In two games with Henne behind center, 10 Dolphins have caught passes and none has gained more than 81 yards receiving. Tight end Anthony Fasano leads the team with 10 targets in the past two games, followed by Davonne Bess and Greg Camarillo (eight each).

It fits perfectly with the Dolphins' spread-it-around philosophy that does not rely on a star player — a philosophy all the more appropriate because they have no star player on which to rely. The Wildcat offense is more about deception and keeping defenses honest.

Having Henne could eventually be a boon to Bess, Camarillo or Ted Ginn Jr. But when — and who will benefit — is anyone's guess.

HOT PICKUPS

Chad Henne: Mia/QB

Nate Burleson: Sea/WR

FREEZING COLD

Justin Gage: WR/Ten

WIDEOUTS TO WATCH

Terrell Owens, Bills — You usually wouldn't need to be told to play Owens, but his 12 catches in five games gives owners pause. Expect him to be motivated against Braylon Edwards and the Jets, and for the Bills to try a different approach.

Bernard Berrian, Min — The usually stout Ravens will have to respect Brett Favre and the long pass, because Berrian will burn them if they don't. He had six games with catches of more than 40 yards last season.

TIGHT WITH TARGETS

Dallas Clark has been the most dominant tight end in football this season, but he has company among the most popular tight ends with their quarterbacks. A pair of lesser stars are among the most-targeted tight ends through five weeks:

title: Whirlwind weekend yields good, bad, ugly
content:
No rowdy sports bar setting for these important NFL divisional playoffs. Time to use the home remote to check whether the telecasters are up to the level of the players.

Saturday

Pittsburgh at Tennessee, CBS.

Atlanta at Philadelphia, Fox.

3:50 p.m. (all times Eastern) -- CBS' NFL Todayhost Jim Nantz follows a tribute by NBC's Bob Costas to former CBS and NBC information man and Boston Globecolumnist Will McDonough, who died at 67 late Thursday. McDonough, a tough talker, spawned the use of the information man on sports TV. He started in 1985 at NFL Todaybefore moving to NBC. Fox's Dick Stockton later added his own salute.

4:20 -- Deion Sanders and Boomer Esiason pick eventual winner Tennessee.

4:40 -- Dan Dierdorf, a premier analyst who combines tough talk with cleverness, says Titans running back Eddie George is at his "healthiest since the Clinton administration" and that oft-injured Tennessee quarterback Steve McNair "should have been playing for Switzerland."

Maybe ABC-bound Jimmy Kimmel of Fox should call Dierdorf for lines.

6:14 -- Even the best can make mistakes. Announcer Dick Enberg is tardy in noticing that George loses a fumble as he's injured.

6:57 -- Referee Ron Blum earns his 15 minutes of fame with his call that he'll check to see if a play can be reviewed. Dierdorf praises Blum's candor, but the panel on ESPN's Sports Reportersblasts the situation Sunday and ESPN's Chris Mortensen says it will rekindle the debate over going to full-time officials.

7:43 -- With Steelers-Titans headed to overtime on CBS, the Fox NFL Sundaypregame show has a meager audience of 2.2% of U.S. homes in overnight ratings. That doesn't stop Howie Long from chiding Kimmel for a subpar segment.

8:00 -- Enberg calls a run to the end zone by a Titans player a "touchdown," but Dierdorf points out that officials had signaled the ball was down by contact. The Steelers retain possession.

8:25 -- CBS downplays whether Titans kicker Joe Nedney "acted" into getting a running-into-the-kicker penalty that gives him a new chance to boot the game-winner. The overtime game posts a 20.3 overnight rating, highest for a Saturday AFC game in six years and 8% better than the early Eagles-Bears game on Fox last year.

Fox's 17.2 overnight for Falcons-Eagles fell 7% from CBS' Raiders-Patriots prime-time "Snow Bowl" last year.

8:32 -- In his third decade in the booth, Fox's Stockton smartly explains before the official's ruling that the Eagles' Duce Staley can't be charged with a safety because of his forward progress.

8:45 -- As an interception return by Bobby Taylor gives the Eagles a 7-0 lead, underrated analyst Daryl Johnston notes that Michael Vick wrongly threw off his back foot.

10:05 -- During halftime, Fox shows Steelers coach Bill Cowher calling the key Pittsburgh running-into-the-kicker penalty "ludicrous" and Nedney joking, "When I'm done, I might try acting."

11:15 -- James Thrash, whom CBS reporter Jerry Glanville said would be the difference against the Falcons, catches a scoring pass to give the Eagles a commanding 20-6 lead.

Sunday

San Francisco at Tampa Bay, Fox.

New York Jets at Oakland, CBS.

12:40 p.m. -- Terry Bradshaw says the 49ers should give coach Steve Mariucci a new contract. Jimmy Johnson correctly forecasts that quarterback Brad Johnson will lead an easy Tampa Bay victory.

1:20 -- Cris Collinsworth suggests the Bucs go to power fullback Mike Alstott, who bulls over for a touchdown five minutes later.

1:45 -- When reporter Pam Oliver says injured 49ers defensive back Ahmed Plummer "officially is listed as doubtful," play-by-play man Joe Buck says, "I'll say. He has no pads or jersey on."

2:32 -- As the 49ers play out the clock at halftime while trailing 28-6, Buck wonders, "Two timeouts left and you're packing it in?"

2:45 -- After Oliver says Mariucci "just wanted to get off the field," a more critical Troy Aikman says, "That sends a message you lack confidence." Collinsworth blurts, "There's no justification for it."

4:11 -- As the 49ers finally lose 31-6 on Fox, Mariucci tells Oliver he plans "on being back in San Francisco," an outcome that Collinsworth and Aikman have questioned.

5:05 -- Several minutes before Oakland scores from inside the 1-yard line, ace CBS analyst Phil Simms points out that opposing coaches say the Raiders have the best short-yardage offense.

5:38 -- When partner Greg Gumbel says the Raiders are "tired of hearing about the Jets," the normally smooth-talking Simms utters the word "Christ" before continuing on to make his point.

6:32 -- With a 10-10 halftime tie, Simms says the Raiders' Rich Gannon needs to return to the passing game.

7:15 -- The Raiders grab a 24-10 lead on two Gannon TD passes.

8:00 -- As the Raiders complete a 30-10 victory, Simms notes they are an effective big team in an age of streamlined players.

Weekend highs and lows

Strongest comment: ESPN Sports Reporters host John Saunders -- "I firmly believe the (NFL) overtime rule is ridiculous. . . . Go to sudden death after each team has had a chance with the ball."

Top tip: On Sunday's PGA Tour show on USA, Tim Rosaforte said Phil Mickelson, not a training advocate, has worked with a martial arts instructor.

Best feature: Armen Keteyian's during Simms' All-Iron Team Show on Saturday. He profiled Chiefs lineman Eric Hicks' 4-year-old daughter, who has recovered from a heart problem at birth.

title: AROUND THE AFC
content:
BENGALS: Sunday's win was Cincinnati's second in 11 games against Baltimore. The team was the last to recover a fumble this season. Corey Dillon, who came back from a strained groin, was slow making cuts and had only 39 yards on 18 carries. Carson Palmer, Heisman Trophy winner and No. 1 pick in April's draft, moved to No. 2 quarterback behind Jon Kitna.Compiled from Wire Reports

BILLS: Drew Bledsoe passed former Bills great Jim Kelly for 12th on the league's career list with 35,611 passing yards. Former linebacker Darryl Talley was honored as the 20th member of Buffalo's Wall of Fame. A second-round 1983 draft pick, Talley never missed a game during his 12-year career with Buffalo, playing in 188 contests and four Super Bowls from 1983 to '94.

BROWNS: During pregame ceremonies, Joe DeLamielleure, Hanford Dixon, Bob Gain and Dick Schafrath were inducted into the team's Legends Club. Referee Jeff Triplette worked his first game in Cleveland since 1999, when he threw a penalty flag and accidentally hit former Browns offensive tackle Orlando Brown in the eye. Kevin Johnson caught a pass in his 71st consecutive game.

CHARGERS: San Diego won for the first time since beating Denver in overtime on Dec. 1. LaDainian Tomlinson had his third career 200-yard game.

DOLPHINS: Left tackle Mark Dixon, who has missed all season with an ankle injury, targeted a return for Monday at San Diego. Miami missed a chance to start 5-1 for the fourth time in five years.

JETS: Curtis Martin had 88 yards rushing, giving him 10,723 for his career and moving him into 13th on the league's all-time list. He passed Ricky Watters, who had 10,643. Defensive end Shaun Ellis had a sack in his ninth consecutive game, a team record.

PATRIOTS: Before his 82-yard overtime winner, Troy Brown's longest reception in 11 seasons went for 67 yards. Running back Kevin Faulk, starting for Antowain Smith, had a combined 69 yards rushing and receiving.

RAVENS: Running back Jamal Lewis gained 101 yards, his fifth straight triple-digit rushing game. Travis Taylor had four catches for 138 yards and two touchdowns.

TEXANS: Houston's 169 yards rushing was a team record, surpassing the high of 141 last season against Buffalo.

TITANS: Robert Holcombe led the team with 53 yards rushing on 16 carries. Eddie George finished with 51 yards.
 
- TIMES WIRES

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Cheers

If Tony Gonzalez hears "Me and My Shadow," playing on his car radio, excuse him for switching to another station.

Kansas City's Pro Bowl TE was limited to one catch for six yards in Sunday's 17-13 loss at San Francisco as 49ers LB Julian Peterson embraced a challenge hurled earlier in the week by defensive coordinator Jim Mora.

"Coach Mora gave me an assignment to take Gonzalez on, and I wanted to make sure I did just that," said Peterson, a first-round pick out of Michigan State in the 2000 draft.

Peterson showed his agility on Kansas City's final drive, running stride for stride and batting away a fourth-down pass intended for Gonzalez deep in 49ers territory. Frustrated by Peterson's tight coverage, Gonzalez complained he was held all day.

"Julian is a special talent," 49ers LB Jeff Ulbrich said of his 6-foot-3, 235-pound teammate. "He runs like a cornerback and has the size of a defensive end."

Give it up for the maligned Colts offensive line, which kept the blitzing Eagles off QB Peyton Manning and opened holes for sixth-string RB James Mungro in a 35-13 shocker at Philadelphia.

Manning wasn't sacked and Mungro ran for 101 of his 114 yards in a dominating opening half as Indianapolis snapped a three-game losing streak.

"We knew we could play this well, that's what was so frustrating," RT Adam Meadows said. "We knew we could run the ball and now the evidence is in. Now we've got to do it again."

Armed with a credible ground game, Manning displayed his exquisite play-action skills, completing 18-of-23 passes for 319 yards and three TDs.

"I'm hyped about this team and what we're doing," Colts LT Tarik Glenn said. "We hadn't had this much fun in I don't remember."

Jeers

The Bears and Panthers are finding creative ways to lose.

At Champaign, Ill., the Patriots erased a 27-6 third-quarter deficit and rallied past Chicago 33-30, handing the 2-7 Bears their seventh consecutive setback.

The Pats somehow reeled off nine plays in a span of 82 seconds following their final timeout, helped by two play reviews and a frantic timeout by Chicago as New England QB Tom Brady was set to spike the ball.

"At that point, there was a lot of stuff happening," Bears LB Rosevelt Colvin said. "We couldn't stop them, we couldn't keep them out of the end zone."

Carolina knows the feeling.

Ahead 24-20, the Panthers allowed New Orleans to drive 95 yards, capped by a 7-yard TD catch by rookie Donte Stallworth with 31 seconds remaining.

It marked the fifth time this season the 3-6 Panthers have lost a lead in the final five minutes.

"The last two minutes is just killing us," S Mike Minter said. "And it's different times and different ways that we lose. I hope there aren't any more ways out there to lose."

Baltimore rookie S Ed Reed is all over the highlight films, and he's comfortable with his current infamy.

The former University of Miami standout was stripped of the ball while holding his right arm up in celebration at the Bengals 8-yard line during an interception return.

"I'm just going to keep playing football and not make the same mistake twice," said Reed, who added a fourth-quarter interception against Jon Kitna in Baltimore's 38-27 triumph. "I was thinking about it right after the play until the next ball was snapped. I let it go."

Player Of The Week

Hall of Famers John Stallworth and Lynn Swann never did it.

Plaxico Burress caught nine passes for a club-record 253 yards Sunday, but Pittsburgh's 6-foot-5 WR didn't feel like celebrating after a 34-34 tie against the Falcons.

Atlanta rallied from a 34-17 deficit in the fourth quarter to force overtime and the game ended with Burress inches short of the goal-line after hauling in a 50-yard desperation pass from Tommy Maddox.

"You play this game to win," said Burress, who caught TD passes of 33 and 62 yards. "You work hard during the week and you play hard on Sunday to get a win. We didn't get a win, so we kind of feel we didn't take a step forward."

Former Buc Update

No NFL player has picked off more passes since 1999 than ex-Buc Donnie Abraham, who could easily have added to his four-year total of 26 during Sunday night's matchup against Miami. Abraham dropped two first-quarter passes from Ray Lucas but his Jets pulled out a 13-10 triumph.

On Miami's first series, Abraham couldn't hold on to an errant pass intended for Chris Chambers. Abraham had another opportunity on a high, hard pass that deflected off the hands of TE Randy McMichael.

In the second quarter, an apparent Abraham interception was nullified by a hands-to-the-face penalty called against DT Larry Webster.

Quote Of The Week

"It's like this game never happened." - Falcons CB Ray Buchanan on the 34-34 tie at Pittsburgh.

title: Cowboys shake NFC East
content:
PHILADELPHIA -- It's not "same old, same old" in the NFC East.

The Dallas Cowboys made that clear with stunning swiftness and force, scoring two late touchdowns 21 seconds apart in shocking the Philadelphia Eagles 21-20 on Monday night.

"They've been bullies for four years in this division," Dallas wide receiver Keyshawn Johnson said of the Eagles. "Now things are starting to shift toward the (New York) Giants, (Washington) Redskins and Cowboys. It's a pretty good thing for us.

"For us, we're at the point where we have to worry about ourselves and take care of Dallas Cowboys business and beat the teams we're supposed to beat. At 6-3, we've got to be smiling."

They've got to be smiling broadly. Their latest success, which tied them with the Giants for the division lead, was gift-wrapped by the defending NFC champions.

After stifling Dallas throughout the evening, the Eagles somehow allowed them to move 72 yards in four plays in just 40 seconds, culminating in Drew Bledsoe's 20-yard scoring pass to Terry Glenn to narrow the deficit to 20-14 with 3:04 remaining.

Then, two plays later, on second-and-7 at the Philadelphia 38, the unthinkable happened.

Donovan McNabb dropped back to pass and did not see safety Roy Williams poised to step in front of rookie Reggie Brown.

Williams had crept toward the line of scrimmage to fake a blitz, then dropped back in coverage.

"I really got a jump on it because Donovan was staring me down," Williams said. "I really didn't think he'd throw the ball. He threw the ball, and my eyes lit up."

Williams' 46-yard interception return kept Philadelphia (4-5) in last place in the division it has ruled for each of the last four years, well behind Dallas, New York and Washington (5-4).

Philadelphia dropped to 0-2 since wide receiver Terrell Owens was removed from the team.

Anyone who doubted the depth of the Eagles' woes needed only to look at McNabb as he slowly stepped to the microphone for his postgame interview. He has attempted to play through a sports hernia and other assorted ailments.

McNabb winced with every move. He needed to be helped down from the interview platform.

McNabb repeatedly refused to answer questions about Owens but did everything he could to assert himself.

"I'm the leader of this team," he said. "I'm the guy who is going to take us where we want to go."

title: Allen grins, but can he bear it? The Vikings defensive end joked about his injured right shoulder, but a grade-3 sprain is serious stuff.
content:
At least Jared Allen's sense of humor remains intact. The Pro Bowl defensive end, asked Wednesday about what type of treatment he is getting on his injured right shoulder, had an answer at the ready.

"Well, they tried to amputate it earlier," he said. "That was tough. I did some electric shock therapy. I don't know what that had to do with my shoulder. They recommended heroin, but I said that was against all league policies, so I stayed well away from that."

Unfortunately for the Vikings, the extent of Allen's injury is no joking matter, and Allen certainly wasn't laughing when asked about comments made by Houston coach Gary Kubiak this week.

Allen said he suffered a third-degree sprain of the AC joint in last Sunday's 28-21 victory over Houston. A third-degree sprain, or separation, is the most serious and often indicates a torn ligament. If this is the case, Allen not only would almost certainly miss Sunday's game against Green Bay, but he could be out for two to four weeks.

Allen did not do any work in Wednesday's practice and said that internally the Vikings have him listed as not being available for Sunday.

"I'm just doing what (the medical staff) tells me to do," said Allen, who is tied with defensive tackle Kevin Williams for the team lead with seven sacks.

"They tell me to get in here and get some treatment on it. It's still early in the week, so we'll see how it plays out later on."

Coach Brad Childress declined to confirm the grade-3 sprain but said the team has talked about whether Allen would be allowed to play if he does not practice all week.

"I think we'll go through the week and ascertain that," Childress said. "We'll see what we're asking him to do in the game plan. You always like, as a coach, to see somebody step through things and keep up with the conditioning element of it. Those things are hard to do with where he is right now."

Allen was injured in the first half when an offensive lineman from the Texans fell on him. Allen needed a pain-killing injection at halftime but made it clear coming out for good was never an option.

"I always tell my mom and everybody -- they always get worried when I get dinged up on the field -- I said, `If you see me laying out there and someone has to come out and get me, then you know I'm hurt,' " Allen said. "But if I can walk off on my own, I'll be playing and I'll be fine."

If Allen can't play Sunday, Brian Robison is the top backup at right end. Keep in mind there might be some gamesmanship at work in all of this. The Vikings wouldn't mind keeping the Packers guessing until Sunday.

"I think (Allen will) play, if you look at his history," Green Bay coach Mike McCarthy said. "I think he is an old-school-type pro, and we are preparing for him to play. There is a reason why they paid him that kind of money. He is an impact player."

Kubiak, for one, didn't have many positive things to say about Allen on his weekly radio show Monday in Houston. He was critical of a few hits by the Vikings, and Allen in particular, because he felt they were below the knees of quarterback Matt Schaub. Schaub suffered a sprained left medial collateral ligament in the second quarter and could miss a month.

"I just heard about (Kubiak's comments) today," Allen said. "If I hadn't read the paper, I wouldn't know who the hell he is. People can say what they want, I'm not a dirty player. I've been in this league a long time; my reputation speaks for itself. I don't ever intend to hurt people. I never do anything intentionally to hurt people. But if a coach wants to spout off that I'm a dirty player because they lost the game, well, whatever."

The play in question occurred when Schaub took a high hit from Williams and a low hit from Allen after delivering a 17-yard pass to tight end Owen Daniels.

"I was coming back and I slipped and he was still in front of me," Allen said. "Obviously I didn't hit him late because the refs were right there. They didn't call nothing."

Said Childress: "When I looked at (the hit) on Monday morning, I did not see it as illegal. ... I thought it was a good throw and a hustle play."

Allen said he had a laugh with Schaub after the game. "He was like, `You got my knee,' and I said, `Well, you guys got me back, you got my shoulder.'"

VIKINGS VS. GREEN BAY

Noon Sunday - Metrodome - TV: Ch. 9 (1130-AM, 100.3-FM)

title: Cover-2
content:
Reporter has NFL's full attention The next thing you know, Ines Sainz will join the Tea Party or disrespect Taylor Swift or go to rehab with Amy Winehouse or sing a duet with Lady Gaga.

And every step she takes, her Mexican television network TV Azteca makes the National Football League look like fools again. The latest faux pas comes courtesy of Redskins running back Clinton Portis, who addressed the NFL's investigation of the New York Jets' treatment of Sainz by saying that both sides have wandering eyes when the sexes are mixed in locker rooms.

"I think you put women reporters in the locker room in position to see guys walking around naked, and you sit in the locker room with 53 guys, and all of the sudden you see a nice woman in the locker room. I think men are going to tend to turn and look and want to say something to that woman," Portis said in his weekly appearance on Washington's 106.7 The Fan.

As expected, the league swiftly condemned his comments as inappropriate and offensive, and Portis later issued an apology through the Redskins. The Jets are being investigated by the NFL after a few players made catcalls directed at Sainz on Saturday while she was waiting to interview quarterback Mark Sanchez. (Profanity and degrading remarks are OK on Hard Knocks because they are on HBO, a cable network.)

This isn't the first time Sainz's actions have caught the eye of the NFL. At the 2007 Super Bowl, an apparently camera-shy Terrell Owens hid behind a curtain at a media day news conference, but upon seeing Sainz, he waved her over and chatted up the former Miss Spain. No sit-ups ensued. The following year, another TV Azteca reporter, Ines Gomez-Mont, showed up on media day wearing a wedding dress and asked Tom Bradyto marry her. He declined and eventually accepted a better offer.

I Remember Mama Portis, you recall, is no stranger in these parts, though no stranger incident may have happened at the Linc than when his mother punched a female Eagles fan after an altercation in January 2006.

Rhonnel Hearn was sitting in the end zone with Portis' stepfather and some friends when an Eagles fan apparently tossed a beer on the group. Portis' mother responded by punching the woman in the face. The Eagles won the game and the fan sued Hearn.

Are you ready for some ratings? The National Football League had the top five shows in the latest Nielsen ratings for the week of Sept. 6-12. The sixth-rated show, The O.T. was not about Donovan McNabb (he knows the rules now) and wasn't a transposed typo. The seventh- and eighth-ranked shows, America's Got Talent, were not about the Dallas Cowboys. The 11th-ranked show was 60 Minutes, which no doubt aired after the AFC game's 60 minutes were up. 1. NFL Football: Minnesota at New Orleans, NBC, 27.49 million.

2. NFL Football: Dallas at Washington, NBC, 25.26 million.

3. NFL Thursday Pre-Kick, NBC, 19.68 million.

4. Sunday Night NFL Pre-Kick, NBC, 19.04 million.

5. Football Night in America, NBC, 14.53 million.

This article contains information from the Associated Press.

title: TOP NFL TEAMS HAVE ONE COMMON THREAD: ONE QB
content:
As many as 10 NFL teams will change starting quarterbacks this week, including two - Miami and Kansas City - that will turn to first-time starters.

What else is new?

A staggering 50 quarterbacks have started games this season, including 35 among 17 teams. Dolphins rookie John Beck and Chiefs second-year pro Brodie Croylewill bring the total to an eye-popping 52.

Fifty-two!

On the flip side, 15 teams have had the luxury of starting the same quarterback in each of the first nine games this season, including all eight division leaders. Those 15 teams are a combined 86-49, the division leaders a decisive 54-18.

The Bucs lead the NFC South at 5-4, and Jeff Garcia has started every game. Coach Jon Gruden knows that combination is more than mere coincidence.

"It's a miracle, really," said Gruden, who dealt with at least one quarterback change in each of the past three seasons in Tampa Bay before acquiring Garcia.

"He has played so well for us and meant so much to our team. He has been a great leader. He has been a consistent play-maker. And his poise, I think, shows through."

Garcia is, perhaps, the best example that consistency at quarterback is a vital element of NFL success. The Bucs have sustained injuries at running back, left tackle, wide receiver and tight end yet still have a winning record.

Nothing disrupts an offense more than changing quarterbacks. The Bucs should know. They started three in 2004 and 2006, resulting in records of 5-11 and 4-12, respectively.

"It's been fun up 'til now," said Bucs receiver Joey Galloway, turning to knock on the wood at his locker. "It's been helpful for the offense. It's been helpful for the team. He has played very well.

"If he can continue to do that, we're going to be a team to be reckoned with later on."

More than half the league has not been so lucky. The combined record of the 17 teams with a revolving door at quarterback? A not-so-spiffy 58-95.

Remove the playoff-contending Browns (5-4), who dumped Charlie Frye after Week 1 in favor of rising star Derek Anderson, and the remaining 16 teams are 53-91, a dismal .368 winning percentage.

Here's the latest:

- In Baltimore, aging Steve McNair is out, Kyle Boller is in. Again.

- In Oakland, Josh McCown is out, Daunte Culpepper is in. Again.

- In San Francisco, Alex Smith is out, veteran Trent Dilfer is in. Again.

- In Kansas City, veteran Damon Huard is out, Croyle is in.

- In Miami, Trent Green is out for the season, Cleo Lemon is out of favor and the Dolphins are running out of time to win at least one game, having lost their first nine. Beck, a second-round pick from Brigham Young, will be the 12th quarterback to start for the Dolphins since Dan Marino retired after 1999.

- In Chicago, Brian Griese is out, Rex Grossman is in. Again. This comes a month after Grossman was benched for poor play and one week after throwing a perfect deep ball to cap a fourth-quarter comeback in Oakland in place of the injured Griese.

- In Minnesota, Brooks Bollinger is out, Tarvaris Jackson is in. Again.

- In Carolina, coach John Fox is doing everything he can to avoid starting David Carr, including propping up Vinny Testaverde, who turned 44 on Tuesday.

Perhaps the most shocking potential change is in Atlanta. Despite winning their past two games with Joey Harrington, the Falcons will start Byron Leftwich as soon as he is ready after ankle surgery three weeks ago. That could come Sunday against the Bucs.

Earlier this season, rookie coach Bobby Petrino benched Harrington in favor of Leftwich, who was injured in his only start Oct. 21 at New Orleans. At the risk of breaking his team's momentum, Petrino will stick with his month-old decision to start Leftwich.

Of the myriad changes this week, only one was an easy choice. In Jacksonville, starter David Garrard returns to the lineup after missing three games with an ankle injury.

Though injuries played a part in some of this week's changes, most are the result of difficult decisions by coaches.

Good teams get consecutive starts.

Bad teams need jump starts.

"Everything is based on one basic thing: Who gives us the best chance to win this week?" Bears coach Lovie Smith told reporters Wednesday after naming Grossman the starter for Sunday's game against the Seahawks.

"You take injuries. You take performance. You take everything into play. That's what we did coming to this conclusion."

Joanne Korth can be reached at korth@sptimes.com or (727) 893-8810.

FAST FACTS

Consistent starting QB, consistent winning

It can't be a coincidence. Among the NFL's 32 teams, 15, including all eight division leaders, have started the same quarterback for all nine games. The division leaders are a combined 54-18, the 15 teams a combined 86-49:

title: Cameras kept eye on Moss and Carter
content:
RSEC:             The emotion captured by Fox's cameras on the Vikings sideline Sunday during the team's 17-10 loss at Chicago was arguably more interesting than the action on the field.

     Fox showed several shots of Vikings receivers Cris Carter and Randy Moss expressing displeasure with teammates and coaches. When Fearon Wright took a 15-yard taunting penalty during a Vikings punt in the second quarter, Fox showed Carter getting in the rookie's face and grabbing his jersey as he reached the sideline.

     Artie Kempner, the director for Fox's No. 2 NFL broadcast team, said such shots develop from isolating cameras on two of the Vikings' best players whenever they are on the field.

   "We always isolate Cris Carter and Randy Moss and we do it all the way through the play," Kempner said. "One of the things that is a rule in our coverage is when we have [a camera] isolated on a player, we always stay with that assignment until the play is completely over. Just in case of a reaction from the player or an altercation that might take place."

     Sideline reporter Pam Oliver contributed to Fox's coverage of the sideline spats. She reported Carter told coaches, "The Bears were playing us soft and too deep and you guys aren't doing anything to adjust to that and you're not doing anything to listen to us as we are trying to make suggestions about what to do."

     Green was asked Monday whether he thought television cameras would focus on sideline confrontations all year.

     "I think it's a story this week but there will be another story next week," he said. "That's part of the sideline reporters. . . . If they can get something like yesterday, that will last for a long time. Or at least it will last all week, or at least last until the next story comes around, and we'll live with that. We don't like it, but we'll live with it."

     Kempner said analyst Troy Aikman, a former Cowboys quarterback who is working with former teammate Daryl Johnston and play-by-play man Dick Stockton, had talked Friday about Carter's and Moss' willingness to express themselves on the sideline and how that could create conflict.

     "Troy brought it up how when you are a young quarterback [like Daunte Culpepper] what it would be like to have a veteran guy and superstar in your face after each play," Kempner said. "Daunte always seems to get an earful. . . . Troy was looking at it and saying it has to be difficult for him because they all want the ball and are telling him the right way to do things."

     Aikman has seen this before from the Vikings. He was the Cowboys quarterback in Dallas' 27-15 loss to the Vikings last season on Thanksgiving Day when the Fox cameras showed Moss and Culpepper having a spirited discussion after the quarterback did not throw to Moss on a third-down play in the third quarter.

     Kempner, however, said he had never seen Carter as upset as he was Sunday. "I've seen him react and give players a tongue-lashing on the sidelines for mistakes," Kempner said. "But Sunday he was frustrated beyond what I've seen."

     Kempner isn't concerned about Carter's reaction to having so much of the receiver's frustration caught on camera.

     "It's our job at Fox to do a good job of covering the story of the game, and the actions on the field and bench are part of covering that story," he said. "We have always done a great job of showing Cris Carter and the Vikings when things are going great. . . . Part of being a pro football player is everything you do can become important and you're under constant scrutiny."

     After Sunday, Carter and Moss can expect further scrutiny from network cameras.

     _ Judd Zulgad is at jzulgad@startribune.com

title: NFL MIDSEASON REPORT
content:
At the midway point of the 2003 NFL season, Kansas City (8-0), Carolina (6-1) and Tennessee (6-2) were cruising toward the postseason. This year, the Chiefs (3-5), Panthers (1-7) and Titans (3-5) are wallowing near the bottom of their divisions.

Conversely, Pittsburgh (2-5), Atlanta (1-6) and San Diego (1-6) were bringing up the rear in the standings in 2003. Today, the Steelers (7-1), Falcons (6-2) and Chargers (6-3) are first-place teams.

Obviously, some of the balance of power has shifted around the league. But other factors remain the same: Super Bowl winner New England (7-1) remains a force in the AFC, while Philadelphia (7-1) could be headed toward a fourth successive NFC title game.

Still, with half of the season to go, much of what has transpired so far could shift dramatically. Rams fans certainly are hoping so. It seems to be the way of the world in the NFL these days.

MOST VALUABLE PLAYER Colts QB Peyton ManningNot only is Manning on pace to challenge Dan Marino's NFL pass-yardage record (5,084), he has thrown for a league-high 26 touchdowns and has been intercepted only four times. His passer rating of 121.2 is other-worldly. Runners-up: Vikings QB Daunte Culpepper has cooled a bit after a surreal start to the year but he's still putting up big numbers; Seahawks RB Shaun Alexander is averaging an NFL-best 5.1 yards per carry and is on pace for a 1,700-yard season; Eagles WR Terrell Owens, who was dubbed the Least Valuable Player in this report a year ago, is No. 2 in the league in receiving yards and has caught nine TD passes, the most among receivers; Steelers QB Ben Roethlisbergeris a rookie, but his status climbs higher with each win.

ROOKIE OF THE YEAR

Steelers QB Ben Roethlisberger

The NFL is supposed to chew up and spit out rookie quarterbacks, but Roethlisberger has performed like a veteran since replacing Tommy Maddox. Runners-up: S Gibril Wilson has become a mainstay in the Giants secondary; Jonathan Vilma has been impressive at MLB for the Jets; Vikings RB Mewelde Moore has totaled 368 yards in his last four games; Broncos WR Darius Watts tops AFC rookies in receptions (22) and yards (264); Texans CB Dunta Robinson has a team-high three interceptions.

LEAST VALUABLE PLAYER

Browns QB Jeff Garcia

Acquired in the offseason to put a veteran at the helm of the Cleveland offense, Garcia has thrown for just nine touchdowns and has tossed eight interceptions. His 77.9 passer rating is exceeded by 20 other quarterbacks. Runners-up: Buffalo Bills QB Drew Bledsoe's numbers are nearly as bad as Garcia's; QB Kerry Collins has been stunningly inept as Rich Gannon's replacement in Oakland; verbose Tampa Bay DE Simeon Rice has done more yakking than sacking this year.

SURPRISE PLAYER

Chargers TE Antonio Gates

Gates, who caught 24 passes for 389 yards and two touchdowns last season as a rookie, has a league-high 54 receptions for 602 yards and eight TDs after nine games this year. Runners-up: Falcons DE Patrick Kerney has seven sacks, topping his total for the 2003 season; second-year RB Chris Brown has been a more-than-suitable successor to Eddie George in Tennessee, ranking fifth in the league in rushing with 810 yards.

TOP COACH

Bill Cowher, Pittsburgh Steelers

Wins the past two weeks over New England and Philadelphia prove that the 7-1 Steelers' turnaround from a 6-10 finish in 2003 is for real. Runners-up: Jim Mora, the former 49ers defensive coordinator, has constructed a resourceful defense and has pushed the Atlanta Falcons (6-2) to the top of the NFC South; Marty Schottenheimer of the San Diego Chargers (6-3) brought in Wade Phillips to fix his broken defense, and that move has turned a last-place team into a playoff contender.

DISAPPOINTING PLAYER

Ravens RB Jamal Lewis

After rushing for 2,066 yards in 2003, Lewis said he was targeting the NFL record of 2,105 this year. But after serving a two-game suspension for legal issues off the field, he has collected just 575 yards, a total topped by 13 other backs. Runners-up: Tampa Bay QB Brad Johnson, who posted the fifth-highest pass-yardage total in the league last year (3,811 yards), lost his starting job to Chris Simms, and then when Simms was hurt, Brian Griese took over; Giants DE Michael Strahan topped the NFL with 18 sacks last season but collected just four in New York's first eight games before suffering a season-ending chest injury.
