title: McNabb answers questions at Redskins Park
content:
ASHBURN, Va. - Head down, flip-flops sliding with a noisy reluctance across a concrete path, a Redskins media person striding at his side like a chaplain, Donovan McNabb resembled a death-row inmate on his last walk as he moved slowly toward an outdoor podium Wednesday morning.

Swing low, sweet chariot, the NFL schedule was comin' for to carry him home.

No last-minute call from the governor, not even Eagle Ed Rendell, was going to get the Redskins quarterback a reprieve from this sentence. Ever since the Eagles dealt him on Easter, McNabb had to have been haunted by thoughts of Sunday's return to Philadelphia and, worse, of having to discuss it with the media before and afterward.

McNabb was characteristically drab and diplomatic in a 16-minute, outdoor Q&A session at Redskins Park. The man Philadelphia loved and hated, cheered and castigated, embraced and ultimately divorced was not about to reveal whether he was feeling sentimental, spiteful, or even particularly motivated.

"For me to sit here and try to explain how my emotions may be or what's going to be going through my mind, is just kind of, as Mike Tyson said, ludicrous," McNabb said. "The thing about it is I think I can respond to it better when the game is over."

His back literally against a wall as a legion of reporters pressed in on him, the Redskins QB tried initially to steer the conversation to 1-2 Washington, which he said faced a "must-win" situation Sunday at Lincoln Financial Field.

Clearly tense at first, with downcast eyes and constantly shuffling feet, McNabb became enlivened when the subject turned to Michael Vick and Kevin Kolb, and gradually he seemed to warm to the talk about this long-anticipated matchup with Andy Reid and his former team.

"Eleven years is [something] you can't just forget about in a day, a week, or a year," he said at one point. "So we had a lot of success. The records that I hold there is due to Andy taking that chance on me in '99 and the guys that I played with, the great players that are future Hall of Famers, those guys. And I'm just excited that I had that opportunity to play with those guys. Life moves on."

It could have moved on, of course, a little farther away than Washington, where he will have to endure being in the Eagles' eye twice each season for as long as he remains Daniel Snyder's quarterback. A huge Fox Sports billboard along Route 295 in the District of Columbia blares "McNabb's Return to Philly!" in promoting Sunday's marquee NFL matchup.

Asked if he already was eager to get this week behind him, McNabb laughed: "Why, so we won't have to answer these questions anymore? We play them twice a year."

Oddly, some of the intense focus McNabb might have anticipated this week was deflected by the ballyhooed rebirth of Michael Vick, a quarterback who, at least in part, owes his Philly renaissance to McNabb. The then-Eagles QB recommended that the team sign Vick, a public pariah after his much-publicized felony conviction for operating a dogfighting ring.

"Mike and I have been very good friends for almost 15 years, so I'm just happy that he's gotten this opportunity. And he's really kind of flourished," said McNabb.

As for Kolb, the man Vick replaced as the Eagles' starter, the man the Eagles drafted to be McNabb's eventual replacement, there was both sympathy and respect.

"This game really isn't fair at times," McNabb said, a quote he could have used after the Eagles sent him here in April. "It's a tough situation, obviously, when you lose your job after being hurt. That's one that's hard to swallow. I'm sure he's not accepting of it, really. But he's being a good teammate. One thing that I am happy about is that he's being a good teammate."

It was early on in the session when a female reporter asked the most obvious question of all: How did he think Philadelphia's notoriously harsh fans would receive him?

"Hopefully, cheers," he said.

"But you know they have tough fans?" the questioner persisted.

"I do know that," he said with a straight face. "It's been 11 great years, and you wouldn't expect me to say I'm going to get booed."

Washington's new QB insisted his major concern Sunday would be making sure he enters through the right tunnel. He'd never been to the Linc's visitors' locker room but was sure that, like most, it's "small, congested, probably not clean."

And while he acknowledged that boos certainly were a possibility in Philadelphia, he was fairly certain that most fans understood and valued what he did there.

"I think a lot of times people focus in on talk radio and their opinions or their judgments," he said. "But there are a lot of people who were truly accepting of me and what I was able to do and respected me as a player and as a person. And so I do feel like I was appreciated."

When the last question was asked and answered, the sea of reporters parted. McNabb, apparently enlivened by the give-and-take, obviously feeling more comfortable than when it began, walked confidently back to the locker room.

His appeal, in Philadelphia at least, will be heard on Sunday.

Contact staff writer Frank Fitzpatrick at 215-854-5068

or ffitzpatrick@phillynews.com.

title: Back from bench, McNabb to start again
content:
Donovan McNabb's benching lasted exactly one half. The five-time Pro Bowl quarterback will be taking the snaps again when the Philadelphia Eagles host the Arizona Cardinals on Thanksgiving night.

McNabb was benched for the second half of Sunday's 36-7 loss to the Baltimore Ravens after committing three turnovers in the second quarter. It was the first time in his 10-year career that McNabb was pulled because of poor performance.

McNabb's replacement, Kevin Kolb, fared no better, throwing two interceptions, including one in the end zone that Ravens safety Ed Reed ran back a record 108 yards.

Eagles coach Andy Reid reinstated McNabb during his Monday news conference. "Sometimes you have to take a step back to step forward in a positive way, and Donovan will do that," Reid said.

McNabb was unavailable to comment, but Reid spread the blame: "I need to coach better. Donovan needs to play better, and the guys around Donovan need to play better."

McNabb has endured a tough two-game stretch -- last week he was picked off three times and lost a fumble in a tie with Cincinnati -- but he's completed 58.8% of his passes for 2,770 yards, 14 TDs and 10 interceptions.

NBC analyst Cris Collinsworth said Monday that should McNabb struggle Thursday, it would give the Eagles (5-5-1), who have an outside chance at a wild-card spot, four games to see what Kolb can do as a starter.

Collinsworth said it would be a mistake to end the McNabb era in Philadelphia, which includes six playoff trips and a Super Bowl berth. "He'll be in huge demand," Collinsworth said. "You put him on some of these teams that lack a quality quarterback, they're going to be really something."

Two other quarterbacks who were benched Sunday -- the Cleveland Browns' Brady Quinn and the Detroit Lions' Daunte Culpepper -- are expected to start this week.

title: Favre's decision looms large
content:
GREEN BAY, Wis. -- Will he or won't he? That was the guessing game in the Green Bay Packers locker room on Monday as the players said goodbye to each other, the 2004 season and maybe Brett Favre.

Favre's emotional comments after the 31-17 playoff loss to the Minnesota Vikings at Lambeau Field rekindled the issue of his retirement, though he made no firm announcement of his intentions.

"This is a decision that can't be made in a week's time," said quarterback Doug Pederson, Favre's backup and one of his closest friends on the team. "It'll probably be several months before anybody knows."

Favre met with coach Mike Sherman for about 45 minutes after throwing four interceptions in the loss to the Vikings. As he has for the last several years at various junctures, Favre, 35, talked about the possibility of retiring and of taking some time to let the emotions of the moment cool before making up his mind.

"My gut tells me he might be back, that he wants to end on a good note," guard Marco Rivera said. "He's been through a lot, and that weighs on a person. We're going to miss him if he goes."

In the last 13 months, Favre's father and brother-in-law have died, and his wife, Deanna, is having treatment for breast cancer. His oldest daughter attends high school in Mississippi rather than splitting time between the family's home there and Green Bay. Favre often talks of how much he enjoys riding his tractor on his property near Hattiesburg and how difficult it is to leave in late July for training camp. But whether he's ready to board the tractor full time is the question only he can answer.

"Too early to call," kicker Ryan Longwell said. "He's done so much, and he's been through so much. It would be weird not to see him, but eventually it's going to happen."

Sherman, who also serves as general manager, was not available Monday. Favre and his $6.5 million salary would affect any offseason roster retooling and commitment of dollars to potential free agents. Some of the Packers free agents might base a decision on whether to re-up on the QB situation.

The Packers never lined up an heir to Favre. Pederson, 36, finished the season on injured reserve and will be a free agent. Craig Nall has had little playing time, and J.T. O'Sullivan none in three seasons spent primarily with the New Orleans Saints.

"There's a sense of anxiety in the upper echelon of this organization because everything hinges on Brett," Pederson said. "Obviously they would like to know so they can make plans, but that's not a decision that can be made in a week."

The three-time NFL MVP led the Packers to back-to-back Super Bowls following the 1996 and '97 seasons. They won the first, beating the New England Patriots, and were upset in the next by the Denver Broncos.

Favre's 225 consecutive starts (including playoffs) are an NFL record for durability at his position. His 376 career TD passes are second to Dan Marino's 420, and he is third in career passing yards behind Marino and John Elway.

"The fans in Green Bay have had the pleasure of enjoying one of the greatest quarterbacks to ever play the game," Pederson said. "If he decides to retire, you have to honor that."

title: Playoffs in shouting range
content:
HEMPSTEAD, N.Y. -- When rookie Eli Manning signed with the New York Giants to be their franchise quarterback, Jets counterpart Chad Pennington advised him to cooperate with an often cantankerous press corps and avoid setting brush fires.

Now, after failing to keep his own counsel, Pennington is in the midst of a firestorm he helped create as he leads the Jets into a crucial game at the St. Louis Rams in Sunday's regular-season finale.

If the Jets (10-5) win to secure a playoff berth that once seemed certain after a 5-0 start, questions about Pennington will largely go away. Questions, which intensified after a recent media spat, such as whether he has the arm and makeup to justify a seven-year, $64 million contract extension and whether he can lead his team to big-game victories.

If the Jets should lose while the Buffalo Bills (against the Pittsburgh Steelers) and Denver Broncos (against the Indianapolis Colts) prevail at home to advance to the postseason, well, Pennington could not be more determined to avoid such a fallout.

"I'm like a volcano inside," he says. "Sunday can't come fast enough."

He insists the immense pressure he faces is simply part of playing a high-profile position in the Big Apple and will not cause him to self-destruct.

"In the NFL, there are no easy ways out," he says. "Everything is hard, everything is tough, and only the tough survive."

Still, the game is difficult enough without off-field distractions -- and Pennington has plenty of them.

"With the combination of the pressure of living up to the contract extension and the pressure of leading his team to the playoffs, he has to overcome a lot of outside influences," former Jets quarterback Boomer Esiason says, "and actually he created one himself."

Esiason, an analyst for CBS, was referring to the tantrum Pennington threw after criticism of his three-interception performance in a 17-6 loss Dec. 12 at Pittsburgh. That loss continued the Jets' season-long pattern of futility against elite teams.

The five-year veteran refused to take questions after a 37-14 rout of the Seattle Seahawks the next week, then lectured reporters the next day by telling them they were "privileged and honored to be beat writers for the New York Jets."

If the former journalism student at Marshall did not understand the line in the sand that exists between reporters and the teams they cover, he does now.

"Just Whine, Baby!" blared a New York Post headline.

Jets to find out what they're made of

With his team coming off a 23-7 loss against visiting AFC East champion New England on Sunday, scrutiny of the passer expected to lead the Jets to their first Super Bowl since 1969 only intensified.

New York's Daily News used his uniform number for fodder with a bold Tuesday headline that read, "The Imperfect 10."

Teammates remain supportive of their offensive leader but wish he had handled the criticism with his usual calm and cordial demeanor.

"My father always told me never to get in an argument with people who buy ink by the barrel," guard Pete Kendall says.

Sports talk-show hosts joined the tabloids in piling on. "They've invested $64 million in this guy being the face of the franchise. I'm very worried," WFAN Radio's Joe Benigno says. "I'm not sure he's the player we thought he was."

Benigno and other fans have largely suffered with the team since 1969, when Joe Namath delivered on his guarantee and the Jets shocked the Baltimore Colts 16-7 in Super Bowl III.

Pennington knows the pot-shot headlines and the negative talk can only end if the Jets win against the Rams and travel to San Diego for a playoff date with the AFC West champion Chargers on Jan. 8.

Given the NFC's widespread ineptitude, Sunday's game is of great consequence for St. Louis despite its 7-8 record. With a victory -- and help -- the Rams could land the NFC West title or a wild card.

"It is funny. You start to prepare from March all the way until now and it all comes down to the last game," Pennington says. "What you have done in the past and how you started the season doesn't matter anymore.

"It is all about Sunday. Take care of business on Sunday, all this goes to the wayside."

New York has excelled at crunchtime since tough-minded Herman Edwards became head coach. The Jets reached the playoffs in each of his first two seasons by winning the regular-season finale, closing out the 2001 schedule with a 24-22 decision at the Oakland Raiders before a 42-17 drubbing of the Green Bay Packers the next year produced an AFC East crown.

Edwards can become the first Jets coach to lead his team to the playoffs three times in a four-year span. He insists he welcomes Sunday's visit to St. Louis, where the built-for-turf Rams boast a 5-2 record with three successive victories.

"It's what you live for as a player," says the former defensive back who fashioned a 10-year NFL career after going undrafted. "You find out what kind of player you are. You find out what kind of team you are."

Shouldering the burden

Edwards would be encouraged if Pennington can flash the brilliance he showed in 2002, when he established himself with a club-record 68.9 completion percentage and a league-leading 104.2 passer rating.

The 6-3, 225-pounder missed the first six games in 2003 when he broke his left wrist in preseason and the Jets finished 6-10. He again was hit by a significant injury this year when he strained his right rotator cuff in a 22-17 loss Nov. 7 at Buffalo and missed three games.

There is reason to believe that Pennington, 28, is still playing hurt. His passer rating, which had been 97.4 while the Jets won six of their first eight games, is 76.7 with his team splitting its four games since his return. His touchdown-to-interception ratio had been 3-to-1, but he's now throwing an equal number of scores and pickoffs.

The Patriots, despite a decimated secondary, built Sunday's defensive game plan on the premise that Pennington was not likely to burn them deep. They consistently jammed inside routes and dared him to complete passes on out patterns, which require greater arm strength.

Pennington completed only three passes to his wide receivers while the visitors built a 16-0 lead in the first three quarters.

Although he acknowledges that his shoulder is sore, he blames recent struggles on poor footwork and rhythm.

Star running back Curtis Martin says it is unfair that Pennington is expected to take the blame for an offense that has been much too quiet against top-flight opponents. The Jets generated one touchdown in each of their two games against the Patriots, who have beaten them in six of their last seven meetings, and eked out two field goals at Pittsburgh.

"It's not just Chad," Martin says.

The conservative play-calling of offensive coordinator Paul Hackett also has come into question, as has the Jets' emotional makeup when they attempt to beat the best. The Jets have victories against teams with a combined 56-79 record. The teams they lost to are a composite 44-16.

Running back LaMont Jordan says of the most recent performance against New England, "We went into a heavyweight fight fighting like lightweights."

Perhaps Jordan was simply trying to stoke his teammates for the challenge that awaits them in the Show Me State. But Pennington knows there already has been too much talk -- and too little action.

"When it's a big game, it's up to me to win those games," he says. "That's how I control the perception."

title:
LACK OF BENGALS PRESSURE CAN HELP STEELERS TO THRIVE;
 NFL CINCINNATI AT PITTSBURGH SKY SPORTS 2 1.15AM
content:
NFL teams struggled to overcome some big handicaps on Sunday, but it's hard to see anything other than a convincing Pittsburgh win over Cincinnati tonight.

Miami, Philadelphia, Indianapolis and Carolina were handed the biggest handicaps of the week and all failed to cover, while the Steelers themselves should have covered a 4.5-point spread against San Diego, but had two touchdowns controversially ruled out.

However, Pittsburgh at least won the game and can overcome a double-digit handicap when they face AFC North rivals Cincinnati, a team they beat 38-10 at Paul Brown Stadium in Week Seven.

While the Steelers managed to post 410 net yards with no touchdowns, despite four trips inside the 20-yard line, and had 13 penalties called against them to San Diego's one, they are expected to handily beat a team who gave it their best shot against Philadelphia, but could not finish them off, settling for a 13-13 tie after overtime.

Cincinnati have the receivers to trouble a secondary missing Deshea Townsend and Bryant McFadden, but with quarterback Carson Palmer still sidelined, they will have trouble getting the ball to receivers TJ Houshmanzadeh and Chad Johnson.

The Bengals have played seven teams with winning records and have not beaten any of them, and despite their determined recent efforts, if they go behind early on, they could fold.

The offensive line allowed eight sacks of quarterback Ryan Fitzpatrick on Sunday - making it 38 for the season - and the Steelers have posted 36 sacks in total.

While the Pittsburgh line is not great in pass protection, the Bengals have managed just 11 sacks, which is indicative of the lack of pressure they have put on opposing quarterbacks.

Give passer Ben Roethlisberger time and he will beat anyone.

Unless the Bengals can run the ball - an unlikely scenario against the best rush defence in the league - they should lose by a wide margin.

title: Persistence pays off Derek Anderson, once viewed as a stopgap, has the Browns in contention for an AFC playoff spot
content:
BEREA, Ohio -- It was never a given Derek Anderson would emerge from the proverbial scrapheap to become perhaps the NFL's biggest surprise for 2007. But the big quarterback (6-6, 230) with the big arm has transformed good timing into the opportunity of a lifetime with the Cleveland Browns.

Anderson, 24, stepped in when the team gave up on former starter Charlie Frye -- apparently just minutes into the season -- and finds himself in the middle of the NFL playoff race as a catalyst for his surprising team.

As he prepares for a crucial game at Cincinnati that can deliver the Browns' first playoff berth in five years, it is apparent Anderson also benefits from an uncluttered mind.

Win Sunday and Cleveland clinches at least a wild card. Simple. Lose? Break out the Rubik's Cube of playoff permutations.

"I really don't have any idea what the scenarios are," Anderson said after a weight-lifting session this week. "Coach (Romeo Crennel) has kind of ingrained it in us to take it one game at a time. We control our own destiny. So we just need to do what we can do and then let 'em fall as they may."

The Browns (9-5) are tied for first place in the AFC North. That's quite the climb from last year's 4-12 finish and the 34-7 pasting by Pittsburgh in Week1. But a loss to the Bengals could set the stage for more heartbreak for long-suffering Browns fans. The Tennessee Titans' hopes include the potential of edging Cleveland on a tiebreaker.

This is a good week to be uncluttered.

"I'm floating through things and not really getting caught up in all of this," says Anderson, whose 26 touchdown passes are four shy of tying Brian Sipe's 1980 club record. "That's the way I've always gone about it, and it has carried over."

It is entirely fitting the Bengals stand in the way at this decisive juncture. Anderson had his breakout game in Week2 against Cincinnati, notching his first NFL win in a 51-45 shootout that included a club-record-tying five touchdown passes.

Entering that game, his first start after replacing Frye, many suspected Anderson was merely filling in until first-round rookie Brady Quinn took over. Instead, Anderson's arm ignited an offense that used the game as a trampoline to a new identity.

"It was crazy," Anderson remembers. "A lot of fun. But I wouldn't want to get into a shootout every week."

Prepared to play

A few months ago, Anderson's place on the NFL map was not unlike that of Scappoose, Ore., his tiny hometown (pop. 5,913) 20 miles northwest of Portland. He was a remote destination.

Despite a spotty preseason, Frye beat out Anderson to become the starter. Quinn, the former Notre Dame star drafted with the 22nd pick after Cleveland dealt next year's first-round choice to Dallas, was in the wings. But this is the NFL, where sixth-round pick Tom Brady morphed into a three-time Super Bowl champion and Kurt Warner ascended from bagging groceries to a league and Super Bowl MVP.

Anderson, who had 57 interceptions at Oregon State, entered the NFL in 2005 as a sixth-round pick with the Baltimore Ravens. Cleveland claimed him off waivers in 2005 and viewed him as a project. It took a dismal opening-day performance by Frye against Pittsburgh -- he was sacked five times on the first 15 pass plays -- for Anderson to get a legitimate shot to start.

"I wouldn't say I expected it, but in my mind I anticipated it," Anderson says. "I was prepared to play. I didn't think I had that bad of a camp. I was kind of frustrated about it, but it was, 'Whatever. I'll get another chance.' I just kept plugging away."

Frye, whose tendency to hold the football was a negative, was traded to Seattle for a sixth-round pick -- just two days after opening as the starter.

Anderson has become among the most productive passers. He's tied for fifth in TD passes and leads the NFL with an average of 13.12 yards a completion. With 3,384 passing yards, he's poised to become the first Browns quarterback to pass for 3,500 yards since Bernie Kosar in 1989.

"He's just really responded to the challenge and everything that we put on his plate," says Rob Chudzinski, the Browns' first-year offensive coordinator. "There are no guarantees, but I think he's learned that he had to do X-Y-Z, and then some, to put himself in the position to be successful."

Chudzinski's offense -- a hybrid of the controlled passing game of the West Coast scheme and the Don Coryell-influenced offense that aggressively attacks downfield -- is similar to the system Anderson ran at Oregon State.

It was Anderson's college coach, Mike Riley, who urged Browns general manager Phil Savage to take a flier on the quarterback. Riley even tipped off Savage, an old friend, to the fact the Ravens were planning to release Anderson in September 2005, with the intent of placing him on the practice squad after clearing waivers. Three days later, Savage got a call from his old boss with the Ravens, GM Ozzie Newsome. Anderson was sitting in Newsome's office. Newsome handed him the phone.

"I had my stuff already packed," Anderson says. Two hours later he was headed to Cleveland.

Browns' fortunes improve

It's about time the Browns got a break. Anderson is the 10th quarterback to start for the franchise since it re-entered the NFL by expansion in 1999 -- and the only one with a win-loss mark of at least .500. Including three starts last year as a fill-in for an injured Frye, Anderson is 9-7.

For a franchise with one winning season (2002) since its rebirth, the misfortune came from many directions. Quarterback Tim Couch, chosen No.1 overall in 1999, was a bust. So was defensive end Courtney Brown, the next year's No.1. Injuries and changes at the top also set back the team.

There has been adversity this year, but in a different vein and with opposite results.

The Browns fell behind 14-0 at St. Louis in Week8 but rallied to win 27-20. The next week they benefited from a replay reversal and made a fourth-down stop in beating Seattle in overtime. A week after blowing a 21-6 lead in a loss at Pittsburgh, the Browns rebounded after blowing a 13-point lead to win in overtime at Baltimore -- after the call on Phil Dawson's 51-yard field goal at the end of regulation, which ricocheted off the upright and the crossbar extension, was reversed in their favor.

"We've had a lot of different experiences," says Savage, who, like Crennel, arrived in 2005. "People say we're an overnight sensation, but we've been building toward this."

Savage shored up the offensive line by signing free agent guard Eric Steinbach and drafting left tackle Joe Thomas No.3 overall. He signed running back Jamal Lewis, another former Raven, who just posted his sixth 1,000-yard season. Young playmakers Braylon Edwards and Kellen Winslow II have blossomed, with the receiver Edwards earning his first Pro Bowl invitation.

Still, not even Savage knew Anderson would become so hot so fast: "He's had a chance to develop the right way. He was No.3 for a year, then a backup last season when he got his feet wet when Charlie got hurt. And he was like a co-No.1 in the offseason, when they split the snaps. So there was definitely an evolution to this process."

Coming out of college, Anderson was knocked for making too many poor decisions. He habitually forced throws, which led to a high turnover rate. Yet in Chudzinski's offense, with all its motion and shifts, Anderson has settled considerably.

That in itself has done much to help lift an offense that ranked 31st in total yards and averaged 14.9 points in 2006. The Browns rank 10th in yards (348.8 a game) and fifth in scoring (26.3 points a game). Since Anderson replaced Frye in Week1, they have allowed just 12 sacks, a figure undeniably linked to his quick release.

Anderson's deep-strike ability has been considerable. Only Dallas and New England, with 52 apiece, have more completions of 20 yards or more this season than the Browns (49). Just four teams have more TD passes of at least 20 yards.

"Through college, I put a lot of pressure on myself to make plays, knowing I had to carry us," Anderson says. "As I've progressed in the (NFL), I've realized I have all these great playmakers around me. I just need to calm down, get them the ball and don't try to do anything extraordinary."

The confident vibe running through the Browns is evident. For many, this is their first taste of winning in the NFL. It's even sweeter because few regarded the Browns as poised for such a dramatic leap this year.

"Nobody gave us a shot," Edwards says. "I think they had us winning three games this year. We're not through, but we stuck together when nobody else believed in us."

A chance to prove themselves in the playoffs looms, too. "I'm thinking, 'Let's just do it,'" Anderson says. "If we get in, it's a one-game season, obviously. ... If you get in, it doesn't matter what your dang record was or how you got there. You get to attack it and leave it all out there."

title: Cowboys' thrashing of Eagles proves they are contenders
content:
Team of the week.The Dallas Cowboys probably saved their season and at least ruined the Eagles' bye week with their 33-10 trouncing of the Birds on Sunday. Not only did the Cowboys improve to 3-2, but they now rightly think they can contend in the NFC East.

Offensive player of the week.Dallas quarterback Drew Bledsoe picked apart the Eagles by completing 24 of 35 passes for 289 yards, three touchdowns and no interceptions in the rout over the Eagles. Bledsoe's passer rating of 122.2 was almost double Donovan McNabb's 64.7 rating.

Defensive player of the week.Carolina strong safety Marlon McCree had two interceptions, two passes defensed, and a team-high 10 tackles in the Panthers' 24-20 win over Arizona.

Should have stayed in bed.New Orleans running back Deuce McAllister suffered a torn anterior cruciate ligament in his right knee in Sunday's 52-3 loss to Green Bay, an injury that likely will sideline him for the season. McAllister gained just 31 yards on 11 carries and had three receptions for 33 yards before the injury.

Inquirer Scouting Report pick of the week.Carolina 24, Arizona 20. Actual score: Carolina 24, Arizona 20.

Inquirer Scouting Report worst pick of the week.Eagles 27, Cowboys 23. You know how that one turned out.

Upset of the week.The guy who does The Inquirer Scouting Report went 9-4-1 against the spread. Several Inquirer editors had to be revived by smelling salts when they saw the record was north of .500.

Game of the week.Jeff Reed's 40-yard field goal with six seconds left gave the Pittsburgh Steelers a 24-22 win at San Diego on Monday night. The Steelers held the dangerous LaDainian Tomlinson to just 62 yards on 18 carries (3.4 average) and one touchdown. Tomlinson becomes the Eagles' headache after the bye week.

Division of the week.The AFC South went 3-1, and the only loss (by Houston) came to AFC South rival Tennessee. Indianapolis won easily over San Francisco, and Jacksonville knocked Cincinnati from the unbeaten ranks.

Wasted effort.San Diego linebacker Donnie Edwards had 15 tackles in the Chargers' loss to Pittsburgh.

Ex-Eagle of the week.Green Bay cornerback Al Harris had two interceptions, including one that he returned for a 22-yard touchdown, in the win over New Orleans. Harris also had one sack and three passes defensed.

Current Eagle of the week.None worthy of nomination.

Local player of the week.Atlanta quarterback Matt Schaub of West Chester East filled in for injured Michael Vick and completed 18 of 34 passes for 298 yards, three touchdowns and no interceptions in the Falcons' 31-28 loss to New England.

Quick impression.With so much parity in the NFC, it looks as if a team with as many as four and possibly five losses could earn home-field advantage. And before anybody hands the Super Bowl trophy to Indianapolis, remember that the 5-0 Colts have beaten Baltimore, Jacksonville, Cleveland, Tennessee and San Francisco, teams with a combined 9-14 record.

Contact staff writer Marc Narducci at 856-779-3225 or mnarducci@phillynews.com

title: Ed Barkowitz: Vick's great night only took you so far
content:
The screams were ubiquitous on Monday night. The problem was distinguishing whether they were screams of unbridled joy or sheer horror.

Michael Vick's amazing six-touchdown effort turned around fantasy results everywhere and left many fans either celebrating the miracle or crying in agony.

Brian DiBricida was among those facing a huge deficit, but he had Vick going Monday night.

"By the end of the first quarter, with the rushing yardage and everything else, I thought I might have something here," said DiBricida, a Roxborough native who lives in West Norriton. "So I stayed up . . .It made my week exciting, because I thought I was done. I thought I was out of it."

Vick became the first player in NFL history to pass for three touchdowns and run for two others in the first half. The residual fantasy effect was obvious. Teams that seemingly were dead had life. Teams that were comfortably in front had a big problem.

An argument could be made that Vick's prolific performance - which ended with the six TDs, 333 yards passing and 80 yards rushing - was the greatest fantasy performance ever for an Eagles quarterback. That's debatable. In a 1989 game, Randall Cunningham had five short touchdown passes and 447 yards passing against the Redskins. In 1962, Sonny Jurgensen threw for 419 yards and a distance-league dream of five touchdowns of 40, 56, 60, 60 and 82.

Three quarterbacks in NFL history have thrown for seven touchdowns in a game, including the Giants' Y.A. Tittle, who had 505 yards in his 1962 romp. For distance leagues, though, the best might be Joe Namath, who threw for 496 yards and six touchdowns - for 7, 9, 10, 65, 67 and 80 yards - in 1972.

Five quarterbacks have thrown six touchdowns in a game in just the last 5 years, including New England's Tom Brady,who had five TD passes in the first half in a game against Tennessee last season. In the all-time annals, Vick's performance was terrific, but not historic.

Not that it's much consolation to DiBricida, who was left needing even more. His opponent, you see, had Jeremy Maclin, and Maclin's touchdown catch from Vick early in the second quarter was just enough to sink DiBricida. In this case, there was a third source for Monday's screaming.

"I guess it's typical of the Eagles in my lifetime," chuckled DiBricida, 36. "They just can't get me over the hump."

Position watch

Quarterbacks: An injury to the bursa sac in his right knee is just adding to the disappointing season for Texans quarterback Matt Schaub. He could be a game-time decision. Backup Dan Orlovsky is appealing only in very large leagues . . . It's easy to snicker at the task Carolina quarterback Brian St. Pierre, a Baltimore retread, has in making his first NFL start (in his 8-year career) against the Ravens, except the Ravens have given up an average of 307 passing yards over the last five games, including 382 to Buffalo's Ryan Fitzpatrick. That said, you'd still be nuts to start St. Pierre.

Running backs: San Diego's Mike Tolbert could be worth bringing off the bench as a No. 3 or flex option. The Chargers take on a Broncos defense that is 30th in rushing yards and has allowed a league-high 14 rushing touchdowns . . . LaDainian Tomlinson owners ought to take note that the Jets gave Shonn Greene got 20 carries Sunday, his biggest workload in five games. LT has been held under 4.0 yards per carry in each of the last four games.

Wide receivers: Kansas City's Dwayne Bowe is on the best run of his career, with eight touchdowns in his last five games. He's been targeted an astounding 30 times over the last two and has a great remaining schedule.

Stat stuff

--The 13 300-yard passers from last weekend (in 14 games) surpassed the previous record of 10 in Week 8 in 1984 (also 14 games) and Week 15 last year (16 games). For the Eagles, Ron Jaworski had 287 yards that week in 1984, Donovan McNabb 306 last year and Michael Vick 333 on Monday.

-- Waiver-wire MVP Peyton Hillis needs two rushing touchdowns to become the fifth Browns player with 10 in a season. The other four are Kevin Mack, Mike Pruitt, Leroy Kelly and Jim Brown.

-- Heads-up LeSean McCoy owners: The Giants have allowed only one 100-yard rusher (Chris Johnson) and only four rushing TDs.

-- Beware if the Sunday pregame shows mention the stat that Buffalo wide receiver Lee Evans has at least 100 yards and a touchdown in his three career games against Cincinnati. He hasn't faced the Bengals since 2007. Steve Johnson remains the Bills receiver to play.

Contest of the week

With Thanksgiving on the horizon next week, the Daily News wants to know what fantasy fans are most thankful for. It could be anything from drafting Philip Rivers in the third round to a key gaffe that helped win a game.

Send what you're most grateful for to fantasyfootball@phillynews.com or fax to 215-854-5524. Include your name and hometown.

Three participants will be randomly selected to win a fantasy football T-shirt.

Last week's contest

Jim Krzywicki, of Somerton, came closest to guessing Randy Moss' putrid receiving yardage total from Sunday. Moss had one catch for 26 yards in his Titans debut. Krzywicki predicted he'd get 24 and earned a Daily News fantasy football T-shirt for his troubles.

X X X X X

Ed Barkowitz, who is all smiles after using David Garrard as a bye-week replacement for Drew Brees last week, has been writing about fantasy football for the Daily News since 2001. Send e-mail to fantasyfootball@phillynews.com

title: Paul Domowitch / Leinart's stock could be falling
content:
EVER SINCE Matt Leinartopted last January for ballroom dancing and a shot at a third straight national title rather than an early-entry ticket into the National Football League, it has been assumed that the USC quarterback will be the very first pick in next April's draft.

And he very well could be. But the more scouts check under his hood, the more reservations they seem to be having about whether he's a take-it-to-the-bank, can't-miss pro prospect.

"It's not a slam dunk with him," one AFC scout said. "It's not a no-brainer. All the check marks aren't there with him like they were with the Manning brothers and some other guys. I have some reservations right now. He's going to go high and might even be the first guy. But I'd be worried if I were faced with [taking] him in that top spot."

Leinart is third in Division I-A in passing with a 166.9 rating. He's thrown 19 touchdown passes and six interceptions and has completed 65.5 percent of his passes. His team is again undefeated.

But his lack of mobility and a just-OK arm have scouts wondering if he's more Tim Couchthan Carson Palmer.

"I'm big on the movement part with quarterbacks,'' an NFC scout said. "In today's game, I think it's absolutely necessary. I'm not talking Michael Vick.But you've got to be able to move around, and I'm not sure he's got that.

"He's got enough of an arm. But if he's not on-balance and can't step into his throws, he's going to be in trouble. And he gets a lot of help from his [receiver] guys the same way all of [Steve] Spurrier'squarterbacks at Florida got. He throws it up, they do jump balls and catch it and the other team doesn't.''

Even with his flaws, though, Leinart clearly is considered the best quarterback of the draft-eligible seniors. None of the other top seniors, including Vanderbilt's Jay Cutler, Texas A & M's Reggie McNealand Missouri's Brad Smith,are considered early first-round material.

Juniors Vince Youngof Texas and Brady Quinnof Notre Dame probably are the second- and third-best quarterback prospects. Young may come out early, but it's highly unlikely that Quinn will. They're already talking about a national championship next year in South Bend. And Quinn knows that if he spends another year playing for Charlie Weis,he'll have an excellent chance of being the first player taken in the 2007 NFL draft.

"Young's gotten really, really better with his accuracy,'' the AFC scout said. "He's a different kind of cat deliverywise. It'll come from everywhere. But he gets it from Point A to Point B. And I love the way he can run.''
 
Around the league

* Cedric Benson'slong training-camp holdout has caused the Bears' rookie running back to be mostly a spectator thus far as Thomas Joneshas gotten most of the carries. But Benson rushed for 35 yards on three overtime carries in Sunday's win over the Lions and likely will see his workload start to increase in the second half of the season.

"He's a good football player,'' Bears coach Lovie Smithsaid of Benson, who was the fourth overall pick in the last draft. "But it takes time. And when you miss training camp, you need even more time. He's finally been getting it.''

* Ex-Eagles defensive tackle Corey Simonstill is feeling his way around the Colts' defense. He doesn't have a sack yet and is eighth on the team in tackles.

"I think Corey has helped mentally more than anything else,'' Colts coach Tony Dungysaid. "I think he's given us a presence. He's used to playing good defense. He has given our guys a little bit of confidence. He is still learning what we do and how we do it. I think he is going to be a tremendous player for us in the future. He has played well already, but I think he is going to be much better as we get into the second half of the year.''
 
By the numbers

* The offensively inept Ravens have yet to score 20 points in a game this season. Their seven touchdowns are the fewest in the league.

* The Bengals already have forced 28 turnovers this season and have converted them into 84 points.

* Browns quarterback Trent Dilferhas committed nine turnovers in the last four games (six interceptions, three fumbles).

* Jets QBs haven't thrown a touchdown pass in five-plus games; 308 minutes, 19 seconds, to be exact. Chargers RB LaDainian Tomlinsonhas thrown as many TD passes (3) as all of the Jets' QBs this season.

* The Chiefs, who rank 31st in the league in pass defense, already have given up 120-plus receiving yards to four different players - the Redskins' Santana Moss(177), the Eagles' Terrell Owens(171), the Chargers' Antonio Gates(145) and the Jets' Chris Baker(124).

* The Panthers have recorded 10 sacks in the last two games after collecting just five in their first five games.

* The Vikings are 3-24 in their last 27 games on grass.

* The Jaguars have failed to score 30 points in 57 straight games.
 
This and that

* Before you pronounce the 6-2 Bengals as a legitimate Super Bowl contender, keep in mind that their six wins have come against teams with a combined 12-31 record.

* The Dolphins used Ronnie Brownand Ricky Williamsin the same backfield for a dozen snaps in last week's win over the Saints. Williams gained 40 of his 82 rushing yards when he was teamed with Brown. Brown, who finished with 106 yards on 23 carries, didn't have any carries when he was on the field with Williams.

* With Daunte Culpepperout for the year with a knee injury, the Vikings have just two quarterbacks on their roster. Wide receiver Koren Robinsonwill serve as the club's emergency QB Sunday against Detroit.

* The Cowboys have won five of their first eight games, but face a rough second-half schedule that features opponents with a combined 35-23 record, three divisional road games and a Thanksgiving Day matchup against the Broncos.

* Since 1998, the Colts have a minus-23 turnover differential in games against the Patriots, which goes a long way in explaining why Indy has lost 10 of 12 games to the Pats during that period. The two teams meet again Monday night.

title: Phil Sheridan: A lesson - if the Eagles will learn it
content:
As harsh as these championship-game defeats are, there are lessons in them - provided the Eagles are willing to learn them.

In the past, Andy Reid and his personnel and coaching staffs generally had gotten the message delivered with each bitter near-miss. If the Eagles are going to remain contenders for the next two or three seasons, then it's vitally important for Reid to learn from what happened to his team in 2008 and on the third Sunday of 2009.

After the Eagles lost to the Rams in their first 21st-century trip to the final four, the lesson was that Reid's program worked and the Eagles were on the right track. That one was easy, and it was borne out when the Eagles claimed the No. 1 seed in the NFC playoffs the next year.

The lesson from their loss to Tampa Bay after the 2002 season? It was a mistake to rely too heavily on veterans who had the smarts but no longer the physical tools to make plays. Injured safety Blaine Bishop and the fatally flawed middle-linebacker platoon of Levon Kirkland and Barry Gardner allowed Jon Gruden's offense to turn that game around.

The Eagles have been younger and more athletic on defense, for the most part, ever since.

The lesson from the awful loss to Carolina the next January? It was time to invest in some difference-makers after the Panthers' defense bullied Eagles wide receivers and shut down the offense in a 14-3 win.

That off-season, Reid went against every previously expressed belief on the subject and acquired Terrell Owens. He also signed Jevon Kearse, the biggest available name on the defensive side. That, of course, set up the Eagles' run to the Super Bowl.

The Owens meltdown and injuries to Donovan McNabb blurred the next few seasons. In the meantime, the Eagles retooled their defense, replacing most of the starters and coming up with what developed into a pretty good unit late in the 2008 regular season. As for the offense, it is worth noting that every time McNabb is healthy for all 16 games, the Eagles go to the playoffs and advance at least a round or two.

Yesterday, Reid fended off questions about what comes next. He would rather wear Owens' leotard during the Pro Bowl than tip his hand in public. You'll know what Reid thinks of his current roster when the transactions start happening.

So what is the lesson from this championship game disappointment? McNabb hinted at one element in his postgame comments.

"I think each year is an opportunity for you to add more weapons and add more guys that contribute heavily and play a major part in what you want to do," McNabb said.

If this echoes his call for playmakers last off-season, fine. McNabb enjoyed playing with rookie DeSean Jackson, an emerging star receiver. But he couldn't have helped noticing from the sideline that Larry Fitzgerald, like some combination of Jerry Rice, LeBron James and Mikhail Baryshnikov, turned every Kurt Warner throw into a perfect pass.

Meanwhile, the Eagles' locker room was like a museum of personnel oddities. Reggie Brown (second-round pick with a long-term contract extension) and Lorenzo Booker (big off-season acquisition on offense) were packing up after being inactive for the NFC championship game. Lito Sheppard (former first-round pick turned invisible man) barely played. Kevin Kolb (top draft pick two years ago) had just completed his second season without contributing directly to a victory.

The overwhelming lesson, now that the Eagles have been exposed to the harsh light of a championship game, is that they have to keep adding the best players they can, every chance they get. No more trading out of the first round because they're more interested in stockpiling future picks than in upgrading their team. No more carrying veterans (Greg Lewis, Correll Buckhalter) because they know the system even though, for whatever reason, they're not actually contributing on Sundays.

Kolb may turn out to be a good quarterback, and it wasn't a bad idea to develop a young guy considering McNabb's injury history. But taking him instead of a player who could help right away was foolish. Passing on the best players available in the draft to acquire Booker, who simply wasn't the Brian Westbrook clone the Eagles thought he was, was foolish.

Not having a fullback was foolish. Not having a running game for long stretches was inexcusable.

Every roster spot should be filled by a player who contributes or who is in development to contribute as soon as possible. Every draft pick should be used to get the best possible talent on the roster right away, in time to improve the team while McNabb is still a championship-caliber quarterback.

That's the lesson. Regardless of Reid's words yesterday, his actions will show whether he learned it.

Contact columnist Phil Sheridan at 215-854-2844 or psheridan@phillynews.com. Read his recent work at http://go.philly.com/philsheridan.

title: Down But Not Out
content:
Brad Johnson Has Taken A Beating This Season, But He Has Avoided Injury

TAMPA - If you add the patio furniture-moving injury that knocked him out of the first week of training camp, Brad Johnson has been sacked 40 times this year.

The takedown by the glass end table was the only time this year an injury has forced him from the football field.

Pretty remarkable, since Johnson has been sacked 39 times in 11 games, more times than any other quarterback in the league. He's on pace for 56 sacks, but that's well off the NFL record of 72 set in 1986 by Philadelphia's Randall Cunningham.

If there were any questions about Johnson's durability, he has answered them this season every time he got off his back, pulled grass from his helmet, readjusted his shoulder pads and stayed in the game.

"Considering we've given up a million sacks, he's proven to be pretty healthy," Tampa Bay center Jeff Christy said. "I've always been behind him. He's doing everything I expected him to do.

"I think it was just a bad rap that he wasn't durable."

The injury rap began during the 1997 season, when Johnson missed the final three games as Minnesota's quarterback after injuring his neck. He came back in 1998, but in only his second start that season, Johnson fractured his right leg. And after another comeback that year, he broke his thumb.

Johnson missed four games after spraining his left knee MCL last year with the Washington Redskins.

"I think if you look at a lot of those injuries, it was fluke things," Bucs strong safety John Lynch said. "I know [the health knock] is something that kind of bothers him, that he wasn't a durable guy.

"He's taken a lot of shots for certain and he's been there every week. He's been extremely durable for us."

Part of the credit for Johnson's survival this season lies in his work ethic.

And staying healthy has allowed him to earn the more desirable tag of accurate passer. Johnson, who has thrown a league-high 401 passes, has completed 64.6 percent of his passes. That's just slightly behind St. Louis' league-leading Kurt Warner (66.8), Oakland's Rich Gannon (65.6), New England's Tom Brady (65.6) and San Francisco's Jeff Garcia (65.4)

"I think one of the main reasons he has stayed injury-free is he does such a good job of taking care of himself," quarterbacks coach Jim Caldwell said. "His workout routine is absolutely flawless. He works extremely hard."

Still, no matter how much he works out, no amount of conditioning can ultimately prepare a quarterback for getting pounded to the ground again and again. Team officials were concerned following the 17-10 loss to Pittsburgh. Johnson was sacked 10 times, the most in one game in his 10-year career.

There should be cause for concern. When Johnson injured his neck in that 1997 game against Green Bay, he was sacked six times. When he injured his knee last season against Tennessee, he was sacked three times.

"If you've got a quarterback taking blindside shots, he could get hurt basically any play," Christy said. "We're supposed to protect him. It's hard enough to stay healthy doing the right thing.

"But when you've got people flying around unblocked hitting [the quarterback], it's harder."

There is also reason to believe Johnson can take a pounding. Of the five games where he has been sacked the most, four of them occurred this season. And Johnson has not missed any playing or practice time.

"Football's a physical game," Johnson said. "Everybody this time of year is going to be pretty much numb anyway. Everybody gets sore this time of year, but I feel fresh up to this point.

"I've had some stuff in the offseason. Actually, injury-wise, knock on wood, I've been pretty healthy."

Knock on a lot of wood.

title: Team notes
content:
Atlanta: Coach Dan Reeves said Michael Vick "needs to start beating people with his passing." Vick is 23 of 53 for 298 yards with a TD and two interceptions in the last two games. "He's got to set up and throw the football," Reeves said. "The runs will come, but we've got to start throwing the football well."

Carolina: DT Brentson Buckner had a sack and a push on a blocker Sunday that led to a safety in his first game back after missing four games for violating the NFL policy on steroids and related substances. "It was like being grounded by your mom and dad for four weeks and you can't come out of the house," Buckner said. "It seems like when you're grounded the world is having great fun. I just wanted to get back out there and have some fun."

Chicago: Fans would like to see more of QB Henry Burris, but coach Dick Jauron said, "There are situations that we'd just as soon not expose him to right now. And the overall reason I think is what I've said a number of times. The integrity of the game dictates, in my opinion, that we play the people that give us the best chance to win." Chris Chandler will start at quarterback Sunday. Jim Miller is out for the season with a knee injury.

Cincinnati: P Travis Dorsch, whose low kicks in his poor NFL debut led to two touchdown returns by Steve Smith of Carolina last Sunday, will be inactive the rest of the season. . . . WR Peter Warrick spent Sunday night in a Cincinnati hospital because of a chest injury suffered at Carolina. He is questionable for Sunday's game against Jacksonville.

Cleveland: Rookie RB William Green rushed 71 times for 161 yards through the first nine games but has gained 413 yards in four games since becoming a starter. The Browns have won three of the four.

Dallas: The Cowboys continue to struggle defensively on third down. Against the 49ers on Sunday, Dallas allowed conversions on 10 of 20 opportunities, including five of six in the fourth quarter when San Francisco came from behind to win. Said DT La'Roi Glover, "We have to be able to get off the field."

Denver: Wide receivers coach Karl Dorrell, a wide receiver at UCLA in the mid-1980s, is very interested in the Bruins' job after Bob Toldeo was fired Monday. Dorrell has the endorsement of coach Mike Shanahan. . . . Owner Pat Bowlen doesn't dictate personnel decisions, but he said veteran TE Shannon Sharpe has his backing if he wants to return for another season.

Green Bay: The Packers rank 29th in run defense (132.2 yards a game) and last in yards per attempt (5.1). One factor is that NT Gilbert Brown (ankle) has missed the last two games, but he is expected to play Sunday against the 49ers. "Gilbert's paid to stop the run, and he does that very well," coach Mike Sherman said. "So I would say that since we're not stopping the run very well right now, we miss him a lot."

Houston: Rookie QB David Carr has not thrown an interception in his last 91 passes, his second-longest streak of the season. He went 97 passes without an interception earlier this season.

Indianapolis: The Colts entered last weekend as the No. 1-seeded team for the AFC playoffs, but dropped to No. 5 by losing at Tennessee.

Jacksonville: QB Mark Brunell has thrown only 33 passes and the Jaguars have had only 95 total plays in the last two games, partially because Jacksonville's defense can't get opponents off the field.

Miami: If RB Ricky Williams averages 166.3 yards in the last three games, he will join Eric Dickerson, Barry Sanders, Terrell Davis and O.J. Simpson as the only players in NFL history to rush for 2,000 yards in a season.

New England: The Patriots have won three consecutive games and are trying to duplicate last season's 6-0 run that led to a Super Bowl victory after being 5-5. But the only games they won all season against teams that have winning records were the first three games of the season -- against Pittsburgh, the New York Jets and Kansas City.

N.Y. Giants: WR Ron Dixon was suspended without pay for one game because of missed treatments with the team's medical staff as well as being late or missing team meetings. In 2000, Dixon was fined for missing a team meeting and was later suspended for arriving late for a walk-through the day before a game. "My philosophy is always to treat the players fairly, and given Ron's track record, this penalty is more than fair," coach Jim Fassel said.

N.Y. Jets: RB Curtis Martin has 778 yards rushing and must average 74 yards in the final three games to become the second back in NFL history to reach 1,000 yards in eight consecutive seasons. Barry Sanders gained 1,000 in each of his first 10 seasons for Detroit.

Pittsburgh: Coach Bill Cowher said he is sticking with QB Tommy Maddox and RB Jerome Bettis in the starting lineup against Carolina on Sunday even though QB Kordell Stewart and RB Amos Zereoue have been much more effective recently.

St. Louis: Coach Mike Martz was asked Wednesday if Marc Bulger plays well in the final three games of the season, would that create a different look for the quarterback spot. Said Martz, "Absolutely. If Marc continues to grow and get better, with the success he had with this football team, you can't ignore that kind of success. And we certainly would not. "

San Diego: RT Vaughn Parker, who has missed the past three games after having triceps surgery, is a native of Buffalo and hopes to play Sunday against the Bills in his hometown.

Tennessee: If the Titans win their last three games, the worst they can be is the No. 2 seed in the AFC and guaranteed a home game in the second round.

Washington: Coach Steve Spurrier isn't happy with all the false starts this season. "I've got to talk to the line coach (Kim Helton)," he said. "We talk too much at the line of scrimmage. He's got the center talking to this guy and that guy and that guy and that guy, and then everybody starts wiggling a little bit. We talk too much, and that's my fault."

title: COWBOYS' GALLOWAY HAS TORN ACL, OUT FOR SEASON
content:
Joey Galloway was supposed to be the guy who turns around the Dallas Cowboys. Instead, his knee injury has turned things upside down.

Tests yesterday revealed a torn anterior cruciate ligament in the wide receiver's left knee that will require season-ending surgery. Making matters worse, quarterbacks Troy Aikman and Randall Cunningham are listed as questionable for Sunday night's game in Arizona.

Aikman sustained his ninth career concussion in the second quarter of Dallas' 41-14 loss in the opener against Philadelphia Sunday. The Eagles mauled him all nine times he tried to throw, resulting in four sacks and five incompletions.

Aikman was still feeling dizzy and having headaches yesterday. If his symptoms continue today, it's highly unlikely he would play Sunday.

"Let me be sure we're clear about one thing: I'm not interested in any way of jeopardizing his long-term well-being," Cowboys owner Jerry Jones said. "If you think I'm sitting here pushing him to get back out there on behalf of winning a ballgame, the answer is no."

Cunningham's thumb on his throwing hand was hurt when he hit it against a defender. Yesterday, while squeezing a glob of Silly Putty borrowed from his kids, he said he expects to be ready.

Jones said he's looking into bringing in another quarterback, preferably a veteran. He already plans to put Galloway on injured reserve and activate rookie quarterback Clint Stoerner off the practice squad. Galloway, incidentally, was Dallas' third quarterback.

Galloway's receiving skills made him the centerpiece of the Cowboys' off-season overhaul of its offense. To get him, Jones gave Seattle two first-round draft picks and signed the speedster to a $ 42 million, seven-year contract.

Galloway caught four passes for 62 yards in the opener, including a 4-yarder in the fourth quarter for the Cowboys' lone touchdown. He was hurt on Dallas' third-to-last snap. Jones said he had no problem with Galloway still being in the game, even though running back Emmitt Smith was long gone.

Galloway had a similar injury two games into his sophomore year at Ohio State in 1992.

"He's distressed about it, very down," Jones said. "But he basically told the doctor and told me there's no issue about rehabbing from this so he can play next year."

Aikman's health is much more of a concern. This is his third concussion in his past nine games and with each one comes the question of how many more can -- or will -- he take.

The cumulative effect of concussions has ended many careers, most recently Steve Young. A generation earlier, Roger Staubach called it quits early.

"I think Troy is astute enough, smart enough to know when he can get on the field and perform at his best and whether he's at risk," Smith said. "He's going to need all the professional advice he can get and make the decision for himself."

Last season, Aikman, 33, suffered a concussion early in a game in Indianapolis and returned. He played the next week and a relatively light blow gave him a second concussion. He sat out the next two weeks.

Jones said doctors consider the latest concussion less severe than the one against the Colts

."We knew we were going to have some adversity during the season," safety Darren Woodson said. "We didn't expect it to happen soon."

title: Boldin a great play; Cutler? Not so much
content:
A look at the upcoming weekend's fantasy football landscape:

Studs

*Anquan Boldin, WR, Baltimore Ravens: Considering how well quarterback Joe Flaccois playing (seven touchdowns, no interceptions in his last three games), Boldin should enjoy a solid night against the Atlanta Falcons' 26th-ranked pass defense.

*Jamaal Charles, RB, Kansas City Chiefs: The Chiefs take their NFL-best rushing game into Denver, where they ran for 317 yards against the Broncos in the 2009 regular-season finale. Charles, banged up last weekend against the Oakland Raiders, had 259 of those yards with two TDs, and the Broncos are still miserable against the run, ranking 31st.

*Chris Cooley, TE, Washington Redskins: Covering tight ends has not been the forte of the Philadelphia Eagles linebackers. Cooley scored in the first meeting this season, and the Eagles allowed Indianapolis Colts TE Jacob Tamme to catch 11 passes for 108 yards and a TD last weekend.

*Braylon Edwards, WR, New York Jets: Edwards has 25 catches but has scored on five of them and is averaging 18.1 yards a reception. He's also fired up to be back in Cleveland facing his old team, the Browns, so if you're usually on the fence on Edwards, stick him in this week.

*Rashard Mendenhall, RB, Pittsburgh Steelers: The Browns' Peyton Hillis manhandled the New England Patriots rushing defense for 184 yards. Now, it is the Steelers' turn. And even with injuries on the offensive line, Mendenhall is strong enough to have a big game.

Duds

*Brent Celek, TE, Eagles: He has struggled to be a factor, but Sunday's shutout against the Colts was terrible. In Michael Vick's three full games as the starting quarterback, Celek has totaled 69 yards.

*Jay Cutler, QB, Chicago Bears: Cutler quieted critics with Sunday's two-TD game and he did throw for four scores last year against the Minnesota Vikings. But Cutler faces a big problem in the Vikings, who cranked up their pass rush against the Arizona Cardinals in Week 9 -- posting six sacks. That kind of pressure will riddle Chicago's offensive line, which has given up an NFL-worst 32 sacks.

*Felix Jones, RB, Dallas Cowboys: So many things about Dallas' trip to face the division-rival New York Giants scream mismatch, including the Cowboys' inability to run. Ever since Jones' 100-yard game in Week 5, his carries have declined to last weekend's five rushes for 16 yards. Keep him on your bench until he gives you a reason to do otherwise.

*Chad Ochocinco, WR, Cincinnati Bengals: At some point, maybe the Bengals will return to making Ochocinco the team's No. 1 receiver. But right now, it's Terrell Owens who leads the NFL in targets (100). Ochocinco, meanwhile, is catching 53% of the passes thrown his way for a modest 11.8 yards a catch.

*Steve Smith, WR, Carolina Panthers: Smith hasn't scored since Week 2 and had one catch for 9 yards in a 34-3 blowout loss to the New Orleans Saints last weekend. With rookie Jimmy Clausen again the starting quarterback, it is hard to imagine Smith being a relevant player the rest of the season.

Sleepers

*LeGarrette Blount, RB, Tampa Bay Buccaneers: Blount is a player you need to pick your spots with after he struggled to 46 yards Sunday after having a monster game in Week 8. This is one of those spots because the reeling Panthers can't control the ball on offense and just lost two linebackers to injury.

*Nate Burleson, WR, Detroit Lions: Detroit's passing game should not miss a beat as Shaun Hill takes over for injured Matthew Stafford at quarterback. Burleson has three touchdown receptions in his last four games and 20 catches over the last three.

*David Garrard, QB, Jacksonville Jaguars: The Houston Texans pass defense is getting worse by the week. The Texans are last in passing yards and passing touchdowns allowed. In his last game, Garrard dissected the Cowboys for five scores (four passing, one rushing).

*Marshawn Lynch, RB, Seattle Seahawks: Lynch has not had much luck, with an average of 47 yards at 3.1 yards a clip in four games since being traded to the Seahawks. But Seattle needs to stick to the run because its passing game is a mess and Arizona's rushing defense is ranked 28th. In addition, the Seahawks expect to get left tackle Russell Okung back from an ankle injury.

*Blair White, WR, Colts: White had a good game earlier at Denver, and he added 42 yards last weekend against the Eagles. With the Colts' Anthony Gonzalez and Austin Collie out, White moves up to the No. 3 receiver and could see a season high in targets against Cincinnati.

title: Sports Briefs
content:
Rivera talks with Cards, 2 to go

LAKE FOREST, Ill. - Chicago Bears defensive coordinator Ron Rivera has interviewed for the Arizona Cardinals' head coaching vacancy and is expected to meet with officials of the Atlanta Falcons and Miami Dolphins.

It's the second straight year Rivera has gone through a round of interviews. A former player with Chicago and assistant coach with the Eagles, he's helped shape the Bears into one of the league' best defenses.

"If anything, just the fact that I've been recognized that I have that type of potential to be a head coach, I think that's kind of exciting," Rivera said.

Raiders' Shell resigns

ALAMEDA, Calif. - Art Shell's second stint as coach of the Raiders ended after just one season, the franchise's worst in more than four decades.

Shell met with owner Al Davis on Thursday to discuss the future of the team following a 2-14 season that was the worst for Oakland since 1962. The team issued a statement after the meeting saying Shell would not return as coach for the final season of his contract

Jets QB honored

HEMPSTEAD, N.Y. - Chad Pennington spent two nerve-racking weeks at home in Tennessee last fall wondering if his right shoulder would ever be the same.

The New York Jets quarterback overcame a second torn rotator cuff in as many years - and incredible odds - to win The Associated Press NFL Comeback Player of the Year award Thursday.

The seven-year veteran has led the Jets to a surprising 10-6 record and a wild-card berth. He finished second in the AFC with a 95.7 passer rating and threw for a career-high 3,352 yards.

Chiefs' Green practices

KANSAS CITY, Mo. - Kansas City Chiefs quarterback Trent Green went through a full practice on Thursday and said he would be ready on Saturday.

Green, who missed eight games earlier this year with a severe concussion, injured his left ankle on Sunday in Kansas City's playoff-clinching victory over Jacksonville. The Chiefs play at Indianapolis on Saturday.

"I practiced today and yesterday was limited. I feel good," said Green, who looked a bit haggard.

Singh in 5-way tie

KAPALUA, Hawaii - Every player walked off the 18th green Thursday and deposited an autographed golf ball into the new FedExCup trophy that will be awarded at the end of the season.

Vijay Singh wants the trophy they hand out Sunday in the Mercedes-Benz Championship.

Convinced that he has figured out his flaws, determined not to let another opportunity on Maui slip away, Singh survived the raging trade wind at Kapalua to make six birdies for a 4-under 69, leaving him in a five-way tie for the lead as the PGA Tour season began in strenuous conditions.

Longtime Illini PA man to lose job this fall

CHAMPAIGN, Ill. - For 22 years, Jim Sheppard's deep, hearty voice has punctuated the ups and downs of University of Illinois basketball and football as the public address announcer.

But this week, Sheppard was told by the university that his run will end after the 2007 football season.

"I had no idea after 22 years and no feedback from the university that I wasn't doing a good job," Sheppard said Thursday. "I understand these decisions were made and there's really not much I can do about it."

Kent Brown, Illinois' sports information director, said the school decided to "go a different direction."

Chelios out after 2 slain at his eatery

DETROIT - Detroit Red Wings defenseman Chris Chelios said Thursday that he will be off the ice indefinitely while he helps employees of his sports bars cope with the stabbing deaths of two co-workers.

Chelios spoke to one of the victims minutes before she was stabbed to death Tuesday by a recently fired employee who later confessed to killing her and a co-worker at the eatery, according to a police report.

He said the deaths of 49-year-old Megan Soroka and 52-year-old Mark Barnard were "senseless ... and there's no explanation."

Chelios was on the phone with Soroka minutes before her death and said she thought she heard a fight and was going to investigate, according to the police report.

Blues ice Blackhawks

ST. LOUIS - Lee Stempniak and Dennis Wideman scored power-play goals to lead the St. Louis Blues to a 2-0 victory over the Chicago Blackhawks on Thursday night.

title: Gruff, old-school coach takes over Vikings defense Linebackers coach Fred Pagac, who played and coached for Woody Hayes at Ohio State, values toughness, discipline -- and his granddaughters.
content:
Fred Pagac is relishing his opportunity as the Vikings' de facto defensive coordinator for the final six games of the season. But he has seen far too much in 33 years of coaching football to put too much stock in what this means.

"It's fun, it's a great situation," Pagac said Thursday. "But basically, just like everybody else on our staff, it's an audition right now. Everybody is on an interim basis. ... All we're trying to do is just win games. Stay focused, try to win the Buffalo game [on Sunday] and go from there. Then if something happens, it happens."

Pagac, who became the linebackers coach in 2006 after Brad Childress was hired to lead the Vikings, continues to work with that position group and hasn't been given a title change. But Pagac has assumed many of the duties Leslie Frazier held as defensive coordinator before he took over as interim coach nearly two weeks ago when Childress was fired.

While Frazier has gone out of his way to make sure everyone knows that defensive line coach Karl Dunbar and defensive backs coach Joe Woods have been given plenty of responsibility, Pagac is the guy making the calls on game day and running the meetings.

Frazier retains plenty of say in the operation, but he admitted Pagac called a few more blitzes than usual in the Vikings' 17-13 victory last Sunday at Washington. The Vikings sacked Redskins quarterback Donovan McNabb four times. "That was the most fun I've had in 10 years," Pagac said. "We all worked together, and the cool thing was we won the game."

Said Frazier: "[Pagac] was great. He handled every situation just like we had talked about in practice and the way we talked about in meetings. Sometimes you wonder if that's going to be the case once the bullets start flying, but he didn't flinch. Even after that first drive [when Washington scored a touchdown], he handled it the way he needed to and the players responded based on the way he handled that situation."

Frazier is from the Tony Dungy school of coaching: cool, calm and collected. If Frazier is upset, the look he gives often speaks louder than any words. Pagac, on the other hand, is a gruff football lifer who played for Woody Hayes at Ohio State, began his coaching career under him and has a penchant for drinking coffee and chewing tobacco. Pagac has implored more than one of his linebackers to "blow up a fullback," adding a few adjectives not fit for a family newspaper.

Pagac admits to being a softie around his granddaughters, ages 4 and 1 1/2, but little else changes his demeanor. "I like to think of myself as an old-school coach," he said. "I like to play basic fundamentals, I like to play aggressive and want guys to be physical. If that's old school, then I'm old school. If it isn't, I don't know what I am then."

Pagac would like to permanently be a defensive coordinator, and he would welcome that opportunity in Minnesota. He was in the running for the Vikings job before Frazier got it in 2007, after Mike Tomlin left to become coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers. Pagac also was a finalist for the Ohio State head coaching job in 2001 but the position went to Jim Tressel, ending Pagac's 23-year stint as an assistant with the Buckeyes.

He was the defensive coordinator from 1996 to '99 before serving as assistant head coach to John Cooper in 2000. Pagac, who was a starting tight end for Ohio State from 1971 to '73, admits to this day that he thought at one point he would finish his coaching career in Columbus.

"I had had opportunities to go other places but I wasn't going to leave there," he said. "I was up for the job, it came down to myself, Tressel and [former Gophers coach Glen] Mason, and Tress got it. I didn't, and so it was time to move on."

Pagac decided his best move would be to the NFL. He spent three seasons as the Oakland Raiders linebackers coach -- the team made the Super Bowl in his second year (2002) -- and then held the same job in Kansas City for two years.

Pagac said Hayes left the most memorable impression on him of any coach he has worked with -- "Toughness, discipline, he cared for the players and loved football," Pagac said -- and perhaps that's why Pagac didn't try to adjust his coaching style when he moved from Ohio State to the NFL.

"To be honest with you, I coach just like I did when I was in college ball," he said. "When I went to Oakland my first year, my first set of [linebackers] had 33 years experience. [Greg] Biekert, Elijah Alexander and Willie Thomas. We were doing duck walks, tackling drills, the whole bit. I think you have to adapt as you go. The 30 years I've been coaching, [I've] adapted to things but it's been more the way football has changed. But as far as coaching style, I'm me."
