title: Paul Domowitch | Will wanted man Vick still have skills?
content:
IF MICHAEL VICK wants a shot at playing in the National Football League again after he's finished making license plates, there's a pretty good chance that somebody will give him the opportunity.

Certainly not at the crazy-money numbers that he got from the

Atlanta

Falcons in 2004 when they foolishly signed the still-unfinished quarterback to a 10-year, $130 million contract extension. But hey, even a 1-year deal at the

veteran minimum would help

defray the cost of a few of his

lawyers' billable hours.

The bigger question is whether Vick will be able to make an NFL team when he gets out of the slammer.

He received a 23-month sentence yesterday from U.S. District Judge Henry E. Hudson for his involvement in a Virginia

dogfighting operation.

He already has spent a month in jail and could get his 23-month term reduced by a couple of months for good behavior. That means he could be released from prison as early as June or July of 2009. But there still will be the NFL to deal with after that. Commissioner Roger Goodell suspended Vick indefinitely in August after he pleaded guilty to the dogfighting chargers. He won't make a determination on whether to extend Vick's suspension beyond his prison term until after Vick is released.

So, the earliest Vick might be able to play football again is 2009. Probably a more likely timetable is 2010. That's 3 years away from the game. That's a lifetime for an athlete.

"I imagine that somebody will give him a look when he gets out," an NFC player personnel

director said. "But how well will he play? How much will his skills have deteriorated? I mean, this is a guy who already was behind the curve as far as his development as a quarterback.

"He'll only be 29 or 30 when he gets out. But trying to gauge the toll being in prison for 2 years and being away from the game for 3 will have on him, that's hard to say."

An AFC GM put the chances of Vick playing in the league again at "slim to none."

"You've seen how hard it is for a running back like Ricky Williams to sit out for as long as he sat out and try to come back," he said. "The body ages. The time away does irreversible damage to your skills.

"There have been guys who have sat out a year and come back. But 2 years? Three years? There's not a lot of those stories out there. That's a long time. Especially when you've spent most of it in prison.

"A guy in a normal situation can throw a thousand balls a day for the next 2-3 years and at least stay sharp mechanically. Vick's not going to get that opportunity in prison."

His age isn't a problem. Vick will only be 29 when he gets out. There are plenty of quarterbacks in the league playing well into their 30s, or even 40s. Vinny Testaverde is 44. Brad Johnson is 39. Brett Favre is 38. Jeff Garcia will turn 38 in February. Kurt Warner is 36 and Trent Dilfer is 35.

The problem for Vick is that he's not a traditional pocket passer. His strength always has been his speed, mobility and arm strength. Last year, he became the first quarterback in NFL history to rush for 1,000 yards. His career yards-per-carry average (7.3) actually is higher than his yards-per-pass-attempt average (6.7). His career completion

rate is just .54.0 percent.

"[The layoff] absolutely is going to have a more profound effect on a guy with his style of play than say, a Testaverde," the NFC personnel man said. "His athletic ability, his ability to run, his ability to create, certainly that won't be what it was in 3 years.

"Quarterbacks who are almost exclusively passers, they're like veteran baseball pitchers who learn to adapt after they lose a couple of miles off of their fastball. They learn to paint the corners. They might not have their fastball anymore, but they've got great control of their pitches. That's not Michael Vick. So that will be a little bit of a dilemma for him."

One possibility for Vick, if he really wants to play again, might be the Canadian Football League. Go up there for a year, sharpen his skills and prove to NFL teams that he can still play.

"That would be a great option for him," the AFC GM said. "Even if it's just for a while. To show that you still have it, if nothing else. Play a year or so there and then come back. Would he be willing to do it? I don't know."

Vick still is technically under contract to the Falcons. That's only because they're still trying to recoup $20 million in bonus money as part of the '04 extension.

They're awaiting an arbitration ruling on that. After that, he will be history. The Falcons want to put as much distance as possible between themselves and the man who once was the face of the franchise. When he gets out of prison in 2009, they won't invite him back.

Falcons owner Arthur Blank, in an interview broadcast over the team's Web site, said: "I would never use the word 'never.' I would say there's always a chance. But quite candidly, we as an organization, as a football team, we have to look forward. We have to go forward assuming Michael will not be back."

But somebody out there will take a flier on him. If not as a quarterback, then possibly as a running back or even a return man.

He'll get a chance somewhere. Whether he'll be able to do anything with that chance, well, that remains to be seen. *

Send e-mail to pdomo@aol.com

title: NFC NOTEBOOK: Vikings present different problems
content:
The Giants know who and what they are, and they have a sense of humor about it.

They're 13-4, yet not even favored at home in Sunday's NFC Championship Game, even though they'll be playing a team with a defense that was ranked 28th in the NFL during the regular season.

That's OK with cornerback Jason Sehorn, linebacker Jessie Armstead and their teammates. They just keep winning, six times in a row now, and somehow forcing opponents into bungling.CONSTITUTION

It's no secret that Sunday's opponent, the Vikings (12-5), will be a step up in class. Certainly, wide receivers Randy Moss and Cris Carter, quarterback Daunte Culpepper, running back Robert Smith and center Matt Birk, all Pro-Bowlers, have the potential to wreak havoc.

Trying to keep Eagles quarterback Donovan McNabb from throwing to Torrance Small and Charles Johnson, while slowing Chris Warren the running back, is a bit different from trying to slow the combination of Culpepper to Moss and Carter, with Smith sprinkled in.

''The difference is, Culpepper has got two Pro Bowl receivers to throw to, '' Armstead said. ''If McNabb had two Pro Bowl receivers to throw to, then the sky would be the limit for him. This guy, Culpepper, has two guys who can change the outcome of any game. We're going to have to do a lot of studying and preparing to handle them.''

Sehorn, whose interception and 32-yard return for a touchdown in the second quarter Sunday will be remembered as one of the great plays in any NFL game, is into self-deprecation. He knows he and fellow cornerback Dave Thomas will be taxed.

''I don't think anybody can cover (Moss). I just have to hope I get some help,'' Sehorn said. "He is just better than people. It's not that he does anything that is any different. He's just plain and simple better than people.

"All I know is I have to show up on Sunday and put on my uniform, or I won't get paid."

Got to do better

Giants quarterback Kerry Collins, who will play in his second championship game, knows something has to get better if his team is to beat Minnesota. The Giants lost three fumbles, had a punt blocked that led to a Philadelphia touchdown, averaged 2.6 yards per rush and passed for just 125 yards. Against a Vikings team that figures to score more than 10 points, that won't do unless weather conditions happen to leave the Minnesota attack limp. ''We were sloppy with the ball, and that's the kind of thing that loses a game for you,'' Collins said.

Interesting story

Vikings cornerback Wasswa Serwanga is expected to earn his third NFL start, in front of fellow second-year pro Kenny Wright. Serwanga was undrafted out of UCLA after playing before that at Sacramento State and Pacific. The Bears took a chance on him, and then he was with the 49ers last year. That's not the half of it. Serwanga and his family fled their native Uganda in 1980 because of a civil war, leaving behind almost everything they owned.

He has a very different perspective. Early this season, when he was inactive for the first seven games, Serwanga felt guilty. "I was getting a (pay)check," he said. "But I was like, 'I'm not doing anything to earn this check.' "

title: Performance on Moss earns Panthers raves
content:
Know what the hottest film of the fall season is going to be? Around the NFL, it's going to be the one of Carolina's 24-13 upset of Minnesota.

The film contains never before seen footage of how to stop Vikings receiver Randy Moss (one catch for 28 yards), which ought to make it a must-see flick for every defensive coordinator from here to Seattle.

Basically, the Panthers double- and sometimes triple-covered Moss, daring Minnesota quarterback Daunte Culpepper to try to force the ball his way. Culpepper obliged and the Panthers made him pay, picking off three passes, two in the red zone.FRY.11

"A couple of times I tried to force the ball down the field," Culpepper said. "I have to hit the other guys and then come back to Randy. Our motto on offense is take what they give you, and I kind of got beside myself trying to get downfield."

Culpepper is not likely to make the same mistakes again, but you have to figure other teams will at least consider trying to take away Moss and dare someone else to beat them.

Of more concern to the Vikings should be the impact this loss might have on their season. After all, Minnesota has the toughest schedule in the league. Beating Carolina was supposed to be one of its few gimmes.

Instead, the Vikings already are swimming upstream at 0-1. Up next are none other than the defending Super Bowl champion Baltimore Ravens on the road. If the Vikings thought being 0-1 was bad, imagine how they'll feel if they fall to 0-2.

MAYBE HE DIDN'T WANT TO GET HIS HANDS DIRTY: The wimpy tackle of the year so far has to go to former Bucs quarterback Vinny Testaverde. It was embarrassing enough that his attempted handoff wound up in the hands of Colts defensive end Chukie Nwokorie, who ran 95 yards for the touchdown. What was worse was Testaverde's lame attempts to try to tackle Nwokorie.

You have to believe Testaverde got plenty of ribbing about that during the Jets' film session Monday.

DID YOU KNOW?: It may not have been a good opening day for the six new head coaches in the league (they all lost their debuts), but it was a pretty decent day for quarterbacks with new teams. They went a combined 6-3.

DID YOU KNOW II?: Among the seven-man crew of replacement officials who worked Sunday night's nationally televised Miami-Tennessee game was Larry Upson, the league's director of officiating who was the back judge, and Casey Moreland, the umpire, who worked a high school game two nights earlier.

QUOTE OF THE DAY: Bears rookie receiver David Terrell, who obviously took one too many shots to the head in Baltimore's 17-6 win over Chicago: "They might be the world champions, but maybe we're better."

HURTIN' IN BEANTOWN: You had to figure New England, which went 5-11 last season, was going to be bad, but a loss to Cincinnati?

Frustration isn't even the word for what the Patriots are feeling.

"We can't let this happen again," defensive end Bobby Hamilton said. "No way, dawg. Nah, dawg. We ain't gonna let this happen this year. We've got too many guys in this room who won't let last year happen again."

WHAT WOULD YOU CALL IT?: Moss wouldn't admit that the Panthers shut him down. "I don't get shut down," he told reporters after the game.

Okay, Randy. What do you think it means when you're held to one catch for 28 yards?

"I don't know what it means," he said, "but I don't get shut down."

Sure, you don't, Randy. Sure, you don't.

- Information from other news organizations was used in this report.

title: Paul Domowitch: NFL didn't change rules on illegal hits, just enforcement
content:
EVERY FEW YEARS or so, after a particularly vicious hit or two gets national attention, the suits that run the NFL love to grab a bullhorn and announce that they're going to crack down on unnecessary violence in their very violent, $9 billion-a-year game.

That almost always is followed by a chorus of boos from defensive players who accuse the suits of trying to turn the game into - to borrow a term from the drill sergeant-turned-therapist in the Geico commercial - mambypambyland.

So it was again this week after the league handed out $175,000 in fines to Steelers linebacker James Harrison, Patriots safety Brandon Meriweather and Falcons cornerback Dunta Robinson.

The league said it will be "enforcing discipline at a higher level" for illegal hits on defenseless players. And defensive players suggested that this could mean the end of football - and quite possibly civilization - as we know it.

"That's what it's starting to feel like," Eagles safety Quintin Mikell said. "Maybe we should go out there and play seven-on-seven and let the receivers run around and catch the ball and just grab a flag or something. It's frustrating. But those are the rules."

"It's a physical game," said Kurt Coleman, the Eagles' rookie safety. "That's why we love it. That's why America loves football. It's a man's sport. It's tough to say you can't hit somebody as hard, or you can't hit them in a certain way."

Actually, the NFL didn't say that. Contrary to many erroneous media reports, the league didn't change any of its rules this week regarding hits on defenseless players. What Coleman and Mikell could do last week to Falcons receivers, they still can do this week to Titans receivers.

The only thing really newsworthy that the league said is that it might start handing out suspensions to players for illegal hits. And even that's not really news. It has suspended players before for that.

In 2002, safeties Rodney Harrison (Chargers) and Kenoy Kennedy (Broncos) both got one-game suspensions for helmet-to-helmet hits, Harrison's for a lick on Hall of Fame wide receiver Jerry Rice.

For a while now, defensive players have been prohibited from hitting a defenseless receiver in the head or neck area with their helmet, facemask, shoulder or forearm. The only thing that changed earlier this year was what constituted a defenseless receiver.

In March, the league's owners approved a competition committee proposal that made it illegal to hit a receiver in the head or neck area until he completed a catch and had an opportunity to protect himself.

"We have tried to draw a very, very bright line as far as [what is] a defenseless player, and how to go about initiating contact," said Titans coach Jeff Fisher, who co-chairs the competition committee along with Falcons president Rich McKay.

"We have a responsibility to protect our players. I think the potential for suspension will get some people's attention. There's a misunderstanding here. People have come out and said this is a violent game. It's a physical game.

"It's not going to take away any of the aggressiveness. Basically, there are [hitting and tackling] techniques being used that are unacceptable. They create a situation where a player's safety is in jeopardy. The league office isn't saying don't run hard, don't hit hard, don't tackle hard. They're saying don't use these [illegal] tactics."

Robinson was fined $50,000 for his hit on the Eagles' DeSean Jackson. Good luck finding a player in the league, including Jackson, who agrees with the league's determination that the hit was illegal. At least $50,000 worth of illegal.

"It's football," said Coleman. "A guy [Jackson] came across [and he hit him]. I thought it was a clean hit. I don't think he led with his head. It was just where he hit him. He kind of hit him under his chin and it knocked his head back. It's tough. It's tough.

"There's an intimidation factor. If a wide receiver or running back or whoever it may be isn't intimidated to come across, it's easy for them. They're not going to be scared. But if you have the intimidation factor, they'll drop balls. That's the name of the game."

STILL LOOKING FOR A RINGSunday's game in Nashville will pit the league's two longest-tenured coaches against each other. Jeff Fisher is in his 16th full season at the helm of the Titans. Andy Reid is in his 12th year with the Eagles.

In a league that generally sees a half-dozen coaches replaced each year, it's amazing that Fisher and Reid have lasted as long as they have, particularly since neither of them has managed to win a Super Bowl yet. Fourteen postseason appearances between them, including two trips to The Big Game. But no ring.

"You have to credit ownership," Fisher said. "In my case, Mr. [Bud] Adams has been a great owner and understands this game and understands the importance of continuity and stability. I think that's the case also with the Luries."

Bill Cowher holds the record for the longest stay in one place before finally winning a Super Bowl. Didn't get a ring until his 14th season with the Steelers. Probably wouldn't have gotten the opportunity to coach that long without winning a Super Bowl in more than a handful of NFL locations.

Reid's failure to win a Super Bowl the last 11 years is a daily topic of conversation in Philadelphia, and will be until he either finally wins one or is fired. Nashville being Nashville, Fisher hasn't faced quite the same kind of public scrutiny that Reid has in Philly.

Does he dwell much on the fact that he still doesn't have a ring?

"Every day," he said earlier this week in a conference call with the Philadelphia media. "That's my job. That's why Mr. Adams hired me. That's why I come to work. That's the goal. Immediately, as we're talking right now, the focus is on the next opponent. But that's why we were hired."

TITANS' EX-EAGLE FACTOROne team's garbage often turns out to be another team's gold bullion. Case in point: defensive end Jason Babin and linebacker Will Witherspoon.

The Eagles had no use for either Babin or Witherspoon after last season, releasing Witherspoon and opting not to re-sign Babin, who was an unrestricted free agent.

Now, both are key contributors to a Tennessee Titans defense that could be a bear for Kevin Kolb and the Eagles offense to deal with Sunday.

Babin and Witherspoon have combined for nine of the Titans' league-best 24 sacks. Babin, who is tied for fourth in the league in sacks with six, and Witherspoon are part of a unit that is fourth in the league points allowed.

The Eagles' decision to release Witherspoon was largely financial. He was scheduled to make $5 million in salary this year, which is a lot of money for a guy they weren't even sure would be starting for them if they kept him around.

The decision not to hang on to Babin was a little less clear. He was cheap and played pretty well in a limited role as a pass-rush specialist last year. He ended up signing a 1-year, $1 million deal with the Titans.

"We felt like both of them would fit in to our scheme," Fisher said. "Will, in particular, is benefiting from having an offseason and a training camp in our defense as compared to the situation he had there [he was acquired from the Rams in a trade in mid-October].

"Jason is just really having fun. We edge him. We keep him out wide. And he's off to a great start. And he's benefited from some good players around him."

Andy Reid said he's not surprised by the success Babin and Witherspoon are having with the Titans.

"I have a lot of respect for both of those guys," he said. "Babin is a high-motor, high-energy [guy]. I really enjoyed him as a person and as a player. And the same with [Witherspoon]. He's very intelligent, flies around.

"They fit perfect in that defense and what they're asking them to do. And I'm happy for both of them."

2-MINUTE DRILLFROM THE LIP (Cowboys version):

* "If you whine and cry about the past and what's happened, you look back instead of looking forward." - Cowboys coach Wade Phillips on his team's 1-4 start.

* "The most talented teams ain't won the Super Bowl since I've been in the league. It's the ones that play well most of the time and get a few breaks. I mean, right now, we have the worst luck in football. As a human, you're saying, 'Man, can we get a break?' You know what I'm saying? But sometimes you have to create your own luck, man." - Cowboys defensive end Marcus Spears.

* "A lot of people say the old saying, 'You are what you are' when you look at 1-4. We are not that. Our team is doing some very good things." - Cowboys owner Jerry Jones.

* "None of us here thought we were going to be in this situation. You don't work as hard as we do and commit to every day doing the things we do to be like this. It's not fun. It's not enjoyable, and you have to figure out a way to win." - Cowboys quarterback Tony Romo.

* "I can't believe they're 1-4 with all the talent they've got on that team. Something's not right there, but we definitely know they're still a very good football team. If we can go there and put them to 1-5 and us go to 5-2, that's going to be very beneficial when it gets close to December and January." - Giants tight end Kevin Boss, whose team plays the Cowboys Monday night.

BY THE NUMBERS:

* The Eagles have intercepted at least one pass in nine straight regular-season games dating back to last season.

* The Eagles have lost six of their last nine games in the week before their bye. They are 11-0 under Andy Reid the week after the bye.

* Jets running back LaDainian Tomlinson has 38 career games with at least two rushing touchdowns. That's the most in NFL history. Emmitt Smith is second with 36.

* Six weeks into the season, there have been just two games between NFC East teams. The Redskins and Cowboys played in Week 1 and the Redskins and Eagles faced each other in Week 4.

* Packers QB Aaron Rodgers is 1-11 in games decided by four points or fewer. That lone win came 2 weeks ago - a 28-26 victory over the Lions.

* Chiefs linebacker Mike Vrabel notched his 10th career touchdown reception last week against Houston. He's the first player who began his career after 1933 to have at least 10 career receptions and have them all be for touchdowns.

THUMBS UPTo the NFL owners and players, who interrupted their stalemate over how to slice up their $9 billion-a-year revenue pie this week long enough to do something good for former players stricken with ALS. They agreed to expand benefits provided under the "88 Plan" to include reimbursement of expenses to qualifying players who are suffering from this horrible disease. The "88 Plan," named after Hall of Fame tight end John Mackey, was developed in 2006 to provide coverage to former players diagnosed with dementia. Eligible players with ALS will receive the same financial benefits - up to $88,000 per year for institutional care or up to $50,000 for home custodial care plus costs for certain physician services, medical equipment and medication. To date, the "88 Plan" has awarded $9.7 million toward the care of 132 former players.

THUMBS DOWNTo former Raiders quarterback JaMarcus Russell for one of the lamest mea culpas by an athlete in recent memory. In an interview with Josina Anderson on Showtime's "Inside the NFL," Russell, perhaps the biggest draft bust in NFL history, acknowledged that he made a lot of mistakes after being selected by the Raiders with the first overall pick in the 2007 draft, and that he's really sorry for those mistakes. But he, uh, isn't sure what those mistakes were. "I don't know where it went wrong," he said. "But for the things that did go wrong, I take full blame for whatever was my fault and the things that did happen." Now, that's some big-time accountability right there.

title: Smith wants Bears prepared for playoff run Coach has decisions to make about resting
content:
Coach Lovie Smith's plan for the final two weeks of the regular season deviates a bit from the one he used to get the Chicago Bears ready for last year's playoffs.

It comes down to a decision between resting the starters and risking rust or playing the regulars and risking injury when his team already has home field advantage wrapped up.

Chicago didn't clinch the NFC North until the next-to-last game a year ago. The Bears then rested most of their starters in the regular-season finale and lost to the Minnesota Vikings. And after a first-round bye, they were beaten badly by Carolina in the playoffs.

Chicago clinched the division earlier this season, on Dec. 3, and now has remaining games against Detroit and Green Bay.

"This is how we will play them: Our plan is to try to win the football game the best way we can. If a player has an injury and he isn't 100 percent ready to go, we won't play him. If a player is ready to go, we're going try to get a win," Smith said Wednesday.

"As far as last year, we kept out a few players. But last year at the end of the season we had a lot of players banged up and we didn't play some of the players based on that. We plan on playing our guys as much as we need to get a win."

Numerous Bears players were on the injury list Wednesday and whether some of them play Sunday in the meaningless game at Detroit might not be determined until the weekend.

Questionable on the list are running back Thomas Jones, defensive backs Nathan Vasher and Charles Tillman and tackle John Tait. Jones, Tillman, Tait, guard Ruben Brown and linebacker Hunter Hillenmeyer all sat out practice. Brown and Hillenmeyer are listed as probable.

Vasher, who's missed two games with a hamstring injury, did practice and hopes to return.

And quarterback Rex Grossman is happy he'll have the opportunity to stay fresh, especially since he's picked up his play the last two games.

"I'd love to play the whole game these next two weeks and I think that is going to be mostly the plan, just to keep getting better and build momentum going into the playoffs," Grossman said.

"I think we're going to do everything we possibly can to be ready for that first playoff game. I think last year will help me and help a lot of the other guys who have never been in that situation. The approach we are all going to take is going to be a little bit better."

title: NO HEADLINE
content:
*It has been a long time since the Steelers blew a game the way they did Sunday, when they lost a 10-point lead with 2 1/2 minutes left to the Philadelphia Eagles, who won in overtime, 26-23. According to two men who have seen many games in Three Rivers Stadium, it has been 31 years. Andy Russell, a former Steelers linebacker who played from 1963-76, was one of the honorary co-captains for Sunday's game. He watched the game from the owner's box, where he sat with Hall of Famer Chuck Noll, who coached the Steelers from 1969-91. "We lost 16 straight games [to end 1969 and start ' 70], and we lost games in some of the most amazing ways you could have thought of," Russell said. "I told Chuck, 'I don't remember a loss this bad since ' 69.' He looked at me and said, 'You're right.' " In Noll's first season, the Steelers went 1-13 in ' 69.

*S Scott Shields will have surgery on a thumb and will miss at least Sunday's game against Jacksonville. The injury occurred on a special teams play. Coach Bill Cowher also listed C Dermonti Dawson (hamstring) and RB Chris Fuamatu-Ma'afala (broken foot) as out for this week's game but said both were coming along fine. The rest of the injury report will list the following as probable: RB Jerome Bettis (thumb), OL Wayne Gandy (shoulder), WR Courtney Hawkins (calf), DL Kevin Henry (groin), TE Mark Bruener (rib), QB Kordell Stewart (knee), DL Jeremy Staat (shoulder), OL Rich Tylski (knee) and DL Kimo von Oelhofen (knee).

*Cowher said LT Wayne Gandy is having "a super year" despite his injuries. Gandy has not allowed a sack in 10 games. "He did a great job against Hugh Douglas," Cowher said of the NFL's leading sacker who had no tackles Sunday. "He definitely got over Hugh Douglas. Now he has Tony Brackens [of Jacksonville]. It doesn't get easier, but he has had a Pro Bowl type of year. He has played pretty good rushers. I can't say enough about him. He is having a great year."

*Cowher praised the blocking of rookie FB Dan Kreider, who has played the past four games after being signed off their practice squad. "I have to single out Dan Kreider. He is playing really, really well right now."

*Cowher also said Kordell Stewart and the passing game "took a step forward a little bit, to be able to move the ball at the end of the first half [Sunday]. We are nowhere near where we need to be, but certainly I think we made some progress. We threw the ball with a little more confidence and made a couple plays up the field. So, I am hopeful that we continue to build from that." He said Stewart is playing with more confidence. "I would like to see him hit more opportunities that we have had. Hopefully, that will continue to come. He took some strides the other day. They may have been baby strides, but I think he did take some strides and hopefully will continue to do so." Stewart will make his fourth consecutive start at quarterback and his sixth this season. Kent Graham, who began the season as the No. 1 quarterback, has started the other five games.

*It still bugs Cowher that he called a blitz near the end of their 9-7 loss Nov. 5 in Tennessee that might have cost the Steelers a victory. The Titans, trailing by one, faced a fourth-and-8 on the Steelers' 42 with 2:12 left. Cowher ordered the blitz that left WR Derrick Mason covered man-to-man. QB Steve McNair found him for a first down, and Al Del Greco kicked the winning field goal with four seconds left. "I sit there and wish I had the call on fourth-and-8 back. I have to live with that one. That is just a call, and that is on me."

title: Football Standing Tall In Big Apple
content:
JETS, GIANTSDRAWING HEADLINES

By IRA KAUFMAN

ikaufman@tampatrib.com

TAMPA — Start spreading the news ...

While it would probably take a fistfight between Michael Strahan and Giantscoach Tom Coughlin to knock the Yankees-Red Sox series off the back page of the tabloids, the Big Apple's two NFL teams are causing quite a stir.

After combining for a 10-22 mark in 2003, the Giantsand Jets rank as two of the league's most intriguing October surprises.

The Jets have reversed last season's 0-4 start behind a well-balanced offense keyed by veteran running back Curtis Martin, who has generated a league-high 36 first downs.

Led by NFL rushing leader Tiki Barber and an opportunistic defense, the 4-1 Giantsalready have matched last year's victory total.

Ronde Barber's twin brother has overcome a penchant for fumbling to emerge as one of the league's elite players.

Tiki leads the NFL with 817 yards from scrimmage, the fifth-highest total in league history through five games.

"Tiki Barber is half man, half amazing," said Strahan, the veteran defensive end whose pass-rushing skills have helped the Giantsregister 15 takeaways and an NFL-best turnover ratio of plus-11. "He's definitely the heart and soul of what we try to do around here."

Giantsfans are starting to discover that behind Coughlin's martinet style lurks the instincts of a good football coach.

New York overhauled its offensive line, plugging in Jason Whittle at left guard after a trade with Tampa Bay and putting rookie Chris Snee at right guard.

Yes, the same Mr. Snee married to Coughlin's daughter.

With Kurt Warner providing stability at quarterback and Barber producing 163 yards per game, the Giantsalready have posted road wins against Green Bay and Dallas.

"I can't even put into words how bad last year was and how satisfying this is to get off to the type of start we're at," Giantsreceiver Amani Toomer said. "But it is just that, it's a start."

Since the end of the 1986 season, the Giantsand Jets have made the playoffs in the same year once (2002).

Jets fans have been pining for a Super Bowl berth since Joe Namath waved that index finger at the Orange Bowl after shocking the Baltimore Colts.

Madison Avenue hasn't asked Jets quarterback Chad Pennington to pose on any white llama rugs, but the low-profile Pennington is a far more accurate passer than Namath.

And Martin, seeking his 10th consecutive 1,000-yard season, is the key factor in an offense whose time of possession (33.23) trails only Tennessee.

Coach Herm Edwards, the former Bucs assistant who clashed with ownership at times last year, now finds himself defending a quick start against four opponents who have combined for a 4-14 record.

"We're not going to give any of them back and not going to feel sorry about being 4-0," Edwards said of wins against the Bengals, Chargers, Dolphins and Bills.

If the Jets beat the 49ers at home Sunday and New England defeats visiting Seattle, a tasty Week 7 matchup would feature the undefeated Jets visiting a Patriots team with a 20-game winning streak.

"Any other team we're playing after San Francisco, I'll tell you, they're not worrying about us," Pennington said, "so why should we worry about them?"

University of Miami rookie linebacker Jonathan Vilma is making an impact for new defensive coordinator Tim Lewis and the Jets are last in the league in penalties and penalty yardage.

While the Jets haven't entered the teeth of their schedule, the Giantsstill have difficult road games at Minnesota and Baltimore after this weekend's bye.

Players are finding it easier to buy into Coughlin's rigid system in the midst of a four-game winning streak.

"What he preaches is working," Barber said. "I knew I had to prove myself to Coach Coughlin. I had a long history with Coach [Jim] Fassel, but Coach Coughlin didn't know me from anyone. I had a lot to prove to him. I had a lot to prove to myself."

title:
FASCINATING;
GAME FOR BEN
content:
Ben Roethlisberger did something yesterday that he hasn't done since becoming the Steelers' quarterback four magical games ago.

He played stupid.

Somebody asked Roethlisberger if he was ready for his official NFL baptism. You might have heard by now that the New England Patriots will be at Heinz Field Sunday. The defending Super Bowl champions. Winners of a league-record 21 consecutive games. A fabulous team coached by Bill Belichick, one of the top defensive minds of all time, a reputation he has earned by tormenting quarterbacks in general, Steelers' quarterbacks in particular.

Roethlisberger's response was playfully dumb.

"Geez, what have these last games been?"

Roethlisberger knows better.

"They tell me that [Belichick] throws more at you than anybody out there."

That's why this game is so fascinating. Forget that a win would legitimize the Steelers. You can't mention their 5-1 record without pointing out their victories came against teams with a combined 10-23 record. A strong performance would legitimize Roethlisberger. It's not that a poor one will mean he is going to be a bad quarterback. We've seen enough in four games to know that won't be the case. But think what it will mean if he has the answers for all of the problems Belichick creates. Think how that could fast-track his career.

"All eyes aren't just going to be on me. They're going to be on our team," Roethlisberger said. "It's going to be a big challenge for us."

The kid seems unfazed by it all. He said he spent a relaxing weekend back at Miami, Ohio, watching his old team crush Central Florida. You might imagine the reception he received. Talk about a Big Man On Campus.

It's just a shame Belichick won't treat Roethlisberger with the same respect.

"The hardest thing about preparing for his team is that he has a different scheme for every opponent," Hines Ward said. "I remember before we played them in the AFC championship game [after the 2001 season], we spent the week studying what their defense did against the Raiders the game before. But when we played them, they didn't play any of those defenses. I don't think they played the same coverage against us twice in any series. We spent the whole game trying to adjust on the sideline. That's a hard way to win."

The Patriots sacked Kordell Stewart three times and intercepted him three times, twice on the Steelers' final two possessions. They broke your heart with a 24-17 win at Heinz Field that sent them to the Super Bowl and the first of Belichick's two championships in three years.

The result also was depressing when the Patriots beat the Steelers, 30-14, to open the 2002 season at New England. Stewart was sacked five times and threw three more interceptions, twice on the Steelers' first two possessions. The Steelers had just 7 points before Stewart scored on a 1-yard run on the game's final play.

You can say that's just Kordell, but the Patriots have been doing it to a lot of quarterbacks. They held the New York Jets' Chad Pennington to 162 passing yards and limited the Jets to 268 total yards in a 13-7 win Sunday. They gave up 349 passing yards to Seattle's Matt Hasselbeck a week earlier, but he had to throw 50 times because the Seahawks fell behind early, 20-3. They sacked Buffalo's Drew Bledsoe seven times. They even held Indianapolis' Peyton Manning to 256 passing yards in the season opener.

"I'm sure with Ben being a rookie, [Belichick] will have a special package for him," Ward said. "But we can't put it all on Ben's shoulders. We can't say, 'You've got to pass the football for us to win.' We can't shy away from running the football. That's what's got us here in the first place."

The Steelers couldn't run against the Patriots in those two losses. Jerome Bettis played in the AFC championship game for the first time after missing the previous five games with a groin injury and had eight carries for 9 yards. Still not healthy the next season, he had eight carries for 35 yards in the opener.

The Steelers think it will be different this time. They're confident the Patriots won't be able to stop Duce Staley, who had three 100-yard rushing days in the past four games and would have been a perfect 4 for 4 if not for a holding penalty on Ward that wiped out a 24-yard run against the Dallas Cowboys.

But even if Staley runs for big yards Sunday, Roethlisberger still must play well for the Steelers to win. There will come a time when he has to make a big throw. At the very least, he must protect the ball. He can't throw three interceptions.

"Every game is a challenge for me," Roethlisberger said. "It's only been four games. You can't get too excited yet."

There he went again, playing stupid.

Like anyone around here would ever believe it's too early to get excited.

title: This isn't a good time to be an NFL quarterback
content:
This isn't a good time

to be an NFL quarterback

The 2010 season might get pinpointed as the time when it no longer was safe, smart or cool to be an NFL quarterback. Consider:

*With 15 sacks in his last six quarters, Jay Cutler's brain might already be too scrambled to donate for concussion tests.

*Aaron Rodgershad a 300-yard game but still took five sacks one week after suffering a concussion and ran into a guy who looks like the Miami Dolphins' second coming of Jason Taylor. Unless the Green Bay Packers snap out of their funk, Rodgers will be guilty of not being who we thought he was.

*Brett Favre is going to have to answer whether he's a tasteless photographer.

*Ben Roethlisbergerhad a big first game back but knows the slightest bobble will ignite another round of wrath from Terry Bradshaw.

*There will never be a cloudless day for Michael Vick.

*Tony Romo should be glad Jerry Jonesis promising he won't fire Wade Phillips. If he did, the attention would turn from the failing coach to the quarterback who's throwing late-game picks.

*And even Tom Bradyis getting mocked by Justin Bieber for going with his Farrah Fawcett hairdo.-- Tom Weir

Forget polls and BCS;

tell us who is No. 1

You can believe the coaches.

You can believe the computers.

Or you can believe your eyes and tell us who is the best team in college football.

The coaches have it Oregon, Boise State, Oklahoma, TCU, Auburn, LSU, Alabama, Michigan State, Utah and Ohio State.

The computers spit it out this way: Oklahoma, Oregon, Boise State, Auburn, TCU, LSU, Michigan State, Alabama, Utah and Ohio State.

Not a huge difference, but enough to change the Bowl Championship Series title game if coaches picked the teams.

And Alabama and Ohio State still might be two of the best in the nation despite being seventh and 10th in the coaches poll and eighth and 10th in the BCS standings.

So we asked you: Who is your No. 1 college football team? Results through 5,968 votes:

Boise State 25%

Oregon 20%

Oklahoma 14%

Alabama or Ohio State 13%

Auburn 12%

Others 16%

-- Reid Cherner

title: Blinded By The Light?
content:
Even with his team 0-3 and looking older by the day, Bucs coach Jon Gruden remains ever the optimist. Perhaps he sees something that we can't.

I t's time for the couch, Jon boy.

We'll hope it was exhaustion, brought on by a late game followed by an all-night flight that made Jon Gruden observe Monday morning, "I think the future's so bright here I've got to wear shades."

With an outlook like that, he can always find work as a political spinmeister if this whole football thing doesn't work out. Gruden's Bucs are 0-3 after the 30-20 loss Sunday at Oakland, and that's just the tip of things.

Charlie Garner is lost for the season, which is piled on the loss of Joey Galloway.

Gruden's game of quarterback roulette landed three more lemons, as the Brad Johnson who tossed wobbling ducks against the Raiders looked a whole lot like last season's Brad Johnson. And the rebuilt offensive line couldn't open holes in a doughnut factory.

But the future's so bright, Jonny's scrambling for Ray-Bans?

An intervention, or least a reality check, is in order.

Thirteen games remain, but the Bucs are out of it. They will win only a handful of games this season, assuming there are at least a few teams on the schedule as dysfunctional as they are.

These Bucs are old, slow, can't run, pass or block.

The old players they brought in aren't doing the job, and the young ones who back them up must not be very good, because Gruden won't play them.

Oh, and Rich McKay's Atlanta Falcons are 3-0.

Lost Their Balance

It's just for fun that we mention the Falcons are undefeated while the Bucs can't win. Truth is, this team's problems go deeper than just a losing streak.

The Bucs have gone 7-12 since Gruden's lips went loose with some bar talk not long after they won the Super Bowl. You remember how Gruden essentially nagged McKay into leaving, then consolidated all the personnel power in himself and his sock puppet, Bruce Allen.

Gruden is a tremendous football coach when that's all he does, but that doesn't make him a top general manager. McKay was an important check and balance for Gruden — whether Gruden wanted to admit that or not. Not having McKay to say "no" to Gruden's ever-changing wants is how you give a four-year contract to the screen door on a submarine known as Todd Steussie.

The fans want changes, but the question begs: Change to what?

Team Geezer is set for now, and it is Gruden's creation. If he accepted the accolades that came with the Super Bowl win, he must now accept the jibes.

"There are a lot of games left. I think we've got a chance to be a heck of a football team if we can put it together," Gruden said.

Whatever.

Denial Is Bliss

You can't blame Gruden for his public face, I guess — as long as those really are shades and not blinders. No one expected him to stand up Monday and say, "You know something? We stink."

It'll be more interesting in the coming weeks to watch what Gruden does rather than listen to what he says. Does he finally concede that Johnson can no longer function with this cast of castoffs? Does he remember Kenyatta Walker is on the roster?

"We have had too many changes already," Gruden said. "I think that's our problem."

He's right about that. Problem is, many of those changes came before the season started. They didn't look good then, and they look even worse now.

Unless you're watching it through dark glasses.

Trust me, though. The view is better through a blindfold.

title: Quarterback Garcia unlikely to be solution for Eagles
content:
The Eagles are not looking to add a quarterback or a running back right now, a source close to the situation said yesterday.

That would seem to indicate that the team views the injuries to quarterback Michael Vick and running back LeSean McCoy as short term, no more than a few weeks.

If Vick's rib cartilage tear is going to keep him out more than a few weeks, the Eagles would seem to need a veteran backup quarterback to play behind Kevin Kolb. The only other quarterback on the roster is rookie Mike Kafka.

That has led to speculation that Jeff Garcia could be available for his third tour of duty with the Eagles. Garcia was brought in last year when Donovan McNabb was out for 2 weeks with a rib injury.

The potential complication this time is that Garcia is playing for the Omaha Nighthawks, of the United Football League, and playing quite well. Terms of his contract would require any NFL team interested in him to buy out his UFL contract by paying the Nighthawks $150,000 and then the move must be approved by the UFL.

Garcia, however, said any suggestion that he is returning to the Eagles, or the NFL right now, is just speculation.

"I know I signed a contract committing myself to the Omaha Nighthawks," Garcia told the Omaha World-Herald. "If there is a way out of it, I don't know the fine print. And it's not something I'm investigating right now.

"I don't even want to talk what-ifs. I've gotten myself in trouble in the past with what-ifs. If it becomes reality, it will be something that we have to think about and discuss. Until then, I'm not going to get distracted with outside talks."

Garcia is 42-for-75 for 509 yards, with five touchdowns and two interceptions, for the 2-0 Nighthawks.

"There are teams out there that have needs, and it's more than just Philadelphia," Garcia said. "Until one of those teams calls and actually talks to my agent, or calls here and talks to [Omaha general manager] Rick Mueller, it's just talk. I've been through this before, and my whole attitude has been not to get caught up in it. I'm going to focus on what I'm doing here and trying to get better and help this team to more wins."

The Eagles have three running backs in addition to McCoy, who played the second half of Sunday's loss to the Redskins with his cracked rib. McCoy's 445 total yards rank fifth in the NFL.

The Eagles might be more likely to make a roster move this week involving a wide receiver, since rookie Riley Cooper suffered a concussion against the Redskins, leaving the Birds with the bare minimum of three wideouts.

title: Roughing the passer
content:
MAYBE THIS IS why Donovan McNabb pumped up so much this summer.

Adam Harris, a 15-year-old quarterback for Lake-Lehman High in Wilkes-Barre, suffered

a broken jaw when he was taped to a wheeled chair and pushed by four of his teammates.

The four, who have been found guilty of simple assault, apparently roughed up Harris after he threw a couple of interceptions in September.

No thumb wrestling?

Big coin flip today in Las Vegas. Antonio Tarver, the IBF and WBC light heavyweight champ and WBA heavyweight champion Roy Jones Jr. will fight on Saturday at Mandalay Bay Events Center. But before that, they must decide who gets to

enter the ring first.

Since both are champions, a coin flip is needed and the winner will have the choice of (a) entering the ring first or second or (b) being announced first or second.

With the sorry state of judging in this sport, maybe a coin flip should decide most fights.

Say what?

Three Class A high schools in Pennsylvania have decided not to participate in the District 4 football playoffs, even if they qualify,

according to the Bloomsburg Press-Enterprise.

Why? Because the competition, namely Southern Columbia, is too good. We kid you not.

"We recognize our limits," Bucktail coach Bruce Ransom said. "It's been a long time since the community could get excited about football. But say we go to districts and we go to [Southern Columbia]. They'd have the mercy rule on us by halftime. That's not the best option for us."

Message to the kids: When you meet a challenge in life, back away. Lourdes Regional and Sayre are the other two schools to bag a postseason appearance. Oddly enough, Southern Columbia has won just two titles since 1994. *

title: FALCONS VS. COLTS: DIFFERENT STROKES 8:15 P.M. TODAY * WATL AND NFL NETWORK * 92.9 FM
content:
The Colts and Falcons are at opposite ends of the spectrum. Indianapolis is the reigning Super Bowl champion. The Falcons, at 3-7, are at ground zero.

The Falcons have first-time NFL head coach Bobby Petrino, blindsided by scandal, who has changed starting quarterbacks four times. His offensive system has been limited by personnel and injuries.

Here's a look at the differences.

ORGANIZATIONAL COHESION

Tony Dungy is in his sixth season as Indianapolis coach. In that time, the Falcons have fired two head coaches, multiple coordinators and assistant coaches, hired general manager Rich McKay and added/lost many players.

"It's the key to what we do here," Dungy said. "[Owner] Jim Irsay has stressed making this an atmosphere where people want to stay. Consequently, we haven't lost a lot of people. You lose players. That's going to happen with free agency and the salary cap, but for the most part, we've been able to keep our organizational structure the same."

DEVELOPING AS A COACH

Communication breakdowns under Bobby Petrino have resulted in controversy for the Falcons (see Grady Jackson, Joey Harrington).

"You go in thinking that you're ready and you're convincing everybody else you're ready to get the job, but you learn a lot of things," Tony Dungy said of his first season as an NFL coach in 1996. "You've got 53 players. You've got a lot of coaches and personnel people. All of a sudden, everything comes at you. Decisions on things from where you stay at hotels to what time you're leaving. ... Coaching the game is the easy part."

QUARTERBACK STABILITY

The Colts have landed a franchise quarterback in Peyton Manning(left), who will start his 155th consecutive game. The Falcons thought Michael Vick was on the same track. His dogfighting case has landed him in jail and left Bobby Petrino to juggle Joey Harrington and Byron Leftwich.

"As of late, I've been the guy who's bounced around, who's been up and down, who's been in and been out," Harrington said. "There may be 10 guys who've been in one spot eight to 10 years."

PERSONNEL DECISIONS

Indianapolis president Bill Polian drafted Peyton Manning,Edgerrin James, Joseph Addai, Reggie Wayne, Mike Peterson, Cato June, Dwight Freeney, Robert Mathis, Bob Sanders and Dallas Clark. He also signed free agents Jeff Saturday, Mike Vanderjagt, Gary Brackett and Dominic Rhodes.

The Falcons' key draft picks and free-agent signings over the past few years: DeAngelo Hall (right), Warrick Dunn, Matt Schaub, Roddy White, Michael Boley, John Abraham, Alge Crumpler, Todd McClure, Jerious Norwood, Keith Brooking and Lawyer Milloy.

title: Graham's value goes beyond his yards, TDs
content:
He has been called too slow by some, too old by others.

In a league that uses 1,000-yard seasons as the benchmark for wide receivers, he had the dubious distinction of being shown the door in Chicago shortly after he followed his 944-yard season with a 1,301-yard year. Chargers wide receiver Jeff Graham doesn't use the word underappreciated when discussing his 10 years in the NFL, but no one could blame him if he played the Rodney Dangerfield card.

Some family members won't even give him a little love. When Graham returned home to Dayton, Ohio, during the club's bye week in late October, his 7- and 9-year-old nephews greeted him with hugs, high-fives and assertions that the winless Chargers need to acquire Minnesota wideouts Cris Carter and Randy Moss. Told by an outsider that they were being hard on their uncle, the boys offered to make him their third wideout. Graham chuckled. "There are certain things that you can't control, such as having an opportunity to get the ball or staying with a team," said Graham, who has played for five teams.

"The only thing I've ever tried to do is keep on plugging and keep on working hard, knowing that things will eventually turn my way.

It just so happens that it's happening in my 10th year." Graham possesses great hands, a level head and deceptive speed.

The people who say he's too slow have never had to cover him. Graham has three touchdown catches this season.

They've covered 45, 52 and 83 yards, with the last ranking as not only the second-longest touchdown catch of the season in the NFL, but also the second-longest completion. Only Kurt Warner's 85-yard scoring strike to Torry Holt in the Rams' Sept. 24 win at Atlanta was longer. As for his age, Graham, 31, is showing no signs of it.

He ranks first on the team in receiving yards (620) and second in receptions (40) and touchdowns (three). Last season, with no complementary wideout and no running game for relief, he came amazingly close to reaching 1,000 yards, finishing with 968 on 57 receptions, both team highs. "What's that saying?"

Graham said, smiling.

"Fine wine?

Aging like fine wine." Trying to define Graham by numbers alone is like using Cliff Notes to read Shakespeare; you just can't appreciate the work.

His value to the team transcends the field.

Name a trait you want in a player or person, and Graham has it. Conscientiousness?

When the wide receivers repeatedly dropped passes in practice and games early in the season, Graham implemented a system of fines for wideouts for drops. Caring?

When wideout Trevor Gaylor was demoted after hitting the rookie wall, Graham was among those who pulled him aside and schooled him on how to handle the situation.

He also helped tutor Keyshawn Johnson when the two were with the New York Jets as well as current teammate Curtis Conway when the two were in Chicago. Selfless?

Many of Graham's routes call for him to go where many receivers do not like to go: across the middle.

He has no problem giving up his body, such as in the preaseason when he lunged for a pass in the end zone, even though he knew he was going to get drilled; or earlier this season when he reached high for a pass, even though he knew a defensive back was preparing to deliver a blow to his back. The hit cracked two vertebrae in Graham's lower back, sidelining him for one game and parts of two others.

But rather than wait until he was completely healthy, Graham rushed back, playing with pain, because he knew the offense was struggling without him. "Jeff is the best," said offensive coordinator Geep Chryst, who was with Chicago when the Bears chose not to re-sign Graham as a free agent in 1996. "He's a real pro and he deserved to be our (offensive) MVP last year, and I think even this year he brought life back to the offense when he came back from his injury. "Without a doubt he's been underappreciated in his career.

I don't know what happened in Philadelphia, because you turn on the tape and he makes plays.

I know he's from the same part of Ohio as Cris Carter, and in my opinion I have him in that same category." Added coach Mike Riley: "I can't say enough good things about him.

He's one of the best I've ever seen." Conway said he learned to be a pro receiver from studying Graham when the two were with the Bears.

Johnson, now with Tampa Bay, also has praised Graham for helping him develop.

Ditto Gaylor. "Whatever I have learned or can say that will help improve their game, I'm willing to assist them," Graham said.

"Sometimes people say, `Why would you do that?

They'll take your job.' So what? "At least I know I helped them develop.

I might not get the glory, but I know the friendship part of it lasts a lot longer than the football part, and that's more important to me.

You always need a friend, for whatever reason." Especially when you've got tough nephews. Nuts 'n' Bolts Chiefs quarterback Elvis Grbac (injured right index finger) did not practice for the second straight day, although he did throw on the sideline.

A decision on his status for Sunday's game at Qualcomm Stadium is expected today . . . Chargers safety Rodney Harrison (hamstring) did not practice and said his status "probably will be a game-time decision." . . . The Chiefs announced that Eric Warfield has replaced Pat Dennis as the starting left cornerback . . . The Chargers' offensive players wore yellow practice jerseys instead of their traditional white yesterday.

"Breaking the curse," fullback Fred McCrary said, referring to the team's 0-11 start. "We've got a curse on these (white jerseys)."

title: THE SILENT TREATMENT THE GIANTS ARE HEEDING THEIR COACH'S ADVICE: THEY ARE KEEPING QUIET AND LETTING THE BIRDS DO ALL THE TALKING.
content:
The New York Giants provided answers to hundreds of questions after practice yesterday, none likely to inflame Philadelphia passions. But their blizzard of bland words could not conceal a desire to join the Eagles in some trash talk as Sunday's NFC divisional playoff game approaches.

If bitten tongues sent NFL players to the disabled list, the Giants could be short of bodies Sunday.

For all their talk of Donovan McNabb's skills, and their opponents' blitz-happy defense, they so far have heeded coach Jim Fassel's advice to leave the billboard material to Eagles such as Brian Mitchell and Bobby Taylor.

Yet, it wasn't hard to detect that the Giants desperately wanted to scream down the New Jersey Turnpike at their playoff rivals: "If you guys had studied the last two games, you'd be more interested in blocking than bragging!"

Asked about the heated words he exchanged with Eagles tackle Jon Runyan following the second of two New York victories this season, the Giants' Michael Strahan paused briefly:

"Ask me after this game," said the normally outspoken Strahan.

Asked to respond to Mitchell's vow that Eagles defenders would go after Tiki Barber's broken arm, linebacker Jessie Armstead demurred:

"We've been through this before," he said. "So say what you want to say; you still got to come to Giants Stadium and play us on Sunday."

Even offensive tackle Lomas Brown, who in 1995 predicted a Detroit playoff victory at Philadelphia only to see the Eagles demolish the Lions, 58-37, wasn't in the mood.

"There's no reason for us to get into trash-talking with them," Brown said. "They're putting up chalkboard material for us."

Personal embarrassment was at least as responsible as good sense for Brown's decision.

"I learned my lesson, believe me, when I did that in Philly," he said. "It would have been better to leave that alone, especially in the playoffs. Because when there is gasoline poured on the fire, it can ignite. They put up 50-some points against us. I ignited a big fire."

Did the veteran lineman use his 1995 example as a cautionary tale for his younger New York teammates?

"No," he said. "I don't want them to know I'm that stupid."

Fassel, who at one point this season banned all newspapers from the Giants locker room, took his NFC East champions aside earlier this week and demanded they keep quiet.

"Coach Fassell just said talk doesn't do anything," Strahan said. "He said, 'Limit it to what's on the field. Let's talk about that.' "

That strategy, Strahan said, matched the Giants' taste. Overlooked by most forecasters before the season, and derided by many as a fluke champion during it, the Giants went 12-4 in large part because they wanted to quiet their critics.

"We're not the type of team that needs to go out and brag about what we're going to do," Strahan said. "We're the type of team that needs to let somebody else brag about something. That ticks us off and makes us want to go out and prove them wrong. I prefer that method."

Brown indicated that the Eagles had bothered some Giants before their two games this season - lopsided New York wins of 33-18 and 24-7.

"The first game we went up there, and there were a lot of things said because of the [Eagles' easy 41-14 win at Dallas]," Brown said. "And actually, I think there were some things said before the second game.

"But we're making a concerted effort to keep what we have to say between us," he said. "We're not going to get into trash-talking in the papers. We know it's going to come down to what happens Sunday. We'll leave that to them."

Still, when the whistle blows on Sunday afternoon and the game begins, no one doubts that angry words will be exchanged.

"The coach can't tell us not to say anything when we're out there on the field," Armstead said.

Notes. Armstead, who compared McNabb to Michael Jordan at one point, said the Giants had no plans to put a spy on the Philadelphia quarterback. "We have to try to keep him in the pocket," he said. "And if he gets out, we have to rally to the ball." . . . Strahan said that what separates McNabb from other running quarterbacks is McNabb's strength. "With those other guys, you can throw a hand at them and bring them down," he said. "You can't do that with Donovan. He's way too strong." . . . Injured Giants Barber and wide receiver Joe Jurevicius practiced yesterday. "Tiki's wearing a protective cast right now, a smaller one than he'll wear in the game," Fassel said. "Joe Jurevicius . . . actually did better than I thought he would do."

Frank Fitzpatrick's e-mail address is ffitzpatrick@phillynews.com

title: The noon game blues
content:
Football schedulers always seem to forget the lifestyle of the common college student when divvying out noon football games. Sure, students could stay in Friday night and have a proper amount of sleep to wake up and start tailgating by 8 a.m., but who honestly stays in on a Friday night? Besides, what is the value of waking up at eight when all of your friends are still sleeping off their hangovers until 10?

Let"(EURO)(TM)s just face the facts and admit that noon games were invented specifically to torture college students. If my theory proves to be true, you may as well consider Clemson"(EURO)(TM)s campus "(EURO)oeGuantanamo Bay: North."(EURO) Just six games into the season, Clemson"(EURO)(TM)s team has already been on the field and ready to play football three times before even getting to eat lunch.

A lack of an official ACC television network, along with a lack of quality teams in the conference, has finally started catching up to our weary Tigers. No more can a 3-3 Clemson Tigers team find themselves on national or regional (and sometimes even local!) television on a Saturday while competing teams from other BCS conferences get the spotlight.

This was not a problem until recent years; specifically when the SEC signed a 15-year deal with ESPN stating that the network has exclusive rights to air all the football games, as well as men"(EURO)(TM)s and women"(EURO)(TM)s basketball games, within the conference. By doing this, every SEC football game being played on Saturday has a proverbial trump card over the broadcast of games from any other conference.

For once, ESPN was not the bad guy in all of this. In fact, the network tried not to commit in its relationship with the SEC. I always like to think about the different conferences in the NCAA like women that ESPN hangs out with whenever it feels the need. The ACC represents the common-looking, yet dependable woman in this ESPN metaphor, while the SEC is the beautiful and leggy blonde with the extremely hot Southern accent.

What happened is that after spending time with the SEC, ESPN began to get too used to having the conference around. The SEC started getting too comfortable, spending numerous nights with ESPN and eventually leaving its toothbrush and unmentionables at ESPN"(EURO)(TM)s apartment.

One day, the SEC ran into its old friend, the Big-10, and noticed that the conference was happily married to its own network. The SEC also happened to notice that even the retarded stepchild of the NCAA, Notre Dame, had its own relationship on a contract with NBC.

It did not take long before the SEC got into crazy/jealous girlfriend mode and threatened to walk out on ESPN if it did not commit. While ESPN hated to give up its random play with the other conferences, it wasn"(EURO)(TM)t about to let that hot blonde walk out of its life. In the end, the network did what most men are eventually forced into and made a grand proposal to lock down the beautiful woman before someone else did.

While the loss of a viable outlet for Clemson games is not particularly positive, I cannot honestly say I blame ESPN for its actions. Upon facing the tough decision of losing either the best football conference in the NCAA or the rest or the football conferences, the network ended up making the smartest business decision.

From top to bottom, the SEC is by far the most talented and well-coached conference in college football. While it is well known that the SEC has great teams at the top, it is amazing how talented the cellar teams are in the SEC when compared to the bottom teams in other leagues. I don"(EURO)(TM)t know about you, but I"(EURO)(TM)d rather watch Vanderbilt battle against Mississippi State over the ensuing pillow fight of a UVA versus Duke matchup any day.

Not to mention the National Championship appearances that the SEC has appeared in over recent years. The SEC has sent five squads to the National Championship since the inception of the BCS system. Their combined record? A paltry 5-0. For those who need it in baseball terms, the SEC is currently batting one thousand when it comes to National Championship games.

The SEC has gone 12-5 in BCS bowls, winning 71 percent of the nationally prestigious bowls that they have played in. Year after year, the vaunted conference seems to send multiple teams to the highest level of bowls.

The ACC, on the other hand, is in dire need of a massive facelift. While the SEC always seems to have a competitor in the National Title game, the last time an ACC team was in the Championship game was FSU in 2001. The Seminoles lost that game, as well as the following three BCS matchups that they only gained access to through winning the championship in a weak ACC.

Florida State did, however, win the ACC"(EURO)(TM)s only BCS National Championship in 2000, taking down Michael Vick and the Virginia Tech Hokies on the shoulders of Peter Warrick and Chris Weinke. The lone National Championship marks one of the ACC"(EURO)(TM)s two BCS bowl game victories since the system was implemented in 1998. In fact, the ACC has been so competitively inept that the conference has never even sent an at-large team to a BCS bowl.

Looking over the statistics from the past decade of football, it would seem that the ACC has been undeserving of a major television contract as well as the automatic BCS bid that is bestowed upon the conference champion. The ACC has quickly aged to become the ugly and boring girl at the party who everyone wishes would leave.

What the ACC needs most to shed itself of the new role the conference has taken on over the past years is a complete makeover from top to bottom.

There was a time when the top teams in the ACC were playing every Saturday with national title implications, but now all of the teams are just trying to eek their way into the Orange Bowl. The ACC desperately needs to find a new identity; preferably one that involves double digit win teams playing for the conference title in November.

But until that happens, it would seem that the middle of the pack teams in the Atlantic Coast Conference will be relentlessly subjected to the doldrums of noon games due to a lack of media outlets. While noon games may be the most god-awful invention since Brett Favre created indecision, it looks like we will be seeing plenty more of them over the ensuing years.

My advice? Make the most of it and enjoy the games because the ACC scene doesn"(EURO)(TM)t look like it will be changing for a while. While it is tough to get up and face a noon game, it"(EURO)(TM)s amazing how much football you can watch on television after a noon kickoff for Clemson.

So, for the next time our campus is subjected to yet another lunchtime start, I will leave you with this knowledge: Lucky Charms and beer go together a lot better than you would ever guess.

title: PHIL AGIUS' WEEK 2 VERDICT: BUFFALO CAN PAY THE BILLS
content:
Green Bay at Buffalo

1999: No corresponding fixture

Packers: Brett Favre says he is fine to play despite the tendonitis in his throwing arm - and as long as you don't mind a quarterback who can't throw properly feel free to back his side, who never got going last week. Linebacker Bernardo Harris is doubtful and cornerback Mike McKenzie (knee) is sidelined for four to six weeks.

Bills: The Bills look to improve their record of 13 wins in their last 16 outings at the Ralph Wilson Stadium. Quarterback Rob Johnson has completed 102 passes without an interception.

Verdict: Anyone who saw the faltering Packers in last week's televised defeat to the Jets will not be keen to support them. The Bills' impressive defence can help them to a clear victory.

Selection: Buffalo -xpts

Oakland at Indianapolis

1999: No corresponding fixture

Raiders: Left it late before becoming the only AFC West side to win last week, but veteran quarterback Rich Gannon kept his head to find Andre Rison with three minutes left. Pounding defence will provide a tough test for the Colts' stars.

Colts: Have won seven of their last eight home matches. Wide receiver Terrence Wilkins is doubtful after concussion.

Verdict: It was business as usual for Peyton Manning and Co against the Chiefs but they will find this tougher. This is the hardest call of the week as Oakland never win or lose by many points but the Colts may just cover the handicap.

Selection: Indianapolis -xpts

Chicago at Tampa Bay

1999: Tampa Bay 20 Chicago 6

Bears: Restricted Carter and Moss to just six receptions last week but will have more pressing problems trying to halt the Bucs' runners. Second-season QB Cade McNown clocked up 290 pass yards for two TDs last week.

Bucs: Full back Warrick Dunn is nursing a shoulder problem but should be fit to line up alongside his phenomenal backfield partner Mike Alstott, who rumbled in for two TDs last week.

Verdict: McNown v King is an interesting clash between the Class of 99 quarterbacks. The Bucs are expected to win, but are never bothered about their margin of victory, so have the handicap on your side and be thankful for every point.

Selection: Chicago +pts

St Louis at Seattle

1999: No corresponding fixture

Rams: Defensive tackle D'Marco Farr is out for a month after a cartilage tear. His place will be taken by eight-year veteran Jeff Zgonina. Linebacker Todd Collins' hamstring is also a concern.

Seahawks: Defensive end John Hilliard misses the match with a groin strain and defensive tackle Tim Watson is doubtful with a knee problem.

Verdict: Live on Sky Sports 3, see preview page 83.

Selection: St Louis -xpts

Jacksonville at Baltimore

1999: Jacksonville 30 Baltimore 23

Jaguars: Lead the series against the Ravens 8-0 but need running back Fred Taylor to return from injury in top shape as his replacement, Stacey Mack, is out after a great game against Cleveland. Defensive end Tony Brackens (back) is doubtful.

Ravens: Come into the game with five wins in their last six, including two shutouts. They have not allowed an opponent to rush for 100 yards in 18 games. Their own unsung RB, Priest Holmes, racked up 119 yards against the Steelers.

Verdict: Jacksonville will find the Ravens defence a very different proposition to lowly Cleveland. The improving Ravens can claim a major scalp.

Selection: Baltimore -xpts

Miami at Minnesota

1999: No corresponding fixture

Dolphins: Will again rely on their decent defence, but new quarterback Jay Fiedler has made a sound start, despite criticism from his predecessor, the great Dan Marino. Secondary could be downgraded by the loss of cornerback Terrance Shaw (knee) and safety Greg Jeffries (hamstring).

Vikings: Star receivers Randy Moss and Cris Carter could not really make their mark against the Bears, but RB Robert Smith did the business on the ground. QB Daunte Culpepper rushed for three TDs but needs to discover his throwing arm.

Verdict: If Culpepper can keep his nerve for a second week, the Vikings can make it two out of two against the Dolphins, who are often poor travellers.

Selection: Minnesota -xpts

Kansas City at Tennessee

1999: No corresponding fixture

Chiefs: Sources, including players, at Kansas City report that coach Gunther Cunningham told his team after last week's home loss to Indianapolis that he had considered resigning. Cunningham refuses to comment on the matter, but it is safe to say the Chiefs are not in good shape.

Titans: Dropped passes by Frank Wycheck and Eddie George cost them dear against Buffalo, but their defence were back in top gear with five sacks. The Titans were undefeated at the Adelphia Coliseum last season.

Verdict: Running back Eddie George managed only 37 yards last week but can expect a confidence-booster against the Chiefs, who look in disarray.

Selection: Tennessee -xpts

Cleveland at Cincinnati

1999: Cleveland 28 Cincinnati 44, Cincinnati 18 Cleveland 17

Browns: Will have taken positives from their loss to Jacksonville, such as the display on QB Tim Couch and four sacks of Mark Brunell. Couch is set to play despite a touch of tendonitis.

Bengals: Champing at the bit after sitting out week one but will be determined for a solid display in the first game at the Paul Brown Stadium. Wide receiver Peter Warrick makes his NFL debut.

Verdict: The Browns gave up 721 total yards to the Jags and are the ideal visitors for the Bengals to christen their new home with a victory.

Selection: Cincinnati -xpts

NY Giants at Philadelphia

1999: NY Giants 23 Philadelphia 17 (OT)

Giants: Have won their last six against the Eagles but will miss key tight end Pete Mitchell for a further week and safety Sam Garnes is a doubtful starter after concussion. Defence limited Arizona to just 43 yards rushing last week.

Eagles: Talked of as the St Louis of 2000 in some circles, the new-look Eagles will be buzzing back on home territory. It was a sorry Dallas outfit that they took apart last week, but running back Duce Staley impressed many with his 201 -yard haul.

Verdict: An interesting battle in prospect between the Eagles offence and the visiting defence but Philadelphia's star is rising fast.

Selection: Philadelphia -xpts

Atlanta at Denver

1999: No corresponding fixture

Falcons: Coach Dan Reeves returns to the stadium where he led the Broncos to three Super Bowls for the first time since those glory days. Wide receiver Terence Mathis should be fit to join his side.

Broncos: Hit by the double blow of Terrell Davis' sprained ankle and the news that his back-up, the impressive Olandis Gary, will miss the rest of the season after tearing his right knee ligament. Linebacker John Mobley (knee) is also doubtful.

Verdict: Reeves can take one last happy memory from Denver against his former side, who are paying the physical cost of running St Louis close on Monday night.

Selection: Atlanta +pts

Carolina at San Francisco

1999: San Francisco 24 Carolina 31

Panthers: Came within four points of causing the upset of the season so far when losing 20-17 at Super Bowl favourites Washington last week. Another 92 -yard kick-off return by Michael Bates would not go amiss.

49ers: Looking to record their eighth successive win in the first home game of the season. Full back Fred Beasley went in for three TDs against Atlanta. Safety Lance Schulters (ankle) is doubtful.

Verdict: Carolina took their chances well last week but, poor as the Niners are, they can be given a chance with the points in their favour in front of their noisy fans.

Selection: San Francisco +pts

Washington at Detroit

1999: Washington 17 Detroit 33, Washington 27 Detroit 13

Redskins: Did not look the finished article against Carolina but got the job done. Running back Stephen Davis is now locked into a long-term deal and the Skins rearguard (six sacks last week) will target whichever Lions QB dares to turn up.

Lions: Charlie Batch is doubtful with a leg injury but Stony Case may get the QB slot regardless. Wide receiver Herman Moore could return.

Verdict: Those who felt let down by the Redskins last week could see a more polished display in a rematch of last season's wildcard play-off, which the Skins won 27-13.

Selection: Washington -xpts

New Orleans at San Diego

1999: No corresponding fixture

Saints: Star running back Ricky Williams plays in his home town having recorded almost half of the Saints' poor 252 total yards against Detroit. Cornerback Charlie Clemons (calf) misses the game.

Chargers: Defence kept them in the game against the Raiders when quarterback Ryan Leaf registered just 17 completed passes in 39 attempts, was intercepted three times and twice forced out of action through injuries.

Verdict: This one could be close, with both offences struggling, so take the Saints on the handicap.

Selection: New Orleans +pts

Dallas at Arizona

1999: Dallas 9 Arizona 13

Cowboys: Someone in Texas must have been walking under a lot of ladders. The Boys' 0-5 pre- season record was compounded by a 41-14 home loss to Philadelphia and the news that receiver Joey Galloway is out for the season with a torn knee ligament. Quarterback Troy Aikman is out (concussion) and replacement Randall Cun -ningham (sprained thumb) is also in the wars.

Cardinals: Jake Plummer's 318 yards against the Giants were second only to Kurt Warner's haul last week. Centre Mike Gruttadauria is considering a surprise early comeback but offensive tackle OJ Shelton (knee) is out for two weeks.

Verdict: With no Galloway, no Aikman and no worthwhile defence to speak of, the Cowboys are in freefall. Take the Cards to leave them searching for their first win of the season.

Selection: Cardinals -pts

MONDAY

New England at NY Jets

1999: NY Jets 24 New England 17

Patriots: Their offensive line will have been given a real ear bashing by coach Bill Belichick after letting QB Drew Bledsoe take six sacks last week. But the offence did well to move the ball at all against the mighty Tampa Bay defence.

Jets: Took their time to finish off the Packers in Sunday's televised match but Vinny Testaverde came good in the second half. Running back Curtis Martin is set to play despite the knee injury he picked up in that game.

Verdict: Bill Parcells will be a keen observer as two of his former underlings, Belichick and Al Groh, fight it out. The Jets can win well enough if they take their chances better than they did on the opening day.

Selection: Jets -xpts

Bye week: Pittsburgh

title: DRAFT-DAY ERROR? WE'LL KNOW SOON
content:
Too good to pass up -- that was Rashard Mendenhall in April, in that first round of the NFL draft.

Maybe not too good to have passed up -- that was Rashard Mendenhall in August, when he was littering preseason lawns with fumbled footballs.

Not too good at all -- that has been Rashard Mendenhall in September, when he has managed less than 3 yards per carry and compelled no one to clamor for his presence on a troubled Steelersoffense.
