Not too good to pass to -- that was Rashard Mendenhall Sunday in Philadelphia, where he dropped the only ball thrown his way.

And finally, just plain not -- that's what has defined Rashard Mendenhall's attendance in the running game the past two weeks.

"He's not Willie Parker," Ben Roethlisberger noted as the Steelersbegan the intense portion of their preparations for the looming Monday nighter against Baltimore. "But he doesn't need to be a Willie Parker, he just needs to be the best Rashard Mendenhall he can be."

You know, like in April, when he was too good to pass up. Which introduces a wicked little irony, does it not?

Here's a running back with a CliffsNotes resume who somehow becomes the first pick of a club with serious offensive line issues, a club that ignored that particular shrieking need for the first three rounds on draft day, swooning at the likes of Rashard Mendenhall and Limas Sweed, a club that, with an injured Parker, now turns to Mendenhall to re-enable an offense that scored only one touchdown in nine quarters.

So now, No. 34 floats through a surreal week of practice at the far end of which he will take the field for his first pro start, in prime time, in that goofy yella throwback helmet, and what does he see in front of him? A club with serious offensive line issues, due in some part to its enchantment with him.

"I'm not concerned about it," Mendenhall was saying yesterday. "I just want to take it all in stride and come in well-prepared. I'm going to try to keep everything as normal as I can this week."

Yeah, good luck with that.

On a team averaging worse than one fallen starter per week so far, Mendenhall is in a real difficult spot. Even with the All-Pro Parker at its disposal, the Steelershave converted exactly five of their past 25 third-down opportunities. With its front line ripped to tatters Sunday in Philadelphia and its quarterback found mostly under 600 pounds of Eagles defensive personnel, the chance that suddenly Mendenhall can set up makeable third downs seems dubious.

"It's not gonna be easy; they have the No. 1 defense in the league," right tackle Willie Colon said of Rashard vs. the Ravens. "For the most part, I think he'll be able to handle it. He's a mature kid. He's a downhill kid. He likes the contact. He'll be ready. It's just a matter of making him aware of what he needs to be aware of."

Roethlisberger spent a few minutes with Mendenhall after practice yesterday, as did head coach Mike Tomlin, as did running backs coach Kirby Wilson. Presumably, they were not making him aware of how much they'd have liked to have had Jeff Otah, the Pitt offensive tackle who has started his first three games with Carolina, which swiped him four picks in front of the SteelersApril 26. Nor were they expressing any second thoughts about passing up left tackle Duane Brown, who has started both games for the Houston Texans.

It's true the Steelersweren't the only club that desperately needed offensive line help that day. Seven of the first 21 players taken were blockers. It's also true the Steelershad no business waiting until the fourth round to attempt to get help up front. Tony Hills of Texas, your fourth-round pick, hasn't given much indication that help is even on the way.

Roethlisberger spent part of yesterday listing the ways the Steelerscan help Mendenhall operate in a storm of inexperience and ragin' Ravens pressure. He mentioned that fullback Carey Davis will do "a lot of helpin'," and the offense lined Sean McHugh up in front of Mendenhall for a few plays in practice yesterday as well. Roethlisberger didn't mention who, if anybody, will help No. 7.

"It wasn't anything they did," Hines Ward kept insisting about the Eagles' onslaught Sunday. "It was a communications breakdown. Ben has to read hot routes, and the receivers have to break hot."

No one seemed to want to mention what Mendenhall's gotta do. He's gotta get 25 carries. He's gotta get 100 yards. Should he fail, even for reasons well beyond his control, Tuesday will bring a lot of discussion on the matter of who was and who wasn't too good to pass up.

title: Give us Philly's Raiders
content:
NOW THAT THE Eagles' loss to the Bucs is beginning to lose its sting, it's time to ask that all-important question: Which team are you rooting for in the

Super Bowl?

For us, it's a no-brainer.

We're pulling for the Oakland Raiders, led by Philly's own Rich Gannon and Rod Coleman, to pound the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

Gannon and Coleman are tough, hard-nosed guys weaned on Tastykakes and cheesesteaks with.

Gannon, the Raiders' quarterback, grew up in Fox Chase and starred at St. Joe's Prep.

Coleman, a defensive tackle from 32nd and Lehigh, excelled for Simon Gratz. He started at W.B. Saul Agricultural School because he wanted to be a veterinarian. But that didn't last.

"When they started cutting up cows that was it for me," he once told Daily News sports writer Ted Silary. "I didn't like being around any dead animal."

Go Raiders!

Like father, like son? Maybe so.

The International Olympic Committee is

investigating accusations that Odai Saddam Hussein - the oldest son of that nutcase who rules Iraq - tortured and jailed Iraqi athletes.

In December, Indict, a human-rights group, said the Iraqi National Olympic Committee was in violation of the IOC's ethics code.

The group contends Odai, the head of Iraq's Olympic association, once made a group of track athletes crawl on newly poured asphalt while they were being beaten, and ordered that some be thrown off a bridge.

If the accusations turn out to be true - and why do we think they just might? - there's only one thing to do. Introduce knife-throwing as an Olympic sport and have Odai and his dad serve as permanent targets.

Tom Mahon Send e-mail to high&inside@phillynews.com

title: Prisuta: Offense must produce for Steelers to become Super
content:
By now, it's something of a laughing matter on the South Side, and one the Steelers have good-naturedly broached at wide receiver Limas Sweed's expense.

"Little, friendly jokes," Sweed said, "something about 'Stickum.'

"You gotta expect that, especially in the NFL."

Sweed appeared to be in dire need of the long-since outlawed substance made famous by the likes of Fred Biletnikoff and Lester Hayes after dropping a Ben Roethlisbergerpass that should have gone for a 50-yard touchdown in the second quarter of last Sunday's AFC Championship Game.

It was a memorable gaffe, and it helped the Ravens hang around long enough to a point where they had the ball and were still within a field goal of winning the game with less than five minutes remaining.

But that was far from the only time the Steelers' offense dropped the ball.

Wide receiver Santonio Holmes lost possession trying to stretch what should have been a 23-yard gain to the Baltimore 1-yard line into a touchdown, and the play was ultimately replay-reviewed into an incompletion. The Steelers settled for a field goal rather than a touchdown.

None of that would have mattered had running back Willie Parker not dropped another perfectly placed Roethlisberger pass that probably would have found the end zone on the first snap of the same first-quarter series.

And the Steelers would have had at least three in the wake of Sweed's misplay had they done something other than what they did on second-and-10 from the Baltimore 16 with 16 seconds left until halftime, which was complete an underneath pass to running back Mewelde Moore, who didn't get to the ground in time for the Steelers to prevent the clock from running out.

That's a lot of points to leave out on the field with so much at stake.

A week from Sunday in Tampa, Fla., the stakes will be even greater.

And it'll be incumbent upon the Steelers' offense not to leave so much potential value lying around the Raymond James Stadium turf.

It's Kurt Warner the Steelers will be trying to pressure in Super Bowl XLIII, not Joe Flacco.

And it's Larry Fitzgerald they'll be trying to cover, not Derrick Mason.

The Cardinals are going to score some points.

To win the game, the Steelers are going to have to outscore them.

The offense met its minimum standard against Baltimore by kicking field goals and avoiding hideous turnovers that wound up in the Steelers' end zone.

But against Arizona, one offensive touchdown isn't going to cut it.

They'll need more from Parker, especially on the ground and potentially through the air.

They'll need more from Holmes, who, with the exception of the play that got away against the Ravens, has been delivering in the playoffs at a higher level than he did all season.

They'll need more from tight end Heath Miller, who remains a reliable if somewhat under-utilized weapon on the receiving end of passes.

If they get all of the above, Roethlisberger may well emerge as the 10th QB to win at least two Super Bowls.

All of that can happen, as long as they don't drop the ball.

title: Dilfer to start for Ravens
content:
Desperate to revive an offense that hasn't produced a touchdown in four games, Baltimore Ravens coach Brian Billick said former Bucs quarterback Trent Dilfer will start Sunday against the Pittsburgh Steelers.

After the Ravens stumbled through a 15th straight quarter without a touchdown with Tony Banks running the offense, Billick turned to Dilfer in the fourth quarter of Sunday's 14-6 loss to Tennessee.

Dilfer went 7-of-13 for 58 yards and failed to produce a score, but he nevertheless showed Billick enough to gain the starting job.

"Clearly we have got to do something to change the impetus," Billick said Monday. "Trent brings certain aspects to the offense: his experience, a certain tempo that we can benefit by. And he's been down this road before."Compiled from Wire ReportsNFLNEWS.24

Dilfer started 76 games for Tampa Bay before signing with Baltimore as a free agent in March. He can well understand what Banks is going through; Dilfer was benched Oct. 31, 1999, by the struggling Bucs and returned only after his replacement, former Ravens backup Eric Zeier, sustained bruised ribs.

"One of the biggest things about this position is you have to prepare to deal with failure," Dilfer said. "Tony has handled it like a total pro. Tony is going to be a player in this league for seven, eight, nine more years and be very successful. He can't let this setback affect him too negatively, the same way I didn't let my setback last year affect me."

Cards fire coach Tobin

TEMPE, Ariz. - Vince Tobin, who coached Arizona to its first playoff victory in 51 years two seasons ago, was fired a day after the Cardinals lost to the Cowboys 48-7.

Defensive coordinator Dave McGinnis was named interim coach.

"I felt it was time," owner Bill Bidwill said at a news conference. "Vince is a very fine man. He's worked very, very hard here. I wish him well in the future."

Bidwill said McGinnis will be "a prime candidate" for the job after this season.

McGinnis said his top priority will be to instill some enthusiasm in the team.

"The last three weeks we haven't been playing, we've been pressing," McGinnis said. "I want the guys to play instead of press."

Tobin's firing came with the Cardinals in last place in the NFC East and two weeks before a vote in Maricopa County on a proposed $ 331-million stadium for the team.

WARNER OUT, FAULK OKAY: Rams running back Marshall Faulk should be ready for Sunday's game at San Francisco, but quarterback Kurt Warner will miss at least three weeks with a broken little finger on his throwing hand. The team's top offensive players were injured in Sunday's loss to Kansas City. Coach Mike Martz said Faulk has a Grade 1 bruise on his left shoulder but no separation of the clavicle.

MCNOWN OUT A MONTH: Bears quarterback Cade McNown could be sidelined for up to a month after separating his throwing shoulder. Veteran Jim Miller is the likely starter for the next game Nov. 5 against the Colts.

GEORGE DAY-TO-DAY: Pro Bowl running back Eddie George has a sprained ligament in his right knee, and the Titans aren't sure whether he'll be able to play Sunday at Washington. George, 27, was injured on his first carry against the Ravens. His status is day-to-day.

CARRUTH TRIAL: Jury selection began in the Charlotte, N.C., murder trial of former Panthers receiver Rae Carruth, accused of masterminding the shooting death of his pregnant girlfriend to avoid paying child support. No jurors were selected in the first day. If convicted of first-degree murder, Carruth could be sentenced to death.

CHIEFS: Quarterback Elvis Grbac is expected to play Sunday despite an elbow injury that knocked him out of the victory over the Rams.

COLTS: Linebacker Rob Morris, the team's first-round draft pick, is out for the season with a ruptured patella tendon.

FALCONS: Linebacker Jessie Tuggle will miss at least the next 4-6 weeks after tearing the medial collateral ligament in his left knee.

49ERS: After another emotional outburst, Terrell Owens spent the day apologizing to his teammates. After Sunday's loss at Carolina, Owens blasted his teammates in a postgame rant, accusing them of quitting and playing only for their paychecks. The next day, Owens apologized before the defensive players' meeting.

JAGUARS: Right tackle Zach Wiegert is out for the season with a torn ligament in his left knee. Wiegert had replaced Leon Searcy, who was injured in July and is out until at least November.

PANTHERS: Defensive end Chuck Smith was placed on injured reserve because of an ailing right knee and is out for the season. Doctors found a stress fracture in Clarence Jones' leg that will sideline the left tackle for at least four weeks.

SEAHAWKS: Quarterback Brock Huard, who has started the past two games, might miss Sunday's game against the Chiefs with a concussion. Jon Kitna, who also sustained a concussion, was much better and is expected to take the snaps as the No. 1 quarterback this week.

title:
Simon flashes display of old form;
The defensive tackle has been a bit sluggish this season, but he made his presence felt Sunday.
content:
It was biting commentary.

Eagles defensive tackle Corey Simon was having what many people believe was his best game of the season. He had just registered a first-quarter sack of the Giants' Eli Manning on Sunday when lead Fox announcer Joe Buck described Simon as "a bit of a disappointment this season."

Color commentator Troy Aikman joined in, saying, "Yeah, and part of the reason Corey Simon has been a disappointment is because he has put on a lot of weight. He's not playing at near the playing weight he was a year ago and, as a result of that, his production has declined."

There's no arguing that Simon's production is down this season, and he acknowledged yesterday that his weight is up. Even with his 1 1/2 sacks in Sunday's game against the New York Giants, he has just two for the season. Last year, he led the team with 7 1/2 sacks.

When Brian Dawkins was asked about Simon's performance Sunday, the Eagles free safety was impressed but also sounded as if he wanted to see more.

"That is what we expect from Corey all the time," Dawkins said. "He's a dominating guy who can control the line of scrimmage and move people back off the ball."

Defensive coordinator Jim Johnson said something similar yesterday.

"Let's face it, when you look at the sacks and the tackles for losses, those numbers probably aren't up to the same standard of last year," Johnson said. "He's had some good games and some average games. [Sunday] was more of the type of game we expect to see from Corey."

Simon, who will be a free agent after this season, said the team's bottom line is all that matters to him.

"It's not about my stats . . . it's about winning ball games, and that's what we're doing," said Simon, whose listed weight is 293 pounds. "None of the other stuff matters. When it comes down to it, we're 10-1, we're playing well, and the defense is finally coming into its own. That's what is important."

He did not shy away from the weight issue.

"I've gained weight," he said. "No question about that. Has it gotten to the point where I can't play the way I've played around here? Not at all."

Interestingly, Simon didn't think Sunday was his best game of the season. He thought his best performance came in the Eagles' only loss.

"I think I played a pretty darn good game against Pittsburgh," he said. "But we gave up a bunch of yards, and nobody sees that. They just see they ran the ball.

"The run [defense] is predicated on more than just the two defensive tackles, but that's the way it always gets drawn up - like we're the bad guys out there. We gave up this, and we gave up that. We'll take that, that's fine."

The 27-year-old said his contract is not an issue right now.

"Like I said at the beginning of the year, that part of the game starts when the season is over," he said. "Right now my focus . . . is on helping this team get to the Super Bowl.

"My faith is in God, and I trust Him more than I trust anybody. What He has for me He has for me, and where I'm going to be is where I'm going to be. I'm just worried about wins and losses. That's from a guy who has been around a few years and understands how hard it is to win a Super Bowl."

Simon believes he had a good game Sunday because he was not double-teamed as much as usual.

"It's kind of hard to be around the ball when you've got a couple of guys hanging on you all the time," he said. "I knew that was going to happen going into this season. Guys know what you've done in this league. Any time you go out there, they are going to go out there and try to stop you from making plays and make somebody else beat you."

As stopping the run became more of a problem earlier this season, the playing time for both Simon and Darwin Walker was reduced. They now are playing just slightly more than backups Sam Rayburn and Hollis Thomas, who are considered better run-stoppers.

That, too, is fine with Simon.

"We'll feel a whole lot better at the end of the season than I did the last couple of years when we didn't have any rotation," Simon said. "I feel 100 times better than I did last year. I was going out last year and just trying to fight through and help this team win ball games, and now I feel a lot fresher than I ever have."

Contact staff writer Bob Brookover at 215-854-2577 or bbrookover@phillynews.com.

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Cheers

Step aside, Mr. Favre. Ahman Green is driving the train right now.

With QB Brett Favre hampered by a fractured right thumb, Green and his talented backfield mates are keying Green Bay's playoff drive.

One of the league's premier offensive lines followed up last week's 190-yard ground game in Tampa by opening up 243 yards worth of holes in a 20-10 triumph against the 49ers.

"They're like a well-oiled machine," said San Francisco DE Chidi Ahanotu, a former Buc. "Somebody's got to put a wrench in their plans, or they are going to keep moving over people."

Favre completed 10 passes to teammates Sunday and three to the opposition, but the Packers maintained possession for almost 35 minutes as Green reeled off his fourth consecutive 100-yard game.

Green Bay leads the league in rushing, averaging 173.5 yards per game.

The Patriots needed OT to fly out of Houston with a 23-20 victory, but don't blame Bill Belichick's defense for New England's struggles.

Led by DT Richard Seymour, the Pats limited the Texans to 11 first downs and 169 yards, turning in two critical defensive stands.

Houston settled for a field goal in the opening quarter, despite a first down at the 1-yard line. Late in OT, the Texans assumed possession at the New England 35, only to see Willie McGinest drop Domanick Davis for a key 5-yard loss to take Houston out of field goal range.

"We made stands all day," said Seymour, who had a sack and four deflected passes. "That's what it came down to - who was going to step up in crunch time?"

Jeers

In his 17 NFL games, former UM standout Phillip Buchanon has supplied Oakland fans with plenty of thrills.

But the second-year cornerback also continues to demonstrate a maddening tendency for the bonehead play.

Buchanon turned in a couple of clunkers in a 27-24 loss at Kansas City. By taking his helmet off after a 27-yard punt return, Buchanon drew a 15-yard penalty, and the Raiders were forced to settle for a tying field goal with 2:18 remaining.

He saved his worst for last, however.

With KC facing fourth-and-14 from the Oakland 33, Buchanon inexplicably backed up 20 yards and yielded a 16-yard catch by Marc Boerigter, setting up the winning field goal.

The last time anyone saw a cushion that large, Tony Siragusa was sprawled across it.

Buchanon has scored five TDs as a pro, but his lack of focus is costing the Raiders games.
"Phillip giveth, and Phillip taketh away," said Oakland WR Tim Brown.

When the Saints watch the playoffs on TV, they can look back at Sunday's 33-20 loss at Philadelphia as a microcosm of their puzzling season.

Despite a solid passing game and brilliant RB Deuce McAllister, New Orleans tumbled to 5-6 with its latest act of self-destruction.

"We have a lot of offensive firepower, but it's like someone's got a machine gun," said veteran RB Fred McAfee. "You can't be reckless with it."

The Saints, who generated 466 yards against a solid defense, suffered four false-start penalties in the opening quarter.

One of their three fumbles came at Philadelphia's 1-yard line, and they also had a field goal attempt blocked.

After the Eagles rolled up 448 yards of their own, Saints coach Jim Haslett was asked how his defense could improve Sunday at Washington.

"Tackling would be a good start," said Haslett, who wasn't laughing.

Dunce Cap

The Jags had stymied the Jets all afternoon, but just as a crowd of 77,614 began to look for the nearest exit, Jack Del Rio tried to take the safe route home.

And walked right into disaster.

With New York trailing 10-6 and backed up on its 6-yard line, Jacksonville switched to a three-man front in the final minutes. Jets QB Chad Pennington picked apart a soft zone in an 11-play drive, capped by a 3-yard scoring pass to Santana Moss that left the Jags steaming.

"You saw it," said DT John Henderson. "I don't want to talk about it. I don't want to say anything wrong."

As Carolina's defensive coordinator, Del Rio built a reputation for aggressive schemes, but he coached scared Sunday.

"When you have a team backed up like that, it's the law of the jungle," said Jags LB Akin Ayodele. "You suffocate them. We didn't do that."

Stat Of The Week

After yielding 111 points during an 0-3 start, the Bears have allowed an average of 17 points during their past eight games.

Quote Of The Week

"I thought I got it. I was at the bottom of the pile, and one guy was grabbing me where the sun don't shine, and another guy was choking me." - Seahawks QB Matt Hasselbeck on his unsuccessful attempt to convert a critical fourth-and-inches

(CHART) POWER RANKINGS
1. Titans. That Jeff Fisher guy is pretty good.
2. Colts. Must face Pats and Titans in the next two weeks.
3. Chiefs. Trent Green has never been better.
4. Patriots. This defense may top the 2001 Super Bowl unit.
5. Cowboys. Proved a lot to their coach and themselves Sunday.
6. Eagles. Physically tough, mentally tougher.
7. Panthers. Finally lost a close one.
8. Rams. Marshall Faulk starting to roll.
9. Vikings. Beleaguered defense finally made some plays.
10. Dolphins. For better or worse, this is Fiedler's team again.
11. Seahawks. Can you go 8-0 at home and not make the playoffs?
12. Packers. Running game appears unstoppable.
13. Ravens. S Ed Reed looks like a great one.
14. Bengals. So does WR Chad Johnson.
15. Broncos. Home loss to Chicago could be a playoff-killer.
16. 49ers. No margin for error anymore.
17. Bucs. Could be walking into a trap at Jacksonville.
18. Saints. Defense just not good enough.
19. Jets. Will be primed for Tennessee Monday night.
20. Bills. This offense is a Travis-ty.
21. Bears. 3-0 vs. the AFC West.
22. Redskins. Why is this defense so lousy?
23. Texans. You can see them maturing.
24. Steelers. Back to the basics.
25. Giants. What an ugly free-fall.
26. Browns. Butch Davis needs a big December to save his job.
27. Raiders. At least Jerry Rice is on the board.
28. Jaguars. Can't close deal.
29. Lions. Forgettable year for Joey Harrington.
30. Falcons. Better off trailing at intermission.
31. Cardinals. McGinnis is McDone.
32. Chargers. Can you think of a more inept secondary … ever?

title: Steelers blitz Ravens, await Brady, Pats
content:
PITTSBURGH -- After booting Elvis from the building, the Pittsburgh Steelers are looking forward to meeting a man named Brady.

Tom Brady, that is.

The Steelers dismissed Elvis Grbac and the Baltimore Ravens from the NFL's playoff party Sunday with a dominating 27-10 AFC divisional playoff win. Now the Steelers can welcome the New England Patriots quarterback to Heinz Field in typical Blitzburgh fashion in the AFC title game.

At stake: a trip to New Orleans for Super Bowl XXXVI.

The Patriots (12-5) advanced with a thrilling-but-controversial 16-13 overtime victory against the Oakland Raiders (11-7) on Saturday night, when Brady rallied New England from a 10-point fourth-quarter deficit amid a steady snowstorm.

"I hope that it doesn't snow," Steelers coach Bill Cowher said. "He looks like he's pretty acclimated to the snow."

If Pittsburgh's latest effort is any indication, Brady can expect to see a blizzard of blitzes from the NFL's No. 1 defense. The Steelers (14-3) pressured Grbac, sacking him three times and forcing three interceptions. Baltimore (11-7) never scored an offensive TD, mustered just seven first downs and 22 rushing yards as hopes to repeat as Super Bowl champs met an avalanche.

"My mind-set is to win a championship," said Grbac, signed as a free agent after Baltimore dumped its Super Bowl-winning quarterback, Trent Dilfer. "If you come up short, you're a failure."

Pittsburgh is an eight-point favorite Sunday, and Ravens tackle Jonathan Ogden agrees: "I don't see New England beating them."

In the NFC title game matchup, St. Louis (15-2) will host Philadelphia (13-5) as 11-point favorites. The Rams crushed Green Bay (13-5) 45-10 Sunday; the Eagles advanced with a 33-19 victory at Chicago (13-4).

Pittsburgh will host the AFC Championship Game for the fourth time in eight years, but Sunday's win marked the Steelers' first postseason appearance since a 24-21 loss in the 1997 title game sent Denver to Super Bowl XXII.

"I remember," Steelers quarterback Kordell Stewart said. "We came three points shy. It's always stuck in my head and I've always wanted to have the opportunity to clean that up and get where we are now -- with an opportunity to seize the moment."

title:
A RITE OF PASSAGE;
 BENGALS' LEWIS BELIEVES HIS TEAM MUST DEFEAT STEELERS TO MAKE THE PLAYOFFS
content:
One way or another, the road to Cincinnati's first playoff appearance in 13 years must go through Pittsburgh.

It's not that the Bengals' surprising revival has come under first-time coach Marvin Lewis, who is from McDonald. Nor that he coached his team to its first winning record at 6-5 by beating fellow Fort Cherry High School alumnus, coach Marty Schottenheimer of the Chargers, last week. It's not even that he must coach Sunday against Carlynton High graduate Bill Cowher, who gave him his first NFL coaching job 11 years ago and then denied him his first victory in the third week of this season before thousands of Steelers fans in Cincinnati.

No, Lewis and the Bengals likely must win Sunday in Heinz Field to make the playoffs.

"It's Pittsburgh. If we're going to go where we want to go, then we have to beat Pittsburgh," Lewis said in Cincinnati this week. "You have to beat them, no question about it. You can't fool them or trick them. We have to go play with them. They are the best right now. They are on the schedule. They're the best. Let's go play."

The Steelers haven't been the best since they beat Lewis' team, 17-10, Sept. 21 in Cincinnati . It was the Steelers' second win in three games and put them in first place in the AFC North. All was right with their NFL world.

Then, the Steelers went out and lost five in a row. Today, their world is upside down at 4-7 while they chase Baltimore and Cincinnati, tied at the top of the division at 6-5. The Steelers believe they must win all of their games to salvage a playoff spot. The Bengals might not have to do that, but they believe they must win Sunday to make it.

Cincinnati probably has a tougher schedule than the Steelers or Baltimore. The Bengals play Sunday at Heinz Field, then at Baltimore, at home against San Francisco, at St. Louis and then finish up at home with Cleveland. A loss to the Steelers would leave the two teams only one game apart with four to go, and the Steelers holding the tiebreaker advantage because of a sweep against the Bengals. The Steelers play their three of their final five at home.

"Without us beating them, we don't have to worry about any playoffs," Lewis said. "That's the most important thing. We just have to take care of the Pittsburgh Steelers."

The astonishing Bengals are among the hottest NFL teams. They've won three in a row and five of their past six, which includes a victory against the 10-1 Kansas City Chiefs, after a 1-4 start. They last had a winning record this late in the season in 1990, when Sam Wyche was their coach and Chuck Noll coached the Steelers.

Cincinnati selected quarterback Carson Palmer with the first pick in the 2003 draft, but it might be awhile before he plays because veteran Jon Kitna is third in the AFC with 2,540 yards passing and is tied at the top with 19 touchdowns. Chad Johnson leads the AFC with 988 yards receiving and is tied with Hines Ward at No. 1 with 65 receptions. The Bengals long have had a dangerous running back in Corey Dillon, who had 108 yards rushing last week in San Diego as he rounded back into shape from a groin injury that bothered him much of the season. Now they have added Rudi Johnson, who leads them with 672 yards rushing and a 4.4-yard average per carry.

No one calls them the Bungles anymore.

"I think they are playing with a lot of confidence," Cowher said during his weekly news conference yesterday. "Their quarterback is playing extremely well. They have a lot of weapons. All of a sudden, Rudi Johnson has kind of emerged as a back. Now they have Corey Dillon and they have a tremendous one-two punch with those guys.

"They have so many weapons on offense and I just think, defensively, they are playing very sound and very solid. They are playing hard. They are playing like a team that has won five out of six games. They are playing with a very high energy and with a lot of confidence. You can see that particularly when they went on the West Coast last week and won very impressively."

Cowher applauded Mike Brown for hiring Lewis, passed over by number of teams the previous two years. Cincinnati's head coaches after Wyche all were former Bengals coordinators - Dick LeBeau, Bruce Coslet and Dave Shula. Lewis had no Cincinnati connections.

"I think the best thing that they did was they kind of cut the ties to the past," Cowher said, "and they brought in somebody new who did not know anything about the past. I think the players kind of read that. I think they kind of said they wanted a new body, and a new approach.

"There is a lot of talent there. Right now, they are playing as a football team. They are playing with some confidence. Like I said, all the things from the years past, a lot of those people don't understand years past, particularly the head coach. He is just dealing with this year. They understand that and they are playing well."

BITING BACK

How the Bengals rank in 2003 (AFC):

Total offense.....................3

Run offense......................4

Pass offense....................6

Total defense.................11

Run defense...................10

Pass defense...................9

title: Favre: Shoulder's 'OK' and there's still hope
content:
EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn. ? This week will likely be the last of Brett Favre's four grudge games against Green Bay since he shook up the sports world by signing with former rival Minnesota more than a year ago. If Favre and the Vikings (3-6) aren't victorious on Sunday, well, the NFC playoffs will probably take place without any purple.

This season has been so strange, though, and so frustrating for Favre and his teammates that the rematch against the Packers (6-3) appears to offer far less drama than all the other subplots surrounding the 41-year-old quarterback and his underperforming team. The NFL, after all, at some point will announce the findings of its investigation into allegations that Favre sent texts and lewd photos to a female Jets employee two years ago. "We can't be concerned about anything else but winning the football game," Favre said, addressing a question about whether finality is on his mind this week. "There's no time to think about those types of things." Favre has produced 21 of Minnesota's 23 turnovers nine games into the season, and his 72.2 passer rating ? stuck near the bottom of the rankings all year ? is 30th in the league. The only mark lower in his 20-plus NFL seasons was 70.9 in 2005. "The question's been asked, it seems like every week: 'You glad you came back?' I am," Favre said. "Am I pleased with the way the season has gone up to this point? Absolutely not. But there's still some hope left, but that is diminishing. We'll put what's happened up to this point in the past and see if we can change it." Vikings coach Brad Childress was asked Wednesday to spell out why he still feels Favre is the team's best bet to run the offense. "Knowledge of the system, the way he plays the game, the way he's able to throw the football," Childress said, then pointing to Favre's 446-yard output in the comeback win over Arizona the week before last Sunday's loss at Chicago. He added: "It's the same reason that he came back here in the first place, because I feel like we have a chance to win and score the most points with him in the game." Childress, who was sharply critical of Favre's interceptions following last month's loss to the Packers, refused to fault him for any of his three picked-off passes against the Bears. In fact, Childress ? while acknowledging he'd always like to "coach those out" of his quarterbacks ? essentially chalked up all the turnovers up to Favre's aggressive style. "Those guys have to live on the edge," Childress said. Favre defended his accuracy in last week's 27-13 loss. On two of his interceptions, his intended receiver slipped on the grass as the ball arrived. The other one, intended for Percy Harvin, was tipped at the line. "What can you do? If Percy catches that ball he may go for 30," Favre said. "He did the week before on a similar-type play. I don't know if it has anything to do with the receivers. It would be easy to point to that. Were we off a little bit on some other plays? Yeah, probably so." Favre threw 24 interceptions in 1993, 23 each in 1998 and 1999 and 29 in 2005, so he's endured these turnover rashes before. But he said he doesn't typically alter his approach in such stretches. "If anything, the last few years I've probably become more conservative in situations like the other day," Favre said. "You've just got to play the game the same way. You've got to prepare yourself the same way. That hasn't changed. It won't change." Playing with pain hasn't changed for Favre, either, who said his shoulder feels "OK" and that he's not "overly concerned" about it. It wasn't bad enough for the Vikings to list it on the injury report Wednesday, with his ankle and foot getting the only billing. Childress termed Favre's soreness as a normal part of the recovery from the last game, and Favre took part in a limited portion of practice.

title:
TIPSHEET;
 LOOKING AT WHO'S IN AND WHO'S OUT IN THE WORLD OF SPORTS
content:
The Cincinnati Bungles

The Rams aren't the worst team in the NFL anymore. Cincinnati sank to the bottom of that list by resolutely refusing to win Sunday, falling 34-7 at home to the arch-rival Pittsburgh Steelers.

These are not happy times for the Bungles. When a Cincinnati fan called cornerback Artell Hawkins "a jackass" after the game, Hawkins challenged the heckler to visit with the team in the locker room.

"It's not easy to go out and be openly ridiculed and face criticism every week from the fans and media," Hawkins told the Cincinnati Enquirer.

Well, you know, winning might help alleviate some of that.

"We're not that bad of a team, regardless of what people say from the East Coast to the West Coast," linebacker Takeo Spikes said. "We'll see what we have to do to come together collectively as a team. Until that happens, the bleeding is going to continue."

More salad for you

College basketball practices are under way and you can bet 6-foot-6, 313-pound Florida center Mario Boggan is running some extra laps in Gainesville.

"Yeah, he's lost some weight," Gators coach Billy Donovan said, "but like I told our conditioning coach, it's like throwing a lawn chair off the Titanic."

Ah, the memories

Reporters asked Broncos quarterback Brian Griese for his recollections of his first game against the Miami Dolphins, his father Bob's old team.

"Lots of pain after the game," Brian said. "I had a thigh bruise, a broken pinkie, a cut on my hand and a torn labrum. That wasn't a good way to start my career."

Cheap goal of the week

Talk about effortless goals. Brendan Shanahan got credit for a Red Wings goal Sunday night when Adam Oates passed the puck into his own net in Anaheim.

There was a penalty pending on the Red Wings, so not-so-Mighty Ducks goa ltender Jean-Sebastien Giguere left his goal to give Anaheim an extra attacker. Oates executed a drop pass to teammate Vitaly Vishnevski . . . but the lad was headed to the bench for a line change.

The puck slid all the way down the ice and into the empty net.

"I guess it went off my skate to Oates," said Shanahan, who was as surprised as anybody by the turn of events. "I didn't even know we were getting a penalty. But when he passed it, I knew it was in all the way."

Mysteries of the universe

Questions to ponder while watching those Marc Bulger No. 10 Rams jerseys fly off the rack at local sporting-goods stores:

* Did you get the idea Sunday that Marshall Faulk was tired of losing?

* Could Adam Archuleta just eat a few more steaks and move up to linebacker?

* Wasn't it nice to see Damione Lewis show up for a game and remind us that he really does have a future in the NFL?

* Why was former Ram Roland Williams taunting his old teammates after catching a short, inconsequential pass along the sidelines while his Raiders were getting drubbed?

MEGAPHONE

"It just gets tougher to every week step on the field and be Brett Favre. It's not that I don't want to throw touch-downs every week. The real good ones expect that of them-selves. That gets difficult after so many years, after so many games."

-- Packers QB Brett Favre, explaining why he is thinking more about retirement

title: The Tribune's Midseason NFL Report
content:
SURPRISE TEAMS

Pittsburgh Steelers

After seven games, Pittsburgh has already equaled its win total [six] from last season. The additions of Duce Staley and Ben Roethlisberger, as well as a more aggressive defensive scheme used by Dick LeBeau, have made the Steelers a Super Bowl contender. Pittsburgh broke New England's win streak. But will the Steelers be able to beat the Patriots again when it really counts?

San Diego Chargers

The Chargers supposedly had the thinnest roster in the NFL, Marty Schottenheimer's days as coach were numbered and Drew Brees was only going to play quarterback until Phillip Rivers was ready. So much for preseason prognostications. Brees has thrown 14 touchdown passes and has the NFL's third-best quarterback rating at 106.4. San Diego is tied for first place in the AFC West with Denver at 5-3.

New York Giants

At 5-2, the Giants have surpassed last season's win total. Quarterback Kurt Warner has been serviceable, Tiki Barber has stopped fumbling and the Giants' defense has greatly improved from last season. The hiring of coach Tom Coughlin has turned around the franchise's fortunes.

DISAPPOINTMENTS

Carolina Panthers. The Super Bowl hangover is nothing new to the NFL. The Panthers, however, were supposed to be different. Carolina was built with a solid mix of veterans and young players, and with the old-school mentality of running the ball and playing defense. But injuries to several starters, including former University of Utah receiver Steve Smith, have led the Super Bowl runner-ups to a 1-6 start.

San Francisco 49ers. The Niners weren't expected to contend for the Super Bowl this season. But the owners' chintzy ways have made the once-proud franchise an embarrassment. The not-so-dynamic quarterback combination of Ken Dorsey and Tim Rattay has ended San Francisco's long string of superstar quarterbacks.

MEMORABLE MOMENTS

Indianapolis-Green Bay

This shootout in Week 3 was special. The two teams combined for 35 points in the first quarter and Hall-of-Fame quarterbacks Peyton Manning and Brett Favre spent the game trying to one-up each other. In the end, a late Packers fumble was the difference in the Colts' 45-31 win.

Terrell Owens' dance

Owens' moves in Philadelphia's win over Baltimore was entertaining. OK, so the moment would have been more enjoyable if Lewis would have gone over and knocked Owens' helmet off.

St. Louis-Seattle

St. Louis trailed Seattle by 17 points with less than six minutes remaining in a key Week 5 matchup. Rams quarterback Marc Bulger heated up and threw three touchdown passes, including one to former Bingham High star Kevin Curtis.

OUTLOOK

It's nearly impossible to repeat in the free-agency era of the NFL, so count the Patriots out. The Chiefs, Steelers, Colts and Jets should be division winners in the AFC with the Jaguars and Patriots winning wild-card berths. Expect the Eagles, Rams, Vikings and Falcons to win division titles in the NFC and the Packers and Giants to earn wild-card berths. Give McNabb the MVP and Roethlisberger the Rookie of the Year award. The Eagles will win their first Super Bowl, 42-35 over the Colts.

IGNOMINIES

Matt Hasselbeck's performance against Arizona in Week 7 was pitiful. He completed 14 of 41 passes and threw four interceptions against an average defense. The Seahawks could have done better with veteran Trent Dilfer on the sidelines.

Jake Plummer has given Broncos fans reason to jeer this season. His left-handed pass against Kansas City was horrible, but maybe not as bad as the three interceptions he threw against Atlanta on Sunday.

The Week 3 matchup between Pittsburgh and Miami was played in hurricane-prone Florida. The two teams combined for six points in the first three quarters. The rain died down in the fourth, enabling Ben Roethlisberger to lead the Steelers on a game-clinching drive.

title: Farrior pays no attention to Bengals' Owens, Ochocinco
content:
• Defensive captain James Farrior doesn't pay much attention to the verbal rants of Cincinnati Bengals receivers Terrell Owens and Chad Ochocinco. Owens called quarterback Ben Roethlisberger"soft" during a segment of his reality show with Ochocinco. Farrior said the mouthy receivers are quiet during games. "It's kind of surprising. At game time, those guys don't say too much. They go about their business," Farrior said Thursday. "We know those guys do a lot of talking. We can't even worry about anything they're saying, because most of the time it's a bunch of junk."

• Cornerback Anthony Madison has been on and off the roster since 2006. Madison recorded his first tackle since 2008 when he opened the Buffalo game two weeks ago in a nickel package and totaled five stops. Madison recorded one tackle last Sunday against Baltimore. For a special teams standout, being a regular part of the defense was a strange feeling. "That's the most significant action I had since 2006," said Madison, who is tied for second on the team with 12 special teams tackles. It's a big turnaround for a player who was released before the start of last season only to be brought back for the final five games. "Last year was a trying time for me," he said. "Certain things you don't have much control over. Just having the opportunity to play here again is worth it."

• Tackle Flozell Adams and wide receiver Hines Ward returned to practice yesterday. Safety Troy Polamalu (ankle), tight end Heath Miller (concussion) and defensive end Aaron Smith (triceps) continued to sit out. Polamalu is expected to practice today and play Sunday. Miller isn't expected to play. Smith still isn't ready to practice.

• For Cincinnati, cornerback Johnathan Joseph (ankle) and tight end Jermaine Gresham (not injury-related) missed practice for the second consecutive day. Cornerback Rico Murray (ankle) also sat out.

title: Jackson, Jets 'D' make good plays coming off bye weeks
content:
A look at the upcoming weekend's fantasy football landscape:

Studs

*Steven Jackson, RB, St. Louis. He put up 166 total yards and a touchdown in Week 8 before his bye. New Orleans has given up an average of 160 rushing yards and seven total rushing TDs over the past three games.

*New York Jets defense. The Jets, on a 1-4 skid, appear rested and angry coming off their bye week. Jacksonville hasn't handled success well and is fit to be taken apart again on the road.

*Ben Roethlisberger, QB,Pittsburgh. Out to avenge an earlier loss to the Bengals, Roethlisberger will take advantage of the league's No. 25 pass defense. Since that Week 3 loss, Big Ben has at least two TDs and a 100-plus passer rating in four of five games.

*Steve Smith, WR, Carolina. A tough sell all season but this is a good week for Smith. He had a season-high 131 yards in Week 2 against Atlanta, he is averaging 131.7 yards in his past three games against the Falcons and Atlanta has allowed a league-worst 31 pass plays of at least 20 yards.

*Ricky Williams, RB, Miami. He isn't taking Ronnie Brown's job, but Williams has been better over the past three games with four rushing TDs. Tampa Bay is No. 30 against the run with 163.4 rushing yards allowed per week.

Duds

*Cedric Benson, RB, Cincinnati. Benson had a solid 76 yards and a TD in the Bengals' first game against Pittsburgh. But safety Troy Polamalu is playing now and the Steelers' run defense is back to No. 1.

*Greg Jennings, WR, Green Bay. He couldn't take advantage of the Buccaneers and still has only two TDs on the season and no 100-yard games since September. Dallas, meanwhile, shut down Eagles big-play threat DeSean Jackson and will not give QBAaron Rodgersmuch time to throw.

*Calvin Johnson, WR, Detroit. Johnson and rookie QBMatthew Stafford are struggling to get on the same page. Stafford threw five interceptions last week, threw two to the Vikings in a Week 2 loss, and all of those mistakes hurt Johnson's chances.

*Kyle Orton, QB,Denver. Denver's offense clearly is struggling with one offensive touchdown in the past two games. Meanwhile, Washington's pass defense has jumped up to No. 1 in the league -- mostly because teams are running on them more.

*Beanie Wells, RB, Arizona. Wells has yet to follow up on any hints of success and there is little reason to expect that to change against visiting NFC West rival Seattle. He is averaging 24.5 rushing yards in four home games vs. 53.0 on the road.

Sleepers

*Lance Long, WR, Kansas City. Along with new teammate Chris Chambers, Long is earning looks from owners desperate for receiving help. He has 10 catches in his two games working out of the slot and could be useful in point-per-reception leagues.

*Darren McFadden, RB, Oakland. The Raiders' 26th-ranked run game can use some help from McFadden, returning from a Week 4 knee injury. McFadden could have immediate success as Kansas City comes in with the No. 28 run defense.

*Antwaan Randle El, WR, Washington. Despite not having more than 39 yards since Week 1, Randle El could have success. The Broncos have been so bad defending slot receivers, they had to sign Ty Law, who might not be up to handling agile Randle El.

*Alex Smith, QB,San Francisco. Seeing the Bears tonight should help erase doubt about Smith's performance last week. In two of their past three games, the Bears have allowed the opposing quarterback to throw five TD passes.

*LenDale White, RB, Tennessee. Thanks to QBVince Young making them competitive, the Titans have run 83 times the past two weeks. Some of those carries will trickle down to White against the Bills' league-worst run defense.

title: In a flash, Brady's injury shakes up the NFL season Loss of star QB changes game on and off the field
content:
FOXBOROUGH, Mass. -- The New England Patriots were perfect until the final moments of the 2007 NFL season, when the New York Giants came from behind to win the Super Bowl and shatter the Patriots' dream of an undefeated season.

Now, to start 2008, the NFL's most successful team this decade has lost quarterback Tom Brady-- leader in their huddle, supermodel-dating star, darling of fantasy fanatics and the league's premier player -- to a season-ending knee injury. The Patriots said Monday that Brady -- injured Sunday during the first quarter of the season opener against the Kansas City Chiefs -- soon will have surgery on his left knee and did not reveal a timetable for the recovery of the quarterback who threw a league-record 50 touchdown passes as the Patriots went 18-1 last season.

Brady's injury has dramatic consequences not just for the Patriots, the once-downtrodden team he has led to three Super Bowl titles since 2001, but also for the NFL. Brady, at 31 a likely Pro Football Hall of Fame inductee, is among the league's most marketable players. In the spectrum of sports celebrities, Brady is perhaps the closest thing the NFL has to golf's Tiger Woods, who coincidentally is recovering from a serious knee injury.

"He is not only the face of the New England Patriots, he is the face of the NFL," commentator and former NFL coach Steve Mariucci said of Brady on the NFL Network on Sunday.

Brady's injury also puts the AFC East in play for one of the few times this decade, giving hope to the Brett Favre-led New York Jets and the young Buffalo Bills.

Former Dallas Cowboys coach Jimmy Johnson, an analyst for Fox Sports, says the absence of Brady could clear the way for a team such as the Jets, who won four of 16 games last year, to contend for the division title.

Brady's team, for which he had started 111 regular-season games in a streak that dated to 2001, reacted with a mix of compassion and reality that comes with playing a violent sport in which a player's season can end in a split second.

In Sunday's AFC East matchup with the Jets, Brady will be replaced by fourth-year pro Matt Cassel, who came off the bench Sunday and helped the Patriots post a 17-10 victory against the Chiefs.

"We're all thinking about" Brady, Patriots offensive tackle Matt Light said. "This might sound a little cruel, but the focus has been what do we have to do to get ready for next week."

For the Patriots, it has been all about focus since Super Bowl XLII last Feb. 3.

The Patriots took an 18-0 record into their matchup with the Giants. With 2:42 left in the game, the Patriots went ahead 14-10 on a Brady touchdown pass. With a fourth Super Bowl title in seven seasons in reach, the unraveling began. Eli Manning led a late touchdown drive that gave the Giants a 17-14 win.

Then came the offseason revival of the "Spygate" controversy over the Patriots' videotaping of opponents' coaches giving signals during games. Now comes the injury to Brady, maybe the one irreplaceable cog in a New England machine that routinely has overcome losses of players to injury or free agency.

The sky was blue over Gillette Stadium on Monday, but area newspaper headlines were grim. "TWIST OF FATE," said the Boston Herald. "Damaging blow," said The Boston Globe. "THE HIT heard 'round New England," said the Providence Journal.

Patriots coach Bill Belichick, clad in his customary gray sweatshirt with cut-off sleeves, was typically less dramatic.

"We feel badly for Tom. ... Nobody's worked harder and done more for this team than Tom, so it's a tough setback for him," Belichick said. "We all just have to do our jobs. ... We have a lot of confidence" in Cassel.

Brady was an example of that plug-in-the-next guy mentality when he took over as an obscure backup for injured Drew Bledsoe early in the 2001 season. The former sixth-round draft choice blossomed into a Pro Bowler, a two-time Super Bowl MVP and a GQ magazine cover boy who fathered a child by actress Bridget Moynahan and dates Brazilian model Gisele Bundchen.

This will be the first NFL start for Cassel, 26, a seventh-round draft pick in 2005 who didn't start a game during his college career at Southern California, backing up Heisman Trophy winners Carson Palmer and Matt Leinart.

Linebacker Mike Vrabel, who joined the Patriots in 2001, said, "Our thoughts are with Tom ... but life goes on. There will be a game (Sunday) in the Meadowlands regardless of who our quarterback is and who's playing receiver and who's playing nose guard."

'It will all be OK'

Sunday's game began as a test of more than whether the Patriots could put the Super Bowl loss behind them. Their offseason also was marked by renewed debate over the Spygate controversy. Stories about the scandal involving the Patriots' illicit taping of opposing coaches resurfaced during Super Bowl weekend.

NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell fined Belichick and New England and stripped it of a first-round draft pick. The scandal made its way to Capitol Hill as Goodell answered questions about the league's investigation. Goodell took no further action against the team.

There's plenty of tape to be found of Sunday afternoon's hit on Brady's left knee. TV outlets across the country have shown it repeatedly.

"The thing about it is, this is the game of football, and accidents happen and things happen on the field," Chiefs safety Bernard Pollard said of his hit on Brady.

Pollard, who was rushing Brady, was on the ground while being blocked by running back Sammy Morris. Pollard dived and hit Brady in the knee as the quarterback followed through on a pass.

The NFL rule book says: "A rushing defender is prohibited from forcibly hitting in the knee area or below a passer who has one or both feet on the ground." However, it also states, "It is not a foul if the defender is blocked (or fouled) into the passer and has no opportunity to avoid him."

Pollard, who was not penalized, said he was praying for Brady.

Belichick, who has made secrecy a hallmark of his tenure with the Patriots, declined to say Monday whether Brady had torn his anterior cruciate ligament (ACL), a common injury in the knee joint. "I don't have a lot of details, medical details," he said.

In an e-mail to NBC Sports, Brady expressed confidence in the Patriots.

"It will all be OK," he wrote. "I'm excited to see what our team is made of. ... I still like our chances."

The Brady brand

While Brady faces rehab and the Patriots try to move on, Brady's endorsement deals appear safe.

He does advertisements for NetJets private plane service and Movado watches.

"As long as this isn't career-ending, I don't think this will curtail his long-term endorsement contracts. Those are safe because he transcends sports," said Al Moffatt, president-CEO of the Denver-based ad firm Worldwide Partners.

"He's almost more of a celebrity or pop culture figure as opposed to being just a sports figure. ... He's transcended sports like Michael Jordon did late in his career or Tiger Woods is doing now. You haven't seen any of Woods' endorsements deals go by the wayside since he got injured" this summer.

Brady is among the most recognizable players in the NFL.

"The way the NFL is structured, the likelihood that one player has a meaningful impact over time is very, very remote," said David Carter, executive director of USC's Sports Business Institute. "The business model the league employs is wide and varied. It includes fans who are fiercely loyal to one team rather than one player. That's why the league has a certain amount of immunity since it's not dependent on one player."

'Get ready to go'

The Patriots have been dependent on Brady. Now they turn to Cassel. Going into this season, he had completed 22 of 39 passes for 253 yards and two touchdowns with two interceptions in three NFL seasons.

With Brady sitting out this preseason with a foot injury, the Patriots went 0-4. Cassel started all four games and had lackluster production. However, when Cassel was put on the hot seat Sunday, he responded. During his first series, with New England facing third-and-11 at its 1-yard line, Cassel connected with Randy Moss on a 51-yard pass. That got the Patriots going on a touchdown drive that was capped by Cassel's 10-yard pass to Moss.

"Everybody just said words of encouragement, 'Get ready to go' and "We need you,'" Cassel recalled. "I felt their support throughout the entire game."

When had Cassel last played that much in a game?

"It had to be in high school," he said.

For the game, Cassel completed 13 of 18 passes for 152 yards with no interceptions. This week, he is starting against the division rival Jets and superstar quarterback Favre.

Trent Dilfer, ESPN analyst and former Super Bowl-winning quarterback with the Baltimore Ravens, doesn't expect the Patriots to collapse. "They're still going to be good," he said. However, "they won't be great."

Contributing: Jim Corbett, Michael Hiestand, A.J. Perez 

Losing the starting QB not always bad
