title: PRO FOOTBALL: SUPER BOWL: Refs roasted on costly flags Seahawks bite tongues over tough calls
content:
Hines Ward spoke calmly Monday morning of how as a child in Forest Park playing pick-up games of football he'd pretend to be Joe Montana one day and Jerry Rice the next.

After catching five passes for 123 yards and a touchdown in the Pittsburgh Steelers' 21-10 win over the Seattle Seahawks on Sunday, he joined Montana and Rice as a Super Bowl MVP.

"I'm still living my dream," he said.

The Seahawks are living a nightmare.

The disparity in penalties was one thing. Seattle had seven penalites for 70 yards, which wiped out 91 yards of offense and returns. Pittsburgh had three penalites for 20 yards, including false starts on their first and third plays and only one penalty on 52 snaps thereafter.

"The unusual part of it is that typically our team is not a very penalized team ... in general," Seahawks coach Mike Holmgren told reporters Monday. "And in Super Bowls, they kind of let the guys play. Historically, they let the teams play. So, put those two things together, and it was a little unusual. And they were very, very costly, obviously."

No call hurt more than the one on an apparent 16-yard touchdown pass from quarterback Matt Hasselbeck to wide receiver Darrell Jackson in the first quarter. A pass interference call against Jackson wiped it away. Seattle settled for a field goal.

There was contact between the players, although neither appeared to literally "interfere" with the other.

"I guess that's how it is when you are ... going against the world," Jackson said.

Longtime NFL personnel man Gil Brandt, now of NFL.com, wrote, "based on the [degree of] contact, it's not a call you see that often."

On Fox.com, former NFL lineman and current announcer Brian Baldinger wrote on an in-game blog, "You gotta be kidding me. That's an absolutely horrendous call. That's not a push-off. That's a legitimate touchdown they had taken away there."

Pittsburgh made just enough plays: Ben Roethlisberger'sscrambling 37-yard pass on third-and-28 set up the Steelers' first touchdown, Willie Parker's score on a Super Bowl-record 75-yard run and Ward's 43-yard scoring catch on an option pass from wideout Antwaan Randle El.

But even the close calls didn't go the Seahawks' way.

Roethlisberger's controversial 1-yard touchdown run with 1:55 left in the first half gave the Steelers a 7-3 lead. It was not clear if the quarterback broke the end-zone plane with the football. After reviewing the play, officials ruled it would stand as called.

Penalties, however, kept popping up.

An 18-yard pass to tight end Jerramy Stevens to the 1-yard-line --- after Seattle drove from its 2 --- was wiped out by a holding penalty against right tackle Sean Locklear. Replays showed Locklear's man, Clark Haggans, appeared to jump offsides before the play.

The drive ended three plays later, with Seattle trailing 14-10, when Hasselbeck overthrew Jackson and was intercepted by Ike Taylor. Then, Hasselbeck drew a penalty for an illegal block when he dived low to tackle Taylor, a call that gave Pittsburgh 15 more yards.

"Referees have a hard job," Seattle running back Shaun Alexander said. "I'm not going to just go out there and bash them. ... We definitely did [leave points on the field]. We're not the team that you see leaving points on the field."

Even if some points were taken, the Seahawks hurt themselves. They dropped several passes, including four by Stevens, and missed field goals of 54 and 50 yards.

"Anything I say sounds like an excuse," Holmgren said. "We had our chances."

title: Ryan's status: out vs. Eagles Falcons QB will miss game with toe injury. Redman will make 11th career NFL start; injured Turner not ruled out.
content:
FLOWERY BRANCH --- With their playoff hopes teetering in the balance, the Falcons will have to march onward without their starting quarterback.

Matt Ryan will not play against Philadelphia on Sunday at the Georgia Dome. A time for his return is uncertain.

Ryan left Sunday's game against Tampa Bay after one series with a turf toe injury. He was replaced by Chris Redman, who tossed a game-winning touchdown pass with 23 seconds left to lift the team to a 20-17 victory.

Coach Mike Smith offered few specifics on the injury Monday, saying, "During the week he's going to work with our medical staff diligently to get back as quick as he can."

Depending on the severity of Ryan's injury, he could miss several games. He underwent a MRI test on his right foot Monday and visited a foot specialist in Charlotte. He was back in Atlanta by the afternoon. Smith said the team will not have an update until next week.

Redman, who completed 23 of 41 passes for 243 yards and two touchdowns against Tampa Bay, will make his first start in more than 23 months against the Eagles.

"Starting on Wednesday, he'll take the majority of the snaps in practice," Smith said. "I can assure you that myself and everybody in the locker room has got all the confidence in the world in Chris Redman."

The Falcons plan to face the Eagles with only two active quarterbacks and will not make a quarterback roster move. "There are about seven or eight teams in this league that do not have a third quarterback on their roster," Smith said.

That means rookie John Parker Wilson, an undrafted free agent from Alabama who hasn't played a down in the NFL, will be one snap away from directing the offense.

Smith was similarly vague about running back Michael Turner, who was re-injured against Tampa Bay just 14 days after suffering a high ankle sprain against Carolina.

"Michael Turner's status ... it's not clear at this time," Smith said. "Michael will continue to work and we'll see where he is on Wednesday. He has not been ruled out of the ballgame."

Last season, the Falcons largely dodged the injury bug. Only one starter, left tackle Sam Baker, missed starts. This season, the team has been hammered.

Thus far, Turner, Baker, backup running back Jerious Norwood, fullback Ovie Mughelli, cornerback Brian Williams, long snapper Michael Schneck and backup wide receiver Harry Douglas have all missed games.

Before the Tampa game, Redman hadn't thrown a pass in the Falcons' past 27 regular-season games. He made four starts in 2007, but the Falcons were already out of the playoff hunt when he took over. His last live action in a meaningful game came during the 2002 season, when he went 3-3 as a starter for Baltimore. This will be Redman's 11th career start.

"I think it will help Chris this week simply because he had 50 snaps in that [Tampa Bay] ballgame," Smith said.

Redman said he's prepared to take over.

"That's just part of being a backup," he said. "When you don't have that many practice snaps, you just have to take mental reps. It's definitely different than getting out there and doing it yourself."

Ryan was hurt while being sacked by Tampa Bay's Stylez G. White and Tim Crowder.

"It was a situation where Matt was able to leave the field ... which I think bodes well for his recovery," Smith said.

Owens to start

Rookie Chris Owens nailed down the starting left cornerback job against Philadelphia with a strong effort against Tampa Bay.

The Falcons played all four cornerbacks, but when Tye Hill and Brent Grimes were on the field, the Bucs moved the ball through the air. Hill was beaten in coverage on Antonio Bryant's 42-yard touchdown pass.

"We wanted to get guys in and rotate them," Smith said. "After getting the four guys through the rotation, we wanted to stick with Chris Houston and Christopher Owens."

Next for Falcons

Who: vs. Eagles

When: 1 p.m. Sunday

TV; radio: Fox; 92.9

title: From where he sits, Smith must see need for a change
content:
Ben Bernankehe isn't, but maybe there is some method to Lovie Smith's mad defense of Rex Grossman.

When the Fed Chair speaks today, the world markets will await with bated breath, having already priced in a quarter-point rate cut but begging for a half.

Maybe in his own way, Smith has done the same.

Maybe he really does see how bad Grossman has been, and with the Grossman Apologist Society (GAS) losing hot air and suffering defections by the hour, it's hard to believe Smith is as firmly behind the Grossman policy as he was this time a year ago.

Perhaps, Smith already has priced in a Grossman cut and wants only to get anything he can out of this starting quarterback until absolutely forced to shift, knowing the risks of policy change.

Smith knows he also can't be viewed as wishy-washy on the subject and doesn't want to go back and forth between QBs haphazardly, so if he's already factored in the Grossman move and knows he has to go to Brian Griese eventually, maybe now it's only about getting inflated, ugly wins from Grossman before pulling the trigger.

What we don't know is how Griese will play or how Grossman will react.

It's possible that some time to sit and watch might be good for Grossman, that he might come back a better football player.

But as it stands now, he's the same player he was last year, unable to make a play when there's any hint of a pass rush, or when there's any sort of physical, mental or emotional pressure, and it's left to Devin Hester and the defense to win the game.

Grossman isn't soft, not by any stretch, and any suggestion to the contrary is unfair and foolish. He's extremely tough, but he acts like a guy who doesn't want to take the hits because he doesn't want to get hurt again and miss football games.

The problem is, any team watching film knows that pressure makes him do crazy things, and that's going to become more a rule than a trend.

Smith has to be able to see this, so you have to think he has some sort of plan for Griese and/or Kyle Orton.

But this team has a Super Bowl quality defense and special teams, and it's unfair to them to have to suffer with a 7-9 offense.

You know it's bad when even the GAS can't stand the smell any longer.

Tracking a billion

The Oct. 1 issue of Forbes lists the annual NFL team valuations and has the Bears jumping to $984 million (up from $945 million a year ago), with revenues of $209 million (up from $201 million), and an operating profit of $36.9 million (down from $40.1 million).

Since the Bears were given a new stadium, the franchise value has skyrocketed to seventh overall in the NFL, up from 10th a year ago.

Hester hysteria

If you weren't convinced already, maybe you believe now that Devin Hester is the best return man in NFL history.

It's just a thought, but since he represents the Bears' best chance to score or gain field position, is it worth risking an injury by having him play offense, or having him distracted by trying to learn offensive sets and a new position?

Seam stress

Lou Piniella is going to get roasted if throwing Carlos Zambrano and Ted Lilly on three days' rest this week doesn't work, but it's the right move under the circumstances and better than any other options he's got.

So even if it fails, give Piniella credit for managing his team, rather than sitting back and making no decisions.

Dusting off

Think the previous manager would have played the subs Sunday, giving his regulars a rest, while managing to steal a win in St. Louis?

Flipping

If you're thinking ahead to a one-game playoff with Milwaukee, the Cubs won the coin flip and would play at home on Oct. 1.

The Cubs would be returning from Cincinnati, while the Brewers would be driving south from Milwaukee.

As for the remaining schedule, the Cubs with two off-days face the woeful Reds five more times and the incredibly woeful Marlins and Pirates a total of six.

The Brewers with zero off-days have five with Houston and St. Louis but travel to Atlanta for four and finish with four at home against the Padres, who will be fighting for their own playoff lives.

The good cause

Don't forget the 29th Annual Ron Santo Walk to Cure Diabetes, which takes place Oct. 14 in - among other locations - Schaumburg, Lisle and Libertyville. For more info visit jdrfillinois.org/walk/walk.html.

Dialing

Mike Golic, of ESPN Radio's "Mike and Mike'' morning show, says his son Mike is still committed to attending Notre Dame on a football scholarship next year, just as did his dad.

"He's been texting all the other guys he knows who committed and they're not wavering. They're all still going,'' Golic said. "He thinks this program will get turned around and he wants to be a part of that.''

Just thinking

Do you suppose O.J. Simpson burst into that Vegas hotel room in search of the real killer?

And finally ...

NBC's Conan O'Brien, on New England's video-scandal penalties: "Coach Bill Belichick had to give up a first-round draft pick, and quarterback Tom Brady had to give up one of his supermodels.''

brozner@dailyherald.com

- Listen to Barry Rozner throughout the week on WGN 720-AM.

title: He's also a fantasy-league champ as a rookie owner
content:
To succeed at fantasy football, you must let it get into your blood. You go to church early, so you don't miss a blink of ESPN's "Sunday NFL Countdown." You buy the papers. You spend hours on the Internet. You talk garbage to your opponents.

Eagles linebacker Shawn Barber was a fantasy rookie this season, and confesses he wasn't quite sure what to expect.

"I saw myself getting into it on Fridays. But after a few weeks, when the message board was lighting up with all this trash talk, I got drawn into it very quickly," Barber said. "I was adding and dropping players, making trades, checking injury reports, checking the Daily News, worried about my studs getting hurt."

A true fantasy-head refers to star players as studs. Barber had lots of studs, and won his league's championship.

With the No. 5 pick overall, he drafted 49ers receiver Terrell Owens. He picked Packers quarterback Brett Favre in the second round and Saints receiver Joe Horn in the third.

He traded Patriots back Antowain Smith and Texans receiver Corey Bradford for Clinton Portis before Portis had become Denver's No. 1 option and dealt Cardinals back Marcel Shipp and Vikings back Moe Williams for the Eagles' Duce Staley.

Barber was prohibited from drafting Eagles, but he certainly went through his share during the season (Chad Lewis, James Thrash, Todd Pinkston, Koy Detmer). He also had one self-imposed rule.

"I would never start guys I was playing against," he said.

The Virginia Football League expanded from eight to 10 teams to give Barber, a Richmond native, a franchise.

NFL commissioner Paul Tagliabue thanked the VAFL for not playing for money. All that was at stake are bragging rights and a trophy, one with a very famous name on it now.

"I had to make my lineup on Saturday, and the other guys could make moves and trades on Sundays," Barber said. "But I didn't have time on Sundays. I had a game. The last thing I was thinking about was fantasy."

Barber has a career-high 110 tackles this season, and Saturday scored his second career touchdown on an 80-yard interception return.

"[Fantasy] kept me aware of who's hot, who we might see later in the schedule," he explained. "As a defensive guy, it helped me keep track of offensive guys."

As he said that, an ear-to-ear smile crept onto his face. A champion's smile.

* Chiefs wide receiver Marc Boerigter at Raiders: Hard to believe this guy has eight touchdowns on only 18 receptions. He's not a bad option as a third wide receiver.

* Bengals running back Corey Dillon at Bills: As galling as it was watching backup Nick Luchey rush for two touchdowns Sunday in a win over New Orleans, playing Dillon against Buffalo's 31st-ranked run defense is recommended.

* Seahawks running back Shaun Alexander at Chargers: Look for coach Mike Holmgren to make sure Alexander gets the one rushing touchdown he needs to break Chris Warren's 1995 team record of 15.

* Chiefs running back Priest Holmes at Raiders: His hip injury is the latest example of how unfair life can be.

* Broncos quarterback Brian Griese vs. Cardinals: Steve Beuerlein is starting. Abandon any thoughts of retaining Griese for keeper leagues.

* 49ers wide receiver Terrell Owens at Rams: San Fran is locked in to the No. 4 seed, and coach Steve Mariucci is hesitant to play Owens and his cranky groin on artificial turf.

* Double-digit winds are forecast throughout the country, so it might be wise using a kicker playing indoors.

Rain is expected in Oakland tomorrow and San Diego on Sunday. New England, Buffalo and New York should see snow on Sunday. Tomorrow's Eagles game at the Giants is expected to be dry, with temps in the 30s and wind reaching 11 mph.

This is the final Fantasy Football report of the season.

title: Bills owner pops off on ... rivalry with Bolts?
content:
"I'd rather beat the Chargers this year than win the Super Bowl!" -- Bills owner Ralph Wilson See what happens when you own a team that lost four straight Super Bowls? You tend to lose it, yourself.

Is this the same Mr. Wilson driven batty by Dennis The Menace? Good ol' Ralph, who has owned the Bills since their inception, never has won a Super Bowl, so maybe we should forgive him.

He can't know what it's like, so he knows not of what he speaks. But, on second thought, why forgive him?

That was beyond asinine and I have a column due. What's the big deal about beating the Chargers?

Who hasn't? The Bills beat the Chargers last year, as did every other team on their schedule.

Nobody made a fuss about it. Down through the years, there have been a whole lot of dumb things said by a whole lot of people in and around the world of sports, but Wilson's little peroration from earlier this year ranks up there.

Owners.

Go figure. "Yes," he added, "I'm really looking forward to that game." Well, it's almost upon us, Mr. Wilson.

The world holds its breath. Be careful of what you wish for, especially if your team has won just one game. This had to be really something for the Bills to point to, that Oct. 28 date at San Diego Wilson had circled on the locker-room calendar.

Such inspiring words.

Historic. "Don't fire until you see the whites of their eyes! "Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!" "Give me liberty or give me death!" "I regret I have but one life to give for my country!" "Nuts!" "I shall return!" "This was their finest hour!" "I'd rather beat the Chargers this year than win the Super Bowl!" Fits right in, doesn't it? Granted, Wilson obviously was miffed about half of his franchise moving west to San Diego.

His GM of many years, Buffalo John Butler, took off to run Chargers football ops and he brought football men A.J. Smith and Buddy Nix with him.

Buffalo John then signed quarterback Doug Flutie, defensive end Marcellus Wiley and linebackers John Holecek and Sam Rogers, all free agents out of Buffalo. These things happen in the NFL. A general manager moves on to a franchise in need and brings people he knows and trusts, along with some players he evaluated and brought in.

But Butler got under Wilson's skin. "I'm very surprised he said that," Rogers says.

"I don't know what happened between him and John Butler, but that's putting an awful lot on your season.

Those were some harsh words." Right.

So the owner and GM did not part on good terms.

Big deal.

Get over it. "When you talk about something like the Super Bowl, it's not fair to the players and the fans," Butler says.

"If he's upset with me, so be it, but don't take it out on the whole community and the fans.

Nobody's paying to see Butler and Wilson play." Well, I would.

But that's just me. Flutie, who is close to sainthood in Buffalo -- Chargers games now regularly televised in Western New York gather ridiculously high 14 ratings -- loves the Buffalonians.

But he echoes Butler when discussing Wilson's statement. "He's got a lot of explaining to do to his players," Flutie said. Wilson's an owner, so he really doesn't have any explaining to do to his players if he doesn't want to, and I doubt Wilson wants to.

This is a grudge match he invented.

And, while Butler and the former Bills on the Chargers roster may feel a bit different about this one, they aren't admitting it. And I'm positive, to the man, they would pass on a win over Buffalo this week if they knew they could win the big game in February.

You play to win Super Bowls, not to beat the Chargers. "That, to me, is a distraction," Butler says.

"Who cares about words?" Well, I do. But that's just me. I can't ever recall an owner saying such a thing about a team on his schedule.

Come on.

This isn't Army-Navy. This isn't USC-UCLA. Since 1960, the Chargers have played Buffalo 30 times and San Diego has an 18-10-2 advantage.

I doubt if there's one Charger who's even aware of it.

I doubt if one Bill is aware of it. Because it's just another game.

It's the same old, trite thing, but there's no getting around it.

That's what it is.

The Chargers don't even get that emotional when they have to play Oakland.

The fans hate the Raiders infinitely more than the players do. Thing is, right now, with Flutie here, there could be a gaggle of Bills fans torn this week.

Buffalo East meets Buffalo West. "The great thing about Buffalo is the fan support," says Flutie, who lost the celebrated hair-pull with Bills QB Rob Johnson and became odd-man out. "They're fanatical.

Every Wednesday night, the RVs pull into the parking lot and set up shop until Sunday.

It makes losing real hard." Me, I like what Chargers safety Rodney Harrison, who has no Buffalo ties, has to say. "That Buffalo thing?"

Harrison says.

"It's just another game.

I don't give a crap about any of that stuff at all.

It means nothing whatsoever to me." See, Mr. Wilson?

It's just a football game.

Mix in some roughage and relax.

title: 49ERS SLIP PAST SEAHAWKS ON OWENS' TOUCHDOWN CATCH
content:
Terrell Owens had it all over Shawn Springs on Monday night. Twice.

Not only did the All-Pro receiver catch a 37-yard touchdown pass over the Seattle cornerback to give San Francisco a 28-21 victory over the Seahawks, but he added an extra flourish by pulling out a pen and autographing the ball for a friend sitting in Springs' suite.

Seattle (1-4) led 21-20 and was driving for another score when Ahmad Plummer intercepted an ill-advised pass by Trent Dilfer at the San Francisco 32 with 10 minutes left in the game.

"We're a young team, especially on offense," Holmgren said. "Dilfer has to hold the thing together. He's kind of like a father out there."

The 49ers moved from there to the Seattle 37. Garcia found Owens deep down the left side and he took the ball over Springs for the score.

He then took a pen from his sock and signed the ball.

"That's what big players do," Seattle coach Mike Holmgren said, referring to the catch, not the autograph.

Garcia then hit Garrison Hearst for a 2-point conversion.

The win kept the 49ers (4-1) one game ahead of Arizona in the NFC West, which Seattle joined this season, moving from the AFC West in realignment.

The 49ers won the division 14 times between 1981 and 1997, the last year they won it. With the Rams' troubles this season, they are a heavy favorite to win it again this season.

Garcia threw for 202 yards and the two TDs and ran seven times for 48 yards, including 17- and 12-yard scrambles on a second-quarter drive that led to a field goal.

San Francisco safety Zack Bronson had an interception but suffered a broken foot.

Miami QB Fielder is out

Miami quarterback Jay Fiedler will be sidelined at least four weeks because of a broken right thumb. Fiedler underwent surgery on Monday, Dolphins trainer Kevin O'Neill said. Ray Lucas will start at quarterback against Buffalo on Sunday.

Fiedler was injured with about 3:30 left in Sunday night's victory over Denver. He hit his throwing hand on a helmet while completing a pass to Ricky Williams.

But Fiedler remained in the game and played the final series, setting up Olindo Mare's 53-yard winning field goal with two completions totaling 39 yards in the final seconds.

Also, receiver Chris Chambers might not play Sunday after he suffering a concussion in a helmet-to-helmet hit from Denver safety Kenoy Kennedy in the second quarter.

* Chiefs: Defensive tackle Ryan Sims, the sixth player taken in this year's draft, is probably out for the season with a broken elbow. If so, it will be a blow to a defense already ranked last in the NFL.

Coach Dick Vermeil said Sims broke and dislocated his left elbow and would probably be placed on injured reserve.

* Panthers: Quarterback Rodney Peete needs surgery on his knee and will be sidelined at least three weeks, putting Chris Weinke back in the starting lineup. Coach John Fox said Peete has been playing with a torn meniscus in his right knee all season and will have it repaired today.

* Jaguars: Tackle Maurice Williams is out for the season with a broken right leg, while quarterback Mark Brunell still is recovering from a moderate concussion.

It is not expected to be known until at least Wednesday if Brunell will be able to play next weekend. He was injured Sunday.

* Titans: Defensive end Jevon Kearse is not expected to play for another four weeks because his broken left foot has been healing slowly.

* Falcons: Rookie linebacker Will Overstreet is scheduled to have shoulder surgery soon and is out for the season. He was put on the injured reserve list.

* Lions: Tight end Mikhael Ricks has a pulled groin and is questionable for this weekend's game, against Chicago.

title: TUESDAY MORNING QUARTERBACK
content:
Three best performances

QB Matt Ryan, Falcons

QB Joe Flacco,Ravens

Let's hear it for the kids. Both won their NFL debuts. Sports Illustrated dug up this nice nugget. In this decade, 21 quarterbacks were drafted in the first round. Only one of those - Houston's David Carr in 2002 - had won his team's season opener as a rookie. True, neither beat a great team. But this is the NFL. Just win, baby.

RB Willie Parker, Steelers

The Steelers drafted RB Rashard Mendenhall in the first round, and everyone whispered how they were going to slowly phase Parker out of their scheme. Instead, Parker was handed the ball 25 times. He raced for 138 yards and three touchdowns and proved that he's not only the best running back the Steelers have but one of the best backs in the NFL.

QB Brett Favre, Jets

He wasn't spectacular and he was facing the less-than-menacing Dolphins, but he was, well, Brett Favre. Running around like his pants were on fire, heaving passes into coverage, jumping around like a kid on the playground and, oh yeah, winning. And give him credit for doing all that while playing with a new team in front of the eyes of the nation, including the New York media that was ready to pounce.

Three worst performances

QB Peyton Manning,Colts

It wasn't so much that Manning was bad; he just wasn't the Manning we're used to seeing. He was 30-of-49 for 257 yards but only one touchdown despite three trips to the red zone. He had to burn timeouts because the play clock was about to run out. His throws were off target, and he shockingly appeared, at times, rattled. He looked like a guy who didn't have a preseason.

The Bucs defense

Like Peyton Manning,the Bucs defense wasn't awful; it just wasn't what we're used to seeing. Missed tackles, little pressure on the quarterback, cornerbacks tripping over their own feet or, when they managed to stay upright, getting burned on bombs. The Bucs traditionally never give up big plays. Sunday's game against the Saints turned into a highlight film for Reggie Bush, above.

QB Carson Palmer,Bengals

What in the heck has happened to Carson Palmer?Wasn't he one of the best quarterbacks in the league just a couple of years ago? Did that injury in the playoffs against the Steelers a few years ago turn him into Steve DeBerg? On Sunday, he threw 25 passes and completed 10 to teammates for a mere 99 yards and one to the Ravens. Worse, Cincy's only score came on defense.

Three bad decisions

Texans coach Gary Kubiak

First drive of the season. Texans have a fourth and 1 from the Steelers 48. Instead of punting and pinning the Steelers deep in their own territory, theTexans went for it and were stopped. The Steelers scored on the ensuing short drive, and the next thing you know, Pittsburgh was up 35-3.

Bucs coach Jon Gruden

Yeah, we heard Gruden's excuse that too many penalties took the Bucs out of running situations. Still ... Earnest Graham averaged 9.1 yards a carry. Warrick Dunn averaged 6 yards. And yet the Bucs ran the ball only 20 times. We don't care if it's second and 5 or first and 15, if the run is working, it's working. And if the run is working, everything else should be working.

The Patriots

This might be premature, but the Patriots could be in big trouble if quarterback Matt Cassel stinks. NBC's Cris Collinsworth had a good line Sunday night: "The most important player on your team is your starting quarterback. The second-most important player is your backup quarterback.'' Did the Pats take Tom Brady for granted and assume he would never get hurt? You have to wonder when the backup is a guy who not only hasn't started a game in the NFL but never started a game at quarterback in college, either.

Three teams that surprised us

Carolina Panthers

Perhaps we just forgot how good Carolina's Jake Delhomme is and how much he means to the Panthers. Carolina went 7-9 last season when Delhomme missed 13 games with an elbow injury. Still, even with Delhomme, did anyone really think the Panthers could go into San Diego and beat a Chargers team that many are predicting will be in the Super Bowl? Chargers coach Norv Turner, by the way, is now 69-88-1 as a head coach. Just thought we'd throw that in there.

Atlanta Falcons

Let's not start mapping the parade route just yet. After all, the Falcons did play the Lions, the NFL's version of a I-AA team. But a nice start for rookie QB Matt Ryan and, especially, RB Michael Turner. We would not have been surprised if the Falcons had gone a month without scoring 34 points, and here they go scoring 34 in their first game.

Chicago Bears

We're surprised any time a team with Kyle Orton at quarterback wins a game - not that Orton (13-of-21, 150 yards) did all that much. The Bears defense is the strength of the team, but we didn't think it was good enough to hold Peyton Manningand the Colts to one touchdown and only 13 points, especially as the Colts were opening a new stadium.

Three teams that disappointed us

Houston Texans

Wasn't this supposed to be the Texans' breakout year? Turns out, the Texans are just still broke. Maybe winning at Pittsburgh was too much to ask (although the Texans were the chic upset pick Sunday), but they didn't even show up. At one point Sunday, the Steelers had as many touchdowns (five) as the Texans had first downs. Starting off your first game trailing 35-3 is, how do you say, not good!

Seattle Seahawks

See, we have this theory about the Seahawks. The only reason they make the playoffs every year is because they play in a stinky division. Take last season. They went 5-1 in the division and 5-5 against everyone else. Then games like Sunday's prove how ordinary they are. Not only did they get beat at Buffalo, they got hammered, giving up 338 yards.

San Diego Chargers

This is typical San Diego. Everything is lined up for them. New England just lost its starting quarterback. Indianapolis looks vulnerable. Jacksonville loses. Here's a chance to get a jump-start on the conference and what do the Chargers do? Give up a touchdown on the last play and lose at home to Carolina.

Three big games this weekend

Buffalo at Jacksonville

Bills believers thought they had a shot this season anyway. Now with Tom Brady out in New England, the Bills are thinking about winning the division.

New England at New York Jets

This would've been a juicy showdown between Tom Brady and Brett Favre, but with Brady out, we'll find out just how much trouble the Pats might be in with Matt Cassel making his first start at quarterback since high school.

Philadelphia at Dallas

This Monday matchup features what just might be the two best teams in the NFC. And with the way Philly's Donovan McNabb and Dallas' Tony Romo fling the ball around, the total score might break 80.

Times staff writer Tom Jones looks back at the weekend in the NFL and ahead to next weekend.

title: If making list of top wideouts, Holt on
content:
ST. LOUIS -- Torry Holt may find himself in the record books with the great old receivers before anyone determines where he ranks among his own generation.

Rarely does a discussion of today's top pass catchers move beyond Marvin Harrison, Randy Moss and Terrell Owens. Clearly, someone's missing. And at 27, with three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and two Pro Bowls behind him, Holt should he heard from and heard about. If the absence of a 100-catch season or double-digit touchdown production is the problem, he's well on his way to solving it.

Anonymity melted away for good Monday night. Holt made 11 catches for 161 yards and two touchdowns on the NFL's biggest stage in the St. Louis Rams' 36-0 whipping of the Atlanta Falcons. That performance followed one against the Arizona Cardinals that included 12 receptions for 133 yards and a score. He's no longer second banana to Isaac Bruce, the Rams' all-time leading receiver.

The Rams showed how much they valued Holt by signing him to a seven-year, $ 42 million deal just as training camp opened. He has 41 catches for 537 yards, tying him for first in the NFL. Five receptions went for scores. Unlike some beneficiaries of large bonuses (about $ 11.5 million), Holt says he's grateful the Rams want him to be part of their future and he's determined to pay them back for that faith.

"It helps knowing I'm going to be here, that they've got to put up with this cat with the beautiful face for a few more years," he says with a grin. "It feels good. But one of the things that's pushing and challenging me is that when guys get new contracts, people ask, 'Is he worth it?'

"I've got that in the back of my mind, that I want to prove I'm worth some little bit of that money they gave me."

That's why he strives to make himself a better, more complete receiver as he moves from Bruce's complement to go-to guy in an offense that loves to stretch the field and attack deep.

"You look at film from a couple of years ago and he was a speed guy, getting to the spot," quarterback Marc Bulger says. "Now he uses his experience with his speed. He understands what he's supposed to do so much better."

With Holt shaking free of coverage and Bulger having time to throw and hitting him in stride, the Rams resemble the freewheeling 1999 group known as "The Greatest Show on Turf." They rolled up 496 yards on the Falcons, a figure they last approached Nov. 10, 2002, with 524 against the San Diego Chargers, but they're more interested in what results from such outings than in reviving their nickname.

"We're trying to put together some wins to continue on doing the things that we've done in the past," Holt says. "We'll let you guys (the media) call us 'The Greatest Show on Turf.' We'll let you tell us if we're back."

Maybe we should tell him, or everyone, that a list of the game's top wideouts today isn't quite complete without him.

Quotable:"Don't get too full of yourself or somebody will burst your bubble. Anything can happen right now." -- Dallas Cowboys defensive tackle La'Roi Glover, on the club's outlook after a 4-1 start. . . . "I'm trying to stay as positive as possible because we're a few points from being a 5-1 team, but would that make us any better of a team? I don't know."-- quarterback Jeff Garcia of the 2-4 San Francisco 49ers. . . . "It's not like we're not trying. It's not like we enjoy what's going on." -- Atlanta Falcons coach Dan Reeves after his team's fifth consecutive defeat.

Extra points: The Green Bay Packers' overtime loss to the Kansas City Chiefs marked the first time they have lost when Ahman Green has rushed for more than 100 yards. Green rolled up 139 yards, but a fumble in OT set up the Chiefs for the winning score. Green Bay is 18-1 when Green cracks 100 yards. . . . Packers players rallied nicely around backup DB Bhawoh Jue, who got toasted for the game-winning touchdown by WR Eddie Kennison. "The good thing is my teammates won't let me put it on myself, because that's the first thing you want to do," Jue says. "It's just life as a cornerback." . . . Arizona vice president Michael Bidwill told a radio show he thinks the club has the personnel to win. With the Cards 1-5 (2-14 in their last 16) and the talent sufficient in the eyes of the son of owner Bill Bidwill, any likely change would involve coach Dave McGinnis rather than the roster. What a surprise. Get the mental picture of vultures swooping
in among the cactuses and picking McGinnis clean? The Cards apparently let WR David Boston and QB Jake Plummer walk away freely and somehow got better. Short list of other ex-Cards still contributing around the NFL: DE Simeon Rice (Tampa Bay Buccaneers), FB Larry Centers (New England Patriots), S Corey Chavous (Minnesota Vikings). . . . The Detroit Lions have no players ranked among the top 20 in passer rating, rushing or receiving.

title: Culpepper to Moss spells danger for Bears' defensive unit But high-flying Vikings can still run the ball
content:
The Bears' defense has already allowed 30 or more points in a game three times this season, so the prospect of facing the explosive and multi-dimensional offense of the Minnesota Vikings is not a comforting thought.

But to defensive coordinator Greg Blache, it's an opportunity, especially considering the Sunday night game will be on national television (7:20 p.m., Channel 7, ESPN).

"They're the No. 1 rushing team in the NFL right now," Blache said. "They're outrushing everybody else in the league. They're fourth overall (in total offense). They've got more weapons than the defense department, these guys got it all going on.

"So this is a big challenge for our guys. But we had a conversation on Wednesday morning, and we talked about one thing: If we can, each guy, eliminate their worst play from last game, we win the ball game. We'll beat these guys if we can just, each guy, eliminate his worst play from last time."

That would include the 66-yard pass that a well-covered Randy Moss caught because Bears cornerback Thomas Smith misjudged the deep pass and mistimed his leap. That play set up the TD that put the Vikings up 23-20. It would also wipe out the 59-yard run by Robert Smith that set up the Vikings' final TD in their 30-27 victory.

Although the Vikings have defeated the Bears in six of the last eight meetings, four of those victories were by 3 points or less. And the Bears beat the 12-point-favorite Vikings in Minnesota last season, 24-22.

"So it's not a mountain that we can't climb," Blache said. "We've been up this mountain before, and this is something we can get done. We've just got to focus, concentrate and execute, and we'll get it done. They're good, but we can take them. Particularly here at home with our fans, we can take these guys."

Defenses determined to stop opponents from running will play eight defenders in the box, moving a safety up near the line of scrimmage, along with four down linemen and three linebackers.

That's a good idea against a team that has Smith in the backfield, along with 6-foot-4, 265-pound quarterback Daunte Culpepper, who ran for 3 touchdowns and 73 yards against the Bears on opening day. But that leaves little help for the cornerbacks who have to contend with Randy Moss and Cris Carter, the best wideout tandem in the NFL.

"You try to disguise as much as you can, hopefully without messing up your team," Bears head coach Dick Jauron said. "You don't want to disguise (too much) and jeopardize what you have to do to have a chance to execute. And then you hope that you're right a lot more than they are, and that when they're right that it doesn't result in a big play. They put up lots of big plays, lots of them. The first time we played them, they had the 66- and the 59-(yard plays).

"All you can do is work to disguise, hope that you get it right more than they do, and that your guys make plays, that when you get caught in those 1-on-1 situations that your guys make plays, or limit (their) plays."

Blache was asked if the strategic battle between his defense and the Vikings' offense was like a cat-and-mouse game.

"It's a little bit more involved than that," he said. "I'm trying to figure out how to do some multiplication, I'm trying to do some creative math. It's a big problem because you do have to keep people in the box to handle the run, but at the same time you have to pay attention to the wide receivers and handle those people. That's going to be the biggest challenge is to try to not let them know until right before the snap (where the extra player is). It's going to be important for our guys to move around because they know you only have 11 and you have to just try to keep the count where they don't see it until it's the last second."

Playing 12 on defense would seem an effective though illegal tack, but even that may not work against the Vikings.

"Against these guys, I think 13 or 14 would be better," Blache said. "I saw a game film the other day where (the other team) had 12 guys on the field, and they still scored a touchdown."

title: Tuesday Morning Quarterback
content:
Cheers

A pair of AFC West coaches have turned in stellar efforts this season, exemplified by Sunday's victories.

At Kansas City, Dick Vermeil proved he can still motivate with the best of them, guiding the Chiefs past the Chargers 24-22. Playing without RBs Priest Holmes and Tony Richardson, WR Johnnie Morton and K Morten Andersen, the Chiefs played a sound, smart game, improving to 8-7 and remaining alive for a wild-card berth.

"It was time," Vermeil said after K.C. committed one turnover and was flagged for one penalty. "We've lost some tough games this year. To be able to win one in the final seconds - hey, we deserved it."

The Chiefs have been saddled with the NFL's worst defense most of the season, but Vermeil's enthusiasm has never waned. His offense averages an NFL-high 32 points and the Chiefs converted seven of 16 third-down situations against San Diego before ex-Buc Michael Husted kicked the winning 38-yard FG.

At Oakland, the Raiders swept the Broncos with a 28-16 triumph as Bill Callahan became the first Oakland coach since John Madden in 1969 to win a division title in his first year. Taking over for Jon Gruden, Callahan has his 10-5 Raiders primed for homefield advantage through the playoffs if they beat Vermeil's Chiefs at home Saturday.

"He's done a great job," Raiders WR Tim Brown said. "He kept us off our feet in practices and kept us fresh."

Callahan steadied Oakland after a four-game losing streak that threatened to wreck a brilliant opening month.

"Bill Callahan has been an unbelievable leader," QB Rich Gannon said.

Jeers

After four years as a pro, Chris McAlister should have known better.

Baltimore's defense fell apart in the final minutes of a 14-13 loss to Cleveland that eliminated the Ravens from playoff contention, and McAlister contributed to the collapse with a dumb move.

Browns RB Jamel White had just turned Tim Couch's short pass into a 28-yard gain to the Ravens 27 when McAlister blasted him two steps out of bounds. The unnecessary roughness penalty advanced the ball to the 13 and Cleveland completed the comeback when Couch found Mark Campbell alone in the end zone from a yard out.

"You've got to be smart," Ravens coach Brian Billick said of McAlister's late hit. "It was unfortunate to do that at that time."

McAlister bolted the locker room after showering, declining interview requests.
Tony Dungy feels Dave Campo's pain.

Once again, an embattled NFL coach finds himself engulfed in the Bill Parcells web of intrigue before the regular season is complete. Parcells met with Cowboys owner Jerry Jones for five hours last week, yet both principals maintain the head coaching job wasn't discussed.

Yeah, and Warren Sapp just received a Christmas card from Chad Clifton.

After his last-minute rebuff of the Bucs in January, Parcells has zero credibility. What he still has, however, is a coaching resume that can scare the headsets off a sitting NFL coach such as Campo, who deserves better treatment.

By meeting with Jones with two games remaining in Dallas' season, Parcells displayed no respect for his profession.

Enshrinement in Canton remains questionable, but Parcells certainly deserves a place in the Pro Football Hall of Shame.

Player Of The Week

With his team's season on the line, Jets QB Chad Pennington accepted the challenge Sunday night with a superb performance against the defending NFL champions.

Pennington shredded New England for 285 yards and three TDs, completing 23 of 33 passes in a 30-17 triumph that kept hope alive. If the Pats beat the Dolphins in Foxboro and New York defeats Green Bay at home, the Jets win the AFC East.

"I don't know what Chad's numbers were, but he played a near-perfect, if not perfect game," said Jets WR Wayne Chrebet, who atoned for last week's critical fumble against Chicago by snaring a 15-yard scoring pass midway through the fourth quarter.

Pennington completed his first 11 passes and directed a mistake-free attack that rolled up 393 yards, keeping possession for 34:22 and outplaying counterpart Tom Brady.

Now, he braces for Brett Favre.

"I'm not worried about Favre because I don't play against him," Pennington said. "I'm worried about the Packers defense."

Former Bucs Update

It's fair to say David Gibson has seen better days.

Tampa Bay's former special teams standout is the starting SS for the Colts, and Gibson suffered through a forgettable game as Indianapolis was hammered 44-27 by a Giants offense led by rookie TE Jeremy Shockey and veteran WR Amani Toomer.

Gibson caught New York's attention before the game by saying Shockey was "no Tony Gonzalez," referring to Kansas City's perennial Pro Bowl TE. Then Shockey caught Gibson's attention, bowling him over during a memorable second-quarter reception.

"He won't be saying nothing else about me, I don't think," said Shockey, who finished with 116 yards on seven catches.

Toomer, who caught 10 passes for 204 yards, victimized Gibson on the opening play of the second half, hauling in an 82-yard strike from Kerry Collins off a flea-flicker.

"It's tough," Gibson said. "They pitch it and your first inclination is the run."

Quote Of The Week

"You saw what the Bengals were doing … they were pass-rushing their butts off." - Saints WR Joe Horn after Aaron Brooks was sacked four times in a 20-13 setback.

title: Coach backs off on long-term support of Brees; Team leaving early to practice in Illinois
content:
The Chargers will be getting out of town early this week. The status of Drew Brees as their ranking quarterback unchanged, they're taking him with them.

The team is scheduled to go through a walk-through at an indoor site reported to be UCSD's RIMAC Arena today, then in late afternoon fly to Champaign, Ill., where it will prepare for a Sunday game against the Bears in Chicago.

Coach Marty Schottenheimer said the exodus to Illinois is for health reasons. The club is acting to spare its athletes from breathing air that in the San Diego area has been fouled by fires.

Schottenheimer, meantime, said he is not considering making a change at quarterback despite Brees' continuing trials.

"Not at the moment," said the coach.

But Schottenheimer hedged somewhat when asked if the club has committed long term to Brees.

"I think everything to this point would indicate we have," said Schottenheimer, "but this is a performance-driven business. I think Drew would be the first one to tell you that he can perform better."

Brees was intercepted three times and had a fumble turned into a Miami safety while the Dolphins were outscoring the San Diegans 26-14 Monday evening in Tempe, Ariz. The Chargers' defeat left the team's two-season record at 9-14 in the games Brees has started. The Chargers have lost 10 of their last 11.

"But there is a lot more that can go into a quarterback's performance other than just the quarterback," Schottenheimer noted. "It has to do with pass protection; it has to do with running precise routes; it has to do with catching the ball.

"Having said all that, ultimately it goes back to the quarterback. While Drew needs to perform better -- and he recognizes that -- we also need to perform more effectively around him."

Schottenheimer said he has greater concern with the inconsistency of his offensive line than he does with his quarterback. "We have to play better there because it affects everything we do," the coach said. "If you want to win in the NFL, you better be able to play good, solid football up front, because that's where it begins. It starts there."

In this area, the Chargers have been diminished by injuries that have caused numerous changes in their offensive front. "We don't have the kind of continuity we need to secure the pocket," Schottenheimer said.

Does the team possess the sort of personnel necessary to create a protective pocket for a quarterback? "We have the personnel that are going to have to improve to get it done," answered Schottenheimer.

In Schottenheimer's thinking, in the Dolphins the Chargers were engaging the league's premier defensive unit. "I don't think there is anyone close," judged Schottenheimer. "It's the best tackling defense in the NFL. They have quality players at the cornerback position, at the safety position, at the linebacker position, inside at defensive tackle and in the guys who come off the edge. They all run fast."

Against this unit, Brees' three interceptions came, in Schottenheimer's accounting, from a pass that was late, another that was behind the receiver and a third one that was deflected.

"His accuracy has not been as good as it needs to be," Schottenheimer said, "and that always has been a strength of his."

A Madden critique

In his commentaries on ABC's Monday telecast, John Madden was critical of the Chargers' play selection, in particular taking issue with handoffs to the fullback in the red zone. Madden also found fault with the club's pass routes and the team's practice of having its cornerbacks align well off the Miami receivers.

Said Schottenheimer: "He (Madden) has a license to remark on anything he wishes." But Schottenheimer said his secondary has been more effective playing off receivers than pursuing the bump-and-run techniques it used in the season's beginning.

INEFFICIENT

The Chargers' Drew Brees, coming off a three-interception performance, has the fifth-worst quarterback rating this season among QBs who have seen substantial playing time. Here are the worst:

Mark Craig: Thoughts on the Vikings as the page turns toward the 2010 season

-Adrian Peterson needs to stop talking about fixing his fumbling problem and DO SOMETHING TO FIX IT! It can't continue, and I wonder if the Vikings maybe need a new running backs coach to get the point across, or to find the solution. Other great backs don't fumble. Why can't AD hold onto the ball, too? One thing he should be taught is to stop flailing around just to get an extra half a foot. title: PATS' VIDEO RAISES ISSUE OF ETHICS
content:
As a former high school baseball star, Brett Favre knows it's not unheard of for a ballplayer to sneak a peek at the signals a catcher is sending to his pitcher.

But if the New England Patriots really were using a video camera to steal opposing defensive coaches' signals - perhaps even in their game against the Green Bay Packers last year - Favre said they might have gone too far.

"Can it cross the line? I'm sure it can," Favre said. "It can give you a huge advantage."

NFL security officials confiscated a camera and tape from a Patriots employee on the sidelines during New England's 38-14 victory on Sunday. The league now is investigating whether the Patriots were spying on signals from the Jets' defensive coaches.

The Packers also had an incident with a Patriots employee shooting unauthorized video during New England's 35-0 victory at Lambeau Field last November. Packers officials simply asked him to stop.

But given the current controversy, last year's incident has taken on added significance.

Favre called the sign-stealing allegations a "serious issue," one that goes above and beyond the normal scope of gamesmanship in the NFL - or sign-stealing in baseball, for that matter - mostly because of the use of technology.

"It seems different," Favre said.

Packers cornerback Al Harris was more direct in questioning the Patriots' ethics.

"When you look back, it's scary," Harris said. "I don't want to say anything wrong towards their organization, because I think highly of their coaching staff and their personnel. But if that's the case, that's not right. I would consider it cheating. I honestly would."

And New York Giants defensive end Michael Strahanseemed to think the controversy could cloud the Patriots' recent success.

"It does make you wonder," Strahan said Wednesday in a conference call with Wisconsin media members in advance of Sunday's Packers-Giants game. "You look at that situation and go, 'How much did it help, and if it did help, in what situations and what games did it help?' Because that team has won some big games. I'm not saying that stealing signals definitely did it, because they have extremely talented players. But obviously it didn't hurt if they were doing it."

Favre, who hurt his elbow during last year's Patriots-Packers game and didn't play in the second half, doesn't remember seeing anything out of the ordinary on the field.

But he does remember watching game film of the Patriots' 31-7 victory over the Minnesota Vikings a few weeks earlier.

"They just were flawless," Favre said.

Almost like they knew what was coming.

Does that make Favre wonder what was really going on?

"Now? Maybe," Favre said. "Before? No."

Packers coach Mike McCarthy punted on the issue, saying he found out about the Patriots staff member after last year's game and wasn't sure if the Packers reported it to the league.

But isn't McCarthy at least a little curious as to what really happened in that game?

"We didn't play very well in that football game," McCarthy said. "All the other things that potentially went on around it, I don't really have a comment on it because I don't know the specifics of it. And to me it's kind of irrelevant, because I'm not going to sit here and talk about a game that we got beat thoroughly in and blame it on something like that."

Favre said it would be difficult for a quarterback to steal a defensive coach's signals, mostly because he wouldn't have time in the huddle.

"Not to mention, that would be so obvious," Favre said.

title: c.j. the dish 'Idol' hoopla hasn't spoiled Maroulis
content:
Constantine Maroulis is obviously Greek, but he was complimented as a mensch over and over again Tuesday night.

The "American Idol" alum dropped by D'Amico Kitchen, just before Maroulis was about to take the stage for an opening night performance of "Rock of Ages" at the Orpheum, which is conveniently located across the street.

Maroulis was making a special appearance at a birthday party for Liberty Carton Co.'sJack Fiterman.

"My best friend and college roommate is Scott Prisand. He is the head producer of the show on Broadway," said Fiterman. "He called me and said, I'm coming in, let's get all your gang together. I'll get Constantine and the whole gang to come to a party. We had a great time, about 85 of us. My wife, Amy, threw the party for me."

Melinda Jacobs, of theadventures ofmelindajacobs.com, said, "Constantine just hung out with everybody at the restaurant before the show. People thought he was being a mensch. I looked at him and said, 'Honestly, it's opening night. How do you feel about being surrounded by all these young and old bubbies?' There wasn't another word I could think of that was Yiddish. And he started laughing and said, Are you kidding? I'm fine with this."

Fiterman said his friends also hung out with Prisand and Maroulis at the Hard Rock Cafe at an after-party until 1 a.m.

Will Deanna dish?

Deanna Favre is scheduled to make appearances on ABC's "Good Morning America" and "Fox & Friends" Thursday morning.

Although the breast cancer survivor was probably booked because it's Breast Cancer Awareness Month, there are sure to be uncomfortable questions as her husband, Vikings QB Brett Favre, is being investigated for a "sexting scandal."

I'm guessing Mrs. Favre would not cancel, because her husband hasn't been avoiding media mics, even though he hasn't answered the questions foremost on our minds: Did you send photos of your privates to Jenn Sterger? Are you really such a publicity hound that you wouldn't simply say This is a setup to these charges weeks ago, thus stopping the corrosive damage to your image? If these charges are true, why didn't you pay for Sterger's silence in August before jumping on a private plane back to Minnesota for the 2010 season?

The morning show interviews will be a success if these questions are tossed at Mrs. Favre: When did Brett apprise you of his sexting scandal mess? Did you accompany Brett to his interview with NFL investigators? Has the NFL asked its all-time leading QB to endure the indignity of dropping trou? Have you looked at the photos on Deadspin? Has Brett ever "sexted" you?

In search of a coach

How low should the Gophers go to find a new head football coach?

On ESPN's "PTI" Wednesday, Tony Kornheiser and Mike Wilbon discussed whether the U should interview disgraced former Texas Tech coach Mike Leach for the vacancy created by the firing of Tim Brewster. (By the way, Brewster's coaching was almost as embarrassing as his Twitter posts, where he used exclamation points like a teenager who'd just discovered what happens when you shift the 1 key.)

"If you can get Minnesota up to the point where they are not losing to North Dakota State, actually get to a bowl game," Wilbon suggested, then someone like Leach can survive at the U a lot longer than he could in Lubbock.

"Mike Leach got fired for the inhumane treatment of [Adam James, son of ESPN's Craig James]," reminded Kornheiser. "He is serving his purgatory, publicly humiliated. But nobody would say he's not a good coach. There are second chances in America. If he goes to Minnesota, if you are in the Big Ten and you bring that spread offense to the Big Ten, Jim Tressel will set his hair on fire. This would be all right with me."

Some Gophers fans might get a kick out of seeing an inferno atop the impeccably neat head of Tressel, Ohio State's football coach.

But be aware that Kornheiser was speaking facetiously.

C.J. is at 612.332.TIPS or cj@startribune.com E-mailers, please state a subject -- "Hello" doesn't count. Attachments are not opened, so don't even try. More of her attitude can be seen on Fox 9 Thursday mornings.

'A MENSCH'

"Rock of Ages" star Constantine Maroulis "just hung out" at a party for a friend of a friend.

QB'S WIFE

Will "Good Morning America" or "Fox & Friends" ask Deanna Favre if Brett has ever "sexted" her?

EX-COACH

Can anyone fill Tim Brewster's shoes?

title: Petrino switches to Leftwich Harrington demoted after tumble to 1-5
content:
Flowery Branch --- Falcons coach Bobby Petrino replaced starting quarterback Joey Harrington with Byron Leftwich Wednesday in hopes of jump-starting a reeling team that has lost five of its first six games.

Leftwich will be the starter for the rest of the season, Petrino said, with Sunday's game at New Orleans being his first for Atlanta.

"We're 1-5 and we just didn't feel good about some of the things we did, particularly after turnovers, so we're making the change," Petrino said. "Joey took it good. He's a professional. One thing about playing quarterback: A lot of times when things aren't going good, all 11 guys on the field play a part into it, but you know in the business that the quarterback is the one you make a change with."

Harrington, who was demoted to No. 2, was not available for comment after practice. Petrino said he notified Harrington first, then Leftwich, of the decision at separate meetings Wednesday morning.

Leftwich, who signed with Atlanta as a free agent a month ago after being released by the Jaguars this preseason, assumed his new role with the first team at practice.

"It was great, just like old times," Leftwich said. "It was a great feeling to be in there and for those guys to welcome me with open arms. That's the thing that was special about it. Those guys welcomed me in and treated me like they wanted me to be there. That's a good feeling anytime as a player."

Though Harrington (123-for-195, 1,279 yards, four touchdowns, four interceptions) had not played poorly, he failed to generate points and consistency with an offense that ranks among the NFL's worst in every category. He was not helped by pass protection that has allowed 21 sacks and a dormant running game that made Atlanta easy to defend against.

The Falcons have just six offensive touchdowns, one apiece in the past two games, both losses.

How will Leftwich, who throws the ball much harder but might not be as mobile as Harrington, be different?

"We'll see Sunday," Leftwich said. "I'm just going to be me. I'll try to lead this team, try to make football plays, try to score some points and try to help this team win."

Teammates responded positively to the switch.

"You got another experienced quarterback who knows this game who's trying to help our team win," tight end Alge Crumpler said. "Joey's done a lot of positive things for our team, especially the way he handles himself and the way he distributes the ball. We're going to look for Byron to give us that spark, and hopefully we'll get a win out of it."

Added wide receiver Joe Horn: "We have to do our jobs first before Byron can be successful. Everyone has to join in and beat the man in front of him. If that doesn't happen, if we drop balls and the line doesn't block, what's the need to have Byron starting anyway?"

Leftwich replaced Harrington in the fourth quarter of a 20-13 loss at Tennessee two weeks ago and completed just 2-of-8 passes. He also threw an interception and badly missed wide receiver Laurent Robinson in the end zone on a potential game-tying play. His rustiness and unfamiliarity with the offense were obvious.

The fifth-year veteran was elevated to second string following the loss to the Titans. But he sprained his right ankle in practice last week, missed most of the next two practices and was designated the No. 3 quarterback for Monday night's 31-10 loss to the New York Giants.

Leftwich's tenure in Jacksonville, which was mainly successful but routinely interrupted by left ankle injuries, ended unceremoniously after four seasons when the seventh overall pick in the 2003 draft was released at the end of the exhibition season in favor of David Garrard.

The Falcons' in-season quarterback change comes after nearly five years of relative stability at the position. Michael Vick started 67 games since being drafted first overall in 2001, and he helped lead Atlanta to the playoffs twice.

Thinking Vick would be the starter this season, the Falcons traded backup Matt Schaub to Houston in March and signed Harrington to be Vick's backup.

Things radically changed months later when Vick was indicted on federal charges related to dogfighting, for which he later reached a plea agreement. That led to his indefinite suspension by the NFL and Harrington being elevated to starting status.

It was the third time Harrington, the No. 3 pick in the 2002 draft, had been afforded a starting job, after stints in Detroit and Miami. Now, it's the third time he's lost it.

NEXT FOR FALCONS

* Who: at Saints

* When: 1 p.m. Sunday

* TV; radio: Fox; 92.9 FM

title: Monday Night Football: Green Bay rises above slop
content:
Green Bay, Wis. » The standings say the Green Bay Packers are in good shape for the playoffs. But Monday night's victory over the Baltimore Ravens showed they still have some work to do to be taken seriously once they get there.

Aaron Rodgers threw three touchdown passes, including two to tight end Jermichael Finley, and the Packers beat the Ravens 27-14 in a penalty-filled game Monday night.

It was the fourth straight win for the Packers (8-4), solidifying their spot in the NFC wild-card race. But it certainly wasn't pretty.

The teams committed 23 penalties for 310 yards, tying for the second-highest yardage total in an NFL game.

Packers coach Mike McCarthy called the penalties "unbelievable for both sides" but said he was happy with the way his team responded to trying circumstances -- something they hadn't done well early in the season.

"I'll say this about our football team: I was very proud and excited the way they overcame the adversity," McCarthy said.

The Ravens (6-6) struggled in coverage without star safety Ed Reed, who sat out with hip and ankle injuries.

But Ravens coach John Harbaugh insisted his team still can save its season.

"We are still breathing," Harbaugh said. "We are still alive."

Baltimore was called for five pass interference penalties, and the Packers were flagged for four.

Rodgers was 26-for-40 for 263 yards with two interceptions, only his sixth and seventh of the season.

While Reed's replacement, Tom Zbikowski, came up with one of the interceptions, the Ravens struggled in coverage.

Baltimore's Joe Flacco was 15-for-36 for 137 yards with a touchdown and three interceptions. The Packers sacked him three times.

The Packers, who were 4-4 after looking bad in back-to-back losses to Minnesota and Tampa Bay in early November, haven't lost since.

They've worked out some of their pass protection problems -- Rodgers was sacked only once Monday night -- and their defense appears to be getting more comfortable in the 3-4 scheme installed by coordinator Dom Capers in the offseason. In short

Aaron Rodgers throws three touchdown passes to lead Green Bay over Baltimore.

» The teams combine for 23 penalties for 310 yards, tying the second-highest yardage total in NFL history.

When: 8:30 p.m. Sunday at Jacksonville, Fla.

Records: Steelers (10-1); Jaguars (6-5)

The skinny: Enough with the Fred Taylor concerns. He has had one big game against the Steelers, and that was four years ago when he rushed for 234 yards on 30 carries and scored four touchdowns in a 34-24 Jacksonville victory at Three Rivers Stadium. The Steelers were 5-5 at the time and in the throes of a three-game losing streak. This Steelers team is 10-1 and riding a league-best nine-game winning streak. What's more, in the past five games, the Steelers have allowed a total of 209 yards rushing, an average of just 41.8 yards per game. That is the reason they lead the league in rush defense. The Jaguars are like all the other pro teams in Florida -- they can't score points.

Prediction: Steelers, 17-9

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Baltimore

Records: Bengals (5-6); Ravens (7-4)

The skinny: After scoring 58 points against the Browns -- its most since 1989 -- Cincinnati might be lucky to score half that against Baltimore. The Bengals are coming off the second-highest scoring game in NFL history and perhaps wish they could have saved some of those points for the Ravens, who have allowed just 22 in the past two meetings with their AFC North opponent. On the subject of wishing, maybe the Bengals would have preferred if Jamal Lewis' suspension could have run a little longer. Lewis has rushed for more than 100 yards in all seven career meetings with the Bengals. Ravens coach Brian Billick has all but conceded the AFC North to the Steelers, who have a three-game lead. Hey, Brian, how about home-field advantage?

Prediction: Ravens, 20-13

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Cleveland

Records: Patriots (10-1); Browns (3-8)

The skinny: New England continues to make its way through the AFC North, beating Baltimore last week to give the Steelers a three-game division cushion and heading to Cleveland to face the Clowns … er … Browns. Next week, the Patriots entertain the Bengals, the second game in a finishing five-game stretch that is softer than a down comforter. The Browns haven't done the Steelers many favors, but they could start by beating the Patriots, an unlikely task in that the defending Super Bowl champions have won 25 of their past 26 games. The Patriots don't look as if they're going to lose again, meaning the Steelers might have to finish 15-1 -- at worst, 14-2 -- to get home-field advantage.

Prediction: Patriots, 23-9

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Chicago

Records: Vikings (7-4); Bears (4-7)

The skinny: Poor Lovie Smith. Desperation moves even the strongest men to foolish decisions. OK, so maybe he was delusional, still disoriented, from watching Chicago lose to Dallas, 21-7, on Thanksgiving, a game sure to give indigestion to anyone who watched. Or maybe, just maybe, there isn't another decent quarterback out there. How else, though, do you explain a seemingly rational, likable man going out and signing Jeff George a. k. a. the coach killer -- to the 53-man roster. Forget that George hasn't played since 2001. Putting George on a roster is like putting arsenic on an open wound. Chad Hutchinson, another quarterback castoff, gets the start. Let's hope he succeeds.

Prediction: Vikings, 23-12

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Detroit

Records: Cardinals (4-7); Lions (4-7)

The skinny: Dennis Green has turned into something of a mad scientist, experimenting with quarterbacks as if they were lab rats. The latest to get a shot as the starter is rookie John Navarre, a seventh-round pick. With the exception of Ben Roethlisberger, who has tied an NFL record for wins by a rookie quarterback (9), rookie quarterbacks have not fared well the past four weeks, posting an 0-7 record. Navarre, though, won't be the only rookie starter in the backfield. RB Larry Croom, an undrafted free agent, makes his first NFL start. At least it's against Detroit, which has lost five in a row. Say this for Dennis Green: At least he didn't sign Jeff George.

Prediction: Lions, 23-13

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Indianapolis

Records: Titans (4-7); Colts (8-3)

The skinny: The seasons for last year's co-MVPs -- Peyton Manning and Steve McNair -- have gone in opposite directions. Manning has thrown four or more touchdowns in an NFL-record five games in a row and is on the verge of eclipsing Dan Marino's single-season record for touchdowns in a season (48, Manning has 41). Indianapolis (380) has scored nearly twice as many points as eight NFL teams. McNair, though, has struggled with injuries, and his team has dropped into last place in the AFC South after blowing an 18-point lead to Houston. The Colts have won seven of their past eight, but it's not just Manning. RB Edgerrin James leads the AFC with 1,186 yards rushing.

Prediction: Colts, 31-20

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Miami

Records: Bills (5-6); Dolphins (2-9)

The skinny: After an 0-4 start that included a couple last-minute losses, Buffalo has bounced back quicker than fuel prices, winning five of its past seven. The Bills might have to run the table if they want to get a wild-card playoff spot because there are four non-division-leading teams with four or fewer losses. But their offense is finally performing the way everyone expected when Mike Mularkey became coach, and the defense held the Seahawks to 9 points last week in Seattle. Miami finally got a win for its interim coach, Jim Bates, but only because San Francisco, another team with only one victory, had five turnovers. Mularkey's first NFL victory was Oct. 17 against the Dolphins.

Prediction: Bills, 24-13

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at New Orleans

Records: Panthers (4-7); Saints (4-7)

The skinny: Carolina is trying to shake some of the lethargy from a 1-7 start, winning three in a row and, in the talent-deprived NFC, retaining some slim hope of a late-season wild-card playoff push. New Orleans, meantime, is trying to shake the lethargy that spawns: 1) The owner to label the team's performance as high-schoolish; 2) Causes the emotionally combustible coach, Jim Haslett, to spew halftime venom at his players. The Panthers, though, have to do a better job against Saints RB Deuce McAllister, who has rushed for more than 100 yards in each of the past three games against them. The Saints need something to avert further implosion.

Prediction: Panthers, 24-19

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at East Rutherford, N.J.

Records: Texans (5-6); Jets (8-3)

The skinny: In its past two games, New York has scratched out victories that, in the immortal words of Bill Cowher, wasn't a Mozart. Nonetheless, they never ask how, just how many, and there are the Jets, staying alive as one of the wild-card teams for the AFC playoffs with eight victories. They have managed to do so without QB Chad Pennington, who might return against Houston after missing the past three games with injury. The Jets have scored just 23 points in their past two games combined but have allowed the opposition only 10. The Texans, down 18 to the Titans, rallied for a 31-21 victory that has them thinking they can still make the playoffs. Only in the NFL.

Prediction: Jets, 19-13

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at St. Louis

Records: 49ers (1-10); Rams (5-6)

The skinny: It's not very difficult to identify the worst division in the NFL -- the NFC West, where a 5-6 record and a two-game losing streak will get you just a game off the lead. That is the situation in which St. Louis find itself, despite a run of horrid performances that continued Monday in a 45-17 loss in Green Bay. Still, the Rams are just a game behind Seattle. And, really, the margin is less than that because the Rams have defeated the Seahawks twice, should the teams finish tied. The Rams already have one loss to the team that used to be the worst in the NFL -- Miami. It would be downright embarrassing to lose to the only other team that lost to the Dolphins.

Prediction: Rams, 31-17

When: 1 p.m. Sunday at Tampa, Fla.

Records: Falcons (9-2); Buccaneers (4-7)

The skinny: Tampa Bay's attempt to come back from an 0-4 start and possibly make a run at the playoffs was derailed last week with a 21-14 loss to Carolina. Of course, in the NFC, seven losses, even eight, might not prevent a team from qualifying for the postseason. The Buccaneers, though, are staring very hard at loss No. 8, particularly against Atlanta, which won the first meeting just three weeks ago, 24-14. The Falcons rallied for a victory in the final minute in New Orleans to put themselves in position to clinch the NFC South title with a victory against the Buccaneers. You might find it hard to believe that Michael Vick pulled out the game for the Falcons.

Prediction: Falcons, 20-17

When: 4:05 p.m. Sunday at Oakland

Records: Chiefs (3-8); Raiders (4-7)

The skinny: This used to be one of the great rivalries in the NFL. Ben Davidson vs. Len Dawson. Emmitt Thomas vs. Daryle Lamonica. Willie Lanier vs. Marv Hubbard. Not anymore. This will be the 88th regular-season meeting between the teams, and Kansas City has a slight edge, 43-42-2. But the Chiefs and Oakland are among the few teams who, realistically, have no shot at the playoffs. The Chiefs' fate was sealed about the time Priest Holmes went out of the lineup. Since then, they are 0-4. Once again, the Raiders look like a better team in the snow than a dry field. Last time they played in the white stuff, they thought they had beaten the Patriots in the 2001 AFC playoffs.

Prediction: Chiefs, 38-34

When: 4:05 p.m. Sunday at San Diego

Records: Broncos (7-4); Chargers (8-3)

The skinny: Denver has dominated this series, winning 12 of the past 15, including a 23-13 victory Sept. 26. But, since then, San Diego has been hotter than a desert sun, winning five in a row and seven of the past eight. Included in that run is a four-game winning streak at home, in which QB Drew Brees has been flawless, throwing 14 touchdowns and no interceptions. But one of the Chargers' secret weapons is TE Antonio Gates, a former Kent State basketball player who is tied for the AFC lead with 11 touchdown catches, a club record for tight ends. The Broncos are too inconsistent to be considered a serious threat in the AFC playoffs.

Prediction: Chargers, 30-20

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at Philadelphia

Records: Packers (7-4); Eagles (10-1)

The skinny: If this is a preview of the NFC championship game, it might be bad news for Philadelphia, which has lost the past three conference title games. Right now, Green Bay is hotter than Jessica Simpson, winning six in a row and looking like a team that could be troublesome in the playoffs. Eventually, though, the Packers defense will catch up with them. The Eagles, meantime, continue to hammer every team in the NFL … except the Steelers. They are the only NFL team to be unbeaten in conference play (8) and last week became only the third team in history to clinch the division title after 11 games (1985 Bears, '97 49ers) since the 16-game schedule was introduced.

Prediction: Eagles, 28-24

When: 4:15 p.m. Sunday at Washington

Records: Giants (5-6); Redskins (3-8)

The skinny: Finding something good to write about these teams is like finding something bad to say about Christmas. After a 5-2 start, New York has lost four in a row and appears to be no closer to turning around its fortunes, not even with rookie quarterback Eli Manning taking over for Kurt Warner. After winning its first game in Joe Gibbs' return to coaching, Washington has lost eight of 10, including last week's 16-7 loss in Heinz Field. The Giants won the first meeting, 20-14, with seven takeaways. But the Redskins are playing better defense, which means Manning should continue to search for his first victory.

Prediction: Redskins, 13-11

When: 9 p.m. Monday at Seattle

Records: Cowboys (4-7); Seahawks (6-5)

The skinny: Now you know why the networks want to be able to reschedule their prime-time games during the season? Dallas has made more national TV appearances than John Kerry, primarily because the Cowboys were 10-6 and one of the surprise teams in the league last season. But the only team they're capable of beating this season is Chicago, only because the Bears are so desperate for quarterback help they have to resort to signing Jeff George. At the start of the season, Seattle was 3-0 and looking like a Super Bowl team. After getting hammered at home last week by the Bills, the only positive thing to say about the Seahawks is that they are fortunate to play in the NFC West.

Prediction: Seahawks, 30-17

-30-

-30-

It's been a confusing week to be a Vikings fan.

     First we're told to be quiet when the guys in Purple have the ball. OK, sure, glad to help. Even turned off the cell phone and ordered beer in sign language.

   Then we're told to be screaming banshees from start to finish in the Metrodome, so we can lead the NFL in bleeding eardrums. Umm, sure. Tell me if something happens on the field, I don't wanna miss the next set of instructions on "How to Watch a Football Game" on the Jumbotron.

     Actually, the rule of thumb could have been much simpler: Howl like maniacs anytime Randy Moss catches the ball.

     Moss was stunningly efficient in his game-changing contributions Saturday, as the Vikings thumped the Saints 34-16. Two catches, two touchdowns. The beauty of both was in their innocent-looking beginnings. Neither of Daunte Culpepper's tosses to Moss traveled more than 15 yards in the air, but Moss' feet turned them into touchdowns of 53 and 68 yards.
