     Moss is the guy who keeps us in our seat, afraid to miss something, then catapults us out of it when he does. Usually in the NFL, when a team comes up with a dynamic new offensive weapon, it takes defenses around the league a year to adjust (see Chicago Bears offense, 1999 vs. 2000). Moss is in his third year and still the leading cause of ulcers among the league's defensive coordinators.

     Culpepper forces them to take another gulp of Pepto-Bismol. Even while recovering from a ankle sprain and the stomach flu against the Saints, Culpepper again displayed poise uncommon in a young starting quarterback. He made the right reads and ran when his other options evaporated. Generally, he does everything that Ryan Leaf doesn't.

     Like the experienced magician he is, Cris Carter does his best work when attention is briefly diverted elsewhere. As the defense thins to adequately cover Moss and prepare for Culpepper's scrambles, Carter again comes up with another eight catches, 120 yards and a touchdown.

     The Vikings defense _ free at last from that nasty trio of quarterbacks Kurt Warner, Brett Favre and Peyton Manning that made the last three weeks of the regular season a fire drill in the secondary _ finally got a shot at someone they could confuse in Saints quarterback Aaron Brooks. Multiple coverages and blitzes led to interceptions by Robert Tate and Kailee Wong.

     Dare we think it? The only starting quarterbacks left for the Purple to face with Super Bowl dreams are Kerry Collins, Rich Gannon and/or Trent Dilfer. Correct us if we're wrong, but Canton isn't taking head measurements for these guys' busts.

.

FAREWELL PARTIES

     Fire up the crock pot and pour in the meatballs and beanie-wienies. We've got quite a string of going-away parties to throw this week.

     Rick Pitino sounds ready to end his own era with the Boston Celtics, after taking that downtrodden franchise and running it further into the ground. His most recent book was titled "Success is a Choice". Now he'll have time to write the follow-up: "So is Unemployment, When You Make Millions Every Year."

     Wisconsin running back Michael Bennett and North Carolina State receiver Koren Robinson are closing their college textbooks and turning pro. Bennett _ who apparently was one of the few Badger football players to pay full retail for his shoes and avoid suspension this season _ was highly successful in inheriting most of the carries Ron Dayne received for four years.

     Robinson said his seven-catch, 157-yard performance against the Gophers in the Micronpc.com Bowl made his draft stock rise. No word yet on the plans of the other 10 offensive starters for the Wolfpack, who also had their stock rise in the second half against the Gophers. Even one of the Gophers who didn't play, cornerback Willie Middlebrooks (out with a broken ankle), is turning pro.

     We still don't know if we should save a plate for Gophers football coach Glen Mason. John Cooper's choice was made for him at Ohio State, opening the door for Mason to possibly return to his alma mater. Athletic director Tom Moe is hopin' and makin' _ hoping Mason stays, making a contingency plan for a new coach if he doesn't.

     After the Gophers' run to the Sun Bowl just over a year ago, Mason interviewed for jobs at Louisiana State and Michigan State and received nibbles from other schools. Mason said at the time that he didn't actively seeking those positions, but if the phone rang with a college offer that interested him, he would listen.

     Which brings us to one of those college football riddles:

     If a phone rings in the forest and no one hears it, how can Ohio State athletic director Andy Geiger relay an offer to Mason?

.

     Brian Wicker can be contacted at bwicker@startribune.com

title:
Keeping things focused;
Vikings use small steps to accomplish bigger things
content:
As he looked back for the ball on what would be his 1,000th NFL reception, Cris Carter was so focused that he saw each revolution of the spiral as it traveled from Daunte Culpepper's hands to his own.

     "When you are getting ready to run the route," Carter said later Thursday, "you are not thinking you are on [number] 999. You're into the game. You're into that moment."

   Vikings coach Dennis Green credits that kind of narrow approach for his team's undefeated performance during its recent 19-day, four-game blitz. The Vikings have focused on the ball itself, blocking out historical perspective, upcoming celebrations and talk of future opponents.

     With a long-awaited clash with the St. Louis Rams looming Dec. 10, the Vikings took care of business in a 24-17 victory over the Detroit Lions on Thursday night. The victory guaranteed a playoff berth and gave the Vikings a chance to clinch the NFC Central title with a Tampa Bay loss Sunday.

     Just as important, there now is nothing between them and their road matchup with the defending Super Bowl champions.

     "There are a lot of things that are saying we've got a legitimate chance to reach our goal, which is to be world champions," Green said. "When you are plowing the field, you know you want to get to the other side . . . [but] you just keep your eyes focused on where you are stepping. Our guys believe that. This was a very good step, a great win, but it's only one thing of what we want to happen to us."

     The Vikings advanced to the playoffs for the eighth time in Green's nine-year tenure, and their 11-2 record gives Green his third season of at least 11 victories. Beating St. Louis would give the Vikings home-field advantage throughout the playoffs. It is lost on no one that the Vikings are undefeated this season in the Metrodome.

     Coaches began their film study Friday of the Rams, even as players were given a three-day holiday. The Vikings were happy with their defense against the Lions, tying a season high with five sacks, and were relieved their offense awoke in time from a second-half slumber.

     The Lions pulled within 17-10 early in the fourth quarter, before tailback Robert Smith sealed the game with a 43-yard touchdown run. The Vikings' 160 passing yards and 305 total yards both qualified for season lows, yet they still won and were comforted by believing their best effort is ahead of them.

     "We feel that at some stage we are probably going to hit our peak," Green said. "But we are not there yet. Momentum is on our side with four consecutive wins, but we're still really optimistic about what we can do."

     Meanwhile, Carter said he could think of no greater individual feat than joining Jerry Rice in the 1,000-reception club. The typically loquacious Carter admittedly was at a loss for words late Thursday night as he talked about the achievement.

     That the milestone catch was a touchdown, for the man who also ranks second all-time to Rice in scoring receptions, only increased its magnitude. His first career reception, Carter recalled, was a 22-yard touchdown for the Philadelphia Eagles in 1987, against the St. Louis Cardinals in Busch Stadium.

     "If I knew what to say, I would say it," Carter said. "But I really don't know. I'm overwhelmed by it all. I really am. I wish you could understand that it's something I have never, ever had as one of my goals. I'm a very goal-oriented person, but never, ever did I dream of [this]. It's the greatest accomplishment I have ever had, and it's the most humbling thing that I've ever seen."

     In recent weeks, Carter coyly has alluded to his future _ which many believe includes at least one more season on the field. He said last week he has made a decision, which he refuses to reveal officially, and on Thursday he took a moment to assess his place in Vikings history.

     "I think my legacy might be more the fact that when I came here to Minnesota, after a very, very rocky beginning of my professional career, I was a little boy," Carter said. "When I left Minnesota, I was a man. I've truly changed over the years from being a very, very selfish individual to being team-oriented. My legacy is that I want the Vikings to think Cris Carter did right by us, and he did everything above and beyond anything that we could ever do. He was the ultimate warrior, the ultimate employee, the ultimate team player.

     "I want people to say _ if you like me or dislike me _ that man, he works really hard. Everything I have in my career is because I worked hard for it. No one gave me anything."

     And now that the spiraling ball has landed safely in his hands, Carter joined Green in saying he and the Vikings have only one goal left to accomplish.

     "The only thing to polish it up would be a Super Bowl championship," Carter said. "That's what drives me to do the things that I do, the sacrifices that we make. . . . To be able to say you are the best at what you do in one year is the goal that anyone should have.

     "I have more of a personality. I wish I played an individual sport. But to win a championship, to get 60 people focused in one direction and do it over a seven-month period, it's phenomenal. To me, that's all that is left."

.

     Kevin Seifert can be contacted at kseifert@startribune.com

title: Fantasy Football Corner
content:
Start Em
QB- Jay Cutler (Bears): I know, I?know, I can't believe I'm trusting him either. But the fact is that since coming off his bye week, Cutler has been rather reliable, throwing for nine touchdowns and only three interceptions. He dissected a strong Eagles pass defense last week and didn't force the issue, but instead actually took what the defense gave him en route to throwing four touchdowns. The former Vanderbilt star will get to face the Lions this week, a team that ranks 18th in pass defense and a team that Cutler threw for 372 yards against earlier this season.

RB- Jonathon Stewart (Panthers): The Daily Show was a fantasy savior for many teams last year (1,200 total yards, 5.1 YPC, and 11 touchdowns), but in 2010 he has been a major dud. With just five games remaining, Stewart has only one double-digit fantasy effort and eclipsed 50 rushing yards just once. But Stewart seems to have finally put nagging injuries behind him, as he averaged a robust 8.2 YPC last week, totaling 98 yards on just 12 carries. Even with a timeshare still in place with Goodson also in tow, there should be plenty of yards to go around against a shaky Seattle run defense that ranks 22nd in the league.
RB- Toby Gerhart (Vikings): I'm banking this pick solely on the fact that while Adrian Peterson may appear superhuman, in reality that is not the case. Peterson reportedly told reporters that he was in "unbearable pain" after suffering a high ankle sprain against Washington this past week. I can't believe that Peterson will be healthy enough nor the Vikings dumb enough to run him out there come Sunday in a season where they are 4-7. That opens the door for Gerhart, who had a solid day in Peterson's absence (76 yards and a touchdown) and will get to face a rancid Buffalo run defense that ranks worst in the NFL (167.4 rushing yards allowed per game).
WR- DaSean Jackson (Eagles): The flashy Philadelphia receiver with Road Runner like speed has been held in check in recent weeks, totaling just seven catches for 76 yards and no scores in his last two games. But the cure-all for a slumping wideout (unless your QB is Rusty Smith) is to face the dreadful Texans pass defense (31st in pass yards allowed, given up an NFL high 25 passing touchdowns, and surrendered a league high 13 plays of 40+ yards). So fear not Jackson owners, the Road Runner will be set free come Sunday.
WR- Santonio Holmes (Jets)
TE- Kevin Boss (Giants)
K- Nate Kaeding (Chargers)
DEF- New Orleans Saints

Sit Em
QB- Ben Roethlisberger (Steelers): Big Ben has been impressive since returning from his season opening four game suspension, averaging over 260 passing yards per game while throwing for 12 touchdowns. But don't expect Roethlisberger to continue his strong season come Sunday, as he will struggle in a defensive battle against AFC North foe Baltimore. Roethlisberger has thrown for just three touchdowns in his last three games against Baltimore, and the Ravens pass defense is stout once again, ranking seventh in the NFL in pass yards allowed and giving up the sixth fewest passing touchdowns in the NFL this season. To complicate matters further, Big Ben was in a walking boot on Tuesday, battling a foot injury. He is expected to play, but there are just too many red flags.
RB- Darren McFadden (Raiders): The former fourth-overall pick finally started to live up to his unlimited potential earlier this season. But his brief dominance over the league seems like a long time ago now, as McFabulous has 18 carries for just 16 yards combined in his last two games, and it's been four games since he's found pay dirt. With the Chargers and their third ranked rush defense facing Oakland this week, look for McFadden to be bottled up once again.
WR- Roddy White (Falcons): The dynamic receiver out of UAB has had a stellar season, already eclipsing the 1,000 yard receiving mark with five games still to play. However, White has been held more in check in his last four games, being limited to single-digit fantasy outputs in three of those four contests. One of those games was against the Buccaneers, a team who will again oppose him come Sunday. Tampa Bay ranks ninth in the NFL in pass defense and limited White to 49 yards and 0 touchdowns in their earlier matchup.
RB- Ahmad Bradshaw (Giants)
WR- Wes Welker (Patriots)
TE- Jason Witten (Cowboys)
K- Matt Bryant (Buccaneers)
DEF- Philadelphia Eagles

Add Em
QB- Matt Cassel (Chiefs)
RB- Brian Westbrook (49ers)
RB- Tashard Choice (Cowboys)
WR- Jacoby Ford (Raiders)
WR- Ben Obomanu (Seahawks)
TE- Jermaine Gresham (Bengals)
K- Joe Nedney (49ers)
DEF- St. Louis Rams

title: sports in brief
content:
Stoops intends to stay at Oklahoma OKLAHOMA CITY - It didn't take Oklahoma coach Bob Stoops long to take himself out of the running for the Notre Dame job. Stoops said Monday he plans to stay at Oklahoma "hopefully for a good while" and won't be interviewing for any coaching positions after his worst regular season in 11 years with the Sooners. "What I'm saying is I'm going to be at Oklahoma next year, so I can't be in two places at once," Stoops said on a conference call. FSU's Bowden still undecided on future TALLAHASSEE, Fla.

- Bobby Bowden said Monday evening at his home that he has not made a decision about his coaching future at Florida State and plans to meet with university officials again today. The Tallahassee Democrat and ESPN.com reported Bowden - whose 388 career wins are second only to Penn State's Joe Paterno among major college coaches - is expected to announce his retirement this morning. Falcons' Ryan to miss at least 1 game FLOWERY BRANCH, Ga. - The Atlanta Falcons will be without quarterback Matt Ryan as they try to improve their playoff hopes in a key NFC game against Philadelphia this week. Coach Mike Smith said Monday that Ryan will miss at least one game with a turf toe injury. Chris Redman will start against the Eagles. GOLF Woods withdraws from own tourney THOUSAND OAKS, Calif. - Tiger Woods still isn't talking. Now he's not playing, either. Woods withdrew Monday from his own golf tournament, citing injuries from a car crash near his Florida home. His decision comes as questions continue to mount regarding what exactly happened in the wee hours of the morning last Friday - questions that most certainly would have been asked of him had he played. The world's No. 1 golfer posted a statement on his Web site saying that unspecified injuries prevented him from playing in the Chevron World Challenge. He had been scheduled to hold a press conference today for the tournament, which he hosts annually for a small, invited, field. BASKETBALL 76ers, Iverson meet PHILADELPHIA - Allen Iverson and the Philadelphia 76ers are in talks for a surprising sequel. Iverson and top Sixers officials met to discuss a possible return to the team, a scenario once thought impossible after their acrimonious split three years ago. Team president Ed Stefanski said in a statement that both parties remain noncommittal about a final decision after a nearly two-hour meeting Monday in Dallas. Marion Jones wants to play in WNBA SAN ANTONIO - Disgraced track star Marion Jones is training for a comeback - in the WNBA. San Antonio Silver Stars coach Dan Hughes confirmed Monday night that Jones has been training with his assistants to possibly play in the WNBA, more than a year after the 34-year-old was released from federal prison for lying about her doping use. Jones played college basketball at North Carolina, where she was the starting point guard on the Tar Heels' national championship team in 1994. Bulls' Hinrich out CHICAGO - Chicago Bulls guard Kirk Hinrich will be out one to two weeks with a sprained left thumb, an injury he sustained during a practice last week. Vandeweghe to coach Nets rest of season New Jersey Nets general manager Kiki Vandeweghe is set to become the team's coach for the rest of the season, an Associated Press source said Monday. But Tom Barrise, who led the team Sunday night after Lawrence Frank was fired, will coach again Wednesday against Dallas, when the Nets can set an NBA record with an 18th straight loss to begin a season. BASEBALL Jeter named SI's sportsman of year NEW YORK - Derek Jeter has been selected Sports Illustrated's sportsman of the year, becoming the first member of the New York Yankees to win the award. The magazine made the announcement Monday. Sports Illustrated Group editor Terry McDonell said Jeter "brings a dignity and elegance to the game." Caray, TBS part ways ATLANTA - Announcer Chip Caray has called his final baseball game for TBS. A network spokesman said Monday that TBS and the grandson of famed broadcaster Harry Caray have decided to part ways. TENNIS Serena fined record $82,500 for outburst Serena Williams was fined a record $82,500 for her tirade at a U.S. Open line judge and could be suspended from that tournament if she has another "major offense" at any Grand Slam in the next two years. Grand Slam administrator Bill Babcock's ruling was released Monday, and he said Williams faces a "probationary period" at tennis' four major championships in 2010 and 2011. If she has another "major offense" at a Grand Slam tournament in that time, the fine would increase to $175,000 and she would be barred from the following U.S. Open. The Associated Press

title: BOWENS OPTS FOR FLIPPING, HALL OPTS FOR GIVING
content:
One week it’s big hits, the next it’s big picks.

Nine interceptions were returned for touchdowns Sunday, the most since 2003, when 10 were run back for scores.

Linebacker David Bowens led the way with two in the Cleveland Browns’ 30-17 win over the New Orleans Saints.

Bowens’ returns went for 30 and 64 yards, the second of which he punctuated by clumsily somersaulting into the end zone.

“I can’t dance,” Bowens said, explaining that his celebration was a reprise of Reggie Bush’s end zone somersault in the NFC title game in January 2007. “I can’t do it as sweet as he does it. I’m a little heavier than he is and a little older.”

Not to be outdone was Redskins cornerback DeAngelo Hall, who returned one of his NFL-record tying four interceptions 92 yards for a score in Washington’s 17-14 win over the Chicago Bears.

“It’s kind of mind blowing,” he said. “I had my mom, my aunt, and my two cousins in the stands. The first ball went to my mom, the second ball went to my aunt and the next thing you knew everybody had a ball.”

Trivia time

Why was the 1991 World Series called the “worst-to-first” Series?

Parting baseball shot

From Dwight Perry of the Seattle Times: Shouldn’t the Mariners — who traded away Cliff Lee and passed on drafting local phenom Tim Lincecum— be getting a World Series share from both teams?

Football funnies

•Let’s just say that UCLA’s 60-13 battering at No.?1 Oregon didn’t play to rave reviews back home.

“This was the Bruins wearing their Halloween costume 10 days early,” wrote Bill Plaschke of the Los Angeles Times. “They went as a bye week.”

•“If Brett Favre really wants to impress women,” a female friend suggested to The Orlando Sentinel’s Mike Bianchi, “he should text them photos of his paycheck.”

•Headline at TheOnion.com: “NFL fines ‘Monday Night Football’ for helmet-to- helmet hit.”

•RJ Currie of SportsDeke.com, on Vikings Favre amassing 3,467 incomplete passes in his NFL career:

“Even more if you include a) the playoffs, and b) hostesses and massage therapists.”

•High school football games on the island of Kauai have been moved from Friday nights to Saturday afternoons so that migrating Newell’s shearwaters, an endangered species of bird, won’t mistake stadiums’ artificial light for the moon and stars and get disoriented.

Sounds logical. The New Mexico Lobos sometimes have the same problem with the sun on Saturday afternoons. Names in the news

Cam Hutchinson of the Saskatoon StarPhoenix, on the best part about tennis star Maria Sharapova’s upcoming marriage to Lakers guard Sasha Vujacic:

“The ring bearer will be Kobe Bryant.”Trivia answer

It was the first time two last-place teams from the previous season had met in a World Series. The Minnesota Twins beat the Atlanta Braves 1-0 in Game 7 in what many called one of the best World Series in baseball history.

COMPILED BY P.K. DANIEL FROM NEWS SERVICES, ONLINE REPORTS

title: COACHES CALL OUT DEFENSE MIKE MCCARTHY SAYS PROBLEMS STOPPING THE RUN ARE AT THE CORE OF PACKERS' 3-GAME SKID.
content:
In the wake of Sunday's 27-24 loss to the Atlanta Falcons, Green Bay Packers coach Mike McCarthy had this to say of his team's three-game losing streak: "Our house is messy right now."

As it turns out, not only do the Packers need a maidservice, they also need to have their furnace and central air unit fixed, given the way their defense has played in back-to-back-to-back losses to Dallas, Tampa Bay and Atlanta.

"I look at the defense as a thermostat in a football game," McCarthy said Monday, one day after his defense surrendered 176 yards on 36 carries to the Falcons' No. 1-ranked rushing offense. "They need to keep the game in order, and it starts with stopping the run, and we're not doing a very good job of it."

* Wilde on Packers blog * Week 5 effort goes for naught No, they aren't. During their current three-game losing streak, the Packers have allowed an average of 190.3 yards per game on the ground.

Entering Monday night's game between New Orleans and Minnesota, the Packers ranked 27th in the 32-team NFL in rushing defense (161.4 yards per game). They're also 24th in scoring defense (25.6 points per game) and 25th in total yardage (363.2 yards per game).

Of the five teams ranked below Green Bay in run defense, only 32nd-ranked Indianapolis (2-2) has a non-losing record, while No. 28 St. Louis (0-4), No. 29 Cincinnati (0-5), No. 30 Detroit (0-4) and No. 31 Kansas City (1-4) have a combined record of 1-17.

"The biggest glaring weakness we're having right now is we're giving up too many rushing yards. I said it last week," said linebacker A.J. Hawk, referring to the Buccaneers' 178-yard effort, which followed Dallas' 217-yard output. "Rushing defense is not rocket science. You stay in your gaps, you're physical and you tackle. That's not what we're doing. It sounds easy."

The coaches' explanation for why they aren't doing it also sounds oddly simple. Both McCarthy and defensive tackles coach Robert Nunn described the Packers' biggest problem on run defense the same way Monday.

"The biggest thing that factored for us in run defense was a number of times -- it's almost like we took turns doing it -- was guys trying to do too much," said McCarthy, whose team plays at Seattle on Sunday. "Run defense is all about gap control, the proper technique and playing to the guy next to you. And the way we're organized on defense, it's very evident when something goes wrong, (it's because of) someone not being in their gap."

Said Nunn: "That is the biggest thing we were guilty of."

The "perfect example," McCarthy said, came at the end of the game, when the Falcons took over at the Green Bay 47-yard line with 1 minute 55 seconds left after the Packers' failed onside kick. Armed with two timeouts, the Packers needed to force a three-and-out punt to get the ball back to the offense with a chance to drive for the game-tying field goal to force overtime.

After stopping Michael Turner (26 carries, 121 yards) for a 1-yard gain on first down and calling their second-to-last timeout with 1:49 left, Turner gained 8 yards off left guard on second down, with defensive end Jeremy Thompson getting shoved out of his gap and defensive tackle Johnny Jolly being dislodged from his.

Linebacker Nick Barnett couldn't stop Turner in the hole after a 4-yard gain, and by the time Tramon Williams, Brady Poppinga and Aaron Kampman finally brought him down, Turner had set up a third-and-1.

After the Packers' final timeout with 1:42 to go, Turner ran left again, safety Nick Collins couldn't avoid a block and Turner churned his way for a 2-yard gain to ice the game.

"That's exactly what I am talking about," McCarthy said. "It's the details of the things that are involved in good run defense. It just wasn't very clean."

The poor run defense is also having an effect on the scuffling pass rush, which failed to sack Atlanta quarterback Matt Ryan and hit him just once. It was the second straight sack-less game for the Packers, who haven't taken down an opposing quarterback since Cullen Jenkins sacked Dallas' Tony Romo just before halftime on Sept. 21.

Jenkins, who is done for the year after suffering a torn pectoral muscle against Tampa Bay, has 2.5 of the Packers' nine sacks on the season. Pass-rushing end Kabeer Gbaja-Biamila has only a half-sack registered against Detroit on Sept. 14 and has been ineffective.

"I personally thought we were going to get more pressure on (Ryan)," said defensive end Aaron Kampman, who has a team-high four sacks, but none in the last two games. "We're paid to get to the quarterback, and we didn't get there enough."

Against the run, Kampman said, "we've gotten out of our gaps, we've played sloppy, we haven't been stout at the point, we've missed tackles. It's a number of things."

Poppinga said the Packers "are going to have a lot of uphill battles" if they don't improve their run defense because "when the defense has a difficult time stopping the run, it really puts your whole team at a disadvantage."

Cornerback Charles Woodson, meanwhile, said the defensive struggles have called the team's identity into question.

"We as a defense talk about being one of the better defenses in the league, and that's the way we feel. We came in here with the same defense, the same scheme for the third year in a row, and it seems we're actually backtracking at this point," Woodson said. "Everybody just has to look at their individual game and ask what they could have done better -- and work this week on actually doing that and get ready for the next game.

"There are a bunch of different things. Are they correctable? Absolutely. But it's one thing to talk about it, it's one thing to do it. The coach can come in and yell all he wants to, the players can yell all they want to. But if you don't go out on any given Sunday and make sure those things happen for you, you don't win ballgames."

title: JETS NOT CELEBRATING YET. REMAIN WARY OF BELICHICK & CO.
content:
SOME OF THE JETS heard the news as they walked off the field Sunday at Dolphin Stadium: Tom Brady,down. Knee injury. Looks bad.

On the trip home from Miami, they saw the replay on the in-flight TVs, Chiefs safety Bernard Pollard crashing into Brady's left knee. The Patriots confirmed yesterday that the reigning league MVP is done for the season, changing the landscape of the AFC East.

If you think the Jets were celebrating at 30,000 feet, think again.

"That's laughable," guard Brandon Moore said yesterday in Florham Park, N.J., commenting on the notion that the defending AFC champs are dead. "To say their team is done and that it opens things up (in the division) . . . they're too good a team. He didn't make that team. The team made that team. It's still going to be a big battle in the AFC East."

Perhaps fittingly, the Patriots' first game in seven years without Brady will be against the Jets, who have been chasing their rival like a lost puppy running after a speeding car.

They've dropped 12 of 14 to the Patriots in the Brady era, including a wild-card playoff loss, so they're in no position to administer last rites.

Especially not this week, with the Patriots (1-0) coming to town for Sunday's showdown at the Meadowlands. They will have Matt Cassel at quarterback, making his first start since high school, but at least one Jet believes the Patriots still are the team to beat.

"Oh, absolutely," linebacker Eric Barton said. "The last time they were in this situation, they won a Super Bowl, if you can remember correctly."

That happened in 2001, when Jets linebacker Mo Lewis wiped out Drew Bledsoe in Week 2, giving Brady his big shot. The untested Brady took over an unheralded team and wound up leading the Patriots to their first Super Bowl victory.

Eric Mangini was a member of Bill Belichick's staff that year, and you can bet he'll be sharing that history lesson with his own players - if he hasn't already. Mangini lauded Belichick's ability to coach the team through adversity, and he also listed several reasons why he believes the Patriots will remain competitive.

"(Randy) Moss, (Wes) Welker, (Laurence) Maroney, (Ben) Watson, Sammy Morris, you can go right down the line," said Mangini, listing New England's offensive weapons. "They're loaded."

Maybe so, but it will be hard to replace Brady's production. In 14 career starts against the Jets, he has 17 touchdown passes and only six interceptions. The Jets have endured their share of devastating quarterback injuries over the years, so they can relate. Now, for a change, they have the hottest quarterback in the division.

Brett Favre's arrival and successful debut in Miami, coupled with the Brady injury, has changed the perception of the Jets. Suddenly, they're contenders. The gap has closed. The door is open. Pick your favorite cliché.

According to Mangini, nothing has changed. He felt the Jets had a realistic shot to win the division before Brady went down.

"I don't think we went into the year thinking that that wasn't a possibility," he said. "We went in with the expectation that, you know, that was something achievable."

Now it's closer than achievable; it's right there for the Jets, who haven't won the AFC East since 2002. After Sunday's 20-14 win in Miami, where they showed improvement in their two weakest areas from last season (rushing offense and rushing defense), the Jets are feeling pretty good about themselves.

"Going into the year, we looked at the upgrades on our offense and defense," tight end Chris Baker said. "We want to go to the Super Bowl this year, but we can only focus on what we can control. We really can't worry about what (losing Brady) does to them."

title: A reluctant player thrust into ugly game
content:
Unlike his unexpected and unwelcome nemesis, Donovan McNabb still has to go to work Sunday.

Thanks to Rush Limbaugh, who immolated himself while trying to torch McNabb on ESPN, it will be anything but an ordinary day at the office for the Eagles quarterback. He has been singled out, turned against his will into the focal point of a sudden and almost violent spasm of media interest.

He must feel like the victim of a mugging. Limbaugh could have made a general statement about the way the media treat minority quarterbacks, but no. He chose McNabb as his example, and now McNabb's performance against Washington will be viewed through an entirely different lens.

Just like that, it's much more than a football game.

The quarterback handled the off-the-field part with remarkable grace. In a routine press conference made extraordinary by the presence of CNN's satellite truck, McNabb hit just the right notes in responding to Limbaugh's remarks. He sounded for all the world like the people who raised him, Sam and Wilma McNabb of Chicago.

Remember, as a boy on that city's South Side, Donovan McNabb watched the Bears play on TV all the time. He loved Walter Payton, as any Bears fan would, but he also became aware of another player, a quarterback named Randall Cunningham.

The former Eagles star was all the proof McNabb needed that he could someday play quarterback in the NFL. He is keenly aware that somewhere out there, on any given Sunday, a kid in some living room could point at the screen and say that he wants to be just like No. 5. Just like Donovan McNabb.

"You have kids all across the world who wear our jerseys," McNabb said. "You have kids who watch the things that we do and say, 'He's the best player in the league and I want to be just like him.' But when you sit there and pinpoint a guy because of the color of his skin and not really on his performance and the things he's been able to do while he's been in the league, that kind of makes you look like you're the bad guy.

"My worries were not about what was said, but about the people who were watching. What about the people in the African American homes - the kids, the parents - when they hear something like that on national TV, on ESPN?"

That's the poison in the groundwater after a human toxic spill like Limbaugh has his way. That's what ESPN invited into America's Sunday football tradition, the chance for fathers to explain to sons why McNabb's race should be an issue in a discussion about his performance.

When he was drafted by the Eagles, McNabb went out of his way to avoid questions about race. Really, that has continued all along. Ask him about Cunningham's influence and he brings up Steve Young. Ask him about Steve McNair's game, and he subtly shifts the discussion to include Rich Gannon and Brett Favre.

The reason for this is simple. McNabb understands. He knows the code words for "black quarterback" and he sidesteps them as if they were 300-pound defensive tackles. To his credit, he wants to be judged only as a quarterback, as compared to all quarterbacks, and not as a black quarterback.

He understands all of this because of his parents. Sam and Wilma were sports fans, too. They understood the history and where their younger son would fit into that history. They were proud that Donovan was drafted higher than any African American quarterback in NFL history. But here's what defines the McNabbs: They reached out to Michael Vick's family and expressed the same pride when Atlanta took Vick No. 1 overall.

To say these milestones weren't significant is to say the Dodgers' signing of Jackie Robinson was a mere baseball transaction. Sometimes, what's happening in sports shines a light on what is happening in society. So, yes, there has been a lot of media focus on the erasure of one of the last color barriers in professional sports, the one that turned untold numbers of potential black quarterbacks into safeties, wide receivers and running backs.

Ancient history? McNabb himself was asked to change positions by several of the college coaches who recruited him. That was in the early '90s.

"It didn't bother me then," McNabb said, "because it's not going to stop me from fulfilling my dream. My dream is to become a great human being, a person that people can rely on and trust, and a good football player."

All of this makes McNabb a poor choice for someone trying to prove there is a kind of affirmative-action advantage in being a black quarterback. He has earned the right to be here, and he has been praised and criticized, cheered and booed, based on his performance.

And now this. Limbaugh talks his way out of his "dream" gig at ESPN and McNabb ends up getting slimed. Through no fault of his own, he will be watched differently on Sunday. Is he "not that good," as Limbaugh said, or is he the established NFL star that his statistics and his won-lost record say he is?

There can be no question about one thing. Eagles fans reserve the right to boo McNabb, but they can't be happy that an outsider took such a cheap shot at their QB. Limbaugh should listen to the crowd here Sunday. Maybe he'll learn something.

Contact columnist Phil Sheridan at 215-854-2844 or psheridan@phillynews.com.

title: Woebegone in Minnesota? Vikings' maligned defense doesn't care what you think -- it's in the NFC final
content:
EDEN PRAIRIE, Minn. -- They get a little tired of the ugly stepchild
routine.

They hear what's said, read what's written and they know the difference
between a compliment and an insult, a pat on the back and a stab
between the shoulder blades.

No, it's not easy being the Minnesota Vikings' defense and wearing
a target sign for opposing quarterbacks and critics.

"We know we just have to stick together," cornerback Robert
Tate says. "We can't get caught up in what other people say."

The defense got a little bit of its confidence back in Saturday's
34-16 victory against New Orleans that sent the Vikings into the
NFC Championship game for the second time in three seasons.

A pair of interceptions and a couple of sacks served as highlights
as the Vikings limited the Saints to scores on just three of their
12 possessions. They didn't allow a touchdown until midway through
the third quarter when they led 24-3.

"Our defense came in with a lot of confidence," coach Dennis
Green says. "We had to step up and carry the water. We were confident
that we could play better, whether it be forcing turnovers or
putting more pressure on the quarterback. But it really starts
with stopping the run."

The Vikings didn't stop anything (except tackling) through a sorry
stretch that saw them go 0-for-December and plunge to 28th (among
31 teams) in total defense.

They began the month at 11-2 and emerged 11-5, with their chance
for overall home-field advantage throughout the playoffs gone
and in the possession of the New York Giants. In three games against
playoff-caliber teams with capable running backs, the Vikings'
defense could not get off the field.

Against St. Louis in a 40-29 loss, the Vikings gave up 508 yards,
135 to Marshall Faulk on the ground. The Rams dominated time of
possession, holding the ball for 34:24.

In losing to Green Bay 33-28, the Vikings allowed 449 yards, with
Ahman Green running for 161 and the Packers dominating the clock
for 35:41.

In falling 31-10 to Indianapolis, Edgerrin James rushed for 128
yards, and the Colts piled up 411 and held the ball for 33:59.

Sets tone against Saints

So much for the bad news. Against the Saints, good news prevailed.

Rested after a bye week, with some more aggressive tactics in
their bag of tricks, the Vikings' defense actually set the tone.
They blitzed, moved their defensive backs around and made their
coverages more difficult for an inexperienced quarterback, Aaron
Brooks, to read.

"We wanted to bring it," free safety Orlando Thomas says. "I
don't know if we rattled him, but I did see him running out of
the pocket a lot."

In forcing a punt after three plays on the game's opening possession,
the defense gave the offense a little bit of a lift. And the offense
returned the favor by scoring on its third play, a 53-yard pass
from Daunte Culpepper to Randy Moss.

"It was big," strong safety Robert Griffith says. "We wanted
to get off to a quick start."

Wanted to and needed to.

"That was a goal going into the game," receiver Cris Carter
says. "We wanted to get on the field defensively and then get
off. That was big because then Randy comes back and makes the
big play and gets the home crowd involved.

"When you go into the game and you get a three-and-out like we
did and then come right down and score, that's a tremendous amount
of confidence for our defense."

Vikings' style

It also lends itself more to the Vikings' modus operandi. Get
a lead, force the opponent out of its game plan and away from
the run, bend but don't break -- that's the ticket.

"Absolutely," Green says. "We know this. Our style has been
to get out in front if we can, get some points on the board, make
(the other team) have to throw a lot of balls."

That's the way the win against the Saints played out. New Orleans
ran 13 times in the first half, and just four in the second half
with the game slipping away. Brooks' first interception (by Tate)
set up a touchdown. The second, swiped by linebacker Kailee Wong,
stopped a drive that had reached the Minnesota 11.

Confidence, unfortunately, is not the defense's lone problem.
The Vikings had seven players named to the Pro Bowl but only one,
Griffith, plays defense. Balance is just a dream.

Look at that potent offense, with No. 1 picks at both tackles,
at quarterback, at running back, at receiver.

Now deconstruct the defense, with Tate -- a converted receiver
chosen in the sixth round in 1997 -- and free agent Wasswa Serwanga
at cornerback. And with Fernando Smith, cut in training camp and
re-signed before the last regular-season game, jumping back into
the starting lineup at right end.

Attempts to improve

It's not as if the Vikings never draft defensive players. They
spent a No. 1 pick in 1999 on troubled defensive end Dimitrius
Underwood, now in protective custody in Florida after a second
suicide attempt.

They drafted defensive tackle Chris Hovan No. 1 last spring, linebacker
Dwayne Rudd No. 1 in 1997, Wong No. 2 in 1998, defensive ends
Derrick Alexander and Duane Clemons with first-round picks in
1995 and '96, and cornerback Dewayne Washington No. 1 in 1994.
Alexander, Clemons and Washington left as free agents.

And it's not as if they didn't try a new coaching approach. After
Foge Fazio departed, Emmitt Thomas replaced him as defensive coordinator,
repositioned all the linebackers, moved defensive end John Randle
up and down the line and still wound up with a unit that posted
31 sacks, the Vikings' fewest since 1984.

Pointing this out, even in the friendly and helpful manner with
which most newspaper columnists are endowed, only makes the Vikings
angry.

"We're 28th. So what?" Griffith snaps. "We're going to the
NFC Championship game."

They'd have to be optimistic about their chances of coping with
the Giants' offense. The Giants' touchdowns in their 20-10 victory
against Philadelphia came from their defense and special teams.
The offense managed only 237 yards, but the defense dominated
-- sort of a cracked mirror image of the Vikings.

A victory Sunday against New York at Giants Stadium would put
the Vikings in the Super Bowl for the first time since 1977, and
people will wonder, of course, if this defense can get it done
when the NFL watchword is that offense sells tickets and defense
wins championships.

"You said the key word there -- people," linebacker Ed McDaniel
says. "I really don't care what people think about us. Basically,
amongst us defensive players in this room, we know what we can
do."

title: Goodbye to a year, but never to hopes and dreams
content:
He was the boy next door. Friendly, direct, casual. Not afraid to make eye contact, not so serious he couldn't smile at himself or the bedlam around him.

It was another media day at the Super Bowl. Who wanted to talk to Matt Cassel, the perpetual understudy? Me, and two or three others.

A few feet away, dozens of media types elbowed each other and crowded around Tom Brady.In four days he and his New England Patriots teammates would play the New York Giants in the Super Bowl. Now he was simply throwing out answers to questions he had heard before.

On that day in late January, Cassel was asked the what-if question he had heard before. What if Brady can't play?

''If Tom has to come out, my job is to not make the bad play,'' said Cassel, looking very earnest. ''My job isn't to make the big play.''

The headline over my column the next day read: Great insurance, but let's not cash it in.

That's why, when the boss suggested I peer into 2009 and predict what will happen, I weaseled. Better to look back at some of the best surprises of the past year and use them as examples for the tough times ahead.

Cassel was knocked down by fans and football wise guys before he even started his first NFL game back in September. Bring in Phil Simms' kid, Chris. Bring in Daunte Culpepper. See if Vinny Testaverde's arm is still attached to his shoulder. Cassel can't even step into Brady's shoes, let alone fill them, predicted the doubters.

Cassel persevered - a word rarely used anymore - and his example might have been the best for many of us. There were others in 2008.

John Winkin, for one. The oldest college coach in America at age 89 was felled by a stroke last December. By June he still had difficulty with the right side of his body but was very much alive.

''What choice did I have?'' he said at the Husson College baseball field that bears his name. ''I had two. I could live or I could die. If I get taken, I want people to know I tried like hell.''

He sat in a wheelchair pushed by his son, David. You couldn't help but notice his busy left foot helping propel the wheelchair forward.

Winkin was at the Larry Mahaney Senior Baseball All-Star Game. When the time came for the national anthem, David Winkin asked his father if he wanted to stand. ''Absolutely,'' said the man who was a naval officer in the Pacific on Dec. 7, 1941.

I saw John Winkin about three months later at Colby College for the dedication of the Harold Alfond Stadium, where Colby men and women play football and compete in track. Still in a wheelchair, still greeting friends with cries of delight and a big smile. ''I'm not going to quit.''

Many people thought John Wiechman and Quinton Porter had quit playing football. Wiechman was the Bonny Eagle High star and Fitzpatrick Trophy winner who was dismissed by fans as a bad apple, a poor example. His coach, teammates and friends at Southern Connecticut State University found a different Wiechman walking around the New Haven campus.

On the field he led the Owls in touchdowns (17) and rushing (1,047 yards). He was named the Northeast 10 Conference offensive player of the year. Off the field he was simply John Wiechman, the college sophomore enjoying life.

''I enjoy him as a person,'' said his coach, Rich Cavenaugh. Whatever baggage Wiechman had shouldered at Bonny Eagle had been tossed aside.

Porter never seemed to live up to other's expectations after he graduated from Portland High. He's the quarterback who lost his starting job to Matt Ryan at Boston College. Porter made the Houston Texans' game-day roster but never took a snap in the NFL. He was ready to put his BC degree in finance to work when the Hamilton Tiger-Cats of the Canadian Football League called.

On Oct. 4 he played the game of his dreams against Montreal, one of the league's best teams. Five touchdown passes, 27 completions out of 32 passes for 429 yards. He rushed for 41 yards. The Tiger-Cats won and Porter was hailed as the CFL's next new star. It was a dizzying ascent for someone who has been knocked down in life every which way.

Jorge Abiague of the Portland Boxing Club won his first national Golden Gloves titles in May. It wasn't his first try. Ten years after fleeing Cuba with family members, he had a quiet but unshakeable belief in himself that he could succeed after losing.

''I won for my family,'' he said. ''I won for my teammates. I won for Bobby Russo, my coach.''

Look around as you wave goodbye to 2008. Know that Mark Rogers and Tip Fairchild have persevered through their arm and shoulder injuries and still dream of pitching in the major leagues.

Know that 43-year-old Sue Wiemer of Freeport delievered a baby girl five months after competing in the World Masters Athletics Indoor Championships in France.

The pregnancy was a surprise. Her hope, before leaving for the competition, was that she wouldn't finish last in her events, which included the demanding pentathlon.

She beat four competitors on the track and three others who dropped out. ''I'm thinking that if I was missing a limb and on fire, I still would have crawled it in,'' she wrote in an e-mail in March after returning to Maine.

Tough lady. Remember her and so many others.

Happy New Year.



Staff Writer Steve Solloway can be contacted at 791-6412 or at:

ssolloway@pressherald.com

title: Supercharged week for Rams, Patriots
content:
FRESH from their weekend conference successes, the St Louis Rams and New England Patriots arrived here yesterday for the biggest of American football games - Super Bowl XXXVI.

Without the usual week off because of the week lost early in the season following the September 11 attacks on New York and Washington, this year's Super Bowl combatants have barely had time to digest much less savour their hard-fought victories on Monday (SA time).

"We really haven't done much of anything other than unpack, repack and fly down to New Orleans. But it's good to be here," said Patriots head coach Bill Belichick. "We're still flying kind of high."

The Patriots stunned the favoured Steelers 24-17 in Pittsburgh to earn the franchise's third trip to the Super Bowl - coincidentally, all in New Orleans. The Rams held off a stubborn, upset-minded Philadelphia Eagles team 29-24 to earn their second Super Bowl trip in three years.

Heading off the expected barrage of quarterback questions, Belichick said he planned to announce his starting QB for Monday's game following today's practice session.

Tom Brady, who took over from the injured Drew Bledsoe in week two and never surrendered the starting job, suffered a sprained ankle during the second quarter against the Steelers and Bledsoe stepped in to secure the win.

There is no doubt about who will start for the Rams with NFL Most Valuable Player Kurt Warner - the MVP of St Louis's Super Bowl XXXIV victory - having displayed no ill effects from the rib injury that bothered him in the run-up to the NFC title game.

Rams head coach Mike Martz said Warner was "surprisingly not sore".

Martz was also pleased Orlando Pace, the Rams's most dominating offensive lineman, appeared to have a less severe strain to his knee ligaments than the coach had thought.

"He should practice. He's not in as much pain as we thought or feared this morning, so that looks good," Martz said. Because of the unusually tight schedule, both teams left assistant coaches back home to work on their game plans.

But for two of the Rams most outstanding players, yesterday's flight was not just a trip to the Super Bowl but a homecoming.

Superstar running back Marshall Faulk and seven-time Pro Bowl cornerback Aeneas Williams grew up in New Orleans, starred on high school teams here and even worked at Saints games at the SuperDome, site of this Super Bowl.

"This is a great opportunity for them to come down here and play this game in their hometown," Martz said. "I'm excited and extremely happy they have this opportunity."

Both contributed mightily to St Louis' sensational 14-2 season and to Monday's victory. Faulk, the workhorse in the Rams' come-from-behind win, rushed for 159 yards and two touchdowns, while Williams intercepted a Donovan McNabb pass in the final two minutes to thwart the Eagles on their final drive.

title: NFL players to start, and to avoid, in Week 7 of fantasy football
content:
It's hard to believe a guy who's been cut or benched for the likes of Jake Plummer, Rex Grossman, Chris Simms and Jay Fiedler could come back and become a solid quarterback.

But Brian Griese has overcome those devastating blows to his ego to become quite the fantasy football find in just three starts for the Bears -- his fourth team since 2002. He's thrown seven TDs and averages nearly 300 yards. He's had some interceptions, but few as mindboggling or hideous as Grossman's.

The 32-year-old Griese also has a huge experience edge on Grossman. He's put up a couple 20-touchdown seasons as well as a few 3,000-yard seasons in his career, and he's played for QB gurus such as Mike Shanahan, Jon Gruden and Norv Turner.

That's all well and good for real football, but Griese has even more in his favor from a fantasy standpoint: The Bears aren't running well or playing defense. Cedric Benson has been a flop, and the once-feared defense is allowing all sorts of points and yards.

It all adds up to a decent quarterback launching a ton of balls to a good receiving corps, with little threat of a running play or benching. It's fantasy perfection.

As you wonder what Mike Ditka makes of the Bears playing defenseless, run-free football, here's a look at some players to start in Week 7, some to avoid, and a few long shots who just may pan out:

QUARTERBACKS

A SAFE BET

• Well, well, look who decided to show up. Drew Brees' inept play helped put the Saints -- not to mention thousands of misguided fantasy owners -- in a deep, deep hole. But he looked like his old self last week and faces the bumbling Falcons.

• Outside of one monster game, Philadelphia's Donovan McNabb hasn't looked quite right. But he faces a softer-than-usual Chicago defense, and I'm certain if I keep saying McNabb will be good then he'll come through for my fantasy team.

• Washington's Jason Campbell has scored in four straight games. He faces an Arizona defense that last week was picked apart by 43-year-old Vinny Testaverde just days removed from a retirement regimen of watching TV and running errands

• OK, so Kyle Boller and the Ravens offense aren't good, but the odds are long that the Bills' 31st-ranked pass defense flukes its way into another five-interception day. The Bills have allowed an NFL-high three 300-yard passers.

• Jeff Garcia has been just decent, but Detroit is a defensive disaster. Plus, Garcia may want to prove he's not the offensive disaster he was with Detroit in 2005.

TAKE A SHOT

It is silly to suggest a start for the Jets' Chad Pennington (6 TDS, 6 INTs), but everybody should get a start time against the Bengals.

BACK AWAY SLOWLY AND NOBODY GETS HURT

Please, in the name of Jim Miller, don't start these guys:

• Even though Marc Bulger and however many ribs he has left are back this week, the winless Rams are such a mess that Bulger's a bad start even against Seattle's soft defense.

• When did Vince Young and the Titans get so boring? Many will overlook his horrid stats to think he'll put on a big show in his hometown of Houston.

• Pittsburgh's Ben Roethlisberger is playing well, but he'll just hand off against the Broncos and the NFL's worst run defense that's allowing nearly 190 yards a game.

RUNNING BACKS

ALL DAY LONG

• Come on, NFL, is it really fair to make the winless Dolphins play the unstoppable Patriots? Despite the ethical questions, look for Miami's Ronnie Brown to provide his usual shame-saving stats.

• Likewise, watch the inactive lists Sunday morning and start whoever's running for the Patriots, even if it's that Heath guy. The Dolphins are allowing about 170 yards rushing and an NFL-high 10 TDS, and they've chosen this week to start the rebuilding process.

• Frank Gore and the 49ers haven't done squat offensively and face a punishing Giants defense that's led a four-game win streak. But Gore has had two weeks to rest, he's due for a big game, and the Giants are due for a bad one.

• The Jets finally decided to use offseason investment Thomas Jones last week, and he went over 100 yards. He may get 40 carries and perhaps a TD against the Bengals, who only stop the run by leaving receivers uncovered.

• How far has Seattle's Shaun Alexander fallen when he's on the sideline while a random fullback gets the fourth-quarter carries? Even with his 2.4 yards a carry the past two weeks, he should timidly tiptoe through the Rams with great success.

HE COULD FIND A SEAM

Is Buffalo's Marshawn Lynch ever going to face a weak run defense? The Ravens may stop him often, but he'll also catch some passes.

RED FLAGS

• Here's something you never say at Chester Taylor's house: "So, Chester, how's that job share thing going?" He had more carries than Adrian Peterson last week, but even the Vikings must see that Taylor's best position is on the bench.

• It may seem nutty to bench Travis Henry, but a beat-up Denver line may be no match for the healthier Steelers. Henry has scored only one TD, and has been held under 70 yards in two of his past three games.

• Where has Dallas' Marion Barber gone? It is cool to watch him punch guys as he runs down the field, but he hasn't scored in three games. He faces the Vikings, the only team not to allow a rushing TD.

WIDE RECEIVERS

THROW HIM THE DARN BALL!

• Laveranues Coles had just one catch last week, and the Jets seem to have lost the ability to pass. They'll feel better when they see the Bengals, who have allowed more TD passes (13) than everyone but Cleveland.

• It's too easy to say Dallas' Terrell Owens will light up the Vikings' NFL-worst pass defense, so throw in No. 2 receiver Patrick Crayton, too. He's scored four times in his past three games.

• Don't just pick up the Saints' David Patten, but throw him into your starting lineup. Seven weeks in, and the Falcons still have that "What are we doing here?" look about them.

• Tennessee's Derrick Mason is going for about eight catches and only 70 yards a game, but Buffalo's already allowed four 100-yard receiving games.

• Remember way back when Lee Evans used to catch TDs for Buffalo? Here's a wild guess that his scoreless streak ends. (If not, don't cut him; he didn't score in six of his first seven games last year.)

MAYBE THROW HIM THE DARN BALL?

Maybe Atlanta's Roddy White will be so eager to avoid another rah-rah college-type speech from his coach that he'll exploit the shaky Saints secondary.

SHOW HIM THE DARN BENCH

• Bench Arizona's Larry Fitzgerald and Anquan Boldin against a Redskins defense that has allowed only two TD passes, an NFL-low. The Cardinals this week boast the twin QB threat of Tim Rattay and Tim Hasselbeck. (Apparently Kent Graham and Dave Brown were unavailable.)

• In case you're that fantasy owner who checks your roster once a week, here's a public service announcement: Chris Chambers was traded to the Chargers, who aren't playing this week.

• Speaking of Chambers, his departure means more playing time in Miami for Ted Ginn Jr. If you're weighing the merits of the Cleo Lemon-to-Ginn connection, your fantasy team is in some trouble.

DON'T FORGET THE BYE WEEK: Carolina, Green Bay, Cleveland and San Diego are off, meaning this is the only week you want to face the owner who drafted LaDainian Tomlinson.

FREE AGENT SHOPPING LIST

Pick up these guys if they're available in your league: RBMichael Bennett

WHAT DO I KNOW?

Here's the best and worst of last week's projections:

Big Hits: I predicted success for Brian Griese (381 yards, 3 TDs), Derek Anderson (4 TDs), Cleo Lemon (4 TDs), Adrian Peterson (224 yards, 3 TDs), Maurice Jones-Drew (184 total yards, 2 TDs), Matt Hasselbeck (362 yards, 2 TDs) and Bobby Engram (113 yards). I expected failure for Shaun Alexander (35 yards) and James Jones (0 catches).

Big Misses: I expected more bad things for Drew Brees(2 TDs) and Amani Toomer (TD). I saw big things happening for Torry Holt (33 yards), Dwayne Bowe (46 yards) and Vincent Jackson (5 yards). I thought Kurt Warner's aging body would last beyond the first quarter.

title: Hair go the Broncos
content:
DENVER -- Denver Broncos quarterback Jake Plummer, with a scraggly beard and hair he has to brush from his eyes, looks the part of a man leading a quest for the promised land.

Through several games this season, the 6-2, 212-pound Bronco has played it well. Not since John Elway retired after shepherding the Broncos to consecutive Super Bowl victories have the reviews about a Denver quarterback been so rave.

"Some people nationally have talked about him possibly being an MVP candidate," Broncos safety John Lynch says. "I'd agree."

But after a 31-27 loss at the Kansas City Chiefs, Plummer and the AFC West-leading Broncos are at a crossroads as they host the Baltimore Ravens on Sunday. One path leads to the kind of late-season swoon that's been their undoing in recent years. The other magnifies the attention on "Jake the Snake's" apparent change of skin as the Broncos (9-3) try to emerge from the shadow looming largest: They haven't won a playoff game since Elway's departure in 1999.

"We are in a position to take care of our own destiny, and it starts this week," Plummer told reporters Wednesday. "We can't look forward or too far forward to anything."

The Broncos have a schedule cushy enough for a distinct on-paper advantage over their chasers, the San Diego Chargers and Kansas City, both 8-4 with top teams left to battle. Although the Broncos' stiffest challenge figures to be a New Year's Eve game at San Diego, the Chargers have back-to-back road games at the Indianapolis Colts and Kansas City. The Chiefs also face the New York Giants on the road and the Cincinnati Bengals at home.

If the Broncos win out, they would earn at least the No.2 seed in the AFC playoffs. Home-field advantage is critical to the Broncos, who have faltered badly at Indianapolis in first-round games the last two years.

"We have the makings of having a team that can get it done," Plummer says.

Relying on the people around him

In his third season with Denver, Plummer, 31 this month, is playing like a quarterback who can get it done. His 91.2 rating is sixth best in the league.

He is learning to limit the renegade style that long ago earned him his serpentine moniker to facial-hair decisions. He is operating more comfortably, decisively and efficiently within Denver coach Mike Shanahan's highly controlled system.

"He has a better feel for the system, the supporting cast around him and what he's being asked to do," says Shanahan, who gave Plummer a $6 million bonus in March rather than let him leave through free agency. He reasoned Plummer shouldn't be judged until a third year in the system.

The most tangible measure of Plummer's improvement is his drop in interceptions. After throwing 20 last season, he has six this year. While he was going through a team-record stretch of 81/2 games and 229 passes without an interception, local reporters dubbed him "No-Mistake Jake."

"I know that if the defense wasn't playing as well or if we weren't playing as well on special teams or if we weren't running the ball as well, then they would be saying nothing about me," Plummer says.

Indeed, after his two interceptions and a defensive breakdown at Kansas City, the niggling in the local media this week has focused on the Broncos' poor record on third-down conversions and their fourth-quarter scoring stagnation.

The Broncos' modus operandi this season has been to build big leads, then hang on for the victory. As games and tensions get tighter, Plummer's new sense of discipline undoubtedly will be tested.

"The thing that we've tried to convince Jake of is that you don't have to make every play," offensive coordinator Gary Kubiak says. "You've got a lot of good people around you. You've got to play your position well and play it smart. He's done that. He's played very well for us this year."

After Shanahan decided in the offseason not to add anything to what Plummer was taught his first two years here, Kubiak and quarterbacks coach Pat McPherson reviewed every play from 2004 with Plummer. "Some people thought we just broke him down and taught him how to be a quarterback again," McPherson says. "We didn't do that. We just kind of went back and just looked at what people do in certain situations, worked on getting a little better pre-snap read, that type of thing."

No apologies for being who he is

Plummer came to the Broncos as a free agent from Arizona, where the team's overall lack of talent meant success often swung on his ability to slip from defenses and make seemingly unmakable plays. The situation bred habits that had Cardinals fans cheering Plummer one minute and cursing him the next.

He filled a mixed bag of statistics in six seasons there. Most notably negative were his touchdown-to-interception ratio (90-114) and win-loss record (31-52).

But Broncos fans, pining for Elway and the steady diet of adrenaline-surging plays and late comebacks he provided, saw Plummer's audaciousness as a positive. As the Broncos closed out a 9-7 season at home vs. the Cardinals in 2002, some fans chanted, "We want Jake!" Three months later, after signing a seven-year, $40.7 million contract with the Broncos, Plummer said, "I was thinking, 'I want you.'"

A quarterback's relationship with the Denver fans, though, can be complicated. They will always remember their first love.

"It is an extremely hot seat," Broncos running back Mike Anderson says.

Last year, criticism came from all corners when Plummer flashed an obscene gesture at a heckler during a home game. This year, a gossip columnist wrote about him dating a Broncos cheerleader, then reported a day later that Plummer called the columnist to complain and, during the conversation, said, "You think Denver has the greatest fans. Well, they aren't."

Plummer apologized after both incidents, but he makes no apologies for being himself on and off the field -- something Elway advised Plummer to do soon after he signed with Denver.

"If we're winning ballgames and at the end of the season we're standing on that podium, hey, if (the fans) still don't like me then, how can you win them over?" Plummer says. "I'm a passionate guy that says things sometimes that I probably shouldn't, but I was never raised to not be honest and stand up for what I believe in.

"That's what I am, and that's what fans have to deal with, I guess. If some don't like my hair and beard, then shame on them for judging."

His hair, an antithesis to Elway's clean-cut look, has drawn almost as much notice as his improved play this season. Plummer grew the beard last season, he says, in silent tribute to Cardinals teammate Pat Tillman after the NFL threatened to fine him $30,000 for wearing a decal with Tillman's jersey number on his helmet. Tillman, who also played with Plummer at Arizona State, died in combat in Afghanistan.

"This year I've been playing well when I've had it," Plummer says of the beard, "so why not?"

Shanahan finds his man

Shanahan, who helped mold Steve Young's natural talents into magic with the San Francisco 49ers and who installed the team and system around Elway that finally put the Lombardi Trophy in his hands, drafted Brian Griese as Elway's immediate successor. When Griese not only struggled on the field but also chafed at talk of Elway's legacy, the fans turned on him.

Plummer immediately embraced the legacy, posing for a photo with a life-size poster of Elway the day he was introduced as the Broncos quarterback. He fully realized its implications when he was booed in his first home preseason game.

"That persona will always be here," says Kubiak, Elway's backup from 1983-91. "But I think if there's any guy that can handle it, (Plummer) has proven over a period of time that he's the guy, because he's so confident in what he does and he really loves to play. He plays a lot like John did, from that standpoint."

That's high praise, and not just in Broncos country.

title: Injuries to QBs hurting owners
content:
The quarterback carousel is now in full spin after a flurry of injuries has some fantasy owners feeling queasy.

While the league's best are still upright and slinging, a growing list of casualties is having an impact on fantasy rosters.

At least eight opening-day starting quarterbacks are hurt or otherwise benched, including two on injured reserve for the season.

Not surprisingly, the waiver wire is, at best, a gamble. The most palatable choices might include the Bears' Brian Griese, the Raiders' Daunte Culpepper or the Chiefs' Damon Huard.

In Atlanta, the underachieving Byron Leftwich is now starting ahead of the underachieving Joey Harrington. Leftwich has never thrown for more than 15 touchdowns in a season, and his 6-foot-5, 242-pound frame rests upon famously injured ankles. His lack of mobility could mean we haven't seen the last of Harrington.

Another newly anointed starter, the Bills' Trent Edwards, is a mystery. What is known about Edwards? The late Bill Walsh praised him. He is a rookie on a bad team with more interceptions than touchdown passes.

Cleo Lemon, the Dolphins' unknown, is another option. But his best target, receiver Chris Chambers, is gone. With Trent Green game for more punishment, Lemon is a risky pickup with a limited upside.

Speculators might consider Chad Pennington's backup, Kellen Clemens, on the likelihood that Pennington's days as a Jet are nearly through. In early-season action against the Ravens, Clemens made mistakes but did move the ball on a tough defense.

If the youth movement doesn't appeal, there is NFL elder Vinny Testaverde in Carolina or Arizona's Kurt Warner.

Warner's NFL history is well-established. But how many fantasy championships have been won by a quarterback taking snaps with a torn elbow ligament?

Last week, Testaverde became the oldest starting quarterback to win an NFL game, though his fantasy stats were unremarkable, and the job may belong to David Carr. It is worth noting that Testaverde has four seasons of at least 20 interceptions. Five to start Here are five players with favorable matchups this weekend:

Dallas Clark, TE, Colts - Clark is having a solid 2007 and might be the key to beating the Jaguars this week.

Jeff Garcia, QB, Buccaneers - The savvy veteran should dissect the Lions with ease. He'll need to without a decent running game.

Terrell Owens, WR, Cowboys- Get your popcorn ready. The Vikings allow more passing yards than any other team.

Carson Palmer, QB, Bengals - He's putting up numbers everywhere except the red zone. The Jets should help fix that. They've allowed 10 touchdown passes in six games.

Brian Westbrook, RB, Eagles - Westbrook can be expected to attack the struggling Bears by land and air. He's an ideal play in leagues rewarding running back receptions.

Contact staff writer Dylan Purcell at fanfootball@phillynews.com.

title: Manning directs a mistake-free attack and defense swarms Romo asNew York knocks off top-seeded Dallas, 21-17.
content:
By IRA KAUFMAN

The Tampa Tribune

IRVING, Texas - Having polished off America's Team, the New York Giantsnow turn their steely gaze toward America's Quarterback.

Capitalizing on another mistake-free attack directed by the suddenly poised Eli Manning, the Giantsupset Dallas 21-17 in a divisional playoff matchup Sunday for their ninth consecutive road victory.

Amani Toomer, who burned the Buccaneers repeatedly last week, caught two touchdown passes and the Giantsswarmed embattled Tony Romoin the second half as the NFC's No. 5 seed advanced to the conference title game at Green Bay on Sunday evening.

"Go ahead people, count us out again next week," crowed Giantsleft tackle David Diehl. "Brett Favre's a legend, he's a Hall of Famer. Count us out. We love it. Words can't describe how awesome it's going to be to play at Lambeau Field with a chance to go to the Super Bowl."

Facing a rugged defense for the second consecutive week, the wild-card Giantsonce again avoided any giveaways in improving to 12-6 and handing the NFC's top seed a devastating setback.

"We have a special team, but someone else has a more special team," Dallas cornerback Terence Newman said. "For us to lose at home after a 13-3 season, it definitely hurts."

The Giantstook the lead for good early in the fourth quarter when Brandon Jacobs went over right guard from a yard out, capping a short drive set up by a 25-yard punt return from R.W. McQuarters, who would emerge as a defensive stalwart in the frantic closing seconds.

With New York's secondary short-handed, McQuarters accounted for the game's only turnover with nine seconds remaining when he stepped in front of Romo's fourth-down pass intended for Terry Glenn in the end zone.

"We've got a lot of unknown people playing important roles and playing them very well," said Giantslinebacker Kawika Mitchell, the former University of South Florida standout credited with six tackles and a 14-yard sack. "We kept hitting Romo in the second half and by the end of the game, he wasn't smiling as much as usual."

After breaking a 14-14 halftime tie on Nick Folk's 34-yard field goal midway through the third quarter, the Cowboys punted three times and turned the ball over in their final four possessions.

Dallas had been 19-1 when Marion Barber carried at least 11 times, but Barber finished with 129 yards on 27 attempts after burning New York for 101 yards by intermission.

"It's not easy to come into the house of the Dallas Cowboys and beat 'em," Giantsdefensive end Justin Tuck said. "We gave it our all and it was just enough. Give Dallas credit - they weren't going to lay down today. We threw a lot of stuff at them and they kept coming."

Romo, forced to defend himself for a getaway trip to Mexico with Jessica Simpson during the bye weekend, completed only 18 of 36 passes for 201 yards. His 64.7 passer rating was dwarfed by Manning's 132.4 figure and New York didn't allow a completion longer than 20 yards.

Dallas had beaten New York twice in the regular season, scoring 76 points, and the Cowboys rolled up 14 first downs in the first half, converting six of seven third-down situations and posting touchdown drives of 96 and 90 yards.

After Barber's 1-yard plunge capped a 20-play march with 53 seconds left in the second quarter, Manning responded with a critical 71-yard scoring drive of his own.

"That touchdown just before the half was huge for us," said Toomer, whose 52-yard scoring catch capped New York's opening possession. "They had just used up almost the entire quarter and they might have thought we were going to kneel down, but we didn't. That was a pivotal moment."

Manning hit rookie receiver Steve Smith twice and added a 19-yard strike to tight end Kevin Boss before finding Toomer open for a 4-yard score that let the crowd of 63,660 know the Giantsweren't going away.

With veteran Sam Madison still sidelined by an abdominal pull, the Giantslost another starting cornerback when Aaron Ross went down with a shoulder injury.

McQuarters and rookie Geoffrey Pope stepped into the void against the NFC's highest-scoring attack and helped limit Terrell Owens to four catches for 49 yards.

"We beat ourselves a lot at the beginning of the year, but now we're taking good care of the football," said Giantscenter Shaun O'Hara, who didn't dress at Tampa because of a bruised knee. "We've been doing a good job on the road this season and the funny thing is I'm not sure why. Maybe it's because we get booed so much at home."

title: Ed Barkowitz| Here's hoping Tomlinson starts producing like a top pick
content:
AFTER 2 WEEKS, LaDainian Tomlinson is tied for 39th in the NFL in rushing yards. He sits behind luminaries such as Sammy Morris, Najeh Davenport and Brandon Jackson.

He trails one quarterback (Vince Young) and is tied with another (Jason Campbell). Pass the Pepto.

This has led to the panic button getting a full workout from Tomlinson owners in fantasyland, but the Chargers running back insists, "We're going to be all right."

That's easy for him to say. Through two games, Tomlinson - the undisputed No. 1 fantasy selection - has been held to 68 yards on 35 carries with just one rushing touchdown. While his Chargers are 1-1, there are a lot of people like us who are 0-2. Tomlinson's advice is to relax.

"The first two games have been tough," he said. "I didn't expect to run up and down the field on either team. I knew it was going to be hard yards to get out there. That's what happens when you play two really good teams."

The Chargers beat the Bears and were drilled by the Patriots. They visit another tough defense in Green Bay this week, before games against Kansas City, Denver and Oakland.

While it's ridiculous to consider benching Tomlinson, it's just as foolish to think about trading him. It is acceptable to bench quarterback Philip Rivers and wide receiver Vincent Jackson, especially this week against Green Bay's decent pass defense.

"Again, we're 1-1," Rivers said. "We're not 0-8. I think it's important we don't blow it up. And nobody here is pleased with the way we played or are trying to downplay the loss. It is just what it is, it's one game and we're going to learn from it and get better."

For a lot of fantasy players, it can't get much worse.

Looking aheadThe first of the byes comes next week as Jacksonville, New Orleans, Tennessee and Washington will be idle. If you are an owner of Washington tight end Chris Cooley and need a 1-week replacement, consider the Eagles' L.J. Smith. The Birds are playing the Giants in Week 4 and New York has given up three touchdowns and an average of 86 yards to opposing tight ends through two games.

Smith's groin injury has helped lead to dismal numbers so far (four catches, 26 yards). Don't commit to him until after this week just in case he suffers a setback on Sunday. But if he plays well, he's worth a 1-week pickup.

Contest resultWe asked readers to project Clinton Portis' rushing total against the Eagles last Monday night and John Davis, from Croydon, nearly pegged it exactly. Davis' guess of 68 yards came closest to Portis' total of 69. For his prognostication, Davis earned a Daily News fantasy football T-shirt.

Catch the waive* This week's most popular free agent add/drops, according to CBS.SportsLine.com:

Added

DeShawn Wynn, RB; Packers

Derek Anderson, QB; Browns

Shaun McDonald, WR; Lions

Texans defense/special teams

Nick Folk, K, Cowboys

Dropped

Bengals defense/special teams

Olindo Mare, K, Saints

Patrick Crayton, WR, Cowboys

Alex Smith, QB, 49ers

Devery Henderson, WR, Saints

Three up* Donovan McNabb, QB, Eagles: Resist the temptation (for 1 more week) to bench him. The Detroit defense he'll see this week is inferior to the two he's seen thus far.

* Jon Kitna, QB, Lions: The only guys who are more favorable starters this week are Peyton Manning and maybe Tom Brady.

* Bernard Berrian, WR, Bears: Breakout alert! Berrian might even crack the end zone for the first time against a Cowboys secondary that's susceptible to big plays.

Three down* Edgerrin James, RB, Cardinals: Yes, Arizona coach Ken Whisenhunt has experience preparing for the Ravens' defense from his days as a Steelers assistant. But it didn't translate into much success for running back Willie Parker, who was held under 30 yards by Baltimore twice last year.

* Donté Stallworth, WR, Patriots: Wouldn't it be nice if he got an invite to these Patriots romps? If you have the space, hold onto him for a few more weeks.

* Brandon Jackson, RB, Packers: The presence of DeShawn Wynn and an upcoming schedule that includes the Chargers, Minnesota, Chicago and Washington make Jackson a candidate for the waiver wire. *

Ed Barkowitz, who will play Donovan McNabb (ugh) ahead of Alex Smith (double ugh), has been writing about fantasy football in the Daily News since 2001.

title: Forte miffed at Urlacher's jabs
content:
Running back Matt Forte said he was unaware of linebacker Brian Urlacher's comments in an Internet story that were critical of his ability to make tacklers miss.

He wasn't thrilled when he was told.
