"We've got to make people miss, we've got to block people, we've got to do a lot of things," said Forte, whose average per carry has dropped from 3.9 last year to 3.3. "It's not one particular thing that is hampering the offense. We're hurting ourselves most of the time."

Asked if he felt slighted by one of the team's defensive captains, Forte said: "Urlacher has his own opinion. If I couldn't make anybody miss, I probably wouldn't be playing pro football."

Among the 45 players with 300 or more rushing yards, Forte's average is the second lowest, ahead of only Larry Johnson, who is averaging 3.1 yards per carry.

"It's frustrating, but you can't harp on being frustrated because it carries over into other games," Forte said. "Then, you'll be focused on being frustrated instead of going out there and trying to do things right on the field."

Forte averaged 20 carries per game last season as a rookie, but he's getting just 15 attempts per game this season.

"When you don't get rushing opportunities as a running back, you have to try to make an impact somewhere else," he said, which is catching the ball and pass blocking."

Forte is third in the NFL among running backs with 46 receptions and second in receiving yards with 433.

Running on fumes: Quarterback Jay Cutler was asked if his presence has changed the identity of the Bears' offense from run first to a pass first, as linebacker Brian Urlacher said in an Internet story.

"We've struggled running the ball," Cutler said. "Brian's right. You don't have to run the ball every play. You don't have to be a running team. But when you run the ball, you have to be effective, and I think that's the most important part. There are a lot of teams that throw the ball a lot, but when they do run the ball it's effective. I think that's the main issue."

The Bears are last in the NFL in rushing yards and 23rd in average gain per run.

Offensive coordinator Ron Turner said it's not a philosophical change but a practical one because of the struggles in the run game.

"It's just the way it's turned out," Turner said. "We know we've got to be able to run the ball, we want to be able to run the ball, and we're committed to running the football."

Injury update: Linebacker Lance Briggs (knee/hip), offensive left tackle Orlando Pace (groin), cornerback Charles Tillman (concussion) and defensive end Alex Brown (leg) did not practice Wednesday.

None of them have been ruled out yet for Sunday, but right tackle Chris Williams worked at Pace's left tackle spot Wednesday, while backup Kevin Shaffer was at right tackle with the first team. The team will be especially cautious with Tillman because of the league's focus this year on head injuries.

Here and there: Linebacker Pisa Tinoisamoa was placed on injured reserve Wednesday to make room for linebacker Cato June. Linebacker Kevin Malast was added to the practice squad, replacing defensive end Joe Clermond, who was cut Tuesday night.

title: Police say Owens did not try to commit suicide
content:
Dallas police have classified Terrell Owens' case as an "accidental overdose," not an attempted suicide, closing their investigation Thursday of the Cowboys receiver's hospitalization.

Authorities also released a recording of the brief 911 call that brought rescue workers to Owens' home, in which publicist Kim Etheredge said "I think he took too many pills" but never mentioned her client's name or said anything about a suicide attempt.

Police Chief David Kunkle said he had "great confidence" in his officers' initial report, which said rescue workers responded late Tuesday night to an attempted "suicide by prescription pain medication."

"The report, in my opinion, reflects what the officers were told and represents their best interpretation of what happened," Kunkle said Thursday. "But that doesn't mean it's the definitive account of the incident. Like all these situations, we're dealing with incomplete information and facts that change."

Meanwhile, Owens looked fine in practice Thursday, but Cowboys coach Bill Parcells said he might not decide until Saturday morning whether Owens makes the trip to Tennessee. And Parcells said he'll probably wait until that night to determine whether to use him in the game.

CHIEFS - Quarterback Trent Green says the severe concussion that left him unconscious on Sept. 10 will keep him sidelined at least until after the Chiefs' game against Arizona Oct. 8.

Speaking with Kansas City reporters for the first time since the head-snapping hit in the season opener, the two-time Pro Bowler from Vianney High said he's feeling much better and finally able to drive. He may even join the Chiefs on the sideline Sunday against San Francisco.

"It's just a day-to-day, week-to-week thing. That is what the doctor told me my last visit last week, to not expect anything other than that," he said.

REDSKINS - Quarterback Mark Brunell tested his injured elbow with a few 20-yard tosses at the end of practice. He remained probable for Sunday's game against Jacksonville.

OVERSEAS GAMES? - NFL owners will consider next month whether to play two games a year outside the United States, starting with contests in Canada, Mexico, Britain and Germany.

The league has played numerous exhibition games overseas for the past two decades. The New England Patriots and Seattle Seahawks will play a preseason game in August in Beijing.

title: Depth perception for QBs is short-sighted
content:
Depth. NFL teams talk this issue to death.

Should we carry nine offensive linemen or eight? Five receivers or four? Kicker, punter and kickoff specialist, or will two out of three suffice?

Then they hang the "No Diving" sign next to their shallow pool of quarterbacks.

There's a reason teams carry three or fewer quarterbacks. Only one takes the field at a time. Nor do the reserves get many, if any, snaps in practice, other than running the scout team. The backups generally don't figure on special teams -- unless they hold for kicks -- so coaches prefer to carry more versatile personnel.

Then the injuries strike and the desperate (or is it depth-perate?) scramble starts.

Look at the New York Jets. They actually prepared for the possibility Chad Pennington might not be whole after offseason shoulder surgery by signing Jay Fiedler. This 1-2 punch got knocked out in Week3.

"I've never been in a situation where you lose the No.1 and No.2 quarterbacks in a span of seven plays," coach Herman Edwards says.

He'll try Brooks Bollinger, a three-year veteran with no starts, one game appearance and nine pass attempts to his credit, as the starter Sunday against the Baltimore Ravens. Backing up young (25) Bollinger will be old (41) Vinny Testaverde.

This plague is everywhere. The Detroit Lions, with Joey Harrington wobbly, have no one to turn to but rookie Dan Orlovsky, who could legally change his name to The Untested Dan Orlovsky. It's not as if Lions didn't prepare for a meltdown by Harrington. They signed Jeff Garcia, but he broke his leg late in preseason.

How about the Chicago Bears? They could have taken part in the quarterback festivities of spring but decided not to saddle the returning and rehabbing Rex Grossman with a competitive veteran. They felt comfortable with Chad Hutchinson and rookie Kyle Orton. Then Grossman broke his ankle, Hutchinson bombed and Orton became the starter. The veteran backup now: Jeff Blake.

The Denver Broncos' fallback position if Jake Plummer goes down is Bradlee Van Pelt, a first-year player who has never taken a snap in a real game. There's no No.3.

Even the Seattle Seahawks, where coach Mike Holmgren favors drafting a quarterback every year if possible, lack their customary depth. They traded veteran Trent Dilfer to the Cleveland Browns; their No.2 is Seneca Wallace, a three-year veteran who got his first snaps in Sunday's rout of the Arizona Cardinals. He completed his only pass attempt for 42 yards. The Seahawks' third quarterback is rookie David Greene.

Holmgren didn't want to part with Dilfer but felt honor-bound to do so.

"I had made an agreement when we signed him that if he got a chance to be a starter that I would help to make that happen, and a deal's a deal," Holmgren says.

The Seahawks eyeballed Brad Johnson (who signed with the Minnesota Vikings to replace departed backup Gus Frerotte) and Garcia, but Holmgren says he didn't believe either would truly be comfortable sitting behind Matt Hasselbeck. He stuck with Wallace.

"Seneca Wallace is a good player. He just hasn't played," Holmgren says.

Holmgren's long-held theory of stockpiling quarterbacks might be worth considering. When he coached in Green Bay, his depth chart included Brett Favre, Mark Brunell and Heisman Trophy winner Ty Detmer, and still he craved more.

In the spring of 1994, the Packers wanted an extra passer for a minicamp. They considered two guys -- one a strong-armed thrower from the Midwest, the other a nimble escape artist from the West Coast. Neither had been drafted.

They signed The Arm. Even though quarterbacks coach Steve Mariucci argued for The Legs. The Arm was so nervous he refused to take the field and run plays. "Who's responsible for this guy?" Holmgren inquired pointedly in the staff meeting later.

So the Packers had to cut Kurt Warner. Yes, that Kurt Warner. The Legs? Garcia.

Sure it's hard to find 'em, develop 'em and keep 'em when they can't get playing time. But to do anything less at the quarterback position is … depth-defying.

Jon Gruden's voice is only in the ears of his quarterback, but he is always in the heads of the opposing defense.

His savvy play-calling is responsible for the Bucs' appearance in Super Bowl XXXVII Sunday.

Time after time this season, Gruden has dialed up the right play in the right situation, sometimes to the wonderment of his players.

"We were playing in Cincinnati earlier in the year, and we had this play-action fake to the tailback and kind of get a combination (route) to the right," Bucs quarterback Brad Johnson said. "And at halftime, he said, "Listen, if they come weak side (on a pass rush), be ready to drift in the pocket, roll out to the right a little bit and buy yourself a little extra time.'

"Son of a gun if he didn't call the play, they come weak and I'm thinking this, it's just clicking. I kind of roll out to the right and hit Ken Dilger on a little underneath route and he scores a touchdown.

"The first time I came over there I said, "Man, that was a great coaching point you had, that you gave me.' I told him I'm here to learn, give me some more. Feed me, feed me."

Anyone can write the alphabet. But put the letters in the right order and you might win a Pulitzer. The same is true about drawing plays on a chalkboard and knowing when to use them.

"That's all about preparation and feel," general manager Rich McKay said. "And he has a very good feel for the moment. There are a lot of play-callers that I have known over the years. They're going to use their sheets and stick to the sheets. My dad was a great play-caller, but one of the best play-callers I've been around is Joe Gibbs. And the reason was he had prepared to a ridiculous level and he had sheets, charts, call sheets, everything lined up. And yet, he had a feel. He didn't just operate on what the sheet told him.

"To me, that's more of an instinct than a learned trait. And Jon is very good at that."

Gruden not only studies defenses, he spends as much time charting his own tendencies.

"Here's the deal. He thinks outside the box, he's not orthodox," offensive line coach Bill Muir said. "He's fundamental. He's unorthodox. So some of the things he thinks about, the motion, the movements, he's very creative. And really, when it's all said and done it presents problems for the defense because he moves in and out of personnel packages and continues to do the same thing."

Muir grabbed three empty water glasses from the table, turned them over, aligned them as two tight ends and a receiver, reached for a couple of peppermints and placed them in what would be the backfield.

"The perfect example of it is when we were playing San Francisco. We come out in the traditional goal-line formation," Muir said. "We line up here with two tight ends, and we've got two backs. Everybody matches up. They've got six defensive linemen on the field, in San Francisco's case, they had three linebackers and two defensive backs."

Then Muir began moving the glasses and peppermints around like he was playing a shell game.

"So on the snap of the ball, this guy goes here and taps him and he steps back," Muir said. "He comes across him and taps him. And when he taps him, this guy goes over here and taps him. He shifts out, he kept going in motion, he came over here and we ran the ball there. You can watch San Francisco go with the first movement and they're lost."

The pitch went to the left to Mike Alstott, who practically walked into the end zone.

The consensus is Gruden is a great motivator. But offensively, he also is an innovator.
 
"I remember when that empty backfield set was en vogue. Right?" Muir said. "Well, that was five wide receivers. Or maybe it was four wide receivers and your best running back. Well, he'll go five wide receivers with the personnel group of two tight ends, two backs.

"He'll combine any shift or motion with any personnel group. If you have a defensive coordinator over there saying, hey, every time they go two tight ends and two running backs, we're going to put an extra linebacker in. Okay, you put an extra linebacker in and they spread out five wide. Now you've got this extra linebacker, who isn't good enough to be a starter anyhow, covering a halfback outside. So it creates mismatches.

"I think he forces the opposing defense to play a little more basic than they want to play. Because you really don't know what to expect. He keeps them off balance."

Bucs players say Gruden often will point out plays in practice he believes could go for long gains or touchdowns.

More often than not, they say, his predictions are right.

"There's been some plays where Coach Gruden will say this play is going to open up a certain way," receiver Karl Williams said. "He says it in practice and you're like, okay, whatever. Then we run it in the game and it turns out just the way he says it. It's like, okay, is this guy psychic or something?

"The play that stands out is in Cincinnati, the Keenan McCardell touchdown on the sideline. He pretty much walked in. And he told us it would turn out that way and it did. It's kind of scary."

Other great calls include receiver Joe Jurevicius' 71-yard catch and run against Philadelphia that led to the first touchdown in the 27-10 win in Sunday's NFC Championship Game and Jurevicius' TD Jan. 12 against the 49ers when he was the only receiver near the goal line in a two-tight end set.

"He's been on it," McCardell said. "I mean, he's been really on his play-calling. He really puts us in great situations and certain plays are for this guy and certain plays are for that guy. He's done it. He's shuffled the deck to make everybody happy. And that's something a good play-caller would do, it's something a good head coach would do.

"It keeps a happy family around here."

title: Bob Ford: Eagles played one game, not a season
content:
The assumptions of September about an NFL team, particularly early in September, are always dangerous if taken too seriously.

Coming out of the Eagles' opener against Carolina, there are things that seem apparent, things still to be determined, and things totally unknown.

But if the game against the Panthers really served as an indicator of the season to come, you would assume that the defense is going to be consistently dominating this season, that Winston Justice is going to be a worthy replacement for Shawn Andrews at right offensive tackle, that Brian Westbrook will average a steady 5 yards per carry, and that Donovan McNabb will get hurt.

So far, only one of those assumptions is definitely correct, and that's the one about McNabb. He is hurt, even though the long-range consequences of the broken rib he suffered in Charlotte won't be known for some time. They will eventually affect Kevin Kolb, Jeff Garcia, and Michael Vick as well, and if you want to make any assumptions about the quarterback position on this team, do so at your own risk.

As for the other assumptions, there is no way to know how much of what happened Sunday was due to the Eagles' being better prepared, how much was due to poor play from the Panthers, how much was a result of Jake Delhomme's being abducted by aliens, and how much was simply the nature of a game that gets out of hand early.

"Momentum is everything," safety Macho Harris said yesterday. "Some days, even if a team isn't better than the other team and they get rolling, that's going to be their day. We've got to leave last week alone and get ready for a new game plan against a new team. We'd like to keep that momentum, though."

The offense didn't have to do all that much to get things going in Carolina, and any assumptions made about that side of the ball - particularly this week - would be foolish.

Twenty minutes into the game, the offense had executed only 10 plays (not including two false starts), with a McNabb-to-Brent Celek touchdown pass the last of those. And that made the score 24-7.

"It was a strange game with the offensive unit, really strange," offensive coordinator Marty Mornhinweg said. No kidding.

With that kind of early advantage, Westbrook got only 13 carries and three pass receptions, and wasn't tested by a full day's effort. The receivers got to work with McNabb for only part of the game, and with a run-the-clock, vanilla version of the offense for most of that. Justice had blocking help almost every time he found himself lined up against defensive end Julius Peppers. The one time Mornhinweg left him by himself in that situation, it didn't go so well.

"I put him in a bad spot that one play," Mornhinweg said. "I took a little calculated risk. I shouldn't have done that at that point in the game probably, but you do have to take some calculated risks."

Take the Carolina game for what it was from the standpoint of the Eagles' offense. "Inconclusive" would be the best description.

On the other side of the ball, there are a couple of things that can be relied upon. The Eagles are going to be aggressive. New coordinator Sean McDermott is going to be innovative in how he uses his personnel. When they get turnovers, the risks they take will look worthwhile. If Delhomme could be traded each week to their next opponent, that would be good.

The difference between the first game and the second could be as simple as the difference between Delhomme and Drew Brees, the quarterback who led the Saints to more than 500 yards of net offense Sunday, even if it was against the Lions.

"It's a good test," linebacker Omar Gaither said. "The Carolina game will have no bearing on this game. We didn't do everything right, but we played fast and gave ourselves a chance to make plays. It's a good test, but Carolina was a good test, too. It's not like we were going against a high school out there."

The defense got great pressure - and five sacks - against the Carolina quarterbacks, but the assumption that pressure will be brought to bear most weeks won't be proven until the Eagles can get to, or at least hurry, a quick-drop, quick-release guy like Brees.

The defense also enjoyed a strong game in the secondary, highlighted by four interceptions from the cornerback position and aggressive support from the safeties. Harris had a slow start, befitting a rookie, but got better as the game went along. Of course, it was a blowout by then.

"It's important that we are a different defense in Week 2 than we were in Week 1," McDermott said. "The defense in Week 1 will not be good enough to beat the crowd that we will face this week."

And the assumptions of the first week will all change and then change again, until they are not assumptions any longer but facts. Either Justice will be reliable or not. Either Westbrook will hold up or not. And either the defense will be as spectacular as it was Sunday on a regular basis or not.

Separating the assumptions from the facts is what the season is all about, and trying to do it after one game almost never works.

"Last week," Gaither said, making a point better than it sounds, "is in the past."

Contact columnist Bob Ford

at 215-854-5842 or bford@phillynews.com.

Read his blog at http://philly.com/postpatterns.

title: CALLS FROM THE PRESIDENT. WHITE HOUSE TRIPS. VICTORY PARADES. PEP RALLIES. Manning, Colts begin their life as champs
content:
Peyton Manningwas talking on the phone to a friend Monday morning when Tony Dungy handed him another cell phone.

"Tony's like: 'You need to take this call,' " Manning said.

"I was like: 'I'm on the phone here.'

Dungy persisted.

"So I hang up, and it's the president," Manning said.

He hung up on President George W. Bush?

"No," Manning said, laughing. "I hung up on my buddy to talk to the president. He was talking about what a great win it was, and playing in those tough conditions. He watched the entire game, and he said he was pulling for us, and looking forward to welcoming us to the White House."

Calls from the president. White House trips. Victory parades. Pep rallies. Such is life as a Super Bowl champion, in Manning's case, Super Bowl MVP. Who knows? As one reporter suggested, tongue in cheek, perhaps Manning can turn Sunday's 29-17 triumph over Chicago into a few endorsement opportunities?

Manning, a man of many endorsements, laughed.

"I don't think I need any more commercials at this point," he said.

Despite his efforts to play down its significance, winning the Super Bowl will change his life forever.

"I'm going on zero sleep right now," Manning said at the traditional "morning after" Super Bowl news conference Monday. "We gave it a good run last night and this morning until about 4:30. Then I got back to the room, and of course, I had to pop on the (television) highlights, and see all that happened during the game. I saw some things that I didn't even know happened during the game."

For example, Manning didn't realize at the time how close defensive back Kelvin Hayden was to stepping out of bounds on his victory-clinching interception return for a score early in the fourth quarter.

Manning and the Colts left Miami on Monday for Indianapolis and the team's victory parade and pep rally. Today, he does something he always wanted to do: Fly out late to the Pro Bowl in Hawaii.

"I was always really envious of seeing the Patriot guys, and the Steeler guys come out there a couple of days late," Manning said.

Of course, you only arrive late for the Pro Bowl if you've played in the Super Bowl. Whether he likes it or not, Manning will always be looked at in a different light now that he has won a Super Bowl. But even on Monday morning, Manning was still talking "team."

"For me, this is not a personal mission," he said. "I was proud to be a part of this team. ... To accomplish your ultimate goal of winning that championship as a team, it really is a special feeling because you're sharing it with your coaches and your teammates."

Manning has seen some Super Bowl quarterbacks get what he calls a free pass in subsequent seasons. They may not play that well in a particular season, but they get that "pass" because they won a Super Bowl.

"I don't want the 'pass,' " Manning said. "I want to be held accountable each and every year."

He wants to be a better quarterback next season than he was this season.

Dungy told Manning and the rest of the Colts to enjoy the Super Bowl victory for at least a couple of weeks.

But Dungy has his doubts whether Manning will heed that advice.

"Knowing him, he won't," Dungy said. "He'll come back from the Pro Bowl and come into my office with his notebook of the seven things he wants to do a little bit better next year. ... Peyton is a guy that always looks forward. He is always looking forward to the challenge. And I think that's what makes him great."

For Manning, Dungy and all the Colts, repeating as Super Bowl champion is the next challenge.

It won't be easy - never is. For one, the season lasts an extra month for Super Bowl participants. That's more wear and tear on the players. It puts coaches and the front office behind the rest of the league in terms of preparing for the draft. The NFL scouting combine, for example, begins just two weeks from Wednesday.

There is free agency to deal with as well, and the Colts' list of prospective free agents includes defensive end Dwight Freeney, running back Dominic Rhodes and linebacker Cato June. There could be staff changes as well. But after some speculation to the contrary, Dungy sounded very much like he intends to be back in 2007.

"I still have a lot of passion for the game, a lot of enthusiasm," Dungy said. "After a night like (Sunday), how can you not love it? So I'm not burned out. I'm not tired at all. I'm very, very fired up and looking forward to coming back.

"But I'll evaluate it and we'll see were we go. But generally, after a couple of days, you feel like everything is rolling and I do look forward to helping these guys defend this title."

title: Favre airs it out, spreads wealth Veteran helps Packers quiet Lions
content:
DETROIT -- Green Bay Packers quarterback Brett Favre's explanation for his success against the Detroit Lions was as precise as the 41 pass attempts he put up against them.

"I threw it. They caught it," he said after completing 31 for 381 yards and three touchdowns in a 37-26 victory Thursday.

At one point Favre completed 20 consecutive passes to set a team record, breaking the mark of 18 shared by Lynn Dickey (against the Houston Oilers in 1983) and Don Majkowski (against the New Orleans Saints in 1989).

"I (said) last week he is playing as good as I've ever seen him play, (and) I've played against him since 1996," said Lions coach Rod Marinelli, whose team fell to 6-5. "That was my concern going into this game: the release, the speed. We played man and we played zone, and we struggled on all."

The Packers put the ball in the air on 31 of their first 35 snaps. It was Favre at his best, spreading out the field and hitting receivers effortlessly, like he was playing catch in his backyard.

"Nothing spectacular," Favre said. "Just get it into our guys' hands and let them make plays."

Favre, 38, has a resume full of records, but his Thanksgiving performance reminded some teammates he might be playing some of the best football of his career in leading them to a 10-1 mark and a four-game lead in the NFC North.

"We all know that Brett Favre can sling that rock," Packers linebacker Nick Barnett said. "But he's coming up with big play after big play. I don't know if he was perfect (against the Lions), but he was darn close."

Favre completed passes to eight receivers, and four had five or more catches, led by Donald Driver's 10 receptions for 147 yards.

"I told him walking off the field, you don't have to score touchdowns to be awesome," Favre said. "He knows that, but he makes those 3-yard catches look unbelievable."

When Favre did throw down the field, he was just as precise.

Greg Jennings caught two touchdown passes, and he said Favre was irritated that a possible third touchdown to pass to him was slightly off the mark.

"He was so unhappy with the placement," Jennings said. "I'm thinking, I like this Brett. He wants to be perfect. That's what he expects out of himself.

"And he's already phenomenal when he's not perfect. If he is playing perfect, the sky is the limit. He was pretty perfect in our eyes."

As the game progressed, Favre said his philosophy was "throw it to a guy as quickly as possible and whatever happens after that ... I'll take my chances."

That quick-decision passing been a mainstay of the Packers' offensive game plan all season.

"It's very tough to sack this guy," Marinelli said.

As impressive as Green Bay's air assault was, concern remains about whether the Packers can mount a sustained running attack.

Going into Thursday's game, the Packers had been talking about adding balance to their offensive game plan.

"There does come a time when you've got to be able to run the ball," Favre said. "We tried to run at the end of the game to eat up the clock. (Coach) Mike McCarthy and I talked about it after the game. He said I should have just kept doing what I was doing."

If Favre continues his 68.5% completion percentage, the Packers' passing attack might be their best form of ball control.

"Until they stop it, I guess that's what we say," Favre said. "We need balance, but we also need to do what we do well. ... Right now we put the ball in the other team's court. You say, 'Tackle him.' I know one thing: Donald Driver is hard to tackle."

title:
Sam Donnellon / Sacking the naysayers;
BIRDS' DEFENSE PROVES LOST PARTS REPLACEABLE
content:
THEY ARE 6-2 and eight points away from a perfect ride. That's right. Screw on the thinking caps a little tighter in Nashville and Jacksonville,

eliminate maybe one defensive or special-teams breakdown in each, and the Eagles are a scarless 8-0 halfway through this

Super Bowl-serious season.

Which leads to this debatable, but interesting, possibility:

Is this Eagles defense as good as last season's?

Might it even be better?

"It's better," says defensive coordinator Jim Johnson. "A bit. I think it's better because our run defense is playing a lot better."

The Eagles were 18th in the NFL against the run last season. They are third this season.

Second against the pass a year ago, they currently are fifth.

No NFL team is better on third down. The Eagles have

allowed just 26 percent to be

converted. In 2001, they were second in third-down efficiency, allowing opponents to convert 30.6 percent.

Last year, they allowed 208 points, second in the NFL. At this juncture last season, the

Eagles had allowed 115 points.

Of the 118 points scored against the Eagles this season, 21 have been scored via punt and kickoff

returns. Take them away, and the performance of this season's

defense compared with last is

virtually identical.

Which is quite amazing, given the preseason and early-season hyperbole surrounding this team. The Eagles' defense, said everyone from your neighbor to Bill Parcells, had slipped considerably from the year before. No Jeremiah Trotter. No Damon Moore. No Mike Caldwell.

And then, no Hollis Thomas.

Those people looked right when the season started in Tennessee. Levon Kirkland, signed to replace Trotter without the benefit of minicamp, was lifted after Eddie George made him look foolish on a pass play. Blaine Bishop, the plug for the departed Moore, looked about the same on a touchdown pass late in the game.

"I said back then that as the season went on, these guys would get better," Johnson says. "I think one of the mistakes I made was that I should have played some of these guys, especially Levon and Blaine, more in preseason. I don't think they worked enough. They needed to play with the first defense just to get the feel, and making the calls."

"I feel more comfortable now than at the start," Kirkland says. "The one thing about playing defense, you kind of want to know all the ins and outs. Especially at middle linebacker…

"I think the last three games… I'm feeling better and better about my role."

Says Bishop, "Now compared to then, I feel a whole lot more comfortable."

The defense has allowed one touchdown in the last three games. In the Eagles' six victories, that number swells to four TDs. In starting 5-0 against the NFC, they have allowed the offenses of those five teams to score exactly one touchdown.

"That's the whole key to defense," says Kirkland. "You stop them from getting in the end zone, make them kick field goals, neutralize the run and prevent big plays. Do that, you'll be fine."

Says Johnson: "Last couple of years I thought we were giving up some big plays. That's the stat I see a big improvement in. Our corner play has been better. Our safety support has been better, it seems. We're keeping out of the red zone about the same, we're good at third down-and-long last year and we're good at third down-and-long this year."

Only Dallas and New York have amassed more than 100 yards rushing against this edition, and then just barely. The Bears accumulated 5 yards on the ground in the second half Sunday, when they needed it the most. And the Eagles' 26 sacks put them on pace to exceed any team of Andy Reid's tenure here, on pace to register their highest total since Reggie White's last Eagles season, 1992.

The Eagles need to maintain that pace, or increase it, when the Colts come to town Sunday. When the Birds have shown weakness, it has come against better quarterbacks such as Jacksonville's Mark Brunell and Tennessee's Steve McNair, against offenses in which, in the words of cornerback Troy Vincent, "the routes are going to be crisp, the balls are going to be there, on time."

The Colts' Peyton Manning

is among the best there is in

that regard. But the absence of running back Edgerrin James should limit his play-action

options and allow the defense

to rush and play with even less trepidation.

And allow for another week like the last 3.

"I've been saying all season long that we're a work in progress," Vincent says. "Over the last 3 weeks, we have seen some progress on where we

really are and what we are made of defensively. We lost some key football players last year, but now we're really starting to come together.

"And we can get better. We're going to be a very competitive football team come this last stretch."

Send e-mail to donnels@phillynews.com

title:
PACKERS;
Defense seeking improvement beyond numbers
content:
Statistically it was hard to find fault with the performance of the Packers' defense in Sunday's 31-14 victory over Chicago. Nine sacks, a forced fumble and a 43-yard interception return for a touchdown by safety Darren Sharper left little to pick apart.

     "I know it was against a non-playoff team, but I saw a lot of improvement against the Bears," Green Bay coach Mike Sherman said.

     But the reality is that Sunday's output came against a Chicago team that only showed up at Soldier Field because the NFL said it must.

    The statistics of the 16-game regular season are a harsh reminder of how fortunate Green Bay was to finish 10-6 and win a third consecutive NFC North title. Overall, the Packers had the NFL's 25th-ranked defense. That was only three spots ahead of the Vikings' unit.

     "It has been an up-and-down roller-coaster, and all we're trying to do is make sure we're better," Packers defensive coordinator Bob Slowik said. "There have been some setbacks in the second half [of the season], but far less than the first half, and we're looking to make sure we prevent those setbacks and keep getting better."

     But can the improvement be dramatic enough for a playoff run, starting with Sunday's game at Lambeau Field?

     "I don't know, you'd have to ask the question, 'Is Indianapolis' team good enough?' Because we're ranked higher than them defensively," Sherman said. "I know people think they have a shot at it. So I would think we do."

     Sherman is right in that Indianapolis' defense finished 29th in the NFL. But the argument falls apart when it comes to one very important statistic: turnover differential.

     While the Colts led the NFL with a plus-19 in takeaways and giveaways, the Packers were among the bottom-feeders, at minus-14. The failure has far more to do with Green Bay's defense than its offense.

     While the Packers turned over the ball 29 times - a number equal or surpassed by 14 teams in the league - their defense forced only 15 turnovers (eight interceptions, seven fumble recoveries). That tied St. Louis for worst in the NFL.

     The Packers, Rams (league worst minus-24) and Denver (minus-9) are the only teams among the 13 with a negative turnover differential to make the playoffs.

     "We need to get more," Sherman said. "We emphasize it in practice. What we need to do is get the forced fumbles in the pocket. You can really force some fumbles in the pocket by going after the quarterback's arm as he's throwing."

     This could be especially true against Vikings quarterback Daunte Culpepper. Although Culpepper fumbled 11 times this season, losing the ball five times, he was perfect in that area in two games against Green Bay and didn't throw an interception.

     Sherman, though, is holding out hope his defense can cause the Vikings some problems, just as it did in the second half of the Packers' 34-31 victory at the Metrodome on Dec. 24. The Packers defense switched to a cover-2 scheme and gave up only three points after surrendering 21 in the second quarter.

     Maybe that's why Sherman won't say he holds the same philosophy as his counterpart in Minnesota - that he just wants his defense to "hold serve." At least, he isn't admitting he's adopted the Mike Tice approach.

     "I've never been a big tennis guy. I don't quite fit into those tennis shorts," Sherman said. "I hope we're better than that. I hope our defense can win a game for us. They have in the past. They've helped us a couple times this year. In the playoffs they're going to have to win a game for us."

title: Favre's Jets in first
content:
FOXBOROUGH, Mass. (AP) - Brett Favre and the New York Jets stand alone atop the tight AFC East standings - barely.

Jay Feely kicked a 34-yard field goal on the only series of overtime after New England tied the game on Matt Cassel's pass to Randy Moss with one second left in regulation and the Jets survived a Tom Brady-like comeback with a 34-31 victory Thursday night.

The win broke a tie for the division lead between the teams and gave the Jets (7-3) sole possession of first place for the first time since Nov. 19, 2001, according to the Elias Sports Bureau. The Patriots (6-4) have won the AFC East the last five seasons and six of the last seven.

New England "had all the momentum," said Favre, who threw two touchdown passes and directed the winning drive. "Based on history, you would have bet against us. This says a lot about our team."

The Jets, who led, 24-6, in the first half, extended their winning streak to four with their sixth victory in seven games and are 1½ games ahead of Buffalo and Miami in the division. They beat the Patriots for only the second time in 13 games.

"It's a great, great feeling for us," Jets coach Eric Mangini said. "Everybody understands that this game was extremely important, and it's important because it allows us to make the next game (against Tennessee) extremely important. It's a really positive step for our team."

The loss wasted a brilliant performance by Cassel, whose first pro start after Brady's season-ending knee injury in the opener came in a 19-10 win over the Jets. Cassel completed 30 of 51 passes for 400 yards, all career highs, and three touchdowns. He ran eight times for 62 yards, sixth-most in team history for a quarterback and the most since Steve Grogan ran for 65 in consecutive games in 1978.

"It's not enough," Cassel said.

title: NFL playoff preview capsules
content:
Sean Payton is coach of the year. Drew Breesis the All-Pro quarterback.

Those honors are nice.

But they are nothing next to the possibilities that await the New Orleans Saints if they can beat Philadelphia at the Superdome on Saturday night. It would get them to the first championship game in the team's mostly dismal 40-season history, and move them one step from the Super Bowl a season after going 3-13 season during a Katrina-enforced exile.

"We've beaten all the odds," says wide receiver Joe Horn, who is still bothered by a groin injury.

"No one outside this locker room was even thinking we'd have a chance to be where we are right now. It speaks volumes to the football players in this locker room, the camaraderie that we have here, the chemistry that coach Payton has definitely given us the opportunity to have."

The Saints (10-6) beat the Eagles (11-6) by 27-24 in New Orleans on Oct. 15 and are the second-seeded team in the NFC behind Chicago. The Bears play host to Seattle at 1 p.m. EST on Sunday in the other NFC game.

In the AFC semifinals, Indianapolis is at Baltimore at 4:30 p.m. Saturday, and New England is at San Diego at 4:30 on Sunday.

All those franchises have far more playoff experience than the Saints, who have only one postseason win in their history, a wild-card victory over St. Louis in January 2001. Born in 1967, they didn't even make the playoffs until 1987, and had the misfortune of having their only consistently good teams during the late '80s and early '90s under the senior Jim Mora, a period when the NFC was loaded with power teams.

This year, they could benefit from the opposite — a paucity of good teams in the conference.

Still, they drew the hottest NFC team in Philadelphia, winner of six straight, including a 23-20 wild-card win over the New York Giants last week on David Akers' 38-yard field goal as time expired. All six wins have come with Jeff Garcia at quarterback in place of the injured Donovan McNabb and thanks to an enhanced running game featuring Brian Westbrook, who rushed for a career-high 141 yards on 20 carries against the Giants.

"We've been doing it these last few weeks and we've gotten better and better," said Westbrook, who finished with 1,217 yards rushing in the regular season. "The offensive line has confidence in themselves and they have confidence in me."

They will face a two-headed running game from New Orleans, with Deuce McAllister inside and Reggie Bush outside. Bush also is a receiving threat.

In any event, the Saints are a long way from 2005, when their season was over before it began because of the exile forced by the ravages of Hurricane Katrina.

"We're still digging, we're still fighting," Horn says. "Hopefully the light you see at the end of the tunnel will be that sunshine in Miami."

———

Indianapolis (13-4) at Baltimore (13-3)

The Colts return to the city they abandoned after the 1983 season for this matchup between the NFL's equivalent of the unstoppable force (Peyton Manning and the Indianapolis offense) and the irresistible object (the Ravens' defense).

The very resistable object that was Indy's run defense stiffened against the Chiefs, allowing Larry Johnson just 32 yards on 13 carries after giving up a league-high average of 173 yards rushing a game during the season. Meanwhile, rookie Joseph Addai rushed for 122 yards in the 23-8 win.

The job will be a lot harder this week against Baltimore's defense, which features three first- or second-team All-Pros: linebackers Adalius Thomas and Bart Scott, and safety Ed Reed. And that's not counting Ray Lewis plus Trevor Pryce and Terrell Suggs, who combined for more than a third of the team's 60 sacks, second in the league.

But these Ravens are more than a dominant defense, which was what won a Super Bowl for Baltimore six years ago. Steve McNair gives Baltimore the best quarterback it's ever had, and Jamal Lewis, while not the Lewis of old, is capable of exploiting the Colts if they revert to their old, generous ways.

New England (13-4) at San Diego (14-2)

While the Chargers are the AFC's top-seeded team, the playoff experience belongs to the Patriots, who are seeking their fourth title in six seasons. That's true especially at quarterback and coach.

Tom Brady is 11-1 in the postseason, with three Super Bowl titles; Philip Rivers is in his first year as a starter and will be in his first playoffs. And coach Bill Belichick is 12-2 with those same three championships, while Marty Schottenheimer is 5-12 in Cleveland, Kansas City and San Diego.

Schottenheimer has laughed at himself this year when he refers to "Martyball," the conservative style that cost him some of those postseason games. He's had no choice but to open up the offense because he has league MVP LaDainian Tomlinson, who set an NFL record with 31 touchdowns and can score from anywhere on the field.

San Diego also is one of the few teams that can dominate on both offense and defense. It is the only team with more sacks (61) than the Ravens, with Shawne Merriman leading the league with 17 despite missing four games after testing positive for a performance-enhancing substance.

The Chargers understand their position — and New England's.

"You definitely respect them for the things they've done in the past and the things they've done throughout the season," All-Pro tight end Antonio Gates says of the Patriots. "You just want to go out and play with respect. But at the same time, you fear no one. That's our mind-set."

Seattle (10-7) at Chicago (13-3)

The Bears seem to be one of the least respected top-seeded teams in NFL history, due in part to a weak schedule, to injuries, and to the sometimes awful play of quarterback Rex Grossman, who had a passer rating of 0.0 in the finale against Green Bay and threw two interceptions for touchdowns.

In fact, Chicagoans spent the bye week speculating how long it might take coach Lovie Smith to replace Grossman with journeyman Brian Griese.

Forgotten is Chicago's 37-7 win over the Seahawks on Oct. 1 in a game that wasn't that close. The Seahawks were without RB Shaun Alexander, last season's MVP, in that game, but they haven't played especially well since he's returned either. Seattle backed into the NFC West title and has been playing what it acknowledges as so-so football all season.

They Seahawks are also lucky to be here. They beat Dallas 21-20 last week when Tony Romo dropped the snap on what would have been a chip-shot field goal to put the Cowboys ahead with just over a minute remaining in Seattle.

Quarterback Matt Hasselbeck, who led Seattle to the Super Bowl last season, acknowledges he's been part of the problem.

"Hasn't been great," he says of his season. "Obviously, the stats are terrible. Some of that is injury. Some of that is bad weather. And some of that is not playing well. It happens."

If it happens this week, the Seahawks are going home.

title: Eagles falter without, well, you know who
content:
Terrell Owens was nowhere to be found as Donovan McNabb scanned the end zone on fourth down from the 7 with about 1 1/2 minutes left.

Playing without Owens, suspended without pay after a week in which he bad-mouthed his team and his quarterback and got into a locker-room tussle with a former teammate, the reigning conference champion Eagles dropped into last place in the NFC East with a 17-10 loss to the Redskins on Sunday night.

"It is tough losing a guy of his caliber, of his ability, but I think we might be better off," said McNabb, forced to throw to the likes of Josh Parry and Stephen Spach instead of the All-Pro Owens. "Nothing against him and his attitude. It's just when you get out there on the football field, it's about playing together."Compiled from Wire Reports

And getting help from up and down the roster, something the Redskins did to end a seven-game losing skid against Philly.

Sure, linebacker LaVar Arrington - a subject of plenty of off-field consternation himself - made a rare start and tied for the team lead in tackles, including two second-half stops of running back Brian Westbrook for losses. Clinton Portis, meanwhile, topped 5,000 yards rushing for his career and scored the go-ahead points on a 6-yard touchdown run in the third quarter. And Mark Brunell completed 21 of 29 passes for 224 yards.

But, otherwise, the Redskins (5-3) improved to 4-0 at home thanks to key plays from unheralded players. Ladell Betts returned a kickoff into Eagles territory then tacked on a 26-yard catch ahead of Portis' score, left guard Derrick Dockery recovered a fumble to keep that drive alive, Mike Sellers' first NFL carry since 2000 produced a 1-yard TD plunge, and safety Ryan Clark intercepted McNabb's final pass, on that fourth and 4 from the 7.

"It seemed like it was a guy here, guy there that made plays," Washington coach Joe Gibbs said. "It wasn't any one person - everyone contributed."

Gibbs wouldn't weigh in on what effect Owens' absence had. And Eagles coach Andy Reid didn't want to talk about it, either.

Losers of three of their past four games, the Eagles are 4-4 a year after finishing 13-3 en route to the Super Bowl. And while the Redskins quickly erased the memory of the 36-0 loss Oct. 30 to the Giants, Philadelphia couldn't rebound from its 28-point loss at Denver.

Even without Owens, and dealing with bruised ribs and a sports hernia, McNabb put up some impressive numbers and made a couple of eye-catching moves. On one play, he eluded Phillip Daniels twice, then Nic Clemons, before completing a 10-yard pass down the sideline.

McNabb went 22-of-35 for 304 yards, including a 56-yard touchdown to Reggie Brown, a rookie starting in Owens' place. The QB spread the ball around, with completions to Spach, Brown, Parry and Billy McMullen on the drive that led to David Akers' 34-yard field-goal tying the score at 10 in the third quarter.

"We showed that we played well together," McNabb said. "I think we also showed, given the opportunity, that guys can make plays for us."

That statement seemed to carry a message for Owens. So did what Brunell said after the game.

"As far as the T.O. thing, we weren't thinking about it, but I'll tell you what: We are about team and we're not about anything that comes before team," Brunell said. "If we had any players that were trying to get all the attention and trying to be the focus, we wouldn't stand for it. It wouldn't be allowed."

title: Gordon Forbes' 5-star guide: Bucs must throw ball vs. 'Skins
content:
* * * * *

Tampa Bay at Washington: Bucs must open it up and get WR
Keyshawn Johnson more involved in offense or Redskins will control
game with forceful run defense, solid pass-run mix in Norv Turner's
changeup offense. . . . Redskins' defense tougher than
numbers, playing matchup scheme using DB Matt Stevens, DT Nolan
Harrison, DE Kenard Lang and starting LB LaVar Arrington. . . . Redskins will double-team DT Warren Sapp, take chances
with rushers Anthony McFarland, Marcus Jones. . . . Could
be chess match of the year.

* * * *

Indianapolis at Buffalo: Bills held QB Peyton Manning to
season-low 163 passing yards last year in meaningless game, but
Manning now has more weapons and Colts have a nasty attitude after
second-half collapse vs. Raiders. . . . Bills coming off
bye week and Colts off short week, meaning Buffalo coach Wade
Phillips could have something special in his defensive package.

New York Giants at Tennessee: Last week Giants appeared
to give up on their running game, which couldn't deliver on early
downs vs. Redskins. . . . With QBs Steve McNair and Neil
O'Donnell hurting and thin WR corps, Titans could be forced into
grind-it-out attack from passing sets to control ball with RB
Eddie George. . . . WRs Amani Toomer, Ike Hilliard have
to step up and keep drives alive, assuming Giants can keep DE
Jevon Kearse away from QB Kerry Collins.

* * *

Dallas at Carolina: Cowboys will rise and fall with up-and-down
offensive line. . . . Panthers love to blitz and will
come after QB Troy Aikman, who has taken pounding in two starts.
 . . . Injury-riddled Panthers still competitive with QB
Steve Beuerlein's passing and DL's interesting seven-man rotation.

Minnesota at Detroit: Vikes don't scare anybody with their
defense, but their offense has enough firepower to grab an early
lead that would allow the young LBs to be turned loose. .
. . Lions haven't found a running game, and QB Charlie Batch
sometimes forces ball into coverage, trying to carry offense.
 . . . Lions DB Kurt Schultz is in Pro Bowl form.

Atlanta at Philadelphia (Sunday night): Young Eagles can
turn it on when RB Duce Staley is hitting the lanes, QB Donovan
McNabb is taking what defense gives. . . . Falcons secondary
riddled by Rams (four TD passes), but front four had its moments
on running downs. . . . Falcons' OL might have a confidence
problem after giving up eight sacks.

Seattle at Kansas City (Monday night): QB Elvis Grbac silenced
critics with dramatic winning drive on road vs. Broncos, might
be headed for breakout season. . . . Seahawks still seeking
an identity and big plays from a scattershot offense.

* *

Baltimore at Cleveland: Ravens think they can win the AFC
Central, assuming QB Tony Banks doesn't revert to old hold-the-ball
habit. . . . Rookie RB Jamal Lewis gives Ravens more balance
in big-play offense. . . . Browns learning on the job,
don't have enough speed or depth.

Chicago at Green Bay: Bears strictly hit-or-miss team that
has tried to live by the pass and offbeat formations. . .
. Packers aren't up to dominating seasons of old, but QB Brett
Favre's arm feels better and running game finally shows life with
import RB Ahman Green.

Miami at Cincinnati: Win one for Dick LeBeau; that's the
rallying cry of the Bengals, who drove coach Bruce Coslet out
of his job with their drab play and now turn to LeBeau. .
. . Miami defense, special teams under pressure, with the
offense playing cozy, low-scoring games.

New England at Denver: Patriots in 0-4 funk, with QB Drew
Bledsoe struggling without a go-to guy. . . . Broncos
a beat-up team but still have enough playmakers to handle punchless
Pats.

Pittsburgh at Jacksonville: Jaguars, who have won five
in a row at home vs. Steelers, should be in angry mood after clunker
vs. Colts. . . . Steelers seeking consistency at QB, RB,
and where have they been hiding WR Plaxico Burress?

*

Arizona at San Francisco: Cards, 49ers need more than new
stadiums.

San Diego at St. Louis: Time of the knockout: 12 minutes
of the first round.

Open dates: New Orleans, New York Jets, Oakland.

title: NFL INSIDER EXTRA POINTS
content:
Pass less, run more?

Pittsburgh QB Ben Roethlisbergeris in a rare statistical funk. He has a 34.3 passer rating that ranks 31st among the 32 QBs listed, and his two interceptions in the red zone last week played a major role in the Steelers' loss to the Bengals. One of the solutions Roethlisberger is considering: scrambling more, throwing less.

"I talked to Coach (Bill Cowher) about it; we decided that maybe I need to get out a little more, run a little more," Roethlisberger said. "I've kind of gotten away from running the ball. He said, `Yeah, take your 6 yards and slide.' We'll see."

Making history

New Orleans' Sean Payton is only the third coach in the past 75 years to take over a team that won three or fewer games the year before his arrival and lead it to three straight wins to start the next season. He joins Vince Lombardi of the Packers and John Fox of the Panthers. No coach has ever opened 4-0 in his first year after taking over a team that won three or fewer games the year before his arrivial.

Carr salesman

This is why statistics can be so misleading: Texans QB David Carr is leading the league with a 113.6 passer rating. His standing has a lot to do with his throwing only one interception against six touchdowns, but a closer look reveals that four of the scores occurred in the fourth quarter, when the game was out of hand for the 0-3 Texans. Carr has played well, but not so well that he should be mentioned among the top players at the position.

Run down

Tampa Bay coach Jon Gruden has been lamenting the state of his teams' running game, and rightfully so. The Bucs have run for 26, 40 and 64 yards in their first three games for an average of 43.3 yards. That's 70.8 yards under their average from 2005 and a big reason they lead the league with 14 three-and-out possessions.

But equally disturbing is the performance of the defense, which has allowed 27, 14 and 26 points after holding 12 opponents to 17 or fewer points last season.

TD challenged

Minnesota had one of the league's more explosive offenses not so long ago, but the unit has now gone nine quarters without scoring a touchdown. The streak dates to the third quarter of the season opener. The offense has scored just two touchdowns overall, which matches the combined total of the defense and special teams. More amazing: Kicker Ryan Longwell has as many touchdown passes as QB Brad Johnson: one.

Gore-y picture

San Francisco coach Mike Nolan has an open door and a free phone line for his players, but he'd just as soon not receive any more Sunday night calls from running back Frank Gore, who called him each of the past two weeks to apologize for losing a fumble at the goal line. One 49ers coach speculated that part of Gore's problem is his running style, in that he tends to twist his body at the line while fighting for extra yards. By doing so, the coach believes, Gore creates better angles for defenders to get clean shots on the ball.

Giant holes

The Giants (1-2) have been among the league's slowest starters this season, trailing by double digits in the first half of each game. They were down 13-0 to the Colts, 17-7 to the Eagles and 35-3 to the Seahawks. Their only victory came in overtime against the Eagles, after they erased a 17-point fourth-quarter deficit.

Look in the mirror

USC coach Pete Carroll has complained to the NFL Players Association about player agents and how the Trojans are having a tough time policing them. Here's one solution for Carroll: Stop allowing them access to your practices and your sideline during games. Carroll's come-one-come-all policy with agents, marketing reps (i.e., Mike Ornstein) and pretty much anyone else is a big part of his problem.

title: MORAN WILL START VS. FALCONS AFTER CARTER RECEIVES PENALTY
content:
Just two weeks after returning from arthroscopic knee surgery, Sean Moran will start his first NFL game since 1998.

Moran will replace Kevin Carter in the starting lineup Sunday in Atlanta. Carter was removed as a starter and was fined after an unexcused absence from work Wednesday.

"It's an unfortunate situation with Kevin," Moran said. "But he'll play, and he'll play a lot. So, nothing's really going to change other than the fact that I'm starting."

Moran was signed by the Rams in the offseason as an unrestricted free agent from the Buffalo Bills. He was brought in to be the team's third defensive end, filling the role manned last season by Jay Williams, who plays for the Carolina Panthers.

Moran had knee surgery Aug. 11 and missed the team's season opener against the Denver Broncos. He has been worked into the defensive line rotation against the San Francisco 49ers and the Seattle Seahawks.

"Last game (against the 49ers), I probably played about 40 percent of the plays," Moran said. "In the Seattle game, I probably played about 12 plays. So as each week goes on, I'm playing more and more.

"And they're using me in different situations. They're using me inside at tackle in dime situations. Sometimes they're playing me at end."

Moran doesn't have a tackle in those two games, but he has three quarterback pressures.

After starting seven games for the Bills in 1997, Moran suffered a season-ending knee injury that required surgery. He started the first two games of the '98 season, as well, but has struggled to get his knee right since that initial '97 surgery.
 
At the moment, Moran said, "The knee feels great."

At 6 feet 3 and 275 pounds, Moran isn't so big or so physically intimidating as Carter. But he has good quickness and plays with the relentless style that the Rams defensive coaches love.
 
"Sean's done very well in our system," coach Mike Martz said.

Martz plans to rotate Carter in behind Moran on Sunday against the Falcons.

"I think between the two of them, we ought to have a really good one-two punch out there," Martz said.
 
Martz added that this will not be merely a token start for Moran.

"We're not going to play Sean Moran and then take him out after one series and play Kevin," Martz said. "We're not going to do that. We're just going to mix him in there, and probably more or less split (the playing time). If Sean's doing real well, then Kevin will play less."

Carter, who was sick with the stomach flu against the 49ers, has only one sack in three games this season after registering a league-high 17 last season to earn a Pro Bowl berth.

"I think he's been very inconsistent so far," Martz said about Carter's play this season. "But I know he's going to play better than he has. I think in the opener, he had some outstanding moments."

Martz, in fact, presented Carter with a defensive game ball for his pass-rush efforts in the closing minutes against the Broncos.

title: Bears should ignore any thoughts of Moss
content:
The Bears should never, under any circumstance, consider picking up Randy Moss.

Don't even think about it.

He was booted out of two universities — Notre Dame and Florida State — and was jailed twice before ever catching a pass in college.

If he's putting up Hall of Fame numbers and giving 100 percent effort, while at the same time behaving like a lout, the trade-off is acceptable.

When he wanted to be, Moss was as dangerous as any wide receiver who ever played the game. But he rarely gives 100 percent effort, and his skills and his speed have diminished. He's just not worth the trouble anymore.

In four games with the Vikings he had 13 catches for 174 yards. In four games with the Patriots he had 9 catches for 139 yards.

Some guys will take a play off here and there. Some guys will even mail it in for a whole game.

Moss essentially blew off an entire season in 2006 when he was with the Raiders and caught 42 passes for 553 yards. And that was when he was still in his prime, which he isn't anymore, at 33.

Consider that just in the past month the Patriots and the Vikings — two teams with playoff potential and both desperate for help at wide receiver — told him to get lost.

Moss is a cancer and a quitter. He is, quite simply, a bad guy. He's a bad teammate and, most important for the Bears or any team with young wide receivers, a horrible example on and off the field.

Is this the guy you would want mentoring Johnny Knox, Devin Hester, Earl Bennett and Devin Aromashodu?

The Vikings didn't want him polluting the mind of spectacular, young wide receiver Percy Harvin, even though Harvin's ankle injury and the earlier hip injury to Sidney Rice leaves them thin at wide receiver.

But not thin enough to put up with Moss' act, which has alienated even his teammates, who recognize a phony when they see one.

At 2-5 the Vikings are desperate for a spark to ignite a team that was expected to contend for the Super Bowl, but they're not desperate enough to keep Moss.

They were so eager to be rid of the ultimate prima donna that they let him walk after just 25 days, even though they had given up a third-round draft pick to get him from the Patriots.

Kudos to embattled Vikings coach Brad Childress for saying, "Enough's enough."

It's bad enough that Childress has to cater to the whims of an over-the-hill, turnover-waiting-to-happen quarterback in the egomaniacal Brett Favre.

But having to listen to Moss complain that the Vikings' coaching staff didn't take his recommendations on how they should play the Patriots was too much.

Moss lacks the conscience and the backbone to put forth the same effort on the field as his teammates, but he has the gall to presume to tell the coaching staff how it should be doing its job.

He's a disgrace to every player in every locker room who puts it on the line every week. Moss only does that when he feels like it.

Would his presence on the Bears open up the field for other receivers, or for the often-neglected running game? Maybe, but only if Moss actually ran his routes full speed when he wasn't the intended receiver, which he rarely does. Or, if he blocked (yeah, right).

Not only is Moss not worth the $3.38 million that remains on his contract for this season, which is what a team that claims him on waivers will have to pay him, he's not worth the $450,000 that he would make if he clears waivers and then is signed as a free agent.

In that case, the Vikings would have to pay the $3.38 million, the remainder of his $6.4 million base for this season. And a lot of players and coaches on that team will consider it money well spent just to be rid of Moss.

*?Follow Bob LeGere's Bears reports via Twitter@BobLeGere. Check out his blog, Bear Essentials, at DailyHerald.com.

title: Owens gets high sign
content:
SEATTLE -- A lot of people didn't even see Terrell Owens pull the pen out of his sock, autograph the game-winning touchdown ball used to defeat the Seattle Seahawks on Monday night and offer it to a mutual friend sitting in beaten cornerback Shawn Springs' private box. But some got a good laugh about it.

Even Springs.

"Hey, I respect that," Springs said, fighting back his trademark grin. "He had to do something. It was the only catch he had all night."

The NFL office, too, seemed to loosen up a bit Tuesday when asked if Owens would be cited for taunting after his second touchdown catch gave San Francisco a 28-21 win. Commissioner Paul Tagliabue attended the game -- the first on Monday night in Seattle since 1992 -- and Seahawks defensive lineman John Randle said he hoped Gene Washington, NFL director of football operations, was watching as well.

"Some saw it as humor, others as taunting," NFL spokesman Greg Aiello said. "If it happens again, the player who does it will receive an autographed letter from Gene Washington."

Said Owens, "I knew I was going to get into the end zone again. I was just trying to have some fun."

Yet because it was Owens, and because no one had ever seen anything like it, the play seemed to take on a life of its own. Especially since not all the Seahawks took it as well as Springs. He jokingly said, "I've officially fired my financial adviser," Greg Eastman, the recipient of the ball and the one who had given Owens the idea in the first place.

Take Chad Eaton, who led the charge on 'Niners quarterback Jeff Garcia in a game that at times looked like it would be an upset for Seattle.

"That's bull," Eaton said. "To come in here and disrespect our (new) stadium . . . just goes to show he has no class."

Then there was Randle, one of the league's biggest trash-talkers, whose first game back after knee rehabilitation sparked Seattle's defense.

"It's an embarrassment," Randle said. "For us and for the league."

Talk of retaliation seemed to be mixed, however. The teams meet again Dec. 1 at San Francisco. Said Seahawks tailback Shaun Alexander, whose 1-yard touchdown run gave Seattle a 21-20 lead with 1:11 left in the third quarter, "I don't know. I don't get into all that stuff."

Besides, the Seahawks (1-4) have enough to worry about. Though they came out of the game relatively injury free -- as opposed to the 'Niners, who lost safety Zack Bronson with a broken foot -- the Seahawks now trail first-place San Francisco (4-1) by three games in the new NFC West.

Seattle coach Mike Holmgren sounded beaten down after the game; a victory would have put the Seahawks right back into the race. Now Holmgren must endure more talk about his future. He says owner Paul Allen has "been very supportive" of his rebuilding project. He is 25-28 since taking over.

And the 'Niners? Coach Steve Mariucci has been proud of his team for carrying over its surprising success from last year. And with St. Louis in last place along with Seattle, the 49ers control the division.

But even Mariucci had to be told about the latest drama surrounding his star receiver. Two years ago, it was Owens celebrating on the star at Texas Stadium. Last year, it was a public feud with Mariucci. Recently, much was made of Owens' working-only relationship with Garcia. And now this. So Mariucci tried to laugh it off, as did the rest of his team.

"I told him to get another pen ready, because we were going to score again," guard Ron Stone said.

"I didn't see it, but I heard about it," running back Garrison Hearst said. "I thought it was pretty funny."

Before the game, the situation with Owens was starting to get sticky again. Owens, who led the league with 16 touchdown catches last season, had just one TD before he ran into Eastman at the team hotel. Owens said some reporters were trying to get him to say negative things about their offense, which entered ranked 30th in passing and struggled against Seattle's 28th-ranked defense.

So, basically, the 49ers hierarchy was just glad to see Owens happy again. Besides, the only person on the San Francisco sideline who knew what Owens was going to do was the equipment man who gave him the ink pen at the start of the fourth quarter.

Owens already had scored on an 8-yard catch in the first quarter. Springs was on the other side of the field for that one, but he and Owens traded competitive-yet-good-natured barbs most of the night. Owens finished with six catches for 84 yards. And a new layer for his image.

"That's T.O.," 49ers receivers coach George Stewart said. "Unbelievable."

title: Injury ends Anderson's season
content:
FLOWERY BRANCH, Ga. -- After two grueling years of rehab and workouts, Jamal Anderson believed his right knee was finally back to All-Pro form.

Then his left knee blew out.

The former University of Utah star learned Monday that he tore a knee ligament in the Atlanta Falcons' victory over Arizona, ending his season and raising doubts about whether he'll make it back again.

"I wouldn't count him out," coach Dan Reeves said. "But it's very difficult. Very few people come back from one. To come back from two surgeries would really be difficult."

Anderson, an All-Pro in 1998 when the Falcons reached the Super Bowl, tore the anterior cruciate ligament in his right knee early the following season.

He went down with the same injury to the other knee in Sunday's 34-14 victory over the Cardinals, meaning surgery in a week or two, up to a year to get back on the field and even longer to run like he once did -- if ever.

"It's sad," cornerback Ray Buchanan said. "We've got to find a way to move on. I don't want to go through what I did two years ago."

Anderson, visibly shaken, refused to comment as he hobbled to his car. The team said he may hold a news conference Wednesday.

The Falcons (2-1) collapsed in 1999 after Anderson was injured. This time, the team feels better equipped to handle the loss.

"I would be very surprised if we threw in the towel," fullback Bob Christian said.

Anderson will be replaced by Maurice Smith, a second-year player who came to the Falcons as an undrafted free agent from North Carolina A&T.

Smith, who didn't carry the ball in Atlanta's first two games, ran for 80 yards and a touchdown on 21 carries against the Cardinals. The 235-pounder also showed better speed than Anderson, going 79 yards for a touchdown on a short pass.

"Assuming Maurice reaches his potential, they are similar runners," quarterback Chris Chandler said. "But he has a ways to go to get to Jamal's level."

The Falcons will be hard-pressed to compensate for Anderson's pass-catching skills, ability to pick up blitzes and vocal role in the locker room.

"It's the intangible things you don't see," Reeves said, "as a leader, the inspiration he is to our players. Those are things you miss, too."

Anderson rushed for 184 yards in his first two games and caught a 94-yard touchdown pass in a victory over Carolina.

Smith had more carries against Arizona than he did in his entire rookie season, when he ran 19 times for 69 yards.

"I didn't think my chance would come this early," Smith said. "It's kind of disappointing to think No. 32 is gone."

Rodney Thomas, who backed up Eddie George in Tennessee the last five years, is nearly recovered from an ankle injury and also will get some carries.

Smith and Thomas can't do much worse than the last combo that tried to fill Anderson's shoes.

Ken Oxendine and Byron Hanspard averaged about 3 yards per carry and managed only two touchdowns between them in 1999. Both players are now out of the league.

Three years ago, Anderson set an NFL record with 410 carries and a franchise mark with 1,846 yards rushing as the Falcons captured their first NFC title.

The next season, after holding out in training camp during a contract dispute, Anderson was injured in Week 2 against the Dallas Cowboys. He took a handoff, tried to change directions and collapsed to the turf without being touched.

The Falcons slumped from 14-2 to 5-11 without their best runner.

Anderson started all 16 games last season, but struggled to regain his form behind a woeful offensive line. He still managed to gain 1,024 yards -- the fourth 1,000-yard season of his career -- while the Falcons went 4-12.

The latest injury occurred during an 80-yard touchdown drive on the game's opening series. Anderson -- on his 29th birthday -- was running right on a routine sweep when he planted hard on his left knee and tumbled to the grass for a 1-yard gain.

Again, he wasn't even tackled by an opposing player. The play didn't count, either; Arizona was offsides.

"I feel bad for this football team, but I really feel bad for Jamal," Reeves said. "He worked so hard to get back to the point where he was 100 percent and playing well."

Even if Anderson comes back next year, history has shown it takes a full season to get comfortable with a reconstructed knee. That pushes his recovery time into 2003, when he'll be 31.

The Falcons already lost receiver Tony Martin for a month with a broken collarbone. Top draft pick Michael Vick, who didn't play against the Cardinals because of sore ribs, should return for Sunday's game against Chicago.

Reeves moaned, "We can't keep losing people like Tony Martin and Jamal Anderson."

title: Bolts to be shaken, not stirred Team will mix in some new players, which means some favorites to leave
content:
This is the time of the year Junior Seau hates.

It's when the calendar flips from regular season to postseason and the Chargers take their customary place among the also-rans. Sunday's 31-28 overtime loss to the Seattle Seahawks was the Chargers' fourth defeat in a row and seventh in nine games.

It leveled their record at 8-8 and meant a seventh straight year without a winning record or playoff appearance. General manager John Butler refused interview requests yesterday and the locker room was closed to the media.

That left four players hand-picked by the organization -- Seau, Donnie Edwards, LaDainian Tomlinson and Drew Brees-- to explain what went wrong and where the franchise goes from here. Seau's words carried the most weight because he has been with the Chargers longer than any other player.

He acknowledged that someone has to take the fall, and since management and the coaching staff are only one or two years on the job, the ax undoubtedly will fall on some players. Seau will return for a 14th season, but longtime running mate and two-time Pro Bowl strong safety Rodney Harrison received no assurances from coach Marty Schottenheimer that he'll be brought back for a 10th season. "I can't give you an answer, yes or no," Harrison said by phone after his exit interview with Schottenheimer, who will meet with the media today. "That's as much as I want to say right now." Seau's eyes appeared to water when asked about Harrison and other veterans being possible casualties to another wasted season. "It's going to be shaken up," he said.

"It's part of the business, and it's the part of the business that you don't like because you become accustomed to a player and a coach.

And now the lockers are moving.

The revolving door will be moving this offseason.

It'll definitely be a sad day." What pains Seau is that he believes he could have prevented the late-season slide.

He injured his left ankle near the end of the third game, at Arizona, and never recovered.

He will have surgery this offseason to remove two bone spurs and will require at least three weeks' recovery. Whether he will have the surgery before the Pro Bowl on Feb. 2 has yet to be decided, but Seau was certain he could have made a difference had he been healthy. "In hindsight, should we have taken the time off during the season to do it?"

Seau said of surgery.

"With the way everything was going, we can second-guess ourselves for that.

And that kills me more now after everything was said and done. "It's easy for me to say that I should have (had the surgery and come back), but it's tough when you love the game so much that you're just wanting to be out there.

But the smart thing to do, looking back -- the New England game I should have sat out." Seau played that week, sat out the next game against Denver, then lasted only the first half the following week against visiting Kansas City. "That really ruined it," Seau said.

"I did not want to touch the ankle during the season, not knowing what would happen and the process of how long it would take me to rehab the ankle.

I didn't know anything about a high ankle sprain." The Chargers gave up the decisive score in the final two minutes or in overtime in four of their final eight games.

Seau was on the field for all but one of them. "It was tough, especially when I knew with two ankles I could have helped," Seau said.

"I really could have.

And not being able to practice during the course of the (final three weeks) and not having a rhythm for each other and a continuity that we always had in the past, it just wasn't there. That's something we're going to build on and hopefully we'll get covered." But with whom?

That's the question everyone wants answered. "I guess you could say that (the talent isn't as good as the team contends) because we don't get it done," Edwards said.

"We're 8-8 and we're not in the playoffs, but we should be.

On paper we look like a Super Bowl-caliber team.

But if you fail to get done what needs to get done, achieve the goal, your performance speaks for yourself, I guess. "Everyone can look at paper and say, oh, this is a great team.

But until you actually execute and get it done, then that's the true determining factor of if you're a good team, an average team, a great team or a bad team." There's no doubt the Chargers must address needs at wide receiver, on both lines and at kicker, but the starting point for Butler and Schottenheimer is expected to be the secondary.

The Chargers allowed eight teams to pass for 300 yards or more, with two surpassing 440 yards.

The defensive backs managed only nine interceptions, one more than cornerback Ryan McNeil had by himself in 2001. Some of that had to do with a lethargic pass rush, some of it had to do with a conservative scheme, but some of it had to do with defensive backs failing to make plays, which is why there could be as many as three new starters in the secondary. The Chargers also would like to find a big-play wideout.

Curtis Conway had an impressive start, but a pinched nerve in his shoulder caused him to miss three games and nearly all of two others down the stretch.

He has proved to be good for 1,000 yards when he plays a full season, but that has happened only three times in 10 seasons. On the lines, the Chargers need more experience on offense and more size inside on defense.

The defensive front was not the same after tackle Jamal Williams dislocated his left ankle. As for the kicking game, free-agent-to-be Steve Christie converted only 2-of-9 field-goal attempts beyond 41 yards.

On a handful of occasions, the Chargers passed up points because they didn't believe Christie had the range for a long attempt.
