Put it all together and you have a team that has gone 13-19 in two seasons under Butler, a team that presented Schottenheimer with his first four-game losing streak to end a season in 17 years as a head coach, a team that has a lot of questions and no tangible answers. "We did get better, but at the same time I don't feel like we got too much better," Tomlinson said in comparing this season with last year's 5-11 finish.

"It's kind of hard to swallow because of the fact that we lost four straight." Tomlinson said Schottenheimer offered limited explanations for the late-season slide. "Maybe it's going to take Marty a couple of more days before he knows what it is," Tomlinson said.

"But one thing he did say is we did lose some of our fundamentals and techniques toward the end of the season, doing things the right way.

So that was a little answer that he gave us." The Chargers can only hope Schottenheimer and Butler come up with better answers in the next few months; otherwise the only change will be the players' names, not the team's record. Best of the Bolts The Chargers handed out their team awards yesterday.

The winners: MVP: RB LaDainian Tomlinson Defensive Player: LB Donnie Edwards Most Inspirational: LB Junior Seau Lineman of the Year: C Jason Ball Special Teams: LB Carlos Polk

title: Wacky world of sports: Satirical year in review
content:
With New Year's Eve less than three weeks away, let's take a look at some of the most memorable moments and intriguing story lines of 2006 ...

The Pittsburgh Steelers won one for the thumb, capturing their fifth Super Bowl title in franchise history with a 21-10 victory over the Seattle Seahawks. Quarterback Ben Roethlisbergercelebrated by throwing motorcycle helmets into the Ohio River.

Free agent Terrell Owens eventually stole the spotlight from Super Bowl XL during one of the busiest offseasons in football history. The defiant wide receiver ultimately signed with the Dallas Cowboys after owner Jerry Jonesforgot to add a "No Attempting Suicide" clause in his contract.

Vince Young led his Texas Longhorns to a stunning 41-38 victory over Southern California in the Rose Bowl thanks to an ill-advised lateral by Reggie Bush, who later dropped to second in the NFL draft behind North Carolina State long snapper Lauren Bernstein.

Trinidad and Tobago captured the West Indian cricket title over Barbados in a game that brought back memories of Willis Reed limping onto the court at Madison Square Garden 36 years ago. Who could have predicted captain Brian Lara returning from such a devastating injury to facilitate Ryan Hinds and Patrick Browne's 40-run sixth-wicket stand?

Shaquille O'Neal returned to prominence in his second season in Miami, helping the Heat erase a 2-0 deficit against the Dallas Mavericks to win the NBA Championship in six games. Former teammate Kobe Bryant sulked for two weeks before finally treating himself to an eight-carat diamond ring.

Bryant led the league in scoring with 35.4 points per game (remember that 81-point performance in January), but still finished fourth in the MVP voting behind a Canadian defensive liability with no vertical leap and a German flop artist who admitted to having a crush on David Hasselhoff in the early '90s.

Florida toppled UCLA 73-57 to win the NCAA men's basketball title, but the story of the tournament was George Mason's upset over top-seeded UConn in the Elite Eight. After the game, Phil Mickelson called the Huskies' collapse "the biggest choke job of all time."

After the NHL's one-year hiatus, Carolina edged the eighth-seeded Edmonton Oilers in seven games to win the Stanley Cup. First-year Phoenix head coach Wayne Gretzky was spotted celebrating during the Hurricanes' title run, but later denied betting on the playoffs.

Roger Federer and Justine Henin-Hardenne may have dominated the tennis front, reaching all four Grand Slam finals, but Andre Agassi's antics will be remembered most. The 36-year-old legend, who announced his imminent retirement before the U.S. Open, was ejected from the tournament's third round after head-butting opponent Benjamin Becker in front of a stunned crowd at Arthur Ashe Stadium.

Speaking of ill-advised head-butts, France's Zinedine Zidane said he was verbally provoked before attacking Marco Materazzi in the FIFA World Cup Championship, which Italy won in a penalty-kick shootout, 5-3. After extra time couldn't settle the 1-1 draw, MLB commissioner Bud Selig said it was the most exciting international title game since Japan defeated Cuba 10-6 in the inaugural World Baseball Classic.

Following Japan's historic victory, the St. Louis Cardinals beat the Detroit Tigers in five games to win the World Series, Barry Bonds passed Babe Ruth for second on the all-time home-run list and after beginning June marred in a 1-for-18 slump, baseball officials declared Alex Rodriguez the coldest athlete since Ted Williams.

Frozen corpse jokes aside, the 2006 Winter Olympics in Turin, Italy, was a smashing success. Shizuka Arakawa took home gold in the ladies' free skate, Sweden defeated Finland for the men's hockey title and, for two weeks, millions of people pretended like they cared about alpine skiing and the luge.

Despite enormous expectations, Barbaro failed to capture the Triple Crown, suffering a leg injury during the Preakness Stakes. Following the race, the thoroughbred took up boxing and knocked Mike Tyson out in three rounds.

Sports may be unpredictable, but there's one thing that you can always count on: With Hank Aaron's home-run record in jeopardy, LaDainian Tomlinson's monster season, Greg Oden's unclear draft status and many other stories to follow in 2007, next year will provide even more memorable moments to look back upon. Happy New Year.

(C) 2006 Pipe Dream via U-WIRE

title: Samoan roots anchor Oregon State QB's journey
content:
In the Samoan culture, as in any culture, family is the most important factor in a person's life, an aspect a person lives in a lifetime, but it's an aspect that Lyle Moevao learned at an early age. Moevao moved from San Diego to Hawaii and back again on numerous occasions growing up, and with his sister and two brothers working as a tight-knit group with their mother and father the entire time, they gave the definition of family. Moevao's two brothers played football at a young age, and it was an easy thing for him to pick up.

"My oldest brother grew too fast, so he ended up a linebacker/defensive end type of guy. My other brother was a speedy guy, more of a bulkier guy, so we knew he was going to be more of a secondary guy like a safety." Moevao said. "In high school I played quarterback and defensive end, and it was pretty cool because I got roughed up as a defensive end, and then as a quarterback anytime I got hit I was able to take it because I was doing it on the other side of the ball."

The 5' 11" 235 pound junior quarterback had a hard time transitioning to quarterback, as teams didn't see the fit. Boise State offered him a scholarship, but Moevao turned it down and turned to junior college in El Camino, who allowed him to play quarterback. A year later, he's lighting up Washington defenders here at Reser Stadium and tied for lead in the Pac-10 in passing with Arizona's Willie Tuitama.

Family was an important aspect of Moevao's life. To remember he had a personalized symbol for his family tattooed on his right arm. The tattoo can be seen on any game day as he may fire a pass to Shane Morales or Sammie Stroughter. The reasoning behind the tattoo was simple: to remember where he came from and who helped get him there.

"When I got it, I went to a guy who specialized in tribal and Samoan tattooing, and in the Samoan culture there are different symbols that have different meanings for things in life," Moevao said, "So when he was doing it he was just asking me to explain my life and the important things that I put my trust into growing up, so it's mostly about my family, about my mom, who was a single parent for about 19 years, it was me, my two brothers and my sister, so four kids, so it was definitely a struggle for her, and it's something I look up to her for."

Moevao started out at Oregon State in competition his sophomore year with Sean Canfield for former Beaver quarterback Matt Moore's position after he graduated. Moevao and Canfield ended up beginning the season splitting time, but when Canfield went down with injury against USC, Moevao took over full-time and led the Beavers to an Emerald Bowl victory against Maryland. In just a year and a half Moevao has transformed into a double threat: a precision passing quarterback that can take off with the ball. Every game, Moevao can be heard shouting plays and audibles from the line, and is one of the more vocal leaders Oregon State has seen in past years.

"I make sure I'm a vocal leader more than anything," Moevao said. "I know the other guys feed off of what you say as a quarterback, and especially being the one that handles the ball all the time, if you're not saying anything and you look like you're not confident, then they're not going to be confident in what they are doing either."

The confident leadership has translated to the field as Moevao has led the Beavers to victories in the last four of five games, including the upset of #1 USC, and has thrown touchdown passes in each of the last five. Although Canfield is once again healthy after recovering from offseason surgery, Moevao still holds down the starting position.

"I have alot of admiration for how he approaches his preparation for the game both on and off the field; the extra watching of film, the extra meeting time with the coaches," head coach Mike Riley said. "He was here after practice watching film with Danny [Langsdorf] and I as we looked back at the practice film of the day. He was the only other player in the building doing that."

Being surrounded by a young supporting cast seemed to be a challenge in the preseason after losing starting running back Yvenson Bernard and wideout Brandon Powers to graduation, but the there was tremendous depth for the Beavers on the offensive line, as well as in the skill positions. True freshman Jacquizz Rodgers leads the Pac-10 in rushing and has helped open up the passing game, which has all been started with the offensive line.

"I think it was a really good learning experience from those two losses earlier on in the season for our offensive line to learn and catch up on the game," Moevao said. "We have a redshirt freshman starting at center and a true freshman at tackle and those are pretty important parts of the offensive line, with the centers making the calls and the tackles having the most athletic defensive linemen coming off the edge, so I think all the time we have put in, we have come a long way and the offensive line has improved."

Moevao made it to 1,500 yards faster than now-Cleveland Brown quarterback Derek Anderson and has spread the ball around all season, as five different players have received a touchdown, and three players have over 300 yards receiving. With still another year of eligibility left, Moevao still has time to leave his print on Oregon State football history, and could be well on his way.

"Lyle is a great example of a guy taking advantage of a year, or at least part of a year of playing the game," Riley said. "Over a year of learning the system, and then combining it with hard, physical work in preparation for a season and then continued dedication to his craft during the season."

(C) 2008 OSU Daily Barometer via UWIRE

title: BLUE EYE ON SPY. BELICHICK'S PATRIOTS LOOM LARGE FOR GIANTS
content:
IT'S STILL SEVEN weeks away, but making the playoffs for the Giants could come down to the final Saturday night of the season when they play the Patriots.

Bill Belichick returns Dec. 29 to Giants Stadium, the scene of his espionage caper gone bad when the Patriots were caught in the Spygate scandal in the season opener against the Jets.

The Giants privately are rooting for two things in the Patriots' next six games: They want them to lose, which would end their run at an undefeated season, and they want them to have clinched the AFC's No. 1 seed before the final game.

That would remove Belichick's motivation to play his starters and could get the Giants a good look at Matt Cassel instead of Tom Bradyand lots of Jabar Gaffney instead of Randy Moss.

If the Patriots, who are more than halfway home to an undefeated regular season with a 9-0 record, come into Giants Stadium at 15-0, they will have the top seed clinched, but there seems little doubt Belichick will go all out to get to 16-0, even at the risk of injury before the playoffs. In the year of Spygate, he clearly would like to stick it to the NFL by running the table straight through the Super Bowl with a perfect season.

After the Giants' crucial loss to the Cowboys, flashbacks to last season's second-half collapse are hard to avoid. Their last seven games are against teams that are 36-27. The Giants ripped off their winning streak against teams that are 15-38.

By losing to Dallas, the Giants basically were eliminated from the NFC East race and now have to maneuver for a wild-card berth. They first play the Lions, who are also 6-3. Clearly, it's another crucial game. Holding the tiebreaker over the Lions will be essential.

The good news for the Giants is that this is another down year for the NFC. The Giants, Lions and Redskins are the only teams in the NFC over .500 other than the division leaders. After the Giants made the playoffs last year at 8-8, another .500 team could get in again this year. It helped the Giants that the Lions, Redskins, Saints and Panthers also lost on Sunday.

With the Redskins one game behind and the Bears and Eagles two games back and on the schedule in the next six weeks, the Giants can determine their own fate. They are 23-9 in the first half of the season with Tom Coughlin but an abysmal 8-17 in the second half. Could be a trend.

Antonio Pierce says he's not concerned about last year's 2-6 finish after a 6-2 start.

"This is a different team, different mentality, different attitude, different players," he said. "You can just look at the past for a learning lesson. You can't look at it for nothing else."

It will be a wild ride for the Giants in the next seven weeks:

. SUNDAY, AT DETROIT: The Giants haven't played there since 1997 and, of course, they've never played at Ford Field. The Lions are 4-0 at home this year, but going into this season, they were 24-72 in the first six years of the Matt Millen era and have not made the playoffs since 1999.

After the Lions' 31-21 loss to the Cardinals on Sunday, you have to wonder if they have the staying power to make a run at the wild card, They've been devoured in their two NFC East games, losing to the Eagles, 56-21, and the Redskins, 34-3, and get the Cowboys next month. They also have two games left against the Packers. If you're a serious playoff contender, you can't lose to Jon Kitna.

. NOV. 25, VIKINGS: You never want anybody to get hurt, but the Giants are not going to cry if sensational rookie running back Adrian Peterson can't play. He's been ruled out of Sunday's game against the Raiders after tearing the lateral collateral ligament in his right knee Sunday against the Packers. Coach Brad Childress said it's not a season-ending injury, but did not say when he would return. "I'm real hesitant to put a time line on that thing," Childress said.

. DEC. 2, AT CHICAGO: Don't kick the ball to Devin Hester and this game will not be a problem. Da Bears are only 4-5 because they are trying to win with Rex Grossman and Brian Griese, which means they are trying to win without a quarterback.

. DEC. 9, AT PHILADELPHIA: The Birds kept their season going with the comeback victory in Washington. Donovan McNabb should lock the doors to the team bus before Osi Umenyiora can get off. Still, with speculation McNabb could lose his job to rookie Kevin Kolb with a couple more losses, and the turmoil in Andy Reid's life, this may not be the typical tough trip to Philly.

. DEC. 16, REDSKINS: The goal-line stand saved the Giants' season in the third week in Washington. If the Skins stay in the race, the Giants will have a chance to put them away.

. DEC. 23, AT BUFFALO: Over the last 10 years, the average high temperature in Buffalo for this date is 33 degrees. The average low is 18. The Giants better hope the Bills, who have won five out of their last six, have no shot at the playoffs and their bags are packed.

. DEC. 29, PATRIOTS: Belichick won't bring his sideline video cameras. The Giants would rather he not bring Brady and Moss.

Nine victories will get an NFC wild card. At the very least, the Giants should beat the Vikings, Eagles and Redskins, unless, of course, '07 becomes '06.

title: Ballot is out for 2007 fantasy surprises, busts
content:
It's that time of year again. Time to vote for the good, bad and the ugly of the fantasy football world.

Whether you are in the playoffs or not, now is the time to vent a little frustration on players who ruined your season, or to heap praise upon those who have helped you reach the promised land.

Remember, however, to stay objective.

Just because Shaun Alexander was on your team doesn't mean he should win the Biggest Bust award. Think about the players on that list and vote for who would have messed up a person's season the most.

And since the MVP, Rookie of the Year and Comeback Player of the Year are all locks, I had a good suggestion to change things up a bit. We're expanding the Biggest Surprises and Busts to each position - QB, RB and WR.

E-mail me at jdietz@dailyherald.com with your votes, make some comments, and the best will make this column in two weeks. Deadline for submission is Dec. 18.

Biggest surprise QB

- The Browns' Derek Anderson. Twenty-nine touchdowns from a guy nobody even thought about on draft day? Amazing.

- Packers QB Brett Favre. Considered a fantasy backup in September, the rejuvenated Favre has eclipsed the 300-yard mark seven times and has thrown for 24 TDs.

- Steelers QB Ben Roethlisberger.After three years of giving us 18-20 TDs, Big Ben has 28 total TDs with three games remaining.

- Cowboys QB Tony Romo.If it weren't for Tom Bradyand Randy Moss, Romo (37 total TDs, seven 300-yard games) would be in the MVP race.

Biggest surprise WR

- Browns Braylon Edwards. With 13 TD catches and 1,106 yards, Edwards is the No. 3 receiver in fantasy football.

- Bengals WR T.J. Houshmandzadeh. With everybody taking teammate Chad Johnson first, it's Housh (96-996-11) who has shocked us.

- The Packers' Greg Jennings. Here's a guy who has missed two games and still has 11 TDs.

- The Patriots' Randy Moss. Considered a huge risk at fantasy drafts, Moss (82-1,264-19) has made teams instant title contenders.

Biggest surprise RB

- The Vikings' Adrian Peterson. Taken around the fifth through seventh rounds, Peterson (1,200-230-11) is neck-and-neck with Joseph Addai for third place in fantasy points among RBs.

- The Browns' Jamal Lewis. Wasn't Lewis suppose to be washed up? Well, he didn't get the message and has shocked his owners with 9 TDs and a pair of 100-yard games the last six weeks.

- The Bucs' Earnest Graham. With 9 TDs and over 1,000 yards from scrimmage, the no-name Graham has surely led teams into the playoffs.

- The Packers' Ryan Grant. Since taking over as the featured back in Week 8, Grant has 717 rushing yards and 5 TDs.

Biggest bust QB

- Rams QB Marc Bulger. A top-six QB on draft day, Bulger has just 6 TD passes and 9 interceptions in nine games.

- The Lions' Jon Kitna. He got off to a good start, but since Week 4, Kitna has just 7 TD passes and 9 interceptions.

- Eagles QB Donovan McNabb. Eight of McNabb's 14 TD passes came in two games.

- The Titans' Vince Young. Some fantasy owners took a chance on Young, thinking his legs might make him an every-week starter. Wrong. Young has 7 passing TDs, 3 rushing and a whopping 16 interceptions.

Biggest bust RB

- Seahawks RB Shaun Alexander. The 2005 MVP has fallen off the face of the earth. One TD since Week 2? Brutal.

- Niners RB Frank Gore. The fourth overall pick in some drafts, Gore has scored in just one game since Week 2.

- Rams RB Steven Jackson. The second or third overall pick in most drafts, Jackson is playing better but still has just 4 TDs and one 100-yard game.

- Chiefs RB Larry Johnson. You'll have to decide if you want to give Johnson (4 TDs) a pass for missing the second half of the season due to injury.

- Jaguars RB Maurice Jones-Drew. MJD has gone over 55 rushing yards just three times and didn't live up to his high draft status.

Biggest bust WR

- Bills WR Lee Evans. Aside from three good outings, Evans has averaged 38 yards per game with no TDs.

- Bengals WR Chad Johnson. Amazingly, Johnson is ranked eighth among receivers. But we aren't fooled - most of that is due to a blazing start. Like Gore, he's scored in just one game since Week 2.

- Panthers WR Steve Smith. No TDs and 42 yards per game since Week 6? Brutal.

My teams: Both were off last week thanks to first-round byes. My semifinals won't be easy, though. Despite going a combined 19-7 in my two leagues, I am 0-3 against the teams I face this weekend.

Good bets

- Niners RB Frank Gore vs. Cincinnati. The Bengals have allowed a rushing TD in nine of their 13 games.

- Ravens WR Derrick Mason vs. Miami. It's not difficult to look good against the woeful Dolphins.

- Browns RB Jamal Lewis vs. Buffalo. Over the last five weeks, the Bills have given up 8 rushing TDs.

- Titans RB LenDale White vs. Kansas City. White has been infuriating lately, but he should find running room against a Chiefs defense that was gutted by the Broncos last week.

- Cardinals QB Kurt Warner vs. New Orleans. If Atlanta's Chris Redman and Tampa Bay's Luke McCown can both throw 2 TD passes and approach 300 yards, what is Warner capable of? Figure 325-plus yards and at least 3 TDs.

- Giants defense vs. Washington. I'd be stunned if the Redskins score over 14 points.

- Vikings WR Sidney Rice vs. the Bears. Rice is a risky play, but if you get bonus points for long TDs, he's worth a look against a sieve-like Bears defense.

- Seahawks RB Shaun Alexander vs. Carolina. Whoever thought Alexander would be a sleeper good bet? I can see him scoring a TD (maybe 2?) in what should be a Seattle blowout win.

Bad bets

- Redskins RB Clinton Portis vs. the New York Giants. With Todd Collins starting at quarterback, the Giants can focus on shutting down Portis (47.5 rush yards per game, 1 TD last four).

- Steelers QB Ben Roethlisbergerand WR Santonio Holmes vs. Jacksonville. Big Ben hasn't gone over 200 passing yards since Week 10 and has just 4 TD passes over that span.

- Jaguars RB Maurice Jones-Drew and WR Reggie Williams vs. Pittsburgh. This is a no-brainer bench game for Jones-Drew against a defense that has allowed 3 rushing TDs all season. As for Williams, he's been hot but this is a tough matchup.

- Panthers WR Steve Smith vs. Seattle. Smith's last productive game came in Week 6.

- Bears RB Adrian Peterson vs. Minnesota. Stay far, far away from Peterson this week.

- Patriots kicker Stephen Gostkowski vs. the New York Jets. If the snowstorm that is forecast for the East Coast is in full swing during this game, stay away from Gostkowski.

jdietz@dailyherald.com

- Catch John on WSCR 670-AM's "Fantasy Football Today" every Sunday at 8 a.m. John also appears with Mike Murphy most Thursdays in the 1 p.m. hour.

title: NFL NOTES: SMITH HOPES TO GET RUSHING RECORD AT HOME SUNDAY
content:
Emmitt Smith wants his pursuit of Walter Payton's career rushing record to end at home.

Smith would like to break the record at Texas Stadium, and his last chance is Sunday when the Dallas Cowboys host Seattle before playing their next two games on the road. He needs a season-best 93 yards against the Seahawks.

"I'm very excited about the opportunity," Smith said Monday. "I want to do it at home very bad, very bad."

After a season-high 82 yards on 22 carries in Sunday's 9-6 overtime loss at Arizona, Smith finally moved within 100 yards of Payton's record 16,726 yards set in 190 games over 13 seasons.

"I'll be glad whenever it's down to single digits," said Smith, who has played 192 games in his 13-season career. "Seattle has a lot to say about it."

Smith insists he's not preparing any differently this week, even with the record in reach against the NFL's worst rushing defense. The Seahawks have allowed 190 yards rushing per game, including 183 yards and three touchdowns by St. Louis' Marshall Faulk in a 37-20 loss Sunday.

Hutchinson named Cowboys starter Quincy Carter is out and Chad Hutchinson is in as the starting quarterback.

The Cowboys made the surprising switch one day after Carter threw four interceptions two around the end zone in a 9-6 overtime loss at Arizona.

Favre may not miss a game Brett Favre won't need surgery on his sprained left knee, and the Packers are optimistic he won't miss any games.

An MRI confirmed the team's original diagnosis of a sprained lateral collateral ligament and showed no structural damage to the star quarterback's knee.

The Packers are idle this week. Their next game is Nov. 4 against Miami.

Rolle fined Cornerback Samari Rolle was fined $ 7,500 by the NFL for a helmet-to-helmet hit that left Jacksonville quarterback Mark Brunell with a concussion.

Redskins back to Matthews Steve Spurrier switched starting quarterbacks for the third time this season, promoting Shane Matthews and benching rookie Patrick Ramsey for the Washington Redskins' game against Indianapolis.

"Patrick had three games, playing very well the first game, and since then it hasn't worked out," Spurrier said. "He's obviously not as prepared as he should be."

Spurrier said he expects Matthews to finish the season, although he said the same thing about Ramsey two weeks ago.

Rams' Williams out for season Rams cornerback Aeneas Williams will miss the rest of the season after surgery to stabilize torn ligaments in his badly sprained left ankle.

Williams also broke his left shin with 1:46 to play in St. Louis' game Sunday against the Seattle Seahawks when teammate Adam Archuleta rolled over him.

Broncos lose Nalen Center Tom Nalen will miss the rest of the season after tearing the anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee against Kansas City.

Nalen was hit from the side late in Denver's 37-34 overtime victory over the Chiefs on Sunday. An MRI revealed a complete tear of the ACL and some cartilage damage.

Buchanon out two months Rookie cornerback and punt returner Phillip Buchanon will miss two months with a broken left wrist yet more bad news for the reeling Raiders.

Buchanon, the team's top draft pick out of Miami, was hurt in the second quarter of Sunday's 27-21 overtime loss to San Diego.

Brad Johnson questionable for Bucs Tampa Bay quarterback Brad Johnson has a fractured left rib and is doubtful for this week's game against the Carolina Panthers.

If he is not ready to play against Carolina on Sunday, he will be replaced by backup Rob Johnson, the former Buffalo Bills starter.

QB list very short in Carolina Quarterback Chris Weinke spent the night in the hospital with a concussion, leaving the Panthers with rookie Randy Fasani as their only healthy option for this week's game against Tampa Bay.

Weinke was knocked out of Sunday's loss to Atlanta and spent the night in a Charlotte hospital being treated for a serious concussion. He was released Monday, but coach John Fox said he didn't know if Weinke would be able to face the Bucs and their top-ranked defense.

Rodney Peete, who started the first six games for Carolina, had surgery on his knee last week and is out until next month. So that leaves Fasani as the only option, unless the Panthers sign someone.

title: Season is off to weird, wacky start
content:
At some point during the first two weeks of the season, the real NFL apparently was abducted and replaced by an insidious imposter. How else to explain the wackiness that is swirling about the league?

Just take a look at the division standings. Of the eight leaders (some based on tiebreaker procedures), only Indianapolis is a defending champion. Three first-place teams -- San Francisco, Chicago and Tampa Bay -- finished at the bottom of their respective standings in 2004.

The four remaining leaders -- the New York Giants, New York Jets, Cincinnati and Kansas City -- had a combined 31-33 record last year.

Seven teams sport 2-0 marks, yet five were no better than .500 in '04. Three of them -- the Giants, Bucs and Washington -- are coming off double-digit-loss campaigns.

An additional seven teams are 0-2. Two were division champions (Green Bay and San Diego), and another had a winning record (Baltimore). Two more (Arizona and Minnesota) were popular preseason picks in their respective divisions.

The first 32 head-to-head matchups have featured some startling results: The Packers (10-6 in '04) lost to the Browns (4-12) and the Lions (6-10); the Vikings (8-8) fell to the Bucs (5-11 in '04) and Bengals (8-8); Carolina (7-9) upended the Patriots (14-2); Tennessee (5-11) thumped the Ravens (9-7); Miami (5-11) steamrolled Denver (10-6); and Dallas (6-10) turned aside the Chargers (12-4).

The odd start also is reflected in the individual performances. How to explain:

- That the league's top two rushers are a rookie (the Bucs' Cadillac Williams) and a guy who had played in only eight NFL games previously (Pittsburgh's Willie Parker)?

- That the top two quarterbacks statistically are the Bengals' Carson Palmerand Cleveland's Trent Dilfer, while reigning league MVP Peyton Manningof Indianapolis stands 22nd in passing yardage and 20th in passer rating? Even younger brother Eli of the Giants has a better rating (76.3) than Peyton (74.7).

This past Sunday, Manning completed just 13 of 28 passes for 122 yards and had an abysmal passer rating of 44.0. Vikings standout Daunte Culpepper was even worse, earning a 36.3 rating after going 21 of 37 for 236 yards while heaving five interceptions.

Already, an offensive coordinator has been fired (Houston's Chris Palmer), and speculation is growing regarding the job security of head coaches Mike Tice of Minnesota, Brian Billick of Baltimore and the Texans' Dom Capers.

Actually, this is bliss for Commissioner Paul Tagliabue, who fairly swoons at the mere mention of "parity." Eighteen of the 32 teams are 1-1, including all four of the AFC East representatives, and three of four in both the NFC West and the NFC South.

If the regular season suddenly were declared over today, the playoffs would have a strange look: the Buccaneers, Redskins and Giants would be in. The Chargers, Packers and Ravens would be out.

Salary caps and free agency have provided a competitive boon for the league. Good teams often are gutted in the subsequent offseason, while bad teams are able to apply quick patches to their gaping holes.

Consider that of the 10 Super Bowl teams over the past five years, five had losing records the following year. Only four made the playoffs, with just one -- New England last season - advancing beyond the second round.

Stuck in the 18-team traffic jam of 1-1 teams are the Super Bowl-champion Patriots -- and remember, the Pats didn't lose their first game last year until Week 8 -- and reigning NFC winner Philadelphia.

Of course, it's way too early to determine if this year's weird start signals a trend or is a mere aberration. Most likely, things will settle down shortly, the heavyweights will start getting in their punches, and the contenders will begin to separate themselves from the pretenders.

But at least for the time being -- and much to Tagliabue's delight -- it does make the NFL charmingly unpredictable.

title: Steelers wary of Bengals AFC North leaders can't afford letup against team with nothing to lose
content:
PITTSBURGH -- After catching two touchdown passes in a big victory here in 2005, T.J. Houshmandzadeh of the Cincinnati Bengals wiped off his muddy shoes with a Terrible Towel, the talisman twirled by fans of the Pittsburgh Steelers.

Is the Cincinnati wide receiver packing another shine rag for tonight's AFC North game between the Bengals and Steelers?

"The way that this season has gone, there will be none of that going on. You can't showboat when you are 1-8-1," Houshmandzadeh says.

"I am just coming down there to have fun. ... They are the No.1 defense, and they are playing to win the division along with Baltimore. So we are just going to come in there and try to spoil it for them and put them back in a first-place tie with Baltimore."

That view comes as no surprise to the Steelers (7-3), who hold a one-game lead over the Baltimore Ravens in the division.

"They don't have anything to lose," Steelers wide receiver Hines Ward says. "They'll go for it on fourth-and-3. They'll do the trick plays. They'll blitz the house. ... At the same time, it's an opportunity to try and improve our record to 8-3. It's a divisional game. So it's a big game for us."

With two-time Pro Bowl quarterback Carson Palmerout with a injured elbow, fourth-year player Ryan Fitzpatrick has started six of the past seven games for Cincinnati.

Pittsburgh sacked Fitzpatrick seven times Oct.19 in a 38-10 victory in Cincinnati.

Fitzpatrick threw for two touchdowns two weeks ago in the Bengals' lone victory, 21-19 against the Jacksonville Jaguars.

Though sacked eight times Sunday in a 13-13 tie with the Philadelphia Eagles, Fitzpatrick threw for a season-high 261 yards and a touchdown.

"(The Bengals) are a team that is different than they were a couple of weeks ago. They are finding a rhythm," Steelers coach Mike Tomlin says.

Does he regard the Bengals as dangerous?

"They're all dangerous. ... ask Jacksonville," Tomlin says. "They (the Bengals) are a good football team. They have Pro Bowl-caliber players, and they have good coaches. For whatever reason, they are what they are from a record standpoint, but we respect those guys.

"We respect this matchup. This is a division game, one that is played at home in front of our fans. It's dangerous, but so are we."

In the last meeting, Bengals rookie linebacker Keith Rivers sustained a broken jaw when blocked downfield by Ward. The Bengals and Steelers must gear up for more hard hitting four days after playing Sunday.

"It's a little different ... especially with us having come off an overtime game where we basically played an extra 15 minutes," Houshmandzadeh says. "So it's tough, man. Guys are a little beat up, but they (the Steelers) are going through the same thing we're going through."

Says Tomlin: "There is no question that this is a unique week. ... It is a unique week for both teams. The playing field will be level."

title: Start Schaub, sit Slaton vs. Vikes Play Bills' Evans this, every week
content:
A look at the upcoming weekend's fantasy football landscape:

Projected fantasy studs

*WR Lee Evans, Bills. You should be starting Evans every week. He has posted 393 yards and three touchdowns over his past four games. And he has averaged 102 yards a game in his last four battles with the Jets.

*RB Matt Forte, Bears. You might as well play any Bear you have against the Lions. Detroit is 31st against the run. Well-rested Forte should have his first 100-yard game since Week 1.

*QB Matt Schaub, Texans. Don't fear the Vikings, who excel vs. the run but are 22nd vs. the pass. Schaub has nine passing TDs in his past four games.

*RB Michael Turner, Falcons. Turner has struggled against top run defenses. Fortunately for him, the Raiders are 27th in run defense and have allowed the second-most rushing touchdowns (11).

*WR Reggie Wayne, Colts. Last week it was Peyton Manningwho quieted antsy fantasy owners with three touchdowns. This week watch for Wayne to break his two-game slump (five catches, 53 yards). Wayne is averaging 92 yards in his past four games against the Patriots.

Projected fantasy duds

*QB Matt Cassel, Patriots. You may be expecting Cassel to benefit from a high-scoring game. The Colts, however, have the No.2 passing defense in the league, and Cassel has one passing TD in three road games.

*RB Steve Slaton, Texans. As good as he has been, there is no reason to play him this week if you don't have to against the Vikings' second-ranked run defense. Houston will spend its time throwing (see Schaub).

*RB Fred Taylor, Jaguars. Once upon a time, a matchup against the Bengals would have looked dreamy for Taylor. But not after he has failed to run for more than 25 yards in the past four games and has recorded a meager 11 carries in the past two.

*TE Kellen Winslow II, Browns. Maybe he shows up determined to have a huge week, but so far this year, Winslow's production has not matched his headlines. He hasn't scored since Week 1 and has yet to go over 55 yards in any game.

*WR Hines Ward, Steelers. This matchup against the Redskins reeks of a defensive showdown, and Ward will have a tough time getting much done when he spends his evening locked up with cornerback Carlos Rogers.

Sleepers

*WR Mark Bradley, Chiefs. Kansas City is suddenly a pass-happy team, and Bradley benefited last week with 42 yards and a score. This Sunday will be more difficult vs. the Buccaneers, but at least he's the clear No.2 receiver.

*WR Joey Galloway, Buccaneers. A big-name player gets mentioned in this category because he finally is worth playing for the first time this season. Watch for him to take advantage of a matchup against the Chiefs, who are 28th in scoring defense.

*RB Tim Hightower, Cardinals. Edgerrin James is moping about playing time while Hightower is making the most of it. The 224-pound banger should fare well against the Rams' 29th-ranked run defense. He already leads all rookies with six touchdown on the ground.

*QB Seneca Wallace, Seahawks. So 105 of Wallace's 222 yards and both of his touchdown passes last week came on two plays to a fullback. The good news is the Seahawks finally have a hint of confidence in the passing game and will let Wallace wing it against the Eagles.

*RB Ricky Williams, Dolphins. If you don't have Ronnie Brown, this might be a week to sneak Williams in. The Broncos are 30th against the run, and defenses lately have been focused on stopping Brown out of the Wildcat formation.

title: He will look at you square in the eye and say, show ... nothing
content:
Wednesday, as Brett Favre avoided answering questions about text photos and NFL investigations with the same tone of voice with which he might order a drive-thru burger, you looked into his eyes and asked one question:

Why doesn't this guy seem worried?

I'd be worried. You'd be worried. Ol' Crazy Brett is so cool you wonder if the white in his beard is permafrost.

Either he's unconcerned, or he hides his emotions more effectively than Pat Williams hides a belt.

Think about Favre's life: He builds a reputation that makes him the most recognizable name in the most popular sport in American history (Peyton Manning is the better quarterback, but Favre was for a long time better known and more beloved by the average fan), and now anyone with a modem can not only view his (alleged) body parts, but they can leave comments about his (alleged) anatomy and personal life.

The rich and famous learn to handle scrutiny. What's strange, in this case, is that Favre hasn't just handled it, he's shrugged it off like Adrian Peterson running through an arm tackle.

Tuesday, Favre met with NFL security officials to discuss his (alleged) texting scandal with an (alleged) sideline (alleged) reporter.

Wednesday, ESPN carried his weekly news conference live, and the first four questions addressed the Tuesday meeting.

Favre sidestepped them. What was more interesting was what he didn't do: Snap. Obfuscate. Make excuses. Say, "No comment." Hide behind a public relations official. Walk off the podium in a huff. Get angry. Grow impatient. Avoid eye contact. Tell us to mind our own business. Tell us he has kids and wants us to respect his privacy.

No, Favre looked the questioner in the eye, told us the meeting and investigation were league matters, and shifted the conversation to football as if this were a Wranglers commercial and he was the director.

Either he's nuts, or he knows something we don't, or -- and this is always possible with Ol' Crazy Brett -- both.

You would think that the NFL's all-time passing leader being ensnared in a scandal would make him feel paranoid and isolated, but when reporters glimpsed the end of the Vikings' walk-through on Wednesday morning, Favre had gathered the team's defensive backs to give them tips on the Packers' offense.

When Favre returned to the field house for his news conference, he was smiling, and joking with a reporter about his wardrobe.

Favre parried four questions about the investigation. Later in the news conference, I asked him if the investigation had "weighed" on him. I was hoping that if he wouldn't answer questions about the investigation itself, he would talk about his emotional state.

"You know what weighs on me, is last week, was the front five for Dallas, really," Favre said with a slight smile. "I was concerned about that, especially after the first play when I got hit in the ribs -- that was a valid concern."

By the time he had finished his (alleged) answer, he had talked about Adrian Peterson, the importance of the running game, growing old, playing against Dallas, facing Green Bay this week, his responsibility in the offense, and, I believe, how the federal stimulus package will affect the sales of Dixie Beer in his hometown of Kiln, Miss.

When peppered with questions after the Monday night game in New York by the East Coast and national press, he answered the same way.

Whatever is happening behind the scenes, Favre's approach is brilliant. He has said nothing that could backfire on him, has offered no emotional outbursts that would conflagrate the story.

We don't know what we don't know, and Favre's demeanor hints that what we don't know could be pivotal.

What we do know at this point is that Favre does not act like a man who thinks he is about to be suspended.

Maybe he's crazy. Maybe he's a better poker player than Phil Ivey. Or maybe he knows something that we don't, and doesn't mind keeping it that way.

Jim Souhan can be heard Sundays from 10 a.m. to noon and weekdays at 2:40 p.m. on 1500ESPN. His Twitter name is Souhanstrib. - jsouhan@startribune.com

title: CHAD MAY BE ONE TO MAKE FAVRE THROW IN TOWEL
content:
CHAD PENNINGTON can not only pile on the Jets' misery on Sunday, but he potentially can send Brett Favre into retirement.

Four months after the Jets unceremoniously cut Pennington to roll out the red carpet for Favre in August, Favre finds himself having to beat Pennington to keep the Jets' fading playoff hopes alive.

At 39, Favre has given no indication of what he will do past this season and whether he will return for a 19th season.

When asked if this could be Favre's last game and if he wants the future Hall of Famer back next season, Eric Mangini said he didn't want to think about that scenario now.

"He and I don't even touch on that subject," a weary-looking Mangini said yesterday at the Jets' practice facility. "You know, whatever decision Brett makes is a decision that he'll make after the season. And I wouldn't expect him to be thinking about it. I'm not going to be thinking about it. You know, that's something for once the season's done."

The sputtering Jets have lost three of their last four games, including Sunday's 13-3 defeat at Seattle that took playoff destiny out of their hands.

The Jets (9-6) can still win the AFC East if they beat Pennington, and New England (10-5) loses at Buffalo (7-8). They can also earn a wild-card berth if they snap the Dolphins' four-game winning streak, and Jacksonville (5-10) wins at Baltimore (10-5).

Either way, Sunday promises to be an emotional day chock-full of pressure and intrigue. Beyond the playoff implications, the Jets will be facing an old friend looking for revenge while knowing that their future Hall of Famer may be suiting up for the last time.

Furthermore, Mangini's future could be hanging in the balance. The Jets have fallen a long way from when they were 8-3 on a five-game streak after a stirring win at Tennessee.

"It is a little bit wacky, I would say, how everything has played out," offensive lineman Damien Woody said. "We had everything in front of us and we didn't take care of our business. We don't have anybody to blame but ourselves. Put it together for one game. That is the whole mantra."

The Jets desperately need Favre to carry them the way they envisioned he would when they traded for him. In the last four games, Favre has thrown six interceptions with just one touchdown. He has completed just 57.7% of his passes and his quarterback rating has not gone over 61.4 during that stretch.

When the Jets were on their five-game roll, Favre threw seven touchdowns and five interceptions while completing 70% of his attempts or better in four of those wins.

According to a person with knowledge of the situation, Favre lobbied offensive coordinator Brian Schottenheimer to open up the offense before the Buffalo game. Since then, Favre has thrown four interceptions and one touchdown.

"He's an inherently competitive guy," Mangini said. "He's going to look at what he did, he's going to try to correct what he did but he's not going to let that affect the next throw. You want quarterbacks to have a short memory in terms of not letting the last throw affect the next throw."

But if the Jets lose, the quarterback won't soon forget about the collapse.

"Our whole focus is on the Miami Dolphins and winning this last game," Woody said. "We are not going in knowing if this is going to be his last game or any type of scenario or things like that. The season is not over. Guys still in the worst way want to get to the postseason. Everything is on Miami. We are not focused on somebody's job security or somebody retiring. That is not in our control."

title: FANTASY FOOTBALL
content:
By WES PHILLIPS

wphillips@tampatrib.com

It's Too Early To Give Up On Your Draft

It really was a stupid decision. How could anyone sit Brett Favre on a Monday night?

Instead, I overanalyzed the statistics and put waiver pickup/Cowboys QB Drew Bledsoe into the starting lineup against the Raiders. After all, Oakland was near the bottom of the league rankings in passing yards allowed entering Week 4.

Aided by a horrible Green Bay defense which put the Packers way behind, Favre (28-for-47, 303 yards, 4 TDs) proved my math wrong and my gut right. With his 45 fantasy points on the bench, I slipped to 1-3 on the season.

Also, a reader asked last week whether she should bench Colts QB Peyton Manningin favor of Bengals QB Carson Palmer.I suggested going with the hot hand, Palmer. But again, wrong choice. Manning had a slow start, but he is still the best quarterback in the game and should not be benched until proven otherwise.

The point: Don’t give up on your guys too early, and trust your draft. Those who picked up and started Browns WR Frisman Jackson know what I’m talking about.



ANOTHER MISTAKE: A week after a column touting the talents of 49ers QB Tim Rattay, the bubble burst. No. 1 draft pick Alex Smith has been handed the starting duties, probably for good.

We got a very limited look at Smith in garbage time on Sunday night against the Cardinals. And even though he didn’t show much, he has talented receivers around him and a defense that will make sure he is playing catch-up. If you are in a keeper league, you should stash him now.



TOO MANY COOKS IN THE KITCHEN: As the Steelers come off their bye week, it just might be time to send RB Willie Parker on his way. With Duce Staley and Jerome Bettis back at full strength, Parker will get fewer touches.

There also are rumors out of Pittsburgh that Parker is struggling to keep weight on his small frame, thus limiting his durability. Keep him if you are only using him as a third running back or at a flex position. Otherwise, sell high.



RACKING ’EM UP: Who says kickers are worthless? If you own Arizona’s Neil Rackers, you aren’t singing that tune. He had six field goals on Sunday night and is 16-for-16 on attempts this year.

This guy makes a great play for a few reasons. The Cardinals are good enough to make it to the red zone, just not good enough to punch it in. And he is accurate. It’s a combination that can help you pull out some close victories over the span of a season.



INJURY UPDATE: With Green Bay running back Ahman Green (knee) likely to be limited this week against the Saints, backup Najeh Davenport makes a good waiver acquisition. Establishing the run will be a priority this week for the winless Packers as they try to keep the New Orleans offense away from their shoddy secondary.

title: Greener pastures loom if Gang catches Johnson's drift
content:
Keyshawn Johnson never wanted to leave and the Jets never should have traded him. Now they need to get him back.

The Bucs don't want him anymore. But the Jets should. Chad Pennington throwing to Johnson and Santana Moss doesn't sound too bad.

A source familiar with the Jets thinking said they are definitely interested in exploring Keyshawn Johnson: The Sequel after the stunning news yesterday that Tampa deactivated him for the final six games of the disastrous defense of their Super Bowl championship.

That move is the precursor to Tampa trading or cutting him once the new salary-cap numbers take effect after the season and the Bucs would then able to handle the acceleration of his signing bonus on their cap.

After a horrific offseason followed by their 3-7 record, the Jets are a mess. They have a ton of work to do as soon as they can get this season over. GM Terry Bradway, whose mistakes cost them Laveranues Coles and Chad Morton to the Redskins, needs to make bold moves to put this team back together. This time, the Jets need to be the aggressor. And bringing back Johnson is the first step. Getting him would allow them to spend all their draft choices fixing the defense.

Four years have passed since the Bill Parcells administration ran away from Johnson because of concerns about his desire for a new contract. They traded him to the Bucs for two No. 1 picks. The Jets never found a big receiver to replace Johnson.

Now he can replace himself.

There are doubts the Bucs will be able to trade Johnson with teams knowing they will likely cut him before having to pay a $1 million roster bonus due April 1. So, it may just come down to the Jets and Johnson working out a contract.

"Tell everyone I'm in New York looking for apartments," Johnson was quoted on ESPN.com.

Johnson may have clashed with Jon Gruden, leading to the Bucs all but dumping him yesterday, but he had a terrific relationship with Herm Edwards in 2000, Johnson's first year in Tampa and Edwards' last as an assistant coach. Johnson used to watch Edwards' son play in his high school football games. Johnson told Edwards it was a shame Edwards wasn't going to be sticking around Tampa very long, knowing he was going to get a head coaching job.

"Herm Edwards is one of Keyshawn's favorite people. Period," said Jerome Stanley, Johnson's agent.

One personnel director said yesterday Johnson would be an excellent fit in the Jets' West Coast offense. "He's a big, physical receiver. He's a guy who is going to go inside and catch balls, no doubt about it," he said.

And how would he work with Pennington?

"He'd be fine, are you kidding me?" he said. "All they have to do is throw around him and he's going to catch it. The room for error is much greater."

Pennington's game is the short and intermediate stuff. Johnson has never been a speed receiver. His strength would play right into Pennington's strength. The Jets have Moss on the other side to stretch the defense. And with Curtis Conway not having a productive season and doubts about Wayne Chrebet's future because of his concussion, the Jets need to load up on wideouts. By the way, Terrell Owens will be a free agent, too.

The Jets drafted John Abraham and Anthony Becht with the Bucs' No. 1 picks. And having four first-rounders gave them the luxury to draft Pennington. Now they can get Johnson back to join them. Coles made up for Johnson's catches, but not his size.

Johnson was made for New York with his flamboyant personality, brashness and ability to back it up. He won his ring in Tampa last year, but his relationship with Gruden deteriorated this year.

Tampa is 4-6, has lost three in a row. Gruden needed to do something to shake his team up going into Monday night's game against the Giants. Tampa GM Rich McKay said Johnson made it clear he didn't want to be back next year and it was having a negative effect on the team. Johnson was quoted saying he told Gruden he would retire before he played for him in 2004.

But Johnson, too often ignored in the Bucs offense, would welcome a return to New York.

"Wouldn't that be great. Wouldn't that be great," Stanley said. "He had wonderful years with the Jets and has a love affair with their fans. It's a special place to play. His heart is there."

Johnson was told by the Bucs he didn't need to come around the rest of the season. But it may not be long before he's stopping by Weeb Ewbank Hall.

E-mail: gmyers@edit.nydailynews.com

title:
Steel alive and definitely well;
 Pittsburgh fights back from stumbling start, climbs into thick of AFC Central chase
content:
The joke may be on the rest of the NFL, but the Pittsburgh Steelers aren't laughing yet.

After opening the season with successive losses to AFC Central neighbors Baltimore, Cleveland and Tennessee, even the most ardent Stillers fans were wondering if Bill Cowher could still coach, if the two-headed quarterback rotation of Kent Graham and Kordell Stewart wasn't a disaster-in-waiting, if Jerome Bettis was still the Bus or just a bust.

Five straight wins later, the Steelers are pushing Tennessee for the lead in the AFC Central, the defiant jut is back in Cowher's jaw, and even club president Dan Rooney could be seen back-slapping players in the locker room last Sunday following a 9-6 win over Baltimore.

"This is a big win for us," Cowher said. "This is everything we've been fighting for -- to get back to this position. We recognize what's in front of us and I give credit to our players and our assistant coaches for a great job of keeping this football team ready to go.#STEELERS.N2.pn#

"We are fighting ourselves back to respectability and that's all we've asked to do. Opportunities are coming and we are seizing them, and that's all you can ask."

An opportunity is all Stewart has asked for since being demoted to backup for the season opener. He and Graham have alternated starts this season, but Stewart has maintained a polished veneer throughout, refusing to give in to the kind of emotional outbursts that caused angry sideline exchanges with Cowher last season.

Against Baltimore Sunday, Stewart overcame swirling, 20-mile-per-hour gusts and a wind chill of 38 degrees to complete 9-of-18 passes for 133 yards and one score. His 45-yard touchdown pass to Hines Ward in the third quarter ended Baltimore's bid for a shutout and keyed the Steelers' second-half comeback.

Stewart, however, dismissed the notion that he received a lot of satisfaction from quieting his critics.

"I don't care about that," he said. "Doing it with class, doing it with style (matters). It's football, man. I had fun. I enjoyed myself. Of course we want to come out and complete 15 out of 15 passes for 250 yards and four touchdowns. (But) Football is played like we played today. It's exciting, it's emotional, and it's got its ups and downs. Whoever comes out with the "W' -- that's all that matters. And I'm smiling about it."

So too, is Bettis, who at the start of the preseason seemed to be in physical decline and wasn't sure how much longer he would still be in a Steelers uniform. Entering Sunday's game in Baltimore, Bettis was fresh off a streak of three straight 100-yard games. Clearly, he remains more Bus than bust.

"We work real hard each week on trying to stay with the run," right guard Rich Tylski said. "As an offense, we're trying to get that balance, where we can pass and run."

Against the Ravens, Bettis ground out 65 yards on 18 carries, with a long of 14. But his effectiveness was measured more by his ability to keep the Baltimore defense honest, forcing Ray Lewis and Co. to honor the run rather than rampaging after Stewart.

"He's fought through it," Bettis said of Stewart's inconsistent play. "A lot of times you expect so much from your quarterback, you put undue pressure on him. He didn't let that affect him, didn't let it bother him, and played well."

As did the Pittsburgh defense, which is rekindling memories of its proud past. After surrendering a combined 62 points in their first three losses, an average of just under 30 per game, the unit appeared to be more sieve than steel.

Since then, however, Levon Kirkland, Dewayne Washington and the rest of the Pittsburgh defense have been stirring memories of the past, memories of the Steel Curtain and Blitzburgh.

In the past five games, Pittsburgh has allowed a total of just 22 points. The Steelers haven't surrendered a touchdown in four straight outings, and they engineered consecutive shutouts in Weeks Six and Seven against Cincinnati and Cleveland, making them the first Pittsburgh defense to accomplish that since the heralded '76 squad of Greene, Lambert and Blount. The Steelers lead the NFL in points allowed, a total of just 84 through eight games.

"Our defense is awesome," Stewart said. "They kept us in this game today."

And they're keeping Pittsburgh in the race for the playoffs, a goal critics would have laughed off not long ago. Cowher and Co., however, may yet get the last laugh.

"We're not laughing yet," Stewart said. "We still have some more games to go. (But) We won five in a row. You can't negate that."

title: End world hunger by turning Patriots fans into food
content:
The idea was hatched in the bathroom at Ruloff's, mere minutes after a bunch of obnoxious Giants fans ruined Trivia Night by screaming "Let's Go Giants!" during every question. In retrospect, it's probably the best idea conceived in a bathroom since Doc Brown from Back to the Future thought up the flux capacitor after hitting his head on the toilet. My idea? End world hunger by turning Patriots and Giants fans into food.

Now, I know this proposal is a little out there, and you may accuse me of watching a little too much Sweeney Todd. But hear me out. We all know that many people in the world are malnourished and dying from hunger, which is one of the greatest afflictions that the world has to deal with. My modest proposal successfully channels Jonathan Swift and might solve the problem.

We all know that Pats fans taste mildly salty and have a faint aroma of pickles. But it is also true that their toes taste like chocolate. Furthermore, I have heard a persistent rumor that the Raisinets company was founded by an angry Dolphins fan from New Hampshire who used the annoying Patriots fans in his town as the secret ingredient.

Since Patriots fans have increased exponentially in size since the team drafted Tom Brady, turning them into food could solve all the hunger issues of a single continent. But the eradication of the Patriots fans could have benefits at home as well. Recently the Cornell campus has turned into Foxborough West, and "I'm Shipping Up to Boston" by the Dropkick Murphys is dangerously close to becoming the school alma mater. Furthermore, Patriots fans' heads have swelled due to the Red Sox winning the World Series and the Celtics storming through the NBA. Luckily, swollen heads make for more edible food.

While Pats fans taste like chocolate, Giants fans have a more herbaceous complexion. Their blood smells and tastes like a fine Cabernet Sauvignon, and their fingernails might as well be Fritos. Using my scientific calculations, Giants fans from around the tri-state area would feed another entire continent.

As opposed to Patriots fans, Giants fans are newcomers to the annoying fan pantheon. The Giants used to be predictable; they would have solid regular seasons, then inevitably get crushed on the road, with quarterback Eli Manninglooking like he had just been forced to watch the movie Norbit. That all changed this year, when Tiki Barber retired. Somehow the team pulled together, and put together three solid games on the road, leading to a berth in the Super Bowl.

Unfortunately, the Giants' improbable run has empowered another group of fans. While Patriots supporters have been insufferable for many years now, Giants fans are new to the club. While they probably were really annoying circa 1991, the fans were humbled by the Rodney Hampton era. But this run has caused them to become as obnoxious as ever, leading to the unfortunate aforementioned incident at Ruloff's.

While my plan is surely sensible, one may wonder how I plan to turn these fans into food. As causes of death, I originally considered electrocution, forcing them to chug pop rocks and soda and hiring the serial killer from the movie Saw. One of my friends even suggested force-feeding them those little poisonous green balls from the movie The Rock. These methods of death might be a little too intense for our purposes, though.

Here is my final plan - I know someone from Utica, N.Y., who has invented a painless procedure that will turn the Patriots and Giants fans into fresh food. In order to get them there, I will take out a full-page ad in the Boston Globe, advertising a Kevin McHale autograph signing in Utica. Once everyone from New England (minus western Connecticut) enters the Utica city border, we will drop a big net down on them, and start the process.

As for the Giants fans, I am going to advertise during the FOX 5 evening news with Rosanna Scotto, the most respected news program in the entire New York area. In the advertisement, I will announce that I have kidnapped Mark Messier's 1994 Stanley Cup ring and am holding it hostage in Utica. Since everyone in New York has an unhealthy obsession with the 1994 Rangers, they will obviously come to Utica to rescue the ring. Of course, I will have a Mo Lewis poster stationed outside of the Utica border to weed out Jets fans like myself and save them.

Clearly, my plan will work to end one of the biggest problems plaguing our planet. So when one of the presidential candidates inevitably mentions it in an upcoming debate, let's make sure they give credit where credit is due.

title: Perhaps Vikings were due for a dud
content:
SID HARTMAN^$^Looking back on Sunday night's loss 30-17 to Arizona, Vikings coach Brad Childress believes the Cardinals had a lot of incentive to play one of their best games of the season.

"They certainly hadn't forgot our 35-14 win over them last year and the week before [without quarterback Kurt Warner] they had lost to Tennessee [on the last play of the game]," Childress said.

What Childress didn't mention was that a year ago as a member of the Jets, Brett Favre threw six touchdown passes against the Cardinals in a 56-35 New York victory.

And the 38-year-old Warner, who sat out against the Titans because of a concussion, ending a streak of 41 consecutive starts, certainly wanted to be up to the challenge of facing the 40-year-old Favre, who on Sunday played his 283rd consecutive game, breaking Vikings great Jim Marshall's NFL record.

The Cardinals also had another incentive when Seattle beat San Francisco earlier Sunday, assuring Arizona of a three-game lead in the NFC West if they could win.

Still, none of those reasons should be an excuse for the Vikings playing the way they did. But maybe the Vikings were due for a game like that after playing so well all season.

Yes on the same Sunday that the Vikings lost to the defending NFC champion Cardinals, the underdog Raiders went into Pittsburgh and beat the Super Bowl champion Steelers, while the Patriots bowed to an ordinary Dolphins team in Miami. So anything can happen in the NFL.

Maybe the loss will give the Vikings a lot more incentive to beat the Cincinnati Bengals, who visit the Metrodome on Sunday with a 9-3 record, one of the best defensive teams in the league and a hot quarterback in Carson Palmer,who like Warner can throw those quick-rhythm passes that seem to give the Vikings defense problems.

And not only did the Vikings lose to the Cardinals, but they suffered a number of key injuries as well. Obviously, the biggest was to E.J. Henderson, who might be the best middle linebacker in the league. He broke his leg and is out for the season and will be replaced by rookie Jasper Brinkley, who has not started a game.

This was the most physical game of the year, according to Childress. Not practicing on Wednesday were running back Adrian Peterson, wide receiver Percy Harvin, safety Tyrell Johnson and right tackle Phil Loadholt. Johnson left Sunday night's game and was replaced by rookie Jamarca Sanford; Loadholt also missed part of Sunday's game but returned.

The Vikings had 13 players on the NFL injury report, compared to the Bengals' six, though their list includes three who are definitely out for Sunday's game.

Wants extension

Believe me, there is nothing to the rumors that Gophers football coach Tim Brewster is a candidate for the Kansas job.

There also was an earlier rumor that Brewster had applied for the UNLV job.

This guy has worked too hard recruiting and also is eager to display his 20-some redshirts who sat out this season to be looking for another job.

Brewster believes he will have his best team next year with a roster made up of last year's and this year's recruiting classes.

Brewster sincerely believes he has done a good job and deserves an extension and that with only two years left on his contract, recruiting will be hurt if he doesn't get it.

Brewster said his agent hasn't talked as yet to athletic director Joel Maturi about an extension.

Jottings

It's somewhat ironic that Iowa State and Minnesota will finally get together and play in the Insight Bowl because Maturi and other Gophers athletic directors have been trying to get the Cyclones on the football schedule for years without any success. Iowa State's reason for not playing the Gophers was because the Cyclones already play Iowa every year, and they wanted to play only one Big Ten team on their nonconference schedule.

The Gophers lost a couple of outstanding football players who had previously said they would play for Minnesota. Four-star running back Josh Huff from Aldine Nimitz High School in Texas has decided to stay close to home in the Dallas-Fort Worth area and attend Texas Christian after previously committing to the Gophers in October. Defensive back Antoine Lewis from Proviso East High School in Maywood, Ill., switched from the Gophers to Purdue.

The good news for the Gophers is that the word now is that Hasan Lipscomb, the running back from Houston who couldn't qualify academically this year, still plans to attend Minnesota after spending this fall at Blinn College in Texas. Meanwhile, Gophers recruit Jonathan Ragoo, an offensive lineman from Monsignor Pace High School in Miami, was honored when he was named one of 40 players selected to play in the Hawaii Prep Football Classic -- Gophers defensive end Matt Garin of Eastview played in the game last year -- and Zac Epping, a lineman from Kenosha Tremper, was named to the Wisconsin Football Coaches Association all-state team as the only one to be named to both sides of the ball.

Charlie Strong, the assistant head coach at Florida who was hired as the new Louisville coach on Wednesday, was the last candidate interviewed for the Gophers job before Brewster was hired three years ago. At the start of the season, the Bengals were the least experienced team in the NFL, with 3.77 years of experience on an average.

Asked about the successor to backup catcher Mike Redmond, who won't return to the Twins, General Manager Bill Smith said: "Drew Butera and Jose Morales both played most of the year in Triple-A and then we've got a young man named Wilson Ramos coming up behind them that played in Double-A that's one of our better prospects. Butera is playing in Puerto Rico, Morales will be playing in Puerto Rico, and Ramos is playing in Venezuela. So, all three of them are playing. Ramos is doing very well down in Venezuela, I think he's hitting close to .400 and he's got a bunch of home runs and RBI." Smith added about Ramos: "He missed some time during the season with a hamstring problem ... so it's good for him to try to make up those at-bats."

It appears Ray McCallum Jr., the outstanding basketball player from Detroit Country Day who visited here and had interest in Minnesota, is going to play for his dad, the coach at the University of Detroit. So now the Gophers are concentrating on Cory Joseph, the brother of Gophers guard Devoe, and they have a good chance of landing him, although he is being sought by a number of schools.

Sid Hartman can be heard weekdays on WCCO AM-830 at 6:40, 7:40 and 8:40 a.m. - shartman@startribune.com

title: Eagles 18th in payroll
content:
The Eagles ranked 18th in the NFL in payroll for the 2002 season, at least before Donovan McNabb's contract extension last week, according to figures released yesterday by the NFL Players Association.

The Birds were scheduled to spend $58,556,0645, well below the New York Jets' total of $87,343,2313, tops in the league.

The Eagles paid only $7,569,550 in signing bonuses to 12 players before extending McNabb. His deal included a $20.5 million signing bonus.

NFL payrolls can change during the season because of deals such as McNabb's, and often differ greatly from the salary cap, which this season is $71.1 million per team. The payroll includes all player expenditures, while the salary cap covers player costs charged against a team that season. Under the cap, signing bonuses are prorated over the life of the player's contract.

The average NFL salary, including bonuses, is $1.123 million, with the Steelers coming in at a league-high $1,605,087, a figure inflated because they signed so many players this year. The Packers had the lowest average salary, $722,183.
